This dramatically affects engine design, storage, and emissions. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics The following constants must be **memorized** for KTU ESE numerical problems: | Constant | Symbol | Value | Units | |----------|--------|-------|-------| | Molecular weight of air | M_air | **28.97** | kg/kmol | | Molecular weight of oxygen | M_O₂ | **32.00** | kg/kmol | | Molecular weight of nitrogen | M_N₂ | **28.016** | kg/kmol | | Molecular weight of carbon | M_C | **12.011** | kg/kmol | | Molecular weight of hydrogen | M_H | **1.008** | kg/kmol | | Molecular weight of carbon dioxide | M_CO₂ | **44.01** | kg/kmol | | Molecular weight of water (vapor) | M_H₂O | **18.015** | kg/kmol | | Universal gas constant | R | **8.314** | kJ/kmol·K | | Mass fraction of O₂ in air | 0.232 | **23.2 %** | — | | Mass fraction of N₂ in air | 0.768 | **76.8 %** | — | | Mole ratio N₂/O₂ in air | 3.76 | — | mol/mol | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **mass-based** air-fuel ratio with **mole-based** ratio. Air-fuel ratio is **always defined on a mass basis** unless explicitly stated otherwise. The mole-based ratio is converted using molecular weights. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Combustion Chemistry Chain Stoichiometric analysis of alternate fuels follows a strict four-step logical chain: ### Step 1 — Establish Fuel Molecular Composition For a pure hydrocarbon fuel **CₓHᵧOᵤ**, identify the molar coefficients (x, y, u). For fuel mixtures (e.g., biogas = CH₄ + CO₂ + H₂S), use the volumetric/molar composition. ### Step 2 — Write the Balanced Combustion Equation Apply atomic balance for **C, H, and O** separately: - **Carbon balance**: moles of C in fuel = moles of CO₂ in products. - **Hydrogen balance**: moles of H in fuel = 2 × moles of H₂O in products. - **Oxygen balance**: moles of O in (fuel + air) = moles of O in products. ### Step 3 — Convert to Mass Basis Multiply each molar quantity by its molecular weight to obtain mass-based A/F ratio. This is what is practically used in engine calibration. ### Step 4 — Apply the Air Composition Factor Air is not pure O₂. By volume: **O₂ : N₂ = 21 : 79** ⇒ **1 mole O₂ is accompanied by 3.76 moles N₂** (since 79/21 = 3.76). By mass: **O₂ : N₂ = 23.2 : 76.8** ⇒ 1 kg O₂ comes with **3.31 kg N₂** (since 76.8/23.2 = 3.31). > [!NOTE] > **Why 3.76 matters:** When calculating "air required," most students forget to add the N₂. The N₂ is *inert* (doesn't participate chemically) but occupies volume, absorbs heat, and reduces flame temperature. This is why excess air lowers thermal efficiency. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print & Carry) | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Applicable When | |---|-----------|---------|-------|-----------------| | 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ for CₓHᵧOᵤ | $n_{O_2} = x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{u}{2}$ | kmol O₂ / kmol fuel | Pure hydrocarbon | | 2 | Stoichiometric Air moles | $n_{air} = 4.76 \cdot n_{O_2}$ | kmol air / kmol fuel | 1 kmol O₂ ⇒ 4.76 kmol air | | 3 | Mass A/F ratio | $(A/F)_s = \dfrac{n_{air} \cdot M_{air}}{n_{fuel} \cdot M_{fuel}}$ | kg air / kg fuel | General | | 4 | Excess air % | $EA = \dfrac{A_{actual} - A_s}{A_s} \times 100$ | % | When actual air is given | | 5 | Equivalence ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_s}{(A/F)_{actual}}$ | dimensionless | Lean: φ < 1; Rich: φ > 1 | | 6 | Air-Fuel equivalence (lambda) | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\phi}$ | dimensionless | Stoich: λ = 1 | | 7 | Moles N₂ in products | $n_{N_2} = 3.76 \cdot n_{O_2, supplied}$ | kmol | Air-supplied basis | | 8 | Mole fraction dry N₂ + CO₂ + O₂ = | 1.0 | — | Orsat analysis (dry basis) | | 9 | N₂ from Orsat | $n_{N_2} = 100 - (\%\text{CO}_2 + \%\text{CO} + \%\text{O}_2)$ | mol | Dry flue gas | | 10 | Air supplied from O₂% | $n_{air,supplied} = \dfrac{n_{O_2,prod} + 0.5 \cdot n_{CO} + 2 \cdot n_{CO_2}}{0.21}$ | kmol | Orsat basis | | 11 | % Excess air from Orsat | $EA\% = \dfrac{\%\text{O}_2}{0.21 \cdot 100 - \%\text{O}_2} \times 100 \cdot \text{(approx)}$ | % | Simplified formula | | 12 | Air supplied (alternate formula) | $EA = \dfrac{\%\text{O}_2}{100 - (4.76 \times \%\text{O}_2)} \times 100$ | % | Orsat analysis | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Math Typo Trap:** In Excel/WPS tables, students often write `|x|` for absolute value. This **breaks markdown tables**. Always use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX for absolute value. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Stoichiometric calculations are the **backbone of every**: - 🚗 **Engine ECU calibration map** (determines injector pulse width). - 🏭 **Industrial furnace design** (sizing burners, heat transfer surfaces). - 🌱 **Bio-gas plant design** (digester gas composition → burner air). - 🔥 **Rocket propulsion** (LOX/kerosene mixture ratio optimization). - 🌫️ **Emissions control** (excess air minimizes CO, soot; but raises NOₓ marginally). For **alternate fuels** (the focus of this course), stoichiometry is even more critical because: - **Hydrogen** (H₂) has 3× the flammability range of gasoline. - **Ethanol** (C₂H₅OH) carries its own oxygen (u = 1), so it needs less air. - **Biodiesel** (C₁₉H₃₆O₂ approx.) has different AFR than petro-diesel. - **Methanol** (CH₃OH) has high latent heat → cold-start problems tied to AFR. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air for Pure Hydrocarbon CₓHᵧOᵤ ### Given A pure hydrocarbon fuel of the form **CₓHᵧOᵤ** (where x, y, u are integers, and u may be zero for petroleum hydrocarbons). ### To Find Stoichiometric air in **kg air per kg fuel**. ### Derivation **Step 1 — Write the unbalanced combustion equation with unknown O₂ coefficient *a*:** $$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_u + a\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ > *Note: The fuel's own oxygen (Oᵤ) is already present, so it is not on the LHS as a separate species; it is "internal" to the fuel molecule. The coefficient *a* represents moles of O₂ that must be supplied externally.* **Step 2 — Apply oxygen atom balance:** Atoms of O on LHS = $u + 2a$ (from fuel O + supplied O₂) Atoms of O on RHS = $2x + \dfrac{y}{2}$ (from CO₂ + H₂O) Equating: $$u + 2a = 2x + \frac{y}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for *a* (the moles of O₂ required per mole of fuel):** $$2a = 2x + \frac{y}{2} - u$$ $$a = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{u}{2}$$ > *This is the **master formula** for stoichiometric O₂ requirement.* **Step 4 — Convert O₂ requirement to air requirement using mole ratio:** Since 1 kmol O₂ is accompanied by 3.76 kmol N₂, 1 kmol O₂ corresponds to (1 + 3.76) = **4.76 kmol air**. $$\text{Moles of air per mole of fuel} = 4.76 \cdot a = 4.76 \left(x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{u}{2}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Convert to mass basis using molecular weights:** $$(A/F)_s = \frac{n_{air} \cdot M_{air}}{n_{fuel} \cdot M_{fuel}} = \frac{4.76 \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{u}{2}\right) \cdot 28.97}{12.011x + 1.008y + 16.00u} \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$ $$\boxed{(A/F)_s = \frac{137.9 \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{u}{2}\right)}{12.011x + 1.008y + 16.00u}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Aid:** The numerator 137.9 = 4.76 × 28.97 is the *mass of 4.76 kmol of air* in kg. Use this constant for any pure hydrocarbon. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Stoichiometric Air for Methane (CH₄) **Given:** Fuel = CH₄ (natural gas / bio-methane primary component). **Step 1 — Identify coefficients:** x = 1, y = 4, u = 0. **Step 2 — Write the balanced equation:** $$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 1\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ > *Verification using master formula:* $a = 1 + 4/4 - 0/2 = 2$ ✓ **Step 3 — Stoichiometric air moles:** $$n_{air} = 4.76 \times 2 = 9.52 \text{ kmol air/kmol CH}_4$$ **Step 4 — Molecular weights:** - M_CH₄ = 12.011 + 4(1.008) = **16.043 kg/kmol** - M_air = **28.97 kg/kmol** **Step 5 — Mass A/F ratio:** $$(A/F)_s = \frac{9.52 \times 28.97}{1 \times 16.043}$$ $$(A/F)_s = \frac{275.79}{16.043} = 17.19 \text{ kg air/kg CH}_4$$ > **Answer:** Stoichiometric A/F for methane ≈ **17.19 : 1** (matches standard reference data). **Step 6 — Verification using the compact formula:** $$(A/F)_s = \frac{137.9 \left(1 + \frac{4}{4}\right)}{16.043} = \frac{137.9 \times 2}{16.043} = \frac{275.8}{16.043} = 17.19 \text{ kg/kg} \checkmark$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Stoichiometric Air for Octane (C₈H₁₈) **Given:** Fuel = C₈H₁₈ (reference gasoline surrogate). **Step 1 — Coefficients:** x = 8, y = 18, u = 0. **Step 2 — Balanced equation:** $$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ > *Verify:* $a = 8 + 18/4 - 0 = 8 + 4.5 = 12.5$ ✓ **Step 3 — Moles of air:** $$n_{air} = 4.76 \times 12.5 = 59.5 \text{ kmol air/kmol C}_8\text{H}_{18}$$ **Step 4 — M_C₈H₁₈** = 8(12.011) + 18(1.008) = 96.088 + 18.144 = **114.232 kg/kmol** **Step 5 — Mass A/F ratio:** $$(A/F)_s = \frac{59.5 \times 28.97}{114.232} = \frac{1723.7}{114.232} = 15.09 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$ > **Answer:** Stoichiometric A/F for octane ≈ **15.09 : 1** (standard reference: 15.13 ≈ matches). --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Stoichiometry with Excess Air — Orsat Analysis **Problem:** A fuel is burned with **30 % excess air**. The Orsat (dry) analysis of the flue gas is: CO₂ = 12 %, O₂ = 6 %, CO = 0 %, N₂ = 82 %. **Find:** 1. The actual air-fuel ratio. 2. Verify the % excess air from Orsat data. ### Part (a): Actual A/F from Combustion Equation **Step 1 — Theoretical O₂ per mole of fuel:** Assume CₓHᵧ (for simplicity, x = 1, y = 4 ⇒ CH₄-type). Then a = 2 kmol O₂/kmol fuel. **Step 2 — Actual O₂ supplied = stoichiometric × (1 + EA/100):** $$n_{O_2,supplied} = 2 \times 1.30 = 2.6 \text{ kmol O}_2/\text{kmol fuel}$$ **Step 3 — N₂ supplied with air:** $$n_{N_2,supplied} = 3.76 \times 2.6 = 9.776 \text{ kmol N}_2/\text{kmol fuel}$$ **Step 4 — Combustion products per kmol fuel:** | Product | Moles (kmol) | Molar Mass | Mass (kg) | |---------|--------------|------------|-----------| | CO₂ | 1.0 | 44.01 | 44.01 | | H₂O | 2.0 | 18.015 | 36.03 | | O₂ (excess) | 0.6 | 32.00 | 19.20 | | N₂ | 9.776 | 28.016 | 273.88 | | **Total dry products** | 11.376 | — | 373.12 | > *Note: 0.6 kmol O₂ remains unreacted = 2.6 − 2.0 supplied O₂ consumed (where 2.0 is stoichiometric O₂).* **Step 5 — Mass A/F:** $$(A/F)_{actual} = \frac{(2.6 \times 32.00) + (9.776 \times 28.016)}{1 \times 16.043}$$ $$(A/F)_{actual} = \frac{83.2 + 273.88}{16.043} = \frac{357.08}{16.043} = 22.26 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$ ### Part (b): Verify Excess Air from Orsat Data Using the **Orsat-based formula** (dry basis): $$EA\% = \frac{\%\text{O}_2}{100 - 4.76 \times \%\text{O}_2} \times 100$$ $$EA\% = \frac{6}{100 - 4.76 \times 6} \times 100 = \frac{6}{100 - 28.56} \times 100$$ $$EA\% = \frac{6}{71.44} \times 100 = 8.40 \text{ \%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Reconciliation Issue:** We assumed 30 % excess air in the problem, but Orsat analysis yields only 8.4 % excess air. This means the fuel is **NOT pure CH₄**; the actual fuel is richer in hydrogen. **This is a classic KTU "data consistency check" question.** A correct exam answer would either resolve the fuel formula or simply state the inconsistency. **Correct approach** (per KTU model answer): Assume the Orsat data is correct and back-calculate the fuel. From the N₂ balance, 82 % N₂ corresponds to actual O₂ supplied of (82/3.76) = 21.81 % O₂ on dry basis. So excess air = (6/21.81) × 100 ≈ 27.5 % (closer to 30 %). The exact formula: $$EA\% = \frac{\%\text{O}_2}{\%\text{N}_2/3.76} \times 100 = \frac{6}{82/3.76} \times 100 = \frac{6 \times 3.76}{82} \times 100 = 27.51 \text{ \%} \approx 30 \% \checkmark$$ > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** Always use the **ratio formula** $EA = \frac{\%\text{O}_2 \times 3.76}{\%\text{N}_2} \times 100$ when Orsat data is given, as it is the most accurate. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Stoichiometric A/F for Common Alternate Fuels (KTU High-Yield Data Table) | Fuel | Formula | Stoich A/F (kg/kg) | Energy Content (MJ/kg) | Notes | |------|---------|--------------------|-----------------------|-------| | Hydrogen | H₂ | **34.28** | 120.0 (LHV) | Highest air requirement | | Methane (CNG/Bio-methane) | CH₄ | **17.19** | 50.0 | Cleanest burning | | Methanol | CH₃OH | **6.43** | 20.1 | Carries own O | | Ethanol | C₂H₅OH | **9.02** | 26.9 | Renewable gasoline substitute | | Propane (LPG) | C₃H₈ | **15.67** | 46.3 | Portable fuel | | n-Butane | C₄H₁₀ | **15.43** | 45.7 | LPG component | | Iso-Octane (gasoline surrogate) | C₈H₁₈ | **15.09** | 44.3 | Standard reference | | n-Decane (diesel surrogate) | C₁₀H₂₂ | **14.71** | 44.6 | Lower A/F than gasoline | | Biodiesel (methyl oleate) | C₁₉H₃₆O₂ | **12.17** | 37.5 | O₂ in fuel | | Coal (approx. CH₀.₈O₀.₁) | — | **10.5–11.5** | 24–32 | High C, low H | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pattern Recognition:** As hydrogen content **decreases** in the fuel (more C, less H), the A/F ratio **decreases**. This is because carbon requires *less* O₂ per unit mass (1 kg C needs 2.67 kg O₂) than hydrogen (1 kg H needs 8 kg O₂). Pure carbon: A/F = 11.45; Pure H₂: A/F = 34.28. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Stoichiometric Analysis Workflow The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the standard procedure followed for any KTU stoichiometry problem: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Identify Fuel Composition CₓHᵧOᵤ] --> B[Write Balanced Combustion Equation] B --> C[Compute Stoichiometric O₂ Requirement] C --> D[Apply Mole Ratio 1 O₂ : 3.76 N₂] D --> E[Compute Stoichiometric Air Moles] E --> F{Convert to Mass Basis?} F -->|Yes| G[Multiply by M_air 28.97 and M_fuel] F -->|No| H[Report Mole-based A/F] G --> I[Apply Excess Air Factor 1 plus EA] I --> J[Determine Product Composition] J --> K{Orsat Data Given?} K -->|Yes| L[Reverse Calculate from Flue Gas] K -->|No| M[Forward Calculate Products] L --> N[Validate via EA from O₂ and N₂ Percentages] M --> N N --> O[Final Answer A/F ratio and Products] ``` ## 4.2 Architecture Flow: Complete Combustion Stoichiometry for Alternate Fuels ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[INPUT STAGE] A1[Fuel Type Selection] --> A2{Pure Compound?} A2 -->|Yes| A3[Use CₓHᵧOᵤ Formula] A2 -->|No| A4[Use Molar Composition e.g. Biogas] end subgraph CALCULATION[CALCULATION STAGE] A3 --> B1[Atomic Balance: C, H, O] A4 --> B1 B1 --> B2[Stoichiometric O₂ = x + y/4 - u/2] B2 --> B3[Stoichiometric Air = 4.76 × O₂] B3 --> B4[Mass A/F ratio] end subgraph ADJUSTMENT[ADJUSTMENT STAGE] B4 --> C1{Excess Air Given?} C1 -->|Yes| C2[Actual Air = A_s × 1 + EA/100] C1 -->|No| C3[Use Stoichiometric Baseline] C2 --> C4[Actual A/F ratio] C3 --> C4 end subgraph PRODUCTS[PRODUCTS STAGE] C4 --> D1[Combustion Products: CO₂, H₂O, O₂, N₂] D1 --> D2[Dry Products for Orsat Analysis] D2 --> D3[Validate against Measured Flue Gas] end ``` ## 4.3 Schematic: Energy and Mass Balance Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TD FUEL[Fuel Inlet
Mass: m_f
Composition: CₓHᵧOᵤ] --> COMB[Combustion Chamber] AIR[Air Inlet
Mass: m_a = m_f × A/F
Composition: 0.232 O₂ + 0.768 N₂] --> COMB COMB --> PROD[Products Outlet] PROD --> P1[CO₂: 3.67x kg/kg fuel] PROD --> P2[H₂O: 9y kg/kg fuel] PROD --> P3[Excess O₂ if any] PROD --> P4[N₂: 3.31 × O₂ supplied] PROD --> HEAT[Heat Released Q = m_f × CV] HEAT --> EFF[Thermal Efficiency Calculation] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why Block Diagrams for Combustion?** In KTU examinations, drawing a clear block diagram of mass flow in a combustion system (Fuel → Combustor → Flue Gas) before solving the numerical earns **½ to 1 mark** in Part A and clarifies your approach for the examiner in Part B. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1.a] **Q: Define Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio. Why is actual air supply always greater than stoichiometric air in practical combustion systems?** **Model Answer:** **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio** is the mass ratio of air to fuel at which *exact* chemical combination occurs — all carbon converts to CO₂, all hydrogen converts to H₂O, and no free O₂ remains in the products. It is given by: $$(A/F)_s = \frac{\text{Mass of minimum air for complete combustion}}{\text{Mass of fuel}}$$ **Reasons for supplying excess air in practice:** 1. **Ensures complete combustion** — In real burners, imperfect mixing of fuel and air occurs within finite residence time. Excess air guarantees that every fuel molecule finds oxygen. 2. **Reduces CO and unburnt hydrocarbons** — Lean mixtures suppress soot and CO formation (critical for emission norms like BS-VI, Euro 6). 3. **Prevents high local temperatures** — Rich zones can create hot spots, leading to thermal NOₓ and burner damage. 4. **Compensates for fuel-bound variations** — Alternate fuels (biogas, biodiesel blends) have variable composition; excess air provides safety margin. > Typical excess air values: **Gas burners** 5–10 %, **Oil burners** 10–15 %, **Coal-fired furnaces** 15–25 %, **IC engines** stoichiometric (λ = 1) with closed-loop lambda sensor control. --- ### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Q1.b] **Q: For a hydrocarbon fuel CₓHᵧ, derive the expression for stoichiometric oxygen required per kg of fuel. List two alternate fuels and their stoichiometric A/F ratios.** **Model Answer:** **Derivation:** The balanced combustion equation is: $$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ Stoichiometric O₂ moles per mole of fuel = $x + \dfrac{y}{4}$ Per kg of fuel: $$O_2 \text{ required} = \frac{\left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right) \times 32}{12x + y} \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$ > *This comes from converting mole basis to mass basis using O₂ molecular weight = 32.* **Two alternate fuels and their A/F ratios:** | Fuel | Formula | Stoich A/F (kg/kg) | |------|---------|--------------------| | Hydrogen | H₂ | **34.28** | | Ethanol | C₂H₅OH | **9.02** | | Biodiesel (methyl oleate) | C₁₉H₃₆O₂ | **12.17** | | Methanol | CH₃OH | **6.43** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why these?** Alternate fuels are explicitly in the KTU 2024 OEMUT615 syllabus. Hydrogen and ethanol are the most commonly tested. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Q3] **A fuel oil has the following composition by mass: C = 86 %, H = 12 %, S = 1 %, O = 0.5 %, N = 0.5 %. The fuel is burned with 25 % excess air. Calculate:** **(a) The stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel.** *(7 marks)* **(b) The actual A/F ratio and the Orsat (dry) analysis of the flue gas.** *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution** **Given:** C = 0.86 kg, H = 0.12 kg, S = 0.01 kg, O = 0.005 kg, N = 0.005 kg (per kg fuel basis). Excess air = 25 %. --- #### **Part (a): Stoichiometric Air** **Step 1 — O₂ required for each element (kg O₂/kg element):** | Element | Reaction | O₂ required (kg/kg element) | |---------|----------|----------------------------| | C → CO₂ | C + O₂ → CO₂ | 32/12 = **2.667** | | H → H₂O | 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O | 32/4 = **8.000** | | S → SO₂ | S + O₂ → SO₂ | 32/32 = **1.000** | **Step 2 — Total O₂ needed for the fuel:** $$O_2 = (0.86 \times 2.667) + (0.12 \times 8.00) + (0.01 \times 1.00) - (0.005 \times 1.00) - (0.005 \times 0)$$ > *Note: The fuel's own oxygen is available to participate, so we subtract O available from fuel. N₂ in fuel is inert and produces N₂ in products (negligible effect in standard analysis).* $$O_2 = 2.2936 + 0.96 + 0.01 - 0.005 = 3.2586 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$ **Step 3 — Convert O₂ to air using mass fraction (0.232):** $$A_s = \frac{3.2586}{0.232} = 14.045 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$ > **Answer (a):** **Stoichiometric air ≈ 14.04 kg/kg fuel** --- #### **Part (b): Actual A/F and Orsat Analysis** **Step 1 — Actual air with 25 % excess:** $$A_{actual} = A_s \times 1.25 = 14.045 \times 1.25 = 17.556 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$ **Step 2 — Mass of O₂ supplied:** $$O_{2,supplied} = 17.556 \times 0.232 = 4.073 \text{ kg O}_2$$ **Step 3 — Excess O₂ leaving unreacted:** $$O_{2,excess} = 4.073 - 3.2586 = 0.8144 \text{ kg O}_2$$ **Step 4 — Mass of N₂ supplied:** $$N_{2,supplied} = 17.556 \times 0.768 = 13.483 \text{ kg N}_2$$ **Step 5 — Combustion products (per kg fuel):** | Product | Mass (kg) | Moles (kmol) | Mole % (dry) | |---------|-----------|--------------|--------------| | CO₂ | 0.86 × (44/12) = **3.153** | 3.153/44.01 = 0.0716 | 0.0716/0.4980 = **14.38 %** | | SO₂ | 0.01 × (64/32) = **0.020** | 0.020/64.07 = 0.000312 | 0.06 % | | O₂ (excess) | **0.8144** | 0.8144/32 = 0.02545 | **5.11 %** | | N₂ | **13.483** | 13.483/28.016 = 0.4813 | **96.65 % (approx, deducting small CO₂/SO₂ share)** | **Recalculate dry mole % (only CO₂, SO₂, O₂, N₂):** Total dry moles = 0.0716 + 0.000312 + 0.02545 + 0.4813 = **0.5787 kmol** - CO₂ % = 0.0716/0.5787 × 100 = **12.37 %** - SO₂ % ≈ **0.05 %** (often neglected in KTU problems) - O₂ % = 0.02545/0.5787 × 100 = **4.40 %** - N₂ % = 0.4813/0.5787 × 100 = **83.17 %** > **Answer (b):** > - Actual A/F = **17.56 kg/kg** > - Orsat (dry) analysis: **CO₂ ≈ 12.37 %, O₂ ≈ 4.40 %, N₂ ≈ 83.17 %** (SO₂ negligible) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Q4] — *ALTERNATIVE* **An Orsat analysis of flue gas from a furnace burning a hydrocarbon fuel gives: CO₂ = 13 %, O₂ = 5 %, CO = 1 %, N₂ = 81 %. Calculate:** **(a) The air-fuel ratio by mass and the equivalence ratio.** *(7 marks)* **(b) The mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel, and the % excess air supplied.** *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution** **Given (dry basis, per 100 kmol dry flue gas):** - CO₂ = 13 kmol - O₂ = 5 kmol - CO = 1 kmol - N₂ = 81 kmol --- #### **Part (a): Air-Fuel Ratio and Equivalence Ratio** **Step 1 — Air supplied from N₂ balance:** $$N_{2,supplied} = 3.76 \times O_{2,supplied}$$ $$81 = 3.76 \times O_{2,supplied} \Rightarrow O_{2,supplied} = 21.54 \text{ kmol}$$ **Step 2 — O₂ consumed in combustion (from product balance):** $$O_{2,consumed} = CO_2 + \dfrac{CO}{2} + \dfrac{SO_2}{2} - \dfrac{O_{fuel}}{2}$$ > *Assuming no S, no fuel-bound O (typical for coal-derived fuel):* $$O_{2,consumed} = 13 + \dfrac{1}{2} = 13.5 \text{ kmol}$$ **Step 3 — Stoichiometric O₂ (stoichiometric = when O₂ excess = 0):** For a fuel of unknown composition but burning to produce CO₂ and CO in given ratio: Stoichiometric O₂ (if all C → CO₂) = **13.5 kmol** (the CO is treated as incomplete; for stoichiometric, we need to consider what would be without excess O₂). Better method: Use N₂/CO₂ ratio for stoichiometric determination. **Step 4 — Excess air from Orsat formula:** $$EA\% = \frac{\%\text{O}_2 \times 3.76}{\%\text{N}_2} \times 100 = \frac{5 \times 3.76}{81} \times 100$$ $$EA\% = \frac{18.8}{81} \times 100 = 23.21 \text{ \%}$$ **Step 5 — Air-fuel ratio:** Total air supplied = $O_{2,supplied} + N_{2,supplied} = 21.54 + 81 = 102.54$ kmol per 100 kmol dry flue gas. Mass of air = $102.54 \times 28.97 = 2970.0$ kg. Mass of dry flue gas = $(13 \times 44) + (5 \times 32) + (1 \times 28) + (81 \times 28)$ $= 572 + 160 + 28 + 2268 = 3028$ kg Fuel mass from carbon balance: All C in fuel → CO₂ + CO. Moles of C in fuel = 13 + 1 = 14 kmol. Mass of C = $14 \times 12.011 = 168.15$ kg. Assume fuel is pure C (for simplicity, KTU often assumes coal-like): $$\text{A/F} = \frac{\text{Mass of air}}{\text{Mass of fuel}} = \frac{2970}{168.15} = 17.66 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$ **Step 6 — Stoichiometric A/F for pure carbon:** For pure C: $A_s = \dfrac{2.667}{0.232} = 11.49$ kg/kg **Step 7 — Equivalence ratio:** $$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_s}{(A/F)_{actual}} = \frac{11.49}{17.66} = 0.651$$ > **Answer (a):** > - A/F ratio = **17.66 kg air/kg fuel** > - Equivalence ratio φ = **0.651** (lean mixture since φ < 1) --- #### **Part (b): Mass of Dry Flue Gas and % Excess Air** **Step 1 — Mass of dry flue gas per kg fuel:** We have 100 kmol dry flue gas per 168.15 kg fuel (per 14 kmol C). Per kg fuel: $100/168.15 = 0.5947$ kmol dry flue gas/kg fuel. Mass per kg fuel: $0.5947 \times \text{(avg molecular weight of dry gas)}$ Average M_dry = 3028/100 = 30.28 kg/kmol Mass of dry flue gas per kg fuel = $0.5947 \times 30.28 = 18.01$ kg/kg fuel > Alternatively, simpler: Mass of dry flue gas = Mass of air + Mass of fuel − Mass of H₂O formed (if H is present). For pure carbon fuel, no H₂O, so dry flue gas = air + fuel = 17.66 + 1 = 18.66 kg/kg. Small discrepancy due to rounding. **Step 2 — % Excess air (recalculated):** Already calculated in Part (a) Step 4: **EA = 23.21 %** > **Answer (b):** > - Mass of dry flue gas ≈ **18.0 kg per kg fuel** (or 18.66 if pure C basis) > - Excess air = **23.21 %** --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Stoichiometry Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting the N₂ in air** (–2 marks): Students often calculate only the O₂ needed and call it "air." Always multiply by 4.76 (mole basis) or divide by 0.232 (mass basis). > > 2. **Mass vs. Mole confusion** (–2 marks): "Air supplied = 21.54 kmol" must be converted to kg using M_air = 28.97 before computing A/F. > > 3. **Neglecting fuel-bound oxygen** (–1 mark): For alcohols (CH₃OH, C₂H₅OH) and biodiesel, the "O" in the formula contributes to combustion — must be subtracted from O₂ requirement using the formula $u/2$. > > 4. **Orsat analysis is DRY basis** (–2 marks): H₂O is condensed out before analysis. If a problem mentions "wet" basis, it includes H₂O in mole fractions — these are NOT the same. > > 5. **Excess air formula misuse** (–1.5 marks): For Orsat, the correct formula is $EA = \dfrac{\%\text{O}_2 \times 3.76}{\%\text{N}_2} \times 100$, not $\dfrac{\%\text{O}_2}{21}$ which is sometimes seen. > > 6. **Unit errors** (–1 mark): Always state A/F in "kg air/kg fuel" — never "mole ratio" unless specifically asked. > > 7. **CO presence ignored** (–1.5 marks): If Orsat shows CO > 0, it means *incomplete* combustion. Equivalence ratio calculation must account for CO formation; otherwise the "stoichiometric" interpretation is wrong. --- ## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) ### **Core Concepts (Must Know)** - ✅ Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio is the *theoretical minimum* air for complete combustion. - ✅ Excess air is always ≥ 0 in practical systems (typically 5–25 %). - ✅ Equivalence ratio φ < 1 ⇒ **lean**; φ > 1 ⇒ **rich**; φ = 1 ⇒ **stoichiometric**. - ✅ Air composition: **21 % O₂ + 79 % N₂ by volume** → **23.2 % O₂ + 76.8 % N₂ by mass** → 1 mol O₂ : 3.76 mol N₂. - ✅ Molecular weight of air = **28.97 kg/kmol** (memorize). ### **Essential Formulas (Memorize & Practice)** - ✅ $O_2$ moles for CₓHᵧOᵤ: $a = x + y/4 - u/2$ - ✅ $A_s$ moles = $4.76 \times a$ - ✅ $(A/F)_s = \dfrac{4.76 \times a \times 28.97}{M_{fuel}}$ (kg/kg) - ✅ $EA = \dfrac{A_{actual} - A_s}{A_s} \times 100$ - ✅ Orsat-based EA: $EA = \dfrac{\%\text{O}_2 \times 3.76}{\%\text{N}_2} \times 100$ ### **Standard Stoichiometric A/F Values to Memorize** - ✅ H₂: 34.28 | CH₄: 17.19 | C₈H₁₈: 15.09 | C₁₀H₂₂: 14.71 - ✅ CH₃OH: 6.43 | C₂H₅OH: 9.02 | Biodiesel: ~12.2 - ✅ Pure C: 11.45 | Pure S: 4.29 ### **Alternate-Fuel Specific Insights** - ✅ Hydrogen has the *highest* A/F (34.28) → requires largest air intake system. - ✅ Alcohols (methanol, ethanol) have *low* A/F because of fuel-bound oxygen. - ✅ Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) behaves like diluted methane — effective A/F higher than pure CH₄. - ✅ Biodiesel (long-chain esters) ≈ 12 kg/kg — close to diesel (14.5) but oxygenated. ### **Common Numerical Traps** - ⚠️ Don't confuse dry vs. wet flue gas in Orsat data. - ⚠️ Check whether excess air is given — if not, assume stoichiometric (EA = 0). - ⚠️ Carbon in fuel → all goes to CO₂ (or CO if incomplete); never to free C (in ideal problems). - ⚠️ Sulfur in fuel → produces SO₂ (sometimes SO₃ at high T, usually neglected in KTU). - ⚠️ Nitrogen in fuel → goes to N₂ in products; do not include in O₂ balance. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Always draw the **mass balance block diagram** before solving any stoichiometry problem — fuel in, air in, products out, with all masses labeled. The examiner awards marks for systematic approach even if the final numerical answer has a minor error. Practice at least 3 Part B questions from previous KTU papers for full confidence.

topics.title: Combustion Analysis of Fossil Fuels by Mass and Volume - Calculation of Air Quantity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Combustion Analysis of Fossil Fuels by Mass and Volume - Calculation of Air Quantity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Combustion Analysis** is the quantitative determination of the chemical constituents of a fuel (solid, liquid, or gaseous) and the precise quantity of air/oxygen required for its complete combustion. It establishes the **stoichiometric (theoretical) air requirement**, the **actual air supplied**, the **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)**, and the composition of the resulting **flue (exhaust) gases** under given conditions of excess air.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEMUT615)**, combustion analysis of fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas) is performed using two principal approaches:

1. **Analysis by Mass (Gravimetric Basis):** Used primarily for **solid and liquid fuels** (coal, diesel, furnace oil). The fuel composition is expressed as mass fractions: Carbon (C), Hydrogen (H₂), Oxygen (O₂), Sulphur (S), Nitrogen (N₂), Moisture (M), and Ash (A).
2. **Analysis by Volume (Volumetric Basis):** Used for **gaseous fuels** (Natural Gas, LPG, Producer Gas, Biogas). The fuel composition is expressed as volume/mole fractions of combustible gases: CO, H₂, CH₄, C₂H₄, CₙHₘ, and inert diluents: CO₂, N₂, O₂.

The **Calculation of Air Quantity** refers to deriving:
- The **stoichiometric oxygen (O₂) demand** of the fuel,
- The **theoretical (minimum) air required** for complete combustion,
- The **actual air supplied** when excess air is used,
- The **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** expressed both by mass and by volume.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bread & Jam" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of combustion like making a perfectly toasted sandwich:**
> - **Bread = Fuel** (C, H₂, S — the combustible stuff)
> - **Butter/Jam = Oxygen (O₂)** (the oxidizer that makes it burn)
> - **Toasted bread = Flue gases** (CO₂, H₂O, SO₂ — the products)
> - **The Recipe Card = Stoichiometric Equation**

If you spread **too little jam**, the bread burns incompletely (smoky, inefficient → **rich mixture**).
If you spread **far too much jam**, the toast becomes soggy and wasteful (→ **lean mixture / high excess air**).
The **perfect recipe** (exactly the right jam for exactly the right bread) is the **stoichiometric ratio**.

The **Calculation of Air Quantity** is exactly this — determining the *perfect jam-to-bread ratio* for engineering combustion systems like boilers, IC engines, and gas turbines.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Reference Values (Used Universally in KTU Board Examinations):**
>
> | Parameter | Standard Value | Remarks |
> |---|---|---|
> | Molecular mass of air | **28.97 kg/kmol** (≈ **29 kg/kmol**) | Engineering rounded value |
> | Oxygen fraction in air by volume | **21 %** (or **0.21**) | Also called the oxygen mole fraction |
> | Nitrogen fraction in air by volume | **79 %** (or **0.79**) | Includes ~1 % Argon (rounded) |
> | **Air-Oxygen mass ratio** | **100/23 ≈ 4.35** | Per kg of O₂, 4.35 kg of air is needed |
> | **Air-Oxygen volume ratio** | **100/21 ≈ 4.76** | Per m³ of O₂, 4.76 m³ of air is needed |
> | Molar volume of ideal gas at STP | **22.414 m³/kmol** | At 0 °C, 101.325 kPa |
> | Molar volume at NTP | **22.4 m³/kmol** | At 20 °C, widely used in India |

## 1.4 The Two Universal Combustion Reactions

For analytical purposes, the combustion of individual elements follows these **stoichiometric chemical equations**:

**Carbon (C):**
$$\mathrm{C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2}$$

**Hydrogen (H₂):**
$$\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O}$$

**Sulphur (S):**
$$\mathrm{S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three reactions are the **fundamental building blocks** of ALL combustion analysis problems at KTU. Every fuel — whether coal, oil, or natural gas — is broken down into its elemental components and reacted individually using these stoichiometric relations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Combustion Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> The universal combustion analysis procedure at KTU follows this **6-step logic chain**:

1. **Identify the fuel type** — Solid (coal), Liquid (oil), or Gas (natural gas).
2. **Express composition** — Either by mass % (for solid/liquid) or by volume % (for gases).
3. **Convert to per-unit basis** — Work with **1 kg of fuel** or **1 m³ of fuel** at NTP.
4. **Determine O₂ demand** of each combustible element using molecular mass ratios.
5. **Compute theoretical air** by dividing O₂ demand by the oxygen fraction in air (**0.21 by volume**).
6. **Apply excess air factor (if any)** to find actual air supplied.

## 2.2 Analysis by Mass — Stoichiometric Oxygen Equations

For **1 kg of solid or liquid fuel** containing mass fractions of C, H, O, and S:

**Oxygen required per kg of fuel:**

$$O_{2,\text{required}} = \frac{C}{12} + \frac{H}{4} - \frac{O}{32} + \frac{S}{32} \quad \text{[kg of O}_2\text{ per kg of fuel]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the minus O/32 term?**
> The oxygen already present **inside the fuel** (e.g., as moisture-bound oxygen in coal or as dissolved oxygen in fuel oil) is **already available** as an internal oxidizer. It can partially satisfy the O₂ demand. Hence, the available oxygen must be **subtracted** from the gross oxygen required.

**Stoichiometric (Theoretical) Air:**

$$A_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{O_{2,\text{required}}}{0.232} \quad \text{[kg of air per kg of fuel]}$$

where **0.232** is the mass fraction of oxygen in air (since air contains 23.2 % O₂ by mass and 76.8 % N₂ by mass).

**Actual Air Supplied (with excess air):**

$$A_{\text{actual}} = A_{\text{theoretical}} \times (1 + \text{Excess Air Fraction})$$

**Air-Fuel Ratio by Mass (AFRₘ):**

$$\text{AFR}_m = \frac{A_{\text{actual}}}{1} \quad \text{[kg air / kg fuel]}$$

## 2.3 Analysis by Volume — Stoichiometric Oxygen Equations

For **1 m³ of gaseous fuel** at NTP, the volume fractions directly represent mole fractions (by Avogadro's Law).

For each combustible gas component, **O₂ volume required per m³ of component:**

| Component | Combustion Reaction | O₂ Required [m³/m³] |
|---|---|---|
| CO | CO + ½ O₂ → CO₂ | **½ = 0.5** |
| H₂ | H₂ + ½ O₂ → H₂O | **½ = 0.5** |
| CH₄ | CH₄ + 2 O₂ → CO₂ + 2 H₂O | **2.0** |
| C₂H₄ | C₂H₄ + 3 O₂ → 2 CO₂ + 2 H₂O | **3.0** |
| C₂H₆ | C₂H₆ + 3.5 O₂ → 2 CO₂ + 3 H₂O | **3.5** |
| C₃H₈ | C₃H₈ + 5 O₂ → 3 CO₂ + 4 H₂O | **5.0** |
| C₄H₁₀ | C₄H₁₀ + 6.5 O₂ → 4 CO₂ + 5 H₂O | **6.5** |

**Total O₂ required per m³ of fuel gas:**

$$O_{2,\text{required}} = \left[ 0.5\,(\text{CO} + \text{H}_2) + 2\,\text{CH}_4 + 3\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \sum n_i \, \text{C}_n\text{H}_m \right] - \text{O}_2^{\text{fuel}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **O₂ already present in the fuel gas** (e.g., in producer gas) must be subtracted, just as in the mass analysis. This is a frequent **KTU examination trap**.

**Stoichiometric Air Volume:**

$$V_{\text{air,theoretical}} = \frac{O_{2,\text{required}}}{0.21} \quad \text{[m³ air per m³ fuel]}$$

**Air-Fuel Ratio by Volume (AFRᵥ):**

$$\text{AFR}_v = \frac{V_{\text{air,actual}}}{V_{\text{fuel}}} \quad \text{[m³ air / m³ fuel]}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the master reference table for ALL KTU board calculations on this topic. Memorize it.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | O₂ for Carbon (mass) | $C/12$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | From C + O₂ → CO₂ |
| 2 | O₂ for Hydrogen (mass) | $H/4$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | From H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O |
| 3 | O₂ for Sulphur (mass) | $S/32$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | From S + O₂ → SO₂ |
| 4 | Net O₂ required (mass) | $C/12 + H/4 + S/32 - O/32$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | Subtract fuel's own O₂ |
| 5 | Theoretical air (mass) | $\text{Net O}_2 / 0.232$ | kg air/kg fuel | O₂ is 23.2% of air by mass |
| 6 | Air-Fuel ratio (mass) | $m_{\text{air}} / m_{\text{fuel}}$ | kg/kg | Always dimensionless ratio |
| 7 | O₂ for CO (volume) | $0.5 \times \text{CO}$ | m³ O₂/m³ fuel | CO + ½O₂ → CO₂ |
| 8 | O₂ for H₂ (volume) | $0.5 \times \text{H}_2$ | m³ O₂/m³ fuel | H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O |
| 9 | O₂ for CH₄ (volume) | $2 \times \text{CH}_4$ | m³ O₂/m³ fuel | CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O |
| 10 | Net O₂ (gas volume) | $\Sigma(\text{vol} \times \text{O}_2 \text{coeff}) - \text{O}_2^{\text{fuel}}$ | m³/m³ | Use mole = volume at NTP |
| 11 | Theoretical air (volume) | $\text{Net O}_2 / 0.21$ | m³ air/m³ fuel | O₂ is 21% of air by volume |
| 12 | Excess air % | $(A_{\text{actual}} - A_{\text{th}})/A_{\text{th}} \times 100$ | % | Standard definition |
| 13 | Stoichiometric ratio | Air : Fuel | kg/kg or m³/m³ | For complete (no excess) combustion |

## 2.5 Why These Calculations Matter in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Boiler Design (Thermal Power Plants):** Determines the size of FD/ID fans, air preheaters, and economizers.
> - **IC Engine Tuning:** AFR directly affects fuel efficiency, emissions (CO, NOx), and knock resistance.
> - **Combustion Stoichiometry in Furnaces:** Excess air above 15–20 % wastes fuel through heat losses in hot flue gases.
> - **Emission Control:** Stoichiometric balance determines CO, CO₂, and unburnt hydrocarbon levels in flue gas.
> - **Energy Audit & Performance Analysis:** AFR measurements via Orsat apparatus verify in-situ combustion efficiency.

The **stoichiometric calculation is the foundation** upon which all subsequent topics in Module 1 (flue gas analysis, Orsat apparatus, excess air effects, combustion efficiency) are built.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: O₂ Requirement for Carbon, Hydrogen, and Sulphur (Mass Basis)

### 3.1.1 Oxygen for Carbon

Consider the complete combustion of pure carbon:

$$\mathrm{C_{(s)} + O_{2(g)} \rightarrow CO_{2(g)}}$$

Reading the **molecular masses**: 12 kg of C react with 32 kg of O₂ to form 44 kg of CO₂.

Therefore, the **mass of O₂ required per kg of C** is:

$$m_{\mathrm{O_2,\,C}} = \frac{32}{12} = \frac{8}{3} \approx 2.667 \quad \text{kg O}_2 \text{ per kg C}$$

If the fuel contains $C$ kg of carbon per kg of fuel, then the O₂ required by carbon alone is:

$$O_{2,\,C} = \frac{32}{12} \times C = \frac{C}{12} \times 32$$

Equivalently, the **moles of O₂ per kg of fuel from carbon**:

$$n_{\mathrm{O_2,\,C}} = \frac{C}{12} \quad \text{kmol O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why the formula uses **C/12** — it gives the **number of moles** of O₂ needed. Multiply by 32 to recover mass, or divide by 0.21 for volume of air.

### 3.1.2 Oxygen for Hydrogen

The reaction is:

$$\mathrm{H_{2(g)} + \tfrac{1}{2} O_{2(g)} \rightarrow H_2O_{(g)}}$$

Molecular masses: 2 kg of H₂ react with 16 kg of O₂. Therefore:

$$m_{\mathrm{O_2,\,H_2}} = \frac{16}{2} = 8 \quad \text{kg O}_2 \text{ per kg H}_2$$

If the fuel contains $H$ kg of hydrogen per kg of fuel, the moles of O₂ needed are:

$$n_{\mathrm{O_2,\,H}} = \frac{H}{4} \quad \text{kmol O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$

### 3.1.3 Oxygen for Sulphur

The reaction is:

$$\mathrm{S_{(s)} + O_{2(g)} \rightarrow SO_{2(g)}}$$

Molecular masses: 32 kg of S react with 32 kg of O₂. Therefore:

$$m_{\mathrm{O_2,\,S}} = \frac{32}{32} = 1 \quad \text{kg O}_2 \text{ per kg S}$$

Moles of O₂ needed:

$$n_{\mathrm{O_2,\,S}} = \frac{S}{32} \quad \text{kmol O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$

### 3.1.4 Net Oxygen Requirement

The **total O₂ demand** for combustion of a solid/liquid fuel is the **sum of individual demands** minus the **oxygen already present in the fuel**:

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\mathrm{O_2,\,net}} &= n_{\mathrm{O_2,\,C}} + n_{\mathrm{O_2,\,H}} + n_{\mathrm{O_2,\,S}} - n_{\mathrm{O_2,\,fuel\,self}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{C}{12} + \frac{H}{4} + \frac{S}{32} - \frac{O}{32} \quad \text{[kmol O}_2\text{ / kg fuel]}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to **mass of O₂** by multiplying by 32:

$$m_{\mathrm{O_2,\,net}} = 8C + 8H + S - O \quad \text{[kg O}_2\text{ per kg fuel]}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Air Requirement (Mass Basis)

Air is composed of **21 % O₂ and 79 % N₂ by volume**. Since **1 kmol of any ideal gas occupies the same volume at NTP**, volume fractions equal mole fractions. But the **mass fraction of O₂ in air** is different:

$$\text{Mass fraction of O}_2 = \frac{0.21 \times 32}{0.21 \times 32 + 0.79 \times 28} = \frac{6.72}{6.72 + 22.12} = \frac{6.72}{28.84} \approx 0.232$$

Therefore, the **mass of theoretical air** needed to supply a given mass of O₂ is:

$$A_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{m_{\mathrm{O_2,\,net}}}{0.232} \quad \text{[kg air / kg fuel]}$$

Or equivalently, **using the volumetric air ratio of 4.35 kg air per kg O₂** (which is the inverse of 0.232):

$$A_{\text{theoretical}} = 4.35 \times m_{\mathrm{O_2,\,net}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: O₂ Requirement for Gaseous Fuels (Volume Basis)

For gaseous fuels at NTP, the **volumetric analysis directly gives mole fractions** (by Avogadro's Law: equal moles = equal volumes).

For a general hydrocarbon $\mathrm{C}_n\mathrm{H}_m$:

$$\mathrm{C}_n\mathrm{H}_m + \left(n + \frac{m}{4}\right) O_2 \rightarrow n\, CO_2 + \frac{m}{2}\, H_2O}$$

The **molar O₂ coefficient** is $(n + m/4)$.

For 1 m³ of each component gas at NTP (= 1 kmol/22.4 at NTP), the **volume of O₂ needed (in m³)** equals the **molar coefficient** because 1 kmol of gas at NTP occupies 22.4 m³, and the O₂ coefficient gives the moles of O₂ needed per mole of fuel.

For example, for **methane (CH₄)**: $n=1$, $m=4$ → coefficient = $1 + 4/4 = 2$ m³ O₂ per m³ CH₄.

> [!NOTE]
> **General rule:** For 1 m³ of any gaseous fuel component at NTP, the O₂ required in m³ is simply the **stoichiometric coefficient of O₂** in its balanced combustion equation.

## 3.4 Derivation: Air-Fuel Ratio Conversion (Mass ↔ Volume)

Sometimes KTU examiners ask to convert between mass-based and volume-based AFR for the **same fuel**.

For a gaseous fuel with **molar mass $M$** kg/kmol:

$$\mathrm{AFR}_v = \mathrm{AFR}_m \times \frac{M_{\text{fuel}}}{M_{\text{air}}} \times \frac{V_m^{\text{air}}}{V_m^{\text{fuel}}}$$

But since both air and fuel are at the **same NTP conditions**, molar volumes cancel:

$$\mathrm{AFR}_v = \mathrm{AFR}_m \times \frac{M_{\text{air}}}{M_{\text{fuel}}} = \mathrm{AFR}_m \times \frac{28.97}{M_{\text{fuel}}}$$

## 3.5 Worked Example — Solid Fuel by Mass

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 1 (Board Pattern):**
> A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 78 %, H = 5 %, O = 8 %, S = 1.5 %, N = 1.5 %, Ash = 7 %. Calculate:
> (a) The theoretical O₂ required per kg of coal.
> (b) The theoretical air required per kg of coal.
> (c) The actual air supplied if 30 % excess air is used.
> (d) The Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) by mass.

### Solution:

**(a) Theoretical O₂ required:**

Using the derived formula on a **per kg of fuel basis** (C = 0.78, H = 0.05, O = 0.08, S = 0.015):

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\mathrm{O_2,\,net}} &= \frac{C}{12} + \frac{H}{4} + \frac{S}{32} - \frac{O}{32} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.78}{12} + \frac{0.05}{4} + \frac{0.015}{32} - \frac{0.08}{32} \\[6pt]
&= 0.0650 + 0.0125 + 0.000469 - 0.0025 \\[6pt]
&= 0.075469 \quad \text{kmol O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to mass of O₂ (multiply by 32 kg/kmol):

$$m_{\mathrm{O_2,\,net}} = 0.075469 \times 32 = 2.415 \quad \text{kg O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$

**(b) Theoretical air:**

$$A_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{2.415}{0.232} = 10.41 \quad \text{kg air per kg fuel}$$

**(c) Actual air with 30 % excess:**

$$A_{\text{actual}} = 10.41 \times 1.30 = 13.53 \quad \text{kg air per kg fuel}$$

**(d) Air-Fuel Ratio:**

$$\boxed{\text{AFR}_m = 13.53 \quad \text{kg air / kg fuel}}$$

## 3.6 Worked Example — Gaseous Fuel by Volume

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 2 (Board Pattern):**
> A producer gas has the following volumetric composition: CO = 22 %, H₂ = 10 %, CH₄ = 4 %, CO₂ = 6 %, N₂ = 55 %, O₂ = 3 %. Calculate:
> (a) The theoretical air required per m³ of gas.
> (b) The AFR by volume with 25 % excess air.

### Solution:

**(a) Theoretical O₂ requirement:**

From the **Combustion Table** (Section 2.3), the O₂ coefficients are: CO→0.5, H₂→0.5, CH₄→2.0. CO₂, N₂, and fuel-O₂ are non-combustibles.

$$\begin{aligned}
O_{2,\text{req}} &= 0.5(\text{CO} + \text{H}_2) + 2 \cdot \text{CH}_4 - \text{O}_2^{\text{fuel}} \\[6pt]
&= 0.5(0.22 + 0.10) + 2(0.04) - 0.03 \\[6pt]
&= 0.5(0.32) + 0.08 - 0.03 \\[6pt]
&= 0.16 + 0.08 - 0.03 = 0.21 \quad \text{m}^3 \text{ O}_2 \text{ per m}^3 \text{ fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

**Theoretical air:**

$$V_{\text{air,th}} = \frac{0.21}{0.21} = 1.00 \quad \text{m}^3 \text{ air per m}^3 \text{ fuel}$$

**(b) Actual air with 25 % excess:**

$$V_{\text{air,actual}} = 1.00 \times 1.25 = 1.25 \quad \text{m}^3 \text{ air per m}^3 \text{ fuel}$$

$$\boxed{\text{AFR}_v = 1.25 \quad \text{m}^3 \text{ air / m}^3 \text{ fuel}}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Combustion Analysis Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Fuel Type] --> B{Fuel State?}
    B -- Solid or Liquid --> C[Use Mass-Based Analysis]
    B -- Gaseous --> D[Use Volume-Based Analysis]
    C --> E[Express Composition as Mass Fractions]
    D --> F[Express Composition as Volume Fractions]
    E --> G[Compute O2 Demand per kg fuel]
    F --> H[Compute O2 Demand per m3 fuel]
    G --> I["Formula: C/12 + H/4 + S/32 - O/32"]
    H --> J["Formula: 0.5(CO+H2) + 2*CH4 + ... - O2_fuel"]
    I --> K[Divide by 0.232 to get Air by Mass]
    J --> L[Divide by 0.21 to get Air by Volume]
    K --> M{Excess Air Given?}
    L --> N{Excess Air Given?}
    M -- Yes --> O["A_actual = A_th × (1 + Excess)"]
    M -- No --> P["AFR_m = A_theoretical"]
    N -- Yes --> Q["V_actual = V_th × (1 + Excess)"]
    N -- No --> R["AFR_v = V_theoretical"]
    O --> S[Final Result: AFR or Air Quantity]
    Q --> S
    P --> S
    R --> S
```

## 4.4 Schematic Table Representation — Volume Basis Logic

| Component | Reaction | O₂ Coeff (m³/m³) | CO₂ Produced (m³/m³) | H₂O Produced (m³/m³) | N₂ Required (m³/m³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CO | CO + ½O₂ → CO₂ | 0.5 | 1 | 0 | 0.5 × (79/21) = 1.881 |
| H₂ | H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O | 0.5 | 0 | 1 | 1.881 |
| CH₄ | CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O | 2.0 | 1 | 2 | 2.0 × (79/21) = 7.524 |
| C₂H₄ | C₂H₄ + 3O₂ → 2CO₂ + 2H₂O | 3.0 | 2 | 2 | 3.0 × (79/21) = 11.286 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Insight:** The "N₂ Required" column shows that **approximately 3.76 m³ of N₂ enters the combustion chamber for every 1 m³ of O₂ consumed**. This is the source of the famous **3.76 factor** that appears in all flue gas analysis problems (N₂ = 0.79/0.21 = 3.76).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Conceptual Short Answer

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q: Define "Stoichiometric Air" and "Excess Air" in the context of combustion. Why is excess air always supplied in practical furnace/boiler operations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Stoichiometric Air** is the **minimum quantity of air** required to provide just enough oxygen for the complete combustion of a fuel, leaving no free oxygen in the flue gases. It is also called **theoretical air** or **chemically correct air**.
>
> **Excess Air** is the **additional air supplied beyond the stoichiometric requirement** to ensure complete combustion under practical conditions where fuel-air mixing is imperfect.
>
> **Reasons for supplying excess air:**
> 1. **Practical mixing limitations** — fuel and air cannot be mixed in perfect stoichiometric proportions in real burners.
> 2. **Incomplete combustion avoidance** — prevents CO formation and unburnt hydrocarbon loss.
> 3. **Safety margin** — avoids fuel-rich conditions that could lead to explosion or smoke.
>
> *(Typical values: 5–15 % for gaseous fuels, 15–25 % for liquid fuels, 20–35 % for solid fuels.)*

---

### Question A2 — Direct Formula/Definition

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q: A furnace oil has 84 % C, 12 % H₂, 2 % S and 2 % O₂ by mass. Calculate the stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Using the formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{O}_2 \text{ required} &= \frac{0.84}{12} + \frac{0.12}{4} + \frac{0.02}{32} - \frac{0.02}{32} \\[4pt]
&= 0.0700 + 0.0300 + 0.000625 - 0.000625 \\[4pt]
&= 0.1000 \quad \text{kmol O}_2 / \text{kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

Mass of O₂ = $0.1000 \times 32 = 3.20$ kg/kg fuel.

$$A_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{3.20}{0.232} = 13.79 \quad \text{kg air / kg fuel}$$

## 5.2 Part B Question — ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q: A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass:**
> **C = 70 %, H₂ = 8 %, O₂ = 10 %, S = 3 %, N₂ = 1.5 %, Moisture = 2 %, Ash = 5.5 %.**
>
> **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the **minimum (theoretical) air required** for complete combustion of 1 kg of this coal.
>
> **Part (b) [7 marks]:** If **40 % excess air** is supplied, determine:
> - (i) The actual air supplied per kg of coal.
> - (ii) The Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) by mass.
> - (iii) The mass of dry flue gas per kg of coal.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:

**Step 1:** Apply the stoichiometric oxygen formula on per-kg basis:

$$n_{\mathrm{O_2}} = \frac{C}{12} + \frac{H}{4} + \frac{S}{32} - \frac{O}{32}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values (C = 0.70, H = 0.08, O = 0.10, S = 0.03):

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\mathrm{O_2}} &= \frac{0.70}{12} + \frac{0.08}{4} + \frac{0.03}{32} - \frac{0.10}{32} \\[6pt]
&= 0.05833 + 0.02000 + 0.000938 - 0.003125 \\[6pt]
&= 0.07614 \quad \text{kmol O}_2 / \text{kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Convert O₂ moles to O₂ mass:

$$m_{\mathrm{O_2}} = 0.07614 \times 32 = 2.437 \quad \text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to theoretical air (using mass fraction of O₂ in air = 0.232):

$$\boxed{A_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{2.437}{0.232} = 10.50 \quad \text{kg air / kg fuel}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:

**(i) Actual air supplied with 40 % excess:**

$$A_{\text{actual}} = 10.50 \times (1 + 0.40) = 10.50 \times 1.40 = 14.70 \quad \text{kg air / kg fuel}$$

**(ii) Air-Fuel Ratio by mass:**

$$\boxed{\text{AFR}_m = 14.70 \quad \text{kg air / kg fuel}}$$

**(iii) Mass of dry flue gas per kg of coal:**

By mass balance, **Fuel + Air = Flue Gas (wet)**. To find dry flue gas, subtract the moisture from H₂ combustion.

Water from H₂: 1 kg of H₂ produces 9 kg of H₂O. So mass of H₂O produced = $0.08 \times 9 = 0.72$ kg.

$$\text{Dry Flue Gas} = (1 + 14.70) - 0.72 = 15.70 - 0.72 = 14.98 \quad \text{kg / kg fuel}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Dry Flue Gas} = 14.98 \quad \text{kg per kg of coal}}$$

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q: A natural gas has the following volumetric composition:**
> **CH₄ = 85 %, C₂H₆ = 8 %, C₃H₈ = 3 %, N₂ = 4 %.**
>
> **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the **theoretical air required** per m³ of gas for complete combustion.
>
> **Part (b) [7 marks]:** If **20 % excess air** is used, determine:
> - (i) The actual air-fuel ratio by volume.
> - (ii) The volumetric analysis of dry flue gas.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:

**Step 1:** O₂ coefficients from the combustion table (Section 2.3):
- CH₄ → 2 m³ O₂/m³
- C₂H₆ → 3.5 m³ O₂/m³
- C₃H₈ → 5 m³ O₂/m³

**Step 2:** Total O₂ required per m³ of fuel:

$$\begin{aligned}
O_{2,\text{req}} &= 2 \times 0.85 + 3.5 \times 0.08 + 5 \times 0.03 \\[4pt]
&= 1.700 + 0.280 + 0.150 \\[4pt]
&= 2.130 \quad \text{m}^3 \text{ O}_2 / \text{m}^3 \text{ fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Theoretical air:

$$\boxed{V_{\text{air,th}} = \frac{2.130}{0.21} = 10.14 \quad \text{m}^3 \text{ air / m}^3 \text{ fuel}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:

**(i) Actual air with 20 % excess:**

$$V_{\text{air,actual}} = 10.14 \times 1.20 = 12.17 \quad \text{m}^3 \text{ air / m}^3 \text{ fuel}$$

$$\boxed{\text{AFR}_v = 12.17 \quad \text{m}^3 \text{ air / m}^3 \text{ fuel}}$$

**(ii) Volumetric analysis of dry flue gas:**

**Dry flue gas components** (per m³ of fuel):
- **CO₂**: $1 \times \text{CH}_4 + 2 \times \text{C}_2\text{H}_6 + 3 \times \text{C}_3\text{H}_8 = 0.85 + 0.16 + 0.09 = 1.10$ m³
- **O₂ (excess)**: $0.20 \times 2.130 = 0.426$ m³
- **N₂ (from air)**: $12.17 \times 0.79 = 9.61$ m³
- **N₂ (from fuel)**: $0.04$ m³
- **Total N₂**: $9.61 + 0.04 = 9.65$ m³

**Total dry flue gas volume** = $1.10 + 0.426 + 9.65 = 11.18$ m³ per m³ fuel.

**Dry flue gas composition:**

$$\boxed{\text{CO}_2 = 9.84\%, \quad \text{O}_2 = 3.81\%, \quad \text{N}_2 = 86.35\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract fuel oxygen** — For coals with high O₂ (e.g., lignite with 15–20 % O), failing to subtract **O/32** leads to **overestimated air** by 10–15 %. **Marks lost: 2**
> 2. **Confusing mass and volume fractions of O₂ in air** — Using **0.21** instead of **0.232** for mass-based calculations (or vice versa) is a **classic KTU mistake**. **Marks lost: 2**
> 3. **Wrong hydrocarbon O₂ coefficient** — Students often write CH₄ + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O (unbalanced). The correct coefficient is **2**. **Marks lost: 1**
> 4. **Not converting mass to moles first** — Direct mass-based equation $O_2 = C/12 + H/4$ is for moles; to get kg, multiply by 32. **Marks lost: 1**
> 5. **Sign errors in excess air formula** — Some students compute $A_{th}(1 - 0.40)$ instead of $A_{th}(1 + 0.40)$. **Marks lost: 1**
> 6. **Not specifying units** — KTU board deducts marks if final AFR answer doesn't carry units. **Marks lost: 0.5**

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (must memorize before KTU exam):**

- [ ] **Stoichiometric air = theoretical air = chemically correct air = minimum air** for complete combustion.
- [ ] **Two universal coefficients to remember:** O₂ in air by **volume = 21 %**, by **mass = 23.2 %**.
- [ ] **Mass-based formula:** $n_{O_2} = C/12 + H/4 + S/32 - O/32$ gives moles of O₂ per kg fuel.
- [ ] **Volume-based formula:** $O_2 = 0.5(CO + H_2) + 2 CH_4 + 3 C_2H_4 + 3.5 C_2H_6 + 5 C_3H_8 + 6.5 C_4H_{10} - O_2^{\text{fuel}}$ gives m³ O₂ per m³ fuel.
- [ ] **Multiply mass-based O₂ moles by 32 to get kg**, divide by 0.232 to get kg of air.
- [ ] **Divide volume-based O₂ volume by 0.21 to get m³ of air**.
- [ ] **Excess air %** = $(A_{actual} - A_{th})/A_{th} \times 100$.
- [ ] **N₂ always accompanies O₂ in air in the ratio 79:21 = 3.76 : 1** (by volume).
- [ ] **Fuel oxygen subtraction is mandatory** when computing net O₂ demand (it is an internal oxidizer).
- [ ] **Moisture, ash, and N₂ in fuel do NOT consume oxygen** during combustion.
- [ ] **AFR for coal/oil is typically 10–18 (mass basis); for natural gas, 9–12 (mass basis).**
- [ ] **AFR for gaseous fuels by volume is typically 8–12 m³/m³.**
- [ ] **Higher AFR (excess air) → lower flame temperature → lower efficiency.**
- [ ] **Lower AFR (deficient air) → CO and smoke formation → incomplete combustion.**
- [ ] **Always convert mass percentages to fractions (divide by 100) before substituting into formulas.**
- [ ] **Final answers must include units** for full marks in KTU valuation.
- [ ] **Verification trick:** For pure carbon combustion, theoretical air = $11.6$ kg/kg C (sanity check: $32/12 \div 0.232 = 11.49$).

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Combustion Analysis of Fossil Fuels by Mass and Volume — Calculation of Air Quantity.**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcomes, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuels Properties - Calorific Value
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fuels Properties - Calorific Value ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Calorific Value (CV)**, also termed **Heat of Combustion** ($Q_{cv}$) or **Heating Value**, is formally defined as the **total quantity of heat liberated per unit mass (or volume) of a fuel when it undergoes complete combustion with oxygen at standard reference conditions** (typically **25 °C** or **298 K** and **101.325 kPa**), with the products of combustion cooled back to the initial temperature of the reactants. The standard reference conditions and units are: | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Reference Temperature | $T_{ref}$ | **25 °C (298.15 K)** | | Reference Pressure | $P_{ref}$ | **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** | | Solid/Liquid Fuels (Mass Basis) | — | **kJ/kg** or **kcal/kg** | | Gaseous Fuels (Volume Basis) | — | **kJ/m³ (N)** or **kcal/m³ (N)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Calorific Value is the **single most critical performance parameter** for any fuel — whether conventional (petrol, diesel, CNG) or alternate (bio-diesel, ethanol, hydrogen, biogas). Without this property, **no energy balance, no combustion calculation, and no engine/aircraft/boiler design** can be performed. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Fuel Calorie" Picture Think of a fuel as a **"locked bank vault of chemical energy"**. Just as your body breaks down food (a chemical reaction with oxygen) and releases measured "Calories" that you can feel as body heat or use to walk, **a fuel molecule (e.g., CH₄, C₈H₁₈) when reacting with O₂ releases its locked energy as heat**. - A **high CV fuel** = a "dense energy bank account" — small mass gives huge energy (think **hydrogen at 142 MJ/kg** vs. **coal at 25 MJ/kg**). - A **low CV fuel** = a "small piggy bank" — you need much more mass to get the same energy. In a kitchen, burning LPG vs. burning a wooden log: the LPG cylinder of 14.2 kg delivers the same cooking energy as roughly **35 kg of firewood** — that 2.5× energy density difference is exactly what **Calorific Value** quantifies. ### 1.3 Classification of Calorific Value Calorific Value is classified into two academically rigorous sub-categories: 1. **Higher Calorific Value (HCV)** — also called **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** or **Higher Heating Value (HHV)**. - Heat released when **all water formed during combustion is condensed back to liquid form** at the reference temperature. 2. **Lower Calorific Value (LCV)** — also called **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** or **Lower Heating Value (LHV)**. - Heat released when **water formed leaves as vapor** (steam) at the reference temperature; the **latent heat of vaporization** is therefore *not* recovered as useful heat. > [!NOTE] > **Why does this distinction matter in KTU problems?** Steam-engine designers, gas-turbine and IC engine exhaust designers (where flue gas exits at > 150 °C) **lose** the water latent heat. Hence they must use **LCV**. Boiler/heating engineers who condense the flue gas (and recover heat) use **HCV**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation — Why HCV > LCV When a hydrocarbon fuel $C_x H_y O_z$ combusts, water is generated as a product. The standard combustion stoichiometry yields: $$C_x H_y O_z + \left(x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}\right) O_2 \rightarrow x\, CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\, H_2O_{(l \text{ or } g)}$$ The latent heat of vaporization of water at **25 °C** is: $$h_{fg}^{25°C} = 2442 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Therefore, every kg of water that remains as vapor (instead of being condensed to liquid) **carries away 2442 kJ of energy** that is not available as heat. This loss is precisely the **HCV – LCV gap**. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | SI Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $HCV = LCV + (m_{H_2O} \times 2442)$ | Water latent heat recovery difference | kJ/kg of fuel | | 2 | $LCV = HCV - 9H \times 2.442$ | For hydrocarbon fuels with H% by mass | kJ/kg | | 3 | $LCV = HCV - (9H + M) \times 2.442$ | Including moisture M% in fuel | kJ/kg | | 4 | $HCV = 33800\,C + 144000(H - O/8) + 9270\,S$ | **Dulong's Formula** from Ultimate Analysis | kJ/kg | | 5 | $GCV = 8080\,C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240\,S$ | Dulong's Formula (kcal/kg version) | kcal/kg | | 6 | $NCV = GCV - 587 \times (9H + M)/100$ | Practical NCV using Dulong's GCV | kcal/kg | | 7 | $Q_{released} = m_{fuel} \times LCV \times \eta_{comb}$ | Useful heat obtained | kJ | | 8 | $\rho_{energy} = LCV \times \rho_{fuel}$ | Volumetric energy density (kJ/m³) | kJ/m³ | > **Where:** $C, H, O, S$ = mass fractions of Carbon, Hydrogen, Oxygen, Sulphur from ultimate analysis; $M$ = moisture % by mass; $\eta_{comb}$ = combustion efficiency (typically **0.85–0.95** for industrial systems, **0.98–0.99** for gas turbines, **0.25–0.40** for SI engines). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Pattern Note:** In nearly every numerical problem on Calorific Value, the examiner tests the student's ability to (i) compute $m_{H_2O}$ generated, (ii) convert between HCV and LCV, and (iii) apply Dulong's formula. Memorize equations 2, 4, and 6 above verbatim. ### 2.3 Dulong's Formula — Origin and Importance **Dulong's empirical correlation** estimates the calorific value of solid/liquid fuels from their **ultimate (elemental) analysis** without needing an actual calorimeter. It works on the principle that **each element releases a fixed average bond-energy contribution**: - **Carbon** to **CO₂**: **33800 kJ/kg** - **Hydrogen** to **H₂O**: **144000 kJ/kg** (but oxygen already present in the fuel steals part of it; hence the **$-O/8$** correction — 1 kg of combined O consumes $1/8$ kg of H, leaving $(H - O/8)$ as effective hydrogen) - **Sulphur** to **SO₂**: **9270 kJ/kg** - **Oxygen correction**: implicit; if oxygen is already bonded in the fuel, less external O₂ is consumed and less heat is released. > [!NOTE] > Dulong's formula is **empirical**, not theoretical — accuracy is typically **±5%** for coals and **±2–3%** for hydrocarbon liquid fuels. For precise measurements, **Bomb Calorimeter (for solids/liquids)** and **Junker's Calorimeter (for gases)** are used experimentally. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications | Domain | Use of Calorific Value | |---|---| | **IC Engines** | Specific fuel consumption (SFC) and Brake Specific Energy Consumption (BSEC) calculations | | **Power Plants** | Coal tonnage per MW-day, boiler efficiency = $(Heat\ output)/(Fuel\ flow \times GCV)$ | | **Gas Turbines** | Air-Fuel Ratio on mass basis = $1/(AFR_{st} \times LCV_{fuel})$ | | **Rocket Propulsion** | Specific Impulse $I_{sp} \propto \sqrt{LCV}$ | | **Hydrogen Economy** | Comparison metric — H₂ has ~3× the CV of gasoline on mass basis but ~1/3000× on volume basis (justifying LH₂ cryogenic storage) | | **Bio-Energy** | LCA (Life Cycle Analysis) compares LCV of bio-diesel vs. fossil diesel | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between HCV and LCV **Step 1:** Combustion of a generic hydrocarbon $C_xH_y$: $$C_x H_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \rightarrow x\, CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\, H_2O + Heat$$ **Step 2:** Heat released by complete combustion (to liquid water) at 25 °C = **HCV** per kg of fuel. **Step 3:** Mass of hydrogen in 1 kg of fuel = $H$ kg (where $H$ is mass fraction). **Step 4:** Mass of water produced from $H$ kg of hydrogen: $$m_{H_2O} = H \times \frac{18}{2} = 9H \text{ kg water per kg of fuel}$$ > **Logic:** 2 kg of H produces 18 kg of H₂O ⇒ mass ratio = 18/2 = 9. **Step 5:** If this water leaves as **steam**, the latent heat **not** recovered per kg of fuel: $$Q_{lost} = m_{H_2O} \times h_{fg} = 9H \times 2442 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ > **Logic:** Latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C = **2442 kJ/kg** (standard reference). **Step 6:** By definition, this loss equals the gap HCV - LCV: $$\boxed{HCV - LCV = 9H \times 2.442 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$ Or equivalently in kcal/kg: $$HCV - LCV = 9H \times 587 \text{ kcal/kg}$$ **Step 7:** If the fuel also contains **moisture M** (% by mass), water from fuel evaporation adds to the latent heat loss: $$\boxed{HCV - LCV = (9H + M) \times 2.442 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Dulong's Formula from First Energy Contributions **Step 1:** Total heat released by 1 kg of fuel = sum of contributions from each element. $$Q_{total} = Q_C + Q_H + Q_S - Q_{O_{correction}}$$ **Step 2:** Carbon contribution: 1 kg of C burning to CO₂ releases **33800 kJ**. $$Q_C = 33800\,C$$ **Step 3:** Hydrogen contribution: 1 kg of free H burning to liquid H₂O releases **144000 kJ/kg**. $$Q_H = 144000\,H$$ **Step 4:** Oxygen correction: Oxygen already combined in the fuel (e.g., as -OH groups, lignin) is **not free to combust**. The oxygen is assumed pre-bonded with hydrogen as water, so it reduces the **available** hydrogen. Mass of hydrogen tied up by oxygen in fuel: $$H_{combined} = \frac{O}{8}$$ > **Logic:** 1 kg of O combines with $1/8$ kg of H (since the molar ratio H:O = 2:1 and masses are 2:16 = 1:8). **Step 5:** Effective free hydrogen available for combustion: $$H_{eff} = H - \frac{O}{8}$$ **Step 6:** Substituting into the total heat equation: $$HCV = 33800\,C + 144000\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9270\,S \text{ kJ/kg}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Typical Style **Problem:** A coal sample has ultimate analysis: C = 70%, H = 5%, O = 8%, S = 2%, N = 1.5%, Ash = 10%, Moisture = 3.5%. Calculate (i) HCV using Dulong's formula, (ii) LCV, and (iii) energy density if heating value is required for 1 tonne/hour of fuel in a boiler at 85% combustion efficiency. **Step (i): HCV Calculation** $$HCV = 33800 \times 0.70 + 144000 \times \left(0.05 - \frac{0.08}{8}\right) + 9270 \times 0.02$$ $$HCV = 23660 + 144000 \times (0.05 - 0.01) + 185.4$$ $$HCV = 23660 + 144000 \times 0.04 + 185.4$$ $$HCV = 23660 + 5760 + 185.4$$ $$\boxed{HCV = 29605.4 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Step (ii): LCV Calculation** $$LCV = HCV - (9H + M) \times 2.442$$ $$LCV = 29605.4 - (9 \times 0.05 + 0.035) \times 2442$$ $$LCV = 29605.4 - (0.45 + 0.035) \times 2442$$ $$LCV = 29605.4 - 0.485 \times 2442$$ $$LCV = 29605.4 - 1184.37$$ $$\boxed{LCV = 28421.03 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Step (iii): Heat Output in Boiler** $$Q_{out} = m_{fuel} \times HCV \times \eta_{comb}$$ $$Q_{out} = 1000 \text{ kg/hr} \times 29605.4 \text{ kJ/kg} \times 0.85$$ $$\boxed{Q_{out} = 25,164,590 \text{ kJ/hr} \approx 6.99 \text{ kW-hr}}$$ ### 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Typical Fuels | Fuel Type | Formula | HCV (MJ/kg) | LCV (MJ/kg) | Stoichiometric AFR | |---|---|---|---|---| | Hydrogen | $H_2$ | 141.8 | 119.8 | 34.3 | | Methane | $CH_4$ | 55.5 | 50.0 | 17.2 | | Propane | $C_3H_8$ | 50.3 | 46.3 | 15.7 | | Octane | $C_8H_{18}$ | 47.9 | 44.3 | 15.1 | | Methanol | $CH_3OH$ | 22.7 | 19.9 | 6.4 | | Ethanol | $C_2H_5OH$ | 29.7 | 26.9 | 9.0 | | Diesel | $C_{12}H_{26}$ | 45.5 | 42.5 | 14.6 | | Coal (Bituminous) | Mixture | 28–32 | 27–31 | 9–11 | > [!NOTE] > **Mass vs Volume Trade-off:** Although hydrogen has the **highest mass CV** (141.8 MJ/kg), its **volumetric CV** is only **~8500 kJ/m³** at STP — about **1/4 that of gasoline (32000 kJ/m³)**. This is the central engineering reason hydrogen vehicles need 700-bar tanks or cryogenic storage. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bomb Calorimeter (HCV Measurement Apparatus) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Coal or Liquid Fuel Sample
of mass m grams] --> B[Bomb Vessel
filled with 30 atm Pure Oxygen] C[Ignition Wires
connected to Electrodes] --> B B --> D[Stainless Steel Bomb
immersed in Calorimeter] D --> E[Water Jacket
mass Mw kg, specific heat 4.18 kJ/kg-K] E --> F[Thermometer with
0.01 deg C precision] F --> G[Stirrer Motor
for Uniform Water Temperature] G --> H[Reading: Initial Ti
and Final Temperature Tf] H --> I[Compute HCV via
Q = Mw Cp dT plus
Water Equivalent corrections] I --> J[Subtract Acid Correction
H2SO4 and HNO3 formed] J --> K[Subtract Fuse Wire Correction
Cotton Thread Ignition] K --> L[Final HCV Value
in kJ per kg of Fuel] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Decision Flowchart — Choosing HCV vs LCV in Engineering Design ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Engineering Problem
Involves Heat Release] --> Q1{Is the water in
combustion products
condensed to liquid?} Q1 -- YES --> UseHCV[Use HCV Gross Calorific Value
Applicable for Boilers with
Economiser and Condenser] Q1 -- NO --> UseLCV[Use LCV Net Calorific Value
Applicable for IC Engines
Gas Turbines and Furnaces] UseHCV --> Example1[Example: Power Plant Boiler
Domestic LPG Burner] UseLCV --> Example2[Example: Petrol Engine
Jet Engine Gas Turbine] UseHCV --> Recap[Recall Dulongs formula
HCV minus LCV equals 9H plus M times 2442] UseLCV --> Recap Recap --> End[Apply in Energy Balance
Q equals m times CV times eta] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Process Map — Calorific Value Determination Methods ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Method1[Experimental Methods] A1[Bomb Calorimeter
For Solids and Liquids] --> A2[ASTM D5865 Standard] B1[Junkers Gas Calorimeter
For Gaseous Fuels] --> B2[ASTM D1826 Standard] end subgraph Method2[Analytical Methods] C1[Ultimate Analysis
C H O N S determination] --> C2[Dulong Empirical Formula
HCV equals 33800C plus 144000 H minus O by 8 plus 9270S] D1[Proximate Analysis
Moisture Ash Volatile Fixed Carbon] --> D2[Boie Formula or
Goutal Formula for Coal] end A2 --> FinalResult[Final Calorific Value
in kJ per kg or kJ per cubic meter] B2 --> FinalResult C2 --> FinalResult D2 --> FinalResult ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define Higher Calorific Value (HCV) and Lower Calorific Value (LCV) of a fuel. Why is LCV always less than HCV? State their SI units. **Model Answer:** HCV (Gross Calorific Value) is the total heat released when 1 kg of fuel is completely burnt and the products of combustion are cooled to **25 °C** such that all water formed is condensed to liquid form, releasing the latent heat of condensation. LCV (Net Calorific Value) is the heat released when the products of combustion are allowed to leave at 25 °C with water remaining as vapor — hence the latent heat of vaporization is **not** recovered. LCV is always less than HCV because in LCV the latent heat carried away by water vapor (≈ 2442 kJ/kg of water formed) is not recovered. **SI Units:** kJ/kg for solid/liquid fuels; kJ/m³ (N) for gaseous fuels. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** A fuel contains 6% hydrogen by mass. Calculate the difference between its HCV and LCV. If moisture content is 2%, what is the revised difference? **Model Answer:** Mass of water formed per kg fuel from hydrogen = $9H = 9 \times 0.06 = 0.54$ kg. Latent heat loss = $0.54 \times 2442 = 1318.68$ kJ/kg. Therefore HCV − LCV ≈ **1318.68 kJ/kg**. With 2% moisture: revised difference = $(0.54 + 0.02) \times 2442 = 1367.52$ kJ/kg. --- ### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Derive the relationship between HCV and LCV of a fuel. Explain why Dulong's formula is preferred for preliminary estimation of calorific value of coal. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 65%, H = 4%, O = 12%, S = 1.5%, N = 1.2%, Ash = 13%, Moisture = 3.3%. Calculate (i) HCV using Dulong's formula, (ii) LCV, and (iii) the heat released per hour if 500 kg/hr of this coal is burnt in a boiler with 80% combustion efficiency. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation:** - Combustion of hydrocarbon $C_xH_y$ with stoichiometric O₂ produces CO₂ and H₂O. - Per kg of fuel, hydrogen mass fraction = $H$ kg; water formed = $9H$ kg. - Latent heat lost when water leaves as vapor = $9H \times 2442$ kJ. - Therefore: $HCV = LCV + 9H \times 2442$ kJ/kg. **Dulong's formula preference (points):** 1. Requires only ultimate analysis data (no expensive calorimeter). 2. Quick estimation for large coal consignments. 3. Accuracy ±5%, adequate for plant design. 4. Industry-standard for over 100 years. **(b) Numerical Solution:** (i) HCV via Dulong's: $$HCV = 33800 \times 0.65 + 144000 \times \left(0.04 - \frac{0.12}{8}\right) + 9270 \times 0.015$$ $$HCV = 21970 + 144000 \times (0.04 - 0.015) + 139.05$$ $$HCV = 21970 + 144000 \times 0.025 + 139.05 = 21970 + 3600 + 139.05$$ $$\boxed{HCV = 25709.05 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ (ii) LCV: $$LCV = 25709.05 - (9 \times 0.04 + 0.033) \times 2442$$ $$LCV = 25709.05 - (0.36 + 0.033) \times 2442 = 25709.05 - 959.31$$ $$\boxed{LCV = 24749.74 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ (iii) Heat released per hour: $$Q = 500 \times 25709.05 \times 0.80 = 10,283,620 \text{ kJ/hr} \approx 2856.56 \text{ kW}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a Bomb Calorimeter. List the corrections applied while calculating HCV from the observed temperature rise. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The following results were obtained during a Bomb Calorimeter test on a coal sample: - Mass of fuel = 1.05 g - Mass of water in calorimeter = 2500 g - Water equivalent of bomb = 750 g - Initial temperature = 24.5 °C - Final temperature = 28.7 °C - Fuse wire correction = 80 J - Acid (H₂SO₄ + HNO₃) correction = 50 J Calculate the HCV of the coal sample in kJ/kg. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Working of Bomb Calorimeter (Brief):** 1. Known mass (1 g) of fuel pellet placed in crucible inside the stainless steel **bomb**. 2. Bomb sealed and filled with **30 atm pure oxygen**. 3. Bomb immersed in a known mass of water in a calorimeter vessel. 4. Ignition via electrically heated fuse wire. 5. Fuel combusts completely; heat transferred to water. 6. Temperature rise ($\Delta T$) measured with precision Beckmann thermometer. 7. HCV calculated from $Q = (M_w + W_b) \times C_p \times \Delta T$. **Corrections Applied:** - Fuse wire (cotton thread / iron wire) combustion heat. - Acid formation (H₂SO₄ and HNO₃ from S and N). - Cooling correction (Newton's law of cooling). - Radiation correction. - Water equivalent of bomb, stirrer, thermometer. **(b) Numerical Solution:** Total heat absorbed by water + bomb = $(M_w + W_b) \times C_p \times \Delta T$ $$\Delta T = 28.7 - 24.5 = 4.2 \text{ °C}$$ $$Q_{absorbed} = (2.500 + 0.750) \text{ kg} \times 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg-K} \times 4.2$$ $$Q_{absorbed} = 3.250 \times 4.18 \times 4.2 = 57.057 \text{ kJ}$$ Total heat released by fuel = $Q_{absorbed} - \text{Corrections}$ $$Q_{fuel} = 57.057 - 0.080 - 0.050 = 56.927 \text{ kJ}$$ HCV per kg of fuel: $$HCV = \frac{56.927 \text{ kJ}}{1.05 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}} = 54,216 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx \boxed{54.22 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert grams to kg when dividing heat by fuel mass. Marks lost: 2–3. > 2. **Missing water equivalent:** Forgetting to add water equivalent of bomb + stirrer to actual water mass. Marks lost: 3. > 3. **Sign errors in Dulong's formula:** Using $(H + O/8)$ instead of $(H - O/8)$. Marks lost: 4. > 4. **Mixing HCV and LCV:** Using HCV where LCV is required (e.g., IC engine design). Marks lost: 2. > 5. **Ignoring moisture:** Not including $M$ in $(9H + M)$ correction. Marks lost: 1. > 6. **Forgetting fuse/acid corrections** in Bomb Calorimeter problem. Marks lost: 2. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Calorific Value = heat released per unit mass/volume on complete combustion at 25 °C and 1 atm. - **Two types:** **HCV** (water condensed, latent heat recovered) and **LCV** (water as vapor, latent heat lost). - **Key relationship:** $HCV - LCV = (9H + M) \times 2.442$ MJ/kg. - **Latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C:** $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg (or 587 kcal/kg). - **Dulong's Formula:** $HCV = 33800\,C + 144000(H - O/8) + 9270\,S$ kJ/kg. - **Mass basis** (kJ/kg) for solids/liquids; **Volume basis** (kJ/m³) for gases. - **Use HCV** in boilers, condensers, and heating applications. - **Use LCV** in IC engines, gas turbines, jet engines, and rockets. - **Bomb Calorimeter** measures HCV of solids/liquids; **Junker's Calorimeter** measures CV of gases. - **Always apply corrections:** fuse wire, acid formation, radiation, water equivalent. - **Mass CV rankings:** $H_2$ (141.8) > $CH_4$ (55.5) > gasoline (47) > coal (28) > methanol (22.7) MJ/kg. - **Trade-off:** Hydrogen has highest mass CV but lowest volumetric CV at STP — justifying 700-bar or cryogenic storage. - **Dulong's formula** has ±5% accuracy; **empirical** — derived from bond-energy averages. - **For KTU numericals:** always show steps, retain 2 decimal places, convert all % to mass fractions (divide by 100). - **Combustion efficiency** $\eta_{comb}$: 0.85–0.95 industrial; 0.98–0.99 gas turbines; 0.25–0.40 SI engines.

topics.title: Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Viscosity
