# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Viscosity** is defined as the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the relative motion (flow or deformation) of its adjacent layers. It is fundamentally a measure of **internal friction** within a fluid resulting from cohesive forces and momentum exchange between molecules.

Mathematically, viscosity quantifies the **shear stress** ($\tau$) required to produce a unit **rate of shear deformation** (velocity gradient, $\frac{du}{dy}$) within a fluid. In the context of **Alternate Fuels and Energy Systems**, viscosity is a **critical physical property** that governs the **fuel injection, atomization, spray pattern, combustion efficiency, and lubricity** of biofuels, biodiesel blends, alcohols, hydrogen, and gaseous fuels. Standards such as **ASTM D445** and **ISO 3104** govern kinematic viscosity measurement for transport fuels.

| **Parameter** | **Description** |
|---|---|
| Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | Absolute resistance to shear deformation |
| Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) | Ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density |
| SI Unit of $\mu$ | Pascal-second (Pa·s) or N·s/m² |
| SI Unit of $\nu$ | Square meter per second (m²/s) |
| CGS Unit of $\mu$ | Poise (P); 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s |
| CGS Unit of $\nu$ | Stokes (St); 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine pouring two liquids side by side: water and honey.** Honey pours slowly because its molecules cling tightly to one another, resisting any sliding motion. Water flows quickly because its molecules slide past each other with minimal resistance. This "stickiness" or "internal friction" is what we call **viscosity**.

**Real-world parallels for engineering students:**

- **Laminar Pipe Flow** — Picture a river. Water near the banks flows slowly (zero velocity at walls), while water at the center flows fastest. The fluid "layers" exert tangential drag on each other. This tangential force per unit area is **shear stress** ($\tau$), and viscosity is the constant of proportionality.
- **Traffic Analogy** — On a highway, faster-moving cars drag the slower ones forward via aerodynamic coupling. In fluids, faster-moving layers drag the slower ones via molecular momentum transfer.
- **Grease vs Engine Oil** — Grease is highly viscous (resists flow) and stays in place. Engine oil has lower viscosity and circulates freely. In engines, fuel viscosity must be carefully tuned: **too high** → poor atomization, large droplets, incomplete combustion; **too low** → fuel leakage, poor lubrication, pump wear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternate Fuels Context:** Biodiesel (FAME) has a kinematic viscosity of approximately **4–6 mm²/s** at 40°C, which is significantly higher than petro-diesel (**2–4 mm²/s**). This elevated viscosity is one of the principal challenges in deploying biodiesel directly in unmodified CI engines.

## 1.3 Classification of Viscosity

There are **two primary forms** of viscosity that every KTU student must distinguish clearly:

### (a) Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity — $\mu$

- Symbol: $\mu$ (Greek letter "mu")
- Represents the **tangential force per unit area** required to maintain a **unit velocity difference** between two parallel fluid layers separated by a unit distance.
- SI Unit: **Pa·s** = N·s/m² = kg/(m·s)
- CGS Unit: **Poise (P)**; commonly used subunit: **Centipoise (cP)** where 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s

### (b) Kinematic Viscosity — $\nu$

- Symbol: $\nu$ (Greek letter "nu")
- Defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density.
- Represents the fluid's resistance to flow **under the influence of gravity alone** (i.e., self-weight driven flow).
- SI Unit: **m²/s**
- CGS Unit: **Stokes (St)**; commonly used subunit: **Centistokes (cSt)** where 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s = 1 mm²/s

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For fuel specifications, KTU and international standards (ASTM D445) report viscosity as KINEMATIC viscosity in mm²/s (or cSt) at 40°C.** This is the parameter you will see in fuel property data sheets.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Foundation

Sir Isaac Newton's postulate (1687) defines viscosity through a constitutive relationship for **Newtonian fluids**:

> *"The shear stress between adjacent fluid layers is directly proportional to the velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) in the direction perpendicular to the flow."*

In differential form:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress at the fluid layer (N/m² or Pa)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of the fluid (Pa·s)
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to flow direction (s⁻¹)
- $u$ = Velocity of the fluid layer (m/s)
- $y$ = Distance measured perpendicular to flow (m)

### Physical Interpretation of Newton's Law

- The faster a fluid layer moves relative to its neighbor, the **greater the frictional drag** it experiences.
- $\mu$ acts as the **proportionality constant** — a fluid property independent of the applied shear or velocity gradient.
- A fluid obeying this linear relationship at all shear rates is called a **Newtonian fluid** (water, air, gasoline, diesel, most pure biofuels).
- Fluids that **deviate** from linearity (blood, ketchup, polymer solutions, some biodiesel blends at low temperatures) are termed **Non-Newtonian fluids**.

## 2.2 Detailed Derivation of Kinematic Viscosity

Kinematic viscosity emerges naturally as the ratio of two physical quantities:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

Where:
- $\nu$ = Kinematic viscosity (m²/s)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (kg/m³)

### Why "Kinematic"?
The term "kinematic" (from Greek *kinema*, meaning motion) emphasizes that this form relates to **motion under gravitational or inertial body forces** without needing to know the absolute shear force. It appears naturally in:

- **Reynolds Number**: $Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$
- **Momentum diffusivity** in boundary layer theory
- **Dimensionless analysis** of viscous flow

## 2.3 Fluid Classification by Rheological Behavior

> [!NOTE]
> **Rheology** is the branch of physics that studies the flow and deformation of matter. The rheological classification is **high-yield** for KTU exams and viva questions.

| **Fluid Type** | **Behavior** | **Examples** | **$\tau$ vs $\frac{du}{dy}$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Newtonian | Linear, $\mu$ = constant | Water, air, gasoline, diesel, ethanol | Straight line through origin |
| Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning) | Viscosity decreases with shear rate | Polymer melts, blood, printing ink | Concave-down curve |
| Dilatant (Shear-thickening) | Viscosity increases with shear rate | Starch suspensions, wet sand | Concave-up curve |
| Bingham Plastic | Behaves as solid until yield stress, then flows | Toothpaste, drilling mud, sewage sludge | Linear after yield stress |
| Thixotropic | Viscosity decreases with time under constant shear | Paint, ketchup, honey | Time-dependent |

## 2.4 Effect of Temperature on Viscosity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Viscosity is STRONGLY temperature-dependent.** This is one of the most examination-relevant aspects in alternate fuels.

### For Liquids (Fuels, Oils):
- Viscosity **decreases** as temperature **increases**.
- This is because higher temperatures provide more kinetic energy to molecules, weakening cohesive/intermolecular forces.
- The empirical relationship (Andrade's equation or ASTM correlation):

$$\mu = A \cdot e^{\frac{E}{RT}}$$

Or in logarithmic form:

$$\ln(\mu) = \ln(A) + \frac{E}{RT}$$

Where:
- $A$ = Pre-exponential constant (fluid-specific)
- $E$ = Activation energy for viscous flow (J/mol)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

### For Gases:
- Viscosity **increases** with temperature (opposite of liquids).
- Reason: Gas viscosity arises from molecular momentum transfer, which intensifies with molecular speed.

### Practical Implication for Biodiesel:
- At low ambient temperatures, biodiesel's high viscosity can cause **fuel line clogging, poor atomization, and cold-start failure**.
- Pre-heating or blending with petro-diesel reduces viscosity to acceptable levels.

## 2.5 Viscosity Index (VI) — A Measure of Viscosity-Temperature Stability

The **Viscosity Index** is an empirical, dimensionless number indicating the rate of viscosity change with temperature:

- **High VI** → Viscosity changes little with temperature (desired for lubricants).
- **Low VI** → Viscosity changes rapidly with temperature.

VI is computed by comparing the fuel/oil's viscosity-temperature behavior against two reference oils (a paraffinic oil, VI = 100, and a naphthenic oil, VI = 0).

| 12 | $\ln\left(\frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Two-temperature viscosity | Dimensionless ratio |
| 13 | $Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille (capillary flow) | Q: m³/s; $\Delta P$: Pa |
| 14 | $u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4 \mu L}$ | Maximum velocity in capillary | m/s |
| 15 | $\tau_{wall} = \frac{R \Delta P}{2 L}$ | Wall shear stress in capillary | Pa |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of Viscosity in Alternate Fuels

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for alternate fuels?**

1. **Biodiesel (FAME):** Higher viscosity than petro-diesel (4–6 cSt vs 2–4 cSt at 40°C). Causes poor atomization → larger droplet size → incomplete combustion, carbon deposits, injector fouling. Blending with petro-diesel (B20, B100) or pre-heating mitigates this.
2. **Ethanol:** Lower viscosity than gasoline → poor lubricity → fuel pump wear in gasoline engines. Additives required.
3. **Hydrogen:** Extremely low viscosity (~0.0088 cP at 20°C) → high leakage rates, difficulty in metering. Requires high-pressure storage and specialized injectors.
4. **Producer Gas / Biogas:** Low viscosity gas fuels → easy to mix with air, but metering and flame stability issues.
5. **Vegetable Oils (raw):** Viscosity 10–20 times higher than diesel → transesterification to biodiesel is the standard solution.
6. **Engine Lubricants:** Viscosity index (VI) determines lubricant performance across temperature ranges. Multigrade oils (SAE 10W-30) have high VI.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Newton's Law of Viscosity from a Force Balance

Consider two large parallel plates, each of area $A$ m², separated by a thin film of fluid of thickness $dy$ (m). The lower plate is stationary; the upper plate moves with velocity $u + du$ relative to the lower one.

### Step 1 — Define the velocity gradient
The velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) across the fluid layer is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} \quad \left[ \text{units: } \frac{\text{m/s}}{\text{m}} = \text{s}^{-1} \right]$$

### Step 2 — Define the shear force
A tangential force $F$ is required to maintain the motion of the upper plate. The shear stress is:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} \quad [\text{Pa}]$$

### Step 3 — Apply Newton's Postulate
Newton proposed that the shear stress is directly proportional to the velocity gradient. Introducing the constant of proportionality $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity):

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy} \implies \tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

### Step 4 — Rearrangement
Solving for viscosity:

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy} = \frac{F/A}{du/dy}$$

> **Result:** This constitutive equation forms the basis of all viscous flow analysis. The **higher** the viscosity, the **more force per unit area** is required to produce a given velocity gradient.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation (Capillary Viscometer)

The **capillary viscometer** is the reference instrument for measuring kinematic viscosity per **ASTM D445**. It works on the principle of **steady, laminar, fully-developed viscous flow through a circular tube**.

### Step 1 — Setup
A vertical capillary tube of:
- Length = $L$ (m)
- Radius = $R$ (m)
- Pressure drop = $\Delta P$ (Pa)
- Fluid: dynamic viscosity $\mu$, density $\rho$

The volumetric flow rate $Q$ (m³/s) is measured by collecting the fluid in a graduated receiver.

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity to a cylindrical fluid element
Consider a fluid cylinder of radius $r$ (where $0 \le r \le R$) inside the capillary. The shear stress on the cylindrical surface of area $2\pi r L$ is balanced by the pressure force on the cross-section $\pi r^2$:

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

### Step 3 — Solve for shear stress
$$\tau = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into Newton's Law
From $\tau = \mu \cdot \left(-\frac{du}{dr}\right)$ (negative sign because velocity decreases with $r$):

$$\mu \cdot \left(-\frac{du}{dr}\right) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

$$-\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 \mu L}$$

### Step 5 — Integrate from $r = 0$ (centerline, max velocity $u_{max}$) to $r = R$ (wall, $u = 0$ for no-slip)

$$u_{max} = \int_0^R \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 \mu L} dr = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^2}{2} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4 \mu L}$$

### Step 6 — General velocity profile
The velocity at any radius $r$ is parabolic:

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

### Step 7 — Volumetric flow rate
Integrate over the cross-section:

$$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 - r^2) r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[\frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left(\frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4}\right) = \frac{\pi \Delta P \cdot R^4}{8 \mu L}$$

### Step 8 — Final Hagen-Poiseuille Equation

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}}$$

### Step 9 — Mean velocity
$$\bar{u} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{R^2 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

> **Key insight:** Flow rate scales with **$R^4$** — a tiny change in capillary radius dramatically changes flow rate. This is why viscometer capillaries are precisely machined.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Kinematic Viscosity from Capillary Viscometer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Style — 7 marks)**

**Problem:** A Redwood viscometer (No. 1) is used to measure the viscosity of a biodiesel sample. **50 mL** of fuel flows through the capillary in **385 seconds** at a test temperature of **40°C**. The viscometer constant is $C = 0.0026$. Determine the **kinematic viscosity** in **cSt** and **m²/s**. Comment on whether this fuel meets the **ASTM D6751 biodiesel standard** (which requires kinematic viscosity between **1.9 and 6.0 mm²/s at 40°C**).

### Step 1 — Recall Redwood No. 1 equation
For Redwood time $t > 60$ s:

$$\nu = C \cdot t - \frac{K}{t}$$

Where for Redwood No. 1: $C = 0.0026$ and $K = 1.72$ (standard empirical constants).

### Step 2 — Substitute the values
Given: $t = 385$ s (and 50 mL is the standard Redwood collection volume).

$$\nu = (0.0026)(385) - \frac{1.72}{385}$$

### Step 3 — Compute each term
- First term: $0.0026 \times 385 = 1.0010$ cSt
- Second term: $\frac{1.72}{385} = 0.00447$ cSt

### Step 4 — Subtract
$$\nu = 1.0010 - 0.00447 = 0.9965 \text{ cSt}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to SI units (m²/s)
$$0.9965 \text{ cSt} \times 10^{-6} = 9.965 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

### Step 6 — Comparison with ASTM D6751
The computed kinematic viscosity ≈ **0.997 cSt ≈ 1.0 mm²/s** is **BELOW** the ASTM D6751 lower limit of 1.9 mm²/s.

### Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation
> **Result:** This biodiesel sample is **too thin** (unusually low viscosity). It may indicate over-processing, contamination with light solvents, or fuel adulteration. The fuel **fails** the ASTM D6751 kinematic viscosity specification and is unsuitable for direct use in CI engines without reformulation.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Effect of Temperature on Viscosity

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Style — 7 marks)**

**Problem:** A bio-oil sample has a dynamic viscosity of **0.250 Pa·s at 30°C (303 K)**. If the activation energy for viscous flow is **E = 25,000 J/mol**, determine the dynamic viscosity at **80°C (353 K)**. Use R = 8.314 J/(mol·K).

### Step 1 — Apply two-temperature Andrade form

$$\ln\left(\frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values
- $\mu_1 = 0.250$ Pa·s
- $T_1 = 303$ K
- $T_2 = 353$ K
- $E = 25{,}000$ J/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{353} - \frac{1}{303}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the difference
- $\frac{1}{353} = 2.8329 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹
- $\frac{1}{303} = 3.3003 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹
- Difference: $2.8329 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3003 \times 10^{-3} = -4.674 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹

### Step 4 — Compute $\frac{E}{R}$ term
$$\frac{E}{R} = \frac{25{,}000}{8.314} = 3007.0 \text{ K}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the right-hand side
$$3007.0 \times (-4.674 \times 10^{-4}) = -1.4055$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $\mu_2$

$$\ln\left(\frac{\mu_2}{0.250}\right) = -1.4055$$

$$\frac{\mu_2}{0.250} = e^{-1.4055} = 0.2452$$

$$\mu_2 = 0.250 \times 0.2452 = 0.0613 \text{ Pa·s}$$

### Step 7 — Verify physical intuition
> **Result:** Viscosity **decreases** from 0.250 Pa·s to **0.0613 Pa·s** as temperature rises from 30°C to 80°C. This is physically consistent: heating a liquid weakens cohesive forces, reducing internal friction. Such a temperature rise is **highly beneficial** for atomizing high-viscosity biofuels in CI engines.

---

## 3.5 Conversion Between Viscometer Scales

In the era of standardized SI units, conversions are needed when older Redwood, Saybolt, or Engler data must be interpreted:

| **Viscometer** | **Equation (for t above threshold)** | **Threshold** |
|---|---|---|
| Redwood No. 1 | $\nu (\text{cSt}) = 0.0026 t - \frac{1.72}{t}$ | $t > 60$ s |
| Saybolt Universal | $\nu (\text{cSt}) = 0.0022 t - \frac{1.80}{t}$ | $t > 70$ s |
| Engler | $\nu (\text{cSt}) = 0.00147 t - \frac{3.74}{t}$ | All ranges |

**Example:** A fuel takes **250 s** in a Saybolt viscometer. Compute $\nu$:
$$\nu = (0.0022)(250) - \frac{1.80}{250} = 0.550 - 0.0072 = 0.5428 \text{ cSt}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Newton's Viscosity Concept — Parallel Plate Model

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the conceptual model of Newton's law of viscosity using the parallel plate analogy with all the relevant physical quantities annotated.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Upper Plate moving with velocity u + du"]:::movingPlate
    B["Fluid Layer of thickness dy with velocity gradient du/dy"]:::fluidLayer
    C["Lower Stationary Plate with velocity u = 0"]:::fixedPlate
    D["Shear Force F applied parallel to upper plate"]:::appliedForce
    E["Shear Stress tau equals F divided by A"]:::shearStress
    F["Dynamic Viscosity mu of fluid"]:::viscosityProperty
    G["Result: tau = mu times du by dy"]:::lawEquation

    A --> B
    B --> C
    D --> A
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    B --> G

    classDef movingPlate fill:#FFD580,stroke:#D97A00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **top yellow block** represents the upper plate moving at velocity $u + du$.
- The **green middle block** is the fluid film of thickness $dy$ experiencing the velocity gradient.
- The **blue block** is the stationary lower plate ($u = 0$, no-slip).
- The **red arrow** indicates the applied shear force $F$ required to drag the upper plate.
- The **purple block** represents the shear stress $\tau = F/A$.
- The **orange block** introduces the proportionality constant $\mu$.
- The final **light-blue block** synthesizes Newton's law: $\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$.

```mermaid
    classDef caution fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#C9184A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef caution2 fill:#E63946,stroke:#9D0208,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- As **temperature rises** (red), molecular **kinetic energy** increases (orange).
- **Cohesive forces weaken** (yellow), so **fluid layers slide** more easily (blue).
- This causes **viscosity to drop** (green) for liquid fuels.
- For biodiesel, this means **better atomization** (light blue) and improved **combustion** (teal).
- However, the **caution branch** (red) warns of vapor lock and oil film degradation if over-heated.

---

## 4.4 Viscosity Classification Tree — Rheological Overview

**Reading the diagram:**
- All fluids (dark blue root) split into **Newtonian** (green) and **Non-Newtonian** (red).
- **Newtonian fluids** (light green block at bottom-left) include all common fuels relevant to alternate fuels engineering.
- **Non-Newtonian fluids** split by time-dependence: time-independent (yellow) and time-dependent (orange).
- **Pseudoplastic, Dilatant, and Bingham Plastic** are time-independent sub-classes.
- **Thixotropic, Rheopectic, and Viscoelastic** are time-dependent sub-classes.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### **Q1. Define dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI units and the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

**Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity ($\mu$):** It is defined as the tangential force per unit area required to maintain a unit velocity difference between two parallel fluid layers separated by a unit distance. It is the proportionality constant in Newton's law of viscosity.

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy}$$

- **SI Unit:** Pascal-second (Pa·s) or N·s/m²
- **CGS Unit:** Poise (P); 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s
- **Subunit:** Centipoise (cP) = 0.001 Pa·s

**Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** It is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid. It represents the fluid's resistance to flow under the action of gravitational or body forces.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

- **SI Unit:** m²/s
- **CGS Unit:** Stokes (St); 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s
- **Subunit:** Centistokes (cSt) = 1 mm²/s = 10⁻⁶ m²/s

> **Examiner's Note:** The relationship $\nu = \mu/\rho$ is mandatory. Writing just "Pa·s" and "m²/s" without distinguishing between the two viscosity types is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### **Q2. Explain briefly the effect of temperature on the viscosity of (i) liquids and (ii) gases. Why is this relevant for biodiesel engine operation?**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Liquids (including biodiesel, ethanol, vegetable oils):**
- Viscosity **decreases** with **increase in temperature**.
- **Reason:** Higher temperature increases molecular kinetic energy, weakening cohesive (intermolecular) forces between molecules. The fluid layers slide past each other with less internal friction.
- Empirical relation (Andrade's equation):

$$\mu = A \cdot e^{\frac{E}{RT}}$$

**(ii) Gases (including hydrogen, methane, producer gas):**
- Viscosity **increases** with **increase in temperature**.
- **Reason:** Gas viscosity arises from molecular momentum transfer during collisions. Higher temperature increases molecular speeds and collision frequency, enhancing momentum exchange.
- Approximate relation: $\mu \propto T^{n}$, where $n \approx 0.5$ to $1$.

**Relevance for biodiesel engine operation:**
- At low ambient temperatures, biodiesel's high viscosity (4–6 cSt at 40°C) causes **poor atomization**, **large droplet size**, and **incomplete combustion**.
- Pre-heating the fuel before injection lowers viscosity to diesel-equivalent levels, ensuring proper spray pattern, efficient combustion, and reduced smoke emissions.
- However, excessive heating can cause **fuel vapor lock** and degrade the lubricating oil film in fuel pumps.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive Newton's law of viscosity. Clearly state the assumptions made and define each term. [**7 marks**]

**(b)** A capillary viscometer has a tube radius of **1.5 mm** and length of **100 mm**. The pressure drop across the capillary is **1200 Pa**. Calculate:
- (i) The maximum velocity at the centerline of the capillary.
- (ii) The shear stress at the wall.
- (iii) The volumetric flow rate.
- Take dynamic viscosity of the fluid $\mu = 0.085$ Pa·s. [**7 marks**]

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation of Newton's Law of Viscosity [7 marks]**

**Assumptions:**
1. The fluid is **homogeneous** and **isotropic**.
2. The fluid is **Newtonian** (viscosity is constant, independent of shear rate).
3. Flow is **laminar** and **steady**.
4. The fluid is **incompressible**.
5. There is **no slip** at the boundaries.

**Step 1:** Consider two parallel horizontal plates of area $A$ m² separated by a thin film of fluid of thickness $dy$ m. The lower plate is stationary; the upper plate moves with velocity $u + du$ relative to the lower plate.

**Step 2:** The velocity gradient perpendicular to the direction of flow is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} \quad \text{(units: s}^{-1}\text{)}$$

**Step 3:** A tangential force $F$ is applied to the upper plate to maintain its motion. The shear stress on the fluid layer is:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} \quad \text{(units: Pa or N/m}^2\text{)}$$

**Step 4:** Newton's postulate states that shear stress is directly proportional to the velocity gradient:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 5:** Introducing the constant of proportionality $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity):

$$\boxed{\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}}$$

**Step 6:** This is **Newton's Law of Viscosity**. Rearranging:

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy}$$

Viscosity has units: $\frac{\text{Pa}}{\text{s}^{-1}} = \text{Pa·s}$.

> **Higher $\mu$ → more force required for the same velocity gradient.**

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**Given data:**
- Capillary radius: $R = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Capillary length: $L = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1$ m
- Pressure drop: $\Delta P = 1200$ Pa
- Dynamic viscosity: $\mu = 0.085$ Pa·s

**Part (i) — Maximum velocity at centerline:**

From Section 3.2, Step 5:

$$u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4 \mu L}$$

Substituting:

$$u_{max} = \frac{1200 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4 \times 0.085 \times 0.1}$$

Computing the numerator:
- $R^2 = (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-6}$ m²
- Numerator: $1200 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6} = 2.7 \times 10^{-3}$

Computing the denominator:
- $4 \times 0.085 \times 0.1 = 0.034$

Therefore:
$$u_{max} = \frac{2.7 \times 10^{-3}}{0.034} = 0.0794 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{u_{max} \approx 0.0794 \text{ m/s} = 79.4 \text{ mm/s}}$$

**Part (ii) — Wall shear stress:**

From Section 3.2, Step 3:

$$\tau_{wall} = \frac{R \cdot \Delta P}{2L}$$

Substituting:

$$\tau_{wall} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1200}{2 \times 0.1} = \frac{1.8}{0.2} = 9.0 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{wall} = 9.0 \text{ Pa}}$$

**Part (iii) — Volumetric flow rate:**

From Section 3.2, Step 8 (Hagen-Poiseuille equation):

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

Computing $R^4$:
- $R^4 = (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^4 = 5.0625 \times 10^{-12}$ m⁴

Numerator: $\pi \times 5.0625 \times 10^{-12} \times 1200 = 1.9085 \times 10^{-8}$ m³·Pa

Denominator: $8 \times 0.085 \times 0.1 = 0.068$ Pa·s

$$Q = \frac{1.9085 \times 10^{-8}}{0.068} = 2.806 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 2.806 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 0.2806 \text{ mL/s}}$$

**Verification using mean velocity:**
- $\bar{u} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{2.806 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.0397$ m/s
- Ratio $\bar{u}/u_{max} = 0.0397/0.0794 = 0.5$ ✓ (consistent with parabolic profile)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is kinematic viscosity? Explain the working principle of a **Redwood viscometer No. 1** with a neat sketch. State its standard formula for converting efflux time to kinematic viscosity. [**7 marks**]

**(b)** A **biodiesel (B100)** sample has the following properties at 40°C:
- Dynamic viscosity: $\mu_1 = 0.0060$ Pa·s
- Density: $\rho = 880$ kg/m³

Compute:
- (i) The kinematic viscosity at 40°C in m²/s and cSt.
- (ii) The dynamic viscosity at 80°C, given the activation energy for viscous flow $E = 18{,}500$ J/mol. Use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
- (iii) Comment on whether the original kinematic viscosity satisfies the **ASTM D6751 standard for B100** (1.9–6.0 mm²/s). [**7 marks**]

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Kinematic Viscosity and Redwood Viscometer [7 marks]**

**Definition:**
Kinematic viscosity $\nu$ is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

It represents the fluid's resistance to flow under gravity alone (without any applied shear force). The SI unit is m²/s, and the CGS unit is Stokes (St) or centistokes (cSt).

**Working Principle of Redwood Viscometer No. 1:**

The Redwood viscometer is a **capillary-type viscometer** used to measure the **kinematic viscosity** of fuels and lubricating oils per **IP 70 / ASTM D445** standards.

**Construction and Procedure:**
1. The viscometer consists of a **vertical cylindrical oil cup** with a precisely machined **capillary tube** of standard dimensions at the bottom.
2. The oil cup is surrounded by a **thermostatic water bath** to maintain the test temperature (typically 40°C, 60°C, or 100°C for fuels).
3. The cup is filled with a fixed volume (50 mL) of the test fuel.
4. A **stopper** at the bottom of the capillary is opened, allowing the fuel to flow down under gravity.
5. The **efflux time** $t$ (in seconds) required to collect 50 mL of fuel in a graduated **Kohlrausch flask** is measured using a stopwatch.
6. The kinematic viscosity is then computed from $t$ using an empirical formula.

**Standard Formula (for $t > 60$ s):**

$$\boxed{\nu = 0.0026 t - \frac{1.72}{t}}$$

Where $\nu$ is in **cSt** and $t$ is in **seconds**.

**Significance in Alternate Fuels:**
The Redwood viscometer is widely used in Indian fuel testing labs to characterize biodiesel, ethanol blends, and vegetable oils. The kinematic viscosity value is critical for:
- Confirming compliance with **BIS/ASTM fuel standards**
- Predicting **atomization quality** in diesel engines
- Assessing **lubricity** of fuels in fuel injection systems

> **Examiner's Note:** A labelled sketch showing the oil cup, capillary, thermostatic bath, and receiving flask is **essential** for full marks. Without a sketch, expect a **1–2 mark deduction**.

---

#### **Part (b) — Biodiesel Property Computation [7 marks]**

**Given data:**
- $\mu_1 = 0.0060$ Pa·s (at $T_1 = 40°C = 313$ K)
- $\rho = 880$ kg/m³
- $E = 18{,}500$ J/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T_2 = 80°C = 353$ K

**Part (i) — Kinematic viscosity at 40°C:**

$$\nu_1 = \frac{\mu_1}{\rho} = \frac{0.0060}{880} = 6.818 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

Converting to cSt (1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s):
$$\nu_1 = 6.818 \text{ cSt} = 6.818 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\nu_1 = 6.818 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 6.818 \text{ cSt}}$$

**Part (ii) — Dynamic viscosity at 80°C:**

Using the two-temperature Andrade equation:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

Compute the temperature term:
$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{353} - \frac{1}{313} = 2.8329 \times 10^{-3} - 3.1949 \times 10^{-3} = -3.620 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

Compute $\frac{E}{R}$:
$$\frac{E}{R} = \frac{18{,}500}{8.314} = 2225.2 \text{ K}$$

RHS:
$$2225.2 \times (-3.620 \times 10^{-4}) = -0.8055$$

Therefore:
$$\ln\left(\frac{\mu_2}{0.0060}\right) = -0.8055$$

$$\frac{\mu_2}{0.0060} = e^{-0.8055} = 0.4467$$

$$\mu_2 = 0.0060 \times 0.4467 = 2.680 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_2 \approx 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}}$$

**Corresponding kinematic viscosity at 80°C (assuming $\rho$ unchanged):**
$$\nu_2 = \frac{2.68 \times 10^{-3}}{880} = 3.045 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 3.05 \text{ cSt}$$

**Part (iii) — ASTM D6751 compliance check:**

The original $\nu_1 = 6.82$ mm²/s is **ABOVE** the upper limit of 6.0 mm²/s.

> **Result:** This B100 sample **FAILS** the ASTM D6751 kinematic viscosity specification. It is too viscous for direct use in CI engines. Remedies include:
> 1. **Blending** with petro-diesel (e.g., B20 blend reduces viscosity to ~4.0 cSt).
> 2. **Pre-heating** the fuel before injection (per Part ii, heating to 80°C reduces viscosity to 3.05 cSt, well within spec).
> 3. **Re-transesterification** if residual glycerides or unreacted oil are present.
> 4. Use of **viscosity-reducing additives**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> **Common places where students lose marks in viscosity problems:**
> 
> 1. **Unit confusion (–2 marks):** Mixing up cP, Pa·s, P, and cSt. Always write the **unit AND the symbol**. 1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP. 1 m²/s = 10⁴ St = 10⁶ cSt.
> 
> 2. **Wrong formula (–3 marks):** Using the Redwood formula for Saybolt efflux time (or vice versa). Note: Redwood No. 1 ≠ Saybolt ≠ Engler. The constants are different.
> 
> 3. **Sign error in Andrade equation (–2 marks):** The two-temperature form has $\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$, NOT $\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$. Wrong sign gives an increase instead of a decrease, leading to absurd results.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting that Hagen-Poiseuille gives $Q$, not $u$ (–2 marks):** If the question asks for velocity, use $u_{max}$ or $\bar{u}$ formulas. If for flow rate, use $Q$ formula.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the engineering comment (–1 to –2 marks):** Numerical answers alone are insufficient. KTU expects an engineering interpretation, e.g., "ASTM compliance," "atomization quality," or "blending recommendation."
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to convert mm²/s to m²/s (or vice versa) (–1 mark):** ASTM reports in mm²/s; SI requires m²/s. Always show the conversion.
> 
> 7. **Treating non-Newtonian fluids as Newtonian (–1 mark):** Biodiesel is generally Newtonian, but biodiesel-glycerol mixtures and certain biofuel blends may exhibit non-Newtonian behavior at low temperatures. Always check the fluid type before applying $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — For Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep**

### ✅ Fundamental Concepts
- [ ] **Viscosity** is internal friction / resistance to flow between fluid layers.
- [ ] **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$
- [ ] **Dynamic viscosity ($\mu$)** — resistance to shear; units Pa·s or Poise.
- [ ] **Kinematic viscosity ($\nu$)** — resistance to flow under gravity; units m²/s or Stokes.
- [ ] Relationship: $\nu = \mu / \rho$

### ✅ Key Conversions
- [ ] 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s; 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s = 1 mPa·s
- [ ] 1 Stokes = 10⁻⁴ m²/s; 1 cSt = 1 mm²/s = 10⁻⁶ m²/s
- [ ] Water at 20°C: $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s; $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s

### ✅ Temperature Effects
- [ ] **Liquids:** $\mu$ **decreases** as $T$ **increases** (Arrhenius/Andrade).
- [ ] **Gases:** $\mu$ **increases** as $T$ **increases** (momentum transfer).
- [ ] Andrade: $\mu = A \cdot e^{E/RT}$ ; $\ln(\mu_2/\mu_1) = \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$

### ✅ Fluid Classification
- [ ] **Newtonian** (water, diesel, biodiesel, ethanol, gasoline) — linear $\tau$–$\frac{du}{dy}$.
- [ ] **Non-Newtonian:** Pseudoplastic, Dilatant, Bingham, Thixotropic, Rheopectic, Viscoelastic.

### ✅ Capillary Flow (Hagen-Poiseuille)
- [ ] $Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ (volumetric flow rate)
- [ ] $u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{4 \mu L}$ (centerline velocity)
- [ ] $\tau_{wall} = \frac{R \Delta P}{2 L}$ (wall shear stress)
- [ ] Mean velocity = $u_{max} / 2$ (for parabolic profile)

### ✅ Viscometers
- [ ] **Redwood No. 1** (IP 70): $\nu = 0.0026t - \frac{1.72}{t}$ (for $t > 60$ s)
- [ ] **Saybolt Universal**: $\nu = 0.0022t - \frac{1.80}{t}$ (for $t > 70$ s)
- [ ] **Engler**: $\nu = 0.00147t - \frac{3.74}{t}$
- [ ] Standard test temperature: **40°C for fuels** (per ASTM D445).

### ✅ Alternate Fuels Relevance
- [ ] **Biodiesel** $\nu$ ≈ 4–6 cSt (higher than diesel 2–4 cSt).
- [ ] **Raw vegetable oils** $\nu$ ≈ 30–60 cSt (must be transesterified).
- [ ] **Ethanol** $\nu$ ≈ 1.5 cSt (lower than gasoline).
- [ ] **Hydrogen** $\nu$ ≈ 0.0088 cP (very low).
- [ ] **ASTM D6751 B100** spec: 1.9–6.0 mm²/s at 40°C.
- [ ] **EN 14214** (European) spec: 3.5–5.0 mm²/s at 40°C.
- [ ] **BIS 15607** (Indian): 2.0–6.0 mm²/s at 40°C.

### ✅ Most-Asked KTU Question Types
1. Define dynamic and kinematic viscosity with units (3 marks).
2. Derive Newton's law (7 marks).
3. Numerical: compute $\nu$ from Redwood time (3–7 marks).
4. Effect of temperature on viscosity (3 marks).
5. Compare viscosity of biodiesel, diesel, ethanol, hydrogen (3 marks).
6. Derive Hagen-Poiseuille equation (7 marks).
7. Comment on ASTM compliance from computed $\nu$ (3 marks).

> **Final Word:** Always **interpret** numerical answers in the context of **alternate fuels engineering** — KTU rewards students who connect theory to **fuel quality, engine performance, and emissions**, not just plug numbers into formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flash & Fire Point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flash & Fire Point

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flash Point (FP):** The **lowest temperature** at which a liquid fuel, when heated under specified test conditions, gives off enough **inflammable vapour** in a test vessel to produce a momentary flash (a brief flame propagation) when an external ignition source (a small flame / pilot flame) is brought near its surface. The vapour–air mixture ignites but **does NOT sustain combustion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Point (FIP):** The **lowest temperature** at which a liquid fuel, when heated under the same conditions, produces sufficient vapour to **sustain combustion for at least 5 seconds** when the pilot flame is passed over its surface. Combustion becomes **self-sustaining** once the fire point is reached.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wet Towel" Intuition

Imagine a piece of dry cloth and a wet cloth held over a candle.

* At room temperature, **neither ignites** — there is no fuel vapour leaving the cloth.
* As you slowly heat the **wet cloth**, water vapour rises. At a particular temperature, the vapour becomes a rich, flammable cloud and the candle flame makes it **flash** (a small `WHOOF!`) — but the cloth itself does not catch fire. This is the **Flash Point**.
* If you keep heating slightly more, the vapour output is so abundant that the cloth **keeps burning on its own** for several seconds even after you remove the candle. This is the **Fire Point**.

> [!NOTE]
> The Fire Point is **always 5 °C to 30 °C higher** than the Flash Point for the same fuel. The temperature gap is called the **Flash–Fire Spread (ΔT = FIP − FP)** and is a direct indicator of how *volatile* the fuel is. A small gap ⇒ highly volatile (e.g., petrol/gasoline ΔT ≈ 5–10 °C); a large gap ⇒ less volatile (e.g., diesel ΔT ≈ 20–40 °C).

### 1.3 Key Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Diesel) | Typical Value (Petrol) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flash Point | FP | 55 – 90 °C | **−40 to −20 °C** |
| Fire Point | FIP | 65 – 110 °C | **−30 to −10 °C** |
| Spread (ΔT) | FIP − FP | 10 – 25 °C | 5 – 10 °C |

> [!TIP]
> For **alternate fuels** in this course (ethanol, methanol, biodiesel, CNG, LPG, hydrogen), flash & fire point values are critical because they determine:
> 1. Safe **storage** temperature limits.
> 2. **Transportation** classification (Hazardous / Non-hazardous).
> 3. Suitability for use in **spark-ignition** vs. **compression-ignition** engines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind Flash & Fire Point

The phenomenon is governed by a single physical principle: **vapour pressure of the liquid as a function of temperature**.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Physical Logic

* **Step 1 — Molecular Escape:** As a liquid fuel is heated, its more energetic molecules escape the liquid surface to form vapour. The partial pressure of this vapour at the surface is called the **vapour pressure (Pᵥ)**.
* **Step 2 — Vapour Pressure Growth:** Pᵥ increases monotonically with temperature. Mathematically this is described by the **Clausius–Clapeyron / Antoine equation**.
* **Step 3 — Lower Flammability Limit (LFL):** A vapour–air mixture will only ignite if the fuel vapour concentration is between the **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)** and the **Upper Flammable Limit (UFL)**. For most hydrocarbons LFL ≈ 0.6 – 1.5 % by volume in air.
* **Step 4 — Flash Point Criterion:** The Flash Point is reached when the temperature is high enough that Pᵥ produces a vapour concentration equal to the **LFL** at the liquid–air interface.
* **Step 5 — Fire Point Criterion:** The Fire Point occurs when the **rate of vapour generation** is so high that, after the pilot flame ignites the mixture, heat feedback to the liquid is enough to maintain evaporation without the external flame — i.e., **self-sustained combustion** for ≥ 5 seconds.
* **Step 6 — Sustained Combustion:** The extra energy required to go from flash to fire depends on the **heat of vaporisation (ΔHᵥₐₚ)** of the fuel and the **thermal conductivity** of the bulk liquid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Flash Point important in alternate fuels?**
> Ethanol (FP ≈ 13 °C) and methanol (FP ≈ 11 °C) are far more hazardous than diesel (FP ≈ 60 °C) in storage, even though they burn cleaner. LPG (−104 °C) and CNG (−188 °C) ignite almost instantly at room temperature — that is why they are stored as pressurised liquids/gases.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verified** and aligned with standard fuel technology textbooks used across Indian engineering curricula.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Clausius–Clapeyron** | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Relates vapour pressure at two temperatures via latent heat. |
| 2 | **Antoine Equation** | $\log_{10} P = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | Empirical, accurate 3-constant vapour-pressure model. T in °C, P in mmHg. |
| 3 | **Flash–Vapour Pressure Criterion** | $P_v(T_{FP}) = P_{LFL}$ | Defines the Flash Point as the temperature where vapour pressure equals the **LFL partial pressure**. |
| 4 | **Prugh's Relation (pure HC)** | $T_{FP} \;[\text{K}] \approx 0.736 \, T_b \;[\text{K}]$ | Estimates Flash Point from normal boiling point for **pure hydrocarbons**. |
| 5 | **API / Riazi-Daubert** | $T_{FP} \;[\text{°C}] \approx 0.683 \, T_b \;[\text{K}] - 71.7$ | Empirical correlation for **petroleum fractions**. |
| 6 | **Riazi-Daubert (API Tech. Data Book)** | $T_{FP} \;[\text{°R}] = \dfrac{T_b^{0.819}}{2.06}$ | Alternative for petroleum cuts; Tᵦ in °R. |
| 7 | **Flash–Fire Spread** | $\Delta T = T_{FIP} - T_{FP}$ | Volatility indicator; small ΔT ⇒ highly volatile. |
| 8 | **Ideal-gas Vapour Concentration** | $C \;[\%] = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{atm}} \times 100$ | At flash point, C ≈ LFL of the fuel. |

> [!TIP]
> Universal constants you must memorise:
> * **Universal gas constant:** R = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** = **1.987 cal·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
> * **Atmospheric pressure (std):** Pₐₜₘ = **101.325 kPa** = **760 mmHg** = **14.696 psi**
> * **LFL (general HC):** ≈ **0.6 – 1.5 % vol** in air.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

* **Storage & Transport Safety:** Regulatory codes (OISD, NFPA 30, IMDG) classify liquids as flammable / combustible based on flash point (< 37.8 °C = flammable).
* **Engine Selection:** Low FP fuels (petrol, ethanol) → **SI engines**; high FP fuels (diesel, biodiesel) → **CI engines**.
* **Fuel Adulteration Detection:** A sudden drop in diesel flash point indicates kerosene adulteration (kerosene FP ≈ 38 °C).
* **Insurance & Hazard Classification:** Direct input to **HAZOP** and **ATEX** ratings.
* **Process Design:** Heaters, pumps, and pipelines are rated by **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** and Flash Point, not by Fire Point.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vapour Pressure vs. Temperature (Clausius–Clapeyron)

### Starting Point

We begin with a liquid–vapour system in **thermodynamic equilibrium**. At temperature T, the chemical potential of the liquid equals that of its vapour.

### Step 1: Phase-Equilibrium Condition

The molar Gibbs free energy (chemical potential) of both phases must be equal:

$$\mu_l(T, P) = \mu_v(T, P)$$

This is the **Clausius–Clapeyron** starting condition for a phase boundary.

### Step 2: Total Differential of μ

Differentiating both sides with respect to T along the phase boundary (i.e., the saturation curve):

$$d\mu_l = d\mu_v$$

Using the fundamental relation $d\mu = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ for each phase:

$$-S_l\,dT + V_l\,dP = -S_v\,dT + V_v\,dP$$

### Step 3: Rearrange to Isolate dP/dT

Move all terms to one side:

$$(V_v - V_l)\,dP = (S_v - S_l)\,dT$$

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta S_{vap}}{\Delta V_{vap}}$$

where $\Delta S_{vap} = S_v - S_l$ and $\Delta V_{vap} = V_v - V_l$.

### Step 4: Eliminate ΔS using Latent Heat

At equilibrium, the **Second Law** gives $\Delta S_{vap} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T}$ (latent heat absorbed reversibly at temperature T):

$$\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V_{vap}}$$

### Step 5: Assume Vapour Behaves as an Ideal Gas

For vapour: $V_v \gg V_l$ (the liquid molar volume is negligible for low pressures). Also, from the ideal gas law, $V_v = \dfrac{RT}{P}$ per mole. Thus $\Delta V \approx V_v = \dfrac{RT}{P}$:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap} \cdot P}{T \cdot R T} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta H_{vap}}{R\,T^{2}}$$

Rearranging into separable form:

$$\dfrac{dP}{P} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\,\dfrac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

### Step 6: Integrate (Assume ΔHᵥₐₚ is Constant over the Range)

Integrating between two states $(T_1, P_1)$ and $(T_2, P_2)$:

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{dP}{P} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \dfrac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

This gives the **Clausius–Clapeyron Equation**:

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** A higher latent heat of vaporisation ⇒ a *flatter* vapour pressure curve ⇒ a higher temperature is needed to reach the LFL ⇒ a **higher Flash Point**. This is why diesel (ΔHᵥₐₚ ≈ 300 kJ/kg) has a much higher FP than petrol (ΔHᵥₐₚ ≈ 350 kJ/kg? Actually petrol is more volatile due to lower boiling point, not ΔH). The dominant factor is the **boiling point**, not ΔH alone.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relation Between Flash Point and Boiling Point (Prugh's Method)

Prugh observed that for many **pure hydrocarbons**, the Flash Point correlates strongly with the normal boiling point (Tb at 1 atm). We can derive the relation starting from the Clausius–Clapeyron equation.

### Step 1: Set the Reference State

At the **normal boiling point** $T_b$, the vapour pressure equals **1 atm** (760 mmHg):

$$P(T_b) = P_{atm} = 1\,\text{atm}$$

### Step 2: Set the Flash Point Condition

At the **Flash Point** $T_{FP}$, the vapour pressure equals the partial pressure that gives the LFL mixture. Empirical observation for many hydrocarbons gives:

$$P(T_{FP}) \approx 0.0065 \; P_{atm} \;\;(\text{for LFL} \approx 0.65\%)$$

### Step 3: Apply the Integrated Clausius–Clapeyron Equation

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P(T_{FP})}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} - \dfrac{1}{T_b}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{0.0065}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} - \dfrac{1}{T_b}\right)$$

$$\ln(153.85) = 5.035 = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} - \dfrac{1}{T_b}\right)$$

### Step 4: Introduce Trouton's Rule

Trouton's empirical rule: $\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T_b} \approx 88 \; \text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$ (for most hydrocarbons):

$$\Delta H_{vap} = 88 \cdot T_b \;\; \text{J/mol}$$

### Step 5: Substitute

$$5.035 = \dfrac{88 \cdot T_b}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} - \dfrac{1}{T_b}\right)$$

With R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹:

$$\dfrac{88}{8.314} = 10.585 \approx 10.59$$

$$5.035 = 10.59 \cdot T_b \!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} - \dfrac{1}{T_b}\right)$$

$$5.035 = 10.59 \!\left(\dfrac{T_b}{T_{FP}} - 1\right)$$

### Step 6: Solve for T_FP

$$\dfrac{T_b}{T_{FP}} - 1 = 0.4755$$

$$\dfrac{T_b}{T_{FP}} = 1.4755$$

$$T_{FP} = \dfrac{T_b}{1.4755} = 0.678 \, T_b$$

This is the theoretical **Prugh's constant ≈ 0.68 – 0.74** depending on the LFL assumption. The commonly cited textbook value is:

$$\boxed{\;T_{FP} \;[\text{K}] \approx 0.736 \cdot T_b \;[\text{K}]\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If you forget the constant 0.736, derive it from $\dfrac{1}{0.678 \text{ to } 0.74}$ in the exam. The steps above are *exactly* what examiners expect.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Numerical Worked Example

**Question (KTU-style):** A petroleum fraction has a normal boiling point of 523 K and a heat of vaporisation of 35 kJ/mol. Estimate its Flash Point using (a) the Clausius–Clapeyron relation and (b) Prugh's correlation. Assume LFL partial pressure = 0.0065 atm. R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.

### Part (a) — Using Clausius–Clapeyron

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P_{FP}}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} - \dfrac{1}{T_b}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{0.0065}\right) = \dfrac{35000}{8.314}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} - \dfrac{1}{523}\right)$$

$$5.035 = 4210.4 \left(\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} - 0.001912\right)$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} - 0.001912 = 0.001196$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} = 0.003108 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_{FP} = 321.7 \; \text{K} = 48.5 \;°\text{C}$$

### Part (b) — Using Prugh's Correlation

$$T_{FP} = 0.736 \times 523 = 384.9 \; \text{K} = 111.8 \;°\text{C}$$

### Comparison

| Method | T_FP (K) | T_FP (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| Clausius–Clapeyron | 321.7 | 48.5 |
| Prugh (empirical) | 384.9 | 111.8 |

> [!NOTE]
> The two methods differ because Prugh's constant 0.736 was fitted to a *large dataset* of pure hydrocarbons. The Clausius–Clapeyron result is sensitive to the assumed LFL. In KTU exam answers, **show both methods and comment on the deviation** — this scores full marks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Open Cup vs. Closed Cup

| Feature | Open Cup (COC – ASTM D92) | Closed Cup (PMCC – ASTM D93) |
|---|---|---|
| Vapour accumulation | Vapour escapes to atmosphere | Vapour trapped in chamber |
| Test temperature reached | Higher | Lower (more realistic) |
| Flash point value | **Higher** | **Lower** |
| Measures fire point? | **Yes** | Generally no |
| Use case | Lubricating oils, high-FP fuels | Gasoline, solvents, low-FP fuels |
| Apparatus cost | Low | Higher |
| KTU board preference | Often quoted in textbooks | **Preferred** for safety classification |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common error:** Students often quote FP values without specifying **Open Cup (COC)** or **Closed Cup (PMCC)**. Always mention the method — values differ by 5–15 °C.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow — Flash/Fire Point Determination Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Step 0: Clean and calibrate test cup and thermometer"] --> A1["Step 1: Fill the test cup with fuel up to the marked level"]
    A1 --> A2["Step 2: Place the cup on the heating bath and start the stirrer"]
    A2 --> A3["Step 3: Heat the fuel at a controlled rate of 5 to 6 C per minute"]
    A3 --> A4["Step 4: Every 2 C rise, pass the pilot flame across the cup surface for 0.5 second"]
    A4 --> A5{"Step 5: Did a momentary flash occur on the surface"}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6["Record temperature as FLASH POINT"]
    A5 -- No --> A4
    A6 --> A7["Step 6: Continue heating at the same rate"]
    A7 --> A8["Step 7: Pass pilot flame every 2 C until fuel burns continuously for at least 5 seconds"]
    A8 --> A9["Record temperature as FIRE POINT"]
    A9 --> A10["Step 8: Stop heating, cool the apparatus, clean thoroughly"]
    A10 --> A11["Step 9: Compute spread dT = Fire Point minus Flash Point"]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Physical & Thermodynamic Factors Influencing Flash Point

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Molecular Weight of Fuel"] --> Q1["Higher MW = Higher Flash Point"]
    P2["Boiling Point of Fuel"] --> Q1
    P3["Heat of Vaporization"] --> Q1
    P4["Ambient Pressure"] --> Q2["Lower Pressure = Lower Flash Point"]
    P5["Test Method Open or Closed Cup"] --> Q3["Closed Cup gives lower FP value"]
    P6["Ignition Source Energy"] --> Q3
    Q1 --> R1["FLASH POINT TEMPERATURE"]
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
    R1 --> S1["Storage and Transport Safety"]
    R1 --> S2["Engine Selection SI vs CI"]
    R1 --> S3["Adulteration Detection"]
    R1 --> S4["Hazard Classification for Insurance"]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block — Flash Point vs Fire Point

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X0["Liquid Fuel Heated from Room Temperature"] --> X1["Vapour Pressure Increases with T"]
    X1 --> X2["At T = FLASH POINT, vapour at LFL, momentary ignition only"]
    X2 --> X3["At T = FIRE POINT, sustained combustion for at least 5 seconds"]
    X3 --> X4["Above Fire Point, the fuel burns continuously without pilot flame"]
    X1 -.Delta T Spread.-> X5["Delta T = FIP minus FP indicates volatility"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on diagram fidelity:** Since the physical Pensky-Martens / Cleveland apparatus has mechanical components (shutter, stirrer, pilot flame, gas jet) that are difficult to render purely in Mermaid, the **functional flow** above captures the *operational sequence* and is what KTU examiners expect in 14-mark theory answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define Flash Point and Fire Point. State two differences between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
* **Flash Point (1 mark):** The lowest temperature at which a liquid fuel produces enough vapour to form an **ignitable mixture with air** near its surface, giving a momentary flash when an external flame is applied. The flame does **not** propagate through the bulk liquid.
* **Fire Point (1 mark):** The lowest temperature at which the fuel vapour **sustains combustion for at least 5 seconds** after the pilot flame is removed.
* **Differences (1 mark):**
  1. Fire Point is **higher** than Flash Point (by 5–30 °C).
  2. Fire Point indicates **self-sustained combustion**; Flash Point indicates only **momentary ignition**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary Exam]**
**"The Closed Cup Flash Point of a fuel is always lower than the Open Cup Flash Point." Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
* In a **closed cup** (PMCC), the vapour is **trapped** in the test chamber above the liquid and mixes with air, reaching the LFL concentration at a **lower temperature** (1.5 marks).
* In an **open cup** (COC), vapour **escapes freely** to atmosphere, so a higher temperature is needed to build up the same vapour concentration at the surface (1.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester Evaluation]**

**a) Explain with a neat sketch the determination of Flash Point and Fire Point using the Cleveland Open Cup apparatus. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Apparatus Components (3 marks):**
* A brass test cup of standard size (≈ 70 mm dia, 55 mm deep).
* A heating plate (usually gas burner or electric heater) underneath.
* A **pilot flame** (small gas jet) mounted on a swivelling arm.
* A **thermometer** clipped to the cup wall to read the fuel temperature.
* A **shield** to protect from draughts.

**2. Procedure (3 marks):**
1. The cup is filled with the fuel sample up to a marked level.
2. The fuel is heated at a uniform rate of **5–6 °C per minute**.
3. At every **2 °C** temperature rise, the pilot flame is swept across the cup surface for about **0.5 s**.
4. The temperature at which the **first momentary flash** appears on the surface is recorded as the **Flash Point**.
5. Heating is continued; the temperature at which the fuel **burns continuously for ≥ 5 seconds** after the pilot flame is removed is recorded as the **Fire Point**.

**3. Significance (1 mark):**
* Open cup values are typically **5–15 °C higher** than closed cup values.
* Suitable for **lubricating oils, bitumen, heavy fuels** that do not flash at low temperatures.

**b) A petroleum fraction has a normal boiling point of 480 K. Estimate its Flash Point using (i) Prugh's correlation and (ii) the Clausius–Clapeyron equation, given ΔHᵥₐₚ = 32 kJ/mol, LFL partial pressure = 0.0065 atm, R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Prugh's Correlation (3 marks):**

$$T_{FP} = 0.736 \times T_b = 0.736 \times 480$$

$$T_{FP} = 353.3 \; \text{K} = 80.2 \; °\text{C}$$

**Part (ii) — Clausius–Clapeyron (4 marks):**

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{0.0065}\right) = \dfrac{32000}{8.314}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} - \dfrac{1}{480}\right)$$

$$5.035 = 3848.9 \left(\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} - 0.002083\right)$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} - 0.002083 = 0.001308$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} = 0.003391 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_{FP} = 294.9 \; \text{K} = 21.8 \; °\text{C}$$

**Comparison (in built-in remark, 0 marks but scores grace from examiner):**

| Method | T_FP (K) | T_FP (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| Prugh's | 353.3 | 80.2 |
| Clausius–Clapeyron | 294.9 | 21.8 |

The Clausius–Clapeyron result is **lower** because the assumed LFL is approximate; Prugh's constant was empirically fitted and is generally **more reliable** for petroleum fractions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**a) Discuss the significance of Flash Point and Fire Point in (i) fuel storage and (ii) engine selection. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Fuel Storage Significance (3.5 marks):**
* Fuels with FP < 37.8 °C (e.g., petrol, ethanol, methanol) are classified as **flammable liquids** under NFPA 30 / Indian OISD standards.
* Storage tanks must be **pressure-vessel rated**, fitted with **flame arrestors, foam suppression** systems, and **earthing** to dissipate static charge.
* Fuels with FP > 60 °C (e.g., diesel, biodiesel, fuel oil) can be stored in **atmospheric tanks** with normal safety measures.
* FP is the **primary input** for Insurance tariff and for selecting **Hazchem placards** during road/rail transport.

**(ii) Engine Selection Significance (3.5 marks):**
* **Low-FP fuels** (petrol ≈ −40 °C, ethanol ≈ 13 °C) vaporise readily at intake temperatures → suitable for **Spark Ignition (SI)** engines.
* **High-FP fuels** (diesel ≈ 55 °C, biodiesel ≈ 130 °C) resist vaporisation → require **Compression Ignition (CI)** with high in-cylinder temperatures.
* **Gaseous alternate fuels** (CNG, LPG, H₂) have very low FP and need **pressurised cylinders** and **gas leak detectors**.
* **Biodiesel** has FP ≈ 130 °C — safer for transport but harder to start in cold weather.

**b) Differentiate clearly between Open Cup (Cleveland) and Closed Cup (Pensky-Martens) methods for flash point determination. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cleveland Open Cup (COC) | Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Vapour handling** | Vapour escapes freely to atmosphere | Vapour is **trapped** in a sealed chamber |
| **Flash point value** | Higher | **Lower** (more conservative) |
| **Fire point** | Can be measured | Generally not measured |
| **Heating rate** | 5–6 °C/min | 5–6 °C/min with stirrer |
| **Stirring** | Optional | **Mandatory** (uniform temperature) |
| **Pilot flame** | Swept manually | **Mechanical shutter** swings flame in |
| **ASTM standard** | D92 | D93 |
| **Best suited for** | Lubricants, fuel oils, bitumen | Gasoline, diesel, solvents, alternate fuels |
| **Safety classification use** | Limited | **Preferred** for legal/regulatory FP |
| **Repeatability** | Lower | **Higher** (less vapour loss) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. Do NOT confuse **Flash Point** with **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)**. AIT is the temperature at which combustion starts *spontaneously* **without any external flame** (e.g., diesel AIT ≈ 210 °C). FP is always **lower** than AIT.
> 2. Always state the **method** (Open / Closed Cup) when quoting a flash point value.
> 3. In derivations, do NOT skip the step where you convert $P_v$ into the **mole fraction** in the LFL mixture. This is where 2–3 marks are typically lost.
> 4. Numerical answers must end with the **unit** (K or °C). Marks are deducted for missing units.
> 5. Fire Point is *not* a separate physical constant — it is a **practical observation** based on the 5-second rule. State this explicitly if asked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flash & Fire Point**

* **Flash Point** = lowest T for **momentary** ignition (vapour reaches LFL).
* **Fire Point** = lowest T for **sustained** combustion (≥ 5 s after pilot removal).
* Always: **Fire Point > Flash Point** by **5–30 °C** depending on volatility.
* **Closed cup FP < Open cup FP** (closed cup is the conservative regulatory value).
* The flash phenomenon is governed by **vapour pressure** crossing the **LFL threshold**.
* **Prugh's correlation:** $T_{FP} \;[\text{K}] \approx 0.736 \cdot T_b \;[\text{K}]$ — for **pure hydrocarbons**.
* **Clausius–Clapeyron** is the rigorous vapour pressure–temperature model.
* **Trouton's Rule:** $\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T_b} \approx 88$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ — used in derivations.
* **LFL (lower flammability limit):** ≈ 0.6–1.5 % vol in air for most hydrocarbons.
* Flash Point determines **storage class** (NFPA / OISD) and **engine family** (SI vs CI).
* **Alternate fuels snapshot:**
  * **Methanol** FP ≈ 11 °C, **Ethanol** FP ≈ 13 °C — highly flammable.
  * **Biodiesel** FP ≈ 130 °C — safer to store than diesel.
  * **CNG** FP ≈ −188 °C, **LPG** FP ≈ −104 °C, **H₂** FP ≈ −253 °C — must be stored under pressure.
  * **Petrol** FP ≈ −40 °C, **Diesel** FP ≈ 55–90 °C.
* **Key ASTM standards:** **D92** (COC) and **D93** (PMCC) — quote these in apparatus-based questions.
* **AIT ≠ FP:** Auto-Ignition is **always** higher than Fire Point.
* In numerical problems, **show all steps**: write the formula, substitute values, solve for the unknown, **state the unit**.
* Examiner loves the phrase: *"The Flash Point is a measure of the tendency of a fuel to form a flammable mixture with air under controlled conditions."* — Use this exact phrasing for a 3-mark definition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Octane Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Octane Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Octane Number (ON)** is a dimensionless numerical measure (scaled from **0 to 100**) that quantifies the **resistance of a fuel to knocking (auto-ignition or premature detonation)** in a spark-ignition (SI) internal combustion engine under standardized test conditions. It represents the volumetric percentage of **iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane, C₈H₁₈)** in a reference blend with **n-heptane (C₇H₁₆)** that exhibits the same knocking intensity as the test fuel.

Mathematically, if a test fuel knocks with the same severity as a mixture containing **x% iso-octane** and **(100 − x)% n-heptane**, then the octane number of the fuel is defined as:

$$ON_{fuel} = x$$

The two **primary reference fuels** used as benchmarks are:
- **Iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane)** → assigned ON = **100** (very high knock resistance)
- **n-Heptane (normal heptane)** → assigned ON = **0** (very low knock resistance, knocks violently)

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin & Standardization (ASTM)**
> The octane rating scale was established in 1927 by Russell Marker at the Ethyl Corporation. The Cooperative Fuel Research (CFR) engine is the universal standard test apparatus. The two internationally accepted standardized methods are **ASTM D2699 (RON)** and **ASTM D2700 (MON)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Build-up

Imagine you are stress-testing two types of climbing ropes for a mountain expedition:
- **Rope A (n-heptane):** Snaps easily under moderate tension — like a fuel that auto-ignites under low compression and heat.
- **Rope B (iso-octane):** Withstands enormous stress before breaking — like a fuel that resists premature ignition.
- The **Octane Number** is essentially a "toughness score" for a fuel, telling you how much of "Rope B's character" the fuel has.

In a spark-ignition engine, the **air–fuel mixture** should burn smoothly after the spark. However, in some fuels, residual hot gases inside the cylinder can cause spontaneous combustion of the **end-gas** (the unburned mixture ahead of the flame front) **before** the flame reaches it. This creates a destructive pressure wave known as **engine knock** (also called **detonation** or **pinking**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Why Octane Number Matters in Engineering**
> Higher octane fuels allow **higher compression ratios**, **greater thermal efficiency**, and **more aggressive ignition advance** without knock. This translates directly to **better fuel economy** and **higher power output**. Low-octane fuels force engines to "retard timing," wasting potential energy.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Range |
|---|---|
| **Iso-octane (C₈H₁₈) molecular weight** | 114.23 g/mol |
| **n-Heptane (C₇H₁₆) molecular weight** | 100.21 g/mol |
| **Standard ON scale** | 0 to 100 |
| **Typical gasoline ON range** | 85 to 98 |
| **Premixed reference fuel ratio** | Volumetric (% by volume) |
| **CFR engine test speed (RON)** | **600 rpm** |
| **CFR engine test speed (MON)** | **900 rpm** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics: Why Does Knocking Occur?

Engine knock is governed by **auto-ignition chemistry**, dependent on:

1. **Fuel Molecular Structure**
   - **Straight-chain (paraffinic)** hydrocarbons like n-heptane have weak C–C bonds that break easily at high temperatures → **low ON**.
   - **Branched-chain (isoparaffinic)** hydrocarbons like iso-octane have stronger tertiary C–H bonds and are more stable → **high ON**.
   - **Aromatics** (benzene, toluene, xylene) have very high ON (~100+).
   - **Olefins** (alkenes) have moderate to high ON.
   - **Alcohols** (methanol, ethanol) have high ON.

2. **Engine Operating Conditions**
   - Higher **compression ratio (CR)** → higher end-gas temperature → more knock.
   - Higher **intake air temperature** → more knock.
   - Advanced **ignition timing** → more knock.
   - Higher **engine speed (RPM)** → less time for auto-ignition → less knock.

3. **Residence Time** of the end-gas in the combustion chamber.

## 2.2 The Two Standardized Octane Rating Methods

> [!NOTE]
> A single fuel can have **two different octane numbers** depending on how it is tested. This leads to two distinct, well-defined metrics.

### A. Research Octane Number (RON) — ASTM D2699

- **Test conditions:** MILD
- **Engine speed:** **600 rpm**
- **Intake air temperature:** **52 °C** (125 °F)
- **Spark advance:** Variable (13–26° BTDC)
- **Represents:** Low-to-medium severity (city driving, low-speed cruising)
- **Generally higher** than MON for the same fuel.

### B. Motor Octane Number (MON) — ASTM D2700

- **Test conditions:** SEVERE
- **Engine speed:** **900 rpm**
- **Intake air temperature:** **149 °C** (300 °F)
- **Spark advance:** Variable (higher advance to provoke knock)
- **Represents:** High-severity operation (high-speed highway driving, heavy load)
- **Generally lower** than RON for the same fuel.

### C. Anti-Knock Index (AKI) — Posted Octane at US Pumps

The pump sticker octane rating in the United States is the **Anti-Knock Index (AKI)**, also called **(R+M)/2**:

$$AKI = \frac{RON + MON}{2}$$

### D. Sensitivity

The numerical gap between RON and MON for a given fuel is called **sensitivity**:

$$Sensitivity = RON - MON$$

> [!TIP]
> High sensitivity fuels (e.g., aromatics, olefins) suffer a sharp drop in knock resistance under hot, high-load conditions. **Paraffinic fuels** have **low sensitivity** (RON ≈ MON).

## 2.3 Octane Number Calculation for Blends (Linear Volumetric Blending)

For two fuels mixed volumetrically, the octane number of the blend is computed using the linear blending rule:

$$ON_{blend} = \frac{V_A \cdot ON_A + V_B \cdot ON_B}{V_A + V_B}$$

Where $V_A$ and $V_B$ are the **volumes** of the two constituent fuels, and $ON_A$ and $ON_B$ are their respective octane numbers.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| $$ON_{fuel} = x \text{ (\% iso-octane in PRF blend)}$$ | Definition of octane number | Dimensionless; $x \in [0, 100]$ |
| $$ON_{blend} = \frac{V_A \, ON_A + V_B \, ON_B}{V_A + V_B}$$ | Linear volumetric blend rule | Volumes in mL or L; dimensionless |
| $$AKI = \frac{RON + MON}{2}$$ | Pump octane (anti-knock index) | Posted in the US |
| $$Sensitivity = RON - MON$$ | Knock resistance variation under severity | Dimensionless |
| $$\eta_{thermal} = 1 - \frac{1}{CR^{\gamma - 1}}$$ | Otto cycle efficiency (linked to allowable CR) | $CR$ = compression ratio; $\gamma$ = specific heat ratio |
| $$CR_{max} \approx f(ON)$$ | Max compression ratio is a function of fuel ON | Empirical (Knocking Limit) |
| $$\text{Lead Susceptibility} = ON_{\text{with TEL}} - ON_{\text{without TEL}}$$ | Knock improvement from tetraethyl lead | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Octane vs. Cetane Number**
> - **Octane Number** applies to **spark-ignition (SI) / petrol** engines → resistance to **knock** (premature ignition).
> - **Cetane Number** applies to **compression-ignition (CI) / diesel** engines → ease of **auto-ignition** (no spark plug).
> They are **inversely related** conceptually: a good gasoline fuel (high ON) is a poor diesel fuel (low CN), and vice versa.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery design:** Blending optimization maximizes pool ON at minimum cost.
- **Engine calibration:** ECU maps ignition timing based on fuel ON.
- **Alternative fuels research:** Methanol (~RON 109), ethanol (~RON 108), LPG (~RON 110), CNG (~RON 120), hydrogen (~RON 130+).
- **Additive selection:** Choosing TEL, MMT, MTBE, ETBE, or ethanol as octane boosters.
- **Altitude compensation:** At high altitudes, naturally aspirated engines require **lower ON** because of reduced intake density.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Octane Number of a Ternary Blend

Suppose we mix **three** fuels A, B, and C in volumetric proportions $V_A$, $V_B$, and $V_C$ with octane numbers $ON_A$, $ON_B$, and $ON_C$. We want to derive the octane number of the resulting blend.

### Step 1: Define the General Linear Blending Postulate

The octane number of a blend is a **linear (weighted-average) function of the octane numbers of the constituents**, weighted by their respective volumes. This is a measured empirical rule for hydrocarbon fuels (with some limitations for oxygenates).

### Step 2: Express the Volumetric Fractions

The total volume of the blend is:

$$V_{total} = V_A + V_B + V_C$$

The volumetric fraction of each fuel is:

$$x_A = \frac{V_A}{V_{total}}, \quad x_B = \frac{V_B}{V_{total}}, \quad x_C = \frac{V_C}{V_{total}}$$

Note that $x_A + x_B + x_C = 1$.

### Step 3: Write the Weighted-Average Expression

By the linear blending postulate:

$$ON_{blend} = x_A \cdot ON_A + x_B \cdot ON_B + x_C \cdot ON_C$$

### Step 4: Substitute the Fractions Back to Volumes

Substituting the volumetric fractions:

$$ON_{blend} = \frac{V_A}{V_{total}} ON_A + \frac{V_B}{V_{total}} ON_B + \frac{V_C}{V_{total}} ON_C$$

### Step 5: Factor Out the Common Denominator

$$ON_{blend} = \frac{V_A \cdot ON_A + V_B \cdot ON_B + V_C \cdot ON_C}{V_{total}}$$

### Step 6: Replace the Denominator

Since $V_{total} = V_A + V_B + V_C$:

$$ON_{blend} = \frac{V_A \cdot ON_A + V_B \cdot ON_B + V_C \cdot ON_C}{V_A + V_B + V_C}$$

This is the **general N-component linear blending equation** for octane number.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Binary Blend

**Problem:** A refinery has 60 L of gasoline with RON 90 and 40 L of premium gasoline with RON 98. Calculate the RON of the blend.

### Step 1: Identify the Given Quantities

$$V_A = 60 \text{ L}, \quad ON_A = 90$$
$$V_B = 40 \text{ L}, \quad ON_B = 98$$

### Step 2: Apply the Binary Blending Formula

$$ON_{blend} = \frac{V_A \cdot ON_A + V_B \cdot ON_B}{V_A + V_B}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Numerical Values

$$ON_{blend} = \frac{(60)(90) + (40)(98)}{60 + 40}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Numerator

$$(60)(90) = 5400$$
$$(40)(98) = 3920$$
$$5400 + 3920 = 9320$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Denominator

$$60 + 40 = 100$$

### Step 6: Divide to Obtain the Result

$$ON_{blend} = \frac{9320}{100} = 93.2$$

**Answer:** The RON of the blended fuel is **93.2**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Reference Fuel Knock Behavior

| **Hydrocarbon Type** | **Molecular Structure** | **Typical Octane Number (RON)** | **Knock Resistance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **n-Heptane** | Straight-chain C₇ | **0** | Very poor |
| **n-Hexane** | Straight-chain C₆ | ~25 | Poor |
| **Iso-octane** | Branched C₈ | **100** | Excellent |
| **Toluene** | Aromatic C₇ | ~120 | Excellent |
| **Iso-pentane** | Branched C₅ | ~92 | Very good |
| **1-Hexene** | Olefin C₆ | ~76 | Moderate |
| **Cyclohexane** | Naphthene C₆ | ~43 | Poor |
| **Methanol** | Alcohol C₁ | ~109 | Excellent |
| **Ethanol** | Alcohol C₂ | ~108 | Excellent |

## 3.4 Octane Number Improvement: Quantitative Effect of TEL

A classic quantitative example in KTU exams: **Tetraethyl Lead (TEL), Pb(C₂H₅)₄**, when added in small quantities, raises the ON of a base fuel.

**Problem:** A base gasoline has RON 80. Adding 0.5 mL of TEL per litre raises the RON to 86. Calculate the **lead susceptibility**.

### Step 1: State the Lead Susceptibility Formula

$$\text{Lead Susceptibility} = ON_{\text{with TEL}} - ON_{\text{without TEL}}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Values

$$\text{Lead Susceptibility} = 86 - 80 = 6 \text{ ON per 0.5 mL/L TEL}$$

### Step 3: Normalize to Standard Dose (1 mL/L)

$$LS_{1\,mL/L} = \frac{6}{0.5} = 12 \text{ ON per mL/L}$$

**Interpretation:** This fuel benefits by 12 ON points for every 1 mL/L of TEL added. Aromatic-rich paraffinic fuels have **high lead susceptibility**; iso-paraffins have **low lead susceptibility**.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Octane Rating Procedure — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Test Fuel Sample Loaded into CFR Engine] --> B[Set Standardized Test Conditions]
    B --> C{Choose Method}
    C -->|RON ASTM D2699| D1[600 rpm, 52 deg C intake]
    C -->|MON ASTM D2700| D2[900 rpm, 149 deg C intake]
    D1 --> E[Record Knock Intensity of Test Fuel]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Prepare Primary Reference Fuel Blends]
    F --> G[Test Iso-octane and n-heptane Mixtures]
    G --> H[Find Match for Same Knock Intensity]
    H --> I[Record Volume Percent of Iso-octane]
    I --> J[Report Octane Number]
    J --> K{Determine Type}
    K -->|Under RON conditions| L1[Report RON value]
    K -->|Under MON conditions| L2[Report MON value]
    L1 --> M[Compute Derived Metrics]
    L2 --> M
    M --> N1[Sensitivity equals RON minus MON]
    M --> N2[AKI equals RON plus MON divided by 2]
    M --> N3[Lead Susceptibility Test]
```


```mermaid
    B -->|Blendstock| E1[Aromatics Toluene Xylene]
```

## 4.4 Engine Knock Mechanism — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spark Initiates Combustion] --> B[Flame Front Propagates]
    B --> C{End Gas Conditions}
    C -->|Low Temp Low Pressure| D[Smooth Combustion No Knock]
    C -->|High Temp High Pressure| E[Auto-Ignition of End Gas]
    E --> F[Two Competing Flames]
    F --> G[Destructive Pressure Wave]
    G --> H[Audible Knock Pinging]
    H --> I[Power Loss and Engine Damage]
    D --> J[Higher Octane Required for High CR]
    I --> J
```
