# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Octane Number. Why is iso-octane assigned a value of 100 and n-heptane a value of 0?

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Octane Number (ON)** is the percentage of iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane) by volume in a mixture with n-heptane that has the **same knocking tendency** as the test fuel, when tested in a standard CFR engine under prescribed conditions.

- **Iso-octane (C₈H₁₈, branched):** Has a **branched molecular structure** with a **tertiary carbon** that is highly resistant to auto-ignition, even under severe temperature and pressure. It barely knocks → assigned the **top of the scale = 100**.
- **n-Heptane (C₇H₁₆, straight chain):** Has **weak, easily broken C–C bonds** along a linear chain. It auto-ignites very readily, producing violent knock → assigned the **bottom of the scale = 0**.
- The scale is **linear** between these two extremes, so a fuel that knocks like a 90% iso-octane / 10% n-heptane blend has **ON = 90**.

### Question 2: Differentiate between RON and MON. What is the Anti-Knock Index?

`[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **RON (Research Octane Number)** | **MON (Motor Octane Number)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | ASTM D2699 | ASTM D2700 |
| **Engine speed** | 600 rpm | 900 rpm |
| **Intake air temperature** | 52 °C | 149 °C |
| **Severity** | Mild | Severe |
| **Numerical value** | Higher | Lower (or equal) |
| **Driving condition simulated** | City, low-speed | Highway, high-load |

**Anti-Knock Index (AKI)** is the **arithmetic mean** of RON and MON, and is the value posted at fuel pumps (especially in the USA):

$$AKI = \frac{RON + MON}{2}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 1, Full Question]`

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **knocking** in a spark-ignition engine. How does it affect engine performance and what determines the knocking tendency of a fuel? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **CFR engine test procedure** for determining the Research Octane Number (RON) of a gasoline sample. What are the standard test conditions? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Definition of Knocking:**
Knocking (also called **detonation** or **pinking**) is the **uncontrolled, spontaneous auto-ignition of the end-gas** (the unburned air–fuel mixture ahead of the advancing flame front) inside the cylinder of a spark-ignition engine. It occurs **before** the flame front reaches it.

**2. Mechanism:**
- The spark plug initiates a **laminar flame front** that propagates smoothly.
- As combustion proceeds, the **end-gas** is subjected to rising temperature and pressure.
- If the end-gas exceeds its **auto-ignition temperature**, it spontaneously explodes, producing a second uncontrolled flame.
- The two colliding pressure waves create a **high-frequency shock wave**, producing the characteristic metallic "ping" sound.

**3. Effects on Engine Performance:**
- **Power loss** (energy dissipated in shock wave instead of pushing piston).
- **Overheating** of spark plug, piston, and cylinder head.
- **Mechanical damage:** eroded piston crowns, broken ring lands, valve damage.
- **Increased emissions** of hydrocarbons, CO, and NOx.
- **Reduced fuel economy.**

**4. Factors Determining Knocking Tendency:**
- **Fuel molecular structure** (branching → less knock).
- **Compression ratio** (higher → more knock).
- **Intake air temperature and pressure** (hotter/denser → more knock).
- **Spark advance** (more advanced → more knock).
- **Engine speed** (higher → less residence time → less knock).
- **Combustion chamber deposits** (carbon → hot spots → more knock).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**1. CFR Engine Description:**
The **Cooperative Fuel Research (CFR) engine** is a single-cylinder, variable-compression-ratio, four-stroke test engine used as the standard apparatus for octane rating worldwide.

**2. Standard Test Conditions for RON (ASTM D2699):**
- **Engine speed:** 600 rpm
- **Intake air temperature:** 52 °C (125 °F)
- **Coolant temperature:** 100 °C
- **Spark advance:** Variable (13° to 26° BTDC) — adjusted to produce a standard knock intensity of 50 on the knockmeter.
- **Carburetor:** Carefully calibrated.

**3. Step-by-Step Procedure:**

**Step 1:** Mount the test fuel in the CFR engine's carburetor.

**Step 2:** Operate the engine at the standard RON conditions and **adjust the compression ratio** until a **standard knock intensity** (typically 50 on the knockmeter) is achieved. Record this CR.

**Step 3:** Replace the test fuel with **Primary Reference Fuel (PRF) blends** of known iso-octane/n-heptane ratios (e.g., 80:20, 85:15, 90:10, 95:5).

**Step 4:** Test these PRF blends at the **same compression ratio** and find the blend that produces the **same standard knock intensity** as the test fuel.

**Step 5:** The **percentage of iso-octane** in that matching PRF blend is the **RON of the test fuel**.

**4. Importance:**
The RON represents the fuel's knock resistance under **mild** conditions, simulating **low-speed city driving**.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 1, Full Question]`

**(a)** A refinery blends **30 L of gasoline A (RON 88)**, **50 L of gasoline B (RON 92)**, and **20 L of gasoline C (RON 98)**. Calculate the **RON of the resulting blend**. If **0.6 mL/L of TEL** is added and the new RON becomes **94**, compute the **lead susceptibility** in ON per mL per litre. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **various methods used to improve the octane number of gasoline**, with emphasis on refining processes and chemical additives. Why was tetraethyl lead (TEL) phased out globally? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Given:**
- $V_A = 30$ L, $RON_A = 88$
- $V_B = 50$ L, $RON_B = 92$
- $V_C = 20$ L, $RON_C = 98$
- $V_{TEL} = 0.6$ mL/L
- $RON_{\text{with TEL}} = 94$

**Step 1: Total Volume**

$$V_{total} = V_A + V_B + V_C = 30 + 50 + 20 = 100 \text{ L}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Linear Blending Formula**

$$ON_{blend} = \frac{V_A \cdot ON_A + V_B \cdot ON_B + V_C \cdot ON_C}{V_A + V_B + V_C}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values**

$$ON_{blend} = \frac{(30)(88) + (50)(92) + (20)(98)}{100}$$

**Step 4: Compute Each Term**

$$(30)(88) = 2640$$
$$(50)(92) = 4600$$
$$(20)(98) = 1960$$

**Step 5: Sum the Numerator**

$$2640 + 4600 + 1960 = 9200$$

**Step 6: Compute the Blend RON**

$$RON_{blend} = \frac{9200}{100} = 92.0$$

**Step 7: Compute the Original RON and the ON Gain**

Base RON (no TEL) = **92.0**
With TEL RON = **94.0**
ON gain = $94.0 - 92.0 = 2.0$

**Step 8: Compute Lead Susceptibility per mL/L**

$$LS = \frac{\Delta ON}{V_{TEL}} = \frac{2.0}{0.6} = 3.333 \text{ ON per mL/L}$$

**Final Answer:**
- **Blend RON (without TEL) = 92.0**
- **Lead Susceptibility ≈ 3.33 ON per mL/L of TEL**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**1. Refining Processes that Improve Octane Number:**

**(i) Catalytic Cracking:**
Breaks large straight-chain hydrocarbons into **smaller branched-chain** molecules (and olefins). Branched structures have higher ON.

**(ii) Catalytic Reforming:**
Converts low-octane **naphthenes and paraffins** into **high-octane aromatics** (benzene, toluene, xylene) using a platinum catalyst. RON can rise from ~40 to ~95.

**(iii) Alkylation:**
Combines low-molecular-weight **olefins** (e.g., isobutylene) with **isoparaffins** (e.g., isobutane) using HF or H₂SO₄ catalysts. Produces **alkylate** with RON ~92–95 (excellent aviation gasoline base).

**(iv) Isomerization:**
Converts straight-chain paraffins (e.g., n-pentane, n-hexane) into their **branched isomers** (iso-pentane, iso-hexane), raising RON by 10–20 points.

**(v) Hydrocracking:**
Combines hydrogenation with cracking for high-yield, high-quality gasoline.

**2. Chemical Anti-Knock Additives:**

- **Tetraethyl Lead (TEL), Pb(C₂H₅)₄:** Historically the most effective (added 6–15 ON points), but phased out.
- **Methyl tert-butyl ether (MTBE):** Adds ~7–10 ON; oxygenate that reduces CO; banned in many US states due to groundwater contamination.
- **Ethyl tert-butyl ether (ETBE):** Similar to MTBE, less water-soluble, increasingly preferred.
- **Ethanol:** Adds ~10 ON, renewable, reduces CO; **E10 (10% ethanol)** is the most common gasoline in many countries.
- **Methylcyclopentadienyl Manganese Tricarbonyl (MMT):** Effective but raises particulate and spark plug fouling concerns.
- **Aromatic amines** (e.g., N-methyl aniline) — used in some racing fuels.

**3. Why TEL Was Phased Out:**

- **Toxicity:** Lead is a **cumulative neurotoxin**, especially harmful to children (causes intellectual disability, behavioural issues).
- **Catalyst poisoning:** Lead deposits foul the **catalytic converters** in modern vehicles, preventing them from controlling CO, HC, and NOx emissions.
- **Environmental contamination:** Lead particles from exhaust contaminate soil, water, and air.
- **Worker exposure:** Severe health hazard during production and handling of TEL.

> **Result:** Most countries phased out leaded gasoline by the **2000s**; the **last country** to ban leaded petrol for road use was **Algeria (2021)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing RON and MON:** Students often forget the test conditions (RON: 600 rpm/52°C; MON: 900 rpm/149°C). Always state them explicitly to get full marks.
> 2. **Blending Calculation Errors:** The blending rule is **volumetric**, not mass-based. If mass fractions are given, you must convert to volumes first using fuel densities.
> 3. **Octane vs. Cetane:** Do NOT write octane number for diesel fuels. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for confusing the two.
> 4. **Forgetting Iso-octane = 2,2,4-trimethylpentane:** Spelling out the IUPAC name is a frequent 1-mark question.
> 5. **AKI = (R+M)/2 ≠ RON:** Many students incorrectly write AKI = RON. Always specify the formula.
> 6. **TEL and Leaded Petrol:** Examiners may ask *why* lead was phased out — list **at least three reasons** (toxicity, catalyst poisoning, environmental damage).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Octane Number (Module 1, OEMUT615)**

- **Octane Number** = volumetric % of iso-octane in a PRF blend (iso-octane + n-heptane) matching the knocking intensity of the test fuel. **Scale: 0 to 100.**
- **Iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane, C₈H₁₈)** → ON = 100 (highly branched, knock resistant).
- **n-Heptane (C₇H₁₆)** → ON = 0 (straight chain, knocks violently).
- **RON (ASTM D2699):** 600 rpm, 52 °C intake air, mild test. Always **higher** value.
- **MON (ASTM D2700):** 900 rpm, 149 °C intake air, severe test. Always **lower** value.
- **AKI (Anti-Knock Index)** = **(RON + MON) / 2** — the pump-posted number in the US.
- **Sensitivity** = **RON − MON** (aromatics have high sensitivity; paraffins have low).
- **Linear Blending Formula:** $ON_{blend} = \frac{\sum V_i \cdot ON_i}{\sum V_i}$ (volumetric, weighted average).
- **Higher ON allows higher CR** → better thermal efficiency → more power.
- **Branched > straight-chain; aromatics > olefins > paraffins** in terms of ON.
- **Methods to improve ON:** catalytic cracking, reforming, alkylation, isomerization (refining); ethanol, MTBE, ETBE, MMT, TEL (additives — but TEL is phased out due to toxicity and catalyst poisoning).
- **Alternative fuel ON values to remember:** Methanol ≈ 109, Ethanol ≈ 108, LPG ≈ 110, CNG ≈ 120, Hydrogen ≈ 130+.
- **Octane Number is for SI (petrol) engines; Cetane Number is for CI (diesel) engines.** They are inversely related.
- **Lead Susceptibility** = ON(with TEL) − ON(without TEL); measured in ON per mL of TEL per litre of fuel.
- **CFR Engine** is the universal standard test apparatus for both RON and MON.
- **Engine knock** is the auto-ignition of end-gas ahead of the flame front, producing a destructive pressure wave (ping sound), power loss, and possible engine damage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cetane Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cetane Number (CN)** is a dimensionless numerical measure of the ignition quality of a diesel fuel, defined as the percentage by volume of **n-cetane (n-hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄)** in a blend with **α-methylnaphthalene** that has the same ignition delay characteristics as the test fuel under standardized engine operating conditions (ASTM D613).

The Cetane Number quantifies how readily a fuel auto-ignites when injected into the hot, compressed air charge of a Compression Ignition (C.I.) engine. A **higher CN** indicates a **shorter ignition delay period**, leading to smoother combustion, while a **lower CN** indicates prolonged ignition delay, causing engine knock (diesel knock) and rough operation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The "Matchstick Race"**
>
> Imagine you are lighting a bonfire. If you use dry tinder (high CN), it catches fire almost immediately when a spark is applied. If you use wet wood (low CN), the spark must sit on it for several seconds before the wood finally bursts into flame. The same principle applies inside a diesel engine cylinder: the fuel must "self-ignite" the moment it meets the hot compressed air. The Cetane Number tells us **how quickly that spontaneous ignition happens**.

### 1.3 Reference Fuels Used in CN Determination

| Reference Fuel | Chemical Formula | Assigned CN | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **n-Cetane (n-Hexadecane)** | C₁₆H₃₄ | **100** | Excellent ignition, short delay |
| **α-Methylnaphthalene** | C₁₁H₁₀ | **0** | Poor ignition, long delay |
| **Heptamethylnonane (HMN)** | C₁₆H₃₄ (iso) | **15** | Secondary reference (modern) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern ASTM D613 methods also use **Heptamethylnonane (HMN)** as a secondary low-CN reference (CN = 15) blended with n-cetane, since α-methylnaphthalene is toxic and produces heavy smoke.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Test Engine:** CFR (Cooperative Fuel Research) single-cylinder, variable compression ratio engine
- **Standard Test Speed:** **900 rpm**
- **Standard Test Condition:** Intake air at **150 °C** to eliminate cold-start variability
- **CN measurement basis:** Ignition delay (crank-angle degrees between injection and peak pressure rise)
- **Required CN for modern highway diesel:** **45–55**
- **Premium diesel CN range:** **55–70**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Fuel Injection into Hot Compressed Air**
   The diesel injector sprays atomized fuel into air that has been compressed to temperatures of **500–900 °C** and pressures of **30–55 bar**.

2. **Physical Delay Period**
   The fuel droplets atomize, vaporize, and mix with the surrounding air. This is purely a **physical** process governed by viscosity, surface tension, volatility, and droplet size.

3. **Chemical Delay Period**
   Pre-combustion chemical reactions occur (slow oxidation → peroxide formation → cool-flame → hot-flame). This is governed by the **molecular structure** of the fuel — particularly the **paraffin content** and **carbon chain length**.

4. **Total Ignition Delay (ID)**
   $$\text{ID}_{\text{total}} = \text{ID}_{\text{physical}} + \text{ID}_{\text{chemical}}$$

5. **Combustion Begins**
   Once auto-ignition occurs, the rapid pressure rise drives the piston down (power stroke).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The Cetane Number is essentially a **macro-indicator of the chemical delay period**. Fuels rich in **long, straight-chain paraffins** (n-alkanes) have high CN because they oxidize readily. Fuels rich in **aromatics** or **branched alkanes** have low CN because their C–C bonds resist initial oxidation.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Relationship | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cetane Number definition | $\text{CN} = \%\,V_{n\text{-cetane}}$ | dimensionless | Blend-match method |
| Modern blend relation | $\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = \text{CN}_{\text{n-cetane}} \cdot x + \text{CN}_{\text{HMN}} \cdot (1-x)$ | dimensionless | ASTM D6890 (IQT method) |
| Ignition Delay correlation | $\text{ID} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{CN}^{n}}$ where $n \approx 0.5$–$1$ | ms or ° crank | Empirical models |
| Cetane Index (calculated) | $\text{CI} = 0.554\,B + 4.13 \cdot 10^{-3} B^{2} - 2.06 \cdot 10^{-5} B^{3} + 5.31\cdot 10^{-3}\,B\cdot \rho$ | dimensionless | ASTM D976 / D4737 |
| Hydrocarbon CN trend | $n\text{-paraffins} > \text{isoparaffins} > \text{cycloparaffins} > \text{aromatics}$ | — | Fuel blending logic |
| Aromaticity penalty | $\Delta\text{CN} \approx -0.6$ to $-2.5$ per **10% aromatics** | per 10% vol | Refinery tuning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Syntax:** Vertical bars for "absolute value" have been replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.3 Why and How of Each Step — The "Why" Behind the Numbers

- **Why n-cetane gets CN = 100:** The straight C₁₆ chain has 15 C–C bonds of similar low bond-dissociation energy, all vulnerable to oxygen attack, so the molecule fragments and ignites rapidly.
- **Why α-methylnaphthalene gets CN = 0:** The fused aromatic ring is chemically stable; resonance stabilization resists oxidation, producing a very long ignition delay.
- **Why CN matters in modern CRDI engines:** Common Rail Direct Injection engines use multiple short injections. A high-CN fuel ensures each injection event auto-ignites within the precise ECU-controlled window. Low CN → misfire, smoke, noise, and emissions.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries** use CN to grade diesel pools; CN improver additives (e.g., 2-ethylhexyl nitrate, di-tert-butyl peroxide) are dosed at **100–1000 ppm** to lift CN by 3–10 points.
- **Biodiesel (FAME)** typically has CN = **48–60**, well-suited for diesel engines.
- **Petro-diesel** has CN = **45–55**.
- **Petrol/gasoline** (CN ≈ 10–15) cannot run in a CI engine — this fundamental difference separates diesel and petrol engines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cetane Number from the Blend-Match Equation

**Problem Statement:** A test fuel is matched in ignition quality by a blend containing **45% (by volume) n-cetane** and **55% α-methylnaphthalene**. Determine the Cetane Number of the test fuel.

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of CN.**

By ASTM D613 convention:
$$\text{CN} = V_{\text{n-cetane}}\,\bigl(\%\bigr)$$

where $V_{\text{n-cetane}}$ is the volume percent of n-cetane in the matched reference blend.

**Step 2 — Identify the blend composition.**

$$V_{\text{n-cetane}} = 45\%,\qquad V_{\alpha\text{-methylnaphthalene}} = 55\%$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition directly.**

$$\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = 45$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

$$\boxed{\text{CN}_{\text{test}} = 45}$$

This is at the lower edge of acceptable highway diesel — the fuel will start a diesel engine reliably only with the aid of glow plugs or a starting aid.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — CN Using the Modern HMN Reference (ASTM D6890 / IQT Method)

**Problem Statement:** The ignition quality of a test fuel is matched by a blend of **70% n-cetane (CN = 100)** and **30% heptamethylnonane (CN = 15)**. Calculate the Cetane Number of the test fuel.

**Step 1 — Write the linear blending rule.**

For two reference fuels mixed linearly in volume:
$$\text{CN}_{\text{blend}} = \text{CN}_{\text{high}} \cdot x + \text{CN}_{\text{low}} \cdot (1-x)$$

where $x$ is the volume fraction of the high-CN reference.

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$\text{CN}_{\text{blend}} = (100)(0.70) + (15)(0.30)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply each term.**

$$\text{CN}_{\text{blend}} = 70.0 + 4.5$$

**Step 4 — Sum the terms.**

$$\boxed{\text{CN}_{\text{blend}} = 74.5}$$

**Step 5 — Validation.**

Since $15 < 74.5 < 100$ and the blend is dominantly n-cetane, the result is consistent — the test fuel has a **high CN, suitable for premium diesel applications**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Calculated Cetane Index (ASTM D4737, 4-variable)

**Problem Statement:** A diesel fuel sample has the following properties:
- API gravity, $B = 40.5$
- Density, $\rho = 0.820$ g/mL
- Mid-boiling temperature, $T_{50} = 270\,°\text{C}$  
- Aniline Point, $AP = 75\,°\text{C}$

Calculate the **Calculated Cetane Index (CI)**.

**Step 1 — Recall the 4-variable ASTM D4737 empirical formula.**

$$\text{CI} = 45.2 + 0.0892\,T_{10} + (0.131 + 0.901\,B)\,T_{50} + 0.000423\,(T_{50})^{2} - 0.0042\,(T_{90})^{2} - 12.8\,AP/\rho$$

> [!NOTE]
> For this problem we use the simpler **2-variable ASTM D976** form (B and ρ only), since $T_{10}$ and $T_{90}$ are not given.

**Step 2 — Recall the ASTM D976 2-variable equation.**

$$\text{CI} = 0.554\,B + 4.13 \times 10^{-3}\,B^{2} - 2.06 \times 10^{-5}\,B^{3} + 5.31 \times 10^{-3}\,B\,\rho$$

**Step 3 — Compute $B^{2}$ and $B^{3}$.**

$$B^{2} = (40.5)^{2} = 1640.25$$
$$B^{3} = (40.5)^{3} = 66430.125$$

**Step 4 — Compute each term.**

Term 1:
$$0.554 \times 40.5 = 22.437$$

Term 2:
$$4.13 \times 10^{-3} \times 1640.25 = 6.774$$

Term 3:
$$-2.06 \times 10^{-5} \times 66430.125 = -1.368$$

Term 4:
$$5.31 \times 10^{-3} \times 40.5 \times 0.820 = 0.1764$$

**Step 5 — Sum all terms.**

$$\text{CI} = 22.437 + 6.774 - 1.368 + 0.1764$$

$$\boxed{\text{CI} \approx 28.02}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering judgement.**

A CI of **28** is well below the **45** highway-diesel requirement, suggesting the fuel needs a **CN improver additive** such as 2-ethylhexyl nitrate at a dosage of ~0.1–0.3% by volume.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CN of Common Fuels (KTU Quick Reference)

| Fuel | Typical CN | Hydrocarbon Class | CN Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **n-Hexadecane** | 100 | n-Paraffin | Reference (best) |
| **n-Dodecane** | 87 | n-Paraffin | Excellent |
| **n-Decane** | 76 | n-Paraffin | Excellent |
| **Petro-diesel (BS-VI)** | 48–55 | Mixed HC | Standard |
| **Biodiesel (Soybean FAME)** | 50–52 | Fatty acid methyl ester | Good |
| **Biodiesel (Palm FAME)** | 62 | FAME | Excellent |
| **Biodiesel (Waste cooking oil)** | 45–50 | FAME | Marginal |
| **Petrol / Gasoline** | 10–18 | Mixed HC/aromatic | **Not usable in CI** |
| **Ethanol** | 8–11 | Alcohol | Very poor |
| **Methanol** | 3–5 | Alcohol | Very poor |
| **Hydrogen (H₂)** | 0 (no delay) | Element | Special handling |
| **Dimethyl ether (DME)** | 55–60 | Ether | Excellent |
| **α-Methylnaphthalene** | 0 | Aromatic | Reference (worst) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Cetane Number Determination Procedure (ASTM D613)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Test Fuel Sample] --> B[Load into CFR Engine]
    B --> C[Set Compression Ratio]
    C --> D[Inject Test Fuel]
    D --> E[Measure Ignition Delay]
    E --> F[Prepare Reference Blends]
    F --> G[Blend n-cetane plus alpha-methylnaphthalene]
    G --> H[Match Ignition Delay of Test Fuel]
    H --> I[Read Volume Percent of n-cetane]
    I --> J[Record CN Value]
    J --> K[End: Report CN]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Influence of Fuel Structure on Cetane Number

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Molecular Structure] --> B[Carbon Chain Length]
    A --> C[Branching Pattern]
    A --> D[Aromaticity]
    B --> E[Long straight chains]
    C --> F[More branching]
    D --> G[More aromatic rings]
    E --> H[HIGH Cetane Number]
    F --> I[LOWER Cetane Number]
    G --> J[LOWEST Cetane Number]
    H --> K[Short ignition delay]
    I --> L[Longer ignition delay]
    J --> M[Very long ignition delay]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cetane Number Engineering Impact

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Cetane Number] --> Q1[High CN greater than 50]
    P --> Q2[Low CN less than 40]
    Q1 --> R1[Short ignition delay]
    Q1 --> R2[Smooth combustion]
    Q1 --> R3[Lower NOx and smoke]
    R1 --> S1[Better cold start]
    R2 --> S2[Quieter engine]
    Q2 --> T1[Long ignition delay]
    Q2 --> T2[Diesel knock]
    Q2 --> T3[Higher emissions]
    T1 --> U1[Hard starting]
    T2 --> U2[Engine noise and wear]
    T3 --> U3[HC and PM increase]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Architecture — Diesel Combustion Phases Linked to CN

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Injection Begins] --> B[Physical Delay]
    B --> C[Chemical Delay]
    C --> D[Premixed Combustion]
    D --> E[Diffusion Combustion]
    E --> F[After Burning]
    B -.->|shortened by high CN| G[Reduced Pressure Rise Rate]
    C -.->|shortened by high CN| G
    D -.->|controlled by CN| H[Smoother Engine]
    E -.->|fuel-bound| I[Power Output]
```

### 4.5 Comparison Table — Octane vs Cetane Number (KTU Favourite)

| Parameter | Octane Number (ON) | Cetane Number (CN) |
|---|---|---|
| Engine type | Spark Ignition (SI) | Compression Ignition (CI) |
| Desired behaviour | **Resists** knock | **Promotes** auto-ignition |
| High value means | More resistance to knock | More easily auto-ignites |
| Reference high | Iso-octane (ON = 100) | n-Cetane (CN = 100) |
| Reference low | n-Heptane (ON = 0) | α-Methylnaphthalene (CN = 0) |
| Test method | ASTM D2700 / D2699 | ASTM D613 / D6890 |
| Effect of high value | Allows higher CR | Shorter ignition delay |
| Affected by aromatics | Aromatics increase ON | Aromatics decrease CN |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define Cetane Number. Why is it important for Compression Ignition engines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Cetane Number is the percentage by volume of n-cetane (n-hexadecane) in a reference blend with α-methylnaphthalene that has the same ignition delay characteristics as the test fuel, when tested in a standard CFR engine under ASTM D613 conditions.
- **Importance (1.5 marks):** It determines the ignition delay of the fuel in a CI engine. A high CN ensures short delay → smooth combustion, easy cold start, lower knock, reduced smoke and noise. Highway diesel requires CN ≥ 45 as per BIS IS 1460 / BS-VI norms.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**List the reference fuels used in Cetane Number testing and state their assigned CN values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **n-Cetane (n-Hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄):** Assigned CN = **100** (high-quality, straight-chain paraffin with excellent auto-ignition).
- **α-Methylnaphthalene (C₁₁H₁₀):** Assigned CN = **0** (highly aromatic, very poor auto-ignition).
- **Heptamethylnonane (HMN):** Assigned CN = **15** (used in modern IQT method, ASTM D6890, as a safer secondary low-CN reference).
- The test fuel's CN = volume % of n-cetane in the blend whose ignition delay matches the test fuel.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — Module 1 Choice A (14 marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **ASTM D613 method** for determining the Cetane Number of a diesel fuel. Discuss the role of reference fuels, the operating conditions, and the principle of matching ignition delay. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **effect of fuel molecular structure** on Cetane Number, with examples. Explain why n-paraffins give high CN while aromatics give low CN. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q3(a):**

1. **Principle (1 mark):** The Cetane Number is determined by comparing the ignition delay of the test fuel with that of reference blends of n-cetane (CN = 100) and α-methylnaphthalene (CN = 0) in a standardized CFR engine.

2. **Test Engine (1 mark):** A single-cylinder, four-stroke, variable compression ratio CFR engine is used. Standard speed = **900 rpm**.

3. **Operating Conditions (1 mark):** Intake air is preheated to **150 °C** to eliminate cold-start variables; injection timing is fixed; injection pressure is set per spec.

4. **Procedure (2 marks):**
   - The test fuel is injected; the **ignition delay** (in crank-angle degrees) is measured between the start of injection and the point of maximum pressure rise.
   - A series of reference blends of n-cetane and α-methylnaphthalene are then prepared and tested.
   - The blend whose ignition delay matches the test fuel is identified.

5. **CN Determination (1 mark):** The volume percent of n-cetane in the matching blend is reported as the Cetane Number of the test fuel.

6. **Limitations (1 mark):** Expensive, slow, requires skilled operator; limited to fuels with adequate volatility; not applicable to fuels with very low or very high CN (>70 or <30) without extrapolation.

---

**Model Solution to Q3(b):**

1. **General trend (1.5 marks):** The Cetane Number depends strongly on the **hydrocarbon family**:
   $$\text{n-paraffins} > \text{isoparaffins} > \text{cycloparaffins} > \text{aromatics}$$

2. **n-Paraffins give highest CN (2 marks):** Long, straight chains (e.g., n-cetane C₁₆H₃₄, n-dodecane C₁₂H₂₆) have many C–C bonds of nearly uniform, low bond-dissociation energy. Oxygen radicals attack these bonds easily → short chemical delay → high CN.

3. **Isoparaffins give moderate CN (1 mark):** Branching reduces the number of easily oxidizable primary C–H bonds and creates steric hindrance. Example: isooctane has CN ≈ 15–20, far below n-octane (CN ≈ 64).

4. **Cycloparaffins and aromatics give low CN (2 marks):** Aromatic rings have resonance stabilization that resists oxidation. α-methylnaphthalene (CN = 0) is the classic example. Aromatic-rich diesel streams reduce CN and increase smoke and unburned HC emissions.

5. **Engineering implication (0.5 mark):** Refineries blend streams to achieve target CN of 45–55; CN improver additives (alkyl nitrates, peroxides) are used to compensate for aromatic-rich cracked stocks.

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Module 1 Choice B (14 marks)**

**(a)** A diesel fuel sample has API gravity **B = 38.0** and density **ρ = 0.825 g/mL**. Compute the **Calculated Cetane Index (CI)** using the ASTM D976 2-variable equation. Comment on whether the fuel is suitable as highway diesel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Cetane Number with Octane Number**. Why is high CN required in CI engines but high ON required in SI engines? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q4(a):**

**Step 1 — Use the ASTM D976 2-variable equation.**
$$\text{CI} = 0.554\,B + 4.13 \times 10^{-3}\,B^{2} - 2.06 \times 10^{-5}\,B^{3} + 5.31 \times 10^{-3}\,B\,\rho$$

**Step 2 — Compute powers of B.**
$$B = 38.0,\quad B^{2} = 1444.0,\quad B^{3} = 54872.0$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term.**
- Term 1: $0.554 \times 38.0 = 21.052$
- Term 2: $4.13 \times 10^{-3} \times 1444.0 = 5.964$
- Term 3: $-2.06 \times 10^{-5} \times 54872.0 = -1.130$
- Term 4: $5.31 \times 10^{-3} \times 38.0 \times 0.825 = 0.1664$

**Step 4 — Sum the terms.**
$$\text{CI} = 21.052 + 5.964 - 1.130 + 0.1664$$
$$\boxed{\text{CI} \approx 26.05}$$

**Step 5 — Comment on suitability.**
- The required CN for highway diesel (BIS IS 1460) is **≥ 45**.
- The calculated CI = 26 is well below this threshold.
- **Conclusion (1 mark):** The fuel is **not suitable as highway diesel** without blending with higher-CN stocks or adding a CN improver such as 2-ethylhexyl nitrate at ~0.2–0.5% by volume.

---

**Model Solution to Q4(b):**

| Comparison Parameter | Octane Number (ON) | Cetane Number (CN) |
|---|---|---|
| Engine | SI (petrol) | CI (diesel) |
| Knock | Resists knock | Resists auto-ignition |
| Desired value | High (RON/MON ≥ 90 for modern petrol) | High (CN ≥ 45 for modern diesel) |
| Why high is desired | High ON → fuel-air mixture can be compressed without premature auto-ignition → enables **higher compression ratio** → higher thermal efficiency | High CN → fuel **auto-ignites quickly** after injection → smooth combustion, easy start, low noise, low emissions |
| Effect of aromatics | Increases ON | Decreases CN |
| Effect of long n-paraffins | Decreases ON | Increases CN |
| Reference high | Iso-octane (ON = 100) | n-Cetane (CN = 100) |
| Reference low | n-Heptane (ON = 0) | α-Methylnaphthalene (CN = 0) |

**Why this contrast (1 mark):** SI engines need fuel that **resists** auto-ignition (otherwise pre-ignition/detonation at high CR destroys the engine). CI engines **depend** on auto-ignition (no spark plug), so fuel must ignite readily on contact with hot compressed air. The two numbers are thus **inversely motivated** by combustion principles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **not** confuse Octane Number and Cetane Number — a common mistake in Part A. Remember: **SI → Octane, CI → Cetane**.
> - In numerical problems, **always show the full arithmetic**, not just the final answer. Examiners award partial marks for each correct intermediate step.
> - When reporting CN, **do not write units** (CN is dimensionless). Writing "CN = 45%" is acceptable since CN = volume % of n-cetane, but "CN = 45 units" is **wrong**.
> - For Cetane Index (CI) problems, **mention ASTM D976 vs D4737** to show syllabus awareness.
> - Always close a 7-mark sub-part with an **engineering interpretation** (e.g., "This fuel needs CN improver additives" or "This is suitable for highway use").

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cetane Number**

- **Definition:** CN = volume % of n-cetane in a n-cetane + α-methylnaphthalene blend matching the test fuel's ignition delay (ASTM D613).
- **Reference Fuels:** n-cetane (CN = 100), α-methylnaphthalene (CN = 0), Heptamethylnonane (CN = 15, secondary).
- **CFR Engine Test Conditions:** single cylinder, 900 rpm, 150 °C intake air, variable CR.
- **Modern Method:** IQT (Ignition Quality Tester) per ASTM D6890 — uses n-cetane + HMN.
- **CN Order by HC family:** n-paraffins > isoparaffins > cycloparaffins > aromatics.
- **Modern Diesel CN Spec:** 45–55 (BS-VI / IS 1460).
- **Biodiesel CN:** 45–62 (FAME) — generally higher than petro-diesel.
- **Petrol CN:** 10–18 → **not usable in CI engines** without major modification.
- **Ignition Delay:** $\text{ID} = \text{ID}_\text{physical} + \text{ID}_\text{chemical}$; high CN shortens chemical delay.
- **Effects of Low CN:** diesel knock, hard cold start, smoke, noise, higher HC and PM emissions, engine wear.
- **Effects of High CN:** smooth combustion, easy start, lower emissions, but very high CN (>65) can cause pre-ignition in some CRDI engines.
- **Calculated Cetane Index (CI):** Empirical correlation from density, API gravity, distillation temperatures; ASTM D976 (2-variable) and D4737 (4-variable).
- **CN Improvers:** 2-Ethylhexyl nitrate (2-EHN), di-tert-butyl peroxide; dosages of 100–3000 ppm; raise CN by 3–12 points.
- **Octane vs Cetane:** SI engines need high ON; CI engines need high CN — the two requirements are **mirror opposites**.
- **Common exam traps:** Confusing ON/CN; forgetting that CN is dimensionless; not stating the ASTM standard; missing the "why" in compare-and-contrast questions.
- **Quick memory hook:** "**C**etane is for **C**ompression-ignition **D**iesel — think **CCD**."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties for SI Engine Fuels & CI Engine Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties for SI Engine Fuels & CI Engine Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Fuel Properties

In the realm of internal combustion (IC) engines, the **fuel** is the chemical energy carrier whose combustion characteristics fundamentally dictate the design, performance, efficiency, and emissions of the engine. KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that the suitability of a fuel for either a **Spark Ignition (SI)** or **Compression Ignition (CI)** engine is determined not by a single parameter, but by a *constellation of physical and chemical properties*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEMUT615 Module 1):**
> The properties of conventional SI engine fuels (e.g., gasoline/petrol) and CI engine fuels (e.g., diesel) form the benchmark against which ALL alternate fuels (CNG, LPG, ethanol, methanol, biodiesel, hydrogen, etc.) are evaluated. Without mastering these baseline properties, comparative analysis of alternate fuels is impossible.

---

## 1.2 Why Do SI and CI Engines Need DIFFERENT Fuels?

| Aspect | SI Engine (Petrol/Gasoline) | CI Engine (Diesel) |
|---|---|---|
| **Ignition Method** | Spark plug initiates combustion | Heat of compression ignites fuel |
| **Mixture Formation** | Premixed (carburetor/MPFI) | Diffused (direct injection) |
| **Key Desired Property** | High resistance to *auto-ignition* (knock) | Easy *auto-ignition* (short delay) |
| **Benchmark Metric** | **Octane Number (ON)** | **Cetane Number (CN)** |
| **Combustion Type** | Premixed flame propagation | Spray auto-ignition & diffusion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for Students):**
> Think of **SI fuels** like a *careful student* — they should NOT spontaneously act (auto-ignite) before being told to (spark). They wait for the spark command.
> Think of **CI fuels** like an *eager student* — they should react QUICKLY and predictably once pressure/temperature conditions are right (high compression). They are *primed* to ignite on their own.
> These opposing needs are why petrol and diesel have fundamentally different chemical structures and volatility profiles.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Fuel Properties (Module 1 Scope)

The KTU Module 1 syllabus broadly divides these properties into:

1. **Combustion-Related Properties** (Octane Number, Cetane Number, Calorific Value, Ignition Delay)
2. **Volatility Properties** (Vapour Pressure, Distillation Range, Flash Point, Pour Point, Cloud Point)
3. **Flow Properties** (Viscosity, Density)
4. **Stability Properties** (Gum formation, Oxidation stability, Carbon residue)
5. **Contaminant Properties** (Sulfur, Ash, Water content)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SI ENGINE FUEL PROPERTIES (Gasoline / Petrol)

### 2.1.1 Octane Number (ON) — The MOST Critical Property
- **Definition:** Measure of a fuel's resistance to *knocking* (uncontrolled auto-ignition) in an SI engine.
- **Reference Scale:**
  - **Iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane):** ON = 100 (high knock resistance)
  - **n-Heptane:** ON = 0 (low knock resistance)
  - **Octane Number** = % of iso-octane in a blend with n-heptane that matches the knock intensity of the test fuel.
- **Two Test Methods:**
  - **Research Octane Number (RON):** Mild conditions (600 rpm, low intake temp) → measures low-speed knock → typically HIGHER value
  - **Motor Octane Number (MON):** Severe conditions (900 rpm, high intake temp) → measures high-speed knock → typically LOWER value
  - **Anti-Knock Index (AKI)** or **(R+M)/2** = Average displayed at Indian fuel pumps.

### 2.1.2 Volatility
- Governed by **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)** and **Distillation Curve**.
- Affects:
  - Cold start (need high volatility at low temperature)
  - Vapour lock (too high volatility causes problem in hot weather)
  - Crankcase dilution
  - Acceleration

### 2.1.3 Other SI Engine Fuel Properties
- **Calorific Value:** Lower than diesel (~44 MJ/kg for petrol).
- **Heat of Vaporization:** High — cools intake charge, improving volumetric efficiency.
- **Gum Formation:** Undesirable; measured as *Existent Gum* and *Potential Gum* (mg/100mL).
- **Sulphur Content:** Should be minimized (< 50 ppm) to prevent catalyst poisoning and acid rain.

---

## 2.2 CI ENGINE FUEL PROPERTIES (Diesel)

### 2.2.1 Cetane Number (CN) — The MOST Critical Property
- **Definition:** Measure of a fuel's *ignition quality* (speed of auto-ignition) in a CI engine.
- **Reference Scale:**
  - **n-Cetane (n-hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄):** CN = 100 (fast ignition)
  - **α-methylnaphthalene:** CN = 0 (slow ignition)
  - **Cetane Number** = % of n-cetane in a blend with α-methylnaphthalene that matches the ignition delay of the test fuel.
- **Relation:** CN and ON are **inversely related** — a high ON fuel has low CN and vice versa.

### 2.2.2 Aniline Point and Diesel Index
- **Aniline Point:** Lowest temperature at which equal volumes of aniline (C₆H₅NH₂) and fuel are completely miscible.
- **Diesel Index (DI):** Empirical measure of ignition quality.
- **Calculated Cetane Index (CCI):** Modern ASTM-derived formula; see Section 3.

### 2.2.3 Other CI Engine Fuel Properties
- **Pour Point:** Lowest temperature at which fuel flows under gravity. Critical for cold climates.
- **Cloud Point:** Temperature at which wax crystals first become visible. Defines low-temperature operability.
- **Flash Point & Fire Point:** For diesel, Flash Point is **above 52°C** (safety in storage/transport).
- **Carbon Residue (Ramsbottom or Conradson):** Indicates tendency to form deposits in injectors and combustion chamber.
- **Ash Content:** Inorganic residue; should be < 0.01% for modern engines.
- **Viscosity:** Critical for injector spray atomization.

---

## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (CHEAT SHEET)

| # | Property | Formula / Definition | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Octane Number (ON) | ON = % iso-octane in iso-octane + n-heptane blend matching test fuel | Dimensionless | SI engine knock rating |
| 2 | Anti-Knock Index (AKI) | $\text{AKI} = \dfrac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2}$ | Dimensionless | Pump octane display |
| 3 | Cetane Number (CN) | CN = % n-cetane in n-cetane + α-methylnaphthalene blend matching test fuel | Dimensionless | CI engine ignition quality |
| 4 | Diesel Index (DI) | $\text{DI} = \dfrac{\text{Aniline Point (°F)} \times \text{API Gravity}}{100}$ | Dimensionless | Older CI fuel quality |
| 5 | Calculated Cetane Index (CCI) — ASTM D976 | $\text{CCI} = 0.49083 + 1.06577(\text{DI}) - 0.00106(\text{DI})^{2}$ | Dimensionless | Approx. CN without engine test |
| 6 | CCI — ASTM D4737 (4-variable) | $\text{CCI} = 45.2 + 0.0892(T_{10}) + (0.131 + 0.901\,B)\times(T_{50}) + (0.0523 - 0.420\,B)\times(T_{90}) + 0.00049(T_{10}^2 - T_{90}^2) + 107\,B + 60\,B^2$ | Dimensionless | Refined CI fuel estimation |
| 7 | API Gravity | $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{Specific Gravity (60°F/60°F)}} - 131.5$ | Degrees | Density expression for petroleum |
| 8 | Specific Gravity (SG) | $\text{SG} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{fuel}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | Dimensionless | Density comparison |
| 9 | Heating Value Ratio (Air-Fuel) | $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{\text{A/F ratio of fuel}}{\text{A/F ratio of reference}}$ | Dimensionless | Equivalence ratio studies |
| 10 | Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP) | Direct measurement at 100°F (37.8°C), 4:1 vapor-liquid ratio | psi or kPa | Volatility for SI fuels |
| 11 | Heat of Combustion (HHV vs LHV) | $\text{HHV} = \text{LHV} + h_{fg}(m_{\text{water}}/m_{\text{fuel}})$ | kJ/kg | Energy content of fuel |
| 12 | Equivalent Cetane Number from CN ↔ ON | $\text{ON} + \text{CN} \approx 100$ (rough empirical) | Dimensionless | Approximate inverse relation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern Note:**
> Questions 3, 4, 5, and 12 in the formula sheet are *guaranteed high-weightage* topics. Any numerical involving **Diesel Index, Calculated Cetane Index, or (R+M)/2** must be practiced thoroughly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Anti-Knock Index (AKI) from RON and MON

The **AKI** is mandated for display at the dispensing pump by BIS (India) and ASTM. It is the arithmetic mean of the Research Octane Number (RON) and Motor Octane Number (MON).

$$
\text{AKI} = \frac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2}
$$

### Step-by-Step Logical Explanation:
- **Step 1:** RON is measured under low-speed, low-temperature conditions (600 rpm) representing *city driving*.
- **Step 2:** MON is measured under high-speed, high-temperature conditions (900 rpm) representing *highway/load driving*.
- **Step 3:** Real driving is a *mixture* of both, so a simple average is the most representative single-number indicator.
- **Step 4:** Therefore, the displayed octane at Indian fuel pumps (e.g., 91, 95) is technically the AKI.

### Worked Example:
A fuel has RON = 96 and MON = 86. Find the pump octane number.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AKI} &= \frac{96 + 86}{2} \\
\text{AKI} &= \frac{182}{2} \\
\text{AKI} &= 91
\end{aligned}
$$

The pump rating is **91 octane**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Calculated Cetane Index (CCI) via ASTM D976

The **Diesel Index (DI)** is a *historical* correlation, while **CCI (ASTM D976)** is the *engineered* polynomial fit. The derivation stems from regression analysis of experimentally measured CN for many diesel fuels.

### Step 1: Compute API Gravity
Given specific gravity at 60°F/60°F:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{\text{SG}} - 131.5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Diesel Index

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DI} = \frac{\text{Aniline Point (°F)} \times \text{°API}}{100}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Calculated Cetane Index (CCI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCI} = 0.49083 + 1.06577(\text{DI}) - 0.00106(\text{DI})^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example:
A diesel fuel has aniline point = 70°C and specific gravity = 0.85. Compute the CCI.

**Step A — Convert Aniline Point to °F:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{A}\,(^{\circ}\text{F}) &= \frac{9}{5} \times 70 + 32 \\
&= 126 + 32 \\
&= 158\,^{\circ}\text{F}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Compute API Gravity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{°API} &= \frac{141.5}{0.85} - 131.5 \\
&= 166.47 - 131.5 \\
&= 34.97
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Compute Diesel Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DI} &= \frac{158 \times 34.97}{100} \\
&= 55.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Compute CCI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCI} &= 0.49083 + 1.06577(55.25) - 0.00106(55.25)^{2} \\
&= 0.49083 + 58.884 - 3.234 \\
&= 56.14
\end{aligned}
$$

The Calculated Cetane Index is approximately **56.14**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship Between Octane Number and Cetane Number

For paraffinic hydrocarbons, an *empirical* inverse relation holds:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ON} + \text{CN} \approx 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### Justification:
- **Iso-octane:** ON = 100, CN ≈ 12 → Sum ≈ 112 (close to 100).
- **n-Heptane:** ON = 0, CN ≈ 53 → Sum ≈ 53 (deviation).
- **n-Cetane:** CN = 100, ON ≈ -25 (extrapolated) → Sum ≈ 75.

The relationship is **approximate**, useful for **rapid cross-checking**, but never used in formal KTU numericals.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Property Comparison of SI vs CI Fuels

| Property | Petrol (SI) | Diesel (CI) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | 720–780 | 820–860 | Affects energy density per litre |
| Cetane Number | 10–20 | 45–55 | CI fuel ignites easily |
| Octane Number | 88–98 | 20–30 | SI fuel resists knock |
| Flash Point (°C) | -43 | 52–96 | Diesel is safer to store |
| RVP (kPa) | 60–90 | < 1 | Petrol highly volatile |
| Viscosity (cSt at 40°C) | 0.5–0.8 | 2.0–6.0 | Diesel needs robust injection pump |
| Sulphur (mass %) | < 0.02 | 0.05–0.25 | Diesel historically dirtier |
| Calorific Value (MJ/kg) | 44 | 45.5 | Diesel more energetic per kg |
| Autoignition Temp (°C) | ~250 | ~210 | Diesel ignites at lower T |
| Latent Heat of Vap. (kJ/kg) | 305–350 | 250–300 | Petrol cools intake better |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The single most exam-relevant comparison is **ON vs CN** with the inverse correlation. Always remember: high-CN fuel has low-ON and vice versa.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Practice Template (For KTU)

### Practice Problem 1: Pump Octane Calculation
A fuel sample exhibits RON = 95.4 and MON = 85.2. Calculate the displayed pump octane.

### Practice Problem 2: CCI from Density
A diesel sample has SG = 0.87, aniline point = 76°C. Estimate CN via ASTM D976.

*(Model solutions in Section 5 Part B.)*

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: SI vs CI Fuel Property Decision Tree

```mermaid

    A[Internal Combustion Engine Fuel] --> B{Application}
    B -->|SI Engine| C[Petrol / Gasoline Family]
    B -->|CI Engine| D[Diesel Family]

    C --> C1[Octane Number - PRIMARY]
    C1 --> C1a[RON, MON, AKI]
    C --> C2[Volatility]
    C2 --> C2a[RVP]
    C2 --> C2b[Distillation 10/50/90%]
    C --> C3[Stability]
    C3 --> C3a[Gum content]
    C3 --> C3b[Sulphur content]

    D --> D1[Cetane Number - PRIMARY]
    D1 --> D1a[Direct CN]
    D1 --> D1b[Calculated CN]
    D1 --> D1c[Diesel Index]
    D --> D2[Low-Temp Flow]
    D2 --> D2a[Pour Point]
    D2 --> D2b[Cloud Point]
    D --> D3[Lubricity]
    D3 --> D3a[Viscosity]
    D3 --> D3b[Carbon Residue]

```

    P4[Low Flash Point in Diesel] --> F4[Fire Hazard]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit Confusion:** Students frequently forget to convert **Aniline Point from °C to °F** before applying the Diesel Index formula. This single error can cost **3–4 marks**.
> - **API vs SG:** Mixing up *specific gravity* and *API gravity* is a classic error. SG > 1 ⟹ °API < 10 (heavy fuel).
> - **AKI vs RON:** Writing "91 octane petrol" without specifying whether it is RON or AKI is incomplete — KTU examiners deduct marks.
> - **Cetane vs Octane inversion:** Stating "diesel has high octane" is a fundamental conceptual blunder.

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Define Octane Number. How is it determined in the laboratory?**

**Model Answer:**
Octane Number (ON) is a numerical measure of a fuel's resistance to knock (uncontrolled auto-ignition) in a Spark Ignition engine. It is determined using a **CFR (Cooperative Fuel Research) engine** under standardized conditions, comparing the test fuel's knock intensity against blends of **iso-octane (ON = 100)** and **n-heptane (ON = 0)**. The percentage of iso-octane in the reference blend that matches the knock intensity gives the ON of the test fuel.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**What is Cetane Number? Why is a higher CN preferred in CI engines?**

**Model Answer:**
Cetane Number (CN) is a measure of the **ignition quality** of a diesel fuel — specifically, the **inverse of the ignition delay period**. It is determined by comparing the test fuel with blends of **n-cetane (CN = 100)** and **α-methylnaphthalene (CN = 0)** in a CFR diesel engine.
A **higher CN** means a **shorter ignition delay**, leading to:
- Smooth combustion,
- Easier cold starting,
- Reduced diesel knock (audible clatter),
- Lower exhaust smoke and unburnt hydrocarbons.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Past Pattern Model]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, explain the **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)** apparatus and its significance for SI engine fuels. How does RVP affect **cold starting**, **vapour lock**, and **crankcase dilution**?

**(b)** [7 Marks] The specific gravity of a diesel fuel is **0.86** and its aniline point is **75°C**. Determine the **API gravity, Diesel Index, and Calculated Cetane Index (ASTM D976)** of the fuel.

---

### Model Solution to Q.A(a) [7 Marks]

**Apparatus Description (RVP Bomb):**
- Reid Vapour Pressure is measured in a **two-chamber bomb** consisting of:
  1. A **fuel chamber** (larger, lower part) filled to ~70% with the cooled fuel sample.
  2. An **air chamber** (smaller, upper part) pre-pressurized to atmospheric.
- The two chambers are connected through a valve.
- The bomb is immersed in a water bath at **37.8°C (100°F)**.
- After equilibrium, the pressure (in psi or kPa) is read on a pressure gauge.

**Significance in SI Engines:**

| Engine Operating Concern | Effect of RVP |
|---|---|
| **Cold Starting** | High RVP → more vapours at low temp → easier starting. Required: **RVP > 60 kPa** in winter. |
| **Vapour Lock** | High RVP in hot weather → fuel vapour bubbles in fuel line → engine stalls. Must be < **80 kPa** in summer. |
| **Crankcase Dilution** | Excessively high RVP → fuel vapours enter crankcase → dilutes lubricating oil → wear. |
| **Acceleration & Driveability** | Affects the distillation curve and front-end volatility. |

> **Engineering Practice (BIS Specification):**
> Indian regular petrol: RVP = 60 kPa (winter), 50 kPa (summer). Bharat Stage VI norms do not directly set RVP, but ASTM D4814 governs it.

---

### Model Solution to Q.A(b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Specific Gravity (SG) = 0.86
- Aniline Point = 75°C

**Step 1 — Compute API Gravity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{°API} &= \frac{141.5}{\text{SG}} - 131.5 \\
&= \frac{141.5}{0.86} - 131.5 \\
&= 164.53 - 131.5 \\
&= 33.03\,\text{°API}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Aniline Point to °F:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_A\,(^{\circ}\text{F}) &= \frac{9}{5} \times 75 + 32 \\
&= 135 + 32 \\
&= 167\,^{\circ}\text{F}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Diesel Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DI} &= \frac{T_A(^{\circ}\text{F}) \times \text{°API}}{100} \\
&= \frac{167 \times 33.03}{100} \\
&= 55.16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Calculated Cetane Index (ASTM D976):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCI} &= 0.49083 + 1.06577(\text{DI}) - 0.00106(\text{DI})^{2} \\
&= 0.49083 + 1.06577(55.16) - 0.00106(55.16)^{2} \\
&= 0.49083 + 58.789 - 3.225 \\
&= 56.05
\end{aligned}
$$

**Results Summary:**
- API Gravity = **33.03 °API**
- Diesel Index = **55.16**
- Calculated Cetane Index = **56.05**

This indicates a **good quality diesel** suitable for high-speed CI engines.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Past Pattern Model]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare the **Octane Number** and **Cetane Number** systems in detail. Explain why they are required to be different for SI and CI engines, with reference to combustion phenomena.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A fuel shows RON = 92 and MON = 84. Determine the **(R+M)/2 rating** and comment on the suitability of this fuel for a high-compression-ratio SI engine.

---

### Model Solution to Q.B(a) [7 Marks]

| Aspect | Octane Number (ON) | Cetane Number (CN) |
|---|---|---|
| Engine Type | SI | CI |
| Reference High | Iso-octane (C₈H₁₈, branched) | n-Cetane (C₁₆H₃₄, straight) |
| Reference Low | n-Heptane (C₇H₁₆, straight) | α-methylnaphthalene (aromatic) |
| Phenomenon Measured | Resistance to *knock* (auto-ignition) | Tendency toward *auto-ignition* (ignition delay) |
| Test Engine | CFR Octane Engine | CFR Diesel Engine |
| Test Conditions | 600 rpm / 900 rpm, variable intake | Variable compression ratio |
| Typical Petrol Value | 88–98 | 10–20 |
| Typical Diesel Value | 20–30 | 45–55 |

**Why are they different?**

In an **SI engine**, the air-fuel mixture is compressed below its auto-ignition temperature, and combustion is initiated by a spark. If the fuel auto-ignites *before* flame arrival, **knock** occurs — destructive pressure waves, piston damage, and efficiency loss. Therefore, the fuel must have a **high ON (high resistance to auto-ignition)**.

In a **CI engine**, only air is compressed (to a very high ratio), heating it above the fuel's auto-ignition temperature. Fuel is injected and must ignite **promptly** upon entering this hot environment. A long ignition delay causes **diesel knock (rough combustion)**, smoke, and incomplete burning. Therefore, the fuel must have a **high CN (short ignition delay)**.

**Chemical Structure Insight:**
- **Branch-chain paraffins** (e.g., iso-octane) → High ON, Low CN.
- **Straight-chain paraffins** (e.g., n-cetane) → Low ON, High CN.
- **Aromatics** → High ON, Low CN.

---

### Model Solution to Q.B(b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** RON = 92, MON = 84

**Step 1 — Compute (R+M)/2 rating:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(R+M)/2} &= \frac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2} \\
&= \frac{92 + 84}{2} \\
&= \frac{176}{2} \\
&= 88
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:**

The displayed pump octane (AKI) is **88**.

**Step 3 — Suitability for High-Compression SI Engine:**

- A **high compression ratio (CR > 10:1)** engine demands an octane requirement of **~92 RON or higher**.
- This fuel's RON (92) is **borderline acceptable**, but AKI = 88 suggests it may **knock at high load or wide-open throttle**.
- **Recommendation:** Either lower the CR of the engine, use this fuel with **retarded ignition timing**, or upgrade to fuel with **RON ≥ 95** (Premium petrol).

**Conclusion:** The fuel is *marginally suitable* for a high-CR SI engine; for sustained knock-free operation, a higher RON fuel is recommended.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Properties of SI and CI Engine Fuels: Quick-Fire Recall**

### SI Engine Fuel (Petrol) — Must Know:
- **Octane Number (ON)** is the PRIMARY quality metric. Two test methods → RON & MON.
- **(R+M)/2 = AKI** is the pump-displayed value.
- **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)** governs cold start AND vapour lock — a *trade-off*.
- **Distillation 10%, 50%, 90%** points define volatility profile.
- **Existent Gum** and **Potential Gum** indicate storage stability.
- **Sulphur < 50 ppm** for BS-VI compliance.

### CI Engine Fuel (Diesel) — Must Know:
- **Cetane Number (CN)** is the PRIMARY quality metric. Higher CN = shorter ignition delay.
- **Diesel Index (DI)** uses **Aniline Point (°F) × API Gravity / 100**.
- **Calculated Cetane Index (CCI)** via ASTM D976 polynomial.
- **Pour Point, Cloud Point, CFPP** define cold-flow operability.
- **Flash Point > 52°C** is a *legal safety* requirement.
- **Carbon Residue (Ramsbottom / Conradson)** predicts injector deposits.
- **Viscosity 2–6 cSt at 40°C** is critical for injector atomization.

### Cross-Comparison (Always Asked):
- **ON and CN are INVERSELY related.**
- **Diesel is denser, more viscous, less volatile, and more energetic (per kg) than petrol.**
- **Petrol has higher RVP, lower flash point, and higher heat of vaporization than diesel.**

### Formula Sheet (At a Glance):
- $\text{AKI} = \dfrac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2}$
- $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{SG}} - 131.5$
- $\text{DI} = \dfrac{T_A(^{\circ}\text{F}) \times \text{°API}}{100}$
- $\text{CCI} = 0.49083 + 1.06577(\text{DI}) - 0.00106(\text{DI})^{2}$
- $T(^{\circ}\text{F}) = \dfrac{9}{5}T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 32$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:**
> When a Part B question involves numericals, always **show all four steps**: (1) unit conversion, (2) intermediate calculation, (3) final formula application, (4) interpretation of the numerical result. Examiners allocate marks for each step, and skipping step (1) is the most common reason for 2–3 mark deductions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuel Properties Affecting Engine Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Fuel Properties

The performance, efficiency, emissions, and reliability of internal combustion (IC) engines are intrinsically governed by the **thermo-physico-chemical properties** of the fuel inducted. Whether the engine is Spark Ignition (SI) or Compression Ignition (CI), every property of the fuel — from how easily it vaporizes to how much heat it liberates upon combustion — directly dictates the in-cylinder thermodynamics, the combustion phasing, and ultimately the indicated/brake work extracted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Fuel properties affecting engine performance refer to the measurable physical and chemical characteristics of a fuel that determine its suitability for a given combustion system, its energy release rate, ignition behavior, and its ability to convert chemical energy into useful mechanical work without abnormal combustion (knock, misfire, or excessive emissions).*

## 1.2 Classification of Fuel Properties

The properties are broadly classified into three engineering categories:

**A. Energy Properties** — Determine how much useful work a unit mass/volume of fuel can deliver.
- Calorific Value (Higher & Lower)
- Specific Energy
- Energy Density (volumetric)

**B. Combustion / Ignition Properties** — Determine how and when the fuel burns.
- Octane Number (RON, MON)
- Cetane Number
- Self-Ignition / Auto-ignition Temperature
- Flash Point & Fire Point
- Flame Speed / Laminar Burning Velocity

**C. Flow / Volatility Properties** — Determine how the fuel is metered, atomized, and mixed.
- Viscosity (kinematic, dynamic)
- Density
- Volatility (Reid Vapor Pressure, Distillation Curve)
- Cloud Point & Pour Point
- Surface Tension

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fuel as an Employee"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Think of fuel as an "employee" hired by the engine:**
> - **Calorific Value** = the employee's *productivity per hour*. A high-CV fuel is a hard-working fuel.
> - **Octane Number (SI)** = the employee's *patience under pressure*. A high-octane employee does not "panic and protest" (knock) when the boss (spark) is delayed.
> - **Cetane Number (CI)** = the employee's *responsiveness*. A high-cetane employee starts working immediately when pressure is applied, without waiting for a spark.
> - **Viscosity** = the employee's *discipline in following rules (flow channels)*. Too thick (high viscosity) → late arrival to combustion chamber; too thin → leaks past injectors.
> - **Volatility** = the employee's *ease of mixing*. Highly volatile fuels mix well with air (good atomization in SI engines).

## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU Exams

Fuel properties form the **conceptual backbone** of OEMUT615 because every subsequent module — gaseous fuels, biofuels, hydrogen, alcohols, and electric/hybrid systems — is benchmarked against these classical petroleum-fuel properties. KTU examiners frequently set **Part B (14-mark) derivation and comparison questions** on stoichiometric AFR, calorific value relations, and octane–cetane duality.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Energy Properties — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Calorific Value (CV) / Heating Value

**Definition:** The amount of heat liberated per unit mass (kJ/kg) or per unit volume (kJ/m³ or kJ/L) of fuel when it undergoes complete combustion with oxygen, with the products cooled back to reference conditions (**25 °C, 101.325 kPa**).

**Two Variants:**
- **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** — Combustion water is condensed to liquid; latent heat of condensation of water is recovered. Used in boilers/furnaces.
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** — Combustion water remains as vapor; latent heat is **not** recovered. Used in IC engines (since exhaust gases leave hot, with water as vapor).

**Why LHV matters in IC Engines:**
In an IC engine, exhaust gases exit at temperatures well above 100 °C. Hence, the latent heat of vaporization of the combustion water **escapes** with the exhaust. Engineers therefore use **LHV** for efficiency calculations:

$$
\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{\text{Work Output}}{\dot{m}_f \times \text{LHV}}
$$

### 2.1.2 Specific Energy & Energy Density

- **Specific Energy** (kJ/kg) → important for weight-sensitive applications (aerospace, automotive).
- **Energy Density** (kJ/L or MJ/m³) → important for volume-limited tanks (passenger cars).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hydrogen has the **highest specific energy (~142 MJ/kg)** but very **low energy density (~8.5 MJ/L at atmospheric pressure)**, which is why hydrogen vehicles require high-pressure tanks (350–700 bar) or cryogenic storage.

## 2.2 Combustion / Ignition Properties

### 2.2.1 Octane Number (ON) — SI Engines

**Definition:** A dimensionless numerical measure of a fuel's resistance to **auto-ignition (knocking)** in an SI engine under standardized CFR (Cooperative Fuel Research) engine test conditions.

- **RON (Research Octane Number)** — measured at 600 rpm, 100 °C intake air.
- **MON (Motor Octane Number)** — measured at 900 rpm, 148 °C intake air (more severe).
- **(R+M)/2** — Anti-Knock Index displayed at fuel pumps (US standard).

**Knock** is the unwanted auto-ignition of the end-gas ahead of the propagating flame front, caused by the auto-ignition temperature being reached before the spark-initiated flame arrives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane)** is assigned **ON = 100** (excellent knock resistance).
> **n-Heptane** is assigned **ON = 0** (poor knock resistance, knocks badly).

### 2.2.2 Cetane Number (CN) — CI Engines

**Definition:** A measure of the **ignition delay** between fuel injection and the start of combustion in a CI engine.

- **n-Cetane (n-hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄)** → CN = 100 (very short ignition delay).
- **Heptamethylnonane (HMN)** → CN = 15 (long ignition delay).

**Trade-off Principle:** Fuels that are good for SI engines (high ON) are *poor* for CI engines (low CN), and vice versa.

### 2.2.3 Self-Ignition Temperature (SIT) / Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)

The minimum temperature at which a fuel-air mixture ignites spontaneously without an external spark or flame. For CI engines, a low SIT is desirable (short ignition delay). For SI engines, high SIT is desirable (knock resistance).

### 2.2.4 Flash Point & Fire Point

- **Flash Point** — Lowest temperature at which fuel vapors form an ignitable mixture with air (a flash is observed but the fuel does **not** continue to burn).
- **Fire Point** — Lowest temperature at which fuel vapors burn continuously for ≥ 5 seconds after ignition.

**Critical for Safety & Classification:** Diesel (~52–96 °C flash point) is safer to handle than gasoline (~−40 °C).

### 2.2.5 Laminar Flame Speed / Burning Velocity

The velocity at which a flame front propagates through a quiescent fuel-air mixture. Affects combustion duration, pressure-rise rate, and engine smoothness. **Hydrogen has ~6–8× the flame speed of methane**, making it excellent for lean-burn engines but problematic for flashback.

## 2.3 Flow / Volatility Properties

### 2.3.1 Viscosity

Resistance of fuel to flow. **Too high** → poor atomization, large droplet size, incomplete combustion in CI engines. **Too low** → leakage past injector plungers, reduced lubrication.

### 2.3.2 Density

Mass per unit volume. Directly affects the mass of fuel injected per cycle in CI engines (since injectors meter by **volume**, not mass).

### 2.3.3 Cloud Point & Pour Point

- **Cloud Point** — Temperature at which wax crystals first appear when cooling the fuel. Relevant for diesel in cold climates.
- **Pour Point** — Lowest temperature at which the fuel will still flow.

### 2.3.4 Volatility (Reid Vapor Pressure)

Measure of a fuel's tendency to vaporize. Critical for **cold-start** in SI engines and for **vapor lock** prevention.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All KTU-board-essential formulas for Module 1 are consolidated below. Memorize these for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) derivations.**

| # | Property / Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric AFR (by mass) for C$_x$H$_y$ O$_z$ N$_w$ S$_v$ | $A\!F_{stoich} = \dfrac{8(4x+y-2z+2v)}{12x+y+16z+14w+32v}$ | kg air/kg fuel |
| 2 | Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline (C₈H₁₈ approx.) | $\approx 15.13$ | kg/kg |
| 3 | Stoichiometric AFR for diesel (C₁₂H₂₆ approx.) | $\approx 14.7$ | kg/kg |
| 4 | Relation: HHV − LHV | $\Delta H = m_{w} \cdot h_{fg}$ at 25 °C; $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg | per kg of fuel water |
| 5 | Mass of water from combustion | $m_{w} = \dfrac{9y}{12x+y+16z+14w+32v}$ | kg water/kg fuel |
| 6 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{W_{brake}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 7 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{W_{brake}} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{bth} \cdot \text{LHV}}$ | kg/kW·s or kg/kWh |
| 8 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{W_{indicated}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_{a,actual}}{m_{a,ideal}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Air-Fuel Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(A\!F)_{stoich}}{(A\!F)_{actual}}$; $\phi<1$ lean, $\phi>1$ rich | dimensionless |
| 11 | Relative Air-Fuel Ratio (λ) | $\lambda = 1/\phi$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Energy Density (volumetric) | $E_v = \rho_f \cdot \text{LHV}$ | MJ/L |
| 13 | Specific Energy | $E_m = \text{LHV}$ | MJ/kg |
| 14 | Combustion Air Requirement | $m_a = (A\!F)_{stoich} \cdot m_f \cdot \phi^{-1}$ | kg/s |
| 15 | Knocking tendency vs. Octane | $\text{Knock} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{ON}}$ for given compression ratio | qualitative |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the stoichiometric coefficients for N and S. Recall: nitrogen in fuel does **not** combust (it passes through as N₂), while sulphur requires **one oxygen atom per S atom** to form SO₂.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Real-World Application

Fuel property analysis is used in:

1. **Fuel Refining & Blending** — Refineries blend multiple hydrocarbon streams to meet ON/CN, volatility, and sulfur specifications (e.g., Euro-VI diesel: < 10 ppm sulfur).
2. **Engine Calibration** — Modern ECUs use **closed-loop lambda control** based on AFR feedback; pre-calibration requires accurate knowledge of stoichiometric AFR and LHV.
3. **Alternate Fuel Drop-In** — Biodiesel (FAME), ethanol, CNG, LPG, and hydrogen are always benchmarked against gasoline/diesel using these same property indices.
4. **Emissions Modelling** — Carbon residue → particulate emissions; sulfur → SO₂ and PM; aromatic content → soot and unburned HC.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for C$_x$H$_y$O$_z$N$_w$S$_v$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **highly recurrent in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions**. Practice until you can write it from memory in under 5 minutes.

### Step 1: Write the generalized combustion equation

For complete stoichiometric combustion of 1 kmol of fuel C$_x$H$_y$O$_z$N$_w$S$_v$:

$$
\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z\text{N}_w\text{S}_v + a\,\text{O}_2 + b\,\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{w}{2}\,\text{N}_2 + v\,\text{SO}_2
$$

Air is supplied with its standard composition: $\dfrac{\text{O}_2}{\text{N}_2} = \dfrac{21}{79}$, hence $b = \dfrac{79}{21}a \approx 3.76\,a$.

### Step 2: Oxygen balance

Oxygen supplied by air must equal oxygen demanded by the fuel reaction:

$$
2a = 2x + \tfrac{y}{2} - z + 2v
$$

(Note: Oxygen in fuel is *subtracted* from demand; nitrogen does not consume oxygen; sulfur consumes one O₂ per S atom to yield SO₂.)

### Step 3: Solve for oxygen coefficient *a*

$$
a = x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2} + v
$$

### Step 4: Calculate masses per kmol of fuel

- Molecular mass of fuel: $M_f = 12x + y + 16z + 14w + 32v$
- Mass of air supplied (per kmol of fuel): $m_a = 32a + 28b = 32a + 28(3.76a) = a(32 + 105.28) = 137.28\,a$

Equivalently, air mass in terms of O₂:

$$
m_a = \dfrac{100}{21} \cdot 32\,a = 137.28\,a \quad \text{[kg air per kmol fuel]}
$$

### Step 5: Form the air-fuel ratio (mass basis)

$$
A\!F_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_a}{m_f} = \dfrac{137.28\,a}{12x + y + 16z + 14w + 32v}
$$

Substituting $a$:

$$
A\!F_{stoich} = \dfrac{137.28\!\left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2} + v\right)}{12x + y + 16z + 14w + 32v}
$$

### Step 6: Equivalent standard-form expression

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 4 to clear the fraction:

$$
\boxed{\;A\!F_{stoich} = \dfrac{34.32\,(4x + y - 2z + 4v)}{12x + y + 16z + 14w + 32v}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU boards often quote the **mass-based** version: $A\!F_{stoich} = \dfrac{8(4x + y - 2z + 2v)}{12x + y + 16z + 14w + 32v}$. This is the same equation written as $(137.28/17.16)\!\approx\!8.0$ factor — verify both forms appear in your KTU model-answer keys.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — AFR for Iso-octane C₈H₁₈

**Given:** Fuel is iso-octane, $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$ (i.e., $x=8$, $y=18$, $z=w=v=0$).

**Step (i):** Calculate molecular mass of fuel.
$$
M_f = 12(8) + 18 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 96 + 18 = 114 \text{ kg/kmol}
$$

**Step (ii):** Calculate oxygen requirement.
$$
a = x + \dfrac{y}{4} = 8 + \dfrac{18}{4} = 8 + 4.5 = 12.5 \text{ kmol O}_2
$$

**Step (iii):** Calculate air required.
$$
m_a = 137.28 \times 12.5 = 1716 \text{ kg air / kmol fuel}
$$

**Step (iv):** Compute AFR.
$$
A\!F_{stoich} = \dfrac{1716}{114} = 15.05 \;\; \dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}
$$

**Step (v):** Cross-check with standard value.
Iso-octane stoichiometric AFR ≈ **15.13** kg/kg (handbook), and our calculated 15.05 matches within rounding tolerance. ✅

## 3.3 Derivation — Relation between HHV and LHV

### Step 1: Define each
- **HHV** = heat released when combustion water is condensed to liquid at 25 °C.
- **LHV** = heat released when combustion water leaves as vapor at 25 °C.

### Step 2: Energy balance
The difference is exactly the latent heat of vaporization of water **not recovered**:

$$
\text{HHV} - \text{LHV} = m_{w} \cdot h_{fg,\,H_2O}
$$

where $h_{fg,\,H_2O} \approx 2442$ kJ/kg at 25 °C.

### Step 3: Compute mass of water from 1 kg of fuel C$_x$H$_y$
From stoichiometry, each kmol of fuel produces $\tfrac{y}{2}$ kmol of H₂O. Mass of water per kmol fuel = $9y$. Mass of water per kg fuel:

$$
m_w = \dfrac{9y}{12x + y + 16z + 14w + 32v} \quad [\text{kg water/kg fuel}]
$$

### Step 4: Final closed-form relation

$$
\boxed{\;\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - 2442 \cdot \dfrac{9y}{12x + y + 16z + 14w + 32v}\;}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — HHV–LHV for Methane (CH₄)

**Given:** Methane, $x=1, y=4$.
**Standard data:** HHV of CH₄ = **55.5 MJ/kg**.

**Step (i):** $M_f = 12(1)+4 = 16$ kg/kmol.
**Step (ii):** $m_w = \dfrac{9(4)}{16} = \dfrac{36}{16} = 2.25$ kg water/kg fuel.
**Step (iii):** $\text{LHV} = 55.5 - (2442 \times 10^{-3})(2.25) = 55.5 - 5.495 = 50.005$ MJ/kg.

**Verification:** Standard LHV of CH₄ ≈ **50.0 MJ/kg**. ✅

## 3.5 Derivation — BSFC in terms of LHV and η

Starting from the definition of brake thermal efficiency:

$$
\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{W_b}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}}
$$

Rearranging for $\dot{m}_f$:

$$
\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{W_b}{\eta_{bth} \cdot \text{LHV}}
$$

Dividing by $W_b$:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{W_b} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{bth} \cdot \text{LHV}}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** BSFC is *inversely* proportional to LHV. A fuel with lower LHV (e.g., ethanol at 26.8 MJ/kg vs. gasoline at 44 MJ/kg) demands **higher mass flow** for the same power output — exactly why flex-fuel vehicles consume noticeably more liters per kilometer on E85.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Property on Engine Performance

| Fuel Property | SI Engine Effect | CI Engine Effect | Emission / Wear Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Calorific Value | ↓ BSFC | ↓ BSFC | Generally favorable |
| ↑ Octane Number | ↑ Knock resistance, ↑ CR possible | — (less relevant) | ↓ Unburned HC |
| ↑ Cetane Number | — (less relevant) | ↓ Ignition delay, smoother start | ↓ Smoke, ↓ Noise |
| ↑ Volatility | Better cold start; vapor-lock risk | — (atomization by injector) | ↓ HC emissions |
| ↑ Viscosity | — | Poor atomization if too high | ↑ Smoke, ↑ PM |
| ↑ Density | — | Larger mass injected per cycle | Affects injector calibration |
| ↑ Sulphur | ↑ Corrosion | ↑ Lubricity loss | ↑ SO₂, ↑ PM |
| ↑ Aromatic content | ↑ ON (aromatics boost octane) | ↓ CN (aromatics lower cetane) | ↑ Soot (PM) |
| ↑ Latent heat of vaporization | Charge-cooling, knock resistance | — | ↓ NO$_x$ (lower peak T) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Calorific Value of a fuel. Differentiate between Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV) with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Calorific Value* of a fuel is the amount of heat liberated per unit mass (kJ/kg) upon complete combustion with oxygen, with products cooled to the reference state of **25 °C and 101.325 kPa**.

- **HHV (Gross CV):** Combustion water is condensed to liquid; latent heat of condensation is recovered. Used in boiler/furnace applications. *Example:* Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) HHV ≈ 50 MJ/kg.
- **LHV (Net CV):** Combustion water remains as vapour; latent heat is *not* recovered. Used in IC engine calculations, since exhaust leaves hot. *Example:* Methane LHV ≈ 50 MJ/kg vs. HHV ≈ 55.5 MJ/kg.

> *(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if the student clearly mentions the 25 °C reference and the latent-heat reasoning.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the significance of Octane Number and Cetane Number. Why are they inversely related for a given fuel?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Octane Number (ON)** quantifies a fuel's resistance to *knocking* in Spark Ignition engines; measured against a blend of iso-octane (ON=100) and n-heptane (ON=0).
- **Cetane Number (CN)** quantifies a fuel's *ignition quality* (short ignition delay) in Compression Ignition engines; measured against a blend of n-cetane (CN=100) and HMN (CN=15).

**Inverse Relation:** Fuels with long, straight-chain paraffinic structures (e.g., n-hexadecane) have *low* thermal stability ahead of flame, hence high CN but low ON. Conversely, branched/iso-paraffins and aromatics (high ON) resist pre-flame auto-ignition, giving them very long ignition delay → low CN. Hence ON and CN are inversely correlated across hydrocarbon families.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Most students lose 3–4 marks in the Part B derivation on AFR for failing to:
> (1) Show the air-supplied-by-N₂ step explicitly ($b = 3.76a$),
> (2) Convert mass-based ratios correctly,
> (3) Substitute units with units (kg/kmol), and
> (4) State the assumption of "complete stoichiometric combustion at 25 °C, 101.325 kPa".
> Memorize the boxed equation from Section 3.1 and present it with every intermediate step — this alone can flip a 7/14 into 11/14.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Module 1 Choice A]**

**(a)** Derive the stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (by mass) for a hydrocarbon fuel having the molecular formula C$_x$H$_y$O$_z$N$_w$S$_v$. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuel with molecular formula C$_7$H$_{16}$ is burned with 30 % excess air. Calculate:
1. Air-Fuel ratio (mass basis),
2. Air-Fuel ratio (mole basis),
3. Mass of dry flue gases per kg of fuel,
4. Mass of water vapour formed per kg of fuel. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Combustion is complete (no CO, no unburned HC).
2. Air composition is 21 % O₂ and 79 % N₂ by volume (mole basis).
3. Nitrogen in fuel does not react.
4. Reference temperature 25 °C, pressure 101.325 kPa.
5. All products are ideal gases.

**Step 2: Combustion equation (2 marks):**
$$
