\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z\text{N}_w\text{S}_v + a\!\left(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2\right) \to x\,\text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + \left(\tfrac{w}{2}+3.76a\right)\text{N}_2 + v\,\text{SO}_2
$$

**Step 3: Oxygen balance (1 mark):**
$$
2a = 2x + \tfrac{y}{2} - z + 2v \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a = x + \tfrac{y}{4} - \tfrac{z}{2} + v
$$

**Step 4: Mass of air per kmol fuel (1 mark):**
$$
m_a = 32a + 28(3.76a) = 137.28\,a \;\text{ kg/kmol}
$$

**Step 5: Mass-based AFR (2 marks):**
$$
\boxed{\;A\!F_{stoich} = \dfrac{137.28\!\left(x + \tfrac{y}{4} - \tfrac{z}{2} + v\right)}{12x + y + 16z + 14w + 32v}\;}
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** C$_7$H$_{16}$ (n-heptane), 30 % excess air.

**Step (i):** Molecular mass.
$$
M_f = 12(7) + 16 = 84 + 16 = 100 \text{ kg/kmol}
$$

**Step (ii):** Stoichiometric O₂.
$$
a_{stoich} = 7 + \tfrac{16}{4} = 7 + 4 = 11 \text{ kmol O}_2 / \text{kmol fuel}
$$

**Step (iii):** Air supplied (30 % excess → 1.3 × stoichiometric).
$$
a_{actual} = 1.3 \times 11 = 14.3 \text{ kmol O}_2
$$
$$
m_{a,actual} = 137.28 \times 14.3 = 1963.1 \text{ kg air/kmol fuel}
$$

**Sub-part (1) — AFR (mass basis):**
$$
A\!F_{actual} = \dfrac{1963.1}{100} = \mathbf{19.63 \;\dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}}
$$

**Sub-part (2) — AFR (mole basis):**
Total moles of air per kmol fuel = 14.3(1 + 3.76) = 14.3 × 4.76 = 68.07 kmol.
$$
A\!F_{mole} = \dfrac{68.07}{1} = \mathbf{68.07 \;\dfrac{\text{kmol air}}{\text{kmol fuel}}}
$$

**Sub-part (3) — Dry flue gas composition (per kmol fuel):**
- CO₂ : 7 kmol
- SO₂ : 0
- N₂ : (16/2) + 3.76(14.3) = 8 + 53.77 = 61.77 kmol
- Excess O₂ : (14.3 − 11) = 3.3 kmol

Total dry flue gas = 7 + 61.77 + 3.3 = **72.07 kmol**.

Mass:
$$
m_{dry} = 7(44) + 61.77(28) + 3.3(32)
$$
$$
= 308 + 1729.5 + 105.6 = \mathbf{2143.1 \text{ kg dry flue gas per kmol fuel}}
$$

Per kg of fuel:
$$
= 21.43 \;\mathbf{\dfrac{\text{kg dry flue gas}}{\text{kg fuel}}}
$$

**Sub-part (4) — Water vapour:**
$$
m_{H_2O} = \tfrac{16}{2} \times 18 = 8 \times 18 = 144 \text{ kg/kmol fuel} = \mathbf{1.44 \;\dfrac{\text{kg H}_2\text{O}}{\text{kg fuel}}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Module 1 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain any **eight** important properties of SI-engine fuels and their effect on engine performance, emissions, and safety. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gasoline engine operates at an indicated thermal efficiency of 30 % on gasoline having LHV = 44 MJ/kg. The engine is tested to deliver 50 kW brake power at a fuel consumption rate of 12 kg/h. Calculate:
1. The brake thermal efficiency.
2. The mechanical efficiency.
3. The BSFC in kg/kWh. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Eight SI Fuel Properties (7 marks = 0.875 per property, 8 properties)

| # | Property | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Octane Number** | High ON → resists knock → permits higher compression ratio → ↑ efficiency |
| 2 | **Volatility / RVP** | High volatility → good cold start; too high → vapor lock |
| 3 | **Calorific Value** | Higher LHV → lower BSFC, greater range |
| 4 | **Density** | Affects energy density (MJ/L); injector calibration |
| 5 | **Viscosity** | Affects fuel pump and injector spray pattern |
| 6 | **Flash Point** | Safety indicator; low flash point = fire hazard |
| 7 | **Sulphur Content** | ↑ SO₂ and acid corrosion; poisons catalytic converter |
| 8 | **Aromatic Content** | ↑ ON but ↑ soot and toxic emissions (benzene is carcinogenic) |

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $\eta_{ith} = 30 \% = 0.30$
- LHV = 44 MJ/kg = 44 000 kJ/kg
- $W_b = 50$ kW = 50 kJ/s
- $\dot{m}_f = 12$ kg/h = $12/3600 = 0.00333$ kg/s

**Sub-part (1) — Brake thermal efficiency:**
$$
\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{W_b}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}} = \dfrac{50}{0.00333 \times 44\,000} = \dfrac{50}{146.67}
$$
$$
\boxed{\;\eta_{bth} = 0.341 = \mathbf{34.1\%}\;}
$$

**Sub-part (2) — Mechanical efficiency:**
$$
\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{\eta_{bth}}{\eta_{ith}} = \dfrac{0.341}{0.30} = \mathbf{1.137 \;\; (or \;113.7\%)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Trap:** A value of $\eta_{mech} > 1$ is *physically impossible* and indicates that the indicated efficiency is **under-reported** or fuel LHV has been mis-stated. In a properly designed engine, $\eta_{bth} < \eta_{ith}$ and $\eta_{mech} < 1$. Award 1 mark for correctly identifying the inconsistency if it occurs.

**Sub-part (3) — BSFC in kg/kWh:**
$$
\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{W_b} = \dfrac{12 \text{ kg/h}}{50 \text{ kW}} = \mathbf{0.24 \;\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{kWh}}}
$$

*Cross-check:* $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{bth} \cdot \text{LHV}} = \dfrac{1}{0.341 \times 44\,000} = 6.67 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/kJ = $6.67 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600$ = **0.240 kg/kWh** ✅.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for Fuel Properties Affecting Engine Performance:**

- **Calorific Value** is *always* quoted at 25 °C, 101.325 kPa; HHV > LHV by $m_w \times 2442$ kJ/kg.
- IC engines use **LHV** for efficiency calculations; boilers use **HHV**.
- **Octane Number** → SI engine knock resistance; **Cetane Number** → CI engine ignition delay.
- **RON, MON, (R+M)/2** are the three reported octane metrics; MON < RON typically.
- Trade-off rule: *aromatics & iso-paraffins → high ON, low CN*; *n-paraffins → low ON, high CN*.
- **Stoichiometric AFR** is derived from the **O₂ balance** with $b = 3.76a$ for air.
- The **AFR equation** to memorize: $A\!F_{stoich} = \dfrac{137.28(x + y/4 - z/2 + v)}{12x + y + 16z + 14w + 32v}$.
- **BSFC = 1 / (η_bth × LHV)** — a direct inverse proportionality.
- **Equivalence ratio** $\phi = (A\!F)_{stoich}/(A\!F)_{actual}$; **relative AFR** $\lambda = 1/\phi$.
- **Viscosity** optimum: ~1–4 mm²/s for diesel (at 40 °C); too high → poor atomization → smoke.
- **Flash point** of diesel (52–96 °C) >> gasoline (−40 °C) → diesel is safer to handle.
- **Cloud & Pour Points** matter for cold-climate diesel; wax crystallization can plug fuel filters.
- **Latent heat of vaporization** of alcohol fuels → charge cooling effect → reduces knock tendency AND reduces volumetric efficiency.
- **Hydrogen flame speed** ~ 6× methane → excellent for lean-burn SI engines; **flashback** risk in pre-mixed systems.
- **Sulphur content**: ≤ 10 ppm mandated by **BS-VI / Euro-VI** norms; lubricity additives required after desulfurization.
- **Energy density** vs. **Specific energy** distinction: gasoline ≈ 32 MJ/L vs. hydrogen ≈ 8.5 MJ/L @ 1 atm — critical for storage sizing.
- Remember that **nitrogen in fuel** does *not* consume oxygen (passes as N₂); **sulphur** consumes *one O₂ per S* to form SO₂.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emissions - Main Pollutants & Their Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Emissions - Main Pollutants & Their Control # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Engine Emissions? **Emissions** are the unwanted by-products of the combustion process inside an Internal Combustion (IC) engine that are released into the atmosphere through the exhaust manifold. They consist of gases, vapours, and particulate matter generated due to **incomplete combustion**, **high in-cylinder temperatures**, and **fuel composition impurities**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > Emissions are the chemical species produced as a result of combustion of fuel-air mixture in IC engines, which are either toxic to human health, contribute to environmental degradation (greenhouse effect, acid rain, smog), or are regulated by statutory emission norms (e.g., **Bharat Stage VI / Euro VI**). ## 1.2 The Six Major Regulated Pollutants | S.No. | Pollutant | Symbol | Primary Source | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Carbon Monoxide | **CO** | Rich mixture, incomplete combustion | | 2 | Unburnt Hydrocarbons | **HC / UHC** | Quench layer, crevice volumes | | 3 | Oxides of Nitrogen | **NOx** | High peak temperatures | | 4 | Particulate Matter | **PM** | Incomplete diesel combustion, soot | | 5 | Carbon Dioxide | **CO2** | Complete combustion (greenhouse gas) | | 6 | Sulfur Dioxide | **SO2** | Sulfur in fuel | ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy (The Human Body Engine) Think of an IC engine exactly like the **human respiratory system**: - A person eats food (the engine takes in **fuel + air**) - The body burns food using oxygen to produce energy (the engine performs **combustion**) - The body exhales **CO2, water vapour, and trace toxins** (the engine expels **exhaust gases**) - If the person eats poorly, smokes, or has asthma → they produce **harmful residues** (just like a poorly tuned engine emits **CO, HC, soot**) - Polluted lungs are like a **dirty catalytic converter** — they cannot filter harmful by-products efficiently > [!NOTE] > A perfectly "clean" engine (100% pure combustion) would ideally produce only **CO2 + H2O + N2**. Every additional substance in the exhaust is a pollutant arising from imperfections in the combustion process. ## 1.4 Why Study Emission Control? 1. **Health impact** — CO is colourless and odourless but lethal (binds haemoglobin 200× more strongly than O2). 2. **Environmental impact** — CO2 and CH4 cause global warming; NOx and SOx cause acid rain. 3. **Legal compliance** — Vehicles in India must comply with **BS-VI norms** (equivalent to Euro VI) as of April 2020. 4. **Fuel economy optimisation** — Emission control systems are tightly coupled with engine management. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Statutory Limit under BS-VI (Petrol, 4-wheelers):** > CO: **1.0 g/km**, HC+NOx: **0.10 g/km**, NOx: **0.06 g/km**, PM: **0.0045 g/km** (direct injection). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Pollutant-Wise Formation Mechanism ### 2.1.1 Carbon Monoxide (CO) - **Why formed:** Incomplete oxidation of carbon in fuel due to **lack of oxygen** locally. - **Conditions:** Rich mixtures (φ > 1, where φ is equivalence ratio), low in-cylinder temperatures, poor mixing, and **quenching** near cold cylinder walls. - **Master reaction:** 2C + O2 → 2CO (instead of the desired C + O2 → CO2). - **Effect:** Reduces blood's oxygen-carrying capacity → **hypoxia**, headaches, death at concentrations > 1000 ppm. > **Equivalence Ratio Definition:** > $$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{stoichiometric}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}$$ > > - φ < 1 → **lean mixture** (excess air) > - φ = 1 → **stoichiometric** > - φ > 1 → **rich mixture** (excess fuel, high CO) ### 2.1.2 Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC) - **Why formed:** Fuel that escapes the flame zone unburned. - **Sources:** 1. **Crevice volumes** — fuel trapped in piston ring grooves and head-gasket region 2. **Wall quenching** — flame extinguishes within ~0.1 mm of cold walls 3. **Oil layer absorption/desorption** — fuel dissolves in lubricating oil film 4. **Fuel leakage** past exhaust valves - **Effect:** Smog formation (photochemical smog), carcinogenic (especially **benzene** and **PAHs**). ### 2.1.3 Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx) - **Why formed:** High peak temperatures (>1800 K) cause **dissociation of N2** in air. - **Three reactions** (Zeldovich Mechanism): 1. O + N2 → NO + N 2. N + O2 → NO + O 3. N + OH → NO + H - **Strongly dependent on:** Peak temperature, O2 concentration, and residence time at high T. - **Effect:** Acid rain, ground-level ozone, respiratory ailments. > [!NOTE] > The **NOx–PM trade-off** is the central engineering challenge in diesel engines: lowering in-cylinder temperature reduces NOx but increases PM, and vice versa. ### 2.1.4 Particulate Matter (PM) - **Composition:** Solid carbonaceous soot (60–80%) + adsorbed hydrocarbons (SOF) + sulphates + ash. - **Why formed in diesels:** Heterogeneous combustion in fuel-rich regions of the spray. - **Effect:** Lung cancer, cardiovascular disease, visibility reduction (haze). - **PM10** (≤10 μm) and **PM2.5** (≤2.5 μm) are the regulated fractions; finer particles penetrate deeper. ### 2.1.5 Carbon Dioxide (CO2) - **Why formed:** Complete combustion product. Stoichiometric for CxHy: 1 mole of fuel → x moles of CO2. - **Effect:** Primary **greenhouse gas** (GWP = 1). Not directly toxic but drives climate change. - **Reduction strategies:** Improved thermal efficiency, alternative fuels (CNG, H2, ethanol, biodiesel), hybridisation. ### 2.1.6 Sulfur Dioxide (SO2) - **Why formed:** Combustion of sulfur impurities in diesel/petrol. - **Reaction:** S + O2 → SO2 → SO3 → H2SO4 (acid rain). - **Control:** Low-sulfur fuels (<10 ppm under BS-VI), exhaust after-treatment. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless | | 2 | $\text{EI}_i = \frac{\dot{m}_i}{\dot{m}_f}$ | Emission Index of pollutant *i* | **g/kg of fuel** | | 3 | $\text{BSEC}_i = \frac{\dot{m}_i}{P_b}$ | Brake-Specific Emission of *i* | **g/kWh** | | 4 | $C_{\text{mass}} = C_{\text{ppm}} \cdot \frac{M_i}{V_m}$ | ppm to mg/m³ conversion | mg/m³ | | 5 | $V_m = 22.414 \cdot \frac{T}{273.15}$ | Molar volume at temperature T | L/mol | | 6 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{P_b}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}}$ | Brake thermal efficiency | dimensionless | | 7 | $\dot{m}_i = \text{EI}_i \cdot \dot{m}_f$ | Mass flow rate of pollutant | g/s | | 8 | $\text{CO}_2 = \frac{44}{12} \cdot m_c$ | CO2 produced from carbon | g/g of C | | 9 | $\dot{m}_{f,\text{stoich}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{A/F_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Stoichiometric fuel rate | kg/s | | 10 | $\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f \cdot 3600}{P_b}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | **kg/kWh** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Molar Volume** at **STP (0°C, 1 atm)** = **22.414 L/mol**. > At **NTP (25°C, 1 atm)** = **24.45 L/mol** — always check which reference state the problem uses. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - Emission indices (EI) form the **basis of every certification test** under the **Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC)** and **Worldwide harmonised Light vehicles Test Cycle (WLTC)**. - Brake-specific emissions (g/kWh) are used for **heavy-duty diesel engines** certified on the **ESC (European Stationary Cycle)** and **ETC (European Transient Cycle)**. - The trade-off between NOx and PM is the reason **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) with AdBlue/DEF** and **DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter)** are simultaneously fitted in modern BS-VI trucks. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion of Volumetric Concentration (ppm) to Mass Concentration (mg/m³) The exhaust analyser displays pollutant concentration in **ppm by volume** (parts per million), but legal limits are written in **mg/m³**. Engineers must convert between them. **Step 1 — Definition of ppm by volume:** $$C_{\text{ppm}} = \frac{V_i}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 10^6$$ Where: - $V_i$ = partial volume of pollutant *i* at the given temperature and pressure - $V_{\text{total}}$ = total volume of exhaust gas **Step 2 — Apply ideal gas law to one mole of mixture:** At temperature T (K) and pressure P, the molar volume is: $$V_m = \frac{R T}{P}$$ At standard atmospheric pressure and temperature T, $V_m = 22.414 \times \dfrac{T}{273.15}$ L/mol. **Step 3 — Express volume in terms of moles:** $$C_{\text{ppm}} = \frac{n_i}{n_{\text{total}}} \times 10^6$$ Since $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$ (mass ÷ molecular weight): $$\frac{n_i}{n_{\text{total}}} = \frac{m_i / M_i}{V_{\text{total}} / V_m} = \frac{m_i \cdot V_m}{V_{\text{total}} \cdot M_i}$$ **Step 4 — Mass concentration in g/m³:** $$C_{\text{mass}} = \frac{m_i}{V_{\text{total}}} = C_{\text{ppm}} \cdot \frac{M_i}{V_m} \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10^6 \;\Rightarrow\; C_{\text{mass}}\;[\text{g/m}^3] = \frac{C_{\text{ppm}} \cdot M_i}{V_m}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to mg/m³:** $$C_{\text{mass}}\;[\text{mg/m}^3] = C_{\text{ppm}} \cdot \frac{M_i}{V_m} \cdot 1000$$ ### 3.1.1 Numerical Example (Worked) **Problem:** An exhaust analyser reports **NOx = 200 ppm** at 25°C. Compute the mass concentration in mg/m³ (treat NOx as NO2, M = 46 g/mol). **Step 1 — Molar volume at 25°C:** $$V_m = 22.414 \times \frac{298.15}{273.15} = 24.466 \text{ L/mol}$$ **Step 2 — Apply conversion:** $$C_{\text{mass}} = 200 \times \frac{46}{24.466} = 200 \times 1.880 = 376.0 \;\text{mg/m}^3$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **molecular weight** of the species determines the conversion factor: > - CO (M = 28): 1 ppm = **1.145 mg/m³** at 25°C > - NO (M = 30): 1 ppm = **1.227 mg/m³** at 25°C > - NO2 (M = 46): 1 ppm = **1.880 mg/m³** at 25°C > - SO2 (M = 64): 1 ppm = **2.616 mg/m³** at 25°C > - HC as CH4 (M = 16): 1 ppm = **0.654 mg/m³** at 25°C ## 3.2 Derivation: Emission Index from Fuel Composition Consider a generic hydrocarbon fuel $C_x H_y$ burning in air. With **equivalence ratio φ** and **mass of fuel burnt $m_f$**, derive the CO2 emission index. **Step 1 — Stoichiometric combustion equation:** $$C_x H_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \left(O_2 + 3.76 N_2\right) \rightarrow x\, CO_2 + \frac{y}{2} H_2 O + 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) N_2$$ **Step 2 — Molecular mass of fuel:** $$M_f = 12x + y \;\;\text{g/mol}$$ **Step 3 — Moles of CO2 produced per mole of fuel = x** (regardless of φ, assuming complete combustion of C): $$\text{Mass of CO}_2 = 44x \;\text{g per mole of fuel}$$ **Step 4 — Emission index (mass of CO2 per mass of fuel):** $$\text{EI}_{CO_2} = \frac{44x}{12x + y}\;\;\text{kg CO}_2 \;\big/\;\text{kg fuel}$$ ### 3.2.1 Numerical Example **Fuel:** Octane C8H18; x = 8, y = 18. **Step 1 — Compute numerator:** $44 \times 8 = 352$. **Step 2 — Compute denominator:** $12 \times 8 + 18 = 96 + 18 = 114$. **Step 3 — Emission index:** $$\text{EI}_{CO_2} = \frac{352}{114} = 3.088\;\text{kg CO}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$ > [!NOTE] > This shows why **petrol vehicles produce ~3.08 kg CO2 per kg of fuel burnt** — a value used in **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** analyses for comparing alternative fuels. ## 3.3 Derivation: Brake-Specific Emission from Emission Index Given: - Fuel mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_f$ in **kg/h** - Brake power: $P_b$ in **kW** - Emission index: $\text{EI}_i$ in **g/kg of fuel** **Step 1 — Mass flow rate of pollutant:** $$\dot{m}_i = \text{EI}_i \cdot \dot{m}_f \;\;\text{g/h}$$ **Step 2 — Energy output per hour:** $$E_b = P_b \cdot 1\text{ h} = P_b \;\text{kWh}$$ **Step 3 — Brake-specific emission:** $$\text{BSEC}_i = \frac{\dot{m}_i}{P_b} = \frac{\text{EI}_i \cdot \dot{m}_f}{P_b}\;\;\text{g/kWh}$$ ### 3.3.1 Numerical Example **Given:** $\dot{m}_f = 12$ kg/h, $P_b = 50$ kW, $\text{EI}_{CO} = 20$ g/kg. **Step 1 — Mass of CO per hour:** $$\dot{m}_{CO} = 20 \times 12 = 240\;\text{g/h}$$ **Step 2 — BSEC:** $$\text{BSEC}_{CO} = \frac{240}{50} = 4.8\;\text{g/kWh}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cause-Effect-Control of Pollutants | Pollutant | Primary Cause | Best Control Strategy | Reduction Potential | |---|---|---|---| | CO | Rich mixture, low T | Oxidation catalyst + closed-loop lambda control | > 90% | | HC | Quench, crevice, oil absorption | Oxidation catalyst + PCV + better combustion | 70–95% | | NOx | High peak T, O2 availability | EGR, SCR, LNT, retarded timing | 50–95% | | PM (Diesel) | Rich diffusion flame zones | DPF, oxidation catalyst, high EGR | 80–99% | | CO2 | Complete combustion | Better efficiency, alt fuels, hybridisation | 5–40% | | SO2 | Sulfur in fuel | Low-S fuel (≤10 ppm), desulfurisation | > 95% | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Emission Formation and Control Loop (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fresh Charge
Air plus Fuel] --> B[Combustion Chamber
High T High P] B --> C1[CO Formation
Rich zones low O2] B --> C2[HC Formation
Quench layer crevices] B --> C3[NOx Formation
Zeldovich at T greater than 1800 K] B --> C4[PM Formation
Diffusion flame soot] B --> C5[CO2 Formation
Complete combustion] B --> D[Raw Exhaust Gas] D --> E1[Three Way Catalyst
CO and HC and NOx] D --> E2[Diesel Oxidation Catalyst DOC
CO and HC and SOF] D --> E3[Diesel Particulate Filter DPF
PM trapping] D --> E4[SCR with AdBlue DEF
NOx reduction] D --> E5[EGR Valve
Recirculate inert gas] E1 --> F[Clean Exhaust] E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F E5 --> B ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Emission Control Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1[Engine Subsystem] A1[Fuel Injection ECU] --> A2[Combustion] A2 --> A3[Exhaust Manifold] end subgraph S2[Sensors] B1[Lambda O2 Sensor] --> A1 B2[NOx Sensor] --> A1 B3[PM Sensor] --> A1 B4[Temp and Pressure] --> A1 end subgraph S3[After Treatment] C1[Three Way Catalyst] --> C2[SCR Unit] C2 --> C3[DPF Unit] C3 --> C4[Tailpipe] end A3 --> C1 B1 -.feedback.-> A1 B2 -.feedback.-> A1 B3 -.feedback.-> A1 ``` ## 4.3 Engine-Cycle Mapping of Pollutant Formation (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Intake Stroke] --> P2[Compression Stroke] P2 --> P3[Combustion Power Stroke] P3 --> P4[Exhaust Stroke] P3 -->|Peak T at 20 to 40 deg aTDC| Q1[NOx Forms] P3 -->|Local rich pockets| Q2[CO and PM Forms] P3 -->|Near wall quench| Q3[HC Forms] P4 -->|Residual gases| R1[HC and CO Released] P1 -->|Oil and fuel vapours| R2[Crankcase Blowby HC] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** > - **Rectangular blocks** = physical/engine components. > - **Arrow direction** = direction of flow or feedback. > - **Dotted feedback arrows** in the functional architecture = sensor-to-ECU control loop (the heart of a modern closed-loop emission control system). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Emission Index. Mention its units. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Emission Index (EI)** of a pollutant is defined as the **mass of that pollutant emitted per unit mass of fuel burnt** in the engine. $$\text{EI}_i = \frac{\dot{m}_i}{\dot{m}_f}$$ - $\dot{m}_i$ = mass flow rate of pollutant *i* (g/h or kg/s) - $\dot{m}_f$ = mass flow rate of fuel (kg/h or kg/s) - **Units:** g/kg of fuel (SI) or g/kWh (when normalised to work output as BSEC). It is a useful metric because it is **independent of engine size and load**, enabling fair comparison between two different engines. --- ### Question 2: List any four major pollutants from SI engines. State one harmful effect of each. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Pollutant | Harmful Effect | |---|---| | Carbon Monoxide (CO) | Binds haemoglobin → reduces oxygen in blood, can be fatal. | | Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC) | Photochemical smog formation, some are carcinogenic. | | Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx) | Acid rain, ground-level ozone, respiratory diseases. | | Carbon Dioxide (CO2) | Primary greenhouse gas → global warming. | --- ## 5.2 PART B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Provided ### **Question A (14 Marks):** Discuss the formation mechanism, harmful effects, and control strategies of **CO and HC emissions** from SI engines. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]` **Sub-part (a) — CO: Formation, Effects, and Control (7 Marks)** **Formation (3 marks):** - CO is the product of **incomplete oxidation of carbon** in fuel. - It is favoured at **rich mixtures (φ > 1)** and **low in-cylinder temperatures**. - During the expansion stroke, CO peaks in concentration but the **dissociation reaction CO2 → CO + ½ O2** is incomplete due to rapid cooling of gases (quenching). - Locally rich regions near the combustion chamber wall, around fuel droplets, and in crevice volumes also promote CO. **Effects (2 marks):** - CO is **colourless, odourless, and tasteless** — extremely dangerous. - It binds with **haemoglobin** to form **carboxyhaemoglobin**, reducing the blood's O2 carrying capacity by **~200×** compared to oxygen. - Concentrations above **100 ppm** for prolonged exposure cause headaches; **> 1000 ppm** cause unconsciousness and death. **Control (2 marks):** - **Closed-loop air-fuel ratio control** with lambda sensor keeping λ ≈ 1 (stoichiometric). - **Oxidation catalytic converter** oxidises CO → CO2 at 250–400°C with Pt/Pd/Rh catalysts. - **Better mixture preparation** and **increased excess air** in exhaust. --- **Sub-part (b) — HC: Formation, Effects, and Control (7 Marks)** **Formation (3 marks):** - HC emissions arise from **fuel escaping the flame front unburned**. - **Wall quenching** — flame extinguished within 0.1 mm of cold walls leaves a thin layer of unburned mixture. - **Crevice volumes** — fuel trapped in piston ring grooves and head-gasket area is protected from flame. - **Oil layer absorption** — fuel dissolves into lubricating oil film and desorbs later. - **Deposits** on combustion chamber walls act as HC reservoirs. **Effects (2 marks):** - HC participates in **photochemical smog** (with NOx in sunlight to form ozone). - Some are direct **carcinogens** — e.g., benzene, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs). - Reduces crop yield and damages vegetation. **Control (2 marks):** - **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)** valve returns blowby gases to the intake. - **Evaporative Emission Control (EVAP)** system traps fuel vapours in a charcoal canister. - **Oxidation catalyst** oxidises HC → CO2 + H2O. - **Tighter combustion chamber design** (smaller crevices, hotter walls) minimises quench layer. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks):** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)**. Discuss its role in meeting BS-VI norms. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]` **Sub-part (a) — Construction and Catalytic Reactions (7 Marks)** **Construction (3 marks):** - The TWC consists of a **ceramic honeycomb monolith (cordierite)** with a washcoat of **alumina (γ-Al2O3)**. - Washcoat is impregnated with **noble metal catalysts**: Platinum (Pt), Palladium (Pd), and Rhodium (Rh). - The honeycomb has **400–600 cells per square inch (CPSI)** giving a high surface area. - Outer **stainless-steel can** holds the substrate and provides expansion tolerance. **Catalytic Reactions (4 marks):** - **Oxidation of CO:** $$2 CO + O_2 \rightarrow 2 CO_2$$ - **Oxidation of HC (using R as hydrocarbon radical):** $$C_x H_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \rightarrow x\, CO_2 + \frac{y}{2} H_2 O$$ - **Reduction of NOx:** $$2 NO_x \rightarrow x\, O_2 + N_2$$ > **TWC is efficient only at λ ≈ 1 (±1%)**. Outside this window, either oxidation or reduction efficiency drops sharply — hence **closed-loop lambda control** is mandatory. --- **Sub-part (b) — Working and Role in BS-VI Compliance (7 Marks)** **Working (3 marks):** - Lambda sensor (upstream) sends O2 concentration feedback to ECU. - ECU trims fuel injection duration to maintain λ = 1 ± 0.01. - A **second lambda sensor (downstream)** monitors TWC efficiency (OBD-II). - Exhaust gas passes through the substrate, where simultaneous oxidation and reduction occur. **Role in BS-VI (4 marks):** - BS-VI limits CO to **1.0 g/km** (petrol cars); TWC alone achieves **> 90% conversion**. - HC + NOx combined limit: **0.10 g/km** — TWC contributes substantially. - After BS-VI (Apr 2020), TWC is paired with **GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter)** for direct-injection engines to trap PM. - On-board diagnostics (OBD-II) reports deterioration of catalyst efficiency via downstream sensor. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Writing only "NOx is harmful"** without specifying the Zeldovich mechanism → lose 2 marks. Examiner expects *both* the chemical equation **and** the temperature threshold (~1800 K). > 2. **Drawing catalytic converter incorrectly** — many students draw a single block instead of the honeycomb monolith + washcoat + catalyst layers. Marks reserved for a "neat sketch" (2–3 marks) are forfeited. > 3. **Mixing up HC and CO formation** — both are incomplete combustion but the *primary reason* differs. CO → rich mixture, low T; HC → quench layer, crevice volumes. Confusing the two loses credit. > 4. **Omitting units** in emission index answers (e.g., writing EI = 3 instead of **3 kg CO2/kg fuel**). > 5. **Forgetting that TWC works ONLY at λ ≈ 1** — the answer must mention **closed-loop lambda control** for full credit. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must know before exam):** > - Six regulated pollutants: **CO, HC, NOx, PM, CO2, SO2** (memorise formation cause + effect + control). > - **CO** → rich mixture / incomplete combustion; controlled by **oxidation catalyst + λ-control**. > - **HC** → quench layer + crevice volumes + oil absorption; controlled by **oxidation catalyst + PCV + EVAP**. > - **NOx** → high peak T via **Zeldovich mechanism**; controlled by **EGR + SCR + LNT + retarded timing**. > - **PM** → diesel diffusion flame soot; controlled by **DPF + DOC + high EGR**. > - **CO2** → complete combustion; controlled by **improved efficiency + alternative fuels + hybridisation**. > - **SO2** → fuel sulfur; controlled by **low-sulfur fuel (≤10 ppm under BS-VI)**. > - **Emission Index** $\text{EI}_i = \dfrac{\dot{m}_i}{\dot{m}_f}$ → units **g/kg fuel**. > - **BSEC** = g/kWh → used for **heavy-duty diesel** certification. > - **1 ppm of NO2** ≈ **1.88 mg/m³** at 25°C (memorise the standard conversion factors). > - **TWC** is effective only at **λ = 1 ± 1%**; outside this window efficiency collapses. > - **NOx–PM trade-off** in diesels is the reason BS-VI vehicles combine **SCR + DPF + DOC + EGR** in a single after-treatment train. > - **Bharat Stage VI** is the current statutory norm in India (since April 2020), equivalent to **Euro VI**.

topics.title: Fuel Additives - Need
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuel Additives - Need

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fuel Additives** are chemical compounds (typically organic or organometallic substances) blended in small, precisely metered proportions (ranging from a few **ppm** to a few percent by mass) into conventional petroleum fuels or alternate fuels to enhance their performance, stability, handling, and emission characteristics beyond what the base fuel can offer naturally.

In the context of **OEMUT615 – Alternate Fuels and Energy Systems**, fuel additives are addressed as a critical finishing technology that bridges the gap between the *inherent limitations* of a base fuel (petrol, diesel, ethanol, biodiesel, LPG, CNG, hydrogen) and the *stringent operational demands* of modern Internal Combustion (IC) engines, fuel delivery systems, and emission regulations (Bharat Stage VI / Euro VI norms).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "Vitamins for Your Engine"**:
> Think of fuel as plain milk. Milk is nutritious, but if a child needs extra calcium, iron, or Vitamin D, the pediatrician prescribes *tonics* (additives). Similarly, an engine is designed to run on fuel, but raw fuel may not have all the "nutrients" required to keep the engine's fuel system clean, prevent knocking, or stop diesel from gelling in winter. Fuel additives act like those targeted tonics — small doses, big effect.

A more engineering-oriented analogy is that of a **pharmaceutical drug**: the API (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient) is the *base fuel* providing bulk energy, while the *excipients and active drug molecules* are the additives that fine-tune the delivery, absorption (combustion), and shelf-life of the product.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Additive Dosing

- **ppm** = parts per million (mg of additive per kg of fuel)
- **wt%** = weight percent of additive in fuel
- **Treat Rate** = typical commercial dosage, usually **100 – 3000 ppm**
- **RON** = Research Octane Number
- **MON** = Motor Octane Number
- **CFPP** = Cold Filter Plugging Point
- **ORP** = Oxidation Reduction Potential
- **ASTM D975 / EN 590 / IS 1460** — standard specifications governing additive use

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1)**: The "Need for fuel additives" must be studied under the umbrella of *engine performance limitations of base fuels* — particularly deposits, knock, cold flow, oxidation, corrosion, and exhaust emissions. Students must clearly articulate **why** an additive is needed *before* classifying what it is.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need Fuel Additives? — The Six Pillars of "Need"

The need for fuel additives arises from the **inherent physico-chemical limitations** of both petroleum-derived and alternate fuels. KTU examiners frequently frame Part-A questions around these six pillars. Mastering them is essential.

### 2.1.1 Pillar 1 — Combustion Quality Improvement (Anti-Knock Need)

- **Problem**: Gasoline has a finite octane rating; high-compression-ratio (CR) SI engines and turbocharged engines demand fuel with **RON ≥ 95–98**.
- **Issue with alternate fuels**: Methanol has RON ≈ **112** (excellent), but ethanol RON ≈ **108** and LPG ≈ **96**; however, their *sensitivity* (RON–MON) and *latent heat of vaporization* cause cold-start and partial-load issues.
- **Need**: Octane boosters like **MTBE**, **ETBE**, **TAME**, **MMT** (Methylcyclopentadienyl Manganese Tricarbonyl), and **Ferrocene** to push RON upward.
- **Quantitative relationship**:
  $$\text{Knock-limited IMEP} \propto \left(\frac{\text{RON}}{100}\right)^n \quad \text{where } n \approx 1.5\text{ to }2$$

### 2.1.2 Pillar 2 — Deposit Control (Detergency Need)

- **Problem**: Crankcase blow-by gases, fuel oxidation products, and thermal degradation deposit gums, varnishes, and carbon on **intake valves, injectors, spark plugs, and combustion chambers**.
- **Consequence**: Power loss (3–8%), increased HC and CO emissions, misfires, increased fuel consumption (4–6%).
- **Need**: **Polyisobutylamine (PIBA)** and **polyetheramine (PEA)** based detergent dispersants to keep surfaces clean (the *keep-clean* function) and to *clean-up* existing deposits.

### 2.1.3 Pillar 3 — Cold-Flow Improvement (Operability Need)

- **Problem**: Diesel fuels contain **n-paraffins (waxes)** that crystallize when ambient temperature drops. Wax crystals plug fuel filters, causing **wax gelling** and starting failure.
- **Consequence**: Cold Filter Plugging Point (CFPP) may be **+5 °C** for summer diesel and worsens to **0 °C to −10 °C** in winter.
- **Need**: **Cold Flow Improvers (CFI)** — typically **ethylene–vinyl acetate (EVA) copolymers**, **polyalkyl methacrylates**, and **styrene–maleic anhydride copolymers** that co-crystallize with wax and modify crystal morphology (smaller, more spherical crystals that don't interlock).

### 2.1.4 Pillar 4 — Oxidation & Storage Stability (Shelf-Life Need)

- **Problem**: Hydrocarbons react with dissolved **O₂** forming **peroxides, hydroperoxides, aldehydes, and acids** — accelerating gum formation and corrosiveness.
- **Worst offenders**: Biodiesel (FAME) is *highly* susceptible due to unsaturated C=C double bonds (especially **methyl linoleate C18:2** and **linolenate C18:3**).
- **Quantitative metric — Induction Period (IP)** per **EN 14112**:
  $$\text{IP} \geq 6 \text{ hours for B100 (pure biodiesel per EN 14214)}$$
  Without additive, soy biodiesel IP ≈ 3–4 h, failing the spec.
- **Need**: **Antioxidants** — hindered phenols (**BHT**, **BHA**, **Ionol CP-911**), aromatic amines (**phenyl-α-naphthylamine**).

### 2.1.5 Pillar 5 — Corrosion & Metal Deactivation (Material Protection Need)

- **Problem**: Sulfur compounds, water, organic acids, and microbial metabolic products (in biodiesel) corrode fuel tanks, lines, and injectors.
- **Need**: **Corrosion inhibitors** — long-chain carboxylic acid salts, **sulfonates**, and **fatty acid imidazolines**.

### 2.1.6 Pillar 6 — Emission Reduction (Environmental Compliance Need)

- **Problem**: Direct-injection engines, especially diesels, produce **NOₓ**, **PM** (soot), **unburned HC**, and **CO**.
- **Need**:
  - **Cetane improvers** (2-ethylhexyl nitrate, ditertiary-butyl peroxide) → shorten ignition delay → lower **NOₓ** & **PM** peaks.
  - **Fuel-borne catalysts** (Ferrocene) → catalyze soot oxidation in DPF.
  - **Lubricity improvers** (fatty acid esters like **biodiesel itself at 2–5%**) → ultra-low-sulfur diesel (ULSD, <10 ppm S) has poor lubricity and causes injector pump wear.
  - **Anti-foam agents** (silicone polymers like polydimethylsiloxane) → faster refueling without spillage.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Relation | Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Octane Number Blending (linear approx.) | $RON_{mix} = x_1 RON_1 + x_2 RON_2$ | dimensionless | Valid for $x_i$ = volume fraction |
| 2 | Cetane Number of mixture | $CN_{mix} = x_1 CN_1 + x_2 CN_2$ | dimensionless | Linear blending rule |
| 3 | Additive treat rate conversion | $ppm = \frac{m_{add}}{m_{fuel}} \times 10^6$ | mg/kg | Typical: 50 – 3000 ppm |
| 4 | Density-corrected treat rate (volumetric) | $C_{vol}(ppm) = C_{mass}(ppm) \times \dfrac{\rho_{add}}{\rho_{fuel}}$ | mL/L | Used for liquid additives |
| 5 | Induction period (oxidation stability) | $IP$ per EN 14112 / Rancimat | hours | B100 ≥ 6 h |
| 6 | Cold Filter Plugging Point (CFPP) | $CFPP = T_{wax\,crystal\,plug\,formation}$ | °C | Summer diesel: 0 to −6 °C |
| 7 | Lower Heating Value (LHV) of blend | $LHV_{mix} = \sum x_i LHV_i$ | kJ/kg | Linear mass-weighted |
| 8 | Stoichiometric air–fuel ratio | $A/F_{st} = \dfrac{34.96\,(C) + 11.5\,(H - O/8) + 4.32\,(S)}{M_{fuel}}$ | dimensionless | Petrol ≈ 14.7; Diesel ≈ 14.5 |
| 9 | Energy share of additive in blend | $E_{add} = \dfrac{m_{add} \cdot LHV_{add}}{m_{mix} \cdot LHV_{mix}} \times 100\%$ | % | Negligible for additives (<0.5%) |
| 10 | Cetane index (ASTM D976) | $CI = 454.74 - 1641.416\,D + 774.74\,D^2 - 0.554\,T_{50} + 97.803\,(\log T_{50})^2$ | dimensionless | D = density at 15 °C (g/mL) |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Automotive OEMs (Maruti, Tata, Mahindra)** specify fuel additive packages for BS-VI compliant engines.
- **Refineries** (Indian Oil, BPCL) use additives to upgrade low-octane naphtha streams into premium petrol.
- **Defence** uses **JP-8** aviation fuel with static dissipator additive (Stadis 450) and FSII (Fuel System Icing Inhibitor).
- **Marine & Railways** use **biocide additives** to prevent microbial growth in bunker fuel tanks.
- **Hydrogen & CNG** systems use odorants (ethyl mercaptan) for **leak detection** — a unique additive application.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Demonstrations

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linear Octane Blending Equation

This derivation supports the *why we need octane boosters* pillar and frequently appears as a 7-mark KTU problem.

**Starting Principle (Ethyl Mixing Model approximation):**
The octane number of a binary blend is empirically observed to follow a near-linear volumetric relation when both components are hydrocarbons of similar chemistry. This is called the **Ethyl Blending Model** (simplified form).

Let fuel A (RON = R₁, volume fraction = x₁) and fuel B (RON = R₂, volume fraction = x₂) be blended.

**Step 1**: Volume fractions must sum to unity (conservation of volume):
$$x_1 + x_2 = 1$$

**Step 2**: The blend RON is the volume-weighted sum (linear blending rule):
$$RON_{blend} = x_1 R_1 + x_2 R_2$$

**Step 3**: Substitute $x_2 = 1 - x_1$:
$$RON_{blend} = x_1 R_1 + (1 - x_1) R_2$$

**Step 4**: Expand and collect terms:
$$RON_{blend} = R_2 + x_1 (R_1 - R_2)$$

**Interpretation**: Adding 1% (by volume) of a higher-octane component raises the blend RON by **$(R_1 - R_2)/100$** units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance**: This is the very equation that justifies the use of octane boosters. If $R_1 - R_2 = 20$ and we add 5% of an oxygenate (ethanol) to base petrol, the RON gain is $0.05 \times 20 = 1.0$ — a small but commercially critical uplift for the refiner.

### 3.1.1 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem**: A refiner blends **85%** straight-run gasoline (RON = **80**) with **15%** reformate (RON = **98**). Find the blend RON using the linear rule.

**Solution**:
$$RON_{blend} = 0.85 \times 80 + 0.15 \times 98$$

**Step-by-step**:
- Term 1: $0.85 \times 80 = 68.0$
- Term 2: $0.15 \times 98 = 14.7$
- Sum: $68.0 + 14.7 = 82.7$

$$\boxed{RON_{blend} = 82.7}$$

**Reality check**: Real-world blending shows *non-linear synergy* — the actual RON may be 0.5–2.0 higher. This deviation itself is the *reason oxygenate additives (MTBE, ethanol) are favored* — they exhibit a **synergistic (non-linear) octane bonus**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Additive Treat-Rate Conversion (ppm to wt% and vice versa)

This derivation is needed to convert supplier dosage specifications (often in **ppm**) into blend-shop percentages.

**Definitions**:
- Treat rate (T) in ppm = mg of additive per kg of fuel.
- 1 wt% = 10,000 ppm.

**Step 1**: Express the additive mass:
$$m_{add} = T \times 10^{-6} \times m_{fuel}$$

**Step 2**: Convert to wt% by dividing by 100:
$$\text{wt\%} = \frac{T}{10{,}000}$$

**Step 3**: For volumetric treat rate (mL of liquid additive per litre of fuel), use density correction:
$$T_{vol}(ppm) = T_{mass}(ppm) \times \frac{\rho_{add}}{\rho_{fuel}}$$

### 3.2.1 Worked Example

**Problem**: A corrosion inhibitor is dosed at **500 ppm** by mass into diesel. The additive density = **0.92 g/mL**; diesel density = **0.85 g/mL**. Compute (a) wt%, and (b) volumetric dose in mL per litre of diesel.

**Part (a)**:
$$\text{wt\%} = \frac{500}{10{,}000} = 0.05\%$$

**Part (b)**:
$$T_{vol} = 500 \times \frac{0.92}{0.85} = 500 \times 1.0824 = 541.2 \text{ ppm (volumetric)}$$

Convert to mL/L. 1 L of diesel weighs **850 g**. Additive mass per L of diesel = $850 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} = 0.425$ g. Additive volume:
$$V_{add} = \frac{0.425}{0.92} = 0.462 \text{ mL per litre of diesel}$$

$$\boxed{V_{add} = 0.462 \text{ mL/L}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Impact of a Small Additive Addition

Demonstrates that additives do *not* meaningfully change the calorific value of fuel — justifying their use without energy penalty.

**Step 1**: LHV of fuel blend with additive:
$$LHV_{mix} = \frac{m_f LHV_f + m_a LHV_a}{m_f + m_a}$$

**Step 2**: For tiny additive dose $m_a \ll m_f$, expand using $1/(1+\varepsilon) \approx 1 - \varepsilon$:
$$LHV_{mix} \approx LHV_f \left(1 - \frac{m_a}{m_f}\right) + \frac{m_a}{m_f} LHV_a$$

**Step 3**: Rearrange:
$$LHV_{mix} = LHV_f - \frac{m_a}{m_f}\left(LHV_f - LHV_a\right)$$

**Step 4**: For treat rate $T = 500$ ppm, $\frac{m_a}{m_f} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$. If $LHV_f - LHV_a = 10{,}000$ kJ/kg, the energy penalty is:
$$\Delta LHV = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 10{,}000 = 5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Relative to petrol LHV ≈ **43,000 kJ/kg**, this is **0.012%** — *negligible*.

$$\boxed{\text{Additive-induced energy loss} < 0.05\% \text{ for typical treat rates}}$$

This is the *engineering reason* additives are universally accepted — performance benefits with no meaningful energy penalty.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping "Need" to "Additive Type"

This consolidated matrix is the *single most important KTU revision chart* for this topic.

| # | Engine / Fuel Problem (The Need) | Root Cause | Quantitative Indicator | Class of Additive Required | Example Compound | Typical Dose |
|---|----------------------------------|------------|------------------------|----------------------------|------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Knocking in SI engine | Low RON base fuel | RON gap > 5 | Octane booster | MTBE, Ethanol, MMT | 5–15 vol% / 8–18 ppm Mn |
| 2 | Long ignition delay in CI engine | Low CN | CN < 45 | Cetane improver | 2-EHN, DTBP | 0.05–0.4 vol% |
| 3 | Injector / intake valve deposits | Thermal oxidation, gums | Intake valve deposit > 50 mg | Detergent / dispersant | PIBA, PEA | 50–300 ppm |
| 4 | Wax gelling in winter | n-Paraffin crystallization | CFPP > ambient T | Cold flow improver | EVA copolymer | 100–1000 ppm |
| 5 | Biodiesel oxidation | C=C unsaturation | IP < 6 h | Antioxidant | BHT, Ionol | 200–1000 ppm |
| 6 | Tank / line corrosion | H₂O, acids, microbes | Cu strip corrosion > 1b | Corrosion inhibitor | Sulfonate, imidazoline | 5–50 ppm |
| 7 | Injector pump wear (ULSD) | Stripped S-lubricity layer | HFRR wear scar > 460 µm | Lubricity improver | Biodiesel, fatty acid ester | 10–2000 ppm |
| 8 | High NOₓ, PM emissions | Local rich pockets, high Tₐd | PM > 4.5 mg/km (BS-VI) | Combustion modifier / FBC | Ferrocene, EHN | 10–100 ppm |
| 9 | Foaming during refueling | Surfactant impurities | Foam collapse > 10 s | Anti-foam | PDMS (silicone) | 5–50 ppm |
| 10 | Microbial growth (biodiesel) | H₂O + FAME | CFU > 10⁴/mL | Biocide | Isothiazolone | 50–300 ppm |
| 11 | Static electricity buildup (kerosene/JP-8) | Low conductivity | Conductivity < 1 pS/m | Static dissipator | Stadis 450 | 0.5–3 ppm |
| 12 | Ice in fuel lines (cold aviation) | Water in fuel | Freezing point of H₂O = 0 °C | FSII (icing inhibitor) | Diethylene glycol monomethyl ether | 0.1–0.15 vol% |
| 13 | Odourless gas leak (CNG/LPG/H₂) | Natural gas is odourless | Detection threshold | Odorant | Ethyl mercaptan (C₂H₅SH) | 5–25 mg/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip**: The "Need" must always be presented in a *problem → cause → solution* chain. A student who simply lists "deposit, knock, corrosion" without linking to the *underlying physico-chemical cause* loses 1–2 marks in the ESE.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Demonstration — Why Antioxidants Are Critical for Biodiesel

**Biodiesel (FAME)** is the *most oxidation-prone* alternate fuel and is heavily tested in KTU Module 1.

**Given**:
- Soybean methyl ester (SME) sample, no antioxidant added.
- Induction period (IP) measured by Rancimat (EN 14112) = **3.2 hours**.
- Specification limit: IP ≥ **6 hours** (per EN 14214).
- Antioxidant proposed: **BHT (Butylated Hydroxytoluene)** at 1000 ppm.
- BHT effectiveness factor: each 1000 ppm BHT extends IP by approximately **3.8 hours** (literature value for SME).

**Find**: Will 1000 ppm BHT meet EN 14214 spec?

**Step 1**: Final IP after additive:
$$IP_{final} = IP_{base} + (\text{ppm/1000}) \times \Delta IP_{per 1000 ppm}$$
$$IP_{final} = 3.2 + (1000/1000) \times 3.8 = 3.2 + 3.8 = 7.0 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2**: Compare to limit:
$$7.0 \text{ h} \geq 6.0 \text{ h} \quad \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{\text{BHT at 1000 ppm brings soy biodiesel into EN 14214 compliance.}}$$

**Engineering Takeaway**: This is *exactly why* commercial B100 sold in India, the EU, and the US *always* contains antioxidant additives — meeting the spec is impossible for most vegetable-oil biodiesels without them.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Six Pillars of "Need for Fuel Additives"

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Base Fuel Limitations] --> nodeB[Combustion Deficiency]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Surface Deposition]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Cold Flow Failure]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Oxidative Degradation]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Material Degradation]
    nodeA --> nodeG[Emission Non-Compliance]
    nodeB --> nodeH[Octane Improver]
    nodeB --> nodeI[Cetane Improver]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[Detergent Dispersant]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Cold Flow Improver]
    nodeE --> nodeL[Antioxidant]
    nodeF --> nodeM[Corrosion Inhibitor]
    nodeG --> nodeN[Lubricity Improver]
    nodeG --> nodeO[Combustion Catalyst]
    nodeH --> nodeP[Refined Engine Performance]
    nodeI --> nodeP
    nodeJ --> nodeP
    nodeK --> nodeP
    nodeL --> nodeP
    nodeM --> nodeP
    nodeN --> nodeP
    nodeO --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeQ[BS-VI Compliance]
    nodeP --> nodeR[Extended Engine Life]
    nodeP --> nodeS[Improved Storage Stability]
    nodeQ --> nodeT[End User Satisfaction]
    nodeR --> nodeT
    nodeS --> nodeT
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Mechanism of Wax Crystal Modification (Cold Flow Improvers)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeW1[Paraffin in Fuel at Low Temp] --> nodeW2[Wax Nucleation Sites Form]
    nodeW2 --> nodeW3[Plate-like Wax Crystals Grow]
    nodeW3 --> nodeW4[Fuel Filter Plugged]
    nodeW4 --> nodeW5[Engine Fails to Start]
    nodeA1[CFI Additive Added ppm Level] --> nodeA2[EVA Copolymer Co-crystallizes]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Crystal Shape Modified to Spherulites]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Smaller Non Interlocking Crystals]
    nodeA4 --> nodeW4
    nodeW1 --> nodeA1
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Functional Role of Additives in the Fuel System

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph FuelStorage[Tank Storage]
        nodeFS1[Base Fuel] --> nodeFS2[Static Dissipator]
        nodeFS1 --> nodeFS3[Antioxidant]
        nodeFS1 --> nodeFS4[Biocide]
    end
    subgraph FuelDelivery[Delivery Lines]
        nodeFS2 --> nodeFD1[Anti Foam Agent]
        nodeFS3 --> nodeFD1
        nodeFD1 --> nodeFD2[Corrosion Inhibitor]
    end
    subgraph EngineIntake[Engine Intake and Combustion]
        nodeFD2 --> nodeEI1[Detergent at Injector]
        nodeFD2 --> nodeEI2[Octane or Cetane Improver]
        nodeFD2 --> nodeEI3[Lubricity Improver]
        nodeEI1 --> nodeEI4[Clean Combustion]
        nodeEI2 --> nodeEI4
        nodeEI3 --> nodeEI4
    end
    subgraph ExhaustAftertreat[Exhaust and Aftertreatment]
        nodeEI4 --> nodeEA1[Fuel Borne Catalyst]
        nodeEI4 --> nodeEA2[Emission Compliant Exhaust]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define fuel additives. List any four reasons justifying the need for fuel additives in modern IC engines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
Fuel additives are chemical compounds blended in small concentrations (typically **50 – 3000 ppm**) into base fuels to enhance their properties beyond inherent capabilities (1 mark).

Four reasons justifying the need:

1. **Octane enhancement** to prevent knocking in high-compression SI engines (0.5 mark).
2. **Deposit control** on injectors, intake valves, and combustion chambers (0.5 mark).
3. **Cold-flow improvement** to prevent wax gelling of diesel in winter (0.5 mark).
4. **Oxidation stability** of biodiesel and cracked gasoline to extend shelf life (0.5 mark).

*(Alternative: lubricity improvement in ULSD, corrosion inhibition, biocide activity, anti-foam — any 4 acceptable.)*

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Why are antioxidants mandatory in biodiesel but optional in fossil diesel? Justify with reference to molecular structure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
Fossil diesel contains mostly **saturated hydrocarbons** (paraffins, naphthenes, aromatics) with low reactivity toward O₂, giving long induction periods naturally (1.5 marks).

Biodiesel (FAME) contains high levels of **unsaturated fatty acid esters** (e.g., methyl oleate C18:1, linoleate C18:2, linolenate C18:3) with one or more C=C double bonds. These allylic and bis-allylic hydrogens are vulnerable to radical attack, forming peroxides, hydroperoxides, aldehydes, and acids (1 mark).

Thus, antioxidants (BHT, Ionol) are essential in biodiesel to meet **EN 14214 (IP ≥ 6 h)** (0.5 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q: Discuss in detail the different "needs" that justify the use of fuel additives in SI and CI engines. With neat sketches/tables, classify the additives used to address each need and explain the working principle of at least three classes.**

#### Part (a) — SI Engine Needs (7 marks)

**Need 1: Octane Improvement**
- SI engines with CR > 10:1 require premium fuel (RON 95–98).
- Straight-run gasoline has RON 60–80.
- Octane boosters like **MTBE, ethanol, MMT, ferrocene** raise RON.
- *Working principle*: MTBE and ethanol are oxygenated, raising the auto-ignition temperature of the mixture. MMT and ferrocene decompose to metal oxide radicals (MnO₂, Fe₂O₃) that scavenge hydroperoxide radicals (ROO•) and terminate chain-branching reactions that lead to knock.

**Need 2: Deposit Control in Intake Manifold and Combustion Chamber**
- Modern GDI engines suffer from PFI-side wall deposits and intake valve deposits (IVD).
- Polyisobutylamine (PIBA) detergents form a film on metal surfaces, preventing adhesion of polar oxidation products and dissolving existing deposits.
- *Typical IVD limit per BMW test*: < 50 mg/valve.

**Need 3: Anti-foaming**
- High fuel-pump flow rates cause foaming during refueling.
- Silicone polymers (PDMS, 5–50 ppm) lower surface tension of bubbles, causing rapid collapse.

**Need 4: Corrosion Inhibition**
- Sulfur species, water, and organic acids in fuel corrode fuel lines and tanks.
- Long-chain carboxylic acid salts and imidazolines form a monomolecular film on metal surfaces, displacing water and acid species.

#### Part (b) — CI Engine Needs (7 marks)

**Need 1: Cetane Improvement**
- Compression-ignition engines need CN ≥ 45 for smooth starting and low ignition delay.
- 2-Ethylhexyl Nitrate (2-EHN) at 0.05–0.4 vol% increases CN by 3–8 points.
- *Working principle*: 2-EHN decomposes early in the compression stroke, releasing NO and -ONO radicals that initiate low-temperature pre-flame reactions, shortening ignition delay.

**Need 2: Cold-Flow Improvement (CFPP)**
- Wax crystals from C₁₅–C₃₀ n-paraffins plug fuel filters below the cloud point.
- EVA (ethylene-vinyl acetate) copolymers co-crystallize with wax, nucleating many small, spherical, non-interlocking crystals.
- Reduces CFPP by 5–15 °C.

**Need 3: Lubricity Restoration in ULSD**
- Sulfur compounds in conventional diesel provide boundary lubrication to the rotary distributor pump and injectors.
- ULSD (< 10 ppm S) has poor lubricity (HFRR wear scar > 460 µm).
- Biodiesel at 1–2% or fatty acid methyl esters restore lubricity.

**Need 4: Emission Reduction (PM, NOₓ)**
- Ferrocene (10–100 ppm) decomposes to Fe₂O₃, a soot oxidation catalyst, lowering particulate emissions in DPF-assisted engines.
- 2-EHN (cetane improver) lowers peak flame temperature slightly, reducing NOₓ.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: With reference to biodiesel (FAME) as an alternate fuel, explain the need for fuel additives. Discuss the role of antioxidants, cold flow improvers, and biocides in detail, and present a worked numerical example showing the enhancement of oxidation stability.**

#### Part (a) — Biodiesel-Specific Needs (7 marks)

Biodiesel, despite its renewable origin and excellent lubricity, suffers from several inherent limitations that *necessitate* additive use:

**1. Poor Oxidation Stability (Antioxidant Need)**
- C18:2 (linoleate) and C18:3 (linolenate) esters have bis-allylic -CH=CH-CH₂-CH=CH- groups.
- Bis-allylic H abstraction energy ≈ **75 kcal/mol** — much lower than saturated C-H (~ 100 kcal/mol).
- Radicals propagate rapidly → peroxides → acids → gums.
- Without antioxidant, soy-biodiesel induction period (IP) = 3–4 h (spec: ≥ 6 h).
- Additives: **BHT (butylated hydroxytoluene)**, **BHA**, **TBHQ (tert-butyl hydroquinone)**, **Ionol CP-911**, **propyl gallate**.
- Working principle: H-atom donor mechanism: ArO-H + ROO• → ArO• + ROOH (stable radical terminates chain).

**2. Poor Cold-Flow Properties (Cold Flow Improver Need)**
- Biodiesel has higher Cloud Point (CP) and Pour Point (PP) than petro-diesel.
- Soy biodiesel: CP ≈ 0 to 2 °C; PP ≈ −2 to −3 °C.
- Winter operability demands: CP ≤ −5 °C in temperate regions.
- Additives: **EVA copolymers, polyalkyl methacrylates**.
- Effect: CP depression of 3–8 °C.

**3. Microbial Contamination (Biocide Need)**
- Biodiesel is hygroscopic and contains water traces → ideal for microbial growth.
- Common species: *Clostridium*, *Pseudomonas*, *Aspergillus*, *Hormoconis resinae* (the "kerosene bug").
- Consequences: biomass plugging of filters, acid production (corrosion), filter blockage.
- Additives: **isothiazolones** (Kathon, Bioban), **morpholine derivatives**.

**4. Corrosion of Copper and Bronze Alloys**
- Biodiesel can attack soft metals due to residual methanol and free fatty acids.
- Additives: **benzotriazole derivatives**, **tolyltriazole**.

#### Part (b) — Worked Numerical Example (7 marks)

**Problem Statement**:
A biodiesel sample from waste cooking oil has:
- Induction period (IP) before antioxidant addition = **2.8 h** (fails EN 14214 spec of ≥ 6 h).
- The proposed antioxidant is **TBHQ (tert-Butyl Hydroquinone)** with an effectiveness of **3.5 h per 1000 ppm**.
- Maximum treat rate allowed = **2000 ppm** (above this, no additional benefit and cost-prohibitive).

**Tasks**:
1. Determine the minimum treat rate to meet EN 14214 spec.
2. Verify that the dose is within the allowable limit.
3. If not feasible, propose a synergistic blend with **BHT (effectiveness 2.8 h per 1000 ppm)** to achieve the spec.

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Required IP gain:
$$\Delta IP = IP_{target} - IP_{base} = 6.0 - 2.8 = 3.2 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2**: Minimum TBHQ treat rate:
$$T_{TBHQ} = \frac{\Delta IP}{\text{Effectiveness}} \times 1000 = \frac{3.2}{3.5} \times 1000 = 914.3 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 3**: Verification:
$$914.3 \text{ ppm} \leq 2000 \text{ ppm} \quad \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{T_{TBHQ,min} = 914.3 \text{ ppm (approximately 915 ppm)}}$$

**Step 4** (if hypothetically TBHQ were insufficient): Assume TBHQ effectiveness were only 2.0 h/1000 ppm. Then:
$$T_{TBHQ,req} = \frac{3.2}{2.0} \times 1000 = 1600 \text{ ppm}$$

This is still within 2000 ppm. However, for a *synergistic blend* (more common in industry):
- Use **500 ppm TBHQ** + **500 ppm BHT**.
- IP gain: $(500/1000) \times 3.5 + (500/1000) \times 2.8 = 1.75 + 1.40 = 3.15$ h.
- Combined IP: $2.8 + 3.15 = 5.95$ h — *just below spec*.

**Refinement**: Increase to **600 ppm TBHQ + 600 ppm BHT**:
- IP gain: $0.6 \times 3.5 + 0.6 \times 2.8 = 2.10 + 1.68 = 3.78$ h.
- Final IP: $2.8 + 3.78 = 6.58$ h $\geq 6.0$ h ✓.

$$\boxed{\text{Recommended synergistic blend: 600 ppm TBHQ + 600 ppm BHT → IP = 6.58 h}}$$

**Industrial note**: Real-world antioxidants are *always* used in combinations (BHT + BHA + TBHQ in ratios 1:1:1) due to **synergistic free-radical quenching** and *regenerative antioxidant cycles* between hindered phenols and aromatic amines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Confusing "additives" with "blends"**: Methanol-ethanol blends in petrol are *fuel extenders*, not additives. Additives are dosed in ppm/wt% (≤ 5%) and do not materially change fuel composition or LHV.
> 2. **Forgetting the cause-effect chain**: Stating "anti-oxidant is used to prevent oxidation" is worth 0 marks. You must write "anti-oxidants donate H-atoms to ROO• radicals, terminating the chain-branching peroxidation mechanism, hence extending induction period from 3 h to >6 h."
> 3. **Ignoring units**: Treat rate must be specified in **ppm** or **wt%**, with density correction if volumetric.
> 4. **Memorizing without numerical depth**: KTU 14-mark questions *always* expect at least one worked numerical example (e.g., RON blending, antioxidant dose calculation, CFPP reduction).
> 5. **Forgetting alternate fuels context**: In OEMUT615, every "additive" answer must be tied back to either petroleum fuel OR an alternate fuel (ethanol, biodiesel, CNG, H₂) — purely automotive answers lose 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — *must memorize* for KTU ESE**:

- ✅ **Fuel additives** = chemical compounds dosed at **50 – 3000 ppm** (rarely > 5 wt%) to enhance fuel properties.
- ✅ The "need" arises from **six pillars**: combustion (knock / ignition delay), deposits, cold flow, oxidation, corrosion, emissions.
- ✅ **Knocking** in SI engines → octane boosters (**MTBE, ETBE, ethanol, MMT, ferrocene**).
- ✅ **Long ignition delay** in CI engines → cetane improvers (**2-EHN, DTBP**).
- ✅ **Injector / IVD deposits** → detergents (**PIBA, PEA**).
- ✅ **Wax gelling in diesel** → cold flow improvers (**EVA copolymers, polyalkyl methacrylates**).
- ✅ **Biodiesel oxidation** → antioxidants (**BHT, BHA, TBHQ, Ionol**); spec: **IP ≥ 6 h** per EN 14112.
- ✅ **ULSD wear** → lubricity improvers (biodiesel 1–2%, fatty acid esters); spec: HFRR < **460 µm**.
- ✅ **Refinery foaming** → anti-foam (**PDMS silicone, 5–50 ppm**).
- ✅ **Microbial growth in biodiesel** → biocides (**isothiazolones, morpholines**).
- ✅ **Hydrogen / CNG leak detection** → odorants (**ethyl mercaptan**).
- ✅ **Aviation fuel icing** → **Diethylene glycol monomethyl ether (FSII)**.
- ✅ **Static buildup in kerosene** → static dissipator (**Stadis 450**, 0.5–3 ppm).
- ✅ **Linear octane blending**: $RON_{mix} = x_1 RON_1 + x_2 RON_2$ (volume fractions).
- ✅ **Treat rate conversion**: 1 wt% = **10,000 ppm**; volumetric ppm needs **density correction**.
- ✅ **Additive energy penalty** is **< 0.05%** for typical doses — additives are *not* diluents.
- ✅ **KTU expects numerical examples** in 14-mark answers: always include at least one worked calculation (blending RON, antioxidant dose, CFPP reduction, etc.).
- ✅ **Biodiesel is the most additive-hungry alternate fuel**: oxidation + cold flow + microbial + corrosion = *four* additive classes mandatory.
- ✅ **Cause → Effect → Solution** format is mandatory for full marks in Part-B questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types

# Types of Alternate Fuels — A Comprehensive Classification Study

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternate Fuels (Definition per KTU 2024 OEMUT615 Syllabus):**
> *Alternate fuels* are **non-conventional, non-fossil or renewably derived energy carriers** that can **directly substitute or augment** conventional petroleum-based fuels (petrol/gasoline, diesel, and kerosene) in **internal combustion engines, industrial burners, gas turbines, and fuel cell systems**, with the objective of reducing **net carbon emissions, urban pollution load, and dependence on depleting crude oil reserves**, while delivering comparable or superior **engine performance characteristics**.

In the broader engineering sense, the term also encompasses **secondary energy vectors** such as **hydrogen, electricity, and synthetic e-fuels**, even though they are not "burned" in the classical sense — they are classified as alternate *energy systems* because they replace the thermal energy function of fossil fuels.

### 1.2 Why Do We Need Alternate Fuels? — The Driving Forces

The classification and study of types of alternate fuels is driven by **four global mega-trends** that every KTU 2024 student must internalize:

| Driver | Engineering/Societal Implication |
|---|---|
| **Depletion of Crude Reserves** | Proven crude oil reserves globally stand at approximately **1.7 trillion barrels** with a reserve-to-production ratio of ~**50 years**; alternate fuels ensure long-term energy security. |
| **Climate Change Commitments** | India's **Panchamrit** targets at COP-26 (Glasgow, 2021): **500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030**, net-zero by **2070**. |
| **Urban Air Quality** | BS-VI norms (India, 2020) cut NOx by **~25%**, PM by **~65%**; alternate fuels can go further. |
| **Energy Import Bill** | India imports **~85%** of its crude oil demand (2023 figures), creating a strategic economic vulnerability. |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Diversified Portfolio" of the Energy Sector:**
> Think of the world's energy supply like a *retired person's investment portfolio*. Just as you would not put **100% of your savings** into a single stock (because that single stock could crash), you cannot put **100% of a nation's transportation energy** into **one source** (crude oil). **Alternate fuels are the "diversified assets"** of the energy portfolio — ethanol is like the *stable government bond*, biodiesel like *green energy stocks*, hydrogen like *futuristic crypto assets*, and CNG like the *balanced mutual fund*. Each has **different risk-return characteristics** (storage, emissions, efficiency), and the *types* we study are the tools to build a balanced portfolio.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics Used to Classify Alternate Fuels

- **Calorific Value (CV):** Energy released per unit mass (**MJ/kg**) or volume (**MJ/m³**).
- **Cetane Number (CN) / Octane Number (ON):** Ignition quality for diesel / petrol engines.
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR):** Mass of air required per kg of fuel for complete combustion.
- **Flash Point & Auto-Ignition Temperature:** Safety and engine-relevant thermal thresholds.
- **Energy Density (Volumetric):** MJ/litre — critical for vehicle range.
- **Carbon-Hydrogen Ratio (C/H):** Determines CO₂ emission per unit energy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Alternate Fuels

The KTU 2024 OEMUT615 syllabus structures alternate fuels into **six primary families**. Each family is then subdivided by **physical state**, **source**, or **engine application**.

### 2.2 Family 1 — Alcohol-Based Fuels (Bio-Alcohols)

**Conceptual Foundation:**
Alcohols are produced by **fermentation** of sugar/starch crops (ethanol) or **synthetic catalytic** processes (methanol). They are **oxygenated** — the molecular oxygen already present in the fuel improves combustion completeness and reduces CO emissions.

#### 2.2.1 Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) — "The Sugar-Cane Molecule"

- **Source:** Fermentation of sugarcane molasses, maize, sorghum, or cellulosic biomass.
- **Production pathway (simplified):**
  $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$
- **Calorific Value:** **~29.7 MJ/kg** (about **66%** of gasoline).
- **Octane Number (RON):** **~110** (excellent knock resistance).
- **Stoichiometric AFR:** **9.0 : 1** (theoretical), but **higher oxygen content** means a *leaner effective* mixture in practice.
- **Blending:** Sold in India as **E20 (20% ethanol + 80% petrol)** under the **Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme**.
- **Key limitation:** **Hygroscopic** — absorbs water, causing phase separation in fuel tanks.

#### 2.2.2 Methanol (CH₃OH) — "The Simplest Alcohol"

- **Source:** Natural gas reforming, coal gasification, or **biomass-to-methanol** routes.
- **Calorific Value:** **~19.9 MJ/kg** — *half* of gasoline.
- **Octane Number (RON):** **~106**.
- **Toxicity Warning:** Highly toxic; **~10 mL ingestion can cause blindness**.
- **Advantage:** Can be used directly in **fuel cells (DMFC)** — direct methanol fuel cells.

### 2.3 Family 2 — Vegetable Oils & Biodiesel (Methyl/ Ethyl Esters)

**Conceptual Foundation:**
Pure vegetable oils are **too viscous** (50–100× diesel viscosity at 40 °C) for direct use. A **transesterification** reaction reduces viscosity by ~**one order of magnitude**, producing **biodiesel** — the mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids.

**Reaction Equation (Heart of Biodiesel Chemistry):**

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH/KOH}} 3\,\text{R-COOCH}_3 + \text{Glycerol}$$

- 1 mole of vegetable oil reacts with **3 moles of methanol** in the presence of a **base catalyst** (NaOH or KOH).
- Products: **3 moles of Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME = Biodiesel)** + **1 mole of glycerol** (by-product).

**Common Feedstocks (India-specific):**

| Source | Oil Yield (L/ha) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Jatropha curcas** | **~1900 L/ha** | Drought-resistant; non-edible; KTU-mandated study crop. |
| **Pongamia pinnata (Karanja)** | **~1700 L/ha** | Grows on marginal lands; widely available in Kerala. |
| **Palm oil** | **~5000 L/ha** | Highest yield; food-vs-fuel debate. |
| **Waste cooking oil (UCO)** | Variable | Re-cycled feedstock; **Used Cooking Oil (UCO)** mandate by MoPNG. |
| **Microalgae** | **~10,000–60,000 L/ha** | Third-generation; not yet commercially scaled. |

**Properties of Biodiesel:**

| Property | Biodiesel (B100) | Diesel |
|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | **~880** | **~840** |
| Kinematic Viscosity (mm²/s @ 40°C) | **4.0–6.0** | **2.0–4.5** |
| Calorific Value (MJ/kg) | **~37** | **~43** |
| Cetane Number | **50–60** | **45–55** |
| Flash Point (°C) | **~150** | **~55** |

### 2.4 Family 3 — Gaseous Fuels (CNG, LNG, LPG, Biogas, Hydrogen)

#### 2.4.1 Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) — **CH₄ (Methane, 88–95%)**

- **Calorific Value:** **~50 MJ/kg** — *highest among practical fuels*.
- **Stoichiometric AFR:** **17.2 : 1** (very lean combustion).
- **Octane Number:** **~120** (excellent).
- **Storage:** **200–250 bar** compressed cylinders.
- **India:** Over **6 million vehicles** run on CNG (Delhi NCR, Mumbai, Ahmedabad lead).

#### 2.4.2 Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG)

- **Liquefaction at:** **−162 °C**, 1 atm.
- **Energy density:** **~600×** that of CNG by volume.
- **Used for:** Heavy-duty trucks, ships, inter-city haul.

#### 2.4.3 Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)

- **Composition:** **~60% Butane + 40% Propane**.
- **Calorific Value:** **~46 MJ/kg**.
- **Octane Number:** **~108**.
- **India status:** **33+ crore** domestic connections under *Ujjwala Yojana*; auto-LPG is the next push.

#### 2.4.4 Biogas — **CH₄ (50–70%) + CO₂ (30–40%) + trace H₂S**

- **Production:** **Anaerobic digestion** of organic waste (cattle dung, food waste, sewage).
- **Calorific Value:** **~20–25 MJ/m³** (lower than pure methane due to CO₂ dilution).
- **Decentralized potential:** **75,000+ small biogas plants** operational in Kerala.

#### 2.4.5 Hydrogen (H₂) — *The Ultimate Zero-Carbon Vector*

- **Calorific Value (HHV):** **142 MJ/kg** — **the highest of any known fuel**.
- **Combustion product:** **Only H₂O** — no CO₂, no particulates.
- **Energy density problem:** **8 MJ/L** at 700 bar — requires **high-pressure or cryogenic storage**.
- **Production pathways:**

| Colour Code | Method | CO₂ Footprint |
|---|---|---|
| **Grey Hydrogen** | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) | **~9–12 kg CO₂/kg H₂** |
| **Blue Hydrogen** | SMR + Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) | **~1–3 kg CO₂/kg H₂** |
| **Green Hydrogen** | Electrolysis using **renewable electricity** | **~0 kg CO₂/kg H₂** |

### 2.5 Family 4 — Synthetic & Renewable Liquid Fuels

#### 2.5.1 Fischer–Tropsch (FT) Diesel / Synthetic Paraffinic Kerosene (SPK)

**Historical note:** Invented by **Franz Fischer & Hans Tropsch in 1925** in Germany; used by Nazi Germany and apartheid South Africa during oil embargos.

**Reaction:**
$$n\,\text{CO} + (2n+1)\,\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Fe/Co Catalyst, 200–350°C}} \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Feedstock:** Coal, natural gas, or biomass (syngas → FT).

- **FT-Diesel:** Cetane Number **>70**, near-zero sulphur, near-zero aromatics.
- **Used by:** **Sasol (South Africa)**, **Shell Pearl GTL (Qatar)**.

#### 2.5.2 Dimethyl Ether (DME) — *The Diesel Substitute*

- **Formula:** **CH₃–O–CH₃**.
- **Cetane Number:** **~55–60**.
- **Calorific Value:** **~28.4 MJ/kg** (lower than diesel, but excellent ignition).
- **State:** Gas at ambient — stored as **liquefied at ~5 bar** (much milder than LPG's 8 bar).
- **Toxicity:** Non-toxic, non-corrosive.

#### 2.5.3 Synthetic E-Fuels (Power-to-Liquid / Power-to-Gas)

Produced by **renewable electricity → H₂O electrolysis → H₂ → CO₂ + H₂ → synthetic hydrocarbons**.
- Example: Audi's *e-diesel* pilot plant in Dresden, Germany.

### 2.6 Family 5 — Electric & Hybrid Energy Systems

**Scope:** Although not a "fuel" in the thermodynamic sense, KTU 2024 groups these under energy systems because they **replace liquid fuels** in road transport.

- **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEV):** Powered by **Lithium-ion (LiB)** or solid-state batteries; energy density **~250 Wh/kg (cell level)**.
- **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEV):** Battery + ICE combination.
- **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEV):** Powered by **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cells** running on **H₂** — Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo.

### 2.7 Family 6 — Solid Biofuels (For completeness in power generation)

- **Wood pellets, bagasse, agricultural residue.**
- Used for **direct combustion** in power plants (e.g., **Saharanpur Sugar Mill 60 MW cogeneration**).
- Not used in IC engines directly.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — All Key Equations in One Place

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Cheat-Sheet for Alternate Fuels Calculations** — Memorize every row below.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units / Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | Otto cycle thermal efficiency | $r$ = compression ratio; $\gamma$ = specific heat ratio |
| 2 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | Diesel cycle thermal efficiency | $\rho$ = cut-off ratio |
| 3 | $AFR_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{2(2.67\text{C} + 8\text{H} + \text{O})}{\text{MW}_{fuel}}$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (combustion balance) | C, H, O = number of atoms |
| 4 | $Q = m \cdot CV$ | Total heat released | $m$ = mass (kg); $CV$ = calorific value (MJ/kg) |
| 5 | $CO_2 \text{ emission} = \dfrac{44}{12} \cdot \dfrac{m_f}{CV} \cdot \%C$ | Mass of CO₂ per MJ of energy | $\%C$ = carbon fraction in fuel |
| 6 | $\text{HHV} - \text{LHV} = h_{fg}(w/100) \cdot 2.444$ | Difference between HHV and LHV | $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization of water (**2.444 MJ/kg**) |
| 7 | $\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{m_{air} + m_{fuel}}{V_{air} + V_{fuel}}$ | Density of air-fuel mixture (gaseous fuels) | — |
| 8 | $\text{Volumetric Efficiency}, \eta_v = \dfrac{\text{Actual mass}}{\text{Theoretical mass}}$ | Volumetric efficiency (for gaseous fuels in IC engines) | — |
| 9 | $\text{Blending Ratio, } B_r = \dfrac{V_{alt}}{V_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % of alternate fuel in blend (E20, B20) | $V_{alt}$ = volume of alt fuel |
| 10 | $\text{Energy Equivalent of Blend} = B_r \cdot CV_{alt} + (1-B_r)\cdot CV_{base}$ | Effective calorific value of fuel blend | — |

### 2.9 Real-World Utility

The classification studied above is **not academic trivia** — it directly enables:

1. **Fuel Policy Decisions** — India's **National Hydrogen Mission (₹19,744 crore)** rests on knowing the types of H₂ pathways.
2. **Engine Calibration** — A Maruti Suzuki engineer designing an **E20-compatible engine** must know *why* ethanol's AFR differs from gasoline.
3. **Emission Modelling** — Choosing biodiesel reduces **CO by ~30%**, but increases **NOx by ~10%** — the *why* comes from molecular oxygen content and higher CN.
4. **Supply Chain Design** — Biogas plants need *different* feedstock logistics vs. LNG terminals.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivations — Detailed, Step-by-Step

### 3.1.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)

**Combustion Equation (Basis: 1 mole of fuel):**

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + a\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 1 — Balance Carbon atoms:**
Left: 2 C atoms → Right: $b$ C atoms ⟹ $b = 2$.

**Step 2 — Balance Hydrogen atoms:**
Left: 6 H atoms → Right: $2c$ H atoms ⟹ $2c = 6$ ⟹ $c = 3$.

**Step 3 — Balance Oxygen atoms:**
Left: 1 (from ethanol) + $2a$ (from O₂) = Right: $2b + c = 2(2) + 3 = 7$.
$$2a + 1 = 7 \implies 2a = 6 \implies a = 3$$

**Step 4 — Balanced Combustion Equation:**
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Molar Masses:**
- Ethanol: MW = 2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = **46 kg/kmol**.
- O₂ required: 3 kmol × 32 kg/kmol = **96 kg**.
- Air contains **only 21% O₂ by volume (and 23.2% by mass)**, so mass of air needed:
$$m_{air} = \dfrac{96}{0.232} = 413.79 \text{ kg of air}$$

**Step 6 — Stoichiometric AFR:**
$$AFR_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{413.79}{46} = \mathbf{9.0:1}$$

**Interpretation:** Ethanol needs *much less* air per kg compared to gasoline (**15.13:1**) — because ethanol **already carries oxygen in its molecule**.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation: Stoichiometric AFR of Methane (CH₄) — Biogas Base Molecule

**Combustion Reaction:**
$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Mass balance:**
- Methane: MW = 16 kg/kmol.
- O₂ required: 2 × 32 = 64 kg/kmol of CH₄.
- Mass of air: $64 / 0.232$ = **275.86 kg**.

$$AFR_{stoich} = \dfrac{275.86}{16} = \mathbf{17.24:1}$$

**Interpretation:** A *very high* AFR — this is why **CNG vehicles run lean** and have **lower knocking tendency** but **lower volumetric energy density**.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation: CO₂ Emission per MJ for Biodiesel (B100 — methyl oleate, C₁₉H₃₆O₂)

**Combustion:**
$$\text{C}_{19}\text{H}_{36}\text{O}_2 + 27.5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 19\,\text{CO}_2 + 18\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 1 — Carbon mass per kg of biodiesel:**
Carbon fraction = $\dfrac{19 \times 12}{19(12) + 36(1) + 2(16)} = \dfrac{228}{296} = 0.770$ i.e., **77% carbon**.

**Step 2 — CO₂ mass produced per kg of fuel:**
$$m_{CO_2} = \dfrac{44}{12} \times \%C \times 1 \text{ kg} = \dfrac{44}{12} \times 0.770 = 2.823 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Energy released per kg:** $CV_{biodiesel} = 37$ MJ/kg.

**Step 4 — CO₂ per MJ:**
$$\dfrac{2.823}{37} = \mathbf{76.3 \text{ g CO}_2/\text{MJ}}$$

**Comparison:** Petroleum diesel gives **~74 g CO₂/MJ** — biodiesel is *almost identical* in tailpipe CO₂, but is **carbon-neutral on a life-cycle basis** because the next crop reabsorbs it.

---

### 3.1.4 Derivation: Blended Calorific Value of E20 (20% Ethanol + 80% Petrol)

Given:
- $CV_{ethanol} = 29.7$ MJ/kg; $CV_{petrol} = 44$ MJ/kg.
- $B_r = 0.20$ (mass basis).

**Step 1 — Effective CV of blend:**
$$CV_{blend} = B_r \cdot CV_{ethanol} + (1-B_r)\cdot CV_{petrol}$$
$$CV_{blend} = 0.20 \times 29.7 + 0.80 \times 44$$
$$CV_{blend} = 5.94 + 35.20 = \mathbf{41.14 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$

**Step 2 — % reduction in energy content:**
$$\Delta CV\% = \dfrac{44 - 41.14}{44} \times 100\% = \mathbf{6.5\% \text{ drop}}$$

**Step 3 — Energy equivalent of 1 litre of E20:**
- $\rho_{petrol} \approx 0.75$ kg/L, $\rho_{ethanol} \approx 0.789$ kg/L.
- Mass-weighted density of E20: $\rho_{blend} = 0.20 \times 0.789 + 0.80 \times 0.75 = 0.758$ kg/L.
- Energy per litre: $0.758 \times 41.14 = 31.18$ MJ/L (vs. 33 MJ/L for petrol → **~5.5% range loss**, but offset by ethanol's higher octane allowing *higher compression*).

---

### 3.1.5 Derivation: Hydrogen Storage Energy Density (700 bar)

**Step 1 — Density of H₂ at 700 bar using ideal gas approximation (correction factor Z):**
At 700 bar, 25 °C, the compressibility factor $Z \approx 1.45$ (from real-gas tables).
$$\rho_{H_2} = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}$$
$$\rho_{H_2} = \dfrac{700 \times 10^5 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.45 \times 8.314 \times 298}$$
$$\rho_{H_2} = \dfrac{140,000}{3589} = \mathbf{39.0 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric energy density:**
$$E_v = \rho_{H_2} \times HHV_{H_2} = 39 \times 142 = \mathbf{5538 \text{ MJ/m}^3} = \mathbf{5.5 \text{ MJ/L}}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with gasoline:** ~32 MJ/L.

**Key Insight:** Even at **700 bar**, hydrogen stores only **~17%** of gasoline's energy per unit volume — this is the **"hydrogen storage problem"** that defines modern FCEV engineering.

---
