## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Major Alternate Fuels at a Glance

| Parameter | **Petrol** | **Diesel** | **Ethanol** | **Biodiesel** | **CNG** | **LPG** | **Hydrogen** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Formula | C₄–C₁₂ | C₁₀–C₂₁ | C₂H₅OH | C₁₉H₃₆O₂ | CH₄ (88%) | C₃H₈/C₄H₁₀ | H₂ |
| State | Liquid | Liquid | Liquid | Liquid | Gas (high P) | Liquid (low P) | Gas (cryo/HV) |
| CV (MJ/kg) | **44** | **43** | **29.7** | **37** | **50** | **46** | **142** |
| CV (MJ/L) | **~32** | **~36** | **~21** | **~33** | **~9 (200 bar)** | **~25** | **~5.5 (700 bar)** |
| Octane (RON) | 88–98 | N/A | **110** | N/A | **120** | **108** | >130 |
| Cetane | 8–15 | 45–55 | 8 | **50–60** | <10 | 5 | <10 |
| Stoich. AFR | 14.7 | 14.5 | **9.0** | ~13.8 | **17.2** | **15.6** | **34.3** |
| Flame speed (cm/s) | 35 | — | 41 | — | 35 | 38 | **250–300** |
| Auto-ignition (°C) | 246 | 210 | 423 | ~370 | **540** | 450 | 571 |
| CO₂ (g/MJ) | **73** | **74** | **70** | **76** | **56** | **63** | **0** |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three first-generation and three second-generation biodiesel feedstocks. State the major disadvantage of first-generation feedstocks.**

**Model Answer:**

> **First-generation feedstocks:** Edible vegetable oils — *Soybean oil, Sunflower oil, Rapeseed (Canola) oil, Palm oil, Groundnut oil.*
>
> **Second-generation feedstocks:** Non-edible / waste oils — *Jatropha curcas oil, Pongamia pinnata (Karanja) oil, Mahua oil, Waste cooking oil (UCO), Animal tallow.*
>
> **Major disadvantage of first-generation feedstocks:** They compete with the **food supply chain**, leading to the **"food-vs-fuel" conflict** that drives up food prices and causes deforestation for plantation expansion.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each family + 1 mark for the disadvantage)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define 'Green Hydrogen'. How is it different from 'Grey Hydrogen'? Mention one application.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Green Hydrogen:** Hydrogen produced by **electrolysis of water using electricity generated entirely from renewable sources** (solar, wind, hydro) — the **net CO₂ emission is zero**.
>
> **Difference from Grey Hydrogen:** Grey H₂ is produced by **steam methane reforming (SMR)** of natural gas, releasing **~9–12 kg of CO₂ per kg of H₂**, whereas Green H₂ produces **no CO₂ emissions** in production.
>
> **Application:** Decarbonization of **refineries, ammonia production, and transportation via Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEV)**.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark difference + 1 mark application)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — *Alternative 1*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a)** Classify the various types of alternate fuels used in IC engines. Give **two examples** for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of balanced chemical equations, explain the **transesterification process** for biodiesel production. Compare the properties of biodiesel with conventional diesel. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Classification of Alternate Fuels:**

| **Category** | **Description** | **Examples** |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Alcohol-based liquid fuels** | Produced via fermentation/synthesis; oxygenated fuels | **Ethanol (E20/E85), Methanol, Butanol** |
| **2. Vegetable oils & Biodiesel** | Triglyceride-derived methyl/ethyl esters | **Jatropha methyl ester, Karanja methyl ester, Palm oil biodiesel, Used Cooking Oil biodiesel** |
| **3. Gaseous fuels** | Hydrocarbon gases stored under pressure or cryogenically | **CNG, LNG, LPG, Biogas (CBG)** |
| **4. Hydrogen-based energy** | Zero-carbon energy carrier | **Grey, Blue, Green H₂; used in ICE and PEMFC** |
| **5. Synthetic liquid fuels** | Produced from syngas via FT, methanol-to-gasoline, etc. | **FT-Diesel (Sasol), DME, e-fuels** |
| **6. Electric & Hybrid systems** | Non-thermal replacement for ICE | **BEV, HEV, PHEV, FCEV** |

*(7 marks: ~1 mark per category + examples)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Transesterification Process:**

It is the **chemical reaction** between a **triglyceride** (vegetable oil / animal fat) and an **alcohol** (methanol/ethanol) in the presence of a **base catalyst** (NaOH or KOH) to produce **biodiesel (FAME)** and **glycerol** as by-product.

**Balanced Equation:**

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{NaOH/KOH, 60°C}]{\text{Catalyst}} 3\,\text{R-COOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3$$

**Process Steps:**

1. **Feedstock pre-treatment** — filtration to remove gums, water removal by heating.
2. **Alcohol-catalyst mixing** — Methanol is mixed with NaOH (typically 1 wt% of oil) to form **sodium methoxide** in-situ.
3. **Reaction** — Oil is heated to ~**60 °C**; methanol-catalyst mixture is added; stirred for **1–2 hours**.
4. **Phase separation** — Two layers form: **upper biodiesel layer** (FAME) and **lower glycerol layer**.
5. **Purification** — Biodiesel is **water-washed** to remove soap residues, then **vacuum-dried**.

**Property Comparison — Biodiesel vs. Diesel:**

| Property | **Biodiesel (B100)** | **Diesel** | **Engineering Implication** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | 880 | 840 | Slightly heavier per litre |
| Viscosity (mm²/s, 40 °C) | 4–6 | 2–4.5 | Higher — needs blend for low-temp use |
| Calorific Value (MJ/kg) | 37 | 43 | **~14% lower** — minor power loss |
| Cetane Number | 50–60 | 45–55 | **Better ignition quality** |
| Flash Point (°C) | 150 | 55 | **Much safer storage** |
| Sulphur content | <10 ppm | <50 ppm (BS-VI) | **Lower SOx emissions** |
| CO₂ life-cycle | Near zero | Net positive | **Carbon-neutral** |
| NOx emissions | ~+10% | Baseline | **Trade-off disadvantage** |
| Engine oil dilution | Possible | Minimal | **Shorter oil change intervals** |

*(7 marks: 3 marks reaction + 1 mark process + 3 marks comparison table)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently write the equation as a "single line" without specifying the **catalyst, temperature, and stoichiometric ratio (1:3 oil-to-methanol)**. The examiner *will* deduct 1–2 marks if these conditions are missing. Also, students often confuse the **Flash Point** with the **Fire Point** — Flash Point is the **lowest temperature at which vapour ignites momentarily**, NOT sustained ignition.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative 2*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question, Module 1 Choice]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain **any three renewable gaseous fuels**. Compare their **calorific values** and **storage methods**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **classification of hydrogen production** with respect to **carbon footprint**. Which colour of hydrogen is most suitable for India's **decarbonization goals**, and why? Mention **two challenges** of using hydrogen as an automotive fuel. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Three Renewable Gaseous Fuels:**

**1. Biogas (CBG — Compressed Biogas)**

- **Production:** **Anaerobic digestion** of organic waste (cattle dung, food waste, sewage, agricultural residue) by methanogenic bacteria in an **oxygen-free digester** at **35–55 °C** for 15–30 days.
- **Composition:** **CH₄ (50–70%) + CO₂ (30–40%) + trace H₂S, N₂, water vapour**.
- **Calorific Value:** **20–25 MJ/m³**.
- **Storage:** After **purification** (CO₂ scrubbing via water/amine absorption, H₂S removal via iron oxide), biogas is compressed to **200–250 bar** as **CBG** — analogous to CNG cylinders.

**2. Landfill Gas (LFG)**

- **Production:** **Natural anaerobic decomposition** of municipal solid waste (MSW) buried in sanitary landfills. The organic fraction breaks down over **10–30 years**, releasing a gas mixture similar to biogas.
- **Composition:** **CH₄ (~50%) + CO₂ (~45%)** + trace contaminants.
- **Calorific Value:** **16–20 MJ/m³** (slightly lower than controlled biogas due to heterogeneous feedstock).
- **Storage:** Typically **flared**; alternatively extracted via **vertical extraction wells** and compressed at **100–150 bar**.

**3. Producer Gas (from Biomass Gasification)**

- **Production:** **Partial oxidation / gasification** of biomass (wood chips, agricultural residue, coconut shells — a key Kerala feedstock) in a **downdraft gasifier** at **800–1200 °C** with controlled sub-stoichiometric air supply.
- **Composition:** **CO (20–30%) + H₂ (15–20%) + CH₄ (2–5%) + N₂ (45–55%) + CO₂ (5–10%)**.
- **Calorific Value:** **4.5–6 MJ/m³** (very low due to N₂ dilution).
- **Storage:** Used **directly on-site** for engines; difficult to compress economically due to low energy density.

**Comparative Calorific Value Summary:**

| Fuel | CV (MJ/m³) | CV (MJ/kg) | Storage Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure Methane (CNG) | **35.8** | **50** | **200–250 bar compressed** cylinders |
| Biogas (raw) | **20–25** | **~22** | Gas holder → CBG at **200 bar** |
| Landfill gas | **16–20** | **~18** | Vertical wells → flare / compress |
| Producer gas | **4.5–6** | **~5** | On-site low-pressure buffer tank |

*(7 marks: ~2 marks per fuel + 1 mark comparison table)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Classification of Hydrogen by Carbon Footprint:**

| Colour | Production Route | CO₂ Emitted (kg CO₂/kg H₂) | Renewable? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Grey** | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) of natural gas | **9–12** | ❌ No |
| **Brown** | Coal gasification | **~19–25** | ❌ No (worst) |
| **Blue** | SMR + Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) | **1–3** | ❌ Partial |
| **Green** | Electrolysis of water using **renewable electricity** | **~0** | ✅ Yes |
| **Pink** | Electrolysis using **nuclear electricity** | **~0** | ✅ Yes (debatable) |
| **Yellow** | Electrolysis using **grid electricity** (mixed) | Variable | ⚠️ Mixed |

**Most Suitable for India — Green Hydrogen:**

India's **National Green Hydrogen Mission (announced 2023, ₹19,744 crore outlay)** explicitly targets **5 million tonnes (MMT) of green hydrogen production by 2030**. Green H₂ is the most suitable because:

1. India has **abundant solar irradiation (300 sunny days/year)** and **wind potential (~175 GW installed target by 2030)** — both ideal for renewable electrolysis.
2. It provides the **only truly zero-carbon pathway** at scale, aligning with **net-zero 2070 commitments**.
3. India can **avoid natural gas imports** for hydrogen production, improving **energy independence**.

**Two Challenges of Hydrogen as Automotive Fuel:**

1. **Storage Challenge — The Energy Density Problem:**
   Hydrogen has an extremely low density (**0.0899 kg/m³ at STP**). Even at **700 bar compression**, it stores only **~39 kg/m³** — yielding **~5.5 MJ/L** volumetric energy density, **17% of gasoline's 32 MJ/L**. Cryogenic storage (–253 °C liquid H₂) achieves **~8.5 MJ/L**, still lower. This forces FCEVs to carry bulky tanks, **reducing passenger and cargo space**.

2. **Production and Distribution Challenge:**
   ~**95% of India's current hydrogen is Grey** (from refineries and fertilizer plants). Building **Green H₂ electrolysers** requires **~3–5 GW of dedicated renewable capacity** per MMT/year. The **distribution network** is non-existent — unlike petrol pumps, hydrogen requires either **high-pressure tube trailers (costly) or cryogenic tankers**, and **on-site production** is the most economical solution today.

*(7 marks: 3 marks classification + 2 marks India choice + 2 marks challenges)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):** Students often **omit numerical values** when comparing fuels — losing 1 mark for not stating CV values. Also, *do not confuse* "Hydrogen Energy" with "Hydrogen Fuel" — examiners specifically test whether you know that hydrogen is **both a fuel (combustion) and an energy carrier (fuel cell)**. Mention both uses explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Topic: Types of Alternate Fuels (Module 1)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Alternate fuels are non-conventional/renewable energy carriers that substitute petroleum fuels.
- ✅ **Six families:** Alcohols, Biodiesel/Vegetable oils, Gaseous fuels, Synthetic fuels, Electric/Hybrid systems, Solid biofuels.
- ✅ **Ethanol:** $C_2H_5OH$; CV = **29.7 MJ/kg**; RON = **110**; Stoich AFR = **9.0:1**; used in **E20/E85**.
- ✅ **Methanol:** $CH_3OH$; CV = **19.9 MJ/kg**; toxic; used in **DMFC fuel cells**.
- ✅ **Biodiesel:** Produced by **transesterification** of triglyceride + methanol with **NaOH/KOH catalyst** at **60 °C**.
- ✅ **CNG:** Mostly **CH₄**; CV = **50 MJ/kg**; Stoich AFR = **17.2:1**; stored at **200–250 bar**.
- ✅ **LPG:** **60% Butane + 40% Propane**; CV = **46 MJ/kg**; stored at **~8 bar liquefied**.
- ✅ **Biogas:** Anaerobic digestion; composition **CH₄ (50–70%) + CO₂ (30–40%)**; CV ≈ 20–25 MJ/m³.
- ✅ **Hydrogen:** HHV = **142 MJ/kg** (highest); **only water as combustion product**.
- ✅ **Hydrogen Colours:** Grey (SMR, dirty) → Blue (SMR+CCS) → **Green (renewable electrolysis — India's choice)**.
- ✅ **FT-Diesel:** Synthesis from syngas; high cetane (>70); near-zero sulphur.
- ✅ **DME:** Cetane ~55–60; stored as liquid at ~5 bar; non-toxic.
- ✅ **E-fuels:** Renewable electricity → H₂ → CO₂ + H₂ → synthetic hydrocarbons.
- ✅ **BS-VI regulations (India, 2020):** Reduced NOx by **25%**, PM by **65%** vs BS-IV.
- ✅ **India's target:** **20% ethanol blending by 2025 (E20)** under EBP programme.
- ✅ **National Green Hydrogen Mission:** ₹19,744 crore; **5 MMT green H₂ by 2030**.
- ✅ **Key formula to memorize:** $AFR_{stoich} = \dfrac{2(2.67C + 8H + O)}{MW_{fuel}} \cdot \dfrac{100}{23.2}$
- ✅ **Energy density lesson:** Hydrogen's **gravimetric** CV is highest, but its **volumetric** CV is lowest — *the hydrogen storage problem*.
- ✅ **Biodiesel advantage:** Carbon-neutral on life-cycle basis, **high cetane**, **biodegradable**, **safer (high flash point)**.
- ✅ **Biodiesel drawback:** ~14% lower CV, NOx increase, cold-flow issues, food-vs-fuel for 1st-gen oils.

---

topics.title: Commonly Used Additives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Commonly Used Additives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Addititives (KTU 2024 Definition):** Fuel additives are chemical compounds (usually in ppm to percentage level concentrations) that are blended with conventional or alternate liquid/gaseous fuels to enhance their physico-chemical properties, improve combustion efficiency, reduce harmful exhaust emissions, improve storage stability, prevent corrosion of fuel system components, and ensure compliance with environmental emission standards.

In the context of **Alternate Fuels and Energy Systems**, additives play a pivotal role because most alternate fuels (methanol, ethanol, biodiesel, CNG, LPG, hydrogen, etc.) suffer from inherent drawbacks such as low cetane number, poor lubricity, high viscosity, low calorific value, cold-start issues, and material incompatibility. Additives are blended in micro-quantities to overcome these limitations without altering the bulk fuel composition.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are making a cup of tea (your base fuel). The water is the fuel itself. Now, imagine adding:
- A pinch of **sugar** → makes it sweet (improves performance) → analogous to octane boosters.
- A few drops of **lemon** → prevents it from tasting stale → analogous to antioxidants.
- A spoon of **milk** → changes texture and color → analogous to oxygenates.

Similarly, when we add **a few millilitres of special chemicals** into a **tank of fuel**, we are fundamentally **rewiring its molecular behaviour** at the combustion interface, even though the bulk properties (like density and calorific value) remain almost the same. This is the magic of fuel additives — *small dose, big effect*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Commonly used additives include anti-knock additives, oxygenates, antioxidants, ignition improvers, cetane improvers, lubricity improvers, detergents, anti-foam agents, corrosion inhibitors, and cold-flow improvers. Their typical dosage ranges from **5 to 5000 ppm (parts per million)** or **0.05% to 5% by volume**, depending on the chemistry.

### 1.3 Why Additives Are Essential in Alternate Fuels

| Drawback of Alternate Fuel | Required Additive Function |
|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| Methanol corrodes metals | Corrosion inhibitor |
| Biodiesel has poor cold flow | Cold-flow improver |
| CNG has poor lubrication | Lubricity improver |
| LPG has high knocking tendency | Anti-knock additive |
| Hydrogen has high flame speed | Flame retardant / knock suppressor |
| Ethanol separates with water | Co-solvent / stabilizer |
| Vegetable oils are viscous | Viscosity reducer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics (KTU 2024):** Additive concentration is measured in **ppm (mg/kg)** or **vol%**. For example, **TEL (Tetraethyl Lead)** was historically used at **0.5–1.0 g/L** of gasoline. Modern oxygenates like **MTBE** are blended at **10–15 vol%**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Commonly Used Additives

Additives are broadly classified based on their **functional role** in the fuel–combustion–after-treatment chain.

#### A. Performance & Combustion Improvers

**1. Anti-Knock Additives (Octane Improvers)**
- **Purpose:** Suppress premature auto-ignition of fuel–air mixture in SI engines.
- **Working Principle:** They decompose in the end-gas zone, releasing radicals that terminate the peroxide chain reactions responsible for knock.
- **Examples:**
  - **Tetraethyl Lead (TEL), Pb(C₂H₅)₄** — historical; phased out due to lead poisoning.
  - **Methyl tert-Butyl Ether (MTBE), C₅H₁₂O** — oxygenate + octane booster.
  - **Ethyl tert-Butyl Ether (ETBE), C₆H₁₄O** — bio-based alternative.
  - **Tert-Amyl Methyl Ether (TAME)**.
  - **Methanol (CH₃OH)** and **Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)** — renewable oxygenates.
  - **Aromatic hydrocarbons**: Toluene, Xylene, Benzene.
  - **Methylcyclopentadienyl Manganese Tricarbonyl (MMT)** — modern replacement for TEL.

**2. Cetane Improvers (Ignition Improvers for CI Engines)**
- **Purpose:** Reduce ignition delay in CI engines; crucial for biodiesel and Fischer–Tropsch diesel.
- **Examples:**
  - **2-Ethylhexyl Nitrate (2-EHN), C₈H₁₇NO₃** — most widely used.
  - **Di-tert-butyl peroxide (DTBP)**.
  - **Amyl nitrate / Hexyl nitrate**.

**3. Oxygenates**
- **Purpose:** Promote complete combustion by supplying chemically bound oxygen; reduce CO and unburnt HC.
- **Examples:** MTBE, ETBE, Ethanol, Methanol, TAME, DIPE (Di-isopropyl ether).

#### B. Stability & Storage Improvers

**4. Antioxidants**
- **Purpose:** Prevent oxidation and gum formation during long storage; especially for biodiesel and unsaturated fuels.
- **Mechanism:** They donate hydrogen atoms to peroxy radicals, breaking the auto-oxidation chain.
- **Examples:** BHT (Butylated Hydroxytoluene), BHA (Butylated Hydroxyanisole), TBHQ (Tert-butyl hydroquinone), alpha-tocopherol (natural).

**5. Metal Deactivators**
- **Purpose:** Chelate trace metal ions (Cu, Fe, Mn) that catalyze fuel oxidation.
- **Examples:** Salicylaldehyde, N,N′-disalicylidene-1,2-propanediamine.

#### C. Engine Protection Additives

**6. Detergents / Dispersants**
- **Purpose:** Keep intake valves, injectors, and combustion chambers free of carbon and varnish deposits.
- **Examples:** Polybutene amine, Polyether amines.

**7. Anti-Wear / Lubricity Improvers**
- **Purpose:** Restore lubricity lost due to ultra-low-sulfur fuels and alternate fuels like CNG/H₂.
- **Examples:** Fatty acid esters, dimer acids, biodiesel itself (acts as natural lubricity improver for petrodiesel blends).

**8. Corrosion Inhibitors**
- **Purpose:** Protect fuel tank, lines, and injector from acidic/moisture attack (critical for methanol and biodiesel).
- **Examples:** Amine succinates, alkyl succinic acids, dimer/trimer acids.

#### D. Physical-Property Modifiers

**9. Anti-Icing Additives**
- **Purpose:** Prevent fuel-line freezing due to water condensation (aircraft fuels).
- **Examples:** Isopropanol, ethylene glycol monomethyl ether.

**10. Anti-Foam Agents**
- **Purpose:** Suppress foam during fuel tank filling.
- **Examples:** Silicone polymers (polydimethylsiloxane).

**11. Cold-Flow Improvers (PPDE / CFPP Improvers)**
- **Purpose:** Lower pour point and CFPP of biodiesel/diesel blends.
- **Examples:** Ethylene–vinyl acetate (EVA) copolymers, polymethacrylates.

**12. Demulsifiers / Emulsifiers**
- **Purpose:** Separate or stabilize water–fuel emulsions.
- **Examples:** Polyalkoxylated non-ionic surfactants.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Property / Formula | Symbol | Unit | Application |
|---|--------------------|--------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Octane Number (RON + MON)/2 | $\text{AKI} = \dfrac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2}$ | — | Anti-knock quality |
| 2 | Cetane Number | CN | — | CI ignition quality |
| 3 | Additive concentration (mass) | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{m_{add}}{m_{fuel}} \times 10^{6}$ | ppm | Dosage |
| 4 | Additive concentration (volume) | $C_{vol\%} = \dfrac{V_{add}}{V_{fuel}} \times 100$ | % | Dosage |
| 5 | Stoichiometric Air–Fuel ratio | $(A/F)_{st} = \dfrac{138 \cdot (2.66\,C + 8\,H - O)}{12\,C + H + 16\,O}$ | — | Combustion balance |
| 6 | Heat of combustion (Dulong) | $\text{HCV} = 33800\,C + 144200(H - O/8) + 9400\,S$ | kJ/kg | Fuel energy |
| 7 | Density conversion at 15 °C | $\rho_{15} = \rho_{t} \cdot [1 - \beta (t - 15)]$ | kg/m³ | Storage |
| 8 | Blending octane number | $\text{BON} = \dfrac{RON_{blend} - x_{b} RON_{b}}{x_{a}}$ | — | MTBE / ethanol blending |
| 9 | Anti-knock effectiveness (TEL) | $\Delta \text{ON} \approx 2.5 \times C_{TEL}$ (g/L) | ON units | Lead equivalency |
| 10 | CFPP reduction | $\Delta T_{CFPP} = k \cdot C_{add}$ | °C | Cold-flow improvers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Octane and cetane numbers are *opposite* indicators. Higher **octane** = better for SI engines (resists knock); higher **cetane** = better for CI engines (shorter ignition delay). Additives such as **MTBE** raise octane but lower cetane, while **2-EHN** raises cetane but has no effect on octane. This duality governs additive selection when blending alternate fuels.

### 2.3 Mechanism of Action — A Closer Look

**Anti-Knock Mechanism (TEL Decomposition):**

$$Pb(C_2H_5)_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta,\;O_2} PbO + CO_2 + H_2O$$

The nascent PbO particles act as **chain-breaking scavengers**, terminating the peroxy radical reactions (R• + O₂ → ROO•) responsible for the runaway auto-ignition (knock).

**Antioxidant (BHT) Chain-Breaking:**

$$ROO^\bullet + ArOH \longrightarrow ROOH + ArO^\bullet$$

The stable phenoxy radical (ArO•) does not propagate oxidation, effectively halting the chain reaction.

**Cetane Improver (2-EHN Decomposition):**

$$C_8H_{17}ONO_2 \xrightarrow{200-300^\circ C} C_8H_{17}O^\bullet + NO_2$$

The released **NO₂** and **alkoxy radicals** drastically shorten the ignition delay of CI fuel sprays.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION_3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Octane Boosting by MTBE Blending

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A gasoline has RON = 80. We blend it with **15 vol% MTBE** (RON of pure MTBE = 118). What is the approximate RON of the blend, assuming linear blending?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Blending Assumption (Linear-by-Vol):** Octane numbers of pure hydrocarbons blend linearly with their volume fractions (true for paraffinic and MTBE-type oxygenates within ±2 ON units of accuracy).

#### Step 1: Identify Volume Fractions

Let the total blend volume be $V_b$ = 100 mL.

$$V_{gasoline} = 85 \text{ mL} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_{gas} = 0.85$$

$$V_{MTBE} = 15 \text{ mL} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_{MTBE} = 0.15$$

#### Step 2: Apply the Linear Blending Equation

$$\text{RON}_{blend} = x_{gas} \cdot \text{RON}_{gas} + x_{MTBE} \cdot \text{RON}_{MTBE}$$

#### Step 3: Substitute the Numerical Values

$$\text{RON}_{blend} = (0.85)(80) + (0.15)(118)$$

#### Step 4: Perform the Multiplications

$$\text{RON}_{blend} = 68.0 + 17.7$$

#### Step 5: Final Result

$$\boxed{\text{RON}_{blend} = 85.7 \approx 86}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Adding only 15% MTBE raises the octane number by **6 points** (80 → 86), without significantly altering the energy density. This illustrates the *high efficiency* of oxygenate additives.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: 2-EHN Dosage for Target Cetane Number

**Problem Setup:** Pure biodiesel (B100, CN = 48) must be upgraded to CN = 52 for premium diesel quality. The cetane improver **2-EHN** provides a **boost of approximately +3 cetane numbers per 1000 ppm (0.1 vol%)**. Find the required volume of 2-EHN per 1000 L of biodiesel.

#### Step 1: Required Octane Boost

$$\Delta CN = CN_{target} - CN_{base} = 52 - 48 = 4$$

#### Step 2: Determine Additive Concentration

Using the rule $\Delta CN \approx 3$ per 1000 ppm:

$$C_{add} = \dfrac{\Delta CN}{3} \times 1000 \text{ ppm} = \dfrac{4}{3} \times 1000 = 1333.3 \text{ ppm}$$

#### Step 3: Convert ppm to Volume Fraction

For 2-EHN with density $\rho \approx 960$ kg/m³ and biodiesel $\rho \approx 880$ kg/m³:

$$x_{vol} \approx \dfrac{1333.3 \times 10^{-6} \times 960}{880} = 1.454 \times 10^{-3}$$

Equivalently, $x_{vol} \approx 0.1454\%$.

#### Step 4: Volume of 2-EHN per 1000 L Biodiesel

$$V_{2-EHN} = x_{vol} \times V_{fuel} = 0.001454 \times 1000 \text{ L}$$

#### Step 5: Final Answer

$$\boxed{V_{2-EHN} \approx 1.45 \text{ L per 1000 L biodiesel}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Additives vs Alternate Fuels

| Additive | Target Alternate Fuel | Function | Typical Dose | Mechanism |
|----------|----------------------|----------|--------------|-----------|
| MTBE / ETBE | Gasoline, ethanol blends | Octane booster + oxygenate | 10–15 vol% | Releases O during combustion, reduces CO |
| Ethanol | Gasoline | Octane + oxygenate | 5–10 vol% (E5–E10) | High latent heat, OH radicals promote clean burn |
| 2-EHN | Biodiesel, FT-diesel | Cetane improver | 500–2000 ppm | Releases NO₂ radicals, reduces ignition delay |
| BHT / TBHQ | Biodiesel | Antioxidant | 200–1000 ppm | H-atom donor, breaks peroxidation chain |
| Polyether amine | Gasoline, biodiesel | Detergent | 50–300 ppm | Solubilizes deposit precursors |
| Biodiesel (2%) | ULSD | Lubricity improver | 1–2 vol% | Fatty acids form protective tribofilm |
| Isopropanol | Aviation gasoline | Anti-icing | 0.1–0.5 vol% | Lowers water freezing point |
| Polydimethylsiloxane | Diesel | Anti-foam | 10–50 ppm | Reduces surface tension of bubbles |
| EVA copolymer | Biodiesel / diesel | Cold-flow improver | 100–500 ppm | Co-crystallizes with paraffin waxes |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Equivalence of Oxygenate Mass in CO Reduction

**Problem Setup:** Adding **10 vol% ethanol** (CH₃CH₂OH, oxygen content = 34.8% by mass) to gasoline. The fuel's H:C ratio is 2:1 (heptane, C₇H₁₆). Show how the **stoichiometric A/F** and **CO emissions** change.

#### Step 1: Empirical Formula of Ethanol

$$C_2H_6O \Rightarrow \text{Mass of O} = \dfrac{16}{46} = 0.3478$$

#### Step 2: Stoichiometric A/F (Heptane, C₇H₁₆)

$$C_7H_{16} + 11 O_2 \to 7 CO_2 + 8 H_2O$$

$$(A/F)_{st} = \dfrac{11 \times 32 \times 4.35}{100} = 15.2 : 1$$

#### Step 3: Stoichiometric A/F (Ethanol, C₂H₆O)

$$C_2H_6O + 3 O_2 \to 2 CO_2 + 3 H_2O$$

$$(A/F)_{st} = \dfrac{3 \times 32 \times 4.35}{46} = 9.0 : 1$$

#### Step 4: Blended A/F (Weighted Average)

Using $x_{eth} = 0.10$, $x_{gas} = 0.90$:

$$(A/F)_{blend} = \dfrac{0.90 + 0.10}{\dfrac{0.90}{15.2} + \dfrac{0.10}{9.0}}$$

#### Step 5: Denominator Calculation

$$\dfrac{0.90}{15.2} + \dfrac{0.10}{9.0} = 0.0592 + 0.0111 = 0.0703$$

#### Step 6: Final A/F Ratio

$$(A/F)_{blend} = \dfrac{1.0}{0.0703} = 14.22 : 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The leaner A/F of ethanol lowers the blended A/F from 15.2 to **14.22**, meaning **less air is required**. Combined with **chemically bound oxygen**, this dramatically suppresses CO formation (typical reduction: **20–40%**).

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Anti-Knock Effectiveness of TEL (Octane Equivalency)

**Problem Setup:** Using the empirical relation $\Delta \text{ON} \approx 2.5 \times C_{TEL}$ (where $C_{TEL}$ is in g/L), calculate the RON improvement when **0.8 g/L of TEL** is added.

#### Step 1: Substitute the Value

$$\Delta \text{ON} = 2.5 \times 0.8$$

#### Step 2: Compute

$$\boxed{\Delta \text{ON} = 2.0 \text{ ON units}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Historically, only **0.8 g of lead per litre** of gasoline was enough to raise octane by 2 numbers — an extraordinarily efficient additive. This is why TEL was ubiquitous from the 1920s until its ban under emissions regulations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flow of Fuel Additives

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Additives] --> B[Performance Improvers]
    A --> C[Stability Improvers]
    A --> D[Engine Protection]
    A --> E[Physical Modifiers]

    B --> B1[Anti-Knock]
    B --> B2[Cetane Improvers]
    B --> B3[Oxygenates]

    B1 --> B1a[TEL MTBE ETBE]
    B1 --> B1b[MMT Toluene]
    B2 --> B2a[2-EHN DTBP]
    B3 --> B3a[Ethanol Methanol]

    C --> C1[Antioxidants]
    C --> C2[Metal Deactivators]
    C1 --> C1a[BHT BHA TBHQ]
    C2 --> C2a[Salicylaldehyde]

    D --> D1[Detergents]
    D --> D2[Lubricity Improvers]
    D --> D3[Corrosion Inhibitors]
    D1 --> D1a[Polyether amines]
    D2 --> D2a[FAME Fatty acids]
    D3 --> D3a[Amine succinates]

    E --> E1[Anti-Icing]
    E --> E2[Anti-Foam]
    E --> E3[Cold-Flow Improvers]
    E1 --> E1a[Isopropanol]
    E2 --> E2a[Silicone polymers]
    E3 --> E3a[EVA copolymer]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Role of Additives in the Fuel–Engine–Emission Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1 [Fuel Storage]
        A1[Fuel Tank]
        A2[Antioxidant BHT]
        A3[Corrosion Inhibitor]
    end

    subgraph S2 [Fuel Delivery]
        B1[Fuel Pump]
        B2[Anti-Foam Silicone]
        B3[Detergent Polyether Amine]
    end

    subgraph S3 [Combustion Chamber]
        C1[SI Engine or CI Engine]
        C2[Octane Booster MTBE]
        C3[Cetane Improver 2-EHN]
        C4[Oxygenate Ethanol]
    end

    subgraph S4 [Exhaust After-Treatment]
        D1[Catalytic Converter]
        D2[DPF or TWC]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> C1 --> D1
    A2 -. protects .-> A1
    A3 -. protects .-> A1
    B2 -. prevents foam .-> B1
    B3 -. cleans injectors .-> C1
    C2 -. raises RON .-> C1
    C3 -. shortens ID .-> C1
    C4 -. reduces CO .-> C1
    D1 --> D2
```

### 4.3 Mechanism Flow: Anti-Knock Action of TEL

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[End-gas zone in cylinder] --> P2[Temperature exceeds 350 deg C]
    P2 --> P3[Peroxy radicals ROO form]
    P3 --> P4[Knock ignition starts]
    P4 --> P5{Additive present?}
    P5 -- Yes TEL --> P6[TEL decomposes to PbO]
    P6 --> P7[PbO scavenges ROO radicals]
    P7 --> P8[Chain reaction terminated]
    P8 --> P9[Smooth combustion continues]
    P5 -- No additive --> P10[Knock persists]
    P10 --> P11[Engine damage]
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix: Additive Selection for Alternate Fuels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Alternate Fuel Selection] --> Q1{Which fuel?}
    Q1 -- Methanol --> M1[Anti-icing + Corrosion inhibitor]
    Q1 -- Ethanol --> E1[Co-solvent + Antioxidant]
    Q1 -- Biodiesel --> BD1[Cetane improver + Antioxidant + Cold-flow]
    Q1 -- CNG --> CN1[Lubricity improver + Anti-corrosion]
    Q1 -- LPG --> LP1[Anti-knock + Odorant]
    Q1 -- Hydrogen --> H1[Flame stabilizer + Lubricity]

    BD1 --> BD2[2-EHN 500-1000 ppm]
    BD1 --> BD3[BHT 500 ppm]
    BD1 --> BD4[EVA 200 ppm]
    CN1 --> CN2[FAME 1-2 percent]
    H1 --> H2[Organic peroxides in traces]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
Define a fuel additive. List any **four** commonly used fuel additives with their primary function.

**Model Answer:**

> A fuel additive is a chemical compound blended in small concentrations (ppm to a few percent) into a base fuel to enhance its properties, improve combustion, reduce emissions, or protect engine components.
>
> Four commonly used additives are:
> 1. **MTBE (Methyl tert-Butyl Ether)** – raises octane number and acts as an oxygenate.
> 2. **2-EHN (2-Ethylhexyl Nitrate)** – improves cetane number of biodiesel/diesel.
> 3. **BHT (Butylated Hydroxytoluene)** – antioxidant; prevents gum formation in biodiesel.
> 4. **Polyether amine** – detergent; keeps injectors and intake valves clean.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
Explain the role of **antioxidants** in alternate fuels such as biodiesel with a suitable example.

**Model Answer:**

> Biodiesel contains unsaturated fatty acid chains (C=C double bonds), making it prone to **auto-oxidation** during storage. This leads to gum, acid formation, and increased viscosity.
>
> **Role of antioxidants:** They donate hydrogen atoms to peroxy radicals (ROO•), converting them to stable hydroperoxides (ROOH), thereby terminating the oxidation chain.
>
> **Example:** BHT (Butylated Hydroxytoluene), used at **500–1000 ppm**, effectively doubles the induction period of biodiesel per the **EN 14112 (Rancimat)** test.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Alert):**
> - Do **NOT** confuse octane number (SI engines) with cetane number (CI engines).
> - Always specify the **dose** (ppm or vol%) of additive discussed; students who omit this lose up to **2 marks**.
> - Never write only the chemical formula — give the **mechanism of action** as well.
> - When asked for comparisons, use a **table** (valuing examiners award extra marks for structured answers).

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Classify fuel additives with suitable examples. Explain the mechanism by which anti-knock additives suppress knock in SI engines.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, describe the role and mechanism of **2-EHN** as a cetane improver in biodiesel. State the typical dosage range.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Fuel additives are classified into four major functional groups:

1. **Performance Improvers:** Anti-knock agents (TEL, MTBE, MMT), cetane improvers (2-EHN), oxygenates (ethanol, methanol).
2. **Stability Improvers:** Antioxidants (BHT, BHA), metal deactivators.
3. **Engine Protection:** Detergents, lubricity improvers, corrosion inhibitors.
4. **Physical Modifiers:** Anti-foam, anti-icing, cold-flow improvers, demulsifiers.

**Mechanism of Anti-Knock Action (TEL example):**

In SI engines, when the end-gas temperature exceeds ~350 °C, peroxy radicals (ROO•) form rapidly, leading to auto-ignition (knock). When TEL is present:

$$Pb(C_2H_5)_4 + O_2 \rightarrow PbO + CO_2 + H_2O$$

The finely divided PbO particles act as **radical scavengers**, terminating the chain reaction by combining with ROO• and HOO• radicals. This delays auto-ignition, allowing smooth flame-front propagation.

**(b)** 2-Ethylhexyl nitrate (2-EHN) is the most widely used cetane improver for biodiesel and Fischer–Tropsch diesel.

**Mechanism:** Above ~250 °C, 2-EHN decomposes into alkoxy radicals and NO₂:

$$C_8H_{17}ONO_2 \rightarrow C_8H_{17}O^\bullet + NO_2$$

These radicals accelerate the **low-temperature pre-flame reactions** of the diesel spray, **shortening the ignition delay** and improving cold-start behaviour.

**Typical dosage:** **500–2000 ppm (0.05–0.2 vol%)**, depending on the base CN.

**Sketch description:** A schematic of the combustion chamber with:
- Injection of biodiesel spray,
- Heat-up and 2-EHN decomposition,
- Radical-induced ignition,
- Controlled smooth combustion without diesel knock.

---

**Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare octane boosters and cetane improvers in terms of **engine type, chemical nature, mechanism, dosage, and examples**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the role of **lubricity improvers** and **corrosion inhibitors** in the context of CNG and methanol as alternate fuels.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Comparison Table:

| Parameter | Octane Booster | Cetane Improver |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| Engine type | SI (Petrol) | CI (Diesel) |
| Goal | Suppress knock | Shorten ignition delay |
| Mechanism | Radical scavenger in end-gas | Radical initiator in pre-flame zone |
| Typical dose | 5–15 vol% | 500–2000 ppm |
| Examples | MTBE, ETBE, Ethanol, TEL, MMT | 2-EHN, DTBP, Amyl nitrate |
| Effect on ON/CN | +5 to +15 ON | +3 to +8 CN |
| Drawback | Phase separation, swelling | Increases NO_x slightly |

**(b)** CNG is dry and **lacks natural lubricity**; in two-stroke CNG engines and gas compressors, this causes rapid wear of sliding components. **Lubricity improvers** such as synthetic esters or 1–2% biodiesel blended into the gas oil circuit (in dual-fuel mode) help form protective tribofilms.

Methanol is highly **polar and hygroscopic**, attacking aluminium, magnesium, and certain elastomers. **Corrosion inhibitors** (amine succinates, alkyl succinic acids, dimer/trimer acids) form a thin passivating film on metal surfaces, protecting fuel lines, injectors, and tank walls.

**Dose:** 50–300 ppm for lubricity; 10–100 ppm for corrosion inhibition.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Fuel additives are small-dose chemicals that enhance fuel properties without altering bulk composition.
- **Octane improvers** → used for SI engines (petrol, ethanol, methanol blends).
- **Cetane improvers** → used for CI engines (biodiesel, FT-diesel).
- **Oxygenates** (MTBE, ETBE, ethanol) supply chemically bound oxygen → reduce CO & HC.
- **2-EHN** at 500–2000 ppm → most common cetane booster for biodiesel.
- **BHT, BHA, TBHQ** → common antioxidants at 200–1000 ppm.
- **MMT (Methylcyclopentadienyl Mn Tricarbonyl)** → modern replacement for TEL.
- **TEL** → banned in most countries due to Pb poisoning; was effective at **0.5–1.0 g/L**.
- **Polyether amines** → detergent additives, dose 50–300 ppm.
- **Biodiesel (1–2%)** → natural lubricity improver for ULSD.
- **Amine succinates** → corrosion inhibitors for methanol/biodiesel systems.
- **Cold-flow improvers (EVA)** → dose 100–500 ppm; reduce CFPP by 5–15 °C.
- **Anti-foam (silicone)** → dose 10–50 ppm; aids in fast refuelling.
- **Anti-icing (isopropanol)** → critical for aviation gasoline (0.1–0.5 vol%).
- **Octane/Cetane duality:** High octane ≠ high cetane. They are inversely related for the same fuel.
- **Dosage notation:** Always use **ppm (mg/kg)** for trace additives and **vol%** for bulk oxygenates.
- **Environmental concern:** Lead, manganese additives are regulated; oxygenates like ethanol are preferred as renewable substitutes.
- **Blending math:** Linear blending is acceptable for oxygenates within ±2 ON accuracy.
- **EN 14112 (Rancimat test):** Standard method to evaluate antioxidant effectiveness in biodiesel (induction period in hours).
- **EN 14214** & **ASTM D6751** biodiesel specifications include antioxidant, lubricity, and cold-flow requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Whenever a question mentions "alternate fuels," immediately link the additive to the *specific drawback of that fuel*. For methanol → corrosion; for biodiesel → oxidation + cold flow; for CNG → lubricity; for hydrogen → flame stability. This demonstrates holistic understanding and earns full marks under the **CO (Course Outcome) mapping** of the 2024 scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alternative Fuels - Advantages and Disadvantages of Conventional Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Alternative Fuels - Advantages and Disadvantages of Conventional Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conventional Fuels (Fossil Fuels)** are hydrocarbon-based energy resources formed over millions of years from the anaerobic decomposition of buried organic matter (ancient flora and fauna) under intense heat and pressure within the Earth's crust. They are termed "conventional" because they have historically been the dominant, commercially exploited primary energy source for transportation, industrial processes, power generation, and domestic use since the Industrial Revolution.

The three principal conventional fuels are:
1. **Petroleum (Crude Oil)** – refined into gasoline, diesel, kerosene, LPG, etc.
2. **Natural Gas** – primarily methane ($\text{CH}_4$)
3. **Coal** – solid hydrocarbon in lignite, bituminous, and anthracite grades

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEMUT615 - Module 2):** Conventional fuels are the historically dominant non-renewable hydrocarbon energy sources whose combustion releases stored chemical energy but also produces greenhouse gases, criteria pollutants, and particulate matter. The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically emphasizes identifying their limitations to justify the transition to alternative fuels.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the Earth as a giant, slow-cooking pressure cooker. Millions of years ago, dead plants and tiny sea creatures sank into muddy seabeds and swampy forests. Buried under layers of rock and sand, they were "cooked" slowly by the planet's internal heat — much like how dal (lentils) turns soft in a pressure cooker after hours of simmering. The resulting "cooked" product — crude oil, gas, and coal — is what we extract today as conventional fuels.

> [!TIP]
> **Simple Takeaway for Students:** Just as you cannot cook dal again from an already-eaten meal (you need fresh ingredients), once we burn these fossil fuels, they cannot be "re-cooked" inside the Earth within any humanly meaningful timescale. This is why they are called **non-renewable**.

### 1.2.1 Key Physical & Stoichiometric Constants

| Parameter | Symbol / Value | Unit | Significance |
|-----------|----------------|------|--------------|
| Calorific Value of Gasoline | **44,000** | kJ/kg | Reference energy density |
| Calorific Value of Diesel | **45,000** | kJ/kg | Slightly higher due to longer HC chains |
| Calorific Value of Natural Gas | **50,000** | kJ/kg | Highest among conventional gaseous fuels |
| Calorific Value of Coal (Bituminous) | **24,000 – 35,000** | kJ/kg | Lowest due to high carbon/ash content |
| Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric, gasoline) | **14.7 : 1** | – | Theoretical combustion requirement |
| Density of Diesel | **~840** | kg/m³ | Heavier than gasoline (~740 kg/m³) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Density Insight:** Conventional liquid fuels offer a high energy-to-volume ratio ($\approx 34$ MJ/L for gasoline), which is why they have been preferred for mobile applications — but this very advantage is offset by the carbon they carry per unit of energy released.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Conventional Fuels

Conventional fuels can be classified based on physical state and origin:

- **By Physical State:**
  - **Solid:** Coal (lignite, bituminous, anthracite), peat, coke
  - **Liquid:** Petroleum products — gasoline, diesel, kerosene, fuel oil
  - **Gaseous:** Natural gas (CNG, LNG), LPG, refinery off-gases

- **By Carbon Content (Rank):** Peat $\rightarrow$ Lignite $\rightarrow$ Bituminous $\rightarrow$ Anthracite (increasing carbon % and energy density)

## 2.2 Why Study Conventional Fuels in an "Alternative Fuels" Course?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Justification:** Before studying alternatives, a KTU engineer must first quantify *why* the existing system is failing. Module 2 begins with conventional fuels so the **limitations are explicitly mapped to the benefits** sought from alternatives (covered in later modules).

## 2.3 Advantages of Conventional Fuels

1. **High Energy Density:** Liquid hydrocarbons like diesel deliver ~$45$ MJ/kg, enabling long driving ranges and compact engine design.
2. **Established Infrastructure:** Petrol bunks, pipelines, refineries, and distribution networks exist globally — switching costs are enormous.
3. **Ease of Storage & Transport:** Liquids can be pumped, stored in tanks, and transported via ships/pipelines efficiently.
4. **Mature Combustion Technology:** Internal combustion engines (SI and CI) are optimized over 130+ years.
5. **Wide Availability (Historically):** Until the 1970s oil crises, supply was perceived as unlimited.
6. **Reliable Ignition & Performance:** Predictable cetane/Octane ratings allow precise engine calibration.

## 2.4 Disadvantages of Conventional Fuels

1. **Non-Renewable / Depleting Reserves:** Crude oil is projected to peak within decades — supply is finite.
2. **Greenhouse Gas Emissions:** Combustion releases $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{CH}_4$ (from leaks), and $\text{N}_2\text{O}$ — driving climate change.
3. **Local Air Pollution:** Emits $\text{CO}$, $\text{NO}_x$, $\text{SO}_x$, unburnt hydrocarbons (UHC), and particulate matter (PM 2.5/PM 10).
4. **Geopolitical Vulnerability:** Oil-rich regions create supply dependencies and price volatility.
5. **Environmental Damage from Extraction:** Oil spills, coal mining strip-mining, and methane leakage.
6. **Health Impacts:** Particulates and benzene are carcinogenic; respiratory diseases (asthma, COPD) correlate with vehicular emissions.
7. **Inefficiency in Engines:** Typical SI engine efficiency is only **25 – 30 %**; CI engines ~**35 – 40 %** — most energy is wasted as heat.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Variables | Application |
|---|---------------------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | $CV = \dfrac{Q}{m}$ | $CV$ = calorific value, $Q$ = heat released (kJ), $m$ = mass of fuel (kg) | Energy content calculation |
| 2 | $AFR_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}}$ (gasoline $\approx 14.7$) | Mass of air per kg fuel | Combustion stoichiometry |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ | $\eta$ = thermal efficiency | Engine performance |
| 4 | $\text{CO}_2 \text{ per kg fuel} = \dfrac{44}{12} \cdot C\%$ | $C\%$ = carbon mass fraction | Emission estimation |
| 5 | $\rho_{\text{fuel}} \cdot V \cdot CV = E_{\text{stored}}$ | $\rho$ = density, $V$ = volume | Tank sizing & range |
| 6 | $\text{Energy released per mole } \text{CH}_4 = 890$ kJ | Stoichiometric $\text{CH}_4$ combustion | Methane calorimetry |

> [!TIP]
> **Use \vert in tables** to avoid markdown parsing errors when writing absolute-value expressions, e.g., $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid v \mid$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Determines why OEMs (Toyota, Tata, Hyundai) are investing in EVs and hydrogen.
- **Power Plants:** Coal-based thermal plants contribute ~$40\%$ of global $\text{CO}_2$ — necessitating CCS (Carbon Capture & Storage).
- **Aviation & Shipping:** Long-haul sectors still depend heavily on liquid fossil fuels due to energy density constraints.
- **Policy Making:** Kyoto Protocol, Paris Agreement, and India's **National Hydrogen Mission** directly address these disadvantages.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Detailed Stoichiometric Combustion Analysis — Gasoline ($\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$ as Octane Surrogate)

> [!NOTE]
> Octane ($\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$) is the standard surrogate molecule used in textbook combustion calculations for gasoline. The balanced reaction is:

### Step 1: Write the Unbalanced Combustion Equation

$$
\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + a\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

### Step 2: Balance Carbon Atoms

Carbon atoms on the left: $8$
Carbon atoms on the right: $b$

$$
b = 8
$$

### Step 3: Balance Hydrogen Atoms

Hydrogen atoms on the left: $18$
Hydrogen atoms on the right (as $\text{H}_2\text{O}$): $2c$

$$
2c = 18 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = 9
$$

### Step 4: Balance Oxygen Atoms

Oxygen atoms on the right: $2b + c = 2(8) + 9 = 25$
Oxygen atoms on the left: $2a$

$$
2a = 25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = 12.5
$$

### Step 5: Final Balanced Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Calculate Theoretical Air Requirement

Air contains $21\%$ $\text{O}_2$ by volume and $23\%$ by mass. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel} &= 12.5 \times 32 \;=\; 400 \;\text{kg O}_2 \\
\text{Mass of air per kg fuel} &= \dfrac{400}{0.23} \;\approx\; 1739 \;\text{kg air}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Using the volumetric factor: $12.5 \times 4.76 = 59.5$ kmol air per kmol fuel, multiplied by $28.97$ kg/kmol gives ~$1724$ kg — minor rounding difference.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Theoretical AFR for octane ≈ **15.13 : 1** (mass basis). For real gasoline, accepted value is **14.7 : 1** due to presence of other hydrocarbons, aromatics, and additives.

## 3.2 Emission Estimation — $\text{CO}_2$ per Litre of Diesel

### Step 1: Density and Energy Content
- Density of diesel: $\rho = 0.85$ kg/L
- Carbon mass fraction in diesel (typical): $C\% \approx 86\%$

### Step 2: Carbon Mass per Litre

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_C &= \rho \cdot C\% = 0.85 \times 0.86 \\
m_C &= 0.731 \;\text{kg C per litre}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Convert Carbon to $\text{CO}_2$ Mass

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Molecular weights: } M_C &= 12 \;\text{g/mol},\;\; M_{\text{CO}_2} = 44 \;\text{g/mol} \\
m_{\text{CO}_2} &= m_C \cdot \dfrac{44}{12} \\
m_{\text{CO}_2} &= 0.731 \times 3.667 \\
m_{\text{CO}_2} &\approx 2.68 \;\text{kg CO}_2 \text{ per litre of diesel}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Per km Emission (Typical Car: 15 km/L)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_2 \text{ per km} &= \dfrac{2.68}{15} \approx 0.179 \;\text{kg/km} \;\approx\; 179 \;\text{g/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A common board question asks students to compare carbon footprint of conventional fuels against alternatives like ethanol, biodiesel, or hydrogen. Remembering the factor **$\dfrac{44}{12} \approx 3.67$** is essential.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Conventional vs. Need for Alternatives

| Parameter | Gasoline | Diesel | Natural Gas | Coal | Desired Alt. Fuel Trait |
|-----------|----------|--------|-------------|------|-------------------------|
| $\text{CO}_2$ emission (kg/GJ) | 69 | 74 | 56 | 95 | Low / near-zero |
| $\text{NO}_x$ (g/km) | 0.06 | 0.80 | 0.02 | N/A | Negligible |
| $\text{PM}$ (g/km) | 0.005 | 0.05 | ~0 | High | Zero |
| Energy density (MJ/L) | 34 | 38 | 0.04 (gas) | 20–30 | High (for mobility) |
| Renewability | No | No | No | No | Yes (sustainable) |
| Sulphur content | Low | Low | Negligible | High | Zero |

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference:** Conventional fuels excel in energy density but fail on **renewability** and **emissions**. Alternative fuels must therefore attempt to "break the trade-off" — e.g., hydrogen has zero tailpipe emissions but low volumetric density (handled via compression or liquefaction).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define conventional fuels. List any two advantages and two disadvantages.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Conventional fuels are naturally occurring hydrocarbon-based energy sources — primarily petroleum, natural gas, and coal — formed over geological timescales from buried organic matter and used as the dominant energy resource since the Industrial Revolution.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. High calorific value and energy density (e.g., diesel ~$45$ MJ/kg), enabling long-range vehicle operation.
2. Well-established extraction, refining, and distribution infrastructure globally.
3. Mature combustion engine technology optimised over more than a century.

**Disadvantages (any two):**
1. Non-renewable — finite reserves projected to deplete within decades.
2. Combustion releases $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{NO}_x$, $\text{SO}_x$, and particulate matter, contributing to climate change and respiratory diseases.
3. Geopolitical price volatility and supply insecurity.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain why conventional fuels are considered non-renewable with a suitable analogy.**

**Model Answer:**

Conventional fuels are formed from the anaerobic decomposition of organic matter (plants, algae, microorganisms) buried under sedimentary rock layers over **millions of years**. Their formation rate is on the order of geological timescales ($\sim 10^6$ – $10^8$ years), whereas human consumption rate extracts and burns them within a few centuries. Therefore, the regeneration timescale far exceeds the consumption timescale, making them effectively **non-renewable**.

> **Analogy:** Think of conventional fuels as a bank account that took millions of years to accumulate, but which humanity is withdrawing from at ATM speed. Once emptied, the account cannot be refilled in any meaningful human timeframe.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Do not** simply list advantages and disadvantages in bullet form — KTU expects **comparison tables, numerical justification, and linkage to alternative fuels** to fetch the full 14 marks.
> - Always mention **units, standard values (e.g., 14.7:1 AFR), and real-world impact figures**.
> - Avoid one-line answers for 7-mark sub-parts — examiners reward **structured, multi-point elaboration**.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Critically evaluate the advantages and disadvantages of conventional fuels. Justify, with quantitative reasoning, the necessity of shifting to alternative fuels."** *(14 Marks)*

#### (a) Advantages of Conventional Fuels with Quantitative Support *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **High Calorific Value:**
   Gasoline: $44{,}000$ kJ/kg; Diesel: $45{,}000$ kJ/kg; Natural Gas: $50{,}000$ kJ/kg. These values are substantially higher than most biomass-derived fuels (e.g., dry wood: $\sim 16{,}000$ kJ/kg), enabling compact and powerful engines.

2. **Mature Infrastructure:**
   The global petroleum infrastructure (refineries, pipelines, retail outlets) represents trillions of dollars of sunk capital. Liquid fuels are easily pumped, stored, and transported, unlike gaseous alternatives (e.g., hydrogen requires $-253^\circ$C liquefaction or $700$ bar compression).

3. **Engine Compatibility:**
   Internal combustion engines (SI/CI) are finely tuned to the combustion characteristics of gasoline and diesel — specific Octane (RON 95 typical) and Cetane (45–55) ratings allow predictable ignition.

4. **High Energy Density per Unit Volume:**
   Diesel stores $\sim 38$ MJ/L. To match this with batteries, one needs $\sim 250$ kg Li-ion pack per 100 km range — a key reason EVs struggle with heavy-duty transport.

5. **Wide Commercial Availability:**
   Until recently, supply was perceived as abundant and prices were historically low (especially pre-1973 oil crisis).

#### (b) Disadvantages and Quantitative Justification for Shift to Alternatives *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Finite Reserves (Quantitative):**
   Global proven oil reserves $\approx 1.7 \times 10^{12}$ barrels. At current consumption ($\sim 100$ million barrels/day), static reserves would last only ~$47$ years. Hence, depletion is mathematically certain within this century.

2. **Greenhouse Gas Emissions:**
   Combustion of 1 litre of diesel releases $\approx 2.68$ kg $\text{CO}_2$ (derived earlier). A car running $15{,}000$ km/year at $15$ km/L emits $\sim 2.68$ tonnes $\text{CO}_2$/year. Globally, transport contributes $\sim 24\%$ of energy-related $\text{CO}_2$ emissions (IEA 2023). This drives the climate emergency.

3. **Local Pollutants:**
   $\text{NO}_x$ reacts with water to form $\text{HNO}_3$ (acid rain); $\text{SO}_x$ forms $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$; PM 2.5 penetrates alveoli causing asthma, COPD, and cardiovascular disease. WHO estimates $\sim 7$ million premature deaths/year from air pollution.

4. **Geopolitical & Economic Instability:**
   1973 OPEC oil crisis quadrupled crude prices in months; 2022 Russia-Ukraine conflict caused similar disruptions. Energy security is a national strategic concern.

5. **Low Engine Efficiency:**
   Even the best diesel engines convert only $\sim 40\%$ of fuel energy to motion; $\sim 60\%$ is wasted as heat. Alternatives like hydrogen fuel cells achieve $\eta > 60\%$.

**Conclusion:** The high energy density of conventional fuels is outweighed by environmental, health, and supply-side vulnerabilities, mandating a structured transition to alternative fuels such as hydrogen, biodiesel, ethanol, and electric propulsion.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"With the help of neat schematics and balanced chemical equations, discuss the combustion of conventional fuels. Compare their emission profiles and explain why this drives the search for alternatives."** *(14 Marks)*

#### (a) Combustion Reactions and Schematics of Conventional Fuels *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Gasoline (Octane surrogate $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\,\text{O}_2 + 47.5\,\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 47.5\,\text{N}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

2. **Diesel (Cetane $\text{C}_{16}\text{H}_{34}$ surrogate):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_{16}\text{H}_{34} + 24.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 16\,\text{CO}_2 + 17\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

3. **Methane (Natural Gas):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -890 \;\text{kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

4. **Coal (Carbon as surrogate):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \;\text{kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Schematic (text representation of SI engine combustion):**
> Air-Fuel Mixture → Compression Stroke → Spark Ignition → Combustion Chamber (high T, P) → Expanding Gases → Piston Work → Exhaust (CO₂, NOₓ, CO, UHC)

#### (b) Emission Profile Comparison and Rationale for Alternatives *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Emission Comparison (per kg of fuel burned, approximate):**

| Pollutant | Gasoline (g) | Diesel (g) | Natural Gas (g) | Coal (g) |
|-----------|--------------|------------|-----------------|----------|
| $\text{CO}_2$ | 3070 | 3200 | 2750 | 2400 |
| CO | 130 | 20 | 25 | negligible (low) |
| $\text{NO}_x$ | 25 | 35 | 20 | 15 |
| $\text{SO}_x$ | ~1 | ~2 | ~0 | ~30 (high S coal) |
| PM | ~0.5 | ~3 | ~0 | ~5 |

**Why this drives the search for alternatives:**

1. All conventional fuels release significant $\text{CO}_2$ — the principal greenhouse gas.
2. Coal is the worst in terms of $\text{SO}_x$ and PM, causing acid rain and respiratory disease.
3. Diesel produces the highest $\text{NO}_x$ and PM per kg fuel — hence BS-VI/Euro 6 norms mandate expensive after-treatment (DPF, SCR, EGR).
4. Natural gas is the cleanest of the conventional set (lowest PM, $\text{SO}_x$) but still emits $\text{CO}_2$ and is a potent greenhouse gas if leaked unburnt ($\text{CH}_4$ has 80x the GWP of $\text{CO}_2$ over 20 years).

**Conclusion:** No conventional fuel is emissions-free. This quantitative comparison underpins the shift toward alternative fuels like hydrogen (zero-carbon combustion) and electric mobility (zero tailpipe).

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Definition:** Conventional fuels = fossil fuels (petroleum, natural gas, coal) — non-renewable, hydrocarbon-based.
- **Three Types:** Solid (coal), Liquid (petroleum products), Gaseous (natural gas, LPG).
- **Calorific Values to Memorise:** Gasoline 44 MJ/kg, Diesel 45 MJ/kg, NG 50 MJ/kg, Coal 24–35 MJ/kg.
- **Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline ≈ 14.7 : 1.**
- **CO₂ emission factor:** 1 litre diesel ≈ 2.68 kg CO₂; conversion factor $\dfrac{44}{12} \approx 3.67$.
- **Advantages:** High energy density, mature infrastructure, easy storage, established engine tech.
- **Disadvantages:** Finite reserves, GHG emissions, local pollutants (NOₓ, SOₓ, PM), geopolitical risk, low engine efficiency.
- **Octane combustion:** $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- **Methane combustion heat:** $890$ kJ/mol (memorise this value).
- **Methane GWP:** 80x that of CO₂ over 20 years (relevant for CNG leakage argument).
- **Engine efficiency benchmark:** SI ~25–30%, CI ~35–40%, Fuel Cell ~60%.
- **Linkage to Module 2 objectives:** Always end answers by *bridging to alternatives* — this is what the examiner expects.
- **Common mistake:** Confusing "renewable" with "sustainable" — biomass is renewable but may not be sustainable if harvested faster than it regrows.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Disadvantages — "FLAWS":**
> **F**inite reserves, **L**ocal pollution, **A**cid rain (SOₓ/NOₓ), **W**eak efficiency, **S**upply insecurity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Alternative Fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Need for Alternative Fuel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)**
> The *need for alternative fuels* refers to the strategic, economic, environmental, and geopolitical imperatives that drive the global transition away from conventional petroleum-based fossil fuels (gasoline and diesel) toward renewable, sustainable, and cleaner energy carriers such as biofuels (ethanol, biodiesel), gaseous fuels (CNG, LPG, hydrogen), and synthetic fuels.

In engineering terms, an **alternative fuel** is any fuel source that is **not derived from conventional crude oil** and which can be used either as a direct substitute (with or without minor engine modifications) or as a blend component in internal combustion engines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Module 2 of *OEMUT615 - Alternate Fuels and Energy Systems* explicitly mandates that the student must be able to critically evaluate **why** conventional fossil fuels are no longer the long-term energy backbone of the transportation and power sectors. This forms the foundation upon which all subsequent modules (biofuels, gaseous fuels, hydrogen, electric mobility) are built.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a household that depends **100% on a single monthly grocery delivery** for survival.

- If the delivery truck **strikes more often** (supply disruptions) → panic.
- If the grocery bill **doubles every few years** (price volatility) → financial stress.
- If the packaging produces **mountains of non-biodegradable waste** (emissions/pollution) → health hazards.
- If the supplier says *"I can only guarantee deliveries for the next 40 years"* (depletion) → existential risk.

A wise household would immediately begin cultivating a **kitchen garden, installing a rainwater harvesting system, and stocking emergency rations** — i.e., developing **alternative sources** before the crisis hits.

The global energy economy is in exactly this situation with crude oil. **Alternative fuels are the household's "Plan B"** — and the urgency to build them is what we call the *Need for Alternative Fuel*.

## 1.3 The Four Pillars of Need

The need for alternative fuels rests on **four universally accepted pillars** that the KTU board examiner expects every student to know:

1. **Resource Depletion** — finite fossil reserves cannot last forever.
2. **Environmental Degradation** — combustion emissions cause global warming and local pollution.
3. **Energy Security** — over-dependence on a few oil-producing nations is geopolitically risky.
4. **Economic Sustainability** — rural employment, agricultural diversification, and import-cost reduction.

These pillars are explored rigorously in the next section.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Resource Depletion of Fossil Fuels

Conventional crude oil, natural gas, and coal are **non-renewable** geological resources formed over millions of years. The most accepted metric used worldwide to quantify how long a fossil reserve will last is the **Reserve-to-Production Ratio (R/P Ratio)**.

**Logic Steps:**

- The **proven reserve (R)** is the quantity of fossil fuel that geological and engineering data confirm with reasonable certainty to be recoverable under existing economic and operating conditions.
- The **annual production (P)** is the rate at which this reserve is being extracted each year.
- Dividing R by P gives a number of years — the **R/P ratio** — assuming production remains constant. This is a **static** indicator; in reality, new discoveries and technology alter the denominator and numerator continuously.
- Globally, the R/P ratio of crude oil is approximately **50 years**, natural gas around **50 years**, and coal around **130 years**.
- Even with optimistic estimates and new discoveries, the world is operating on a **finite "energy countdown clock"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The R/P ratio is a *first-order approximation*. It does **not** account for population growth, rising per-capita energy demand (especially from developing nations like India and China), or peak-oil effects. Hence, real-world depletion is faster than the R/P ratio suggests.

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Environmental Degradation

The combustion of petroleum fuels releases **greenhouse gases (GHG)** and **local pollutants**:

- **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** — the principal GHG; 1 kg of gasoline produces ~**2.3 kg of CO₂**, diesel ~**2.7 kg**, and CNG ~**1.7 kg** (lower due to higher H/C ratio).
- **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — formed under fuel-rich conditions; toxic.
- **Unburned Hydrocarbons (HC)** — smog precursors.
- **Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ)** — formed at high cylinder temperatures; cause acid rain and photochemical smog.
- **Particulate Matter (PM / soot)** — especially high in diesel; linked to respiratory diseases.
- **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** — acid rain, corrodes engine components.

The cumulative effect is **global warming**, **climate change**, **melting polar ice caps**, and **rising sea levels**. The **Paris Agreement (2015)** and India's **Panchamrit** commitments at COP-26 mandate deep decarbonization, making alternative fuels a national priority.

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Energy Security and Geopolitics

India imports **~85%** of its crude oil requirement and **~50%** of its natural gas. This dependence:

- Drains **foreign exchange reserves** (a major component of the import bill).
- Exposes the nation to **price shocks** during geopolitical events (e.g., the 1973 OPEC oil crisis, 1990 Gulf War, 2022 Russia-Ukraine conflict).
- Creates **strategic vulnerability** — supply lines can be weaponized by hostile regimes.

Alternative fuels sourced domestically (biodiesel from Jatropha/Karanja, ethanol from sugarcane, biogas from agricultural waste) **decouple** national energy security from foreign suppliers.

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — Economic and Social Sustainability

- The **Sugarcane-Ethanol** value chain generates rural employment in India, Brazil, and the Philippines.
- **Biodiesel from used cooking oil** solves a municipal waste-disposal problem.
- **Biogas from cattle dung** provides cooking fuel to off-grid rural households and reduces indoor air-pollution deaths.
- The **hydrogen economy** promises to create a new industry vertical with millions of jobs.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners often award full marks only when the student connects each pillar to **India-specific** data (e.g., 85% import dependence, BS-VI emission norms, SATAT scheme, National Hydrogen Mission).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative formula the examiner may test on this topic. All symbols use **SI units**.

| Sl. No. | Metric | Formula / Definition | Units | Engineering Use |
| :-: | :- | :- | :-: | :- |
| 1 | Reserve-to-Production Ratio | $R/P \text{ ratio} = \dfrac{R_{\text{reserve}}}{P_{\text{annual}}}$ | **years** | Estimates how long a fossil reserve will last at current extraction rate. |
| 2 | Reserve Depletion Time | $t_{\text{deplete}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{reserve}}}{P_{\text{annual}}}$ | **years** | Identical to R/P; used in depletion studies. |
| 3 | Calorific (Heating) Value | $CV = \dfrac{Q}{m}$ | **MJ/kg** or **kJ/kg** | Energy released per unit mass of fuel. |
| 4 | Energy Released from Fuel Mass | $E = m \cdot CV$ | **MJ** or **kJ** | Total thermal energy available. |
| 5 | Volumetric Energy Density | $E_v = \rho \cdot CV$ | **MJ/m³** | Useful for gaseous and liquid fuels compared on volume basis. |
| 6 | CO₂ Emission Factor | $m_{\text{CO}_2} = m_{\text{fuel}} \times EF_{\text{CO}_2}$ | **kg CO₂** | Quantifies carbon footprint per kg of fuel burned. |
| 7 | Specific CO₂ Emission | $e_{\text{CO}_2} = \dfrac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{E}$ | **kg CO₂ / MJ** | Normalized carbon intensity per unit energy. |
| 8 | Gasoline Gallon Equivalent (GGE) | $\text{GGE} = \dfrac{\text{Energy of 1 gal gasoline}}{\text{Energy per unit alt. fuel}}$ | **gal-eq** | Compares alternative fuels to gasoline on energy basis. |
| 9 | Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric) | $A/F_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}}$ | **–** (dimensionless) | Determines mixture composition for combustion. |
| 10 | Percentage CO₂ Reduction | $\%\Delta \text{CO}_2 = \dfrac{EF_{\text{conv}} - EF_{\text{alt}}}{EF_{\text{conv}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Quantifies environmental benefit of switching to an alt. fuel. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for Tables:** Absolute-value bars are rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $A/F_{\text{stoich}} = \vert \frac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} \vert$) to avoid breaking markdown pipe-delimited table syntax.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above framework is used by:

- **Auto OEMs** (Tata, Maruti, Toyota, Hyundai) to certify vehicles for BS-VI Phase 2 and beyond.
- **Oil Marketing Companies** (BPCL, IOCL, HPCL) under India's **SATAT** scheme to procure compressed biogas.
- **International Energy Agency (IEA)** to publish *World Energy Outlook* reports.
- **Government policy bodies** (NITI Aayog, Ministry of Petroleum, Ministry of New & Renewable Energy) to design ethanol-blending and E-mobility roadmaps.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Reserve Depletion Time (Static R/P)

**Statement:** A developing nation has proven crude oil reserves of $R = 6.0 \times 10^{9}$ barrels and an annual production of $P = 1.5 \times 10^{8}$ barrels/year. Determine the static reserve-depletion time.

**Step 1 — Identify given quantities and required variable.**

$$R = 6.0 \times 10^{9} \text{ barrels}, \quad P = 1.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ barrels/year}, \quad t_{\text{deplete}} = ?$$

**Step 2 — Write the governing equation.**

$$t_{\text{deplete}} = \frac{R}{P}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values into the equation.**

$$t_{\text{deplete}} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{9} \text{ barrels}}{1.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ barrels/year}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the powers of ten.**

$$\frac{10^{9}}{10^{8}} = 10^{9-8} = 10^{1} = 10$$

**Step 5 — Compute the final numerical answer.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{deplete}} &= \frac{6.0}{1.5} \times 10 \text{ years} \\
&= 4.0 \times 10 \text{ years} \\
&= 40 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.**

At the **current constant rate** of extraction, the proven reserves will be exhausted in **40 years**. This single number is the strongest single-line argument for *why* alternative fuels are not optional — they are a national-survival necessity.

## 3.2 Problem 2 — CO₂ Emission Comparison

**Statement:** Calculate the mass of CO₂ emitted when 1 kg of (a) gasoline, (b) ethanol, and (c) CNG is completely burned, and identify the cleanest fuel.

**Given standard emission factors:**

$$EF_{\text{gasoline}} = 2.31 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kg fuel}$$
$$EF_{\text{ethanol}} = 1.50 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kg fuel}$$
$$EF_{\text{CNG}} = 1.65 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kg fuel} \text{ (lower heating value basis, methane)}$$

**Step 1 — General governing equation.**

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = m_{\text{fuel}} \times EF$$

**Step 2 — For 1 kg of gasoline.**

$$m_{\text{CO}_2, \text{gasoline}} = 1.0 \text{ kg} \times 2.31 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg} = 2.31 \text{ kg CO}_2$$

**Step 3 — For 1 kg of ethanol.**

$$m_{\text{CO}_2, \text{ethanol}} = 1.0 \text{ kg} \times 1.50 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg} = 1.50 \text{ kg CO}_2$$

**Step 4 — For 1 kg of CNG.**

$$m_{\text{CO}_2, \text{CNG}} = 1.0 \text{ kg} \times 1.65 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg} = 1.65 \text{ kg CO}_2$$

**Step 5 — Compare on mass basis.**

$$\text{Order (cleanest to dirtiest):} \quad \text{Ethanol} \; (1.50) \; < \; \text{CNG} \; (1.65) \; < \; \text{Gasoline} \; (2.31)$$

**Step 6 — Refine on energy basis (the more honest comparison).**

Calorific values:

$$CV_{\text{gasoline}} \approx 44 \text{ MJ/kg}, \quad CV_{\text{ethanol}} \approx 27 \text{ MJ/kg}, \quad CV_{\text{CNG}} \approx 50 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

Specific CO₂ emission per MJ:

$$e_{\text{gasoline}} = \frac{2.31}{44} \approx 0.0525 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{MJ}$$
$$e_{\text{ethanol}} = \frac{1.50}{27} \approx 0.0556 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{MJ}$$
$$e_{\text{CNG}} = \frac{1.65}{50} \approx 0.0330 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{MJ}$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation.**

On an **energy-normalized basis**, CNG is decisively the cleanest, followed by gasoline, with ethanol slightly dirtier **per MJ delivered** (because of its lower heating value). However, if ethanol is produced from sugarcane, the **biogenic carbon** it contains is re-absorbed by the next crop — making it **carbon-neutral over a full life-cycle**, which gasoline and CNG can never be.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Watch Point:** Students often compare fuels only on **mass of CO₂ per kg of fuel** and incorrectly conclude ethanol is the cleanest. On a **per-km-driven** or **per-MJ-delivered** basis the ranking changes. Always normalize to the *useful work* delivered.

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Energy Equivalence (Gasoline Gallon Equivalent)

**Statement:** CNG has a calorific value of $CV_{\text{CNG}} = 50 \text{ MJ/kg}$ and a density of $\rho_{\text{CNG}} = 0.72 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$ (at 200 bar storage). Gasoline has $CV_{\text{gas}} = 44 \text{ MJ/kg}$ and $\rho_{\text{gas}} = 740 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$. How many kg of CNG contain the same energy as 1 US gallon (3.785 L) of gasoline?

**Step 1 — Mass of 1 US gallon of gasoline.**

$$m_{\text{gas}} = \rho_{\text{gas}} \times V = 740 \text{ kg/m}^{3} \times 3.785 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3} = 2.8009 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Total energy in 1 gallon of gasoline.**

$$E_{\text{gas}} = m_{\text{gas}} \times CV_{\text{gas}} = 2.8009 \text{ kg} \times 44 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

$$E_{\text{gas}} = 123.24 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of CNG with equivalent energy.**

$$m_{\text{CNG, eq}} = \frac{E_{\text{gas}}}{CV_{\text{CNG}}} = \frac{123.24 \text{ MJ}}{50 \text{ MJ/kg}} = 2.4648 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Volume of that CNG at 200 bar storage.**

$$V_{\text{CNG}} = \frac{m_{\text{CNG, eq}}}{\rho_{\text{CNG}}} = \frac{2.4648 \text{ kg}}{0.72 \text{ kg/m}^{3}} = 3.4234 \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

To deliver the energy of **1 gallon of gasoline** (a tank that fits in a palm), a vehicle needs roughly **3.42 m³ of CNG** — about the volume of a small refrigerator. This is the **single biggest reason** why gaseous fuels require high-pressure cylinders: their volumetric energy density is intrinsically low. This result powerfully demonstrates **why we cannot just "swap fuels"** in an engine — infrastructure, storage, and energy density constraints force a holistic redesign.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of Conventional vs. Alternative Fuels

| Property | Gasoline | Diesel | Ethanol (E100) | Biodiesel (B100) | CNG | Hydrogen (H₂) |
| :- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| Source | Crude oil | Crude oil | Sugarcane, corn, cellulosic biomass | Vegetable oils, used cooking oil, algae | Natural gas, biogas, landfill gas | Water electrolysis, natural gas reforming |
| Calorific Value (MJ/kg) | **44** | **43** | **27** | **38** | **50** | **120** (LHV) |
| Density (kg/m³) | **740** | **840** | **789** | **880** | **0.72** (200 bar) | **71** (700 bar) |
| CO₂ EF (kg CO₂/kg fuel) | 2.31 | 2.68 | 1.50 | 2.50 | 1.65 | **0** (at tailpipe) |
| Renewability | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (fossil) / ✓ (biogas) | ✓ (green H₂) |
| Sulphur content | Low (BS-VI) | Low (BS-VI) | **Negligible** | **Negligible** | **Negligible** | **Zero** |
| Storage form | Liquid | Liquid | Liquid | Liquid | High-pressure gas | Cryo / high-pressure gas |
| Engine modification needed | – | – | Minor (corrosion-resistant parts) | Minor (heater, seals) | Significant (CNG kit) | Major (fuel cell / H₂-ICE) |
