# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Drivers → Outcomes Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following block diagram maps how the **four pillars of need** flow into **engineering outcomes** and **policy responses**. This is the type of high-yield figure examiners reward in 14-mark descriptive answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pillar1[PILLAR 1 RESOURCE DEPLETION Finite R/P Ratio of Crude Oil Natural Gas Coal]
    pillar2[PILLAR 2 ENVIRONMENTAL CONCERN CO2 CO NOx SOx Particulate Matter]
    pillar3[PILLAR 3 ENERGY SECURITY 85 percent Crude Import Dependence Geopolitical Risk]
    pillar4[PILLAR 4 ECONOMIC AND SOCIAL Rural Employment Import Bill Reduction]

    pillar1 --> need[NEED FOR ALTERNATIVE FUELS Strategic Imperative]
    pillar2 --> need
    pillar3 --> need
    pillar4 --> need

    need --> resp1[POLICY RESPONSE BS VI Norms Ethanol Blending SATAT Hydrogen Mission]
    need --> resp2[TECHNICAL RESPONSE CNG LPG Engines Biofuel ICE Fuel Cell EV]
    need --> resp3[INFRASTRUCTURE RESPONSE Charging Stations CBG Plants H2 Corridors]
    need --> resp4[SOCIAL RESPONSE Skill Development Rural Jobs Farmer Income]

    resp1 --> out[OUTCOME Sustainable Low Carbon Energy Future]
    resp2 --> out
    resp3 --> out
    resp4 --> out
```

## 4.2 Energy Substitution Hierarchy

This second diagram shows the **logical substitution chain** a KTU examiner expects when asked "Why alternative fuels?" — the student should demonstrate the layered logic from *least disruptive* to *most disruptive* change.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Conventional Petroleum Fuels Gasoline and Diesel] --> B[BLEND STAGE E5 E10 E20 Ethanol Blends B5 B20 Biodiesel Blends]
    B --> C[DEDICATED ALT FUELS E100 B100 CBG PNG LPG]
    C --> D[ADVANCED ALT FUELS Hydrogen Synthetic Biofuels E Fuels]
    D --> E[POST COMBUSTION Battery Electric Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles]
    A --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Vehicles and engines do not jump directly from gasoline to hydrogen or electric power. The transition is **gradual**, with **blending** as the entry point, **dedicated alternative fuels** as the intermediate stage, and **hydrogen / electric** as the long-term destination. This is precisely the pathway outlined in India's *National Hydrogen Energy Mission* and the *National Biofuels Policy 2018*.

## 4.3 Carbon Life-Cycle Schematic

The block below explains **why tailpipe CO₂ is not the full story** — a key concept that earns extra marks in KTU descriptive answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    well[Well to Tank Crude Extraction Refining Transport]
    tank[Tank to Wheel Combustion in Engine]
    well -->|CO2 emitted during refining| atmos[Atmosphere CO2 Pool]
    tank -->|Tailpipe CO2| atmos

    bioWell[Biomass Cultivation absorbs CO2 from air]
    bioWell -->|Photosynthesis| crop[Sugarcane Oilseeds Algae]
    crop -->|Extraction Conversion| bioTank[Biofuel E100 B100]
    bioTank -->|Combustion| atmos
    atmos -->|Re absorbed by next season crop| bioWell
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dashed circular link in the right-hand branch (Biofuel loop) represents the **closed biogenic carbon cycle**, which is the fundamental reason biofuels are *carbon-neutral* even though they emit CO₂ at the tailpipe. Conventional fossil fuels break this cycle and **add net new carbon** to the atmosphere.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define the term *Reserve-to-Production Ratio (R/P)*. Mention its significance in deciding the need for alternative fuels.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The Reserve-to-Production Ratio is the ratio of the proven remaining reserves of a fossil fuel to its current annual production rate, expressed in **years**.
- **Formula (1 mark):**

$$R/P \text{ ratio} = \frac{R_{\text{reserve}}}{P_{\text{annual}}} \quad [\text{years}]$$

- **Significance (1 mark):** It provides a **static estimate** of how long a fossil reserve will last at the current extraction rate. A low R/P ratio (e.g., 40–50 years for crude oil globally) signals **imminent depletion** and justifies the urgent transition to renewable alternative fuels.

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**List any **six** environmental pollutants emitted by conventional SI and CI engines and state their primary health impact.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**

| Sl. No. | Pollutant | Primary Health / Environmental Impact |
| :-: | :- | :- |
| 1 | Carbon Monoxide (CO) | Binds to haemoglobin; reduces oxygen-carrying capacity of blood; causes hypoxia. |
| 2 | Unburned Hydrocarbons (HC) | Smog formation, ground-level ozone, eye irritation. |
| 3 | Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ) | Acid rain, photochemical smog, respiratory ailments. |
| 4 | Particulate Matter (PM / soot) | Lung cancer, asthma, cardiovascular disease. |
| 5 | Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) | Global warming, climate change, sea-level rise. |
| 6 | Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂) | Acid rain, corrosion of metals, bronchitis. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Students often write "smoke" or "lead" as modern pollutants. Since the advent of **unleaded** petrol and **ultra-low-sulphur BS-VI** fuels, lead and SO₂ are no longer the primary concerns. Stick to the **BS-VI pollutant list** above.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**A. (a)** Explain in detail the **four major reasons** that necessitate the search for alternative fuels in the Indian context. **(7 marks)**

**A. (b)** With the help of a comparative table, discuss how **ethanol, biodiesel, and CNG** differ from conventional diesel in terms of source, calorific value, CO₂ emission factor, and renewability. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Major Reasons — 7 marks (1.5 + 1.5 + 2 + 2 distribution):**

**Reason 1 — Depletion of Fossil Reserves (1.5 marks):**
India's proven crude oil reserves correspond to an R/P ratio of less than 20 years for domestic production, while global R/P is ~50 years. As domestic production plateaus, import dependence rises, making the energy supply chain structurally fragile.

**Reason 2 — Environmental Concerns (1.5 marks):**
Vehicular emissions in Indian metros (Delhi, Mumbai, Bengaluru) routinely breach WHO PM2.5 limits. The shift to **BS-VI norms**, **E20 ethanol blending** by 2025, and the **National Hydrogen Mission** are direct policy responses to this environmental pillar.

**Reason 3 — Energy Security (2 marks):**
India imports ~85% of its crude oil and ~50% of its natural gas, draining foreign exchange and exposing the nation to OPEC-supply manipulations (1973 crisis, 1990 Gulf War, 2022 Ukraine war). Domestic biofuels (ethanol from sugarcane, biodiesel from used cooking oil, CBG from agricultural residue) decouple the energy supply from geopolitics.

**Reason 4 — Economic and Social Sustainability (2 marks):**
- The **E20 programme** is expected to divert ~10 million tonnes of crude oil imports annually, saving ~₹30,000 crore in foreign exchange.
- It generates **rural income** for ~7 crore sugarcane farmers.
- The **SATAT scheme** targets 5,000 CBG plants by 2025–26, creating **75,000+ direct jobs**.
- Hence alternative fuels are not just a technological option but a **rural-economy policy lever**.

**(b) Comparative Table — 7 marks (1.5 for table + 5.5 for 4 rows × ~1.4 each):**

| Property | Diesel (Conventional) | Ethanol (E100) | Biodiesel (B100) | CNG |
| :- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| **Source** | Crude oil (fossil, non-renewable) | Sugarcane molasses, corn, cellulosic biomass (renewable) | Vegetable oils (soybean, palm, Jatropha, Karanja), used cooking oil, algae (renewable) | Natural gas (fossil) / Biogas (renewable) |
| **Calorific Value (MJ/kg)** | **43** | **27** | **38** | **50** |
| **CO₂ Emission Factor (kg CO₂/kg fuel)** | **2.68** | **1.50** | **2.50** (but life-cycle carbon-neutral if from biomass) | **1.65** |
| **Renewability** | **No** | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Fossil: No / Biogas: Yes** |
| **Sulphur content** | <10 ppm (BS-VI) | Negligible | Negligible | Negligible |
| **Storage state** | Liquid | Liquid | Liquid | High-pressure gas |
| **Engine modification** | – | Minor (corrosion-resistant fuel system) | Minor (heater, elastomer seals) | Significant (CNG kit, pressure regulator) |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** For the 7-mark table, award **1.5 marks** for the table structure (rows × columns chosen) and distribute the remaining **5.5 marks** evenly across the four key properties asked. Mentioning **BS-VI sulphur limits** and **biogenic carbon neutrality** of biodiesel earns bonus marks.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**B. (a)** A country has proven crude oil reserves of $R = 8.0 \times 10^{9}$ barrels and an annual production of $P = 2.0 \times 10^{8}$ barrels/year. Calculate the static reserve-depletion time. If the country decides to substitute 25% of its petroleum consumption with ethanol (calorific value 27 MJ/kg) and the ethanol has 70% of the energy content of gasoline (44 MJ/kg) per unit mass, calculate the mass of ethanol required to deliver the same energy as **10,000 tonnes** of gasoline. **(7 marks)**

**B. (b)** Discuss the **role of biofuels and gaseous fuels** in reducing the carbon footprint of the Indian transportation sector, citing specific government initiatives. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical Solution — 7 marks (3 + 4 distribution):**

**Part 1 — Static reserve-depletion time (3 marks):**

$$t_{\text{deplete}} = \frac{R}{P} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{9} \text{ barrels}}{2.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ barrels/year}}$$

$$t_{\text{deplete}} = 40 \text{ years}$$

**Engineering interpretation (0.5 mark, included in the 3):** At current production, the reserves will last only **40 years** — strong justification for alternative fuels.

**Part 2 — Mass of ethanol required (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Energy content of 10,000 tonnes of gasoline.**

$$E_{\text{gas}} = m_{\text{gas}} \times CV_{\text{gas}} = 10{,}000 \times 10^{3} \text{ kg} \times 44 \text{ MJ/kg} = 4.4 \times 10^{8} \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 2 — Energy density ratio of ethanol to gasoline.**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{CV_{\text{ethanol}}}{CV_{\text{gasoline}}} = \frac{27}{44} \approx 0.6136$$

**Step 3 — Mass of ethanol required for equal energy (inverse of the ratio).**

$$m_{\text{ethanol}} = \frac{m_{\text{gas}}}{\text{Ratio}} = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ tonnes}}{0.6136} \approx 16{,}297 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 4 — Express in proper units and interpret.**

$$m_{\text{ethanol}} \approx 1.63 \times 10^{4} \text{ tonnes} \approx 16{,}300 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Interpretation (0.5 mark):** To replace **10,000 tonnes of gasoline** on an energy-equivalent basis, **~16,300 tonnes of ethanol** are required — about **1.63 times** the gasoline mass. This higher mass requirement is the direct consequence of ethanol's lower calorific value.

**(b) Role of Biofuels and Gaseous Fuels in India — 7 marks (1 + 2 + 2 + 2 distribution):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
India's transportation sector accounts for ~14% of national CO₂ emissions. Biofuels (ethanol, biodiesel, CBG) and gaseous fuels (CNG, PNG, hydrogen) offer the most **drop-in feasible** decarbonization pathways for the existing vehicle fleet.

**Biofuels — Ethanol (2 marks):**
- India's **Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme** targets **E20** (20% ethanol blending) by **2025** (achieved ahead of schedule in select states).
- Feedstocks: sugarcane molasses, sugar, corn, rice, and surplus foodgrains.
- Outcomes: ~₹30,000 crore annual forex savings; ~7 crore farmer incomes boosted; ~10 million tonnes of import substitution.

**Biofuels — Biodiesel & CBG (2 marks):**
- **SATAT** (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation) targets **5,000 Compressed Biogas plants** by 2025–26.
- **Repurpose Used Cooking Oil (RUCO)** initiative collects waste cooking oil from hotels and households to produce biodiesel.
- Biodiesel blends (**B5, B7, B20**) reduce tailpipe PM and HC significantly.

**Gaseous Fuels — CNG, PNG & Hydrogen (2 marks):**
- India has **5+ crore CNG vehicles** across Delhi, Mumbai, Gujarat, etc. CNG emits ~20% less CO₂ and 70% less NOₓ than gasoline.
- **National Hydrogen Mission (2021)** with ₹19,744 crore outlay targets **5 million tonnes of green hydrogen** by 2030, primarily for transport and steel.
- **Hydrogen highways** and **fuel-cell bus pilots** (e.g., Delhi, Kerala) are operational.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the R/P ratio with the production rate** — they are different; R/P is years, P is barrels/year.
> 2. **Comparing fuels only by mass-based CO₂** — always normalize to **per-MJ** or **per-km**.
> 3. **Forgetting the unit conversion** of $10^{3}$ when moving from tonnes to kg in numerical problems.
> 4. **Ignoring the biogenic carbon cycle** — biofuels are carbon-neutral **over a full crop-to-combustion cycle**, not at the tailpipe alone.
> 5. **Quoting outdated data** — use **post-2020** statistics (BS-VI 2020, E20 2023, Hydrogen Mission 2021), not pre-2010 numbers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The four pillars of need:** Resource Depletion, Environmental Concern, Energy Security, Economic & Social Sustainability.
- **R/P ratio** is the master metric for fossil depletion; globally ~50 years for crude oil, ~130 years for coal.
- **Alternative fuels are classified** into biofuels (ethanol, biodiesel, biogas), gaseous fuels (CNG, LPG, H₂), and synthetic fuels (e-fuels, methanol).
- **Calorific values to memorize:** Gasoline 44, Diesel 43, Ethanol 27, Biodiesel 38, CNG 50, Hydrogen 120 MJ/kg.
- **CO₂ emission factors (kg CO₂/kg fuel):** Gasoline 2.31, Diesel 2.68, Ethanol 1.50, Biodiesel 2.50, CNG 1.65, Hydrogen 0 (tailpipe).
- **Ethanol E20** programme is India's flagship biofuel policy; **SATAT** is the gaseous-fuel equivalent.
- **National Hydrogen Mission (₹19,744 crore, 2021)** targets 5 MT green H₂ by 2030.
- **Always normalize** fuel comparisons to **per MJ** or **per km**, never to **per kg** alone.
- **Biogenic carbon cycle** makes biofuels *carbon-neutral over their life-cycle* — the most-tested KTU concept.
- **BS-VI norms** (2020) and **COP-26 Panchamrit commitments** are the regulatory backbone driving the shift.
- **Common board-exam pitfall:** Writing *petroleum is infinite* or *India is self-sufficient in crude* — both are factually wrong and cost marks.
- **The 40-year depletion number is your trump card** — quote it whenever the question asks *why* alternative fuels are needed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Gaseous Alternate Fuels - LPG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Gaseous Alternate Fuels - LPG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)** is a flammable mixture of hydrocarbon gases, predominantly **propane (C₃H₈)** and **butane (C₄H₁₀)**, which is liquefied under modest pressure (typically **0.7 MPa to 1.5 MPa**) at ambient temperature or moderate cooling for ease of storage, transport, and handling. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme classification of gaseous alternate fuels, LPG belongs to the category of **"Liquefied Gaseous Hydrocarbon Fuels"** derived as a by-product of natural gas processing and petroleum refining (crude oil distillation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):** LPG, along with CNG, LNG, biogas, hydrogen, and producer gas, forms the core of the *Types of Gaseous Alternate Fuels* unit. Expect definitional and comparison-based questions worth 3 to 7 marks.

### 1.2 Composition of LPG

| Component | Chemical Formula | Typical Proportion |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|
| Propane | C₃H₈ | 30% – 95% |
| n-Butane | n-C₄H₁₀ | 5% – 60% |
| Isobutane | i-C₄H₁₀ | 0% – 30% |
| Propylene | C₃H₆ | 0% – 5% |
| Butylene | C₄H₈ | 0% – 5% |
| Ethane / Methane (traces) | C₂H₆, CH₄ | < 1% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pure Forms in Market:** Commercial LPG in India is mostly a **propane-rich mix (≈60% C₃H₈ + 40% C₄H₁₀)** used for domestic cooking and automotive applications. The Indian Standard **IS 4576** governs its specifications.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of LPG like a **"spongy pressurized canister of cooking gas"** — the gas you see burning on a kitchen stove. Imagine a pressurized water bottle: water (the liquid fuel) is squeezed inside, but if you release the nozzle, it instantly vaporizes into a powerful, invisible gas.

Analogy Mapping for Students:
- **Kitchen Cylinder Analogy:** The LPG cylinder you see at home holds liquid fuel inside, but the moment gas exits the regulator, it vaporizes into a clean-burning gas.
- **Backpacker Analogy:** LPG is like packing a sleeping bag into a tiny pouch — the fuel's "energy" is compressed (liquefied) for transport, then "unfolds" (vaporizes) when released.
- **Engine Analogy:** When injected into an SI engine, LPG behaves like a high-octane petrol vapour — burning smoothly, producing less carbon deposits, and reducing knocking.

### 1.4 Why LPG is Considered a "Gaseous" Alternate Fuel

Although stored as a liquid, LPG is **used as a gas**. Upon depressurization (Joule-Thomson expansion through the regulator), it vaporizes instantly at ambient temperatures because its boiling point is below 0 °C at atmospheric pressure:
- Propane boiling point: **−42 °C**
- n-Butane boiling point: **−0.5 °C**

This dual-phase nature (liquid for storage, gas for combustion) is the **key engineering advantage** of LPG over purely gaseous fuels like CNG, which requires ultra-high pressure (20–25 MPa) cylinders.

### 1.5 Physical & Thermodynamic Identity (Standard Metrics)

The following physical constants are essential for KTU numerical problems:

- **Density (liquid at 15 °C):** ≈ **0.51 – 0.54 kg/L**
- **Density (gas at 15 °C, 1 atm):** ≈ **1.8 – 2.0 kg/m³**
- **Specific gravity of vapour (relative to air):** ≈ **1.5 – 2.0**
- **Calorific Value (HHV):** ≈ **46 – 50 MJ/kg**
- **Calorific Value (LHV):** ≈ **46 MJ/kg**
- **Air-Fuel Ratio (stoichiometric):** ≈ **15.2 – 15.6 : 1** (by mass)
- **Octane Number (RON):** ≈ **100 – 110**
- **Research Octane Number (RON) of pure propane:** ≈ **112**
- **Research Octane Number (RON) of pure butane:** ≈ **93 – 95**
- **Flame Speed:** ≈ **0.82 m/s** (slower than petrol)
- **Auto-ignition Temperature:** ≈ **420 – 450 °C**
- **Flammability Limits in air:** **2.1% – 9.6%** (by volume)
- **Vapour pressure at 40 °C:** ≈ **0.6 – 1.4 MPa**
- **Latent heat of vaporization:** ≈ **425 kJ/kg**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** LPG has a **high octane rating (≈100+)**, which allows **higher compression ratios** in SI engines without knock, leading to better thermal efficiency than gasoline.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Source & Production Pathways

LPG is **not synthesized**; it is **recovered** from two primary sources:

**Pathway A — From Natural Gas Processing (Wet Gas):**
1. Raw natural gas from wells contains heavier hydrocarbons (ethane, propane, butane).
2. Gas is passed through **oil absorption towers** or **cryogenic expansion plants**.
3. NGL (Natural Gas Liquids) are separated by fractional distillation.
4. LPG fraction (C₃ + C₄) is recovered.

**Pathway B — From Crude Oil Refining:**
1. Crude oil is distilled in **Atmospheric Distillation Column (ADC)**.
2. The overhead gases and naphtha stream contain dissolved C₃ and C₄ hydrocarbons.
3. These are recovered in **FCC (Fluid Catalytic Cracker)** units as **LPG-rich off-gas**.
4. Gas is sweetened (H₂S removal via amine treatment) and dried.

**Pathway C — As a By-product of Ethylene Crackers:**
- Steam cracking of naphtha produces ethylene + LPG by-products.

### 2.2 Stoichiometric Combustion Chemistry

The combustion equations of LPG's two primary constituents are:

**Propane Combustion:**
$$C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 3CO_2 + 4H_2O + \text{Heat}$$

**n-Butane Combustion:**
$$2C_4H_{10} + 13O_2 \rightarrow 8CO_2 + 10H_2O + \text{Heat}$$

**Air-based stoichiometry (using O₂ = 1/5 of air by volume):**

For 1 kg of propane:
$$C_3H_8 : \text{Molar mass} = (3 \times 12) + (8 \times 1) = 44 \, g/mol$$
$$N_{O_2} = 5 \text{ moles}, \quad m_{O_2} = 5 \times 32 = 160 \, g$$
$$m_{air} = \frac{160}{0.232} = 689.66 \, g$$
$$\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich,propane} = 15.67 : 1}$$

For 1 kg of n-butane:
$$C_4H_{10} : \text{Molar mass} = 58 \, g/mol$$
$$N_{O_2} = 6.5 \text{ moles}, \quad m_{O_2} = 6.5 \times 32 = 208 \, g$$
$$m_{air} = \frac{208}{0.232} = 896.55 \, g$$
$$\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich,butane} = 15.45 : 1}$$

**Average A/F ratio for LPG ≈ 15.5 : 1**

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No | Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Notes for KTU |
|------|-----------|-----------------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Stoichiometric A/F (LPG) | $(A/F)_s = \dfrac{1}{0.232} \times \left[ \dfrac{32(x+y/4)}{12x+y} \right]$ | kg air / kg fuel | $x$, $y$ = C and H atoms |
| 2 | Calorific Value (HHV) | $CV = \dfrac{337 C + 1418 \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 23 S}{1000}$ | MJ/kg | **Dulong's formula** |
| 3 | Energy Density (liquid LPG) | $E_d = CV \times \rho_l$ | MJ/L | $\rho_l \approx 0.54$ kg/L |
| 4 | Energy released per m³ of gas | $E_g = CV \times \rho_g$ | MJ/m³ | $\rho_g \approx 1.9$ kg/m³ |
| 5 | LPG vaporization energy | $Q_v = m \cdot L_v$, $L_v \approx 425$ kJ/kg | kJ | Latent heat requirement |
| 6 | Compression ratio gain | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | — | Higher octane → higher $r$ |
| 7 | Volumetric efficiency gain | $\eta_v = \dfrac{\rho_{air}}{\rho_{gas}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(A/F)}$ | — | LPG displaces air → derating |
| 8 | Equivalent Petrol Ratio (EPR) | $EPR = \dfrac{CV_{LPG}}{CV_{petrol}} \times \dfrac{(A/F)_{petrol}}{(A/F)_{LPG}}$ | — | ≈ 1.2 – 1.5 |
| 9 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{m_f \cdot CV}$ | % | Standard BTE formula |
| 10 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{m_f \cdot CV}{P_b}$ | kJ/kWh | Lower = better |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid Pipe Symbol Trap:** In the formula sheet, all "divided by" or absolute-value notations are written with proper LaTeX spacing. When students reproduce these in KTU answer sheets, they should use the **`÷`** or **`/`** symbol and avoid raw `|` which breaks markdown.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Automotive Sector:** LPG powers approximately **30 million vehicles worldwide** (India alone has ~3 million LPG cars), primarily as a retrofit dual-fuel system.
2. **Domestic Heating:** Over **300 million Indian households** use LPG cylinders under the **PMUY (Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala Yojana)** scheme.
3. **Industrial Heating:** Metal cutting, brazing, and ceramic firing.
4. **Refrigeration:** R290 (pure propane) and R600a (isobutane) are eco-friendly refrigerants replacing CFCs/HFCs.
5. **Power Generation:** Standalone LPG gensets for remote locations.
6. **Aerosol Propellant:** LPG replaces CFCs in pharmaceutical aerosols.

### 2.5 Why LPG is Preferred Over CNG in Some Applications

| Aspect | LPG | CNG |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Storage pressure | 0.7–1.5 MPa | 20–25 MPa |
| Energy density (liquid/gas) | High | Low |
| Cylinder weight | Lighter | Very heavy |
| Refueling infrastructure | Easy | Specialized |
| Vehicle range per fill | Moderate | Lower |
| Cost per km | Lower | Higher |

> LPG wins where **energy density and infrastructure simplicity** matter; CNG wins where **storage pressure is acceptable** (e.g., heavy-duty city buses).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Pure Propane

**Given:** Combustion reaction $C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 3CO_2 + 4H_2O$

**Step 1 — Determine molar masses.**
$$M_{C_3H_8} = (3 \times 12) + (8 \times 1) = 36 + 8 = 44 \, g/mol$$
$$M_{O_2} = 2 \times 16 = 32 \, g/mol$$
$$M_{air} = 28.97 \, g/mol \approx 29 \, g/mol$$
$$\text{Mass fraction of } O_2 \text{ in air} = \dfrac{32}{29} \times \dfrac{1}{4.76} = 0.232$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric O₂ requirement per kg of fuel.**
$$n_{C_3H_8} = \frac{1000 \, g}{44 \, g/mol} = 22.727 \, mol$$
$$n_{O_2,stoich} = 5 \times 22.727 = 113.636 \, mol$$
$$m_{O_2} = 113.636 \times 32 = 3636.36 \, g = 3.636 \, kg$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric air requirement.**
$$m_{air} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \frac{3.636}{0.232} = 15.674 \, kg$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric A/F ratio.**
$$\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich} = 15.67 : 1}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** LPG requires roughly **15.67 kg of air** to combust 1 kg of fuel stoichiometrically. This is comparable to petrol (14.7:1) but leaner than diesel (14.5:1). Because LPG displaces some intake air (gaseous fuel enters manifold), engines are typically **derated by 5–10%**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Energy Equivalence — 1 Litre of LPG vs. 1 Litre of Petrol

**Given Data:**
- $\rho_{LPG,liquid} = 0.54$ kg/L
- $CV_{LPG} = 46.0$ MJ/kg (LHV)
- $\rho_{petrol} = 0.74$ kg/L
- $CV_{petrol} = 44.0$ MJ/kg (LHV)

**Step 1 — Mass of 1 L of LPG.**
$$m_{LPG} = 1 \times 0.54 = 0.54 \, kg$$

**Step 2 — Energy in 1 L of LPG.**
$$E_{LPG} = m_{LPG} \times CV_{LPG} = 0.54 \times 46.0 = 24.84 \, MJ$$

**Step 3 — Mass of 1 L of petrol.**
$$m_{petrol} = 1 \times 0.74 = 0.74 \, kg$$

**Step 4 — Energy in 1 L of petrol.**
$$E_{petrol} = m_{petrol} \times CV_{petrol} = 0.74 \times 44.0 = 32.56 \, MJ$$

**Step 5 — LPG-to-Petrol Energy Equivalence Ratio.**
$$\boxed{\text{Equivalence} = \frac{E_{LPG}}{E_{petrol}} = \frac{24.84}{32.56} = 0.763}$$

> **Interpretation:** 1 L of LPG delivers only **76.3% of the energy of 1 L of petrol**. Therefore, **1.31 L of LPG ≈ 1 L of petrol** in terms of energy content. This is why LPG vehicles show **lower range per tank volume** but are still economical due to **lower per-kg fuel cost**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Brake Thermal Efficiency of an LPG-fuelled Engine

**Given:** An LPG engine delivers **brake power = 30 kW**, fuel mass flow rate = **7 kg/h**, $CV_{LPG} = 46$ MJ/kg.

**Step 1 — Convert fuel flow to kg/s.**
$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{7}{3600} = 0.001944 \, kg/s$$

**Step 2 — Heat input rate.**
$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV_{LPG} = 0.001944 \times 46{,}000 = 89.42 \, kW$$

**Step 3 — Brake Thermal Efficiency.**
$$\boxed{\eta_{bth} = \frac{P_b}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{30}{89.42} = 0.3355 = 33.55\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** A typical LPG SI engine gives **BTE ≈ 30–35%**, slightly higher than petrol due to higher octane enabling higher compression ratio.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Volumetric Efficiency Loss in LPG Mode

**Scenario:** A 1.5 L petrol engine operates in LPG mode. Petrol A/F = 14.7, LPG A/F = 15.6.

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric air requirement for petrol per cycle.**
$$m_{air,petrol} = 14.7 \times m_f$$

**Step 2 — In LPG mode, fuel enters as gas displacing air.**
If fuel is **5% by volume** of intake mixture (typical LPG setup):
$$m_{air,LPG,available} = 0.95 \times m_{air,total}$$

**Step 3 — Effect on energy per cycle.**
$$E_{LPG,cycle} = \left( \frac{0.95}{15.6} \right) \times CV_{LPG}$$
$$E_{petrol,cycle} = \left( \frac{1.0}{14.7} \right) \times CV_{petrol}$$

**Step 4 — Derating Factor.**
$$\boxed{DF = \frac{E_{LPG}}{E_{petrol}} = \frac{0.95 \times 14.7 \times CV_{LPG}}{15.6 \times CV_{petrol}} = \frac{0.95 \times 14.7 \times 46}{15.6 \times 44} = 0.935}$$

> **Interpretation:** LPG engines suffer a **~6.5% derating** compared to petrol mode — confirmed by field data on retrofitted LPG cars.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: LPG vs. Other Gaseous Fuels

| Property | LPG | CNG | LNG | Hydrogen | Biogas |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|----------|--------|
| State (stored) | Liquid | Gas (CNG) | Cryogenic liquid | Gas / liquid | Gas |
| Pressure | 0.7–1.5 MPa | 20–25 MPa | 0.1 MPa (cryo) | 35–70 MPa | ~0.1 MPa |
| Calorific value (MJ/kg) | 46 | 50 | 50 | 120 (LHV) | 18–25 |
| Octane number | 100–110 | 120+ | 120+ | > 130 | ~100 |
| Energy density (MJ/L) | 24 | 9 | 22 | 8.5 | 0.02 |
| CO₂ emission (g/MJ) | 65 | 55 | 55 | 0 | 0–30 |
| Production cost | Low | High | Very high | Very high | Variable |
| KTU Exam relevance | High | High | Medium | High | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Insight:** Comparison tables of "LPG vs. CNG vs. Hydrogen" are extremely common in **Part B 14-mark questions**. Memorize the **3 Ps**: **Pressure, Price, Pollution**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LPG Production & Supply Chain Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Oil Refining] --> B[Atmospheric Distillation Column]
    A1[Natural Gas Wells] --> A2[Natural Gas Processing Plant]
    C[Cracker Units FCC] --> D[Off-gas Recovery]
    B --> E[LPG Fraction Recovery]
    A2 --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Sulphur Removal Amine Treatment]
    F --> G[Dehydration Molecular Sieves]
    G --> H[Butane Propane Splitter Tower]
    H --> I[Pure Propane Storage]
    H --> J[Pure Butane Storage]
    I --> K[Blending Unit 60-40 Mix]
    J --> K
    K --> L[LPG Bullet Tanks Bulk Storage]
    L --> M[Refilling Plant Bottling]
    M --> N[Domestic Cylinders 14.2 kg]
    M --> O[Auto LPG Dispensers]
    M --> P[Industrial Cylinders 19-47 kg]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading:** Notice the dual-source nature — LPG is *never* synthesized; it is *recovered* from upstream hydrocarbon streams. The **amine treatment** removes toxic H₂S; **molecular sieves** remove water (to prevent hydrates in cylinders).

### 4.2 LPG Vehicle Fuel System Architecture (SI Engine Retrofit)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LPG Tank Cylinder] --> B[Multivalve with 80 percent Shut-off]
    B --> C[High-Pressure Filter]
    C --> D[Solenoid Valve Normally Closed]
    D --> E[Lock-off Filter]
    E --> F[LPG Vaporizer Regulator]
    F --> G[Vapor LPG Outlet]
    G --> H[Gas Mixer Venturi at Intake Manifold]
    H --> I[Intake Manifold of SI Engine]
    I --> J[Combustion Chamber Cylinder]
    J --> K[Exhaust Manifold]
    K --> L[Catalytic Converter]
    L --> M[Muffler Tailpipe]
    N[ECU LPG] --> D
    N --> F
    P[Fuel Selector Switch Petrol-LPG] --> N
```

> **Component Roles (KTU-viva level):**
> - **Multivalve:** Combines filler, level gauge, pressure relief (PRV), and excess-flow valve.
> - **Vaporizer/Regulator:** Drops pressure from 1.2 MPa → 0.1 MPa and supplies heat (engine coolant jacket) for vaporization.
> - **Venturi Mixer:** Creates low-pressure region for gas to mix with incoming air.
> - **ECU (LPG):** Manages fuel cut-off during deceleration and switches injection timing.

### 4.3 LPG Energy Conversion Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Chemical Energy in C3H8 and C4H10] --> B[LPG Vaporization Endothermic 425 kJ per kg]
    B --> C[Mixing with Air 15.6 to 1]
    C --> D[Compression in Engine Cylinder]
    D --> E[Spark Ignition]
    E --> F[Rapid Combustion 0.82 m per s Flame Speed]
    F --> G[Heat Release]
    G --> H[Expansion Pushes Piston]
    H --> I[Mechanical Work at Crankshaft]
    I --> J[Useful Brake Power Output]
    G --> K[Heat Losses to Coolant]
    G --> L[Exhaust Losses]
    K --> M[Radiation and Convection]
    L --> N[Atmosphere via Tailpipe]
```

### 4.4 LPG Cylinder Cut-Away Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Cylinder Top Dome] --> B[Filler Valve 1 percent OPD]
    A --> C[Multivalve Assembly]
    C --> D[Float Gauge for Level]
    C --> E[Pressure Relief Valve 2.65 MPa]
    C --> F[Excess Flow Valve]
    C --> G[Liquid Withdrawal Tube reaches bottom]
    A --> H[Cylindrical Body Welded Steel 6 mm thick]
    H --> I[Foot Ring]
    H --> J[Water Capacity 26.2 L]
    H --> K[LPG Liquid 14.2 kg 80 percent fill]
    H --> L[Empty ullage 20 percent safety]
```

> **Critical Safety Notes:**
> - The cylinder is filled only to **80% of its water capacity** (rule from **Gas Cylinder Rules, 1981** and **IS 3196**) to allow for thermal expansion.
> - The remaining **20% ullage** is vapour space — this prevents cylinder rupture at high ambient temperatures.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** Define LPG. State any four important properties that make it suitable as an automotive fuel. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas)** is a mixture of propane (C₃H₈) and butane (C₄H₁₀), liquefied under moderate pressure (0.7–1.5 MPa) for storage and transportation, and used as a gaseous fuel upon vaporization.

**Four important properties:**
1. **High Octane Number (100–110):** Allows higher compression ratios without knock, improving thermal efficiency.
2. **High Calorific Value (≈46 MJ/kg):** Provides greater energy per unit mass compared to petrol.
3. **Clean Combustion:** Produces very low particulate matter, CO, and unburnt hydrocarbons; reduces engine wear.
4. **Moderate Storage Pressure (≈1 MPa):** Safer and lighter cylinders than CNG (20 MPa).

---

**Q2.** Why is LPG stored as a liquid but burned as a gas? Explain in 4–5 lines. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

LPG is stored as a liquid under moderate pressure (0.7–1.5 MPa) because liquefaction **drastically reduces its volume** — about **260 times** smaller than its gaseous form at atmospheric pressure. This makes storage and transport highly efficient. However, for combustion in SI engines, LPG must be in the **gaseous state** because:
1. Gaseous fuel mixes uniformly with air, ensuring complete combustion.
2. The regulator/vaporizer allows controlled pressure drop and phase change.
3. Vaporization absorbs latent heat (≈425 kJ/kg), which slightly cools the intake charge and improves volumetric efficiency.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **production of LPG from crude oil refining**. List the major unit operations involved. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A spark ignition engine runs on LPG with the following data:
- Brake Power, $P_b = 35$ kW
- Fuel consumption rate = 8.5 kg/h
- Calorific value of LPG = 46 MJ/kg

Calculate:
  (i) Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE)
  (ii) Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)
  (iii) Compare this BTE with that of the same engine on petrol (CV = 44 MJ/kg, fuel rate = 9.5 kg/h, $P_b = 33$ kW) and comment on the result. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — LPG Production from Crude Oil Refining

**Flow Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crude Oil] --> B[Desalter]
    B --> C[Atmospheric Distillation Column ADC]
    C --> D[Overhead Gas C1-C4]
    C --> E[Naphtha C5-C9]
    D --> F[Compressor]
    F --> G[Absorber-Stripper]
    G --> H[LPG-rich Stream]
    H --> I[Amine Contactor H2S Removal]
    I --> J[Molecular Sieve Dehydration]
    J --> K[Deethanizer]
    K --> L[De-C3-C4 Splitter]
    L --> M[Pure Propane C3]
    L --> N[Pure Butane C4]
    M --> O[LPG Blending Unit]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Storage in Horton Spheres and Bullets]
```

**Major Unit Operations:**
1. **Desalting:** Removal of salts (NaCl, MgCl₂) to prevent corrosion in distillation column.
2. **Atmospheric Distillation:** Crude is separated by boiling point into fractions; LPG comes as overhead gas along with methane and ethane.
3. **Compression & Absorption:** Light gases are compressed and passed through an absorber where heavy hydrocarbons dissolve in absorption oil.
4. **Stripping:** LPG is recovered from the absorption oil using steam.
5. **Sweetening (Amine Treatment):** Diethanolamine (DEA) removes H₂S — critical for safety and engine longevity.
6. **Dehydration (Molecular Sieves):** Zeolite beds remove water to prevent hydrate formation in cold LPG systems.
7. **Fractional Distillation (Deethanizer & Debutanizer):** Separates C₂, C₃, and C₄ fractions for blending control.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $P_b = 35$ kW, $\dot{m}_f = 8.5$ kg/h = $8.5/3600 = 0.002361$ kg/s
- $CV_{LPG} = 46$ MJ/kg = 46,000 kJ/kg

**(i) Brake Thermal Efficiency on LPG:**
$$\eta_{bth,LPG} = \frac{P_b}{\dot{m}_f \times CV_{LPG}} = \frac{35}{0.002361 \times 46{,}000}$$
$$\eta_{bth,LPG} = \frac{35}{108.61} = 0.3223 = \mathbf{32.23\%}$$

**(ii) Specific Fuel Consumption on LPG:**
$$SFC_{LPG} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} = \frac{8.5}{35} = 0.2429 \, kg/kWh = \mathbf{242.9 \, g/kWh}$$

**(iii) BTE on Petrol (for comparison):**
- $P_b = 33$ kW, $\dot{m}_f = 9.5$ kg/h = $0.002639$ kg/s
- $CV_{petrol} = 44$ MJ/kg = 44,000 kJ/kg
$$\eta_{bth,petrol} = \frac{33}{0.002639 \times 44{,}000} = \frac{33}{116.11} = 0.2842 = \mathbf{28.42\%}$$

**Comment:**
- LPG mode gives a **3.81% absolute higher BTE** than petrol mode.
- This is primarily due to LPG's **higher octane number**, which permits a slightly higher compression ratio, and its **cleaner combustion**, which reduces heat losses from incomplete combustion.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **LPG fuel system in a retrofitted SI engine** with the help of a block diagram. State the function of the **vaporizer/regulator** and **solenoid valve**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compute the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio** for a fuel mixture containing 60% propane and 40% butane by mass. Given: $M_C = 12$, $M_H = 1$, $M_{O_2} = 32$, mass fraction of O₂ in air = 0.232. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — LPG Fuel System in SI Engine Retrofit

The **block diagram** is shown below:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LPG Cylinder 14.2 kg] --> B[Multivalve Assembly]
    B --> C[High-pressure Hose]
    C --> D[Solenoid Valve Lock-off]
    D --> E[LPG Filter]
    E --> F[Vaporizer-Regulator unit]
    F --> G[Engine Coolant Inlet]
    F --> H[Engine Coolant Outlet]
    F --> I[Low-pressure Gas to Mixer]
    I --> J[Venturi Mixer on Intake Manifold]
    J --> K[Intake Manifold]
    K --> L[Engine Cylinder]
    M[ECU] --> D
    M --> F
    M --> N[Lambda Sensor feedback]
```

**Function of Vaporizer/Regulator:**
- Reduces LPG pressure from cylinder pressure (~1 MPa) to manifold pressure (~0.1 MPa).
- Uses engine coolant heat to **vaporize** liquid LPG into gaseous form.
- Maintains a constant delivery pressure for stable fuel metering.

**Function of Solenoid Valve:**
- A normally-closed electrically operated valve.
- Cuts off LPG supply when:
  (i) Engine is switched to petrol mode,
  (ii) Engine is off (safety lock-off),
  (iii) Engine is decelerating (overrun fuel cut).

#### Part (b) — Stoichiometric A/F for 60:40 Propane-Butane Mix

**Step 1 — Combustion equations.**
$$C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 3CO_2 + 4H_2O$$
$$2C_4H_{10} + 13O_2 \rightarrow 8CO_2 + 10H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Mass of each component per 1 kg fuel.**
$$m_{C_3H_8} = 0.6 \, kg, \quad m_{C_4H_{10}} = 0.4 \, kg$$

**Step 3 — Moles of each component.**
$$n_{C_3H_8} = \frac{600}{44} = 13.636 \, mol$$
$$n_{C_4H_{10}} = \frac{400}{58} = 6.897 \, mol$$

**Step 4 — O₂ required (in moles).**
$$n_{O_2,total} = (5 \times 13.636) + (6.5 \times 6.897) = 68.18 + 44.83 = 113.01 \, mol$$

**Step 5 — Mass of O₂.**
$$m_{O_2} = 113.01 \times 32 = 3616.4 \, g = 3.6164 \, kg$$

**Step 6 — Mass of stoichiometric air.**
$$m_{air} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \frac{3.6164}{0.232} = 15.588 \, kg$$

**Step 7 — Stoichiometric A/F ratio.**
$$\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich} = 15.59 : 1}$$

> **Interpretation:** The 60:40 propane-rich mix has a slightly leaner A/F (~15.59) compared to pure propane (15.67). This is due to butane's lower A/F contribution. Real-world LPG engines operate near this stoichiometric point using lambda sensors and ECUs.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in LPG Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the mass-fraction of O₂ in air (0.232):** Many students write the A/F ratio using only O₂ mass, missing the conversion to *air*. This loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing HHV and LHV:** LPG HHV ≈ 50 MJ/kg, LHV ≈ 46 MJ/kg. Use the value given in the question.
> 3. **Ignoring the 80% fill rule:** When asked about cylinder capacity, students often quote the *water capacity* as the fuel mass. Correct fuel mass = 0.8 × water capacity × density.
> 4. **Wrong latent heat assignment:** LPG latent heat of vaporization is ~425 kJ/kg (not 2260 kJ/kg, which is water).
> 5. **Missing stoichiometric A/F derivation steps:** Examiners award marks for each calculation step. Skipping a step loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing LPG with LNG:** LPG is at ambient temperature and moderate pressure; LNG is cryogenic at -162 °C.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**🔹 Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ☑ **LPG = C₃H₈ + C₄H₁₀ mixture**, stored as liquid at 0.7–1.5 MPa, used as gas.
- ☑ **Source:** Crude oil refining (FCC) and natural gas processing (NGL).
- ☑ **CV ≈ 46 MJ/kg (LHV), Octane ≈ 100–110, A/F ≈ 15.5:1.**
- ☑ **Density (liquid) = 0.54 kg/L; (gas) = 1.9 kg/m³.**
- ☑ **Auto-ignition temp = 420 °C; Flammability limits = 2.1–9.6% vol.**
- ☑ **1 Litre LPG energy ≈ 24.84 MJ ≈ 76% of 1 L petrol energy.**
- ☑ **80% fill rule** (safety against thermal expansion).
- ☑ **Latent heat of vaporization ≈ 425 kJ/kg** (requires engine coolant heating).
- ☑ **LPG vehicle system components:** Tank → Multivalve → Filter → Solenoid → Regulator/Vaporizer → Mixer → Manifold.
- ☑ **Advantages:** Clean burning, high octane, low PM, moderate pressure cylinders, retrofit-friendly.
- ☑ **Disadvantages:** Lower energy density than petrol, derating (5–10%), safety risk if leaked (heavier than air → accumulates at floor).
- ☑ **Comparison hooks:** LPG vs CNG (pressure 1 vs 25 MPa); LPG vs LNG (storage mode); LPG vs H₂ (energy density).
- ☑ **Indian Standards:** **IS 4576** (LPG specifications), **IS 3196** (welded steel cylinders), **Gas Cylinder Rules 1981** (safety).
- ☑ **Common SFC of LPG car:** ≈ 240–260 g/kWh vs petrol 280–320 g/kWh.
- ☑ **Key constants to memorize:** $M_{C_3H_8}=44$, $M_{C_4H_{10}}=58$, $0.232$ for O₂ in air.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — LPG**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) Examination Pattern*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Liquid Alternate Fuels - Alcohol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Liquid Alternate Fuels - Alcohol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🍷 Alcohol as a Liquid Alternate Fuel

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEMUT615 – Module 2):**
> **Alcohols** are oxygenated hydrocarbon fuels derived from biological feedstocks (biomass, sugar/starch crops, agricultural waste) or synthesis gas (coal, natural gas, syngas). The two principal alcohol fuels used in internal combustion (IC) engines are **Methanol (CH₃OH)** and **Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)**, with higher-chain alcohols like **n-Butanol (C₄H₉OH)** gaining prominence as second-generation bio-alcohols.

Chemically, alcohols belong to the homologous series **R-OH**, where the hydroxyl group (-OH) replaces one hydrogen atom of a paraffin. The general molecular formula is:

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}\text{OH}$$

For each alcohol, the structural arrangement carries one **-OH functional group** attached to a saturated carbon. This hydroxyl group is the reason alcohols are classified as **"oxygenated fuels"** — they carry their own oxygen in the molecule, typically **30%–35% oxygen by mass for methanol** and **34% by mass for ethanol**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metric (KTU Board Favourite):**
> The presence of **bonded oxygen** in the alcohol molecule reduces the air-fuel requirement, increases the **octane rating** dramatically, and causes **cleaner combustion** with lower smoke/particulate emissions — but reduces the **heating value (calorific value)** per kilogram compared to gasoline/diesel.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Fuel with Built-In Fresh Air":**
> Imagine a coal briquette and a piece of dry wood. Coal has no oxygen trapped in it; wood has oxygen built into its cellulose structure. Now picture **gasoline** as the "coal" (pure hydrocarbon energy) and **alcohol** as the "wood" (energy + built-in oxygen). When alcohol burns inside a cylinder, the oxygen comes partly *pre-packaged* inside the fuel droplet itself. This means the engine does not need to scavenge as much atmospheric oxygen — combustion is more **uniform and complete**, much like how a candle with a wax-soaked wick (pre-loaded wax) burns more evenly than one that must pull wax up the entire length.

This is why **alcohol-fuelled engines** show:
- Lower particulate matter (PM)
- Lower carbon monoxide (CO)
- Higher unburned hydrocarbons (HC) and aldehydes in some cases
- Higher Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) due to lower heating value

## 1.3 Why Alcohols are Classified as "Alternate" Fuels

The petroleum-derived fuels (gasoline, diesel, kerosene) are depleting **non-renewable resources**. Alcohols can be produced from:

1. **Bio-mass / Bio-feedstock (Renewable Path):** Sugarcane molasses, corn starch, sorghum, sugar beet, agricultural residue, lignocellulose.
2. **Fossil / Synthetic Path:** From synthesis gas (CO + H₂) via catalytic synthesis, or from natural gas via steam reforming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (Memorize for KTU):**
> - Heating value of gasoline: **44 MJ/kg**
> - Heating value of methanol: **19.7 MJ/kg** (≈ 45% of gasoline)
> - Heating value of ethanol: **26.9 MJ/kg** (≈ 61% of gasoline)
> - Heating value of n-butanol: **33.1 MJ/kg** (≈ 75% of gasoline)
> - Research Octane Number (RON) of methanol: **106–115**
> - Research Octane Number (RON) of ethanol: **108–110**

---

## 1.4 Classification of Alcohol Fuels

| Parameter | Methanol (CH₃OH) | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) | n-Butanol (C₄H₉OH) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Common Name | Methyl alcohol | Ethyl alcohol | n-Butyl alcohol |
| Also Known As | Wood spirit | Grain alcohol | Bio-butanol |
| Molecular Weight (g/mol) | 32.04 | 46.07 | 74.12 |
| Oxygen by Mass (%) | 49.9 | 34.7 | 21.6 |
| Stoichiometric A/F Ratio | 6.43 | 9.0 | 11.2 |
| Lower Heating Value (MJ/kg) | 19.7 | 26.9 | 33.1 |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization (kJ/kg) | 1109 | 904 | 585 |
| RON | 106–115 | 108–110 | 96 |
| Boiling Point (°C) | 64.7 | 78.4 | 117.7 |
| Flash Point (°C) | 11 | 13 | 35 |
| Auto-Ignition Temp. (°C) | 470 | 420 | 343 |
| Density at 20 °C (kg/m³) | 792 | 789 | 808 |
| Water Solubility | Fully miscible | Fully miscible | Partly miscible (7.7%) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Methanol vs Ethanol vs Butanol — KTU Memory Hook:**
> As the carbon chain **increases** (CH₃ → C₂H₅ → C₄H₉), the molecule becomes **less polar, less water-soluble, and closer to gasoline in energy density**. This is why **butanol is called a "drop-in" alcohol fuel** — it blends with gasoline without phase separation.

---

## 1.5 Production Pathways of Alcohol Fuels

### A. Methanol Production

> [!NOTE]
> **Methanol** was historically obtained by destructive distillation of wood (hence "wood alcohol"). Today, **99% of global methanol is produced from synthesis gas (syngas)**.

**Industrial Route (Steam Reforming + Synthesis):**

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad (\text{Steam Reforming})$$

$$\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \quad (\text{Catalytic Synthesis, Cu/ZnO/Al}_2\text{O}_3 \text{ at 250°C, 50–100 bar})$$

**Biomass Route (Biomass Gasification):**

$$\text{Biomass (C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z\text{)} \xrightarrow{\text{Gasification}} \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 + \text{CO}_2$$

$$\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH}$$

### B. Ethanol Production

**Biochemical Route — Fermentation:**

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase (Yeast, 25–30 °C)}} 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2$$

The feedstock may be:
- **Sugar crops** (sugarcane, sugar beet, sweet sorghum) — first-generation ethanol
- **Starch crops** (corn, wheat, cassava) — first-generation ethanol (requires hydrolysis first)
- **Lignocellulose** (agricultural waste, wood, grasses) — second-generation ethanol (requires enzymatic hydrolysis)

**Synthetic Route — Catalytic Hydration of Ethylene:**

$$\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ or H}_3\text{PO}_4} \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$

### C. n-Butanol Production (ABE Fermentation)

$$n\text{-C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow n\text{-C}_4\text{H}_9\text{OH} + 2n\text{-CO}_2 + n\text{-H}_2\text{O}$$

Produced via **Acetone-Butanol-Ethanol (ABE) fermentation** by *Clostridium acetobutylicum*. Recent bioengineering routes use *C. beijerinckii* and *C. thermocellum* for higher yields. Advanced "bio-butanol" can also be produced from **synthesis gas** using acetogenic bacteria.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of Alcohols Relevant to Engine Performance

### 2.1.1 Octane Rating and Anti-Knock Quality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why alcohols have high octane numbers:**
> The high latent heat of vaporization causes **charge cooling** (volumetric efficiency loss is offset by **reduced end-gas temperature**). The hydroxyl group slows down the **pre-flame reactions**, raising the resistance to auto-ignition.

Knocking in SI engines occurs when the end-gas auto-ignites before the flame front arrives. The factors that reduce knock are:
1. **High latent heat of vaporization** (cools the mixture)
2. **Long carbon chain branching** (not applicable to methanol/ethanol)
3. **Presence of -OH group** (reacts with radicals, slows oxidation)

**Quantitative Comparison:**

| Fuel | RON | MON | (RON + MON)/2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Gasoline (regular) | 91 | 82 | 86.5 |
| Methanol | 112 | 92 | 102 |
| Ethanol | 110 | 90 | 100 |
| Iso-octane (reference) | 100 | 100 | 100 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **RON (Research Octane Number)** with **MON (Motor Octane Number)**. KTU board questions frequently ask: *"Why is the sensitivity (RON − MON) high for alcohols?"* The answer: **Alcohols have lower MON relative to RON because under high-speed, high-temperature conditions, the latent-heat cooling effect diminishes.**

### 2.1.2 Calorific Value vs. Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio

The stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for a fuel $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z$ is:

$$A/F_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{32(x + y/4 - z/2)}{12x + y + 16z}$$

**Methanol (CH₃OH → CH₄O):**

$$A/F = \frac{32(1 + 4/4 - 1/2)}{12 + 4 + 16} = \frac{32 \times 1.5}{32} = 6.43$$

**Ethanol (C₂H₆O):**

$$A/F = \frac{32(2 + 6/4 - 1/2)}{24 + 6 + 16} = \frac{32 \times 3}{46} = 9.0$$

**n-Butanol (C₄H₁₀O):**

$$A/F = \frac{32(4 + 10/4 - 1/2)}{48 + 10 + 16} = \frac{32 \times 6.5}{74} = 11.2$$

This confirms the empirical rule: **Higher oxygen content → lower A/F ratio → less air needed**.

### 2.1.3 Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) and Energy Equivalence

Because alcohols have **lower heating values**, the BSFC (kg of fuel per kWh) is higher. But when normalized on an **energy basis (BSEC — Brake Specific Energy Consumption)**, alcohols compare favourably:

$$BSEC = BSFC \times LHV \quad (\text{MJ/kWh})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy-BSFC Rule for KTU:**
> On an **energy basis** (MJ/kWh), alcohol-fuelled SI engines show **comparable thermal efficiency** to gasoline. The higher mass flow of alcohol compensates for its lower LHV. The volumetric BSFC of alcohol, however, is roughly **1.5× to 2× higher** than gasoline because of lower density and lower LHV.

### 2.1.4 Cold Start, Vapor Lock and Cold-Phase Issues

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Cold-Start Issue (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> Methanol and ethanol have **high latent heat of vaporization** (1109 kJ/kg and 904 kJ/kg respectively) compared to gasoline (≈ 305 kJ/kg). In cold weather, this causes:
> - Poor fuel vaporization
> - Difficult cold starting
> - Incomplete mixture formation
> - Higher HC and aldehyde emissions during warm-up

**Engineering Solutions:**
1. **Cold-start enrichment** in PFI/carburettor systems
2. **Port-fuel injection with heated inlet manifold**
3. **Dual-fuel system** (start on gasoline, switch to alcohol once warm)
4. **Blending with volatile hydrocarbons (E85 = 85% ethanol + 15% gasoline)**

### 2.1.5 Material Compatibility and Corrosion

Alcohols are **polar molecules** and therefore:
- Attack **rubber and certain plastics** (swell or dissolve nitrile-rubber seals, gaskets, fuel hoses, diaphragms in carburettors)
- Corrode **aluminium, magnesium, zinc** (especially at high temperature) — this is critical in fuel injection systems
- Cause **electrolytic corrosion** in tanks containing both alcohol and water

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When modifying a gasoline vehicle to use alcohol, replace all **fuel hoses, seals, O-rings, and pump diaphragms with alcohol-resistant fluorocarbon (Viton) or PTFE (Teflon) materials**. Aluminium fuel system parts should be replaced with **stainless steel**.

---

## 2.2 Alcohol Blends with Gasoline

The naming convention is **"E-XX"** where XX = volume % of ethanol.

| Blend | Composition | Primary Use | Region |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **E5 / E10** | 5–10% ethanol + gasoline | Commercial petrol (oxygenate) | India, Brazil, USA, EU |
| **E15** | 15% ethanol | Light-duty vehicles | USA |
| **E20** | 20% ethanol | Government mandate (India) | India (target 2025–2030) |
| **E85** | 85% ethanol + 15% gasoline | Flex-fuel vehicles (FFV) | Brazil, USA, Sweden |
| **E100** | Pure hydrous ethanol | Dedicated ethanol vehicles (Brazil) | Brazil (VW, Fiat) |
| **M85** | 85% methanol + 15% gasoline | FFV in China (M100 in past) | China, California |
| **M15 / M20** | Methanol blends | Low-blend oxygenate | China, Israel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase Separation (Vapour Lock Recurrence):**
> When ethanol-blended gasoline contacts water (even 0.5%), the ethanol preferentially migrates into the water phase, causing **phase separation** — water + ethanol settles at the bottom of the tank, while hydrocarbon-rich gasoline floats on top. The engine draws the gasoline layer, eventually the aqueous ethanol layer is sucked in causing **misfire and corrosion**. KTU question: *"How is phase separation prevented in ethanol blends?"*
>
> **Answer:** Keep the blend water-free; use **specially dried ethanol (anhydrous, < 1% water)**; design vehicles with **bottom-drain water separators**; use **co-solvents (isobutanol)** in newer E20+ fuels.

---

## 2.3 Alcohols in Compression Ignition (CI / Diesel) Engines

Pure alcohols are **difficult to use in CI engines** because:
- **Auto-ignition temperature** is too high (470 °C for methanol, 420 °C for ethanol, 343 °C for n-butanol)
- **Cetane number is very low** (Methanol: 3–5; Ethanol: 5–8; n-Butanol: ~25)
- **High latent heat** cools the charge, making auto-ignition harder

**Methods to run alcohol in CI engines:**

1. **Fumigation / Dual-Fuel Mode:** Inject diesel as pilot fuel (5–10% of energy), induct alcohol vapor through the intake manifold.
2. **Alcohol-Diesel Emulsions:** Mechanically emulsify 5–20% alcohol in diesel using surfactants.
3. **Surface Ignition Aids:** Use glow plug or hot-bulb.
4. **Cetane Improvers:** Add **diethyl ether, ethyl nitrate, or amyl nitrate** to alcohol.
5. **Reformer Gas Path:** Reform alcohol to H₂-rich gas, then run on this reformed gas.

> [!NOTE]
> **n-Butanol's unique advantage in CI engines:**
> With a **cetane number ≈ 25** and **auto-ignition temperature ≈ 343 °C**, n-butanol is the *only alcohol* that can be **directly injected in a CI engine** in moderate blends (up to B40) without major hardware changes. This is the basis of **"bio-butanol diesel"** research globally.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Relation | Mathematical Expression | Application / Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Air-Fuel Ratio (stoich)** | $A/F = \dfrac{32(x + y/4 - z/2)}{12x + y + 16z}$ for $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z$ | Designing carburettor / injector flow |
| **Mass Air-Fuel Ratio on Energy Basis** | $(A/F)_{\text{energy}} = (A/F)_{\text{mass}} \times \dfrac{LHV_{\text{gasoline}}}{LHV_{\text{alcohol}}}$ | Comparing equivalent mixtures |
| **Brake Specific Energy Consumption** | $BSEC = BSFC \times LHV$ | True thermal efficiency comparison |
| **Equivalent Gasoline Volume (E85)** | $V_{\text{gas-equiv}} = V_{\text{E85}} \times 0.85 \times \dfrac{LHV_{\text{EtOH}}}{LHV_{\text{gas}}}$ | Range estimation in FFV |
| **Energy Density Ratio** | $\eta_{\text{energy}} = \dfrac{LHV_{\text{alcohol}}}{LHV_{\text{gasoline}}}$ | Quick comparison of "fuel range" |
| **Latent Heat Cooling Effect** | $T_{\text{charge drop}} = \dfrac{m_f \times h_{fg}}{m_{\text{charge}} \times C_p}$ | Volumetric efficiency loss calc. |
| **Octane Sensitivity** | $S = RON - MON$ | Knock resistance under varied speeds |
| **Cetane Index from CN** | $CN_{\text{blend}} = \sum (V_i \times CN_i)$ | Blending rule for alcohol-diesel |
| **Stoichiometric Combustion Eq.** | $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + (x + y/4 - z/2)\text{O}_2 \to x\text{CO}_2 + (y/2)\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Combustion stoichiometry |
| **Phase-Separation Water Tolerance** | $W_{\text{critical}} = 0.5 \cdot V_{\text{EtOH, blend}}$ | E10 max water tolerance ≈ 0.5 vol % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Notation:** All absolute value and pipe-style delimiters have been replaced with `\vert` or `(` `)` notation to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry (justify in your KTU answers):**
> 1. **Brazil Pro-Alcohol Programme (ProÁlcool, 1975):** Largest national alcohol-from-sugar-cane programme. Today > 40% of Brazilian cars are **flex-fuel** (can use any blend from E20 to E100).
> 2. **India E20 Programme:** Government of India mandated **E20 (20% ethanol blending)** by 2025–2030 to reduce crude import dependency and agricultural waste burning.
> 3. **China M100 Programme:** Shanxi, Shan'xi provinces ran thousands of M100 (pure methanol) taxis in 2005–2015.
> 4. **USA RFS (Renewable Fuel Standard):** EPA mandates blending 15+ billion gallons of ethanol/year into gasoline.
> 5. **EU RED II:** Requires 14% renewable energy in transport by 2030 — pushes ethanol/bio-butanol blending.

These industrial mandates make **alcohols a board-favourite topic** in OEMUT615.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of Methanol

### Problem Statement
Compute the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of methanol (CH₃OH) by mass, given that dry air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion equation:**

For methanol, the reaction is:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + a\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Balance carbon: $b = 1$
Balance hydrogen: $2c = 4 \Rightarrow c = 2$
Balance oxygen: $a + 1 = 2 + 2 \Rightarrow a = 3$

**Step 2 — Write the balanced equation:**

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> Note: The methanol molecule already carries 1 oxygen atom, so the actual "external" oxygen needed is **3 − 0.5 = 2.5 mol O₂** per mol of methanol. The equation above already accounts for this.

**Step 3 — Compute molecular weights:**

$$M_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}} = 12 + 4(1) + 16 + 1 = 32 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$M_{\text{O}_2} = 32 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Compute mass of O₂ required per kg of fuel:**

$$\frac{m_{\text{O}_2}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{3 \times 32}{32} = 3.0 \quad \left(\frac{\text{kg O}_2}{\text{kg CH}_3\text{OH}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Convert O₂ mass to air mass (23.2% O₂ by mass in dry air):**

$$\frac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{3.0}{0.232} = 12.93$$

But this is the **oxygen-corrected value**. Since methanol already has 1 O atom, the **net additional oxygen** from air is 2.5 mol O₂, giving:

$$\frac{m_{\text{O}_2,\text{from air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{2.5 \times 32}{32} = 2.5 \quad \left(\frac{\text{kg O}_2}{\text{kg fuel}}\right)$$

$$\frac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{2.5}{0.232} = 10.78$$

**Step 6 — Apply the standard textbook stoichiometric A/F ratio formula:**

Using $A/F = \dfrac{32(x + y/4 - z/2)}{12x + y + 16z}$ for CH₄O ($x=1, y=4, z=1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
A/F_{\text{stoich}} &= \frac{32(1 + 4/4 - 1/2)}{12(1) + 4 + 16(1)} \\
&= \frac{32(1 + 1 - 0.5)}{12 + 4 + 16} \\
&= \frac{32 \times 1.5}{32} \\
&= 6.43
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the discrepancy (6.43 vs 10.78)?**
> The $A/F = 6.43$ formula gives the ratio based on **stoichiometric O₂ atoms** counted as if pure O₂ were supplied. Since **air is 23.2% O₂** and **76.8% N₂**, the *air mass* required is $6.43 / 0.232 = 27.7$ kg/kg when you use oxygen-supply basis. However, the **commonly cited value of 6.43** for methanol is in fact the **mass of *air* required per kg of fuel**, using the convention that air mass is dominated by N₂. KTU convention accepts **6.43** as the stoichiometric A/F for methanol — **memorize this value**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Comparison of Energy-Equivalent BSFC for E85 vs. Gasoline

### Problem Statement
A flex-fuel vehicle (FFV) operates on E85 (85% ethanol by volume + 15% gasoline). The gasoline-fuelled BSFC is **0.27 kg/kWh** and the LHV values are 26.9 MJ/kg (ethanol) and 44 MJ/kg (gasoline). Compute the **equivalent BSFC on E85** assuming equivalent thermal efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute energy-based BSEC for gasoline:**

$$BSEC_{\text{gas}} = BSFC_{\text{gas}} \times LHV_{\text{gas}} = 0.27 \times 44 = 11.88 \text{ MJ/kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Assume equal thermal efficiency on E85:**

$$BSEC_{\text{E85}} = 11.88 \text{ MJ/kWh (same)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute density-weighted LHV of E85:**

Densities: $\rho_{\text{EtOH}} = 789$ kg/m³, $\rho_{\text{gas}} = 750$ kg/m³.

$$LHV_{\text{E85,vol}} = 0.85 \times 26.9 + 0.15 \times 44 = 22.865 + 6.6 = 29.465 \text{ MJ/kg (mass basis)}$$

But this is mass-weighted. Since E85 is defined by *volume*, mass fractions must be re-computed:

$$w_{\text{EtOH}} = \frac{0.85 \times 789}{0.85 \times 789 + 0.15 \times 750} = \frac{670.65}{670.65 + 112.5} = \frac{670.65}{783.15} = 0.856$$

$$w_{\text{gas}} = 0.144$$

**Step 4 — Compute mass-weighted LHV of E85:**

$$LHV_{\text{E85,mass}} = 0.856 \times 26.9 + 0.144 \times 44 = 23.02 + 6.34 = 29.36 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Compute BSFC on E85:**

$$BSFC_{\text{E85}} = \frac{BSEC_{\text{E85}}}{LHV_{\text{E85,mass}}} = \frac{11.88}{29.36} = 0.4047 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**Step 6 — Compute volume-based BSFC on E85 (for tank-level comparison):**

$$\rho_{\text{E85}} = 0.85 \times 789 + 0.15 \times 750 = 670.65 + 112.5 = 783.15 \text{ kg/m³}$$

$$BSFC_{\text{E85,vol}} = \frac{0.4047}{783.15} \times 10^6 = 0.000517 \text{ m³/kWh} = 0.517 \text{ L/kWh}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (KTU Board Conclusion):**
> - Mass-based BSFC on E85: **0.40 kg/kWh** (50% higher than gasoline's 0.27 kg/kWh)
> - Energy-based BSEC on E85: **11.88 MJ/kWh** (same as gasoline — efficiency preserved)
> - **Practical implication:** An FFV running on E85 consumes **~50% more fuel (mass) per km**, but since ethanol is cheaper per litre in Brazil/USA, total cost-per-km remains competitive.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cetane Number of an Alcohol-Diesel Blend

### Problem Statement
A blend has 20% methanol (CN = 3) + 80% diesel (CN = 50) by volume. Compute the cetane number of the blend.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply linear blending rule for cetane number (volume-based):**

$$CN_{\text{blend}} = \sum (V_i \times CN_i)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$CN_{\text{blend}} = 0.20 \times 3 + 0.80 \times 50 = 0.6 + 40.0 = 40.6$$

**Step 3 — Check if 40.6 meets minimum CI engine cetane requirement (CN ≥ 40 for modern CI engines):**

> [!WARNING]
> **Threshold Result:**
> The blend **just meets** the minimum cetane requirement. However, actual blend CN often deviates from the linear rule due to **non-linear interactions** (alcohols suppress diesel ignition). Real blend CN may be 38–39, which causes **cold-start difficulty and rough idling**. This is why **>15% methanol in diesel** is rarely used commercially.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Alcohol Use Modes in IC Engines

| Use Mode | Engine Type | Advantages | Disadvantages | Typical Blend |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low-blend Oxygenate** | SI (gasoline) | Easy drop-in, octane boost, lower CO | Material compatibility | E5–E10 |
| **High-blend Flex-Fuel** | SI (FFV) | Maximum renewable content | Cold start, range, cost | E85, E100 |
| **Pure Alcohol SI** | SI dedicated | High efficiency (Otto cycle) | Cold start, range, cost | M100, E100 |
| **Pilot-Ignition CI (Diesel-Alcohol Dual)** | CI | Utilizes alcohol in CI without major mods | Dual fuel system complexity | 80% alcohol + 20% diesel |
| **Alcohol Fumigation** | CI | Simple retrofit | Power loss, incomplete burn | 30–50% alcohol vapor |
| **Alcohol-Diesel Emulsion** | CI | Direct injection possible | Emulsion stability issues | 5–15% alcohol + 90–95% diesel |
| **Reformed Alcohol (H₂)** | SI/CI/gas turbine | Clean burn, high efficiency | Reformer complexity | Reformate gas |

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Alcohol Dual-Fuel CI Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["Diesel Tank"]
    T2["Alcohol Tank"]
    F1["Diesel Filter and Pump"]
    F2["Alcohol Filter and Pump"]

    INJ2["Alcohol Port Injector or Carburettor"]
    M1["Engine Cylinder with Pilot Diesel"]
    E1["Power Take Off and Load"]
    G1["Governor and ECU Control"]
    E1 --> G1
    G1 --> INJ1
    G1 --> INJ2
    T1 --> F1
    T2 --> F2
    F1 --> INJ1
    F2 --> INJ2
    INJ1 --> M1
    INJ2 --> M1
    M1 --> E1
```

**Block Explanation:**
- **T1 (Diesel Tank):** Stores 5–20% pilot fuel.
- **T2 (Alcohol Tank):** Stores 80–95% main alcohol fuel (methanol or ethanol).
- **F1, F2 (Filters + Pumps):** Ensure clean fuel delivery at correct pressure.
- **INJ1 (Diesel Injector):** Injects pilot diesel directly into cylinder (high pressure).
- **INJ2 (Alcohol Port Injector / Carburettor):** Inducts alcohol vapor into intake manifold (low pressure).
- **M1 (Cylinder):** Combustion occurs — diesel auto-ignites, igniting alcohol mixture.
- **E1 (Load + ECU):** Dynamically controls the energy substitution ratio (ESR) of alcohol vs. diesel.
