# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Q: List any three advantages and three disadvantages of using alcohols as alternate fuels in SI engines.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages:**
> 1. **High octane rating** (Methanol: 112, Ethanol: 110) allows **higher compression ratio** and hence higher thermal efficiency.
> 2. **High latent heat of vaporization** provides **charge cooling** which improves volumetric efficiency and reduces NOₓ formation in some cases.
> 3. **Renewable feedstock** (sugarcane, corn, lignocellulose) reduces dependence on imported crude oil.
> 4. **Lower CO and particulate matter emissions** due to the in-built oxygen in the fuel.
> 5. **Broader flammability limits** allow leaner combustion.

> **Disadvantages:**
> 1. **Low heating value** (Methanol: 19.7 MJ/kg, Ethanol: 26.9 MJ/kg) — about 45–61% of gasoline — leading to **higher BSFC** and reduced range.
> 2. **High latent heat of vaporization** causes **cold-start difficulty** and **vapor lock** in warm climates.
> 3. **Material compatibility issues** — attacks rubber seals, corrodes aluminium, magnesium, and zinc.
> 4. **Phase separation** of ethanol-gasoline blends in presence of water.
> 5. **Lower flame visibility** (nearly invisible in daylight) — a safety concern.
> 6. **Higher aldehyde emissions** (formaldehyde, acetaldehyde) — toxic.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Q: Compare methanol and ethanol as alternate fuels. (Write any 6 points.)**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Methanol (CH₃OH) | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Source** | Mainly from syngas (coal, NG) | Mainly from fermentation of sugar/starch |
| **LHV (MJ/kg)** | 19.7 | 26.9 |
| **Octane Number (RON)** | 112 | 110 |
| **Latent Heat (kJ/kg)** | 1109 | 904 |
| **Water Solubility** | Fully miscible | Fully miscible |
| **Toxicity** | Highly toxic (causes blindness) | Less toxic (used in beverages) |
| **Energy content per litre (MJ/L)** | 15.6 | 21.2 |
| **Vapor pressure** | High (summer volatility issues) | Moderate |

> **Key Engineering Distinction:** Methanol is **cheaper to produce industrially** (from syngas) but **more toxic**; ethanol is **safer to handle** but requires **arable land and water**. Both are completely miscible in water — a storage advantage and a contamination concern.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module Choice, Slot 1]**
**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the production of ethanol from lignocellulosic biomass by fermentation route. Discuss the engineering challenges in using ethanol in SI engines. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A blend of M85 (85% methanol + 15% gasoline by volume) is used in a SI engine. Compute the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of M85 by mass. Given: LHV of methanol = 19.7 MJ/kg, LHV of gasoline = 44 MJ/kg, density of methanol = 792 kg/m³, density of gasoline = 750 kg/m³, stoichiometric A/F for methanol = 6.43, for gasoline = 14.7. **[7 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — Model Answer

**Production of Ethanol from Lignocellulose (Second-Generation Process):**

The lignocellulosic biomass consists of three major polymers:
1. **Cellulose** (40–50%) — a polymer of glucose
2. **Hemicellulose** (25–35%) — a polymer of xylose, arabinose, mannose
3. **Lignin** (15–25%) — a phenolic polymer (not fermentable; used for heat/power)

**Step 1 — Pre-treatment:** Mechanical size reduction, then steam explosion or dilute acid pre-treatment to break down the lignin sheath and expose cellulose/hemicellulose fibres.

**Step 2 — Enzymatic Hydrolysis:** Cellulase enzymes (e.g., from *Trichoderma reesei*) break cellulose into glucose:

$$(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5)_n + n\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Cellulase}} n\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$$

**Step 3 — Fermentation:** Glucose (and xylose, in advanced processes) is fermented by *Zymomonas mobilis* or genetically modified yeast:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast}} 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2$$

**Step 4 — Distillation and Dehydration:** The fermented broth contains 8–12% ethanol. Multi-stage distillation concentrates it to **95.6% (azeotrope with water)**. A molecular-sieve adsorption step removes residual water to produce **anhydrous ethanol (99.5%+)**.

> **Engineering Challenges in SI Engines:**
> 1. **Cold start:** Latent heat of 904 kJ/kg and low vapor pressure in winter cause poor cold starting. **Solution:** Cold-start enrichment, electric intake manifold heaters, dual-fuel systems.
> 2. **Material degradation:** Attacks standard nitrile rubber hoses, aluminium fuel rails. **Solution:** Replace with Viton (FKM) seals and stainless steel lines.
> 3. **Phase separation:** In E5–E20 blends, water contamination causes phase separation. **Solution:** Use anhydrous ethanol (< 1% water) and water-separator filters.
> 4. **Corrosion in aluminium:** Forms aluminium alkoxides at high T. **Solution:** Use stainless steel fuel system components.
> 5. **Lubrication issue:** Ethanol acts as a solvent and can wash lubricating oil from cylinder walls. **Solution:** Use high-detergent oil; modern FFV engines use low-friction piston rings.
> 6. **Energy density:** 1.5× the fuel mass for same range. **Solution:** Larger fuel tanks in FFVs; range anxiety mitigated by station availability.
> 7. **Flame visibility:** Almost invisible flame — safety issue. **Solution:** Use flame detectors and add pilot fuel flame luminophore.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute mass fractions of methanol and gasoline in M85:**

$$m_{\text{MeOH}} = 0.85 \times 792 = 673.2 \text{ kg/m³ (contribution)}$$

$$m_{\text{gas}} = 0.15 \times 750 = 112.5 \text{ kg/m³ (contribution)}$$

$$m_{\text{total}} = 673.2 + 112.5 = 785.7 \text{ kg/m³}$$

$$w_{\text{MeOH}} = \frac{673.2}{785.7} = 0.8568$$

$$w_{\text{gas}} = 0.1432$$

**Step 2 — Apply stoichiometric A/F blending rule (mass basis):**

For stoichiometric combustion, the A/F ratio of a blend is the inverse of the weighted harmonic mean (since the air demand per kg of fuel varies):

$$\frac{1}{(A/F)_{\text{blend}}} = \frac{w_{\text{MeOH}}}{(A/F)_{\text{MeOH}}} + \frac{w_{\text{gas}}}{(A/F)_{\text{gas}}}$$

$$\frac{1}{(A/F)_{\text{blend}}} = \frac{0.8568}{6.43} + \frac{0.1432}{14.7}$$

$$\frac{1}{(A/F)_{\text{blend}}} = 0.1332 + 0.00974 = 0.1430$$

**Step 3 — Invert to get A/F for M85:**

$$(A/F)_{\text{M85}} = \frac{1}{0.1430} = 6.99$$

> **Final Answer:** The stoichiometric A/F ratio of M85 by mass is **≈ 6.99 kg air / kg fuel**.
>
> **Interpretation:** M85 requires only **6.99 kg of air per kg of fuel** — much less than pure gasoline (14.7) because methanol carries 50% of its mass as oxygen. The engine ECU must be recalibrated to deliver this much lower A/F (i.e., richer in fuel mass terms but leaner in air mass terms).

---

### Question B
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module Choice, Slot 2 — Internal Alternative]**
**Q: (a)** Discuss the various methods of using alcohol fuels in Compression Ignition (CI / diesel) engines, with a neat sketch of a dual-fuel alcohol-diesel system. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An SI engine operates on E20 (20% ethanol + 80% gasoline by volume). Given the LHV of ethanol is 26.9 MJ/kg, LHV of gasoline is 44 MJ/kg, density of ethanol is 789 kg/m³, density of gasoline is 750 kg/m³, and the engine's BSFC on gasoline is 0.27 kg/kWh, calculate the BSFC on E20 and comment on the fuel economy. Assume the same thermal efficiency on both fuels. **[7 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — Model Answer

**Methods of Using Alcohols in CI Engines:**

**Method 1: Dual-Fuel Mode (Pilot Diesel Ignition)**
- 80–90% alcohol inducted through intake manifold (as vapor, fumigation, or port injection)
- 10–20% diesel injected directly into cylinder as **pilot fuel** to provide ignition
- The diesel auto-ignites, igniting the alcohol-air mixture
- Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR) typically 70–85%
- Knock resistance: high (auto-ignition controlled by diesel injection timing)

**Method 2: Alcohol Fumigation (Indirect Induction)**
- Alcohol is vaporized in a separate vaporizer using exhaust heat
- Vapor is inducted through the intake manifold
- Mixed with intake air; diesel is injected as usual
- **Limitation:** Vaporization rate limits maximum alcohol substitution
- **Power loss:** up to 15–20% due to charge cooling and displacement of air by alcohol vapor

**Method 3: Alcohol-Diesel Emulsion**
- Alcohol and diesel mechanically emulsified with surfactants (e.g., Span 80, Tween 80)
- 5–15% alcohol in emulsion (higher % leads to instability)
- Direct injection of emulsion in unmodified CI engine
- **Challenge:** Emulsion stability over time; demixing in storage

**Method 4: Cetane Improver Blending**
- Add 5–10% **diethyl ether, ethyl nitrate, or 2-ethylhexyl nitrate** to the alcohol
- Raises cetane number of the blend to 40–45
- Allows direct injection in modern common-rail CI engines

**Method 5: Glow Plug / Hot-Bulb Ignition**
- Use electrically heated glow plug or hot bulb (fired by exhaust heat) to initiate combustion
- Common in tractors and small generators running on ethanol

**Method 6: Reformed Alcohol (Hydrogen-Rich Gas)**
- Onboard reformer converts alcohol to H₂ + CO + CO₂
- Reformed gas is inducted or injected
- Essentially a **gaseous fuel** derived from a liquid

**Sketch of a Dual-Fuel Alcohol-Diesel System:**

```
              ┌──── Diesel Tank ────┐
              │                     │
              │   ┌─── Filter ──┐   │
              │   │             │   │
              └───┤ Pump        │   │
                  │             ▼   │
              ┌───┤       High-Pressure   ┌──────────────┐
              │   │       Diesel Injector │              │
              │   │             │         │   CYLINDER   │
              │   └─────────────┘         │              │
              │                           │   Combustion │
              │   ┌─── Alcohol Tank ──┐   │     occurs   │
              │   │                   │   │              │
              │   │   ┌── Filter ──┐  │   │              │
              │   │   │            │  │   │              │
              │   └───┤ Pump        │  │   │              │
              │       │            ▼  │   │              │
              │       │  Port Injector ─────► Intake      │
              │       │            │      │   Manifold    │
              │       └────────────┘      └──────────────┘
              │                                    │
              └──── ECU Governor and Control ◄────┘
```

**Operational Sequence:**
1. ECU decides energy substitution ratio (ESR) based on load and speed.
2. Diesel pump delivers metered pilot diesel to high-pressure injector.
3. Alcohol pump delivers metered alcohol to port injector.
4. Diesel spray auto-ignites → ignites alcohol-air mixture.
5. ECU continuously monitors combustion via in-cylinder pressure sensor and adjusts ESR.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute mass fractions of ethanol and gasoline in E20:**

$$m_{\text{EtOH}} = 0.20 \times 789 = 157.8 \text{ kg/m³}$$

$$m_{\text{gas}} = 0.80 \times 750 = 600.0 \text{ kg/m³}$$

$$m_{\text{total}} = 757.8 \text{ kg/m³}$$

$$w_{\text{EtOH}} = \frac{157.8}{757.8} = 0.2083$$

$$w_{\text{gas}} = 0.7917$$

**Step 2 — Compute LHV of E20 (mass basis):**

$$LHV_{\text{E20}} = 0.2083 \times 26.9 + 0.7917 \times 44$$

$$LHV_{\text{E20}} = 5.603 + 34.83 = 40.43 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute BSEC on gasoline (baseline):**

$$BSEC_{\text{gas}} = 0.27 \times 44 = 11.88 \text{ MJ/kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Compute BSFC on E20 (same thermal efficiency ⇒ same BSEC):**

$$BSEC_{\text{E20}} = 11.88 \text{ MJ/kWh}$$

$$BSFC_{\text{E20}} = \frac{BSEC_{\text{E20}}}{LHV_{\text{E20}}} = \frac{11.88}{40.43} = 0.294 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Compare and comment on fuel economy:**

$$\frac{BSFC_{\text{E20}}}{BSFC_{\text{gas}}} = \frac{0.294}{0.27} = 1.089$$

> **Final Answer:** The BSFC on E20 is **0.294 kg/kWh**, which is **~8.9% higher** than on gasoline (0.27 kg/kWh).
>
> **Comment on Fuel Economy:**
> - On **mass basis**, E20 uses **8.9% more fuel** per kWh.
> - On **energy basis**, fuel economy is **identical** (same BSEC, same thermal efficiency).
> - On **volume basis (pump-to-wheel)**: Volume BSFC on E20 = $0.294 / 757.8 \times 10^3 = 0.000388$ m³/kWh = **0.388 L/kWh**, versus $0.27/750 \times 10^3 = 0.36$ L/kWh on gasoline — a **7.7% increase in volumetric fuel consumption**.
> - Real-world: this loss is largely **offset by the lower per-litre price of ethanol** in countries with subsidy (India, Brazil). Net cost per km is typically **2–5% lower** on E20.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in OEMUT615 Alcohol Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "mass A/F" with "air-mass A/F":** When computing stoichiometric A/F, many students forget the convention (6.43 for methanol is **air mass / fuel mass**, not O₂ mass / fuel mass). KTU accepts both interpretations but deducts ½ mark if units are unclear.
> 2. **Forgetting the volume-to-mass conversion** in blend problems: E85, M85 are *volume* blends, but LHV is in *mass* basis. Skipping density conversion = lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Writing "alcohols produce more energy" or "alcohols have higher calorific value":** This is the **opposite** of the truth. Alcohols have **lower** heating values. Such statements fetch negative marking.
> 4. **Ignoring material compatibility:** When asked about challenges, students often miss the **rubber swelling, aluminium corrosion, and water-ethanol phase separation** points. These are 1-mark easy points.
> 5. **Wrong production route:** "Methanol is obtained by fermentation" — **WRONG** (this is ethanol). Fermentation is exclusively for ethanol (and butanol via ABE). Methanol is from syngas.
> 6. **Confusing latent heat of vaporization with heating value:** Latent heat causes **charge cooling** (good for knock, bad for cold start). Heating value is a *combustion* property. Do not interchange them.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- [x] **Alcohols = oxygenated fuels** carrying -OH group; general formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}\text{OH}$.
- [x] **Three principal alcohol fuels:** Methanol (CH₃OH), Ethanol (C₂H₅OH), n-Butanol (C₄H₉OH).
- [x] **Methanol** — produced from **syngas** (CO + 2H₂ → CH₃OH); toxic; LHV 19.7 MJ/kg; RON 112; stoichiometric A/F 6.43.
- [x] **Ethanol** — produced by **fermentation** of sugar/starch/lignocellulose ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \to 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2$); LHV 26.9 MJ/kg; RON 110; A/F 9.0.
- [x] **n-Butanol** — ABE fermentation; LHV 33.1 MJ/kg; CN ≈ 25; can run in CI engines directly up to B40.
- [x] **High latent heat** = charge cooling (good: high octane, low NOₓ) and **cold-start difficulty** (bad).
- [x] **High octane** allows **higher compression ratio** → higher Otto cycle efficiency.
- [x] **BSEC remains comparable** to gasoline even though BSFC (mass basis) is **1.5–2× higher**.
- [x] **Material compatibility issues:** Replace rubber seals with **Viton (FKM)**, replace aluminium with stainless steel.
- [x] **Phase separation** in ethanol-gasoline blends when water content exceeds tolerance.
- [x] **Dual-fuel CI engines** use 80–90% alcohol (intake inducted) + 10–20% diesel (pilot injection).
- [x] **Alcohol fumigation** is the simplest CI retrofit but causes 10–15% power loss.
- [x] **n-Butanol is the "drop-in" alcohol** — closest to gasoline in properties; can be blended with diesel in B20–B40 range.
- [x] **Brazil = world's largest bioethanol programme**; **India E20 = 2025–2030 mandate**; **USA RFS** mandates 15+ billion gallons/year.
- [x] **Stoichiometric A/F formula** (must memorize): $A/F = \dfrac{32(x + y/4 - z/2)}{12x + y + 16z}$.
- [x] **Energy density ratio:** Methanol 0.45, Ethanol 0.61, n-Butanol 0.75 (relative to gasoline).

> **Final Tip for KTU OEMUT615 Aspirants:** Always link your answer to a **real-world industrial example** (Brazil ProÁlcool, India E20, China M100, US RFS). KTU examiners reward **practical orientation** in OEMUT615. A 14-mark answer that mentions "Brazil" or "India E20" or "flex-fuel vehicle" gets at least **1–2 extra marks** over a purely theoretical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethanol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ethanol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethanol (Chemical Formula: C₂H₅OH)**, also known as **ethyl alcohol**, **grain alcohol**, or **bio-ethanol**, is a clear, colourless, flammable, oxygenated hydrocarbon liquid with a characteristic mild alcoholic odour. It is a primary monohydric alcohol belonging to the aliphatic homologous series. In the context of alternate fuels, ethanol is a **bio-derived renewable liquid fuel** produced principally from the fermentation of sugars, starches, or lignocellulosic biomass, and is used either in its neat form (E100) or as a gasoline blend-stock (E5, E10, E20, E85, E95) in Spark-Ignition (SI) engines, with limited applications in Compression-Ignition (CI) engines through fumigation, dual-fuelling, or emulsification techniques.

The molecular structure of ethanol consists of an **ethyl group (–C₂H₅)** bonded to a **hydroxyl group (–OH)**. The presence of the hydroxyl group imparts a polar character to the otherwise non-polar hydrocarbon chain, which is the root cause of its unique physical, chemical, and combustion properties relevant to engine applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Breath-Freshener Fuel":**
> Think of ethanol as the alcohol found in your hand-sanitiser or the spirit used in cough syrups — chemically, that is the very same molecule. When yeast consumes sugar (think: rotting grapes producing wine), it exhales ethanol as a "waste product" via fermentation. Now imagine the same liquid that can sterilise a wound or intoxicate a person also being pumped into a petrol tank to propel a 1.5-tonne car. This is the essence of ethanol as an **alternate fuel**: it is a biologically produced liquid that closely mimics gasoline's behaviour in an engine but carries a small parcel of **chemically bound oxygen** (about 35% by mass) inside every molecule, making it burn more completely and emit less carbon monoxide.

The bound oxygen in ethanol's hydroxyl group is the single most important reason it is considered a "cleaner" fuel. Gasoline is a pure hydrocarbon (CHₓ) requiring atmospheric oxygen for complete combustion; ethanol brings its own oxygen to the party, like a guest who brings their own food to a dinner table.

### 1.3 Key Physical and Chemical Properties

| Property | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Chemical Formula | **C₂H₅OH** or **C₂H₆O** | Determines stoichiometry |
| Molecular Weight | **46.07 g/mol** | Lower than gasoline (~110 g/mol) |
| Density at 20 °C | **0.789 kg/L** | Affects energy density per volume |
| Boiling Point | **78.37 °C** | Low — improves cold start |
| Flash Point | **12.8 °C** (closed cup) | High volatility — fire hazard |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | **363 °C** | Higher than gasoline (246 °C) |
| Research Octane Number (RON) | **108–110** | Excellent knock resistance |
| Motor Octane Number (MON) | **89–92** | Sensitive fuel |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **26.9 MJ/kg** | ~67% of gasoline |
| Stoichiometric A/F ratio (mass) | **9.0 : 1** | Lower than gasoline (14.7 : 1) |
| Latent Heat of Vaporisation | **904 kJ/kg** | ~3× gasoline — strong charge cooling |
| Flammability Limits (vol %) | 3.3 – 19.0 | Wider than gasoline |
| Water Solubility | **Miscible in all proportions** | Major storage concern |
| Carbon Content (mass) | **52.17 %** | Lower → lower CO₂ per unit energy |
| Hydrogen Content (mass) | **13.04 %** | — |
| Oxygen Content (mass) | **34.78 %** | Self-oxygenating |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 2 specifically tests the student's ability to **compare ethanol properties against gasoline and diesel**, identify the engineering consequences of the **low LHV and high latent heat**, and explain the role of **bound oxygen** in emission reduction. Memorise the **RON, LHV, stoichiometric A/F ratio, and auto-ignition temperature** — these are recurring 3-mark questions.

### 1.4 Why Ethanol Qualifies as an Alternate Fuel

The KTU 2024 scheme defines an alternate fuel as one that is **renewable, domestically producible, environmentally superior (in well-to-wheel basis), and technologically compatible** with existing or moderately modified internal combustion engines. Ethanol satisfies all four criteria:

1. **Renewable** — derived from biomass through short carbon cycle (CO₂ absorbed by crop = CO₂ emitted on combustion).
2. **Domestic Availability** — India has a National Policy on Biofuels (2018, amended 2022) targeting **20% ethanol blending (E20) by 2025–26 (ESY 2030)**.
3. **Environmental Superiority** — Reduces CO, unburnt HC, particulate matter (in CI), and aromatic emissions. Knock index better than gasoline allows higher CR without knock.
4. **Engine Compatibility** — Used in SI engines with **minimal modifications** (fuel system, cold-start enrichment, materials compatibility).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Production Pathways of Ethanol

Ethanol production for fuel applications is broadly classified into **three generations** based on the feedstock and conversion route.

#### 2.1.1 First-Generation (1G) Ethanol — Sugar/Starch Fermentation

This is the most commercially mature route and the dominant technology in India.

- **Feedstocks:** Sugarcane molasses, sugar beet, maize (corn), cassava, sorghum, sweet sorghum, and damaged food grains.
- **Process Steps:**
  1. **Feedstock Preparation** — Milling sugarcane, crushing corn, or diluting molasses.
  2. **Saccharification (for starches)** — Enzymatic hydrolysis using **α-amylase** and **glucoamylase** to break down starch into fermentable glucose.
  3. **Fermentation** — Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) converts hexose sugars (C₆H₁₂O₆) into ethanol and CO₂ under anaerobic conditions at 25–35 °C over 48–72 hours.
  4. **Distillation** — Separation of ethanol from the fermented "beer" (~10% ethanol) using multi-column distillation (rising to 95.6% v/v — the **azeotrope**).
  5. **Dehydration** — Molecular sieves (zeolite 3Å) or azeotropic distillation (using benzene/cyclohexane) to produce **anhydrous ethanol (>99.5% v/v)** required for fuel blending.

**Core fermentation chemistry:**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast, 25-35 °C}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -92\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical yield:** 0.511 kg ethanol per kg glucose. **Practical yield** is typically 90–95% of theoretical (~0.46 kg/kg).

#### 2.1.2 Second-Generation (2G) Ethanol — Lignocellulosic Route

Uses non-food biomass — **agricultural residues** (rice straw, wheat straw, bagasse, corn stover), **forestry waste**, or **dedicated energy crops** (switchgrass, miscanthus).

- **Major Challenge:** Lignocellulose is a recalcitrant matrix of **cellulose (35–50%)**, **hemicellulose (20–35%)**, and **lignin (15–30%)** that resists enzymatic attack.
- **Process Steps:**
  1. **Pretreatment** — Physical (milling, extrusion), chemical (dilute H₂SO₄, lime, ammonia), physico-chemical (steam explosion at 160–240 °C, 1–4 MPa), or biological (white-rot fungi) to break the lignin sheath and expose cellulose.
  2. **Enzymatic Hydrolysis** — Cellulase and hemicellulase enzymes break cellulose into glucose and hemicellulose into C5/C6 sugars.
  3. **Fermentation** — Requires genetically modified yeasts or *Zymomonas mobilis* capable of co-fermenting **pentoses (xylose, arabinose)** and hexoses.
  4. **Distillation & Dehydration** — As in 1G.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** India has set up India's first 2G ethanol demonstration plant at **Panipat (Indian Oil Corp., 2019)** using rice straw. Expect 2G ethanol to appear in Part B 14-mark questions involving biomass-to-liquid flowsheet design.

#### 2.1.3 Third-Generation (3G) Ethanol — Algal Route

- **Feedstock:** Microalgae (*Chlorella*, *Spirulina*) with very high photosynthetic efficiency.
- **Process:** Cultivation in open ponds or photobioreactors → cell disruption → hydrolysis → fermentation.
- **Status:** Research and pilot scale; not commercially deployed.

#### 2.1.4 Thermochemical / Synthetic Ethanol Route

- **Feedstock:** Coal, natural gas, syngas (CO + H₂).
- **Process:** Catalytic hydration of ethylene (C₂H₄ + H₂O → C₂H₅OH) or fermentation of syngas using **acetogenic bacteria** (*Clostridium ljungdahlii*).
- **Relevance:** Although technically "synthetic" and non-renewable, this provides a hedge against feedstock scarcity. **Synthetic ethanol from natural gas is used in India for industrial-grade ethanol.**

### 2.2 Ethanol as an SI Engine Fuel — Theoretical Framework

#### 2.2.1 Combustion Chemistry of Ethanol in SI Engine

Complete combustion in the presence of atmospheric oxygen:

$$C_2H_5OH + 3\,O_2 \longrightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O \quad \Delta H_{c} = -1366.8\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (by mass):**

$$\text{A/F}_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{3 \times 4.76 \times 28.97}{46.07} = 9.0$$

where 4.76 is the mass ratio of air-to-O₂ in atmospheric air (28.97/32 ≈ 4.76), and 46.07 is the molecular weight of ethanol.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison with Gasoline:** Gasoline (assumed as C₈H₁₈, octane surrogate) has A/F_stoich ≈ 14.7. The much lower A/F ratio of ethanol means **the engine must draw ~63% more fuel mass** (or ~13% more volume, since ethanol density is higher) per unit of air admitted. This directly impacts the carburettor / port-injection calibration and the energy content per intake stroke.

#### 2.2.2 Effect of Ethanol on Octane Rating and Knock Resistance

The **Research Octane Number (RON) of 108–110** and **Motor Octane Number (MON) of 89–92** for ethanol substantially exceed those of regular gasoline (RON 91, MON 82). This is attributed to:
- The **OH radical** chain-branching mechanism that retards auto-ignition.
- The **high latent heat of vaporisation** (904 kJ/kg) which cools the end-gas, reducing its propensity to auto-ignite.

**Engineering Consequence:** A vehicle running on E85 can be designed with a **higher compression ratio (CR ≈ 12:1 vs. 9:1 for gasoline)**. The increase in CR yields **higher thermal efficiency (Otto cycle η = 1 - r^(1-γ))**, more than compensating for ethanol's lower LHV on a per-mass basis. On a **volumetric basis**, E85 has only ~24% less energy than gasoline, but the higher CR recovers most of the deficit and can even give higher **brake thermal efficiency (28–32% vs. 25–28% for gasoline)**.

#### 2.2.3 Cold-Start and Vaporisation Behaviour

Ethanol's **high latent heat of vaporisation (904 kJ/kg)** and **low boiling point (78.4 °C)** have two opposing effects:
- **Positive:** Excellent charge cooling → higher volumetric efficiency → reduced tendency to knock at high load.
- **Negative:** At ambient temperatures below 10 °C, ethanol vapour pressure is too low to form a combustible mixture in the cylinder → **cold-start difficulty**, particularly below 5 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Solution employed in Flex-Fuel Vehicles (FFVs):** A **cold-start injector** squirts a small pulse of neat gasoline into the intake manifold during the first 30–90 seconds of cranking to provide a combustible vapour. Once the engine warms up, the ethanol blend becomes self-sustaining.

#### 2.2.4 Blends of Ethanol and Gasoline

| Blend | Ethanol Volume % | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **E5** | 5% | Universal gasoline — no engine modification required |
| **E10** | 10% | Standard blend in USA, Brazil; compatible with most modern vehicles |
| **E20** | 20% | India target (ESY 2030) under Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme |
| **E25 / E30** | 25–30% | Requires calibration changes |
| **E85** | 85% | Flex-fuel vehicles (FFV) — Brazil, USA, EU |
| **E95 / E100** | 95–100% | Specially adapted engines (e.g., Indian Army vehicles, Brazilian pure-ethanol fleet legacy) |

**Phase Separation in E-Blends:**
Ethanol is hygroscopic and miscible with water. When water content exceeds ~0.5% v/v in a gasoline-ethanol blend, the mixture separates into two phases — an upper gasoline-rich layer and a lower ethanol-water layer. This **phase separation** is the principal technical barrier to E-blend distribution in humid climates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Co-solvent Approach:** Adding **5–15% isobutanol or higher alcohols** suppresses phase separation, improves water tolerance, and is an active area of research. KTU may ask this as a design question.

#### 2.2.5 Ethanol in Compression-Ignition (CI / Diesel) Engines

Ethanol's **high auto-ignition temperature (363 °C)** and **low cetane number (≈ 8)** make it **unsuitable for direct injection in a conventional diesel engine**. Three practical strategies are employed:

1. **Fumigation / Port-Injection Dual Fuelling:**
   Ethanol is vapourised and introduced into the intake manifold using a low-pressure gas mixer or secondary port injector. **Diesel is pilot-injected near TDC** to provide the ignition source. Typical diesel replacement: **20–40% on energy basis**.

2. **Emulsification (Ethanol-Diesel-Water Emulsions):**
   Ethanol is blended with diesel using a surfactant (Span-80, Tween-80) to form a stable microemulsion with 5–15% ethanol and 1–5% water. Reduces NOx and PM simultaneously.

3. **Cetane Improver / Glow Plug Assisted Ignition:**
   Adding 2-ethylhexyl nitrate (cetane improver) raises the cetane number. A glow plug or hot-bullet igniter provides the initial heat source.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table below is the single most important study resource for this topic.** Every formula listed has appeared in KTU past papers. KTU examiners test both the *correct application* of the formula and the *unit consistency*.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Definitions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric A/F ratio (mass) | $\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \dfrac{4.76 \cdot m_{O_2,req}}{M_{fuel}}$ | $m_{O_2,req}$ = mass of O₂ per kg fuel from combustion equation; **4.76** is the mass ratio of air to O₂ |
| 2 | Mass of O₂ required per kg fuel | $m_{O_2} = \dfrac{32}{12}\,C + \dfrac{32}{4}\,H - \dfrac{32}{32}\,O$ | C, H, O are mass fractions of C, H, O in fuel |
| 3 | Theoretical combustion equation of ethanol | $C_2H_5OH + 3\,O_2 \rightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O$ | Stoichiometric O₂ = 3 mol per mol ethanol |
| 4 | LHV of ethanol (Hargrave formula) | $LHV = 0.349\,C + 1.178\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,A$ | Mass-basis MJ/kg; C,H,O,S,N,A = mass fractions; **water is vapour** |
| 5 | Theoretical air required per kg fuel | $m_{air} = \dfrac{m_{O_2}}{0.232}$ | 0.232 = mass fraction of O₂ in dry air |
| 6 | Brake thermal efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP \cdot 3600}{\dot{m}_{f} \cdot LHV}$ | BP in kW; $\dot{m}_f$ in kg/s; LHV in kJ/kg |
| 7 | Brake specific energy consumption (BSEC) | $BSEC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f} \cdot LHV}{BP}$ | MJ/kWh |
| 8 | Equivalent gasoline energy ratio | $EER = \dfrac{LHV_{ethanol} \cdot \rho_{ethanol}}{LHV_{gasoline} \cdot \rho_{gasoline}}$ | Used to compute "gasoline gallon equivalent" (GGE) |
| 9 | Compression ratio for ethanol blend | $r_{ethanol} = r_{gasoline} \cdot \left(\dfrac{RON_{blend}}{RON_{gasoline}}\right)^{1.2}$ | Empirical; allows up to CR 12–14 for E85 |
| 10 | Octane blending index | $RON_{blend} = \sum_i x_i \cdot RON_i$ (linear) | Not strictly linear; **RBOB** (Reformulated Blendstock for Oxygenate Blending) used in practice |
| 11 | Equivalence ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$ | φ < 1 = lean; φ > 1 = rich |
| 12 | Energy density ratio (ethanol/gasoline) | $\dfrac{(LHV \cdot \rho)_{ethanol}}{(LHV \cdot \rho)_{gasoline}} \approx 0.69$ | Pure ethanol has 69% of gasoline's volumetric energy |
| 13 | Octane sensitivity | $S = RON - MON$ | Ethanol: ~16–18; indicates knock margin |
| 14 | Volumetric efficiency correction | $\eta_{v,eth} = \eta_{v,gas} \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_{int,gas}}{T_{int,eth}}\right)^{0.7}$ | Higher latent heat of ethanol → cooler intake → higher $\rho$ → higher $\eta_v$ |
| 15 | Fermentation yield | $Y_{ethanol} = 0.511 \cdot Y_{sugar} \cdot \eta_{fermentation}$ | 0.511 = stoichiometric factor (g ethanol / g glucose) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoiding markdown-pipe conflicts:** When writing absolute values inside table cells (e.g., $|x|$), always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX — never the raw `|` character. The same applies to the KTU question bank in Section 5.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Ethanol in Real-World Applications

| Sector | Application | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Road Transport** | E10, E20, E85 passenger cars | Octane enhancement, CO & HC reduction |
| **Heavy Duty** | Diesel-ethanol dual-fuel buses and trucks | 20–40% diesel substitution; PM ↓ 50–70% |
| **Aviation** | ATJ (Alcohol-to-Jet) — ethanol → ethylene → jet fuel | Drop-in sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) |
| **Marine** | E-diesel microemulsions | NOx ↓ 15–20%, PM ↓ 30% |
| **Power Generation** | Ethanol-fuelled Gensets (Brazil) | Decentralised rural electrification |
| **Fuel Cells** | Direct Ethanol Fuel Cells (DEFC) | H₂-equivalent without reformer |
| **Cooking** | Ethanol stoves (Brazil, India pilot) | Indoor air quality improvement |
| **Defence** | Indian Army "Tatrakshak" ethanol-fuelled vehicles | Strategic oil substitution |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Stoichiometric A/F Ratio and Energy Density of Ethanol

**Problem (KTU-style):** Calculate the **(a)** stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (by mass) of ethanol, **(b)** mass of air required for complete combustion of 1 kg of ethanol, and **(c)** the volumetric energy density of ethanol compared to gasoline, given:
- Molecular formula: C₂H₅OH
- Molecular weight of ethanol: $M_f = 46.07\ \text{kg/kmol}$
- Molecular weight of air: $M_{air} = 28.97\ \text{kg/kmol}$
- Mass fraction of O₂ in air: 0.232
- LHV of ethanol: $26.9\ \text{MJ/kg}$
- LHV of gasoline: $44.0\ \text{MJ/kg}$
- Density of ethanol: $789\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- Density of gasoline: $745\ \text{kg/m}^3$

**Solution (Part a):**

**Step 1.** Write the balanced combustion equation for ethanol.

$$C_2H_5OH + a\,O_2 \longrightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O$$

Step 2. Apply atom balance on carbon. Number of C atoms on LHS = 2, RHS = b.

$$b = 2$$

Step 3. Apply atom balance on hydrogen. Number of H atoms on LHS = 6 (5+1), RHS = 2c.

$$2c = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = 3$$

Step 4. Apply atom balance on oxygen. Number of O atoms on LHS = 1 + 2a, RHS = 2b + c = 4 + 3 = 7.

$$1 + 2a = 7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = 3$$

The balanced equation is therefore:

$$C_2H_5OH + 3\,O_2 \longrightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O$$

Step 5. Convert the molar requirement to a mass basis. Per kmol of ethanol:

$$m_{O_2} = 3 \times 32 = 96\ \text{kg O}_2$$

Step 6. Convert O₂ to equivalent mass of air.

$$m_{air} = \dfrac{96}{0.232} = 413.79\ \text{kg air per kmol ethanol}$$

Step 7. Compute the mass-based A/F ratio.

$$\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{413.79}{46.07} = 8.98 \approx 9.0$$

$$\boxed{\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = 9.0 : 1}$$

**Solution (Part b):**

Step 8. Mass of air per kg of fuel is simply the A/F ratio.

$$m_{air} = 9.0\ \text{kg air / kg ethanol}$$

Step 9. By the principle of mass conservation, the combustion products (CO₂ + H₂O) per kg of ethanol are:

$$m_{CO_2} = \dfrac{2 \times 44}{46.07} = 1.91\ \text{kg}$$

$$m_{H_2O} = \dfrac{3 \times 18}{46.07} = 1.17\ \text{kg}$$

$$\text{Total products} = 1.91 + 1.17 + m_{N_2}$$

$$m_{N_2} = 9.0 \times 0.768 = 6.91\ \text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total products} = 1.91 + 1.17 + 6.91 = 9.99 \approx 10\ \text{kg}}$$

This validates mass conservation (1 kg fuel + 9 kg air = 10 kg products). ✓

**Solution (Part c):**

Step 10. Compute volumetric energy density of ethanol.

$$E_{v,eth} = LHV_{eth} \times \rho_{eth} = 26.9 \times 789 = 21,224\ \text{MJ/km}^3 = 21.22\ \text{MJ/L}$$

Step 11. Compute volumetric energy density of gasoline.

$$E_{v,gas} = LHV_{gas} \times \rho_{gas} = 44.0 \times 745 = 32,780\ \text{MJ/km}^3 = 32.78\ \text{MJ/L}$$

Step 12. Compute the ratio.

$$\text{EDR} = \dfrac{21.22}{32.78} = 0.647$$

$$\boxed{\text{Ethanol delivers } 64.7\% \text{ of gasoline's energy per unit volume.}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is why a vehicle retrofitted to run on E85 suffers a ~24% drop in range unless the fuel tank volume is increased or the engine compression ratio is raised to recover efficiency.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Fermentation Mass Balance for 1G Ethanol

**Problem:** A sugar mill produces 1000 kg/h of molasses containing 50% (mass) total sugars. The fermentation efficiency is 92% of theoretical, distillation recovery is 96%, and dehydration loss is 2%. Compute the rate of anhydrous ethanol production (kg/h) and the CO₂ co-product (kg/h).

**Solution:**

Step 1. Mass of fermentable sugar entering the fermenter.

$$\dot{m}_{sugar} = 1000 \times 0.50 = 500\ \text{kg/h}$$

Step 2. Theoretical ethanol yield (stoichiometric).

$$Y_{th} = 0.511\ \text{kg ethanol / kg glucose}$$

Step 3. Actual ethanol produced in fermentation (accounting for 92% efficiency).

$$\dot{m}_{ethanol,fermenter} = 500 \times 0.511 \times 0.92 = 235.06\ \text{kg/h}$$

Step 4. CO₂ co-produced in fermentation (stoichiometric: 1 kg glucose → 0.489 kg CO₂).

$$\dot{m}_{CO_2,fermenter} = 500 \times 0.489 \times 0.92 = 224.94\ \text{kg/h}$$

Step 5. Recovered after distillation (96% recovery).

$$\dot{m}_{ethanol,dist} = 235.06 \times 0.96 = 225.66\ \text{kg/h}$$

Step 6. Anhydrous ethanol after dehydration (98% retention).

$$\dot{m}_{ethanol,anh} = 225.66 \times 0.98 = 221.15\ \text{kg/h}$$

Step 7. Final answer.

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{ethanol,anhyd} \approx 221.15\ \text{kg/h} \quad ; \quad \dot{m}_{CO_2} \approx 225\ \text{kg/h}}$$

Step 8. Overall yield relative to feed (molasses).

$$Y_{overall} = \dfrac{221.15}{1000} = 0.221\ \text{kg ethanol / kg molasses} = 22.1\%$$

This is consistent with **commercial 1G plant yields of 80–100 L ethanol per tonne sugarcane in India**.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Brake Thermal Efficiency Comparison Between E20 and Gasoline

**Problem:** A 4-cylinder 1.5 L SI engine operates on **gasoline** with brake power $BP_g = 52\ \text{kW}$ and fuel consumption $\dot{m}_{f,g} = 12.0\ \text{kg/h}$. When switched to **E20 (20% ethanol + 80% gasoline by volume)**, all operating conditions are kept identical and the fuel consumption rises to $\dot{m}_{f,E20} = 13.0\ \text{kg/h}$ at the same BP. LHV of gasoline = 44 MJ/kg; LHV of ethanol = 26.9 MJ/kg; density of blend = 0.760 kg/L. Compute:
(a) $\eta_{bth,g}$
(b) $\eta_{bth,E20}$
(c) Comment on the result.

**Solution:**

Step 1. Compute the LHV of the blend (mass-weighted average, given mass fractions need to be derived from volume fractions and densities).

First, the mass fraction of ethanol in the blend:

$$x_{eth,vol} = 0.20 \quad ; \quad x_{gas,vol} = 0.80$$

$$m_{eth} = V \cdot 0.20 \cdot \rho_{eth} = V \cdot 0.20 \cdot 789 = 157.8\,V$$

$$m_{gas} = V \cdot 0.80 \cdot \rho_{gas} = V \cdot 0.80 \cdot 745 = 596.0\,V$$

$$x_{eth,mass} = \dfrac{157.8}{157.8 + 596.0} = \dfrac{157.8}{753.8} = 0.2094$$

$$x_{gas,mass} = 0.7906$$

Step 2. Compute blend LHV (mass-weighted).

$$LHV_{blend} = 0.2094 \times 26.9 + 0.7906 \times 44.0 = 5.633 + 34.79 = 40.42\ \text{MJ/kg}$$

Step 3. Compute $\eta_{bth,g}$.

$$\eta_{bth,g} = \dfrac{BP \cdot 3600}{\dot{m}_{f,g} \cdot LHV_g} = \dfrac{52 \times 3600}{12.0 \times 44000} = \dfrac{187200}{528000}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{bth,g} = 0.3545 = 35.45\%}$$

Step 4. Compute $\eta_{bth,E20}$.

$$\eta_{bth,E20} = \dfrac{52 \times 3600}{13.0 \times 40420} = \dfrac{187200}{525460}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{bth,E20} = 0.3563 = 35.63\%}$$

Step 5. Interpretation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** Although E20 has ~8% lower heating value per kg, the **brake thermal efficiency is essentially unchanged (marginally higher by 0.18%)**. This is because ethanol's higher latent heat and octane rating improve combustion phasing and reduce heat-transfer losses. The slightly higher fuel consumption (8.3%) is offset by these efficiency gains, giving **comparable BSFC on an energy basis** but slightly higher fuel cost per kilometre.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Ethanol vs. Gasoline vs. Diesel — A Comparative Snapshot

| Property | **Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)** | **Gasoline (C₄–C₁₂)** | **Diesel (C₁₂–C₂₀)** | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Source | Renewable (biomass) | Crude oil | Crude oil | Energy security / import bill |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 26.9 | 44.0 | 42.5 | Lower → larger tank needed |
| Density (kg/L) | 0.789 | 0.745 | 0.830 | Volumetric energy still 65% of gasoline |
| RON | 108–110 | 91–98 | — | High CR possible |
| Cetane Number | 8 | 10–15 | 45–55 | Poor CI ignition |
| A/F (stoich) | 9.0 | 14.7 | 14.5 | Fuel system calibration |
| Latent Heat (kJ/kg) | 904 | 305 | 270 | Charge cooling, knock resistance |
| Auto-Ignition (°C) | 363 | 246 | 210 | Cold-start difficulty in SI |
| O₂ in fuel (mass%) | 34.8 | 0 | 0 | Lean burn / lower CO & PM |
| Water tolerance | Fully miscible | Immiscible | Immiscible | Phase separation / corrosion |
| Material compatibility | Damages aluminium, certain plastics, rubber | Compatible | Compatible | FFV fuel lines, tanks, seals |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — 1G Ethanol Production Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feedstock Sugarcane Molasses] --> B[Feed Preparation<br/>Dilution pH adjustment]
    B --> C[Fermentation<br/>Yeast 25-35 degC<br/>48-72 hours]
    C --> D[Beer 8-12 percent ethanol<br/>CO2 by-product vented]
    D --> E[Multi-Column Distillation<br/>Beer Column stripping]
    E --> F[Rectification Column<br/>95.6 percent azeotrope]
    F --> G[Dehydration Unit<br/>Molecular Sieve 3A Zeolite]
    G --> H[Anhydrous Ethanol<br/>99.5 percent v/v]
    H --> I[Denaturant Addition<br/>2-5 percent gasoline]
    I --> J[Fuel Grade Ethanol<br/>IS 15464 Standard]
    C --> K[CO2 Scrubbing<br/>Capture and Liquefy]
    K --> L[Industrial CO2 Supply]
    D --> M[Spent Wash<br/>Vinasse]
    M --> N[Biogas Plant<br/>Anaerobic Digestion]
    N --> O[CBG Compressed Biogas]
    N --> P[Bio-manure / Fertigation]
```

```mermaid
        D4 --> C1
    end

```

```mermaid
    A[Driver Starts Engine] --> B{Engine Coolant Temp}
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> List any three significant differences between ethanol and gasoline as SI engine fuels, highlighting the role of bound oxygen in ethanol.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Oxygen content:** Ethanol contains ~34.8% chemically bound oxygen by mass, while gasoline has none. This bound oxygen promotes **more complete combustion**, reducing **CO and unburnt HC** emissions by 15–30%.
2. **Octane rating:** Ethanol has a **RON of 108–110** versus gasoline's 91–98, permitting **higher compression ratios** (11–13:1 vs. 9:1), which improves brake thermal efficiency by 3–6 percentage points.
3. **Latent heat of vaporisation:** Ethanol's latent heat (904 kJ/kg) is **~3× gasoline's** (305 kJ/kg). This cools the intake charge, increases volumetric efficiency, and suppresses engine knock, but causes **cold-start difficulty** below 10 °C.

*(Award 1 mark for each correct difference, with explicit mention of the numerical values to score full 3.)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> What is "phase separation" in ethanol-gasoline blends, and how is it prevented?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Phase separation** is the splitting of an ethanol-gasoline blend into two immiscible liquid layers when the **water content exceeds ~0.5% v/v**. The lower layer is an ethanol-water rich phase, while the upper layer is gasoline-lean. The blend is no longer homogeneous, leading to erratic engine operation, filter icing, and corrosion.
- **Prevention strategies:** (i) Use **anhydrous ethanol (>99.5% v/v)** for blending; (ii) Maintain **dry storage** in sealed, nitrogen-padded tanks; (iii) Add a **co-solvent** (e.g., 5–15% isobutanol, n-butanol, or higher alcohol) which suppresses phase separation by improving the mutual solubility of ethanol, gasoline, and water; (iv) Distribute blends through dedicated pipelines and bottom-loading tankers to prevent moisture ingress.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEM Module 2]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **production of bio-ethanol from sugarcane molasses** (1G route), with a **neat flow diagram** and the **stoichiometric fermentation equation**. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Discuss the **effect of ethanol-gasoline blends (E5, E10, E20, E85)** on the **performance and emissions of a 4-stroke SI engine**, with a comparative table of properties. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — 1G Ethanol Production (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Feedstock and Pre-treatment:**
Sugarcane molasses, a viscous by-product of sugar refining containing **45–55% total sugars** (sucrose, glucose, fructose), is the most common Indian feedstock. The molasses is diluted with water to bring the sugar concentration to **15–20% w/v**, and pH is adjusted to **4.5–5.0** using **H₂SO₄** to optimise yeast activity. The solution is sterilised (pasteurised at 80–90 °C for 30 min) to eliminate contaminating microorganisms.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Fermentation:**
The diluted, sterilised mash is cooled to **25–35 °C** and inoculated with **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** yeast (1–2% w/v). Fermentation proceeds anaerrobically in large closed fermenters for **48–72 hours**. The biochemistry is:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase (Yeast)}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -92\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

Theoretical yield = **0.511 kg ethanol/kg glucose**. Practical yield is 90–95% of theoretical. CO₂ is vented, scrubbed, and sold as a co-product.

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Distillation:**
The "beer" containing **8–12% ethanol** is fed to a multi-column distillation unit. The **beer column** strips ethanol from the mash; the **rectification column** concentrates the vapour to **95.6% v/v ethanol** — the binary azeotrope with water, beyond which simple distillation cannot proceed.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Dehydration:**
To break the azeotrope, the **95.6% ethanol** is passed through **molecular sieves (3 Å zeolite beads)**, which adsorb water selectively. The product is **anhydrous ethanol (>99.5% v/v)** meeting **IS 15464 / IS 5481 Grade E standards**.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Denaturation:**
Pure ethanol is taxed heavily as a potable liquor. To render it undrinkable, **2–5% gasoline (or denatonium benzoate)** is added, producing **fuel-grade ethanol**.

**Step 6 (2 marks) — Flow Diagram (already covered in Section 4.1) and Co-products:**
Co-products include **(i) CO₂** (~0.49 kg per kg sugar) — sold to beverage / welding industry; **(ii) Spent wash (vinasse)** — anaerobically digested to produce **biogas (CBG)** and **bio-manure** for the sugar mill's own cane fields, closing the nutrient loop.

#### Part (b) — Effect of E-Blends on SI Engines (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Property Table:**

| Property | Gasoline | E5 | E10 | E20 | E85 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RON | 91 | 92.4 | 93.7 | 96.5 | 105 |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 44.0 | 42.4 | 40.8 | 37.7 | 29.0 |
| A/F ratio | 14.7 | 14.0 | 13.4 | 12.1 | 10.0 |
| Stoich. λ-shift | 1.00 | 0.95 | 0.91 | 0.82 | 0.60 |
| Latent heat (kJ/kg) | 305 | 339 | 374 | 442 | 798 |

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Performance Effects:**
- **BSFC increases** with ethanol content (lower LHV) — BSFC for E85 is **~40% higher** than gasoline. However, **BSEC (energy basis)** is comparable or lower because ethanol's higher octane allows **CR increase from 9:1 to 12:1**, raising Otto cycle efficiency by ~10%.
- **Brake thermal efficiency** rises by 3–6 percentage points up to E85.
- **Volumetric efficiency** improves (~3–5%) due to charge cooling.
- **Cold start** is affected progressively — E85 cannot start below 5 °C without a gasoline pilot.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Emission Effects:**
- **CO ↓ 15–30%** (more complete combustion due to bound O₂).
- **HC ↓ 5–20%** (better flame propagation, higher flame speed).
- **NOx:** Ambiguous — slight increase in E20 (higher flame temperature) but decrease in E85 (lean burn).
- **CO₂ equivalent ↓ 20–40%** on a well-to-wheel basis (renewable carbon).
- **Aromatics and benzene ↓ 50%** — ethanol displaces toxic aromatic compounds.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Engine Modifications Required for E20+:**
- Corrosion-resistant **fuel lines, tank, pump** (stainless steel, HDPE).
- **Larger injector orifices** to compensate for higher fuel mass flow.
- **ECU recalibration** for new A/F and cold-start enrichment.
- **Material compatibility check** for aluminium, certain elastomers, and plastics.

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEM Module 2, Alternative Choice]**
> **(a)** Derive the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio** of ethanol (C₂H₅OH) and compute the **brake thermal efficiency** of an SI engine running on E20 with the following data: Brake Power = 50 kW, Fuel consumption = 12.5 kg/h, LHV of blend = 40.5 MJ/kg. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Explain the **dual-fuel fumigation technique** for using ethanol in a **CI engine**. Discuss its **performance, emissions, and limitations**. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Stoichiometric A/F + Brake Thermal Efficiency (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Combustion Equation and Stoichiometry:**

Balance the equation:

$$C_2H_5OH + a\,O_2 \rightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O$$

Carbon balance: $b = 2$
Hydrogen balance: $2c = 6 \Rightarrow c = 3$
Oxygen balance: $1 + 2a = 2(2) + 3 = 7 \Rightarrow a = 3$

$$\boxed{C_2H_5OH + 3\,O_2 \rightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Mass of O₂ per kmol ethanol:**

$$m_{O_2} = 3 \times 32 = 96\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Mass of air per kmol ethanol:**

$$m_{air} = \dfrac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \dfrac{96}{0.232} = 413.79\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — A/F ratio:**

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{413.79}{46.07} = 8.98 \approx 9.0 : 1$$

**Step 5 (2 marks) — Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP \cdot 3600}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV_{blend}} = \dfrac{50 \times 3600}{12.5 \times 40500} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{506{,}250}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{bth} = 0.3556 = 35.56\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** A 1.5 L naturally aspirated 4-stroke SI engine delivering 50 kW at 35.56% bth is operating **above the typical 28–32% range** because the data correspond to a **moderate CR (~10.5) engine** with E20's high octane. Students should comment on this in their answer to earn full marks.

#### Part (b) — Dual-Fuel Fumigation of Ethanol in CI Engines (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Concept:**
In **dual-fuel mode**, ethanol is introduced into the **intake manifold** of a diesel engine through a **vaporiser / port injector / gas mixer**, while **diesel is retained as the pilot fuel** injected near TDC. The diesel auto-ignites first, providing the high-temperature ignition kernel for the ethanol-air mixture. This is called **"fumigation"** because ethanol vapour "smokes" through the intake air, similar to fumigating a chamber.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — System Components:**
- Separate **ethanol tank** with low-pressure pump.
- **Vaporiser/heater** to vapourise ethanol using engine coolant heat.
- **Gas mixer / venturi** to premix ethanol vapour with intake air.
- Modified **diesel injection pump** to reduce pilot quantity at higher ethanol substitution.
- **ECU** for controlling ethanol and diesel mass flows based on load and speed.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Performance:**
- **BSFC on dual fuel** increases because ethanol has lower LHV and incomplete combustion efficiency.
- **Brake thermal efficiency** drops by 5–10% relative to pure diesel operation at the same load.
- **Diesel substitution** of 20–40% (energy basis) is achievable at **medium-to-high loads**.
- At **low loads**, less diesel is burned, cylinder temperature falls below ethanol's auto-ignition temperature → **misfire and efficiency collapse**.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Emissions:**
- **NOx ↓ 15–25%** (lower flame temperature due to ethanol's high latent heat).
- **PM (soot) ↓ 50–70%** (bound oxygen + lower aromatic content).
- **CO ↑ 20–50%** at low load (incomplete ethanol oxidation).
- **HC ↑** significantly if unvapourised ethanol escapes.

**Step 5 (0.5 marks) — Limitations:**
- Cold-start requires pure diesel.
- Ethanol-water phase separation can occur in the tank at low temperatures.
- Narrow operating window; system complexity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Stoichiometric A/F ratio:** Many students confuse the **molar** and **mass** ratios. KTU expects **mass-based A/F ≈ 9.0**. Writing "3 mol O₂ per mol ethanol" without converting to mass = 0.5 mark penalty.
> 2. **LHV value:** The exam permits either **26.9 MJ/kg (LHV)** or **29.7 MJ/kg (HHV)**. State which one you are using. Mismatch = mark deduction.
> 3. **Octane vs. Cetane:** Students often write that "ethanol has high cetane number." **It does not.** Cetane number of ethanol is ~8. Never use ethanol in a CI engine without a cetane improver or pilot diesel.
> 4. **Units in efficiency formula:** Using BP in kW, $\dot{m}_f$ in kg/h, and LHV in kJ/kg, **multiply by 3600** to convert h⁻¹ to s⁻¹. Skipping this factor = 1-mark penalty.
> 5. **Latent heat of vaporisation:** Many texts give ethanol latent heat as **842 kJ/kg** or **904 kJ/kg** depending on temperature. Use **904 kJ/kg at 78.4 °C (boiling point)**. Mismatched values are noted.
> 6. **Phase separation:** Do not confuse "phase separation temperature" with "cold-start temperature." They are unrelated phenomena.
> 7. **E20 vs. E10:** In India, **E10 is the current mandate (since 2022)** and **E20 is the 2025–26 (ESY 2030) target**. Mixing these up = 1 mark penalty.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before the Exam**

#### A. Core Numerical Values (Must Memorise)

- **Chemical formula:** C₂H₅OH
- **Molecular weight:** 46.07 g/mol
- **LHV:** 26.9 MJ/kg
- **HHV:** 29.7 MJ/kg
- **Density:** 789 kg/m³ (or 0.789 kg/L)
- **RON / MON:** 108 / 89
- **Cetane Number:** ~8
- **Stoich. A/F (mass):** 9.0 : 1
- **Boiling Point:** 78.4 °C
- **Auto-Ignition:** 363 °C
- **Flash Point:** 12.8 °C
- **Latent Heat of Vaporisation:** 904 kJ/kg
- **Oxygen content (mass):** 34.78%
- **Flammability limits:** 3.3 – 19.0 vol%
- **Theoretical fermentation yield:** 0.511 kg ethanol/kg glucose

#### B. Critical Concepts (One-Line Definitions)

- **Bound oxygen in ethanol:** The 34.8% chemically combined oxygen in C₂H₅OH that promotes complete combustion.
- **Phase separation:** Splitting of ethanol-gasoline blend into two phases when water exceeds ~0.5% v/v.
- **Fumigation:** Introduction of ethanol vapour into the intake manifold of a CI engine, with diesel as pilot.
- **Cold-start difficulty:** Ethanol cannot start a cold engine below ~5 °C due to low vapour pressure and high latent heat.
- **Anhydrous ethanol:** Ethanol with >99.5% v/v purity, required for fuel blending to avoid phase separation.
- **Azeotrope:** 95.6% v/v ethanol-water mixture, beyond which simple distillation cannot concentrate ethanol.
- **Octane sensitivity:** RON – MON; ethanol's value of ~16–18 is high.
- **Flex-Fuel Vehicle (FFV):** A vehicle designed to run on any blend from E0 to E85.
- **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) emissions:** Total CO₂-equivalent emissions from feedstock cultivation to tailpipe; ethanol typically gives 20–40% reduction.
- **ESY 2030 (Ethanol Supply Year 2030):** India's policy target year for achieving 20% ethanol blending (E20).

#### C. Reaction Equations (Must Memorise)

**Combustion:**
$$C_2H_5OH + 3\,O_2 \rightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O$$

**Fermentation:**
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$

**Catalytic hydration (synthetic ethanol):**
$$C_2H_4 + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4,\ 300\,°C} C_2H_5OH$$

#### D. Indian Policy Snapshot

- **National Policy on Biofuels, 2018** (amended 2022).
- **E20 target by ESY 2030** (originally 2025; extended).
- **2G ethanol plants:** Panipat (IOCL), Bhatinda (HPCL), Numaligarh, Davangere (Praj Industries).
- **Current installed capacity:** ~1,000 crore litres ethanol per annum (CLY target 2025–26).
- **Statutory body:** Ministry of Petroleum & Natural Gas (MoPNG); NITI Aayog oversight.

#### E. Application Snapshot

- **SI:** E5/E10/E20/E85 — direct blending.
- **CI:** Dual-fuel (fumigation), emulsions, E-diesel microemulsions.
- **Aviation:** ATJ (Alcohol-to-Jet) route.
- **Fuel cells:** Direct Ethanol Fuel Cell (DEFC) using proton-exchange membrane.

#### F. Comparative Memorisation Aid (Ethanol vs. Gasoline vs. Diesel)

| Feature | Ethanol | Gasoline | Diesel |
|---|---|---|---|
| RON | 108 | 91 | — |
| Cetane | 8 | 12 | 50 |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 26.9 | 44.0 | 42.5 |
| A/F | 9.0 | 14.7 | 14.5 |
| Latent heat (kJ/kg) | 904 | 305 | 270 |
| O₂ (mass%) | 34.8 | 0 | 0 |
| Source | Renewable | Fossil | Fossil |

#### G. Common Pitfalls to Avoid

1. Confusing **RBOB** (Reformulated Blendstock for Oxygenate Blending) with **CBOB** (Conventional Blendstock for Oxygenate Blending).
2. Stating that ethanol has "no emissions" — it still produces CO₂ and NOx.
3. Forgetting that **denatured ethanol** is what is used as fuel; **beverage ethanol** is not.
4. Confusing the **cold-start temperature** (5 °C) with the **flash point** (12.8 °C).
5. Using **HHV** instead of **LHV** in efficiency calculations without declaration.
6. Neglecting the **phase separation** issue in E-blend storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** In Part A, expect a **direct question on phase separation, octane rating, or A/F ratio**. In Part B, expect a **flowsheet-based question on 1G/2G ethanol production** OR a **comparative SI/CI engine performance question with calculations**. Always include a **neat labelled block diagram** — it almost always earns 1–2 "presentation marks" in KTU evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Methanol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Methanol (CH₃OH)**, also known as **methyl alcohol**, **wood alcohol**, or **methyl hydrate**, is the simplest saturated monohydric aliphatic alcohol. As an alternate internal combustion engine fuel, it is classified as an **oxygenated liquid fuel** because its molecular structure contains one oxygen atom covalently bonded to a methyl group (–CH₃).

In the context of **KTU OEMUT615 – Alternate Fuels and Energy Systems**, methanol occupies a pivotal position as a **first-generation liquid biofuel surrogate** and a **synthetic e-fuel** that can be produced from natural gas, coal, biomass, municipal solid waste, or directly from atmospheric CO₂ and green H₂.

**Chemical Identity Card of Methanol:**

| Property | Symbol / Unit | Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Molecular Formula | — | CH₃OH |
| Molar Mass | M | **32.04 kg/kmol** |
| Carbon Atoms per Molecule | n_C | 1 |
| Hydrogen Atoms per Molecule | n_H | 4 |
| Oxygen Atoms per Molecule | n_O | 1 |
| Oxygen Content (by mass) | w_O | **49.9 %** |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | T_ai | **470 °C** (≈ 743 K) |
| Flash Point (closed cup) | T_fp | **11 °C** |
| Latent Heat of Vaporisation | L_v | **1100 kJ/kg** |
| Lower Heating Value | LHV | **19.9 MJ/kg** |
| Higher Heating Value | HHV | **22.7 MJ/kg** |
| Research Octane Number | RON | **109–114** |
| Motor Octane Number | MON | **89–92** |
| Cetane Number | CN | **≈ 5** |
| Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio | AFR_st | **6.45 : 1** (by mass) |
| Density at 20 °C | ρ | **792 kg/m³** |
| Boiling Point | T_b | **64.7 °C** |
| Flammability Limits (in air) | φ | **6.0 % – 36.5 %** (vol.) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2:** Methanol is studied alongside ethanol, gaseous fuels (CNG, LPG, H₂), and biodiesel. It is *not* a true bio-derived fuel (since most global production is from natural gas via steam reforming), but it qualifies as an **alternate fuel** due to its ability to displace petroleum and its oxygenated, low-emission combustion behaviour.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "Methanol is to gasoline what a smoothie is to a milkshake."**
>
> Imagine gasoline as a thick, energy-dense milkshake made purely of hydrocarbons (C and H atoms only, no oxygen). Methanol is like a lighter, "diluted" smoothie where one of the hydrogen atoms has been replaced by an **oxygen atom** (OH group). This single substitution causes three major observable effects:
> 1. **It is lighter and less energy-dense** (LHV drops from 44 MJ/kg of gasoline to 19.9 MJ/kg of methanol — roughly half).
> 2. **It burns cooler and cleaner**, producing almost no soot or unburnt hydrocarbons because oxygen is *already* inside the fuel molecule.
> 3. **It is water-soluble and corrosive**, behaving more like an industrial solvent than a hydrocarbon fuel.
>
> In an engine, this means methanol can be used as a **pure fuel (M100)**, as a **high-blend with gasoline (M85)**, or as a **low-blend oxygenate (M3–M15)** without engine modifications, similar to how ethanol is blended.

The "single oxygen atom" is the entire reason methanol behaves differently from gasoline in combustion, emissions, and material compatibility.

---

## 1.3 Why Methanol Matters as an Alternate Fuel

The strategic importance of methanol in the energy transition rests on **four pillars**:

1. **Feedstock Versatility** — it can be synthesized from natural gas (currently cheapest route), coal, lignocellulosic biomass, municipal waste, or directly from CO₂ and H₂ ("e-methanol"). Methanol is, therefore, both a **bridge fuel** and a **renewable e-fuel**.
2. **High Octane Rating (RON ≈ 112)** — permits higher compression ratios and more aggressive turbocharging in SI engines, improving **brake thermal efficiency by 5–10 %** over gasoline.
3. **Inherent Oxygen Content (≈ 50 % by mass)** — enables **smoke-free combustion** in CI engines and dramatically reduces CO and particulate emissions.
4. **Liquid at Ambient Conditions** — unlike hydrogen (cryogenic) or CNG (high-pressure), methanol can be stored and transported using existing petroleum infrastructure with only **minor material upgrades (stainless steel / fluoropolymer linings)**.

---

## 1.4 Historical Context for KTU Examiners

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Methanol gained prominence during the **1970s oil crises** when Mobil Oil's **M-85 (85 % methanol + 15 % gasoline)** programme powered a fleet of vehicles in California. China has since become the world's largest producer and consumer of methanol fuel, with over **30,000 methanol-fuelled vehicles** operating in Shanxi and Shaanxi provinces under the **M100 programme**. The modern emphasis is on **green methanol** produced from renewable H₂ and captured CO₂, positioning methanol as a key decarbonization lever for shipping, heavy transport, and ICE-powered hybrid vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Production Routes for Methanol

Methanol is *never* found naturally — it must be **synthesized**. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically examines **three primary routes** and **one emerging route**:

### 2.1.1 Route A — From Natural Gas (Steam Reforming → Synthesis)
This is the **dominant industrial route**, accounting for ≈ 90 % of global methanol production.

**Step 1: Reforming.** Natural gas (chiefly CH₄) reacts with steam over a nickel catalyst at **800–900 °C** and **20–30 bar**:

$$CH_4 + H_2O \;\rightleftharpoons\; CO + 3H_2 \qquad \Delta H = +206 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

$$CO + H_2O \;\rightleftharpoons\; CO_2 + H_2 \qquad \Delta H = -41 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 2: Synthesis.** The resulting **syngas (CO + 2 H₂)** is compressed to **50–100 bar** and passed over a Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst at **220–280 °C**:

$$CO + 2H_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; CH_3OH \qquad \Delta H = -90.8 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The reforming step is **endothermic** and energy-intensive; modern plants integrate this with the exothermic synthesis step for thermal efficiency. The stoichiometric H₂/CO ratio required is exactly **2:1**, but real syngas deviates, requiring gas-ratio adjustment via the water-gas-shift reaction.

### 2.1.2 Route B — From Coal (Gasification → Synthesis)
Used predominantly in **China** due to coal abundance.

Coal is gasified with O₂ and H₂O at **> 1000 °C** to produce syngas with a **low H₂/CO ratio (≈ 0.4–0.7)**. The water-gas-shift reactor is then used to enrich H₂:

$$C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; CO \qquad \Delta H = -111 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

$$CO + H_2O \;\rightleftharpoons\; CO_2 + H_2$$

The downstream methanol synthesis step is identical to Route A.

### 2.1.3 Route C — From Biomass (Gasification)
A **carbon-neutral** route: lignocellulosic biomass (wood, agricultural residue) is gasified, the resulting syngas is cleaned of tars and ammonia, then fed to the methanol reactor. The energy efficiency is lower (≈ 35–45 %) due to drying and gas-cleaning losses.

### 2.1.4 Route D — From CO₂ and Green H₂ (E-Methanol) — **Emerging**

$$CO_2 + 3H_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; CH_3OH + H_2O \qquad \Delta H = -49.5 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

The H₂ is produced by **water electrolysis** powered by solar/wind. This is the **"Power-to-Methanol"** pathway and is a focal point of EU Green Deal research and India's **National Green Hydrogen Mission**.

---

## 2.2 Physical and Chemical Properties — Engineering Implications

The unusual combination of properties in methanol dictates its engine behaviour. KTU examiners frequently frame Part A questions around these properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-OH Functional Group — The Master Variable.**
> The hydroxyl group (–OH) makes methanol:
> * **Polar** (miscible with water, immiscible with hydrocarbons above the water tolerance limit).
> * **Acid-corrosive** to aluminum, magnesium, zinc, and certain elastomers.
> * **Hygroscopic** (absorbs water from atmosphere; reduces storage stability).
> * **High latent heat of vaporization** (≈ 1100 kJ/kg vs. ≈ 305 kJ/kg for gasoline) — cools the intake charge substantially.

### 2.2.1 Engine-Relevant Properties

| Engine Parameter | Methanol | Gasoline | Engineering Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Octane Rating (RON) | **112** | 92–98 | Allows CR ≈ 14:1 in SI engines, **30 % efficiency gain possible** |
| Flame Speed (m/s) | **0.52** | 0.40 | Faster, more complete combustion in SI engines |
| Flame Temperature (K) | 1870 | 2270 | Lower NOx tendency (without EGR) |
| Quenching Distance (mm) | **1.7** | 2.0 | Narrower → fewer unburnt HCs in crevices |
| Latent Heat (kJ/kg) | **1100** | 305 | **High volumetric efficiency**, cold-start difficulty |
| Energy Density (MJ/L) | **15.8** | 32 | Tank size must be ≈ 2× for equivalent range |
| Corrosivity | High (to Al, Zn) | Low | Requires stainless fuel system / fluoropolymer seals |

---

## 2.3 Combustion Chemistry of Methanol

### 2.3.1 Complete Combustion Reaction
The single-step global combustion equation is the **starting point for every KTU air-fuel calculation**:

$$CH_3OH + 1.5\,(O_2 + 3.773\,N_2) \;\longrightarrow\; CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 5.66\,N_2$$

Here, the factor **3.773 = 79/21** is the molar ratio of N₂ to O₂ in standard air. The coefficient 1.5 arises because methanol contains only **one C atom** (needs 1 mole O₂ for CO₂) and **four H atoms** (needs 1 mole O₂ for 2 H₂O), giving **1 + 1 − 0.5 (own oxygen) = 1.5 moles O₂ externally supplied** — the 0.5 mole of O is *already present* in the methanol molecule.

### 2.3.2 Incomplete / Pollutant-Forming Reactions
Real combustion deviates from stoichiometric, producing CO, formaldehyde (HCHO), and unburnt methanol:

$$CH_3OH + O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; HCHO + H_2O \qquad \text{(formaldehyde — toxic, regulated)}$$

$$CH_3OH + 1.5\,O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; CO + 2\,H_2O \qquad \text{(CO at fuel-rich conditions)}$$

### 2.3.3 Laminar Flame Speed
Methanol has a **higher laminar flame speed (≈ 0.52 m/s)** than gasoline (≈ 0.40 m/s), which shortens the combustion duration in SI engines and is one reason for **higher thermal efficiency** on a part-load basis.

---

## 2.4 Engine Application Modes

KTU 2024 examiners test the **five practical application modes** of methanol:

| Mode | Composition | Engine Type | Modification Required |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **M100** | 100 % methanol | SI (dedicated) | Major — fuel system, materials, cold start |
| **M85** | 85 % methanol + 15 % gasoline | FFV (SI) | Moderate — flex-fuel sensor |
| **M30–M50** | Mid-blend | SI | Minor — fuel system upgrade |
| **M3–M15** | Low-blend (oxygenate) | SI (stock) | **None** — works as gasoline additive |
| **M100 CI (dual fuel)** | Methanol + diesel pilot | CI | Pilot fuel injection + port methanol fumigation |

> [!NOTE]
> Methanol's **very low cetane number (≈ 5)** makes auto-ignition virtually impossible, so it **cannot be used as a pure CI fuel**. CI applications require either diesel pilot ignition, dual-fuel fumigation, or ignition-improver additives (cetane boosters or nitrate-based ignition promoters).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 8 equations. Every KTU numerical question in Module 2 revolves around one or more of them.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ demand | $m_{O_2}^{st} = \left( n_C + \tfrac{n_H}{4} - \tfrac{n_O}{2} \right) \cdot 32$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel | For CₙHₘOₚ fuels (universal) |
| 2 | Stoichiometric AFR | $\text{AFR}_{st} = \dfrac{m_{O_2}^{st} \cdot (1 + 3.773 \cdot 28/32)}{1}$ | kg air / kg fuel | Use **28.97** kg/kmol for air |
| 3 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{\text{AFR}_{st}}{\text{AFR}_{actual}}$ | dimensionless | $\phi < 1$: lean; $\phi > 1$: rich |
| 4 | Volumetric Energy Density | $E_V = \rho_f \cdot \text{LHV}_f$ | MJ / m³ | Compare fuels on **tank-size basis** |
| 5 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_c = 1 - \dfrac{\sum \dot{m}_i h_i}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}_f}$ | — | i ∈ {CO, HC, soot} |
| 6 | Brake Specific Energy Consumption | $\text{BSEC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}_f}{P_b}$ | J / kWh | Lower = better |
| 7 | Energy of Fuel Blend | $\text{LHV}_{blend} = \sum x_i \cdot \text{LHV}_i$ | MJ / kg | Mass-fraction-weighted |
| 8 | Density of Blend | $\rho_{blend}^{-1} = \sum \dfrac{x_i}{\rho_i}$ | kg / m³ | **Reciprocal rule** (volume fraction weighted) |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The methanol energy system is not academic — it underpins:

1. **Marine Propulsion** — Maersk's *MAN B&W ME-LGI* dual-fuel engines burn methanol in deep-sea container ships; methanol is a leading "drop-in" replacement for marine diesel under IMO 2030/2050 emission targets.
2. **Racing / High-Performance** — Methanol's high latent heat cools intake charge, allowing very high CR without knock; used by **dragsters, IndyCar (until 2006), and Formula 1 (1965–1968)**.
3. **Heavy-Duty Trucks and Buses** — China's **Geely CV** and **FAW** manufacture dedicated M100 trucks for mining and inter-city logistics.
4. **Fuel Cells** — Direct Methanol Fuel Cells (DMFC) use aqueous methanol for portable electronics power.
5. **Hydrogen Carrier** — Methanol can be **reformed on-board** to release H₂ for fuel-cell vehicles (Toyota's earlier research lines).
