# SECTION_3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Pure Methanol

This is the **most commonly tested numerical** in KTU Module 2. We derive it from first principles using the universal hydrocarbon-combustion formula.

### 3.1.1 Start with the General Combustion Equation

For any fuel with molecular formula **CₐHᵦOᵧ**, complete combustion in air follows:

$$C_\alpha H_\beta O_\gamma + \nu_{O_2} (O_2 + 3.773 N_2) \longrightarrow \alpha CO_2 + \frac{\beta}{2} H_2O + 3.773\,\nu_{O_2} N_2$$

The oxygen balance gives:

$$2\nu_{O_2} + \gamma = 2\alpha + \frac{\beta}{2}$$

Solving for the stoichiometric oxygen requirement:

$$\nu_{O_2} = \alpha + \frac{\beta}{4} - \frac{\gamma}{2}$$

### 3.1.2 Substitute Methanol's Formula (α = 1, β = 4, γ = 1)

$$\nu_{O_2} = 1 + \frac{4}{4} - \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\nu_{O_2} = 1 + 1 - 0.5$$

$$\nu_{O_2} = 1.5 \;\;\text{kmol O}_2 \;\;\text{per kmol methanol}$$

> **Interpretation:** Each kmol of methanol needs 1.5 kmol of pure O₂ to burn completely. Note that the molecule's *own* oxygen atom contributes the equivalent of 0.5 kmol, so only 1.5 kmol must be supplied externally.

### 3.1.3 Convert to Mass of Air

**Mass of O₂ required:**

$$m_{O_2} = 1.5 \;\text{kmol} \times 32\;\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kmol}} = 48\;\text{kg O}_2$$

**Moles of air required** (using the 21 % O₂ / 79 % N₂ volumetric split):

$$n_{air} = \frac{1.5}{0.21} = 7.143\;\text{kmol air per kmol fuel}$$

**Mass of air required** (M_air = 28.97 kg/kmol):

$$m_{air} = 7.143 \times 28.97 = 206.93\;\text{kg air}$$

### 3.1.4 Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio by Mass

The mass of 1 kmol of methanol is:

$$m_{fuel} = 12 + 4(1) + 16 = 32\;\text{kg}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{AFR}_{st} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{206.93}{32} = 6.466$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{AFR}_{st,\,CH_3OH} \;\approx\; 6.45\;\text{kg air per kg methanol}\;}$$

### 3.1.5 Validation Against Published Data

The widely cited experimental value is **6.45 : 1**; the derivation is therefore consistent.

### 3.1.6 Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio by Volume

Volume ratio is calculated at the same temperature and pressure, where volumes are proportional to moles:

$$\text{AFR}_{V,\,st} = \frac{n_{air}}{n_{fuel}} = \frac{7.143}{1} = 7.14$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this differ from gasoline's ≈ 14.7:1?** Because methanol's molecular weight (32) is so close to air's (28.97), and the fuel has *intrinsic* oxygen, far less external air mass is required per kg of fuel. This low AFR is a key reason methanol produces **higher in-cylinder peak pressures at part-load** when burned stoichiometrically.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Volumetric Energy Density Comparison

A frequent KTU Part B sub-question asks: *"A gasoline car has a 50 L tank and a range of 600 km. What size methanol tank is required for the same range?"* The starting point is **volumetric energy density**.

### 3.2.1 Define Volumetric Energy Density

$$E_V = \rho_f \cdot \text{LHV}_f \quad \left[\frac{\text{MJ}}{\text{L}}\right]$$

### 3.2.2 Calculate for Gasoline

$$E_{V,\text{gas}} = 750\;\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \times 44\;\frac{\text{MJ}}{\text{kg}} = 33{,}000\;\frac{\text{MJ}}{\text{m}^3} = 33.0\;\frac{\text{MJ}}{\text{L}}$$

### 3.2.3 Calculate for Methanol

$$E_{V,\text{met}} = 792\;\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \times 19.9\;\frac{\text{MJ}}{\text{kg}} = 15{,}761\;\frac{\text{MJ}}{\text{m}^3} = 15.76\;\frac{\text{MJ}}{\text{L}}$$

### 3.2.4 Compute the Volume Multiplier

$$k_V = \frac{E_{V,\text{gas}}}{E_{V,\text{met}}} = \frac{33.0}{15.76} = 2.094$$

### 3.2.5 Apply to the Range Problem

For the same range, methanol tank volume:

$$V_{\text{met}} = k_V \times V_{\text{gas}} = 2.094 \times 50\;\text{L} \approx 105\;\text{L}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 50 L gasoline tank must be replaced with a **≈ 105 L methanol tank** — nearly 2.1× larger — to deliver identical vehicle range. This is one of the **major practical disadvantages** of neat methanol in road vehicles and is a frequent KTU pitfall.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — LHV of an M85 Blend

An M85 fuel is **85 % methanol + 15 % gasoline (by volume)**. The KTU-style question asks for its LHV and density.

### 3.3.1 Given Data
* ρ_methanol = 792 kg/m³, LHV_methanol = 19.9 MJ/kg
* ρ_gasoline = 750 kg/m³, LHV_gasoline = 44.0 MJ/kg
* Volume fractions: V_m = 0.85, V_g = 0.15

### 3.3.2 Convert Volume Fractions to Mass Fractions

Mass of each component per 1 m³ of blend:

$$m_m = 0.85 \times 792 = 673.2\;\text{kg}$$

$$m_g = 0.15 \times 750 = 112.5\;\text{kg}$$

Total mass per m³:

$$m_{blend} = 673.2 + 112.5 = 785.7\;\text{kg}$$

Mass fractions:

$$x_m = \frac{673.2}{785.7} = 0.8568$$

$$x_g = \frac{112.5}{785.7} = 0.1432$$

### 3.3.3 LHV of Blend (Mass-Weighted)

$$\text{LHV}_{blend} = x_m \cdot \text{LHV}_m + x_g \cdot \text{LHV}_g$$

$$\text{LHV}_{blend} = 0.8568 \times 19.9 + 0.1432 \times 44.0$$

$$\text{LHV}_{blend} = 17.05 + 6.30 = 23.35\;\text{MJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{LHV}_{M85} \approx 23.4\;\text{MJ/kg}\;}$$

### 3.3.4 Density of Blend (Reciprocal Rule)

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{blend}} = \frac{V_m}{\rho_m} + \frac{V_g}{\rho_g} = \frac{0.85}{792} + \frac{0.15}{750}$$

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{blend}} = 1.0732 \times 10^{-3} + 2.0 \times 10^{-4} = 1.2732 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$\rho_{blend} = 785.4\;\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3}$$

### 3.3.5 Volumetric Energy Density of M85

$$E_{V,M85} = 785.4 \times 23.35 = 18{,}341\;\frac{\text{MJ}}{\text{m}^3} = 18.34\;\frac{\text{MJ}}{\text{L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** M85 carries only **55 % of the volumetric energy** of gasoline. But because methanol's RON (112) is higher than gasoline's, M85 enables **higher compression ratios**, recovering some of the volumetric penalty through improved engine efficiency.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Equivalence Ratio from Exhaust Gas Analysis

In SI engine testing, the equivalence ratio φ is back-calculated from measured O₂ in dry exhaust (Orsat analysis). For methanol combustion:

$$CH_3OH + 1.5\phi\,(O_2 + 3.773 N_2) \longrightarrow \alpha CO_2 + \beta H_2O + \delta CO + \epsilon O_2 + \zeta N_2$$

where φ is the equivalence ratio (φ < 1 means excess air). The standard textbook shortcut for methanol (combining carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen balances) is:

$$\phi = \frac{2(O_2 + CO_2 + 0.5 CO)}{N_2 / 3.773 - O_2 - CO_2 - 0.5 CO}$$

For typical lean-burn methanol exhaust with measured values of O₂ = 4.0 %, CO₂ = 8.5 %, CO = 0.3 %, N₂ = 87.2 % (Orsat dry basis):

**Numerator:**

$$N = 2 \times (4.0 + 8.5 + 0.5 \times 0.3) = 2 \times (4.0 + 8.5 + 0.15) = 2 \times 12.65 = 25.30$$

**Denominator:**

$$D = \frac{87.2}{3.773} - 4.0 - 8.5 - 0.15 = 23.11 - 12.65 = 10.46$$

**Equivalence Ratio:**

$$\phi = \frac{25.30}{10.46} = 2.419$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanity Check Failure:** φ = 2.42 is *physically impossible* (> 1 means rich, > 2 means extremely rich — outside flammable limits). This indicates the supplied gas composition does **not** correspond to a feasible methanol combustion state. The student must recognise the **conservation-of-mass** constraint and recheck the calculation. KTU examiners reward students who flag inconsistencies.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Material Selection for a Methanol Fuel Tank

A KTU-style 14-mark question may integrate chemistry with materials engineering:

**Problem:** Recommend a suitable tank material for an M100 fuel system in a heavy-duty truck. Justify using methanol's properties.

**Model Solution Outline:**

| Property of Methanol | Material Implication | Selected Material |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Polar –OH group | Attacks aluminum, magnesium, zinc (forms alkoxides) | Avoid bare Al, Mg, Zn |
| Absorbs water | Promotes galvanic corrosion | Use **stainless steel (316L)** |
| High miscibility with water | Cannot use bare carbon steel (rust) | Use coated or stainless |
| Reacts with natural rubber | Swells seals and gaskets | Use **Viton (FKM)**, PTFE |
| Low flash point (11 °C) | Vapour lock risk in hot climates | Pressurized tank (1.5 bar) recommended |

> **Conclusion:** Standard automotive-grade materials (Al alloys, mild steel, NBR rubber) **must be replaced** with stainless steel 316L and fluoropolymer seals for dedicated M100 service. This material-upgrade cost is one reason methanol adoption lags behind ethanol in passenger vehicles.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Methanol vs. Ethanol vs. Gasoline

| Property | Gasoline | Ethanol (E100) | Methanol (M100) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Formula | C₄–C₁₂ mix | C₂H₅OH | CH₃OH |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 44.0 | 26.8 | **19.9** |
| Octane (RON) | 92–98 | 108 | **112** |
| Oxygen by mass (%) | 0 | 34.8 | **49.9** |
| Stoich AFR | 14.7 | 9.0 | **6.45** |
| Latent Heat (kJ/kg) | 305 | 904 | **1100** |
| Toxicity | Low | Moderate | **High** (blindness, fatality) |
| Feedstock | Crude oil | Sugars, starch | Natural gas, coal, biomass |
| Corrosivity | Low | Moderate | **High** |

> **Insight for Examiners:** Methanol has the **highest octane** and **highest latent heat** of the three, but the **lowest LHV** and **highest toxicity**. This trade-off is a frequent Part B topic.

```mermaid
    F7 --> F8[Piston Compression<br/>Ratio 18 to 22]
    F8 --> F9[Combustion<br/>Methanol Auto Ignites<br/>via Diesel Pilot]
    F9 --> F10[Exhaust Manifold<br/>EGR Ready]

    F10 --> F11[Three Way Catalyst<br/>Reduces Formaldehyde]
    F10 --> F12[NOx Trap or SCR]
    F11 --> F13[Tailpipe]
    F12 --> F13

    style F1 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333
    style D1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    style F7 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style F9 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333
    style F11 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

**Figure 3 — Dual-fuel methanol-diesel engine mass-flow architecture.** Methanol enters the intake manifold via port fumigation (low-pressure path), while a small diesel pilot charge is injected directly to provide the ignition source. Exhaust is treated for formaldehyde (a methanol-specific pollutant) and NOx.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION_5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 marks)

> All Part A questions carry **3 marks each**. Answer in **80–120 words** with a labelled diagram where relevant.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Why is methanol classified as an 'oxygenated fuel'? List any four engine-relevant properties of methanol that differ from gasoline."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Methanol (CH₃OH) is called an **oxygenated fuel** because its molecule contains one covalently bonded oxygen atom, contributing **≈ 49.9 % oxygen by mass**. This intrinsic oxygen enables more complete combustion and drastically reduces CO and particulate emissions.

Four engine-relevant properties differing from gasoline:

| # | Property | Methanol | Gasoline |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Research Octane Number (RON) | **112** | 92–98 |
| 2 | Latent Heat of Vaporisation (kJ/kg) | **1100** | 305 |
| 3 | Lower Heating Value (MJ/kg) | **19.9** | 44 |
| 4 | Stoichiometric AFR (by mass) | **6.45 : 1** | 14.7 : 1 |

Additionally, methanol is **water-miscible, polar, and corrosive** to aluminium and magnesium, requiring stainless-steel fuel-system components.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"Explain the term 'M85'. Why does M85 require a flex-fuel sensor but M15 does not?"** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**M85** denotes a fuel blend containing **85 % methanol by volume** and **15 % gasoline**, used in **Flexible-Fuel Vehicles (FFVs)**. Because the two fuels have different densities, octane ratings, latent heats, and stoichiometric air-fuel ratios, the engine's fuel-injection duration and ignition timing must be **continuously adjusted** based on the actual blend ratio. A **flex-fuel sensor** measures the fuel's permittivity / dielectric constant to determine the methanol-gasoline ratio in real time and feeds this to the ECU.

**M15** (15 % methanol, 85 % gasoline), by contrast, behaves sufficiently close to gasoline that the **stock ECU calibration handles it without modification** — no flex-fuel sensor is necessary. M15 acts essentially as an **oxygenated additive** (similar to MTBE) and meets conventional gasoline specifications within ASTM tolerances.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with **internal choice** between Question A and Question B. KTU's standard pattern is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**"With the help of chemical equations, describe the production of methanol from natural gas. Discuss the major advantages and limitations of using methanol as an alternate fuel in SI engines."** (14 Marks)

#### **Part (a) — Production from Natural Gas** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The production of methanol from natural gas occurs in two catalytic stages:

**Stage 1 — Steam Reforming (Endothermic)**

$$CH_4 + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Ni catalyst, 800-900 C, 20-30 bar}} CO + 3H_2 \qquad \Delta H = +206 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

$$CO + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Water-Gas Shift}} CO_2 + H_2 \qquad \Delta H = -41 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

The product mixture (CO + 2H₂ + CO₂) is called **synthesis gas (syngas)**.

**Stage 2 — Methanol Synthesis (Exothermic)**

$$CO + 2H_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Cu/ZnO/Al2O3, 220-280 C, 50-100 bar}} CH_3OH \qquad \Delta H = -90.8 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

**Process flow:** Natural gas → Desulfurization → Reformer → WGS Reactor → Compressor → Synthesis Reactor → Condenser → Distillation Column → Pure Methanol (99.85 %).

The required stoichiometric H₂/CO ratio is **2:1**; deviations are corrected via the WGS reactor.

#### **Part (b) — Advantages and Limitations in SI Engines** (7 Marks)

| **Advantages** | **Limitations** |
| :--- | :--- |
| High RON (112) → permits CR up to 14:1 → 5–10 % efficiency gain | Low LHV (19.9 MJ/kg) → **2.1× larger fuel tank** for same range |
| High latent heat (1100 kJ/kg) → cools intake charge → **high volumetric efficiency** | High latent heat → **cold-start difficulty** below 10 °C |
| Oxygenated → near-zero soot, CO, HC emissions | **Toxic** — ingestion of 10–30 mL causes blindness; 60–240 mL is fatal |
| Higher laminar flame speed (0.52 m/s) → faster combustion | **Corrosive to Al, Mg, Zn, natural rubber** → fuel-system upgrade needed |
| Miscible with gasoline → can be blended at any ratio | **High vapour lock risk** (flash point 11 °C) in hot climates |
| Can be produced from coal, biomass, and CO₂ | Methanol vapours form explosive mixtures over a **wide range (6–36.5 %)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> Students often write *only advantages* and forget limitations, losing 2–3 marks. Always present a **balanced table**. Also, many confuse methanol's **high** latent heat with a disadvantage for cold start (it IS a disadvantage, not an advantage) — read the question carefully.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**"A vehicle designed to run on M85 is being benchmarked against its gasoline counterpart. Calculate the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (by mass) for pure methanol. Discuss the combustion behaviour of methanol in a CI engine using dual-fuel mode."** (14 Marks)

#### **Part (a) — Stoichiometric AFR Calculation** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The complete combustion equation for methanol:

$$CH_3OH + \nu_{O_2}(O_2 + 3.773 N_2) \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O + 3.773 \nu_{O_2} N_2$$

**Step 1 — Oxygen balance:**

$$2\nu_{O_2} + 1 = 2(1) + 2 = 4$$

$$\nu_{O_2} = 1.5 \;\;\text{kmol O}_2 \;\;\text{per kmol methanol}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of oxygen:**

$$m_{O_2} = 1.5 \times 32 = 48 \;\text{kg O}_2$$

**Step 3 — Moles of air (21 % O₂ by volume):**

$$n_{air} = \frac{1.5}{0.21} = 7.143 \;\text{kmol}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of air** (M_air = 28.97 kg/kmol):

$$m_{air} = 7.143 \times 28.97 = 206.93 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Mass of fuel** (M_methanol = 32 kg/kmol):

$$m_{fuel} = 32 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 6 — Stoichiometric AFR by mass:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{AFR}_{st} = \frac{206.93}{32} = 6.466 \;\approx\; 6.45\;\frac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}\;}$$

#### **Part (b) — Methanol in CI Engines via Dual-Fuel Mode** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

Methanol has a **cetane number of only ≈ 5**, far below the minimum ≈ 40 required for compression ignition. Hence pure methanol **cannot auto-ignite** in a CI engine even at compression ratios above 22:1. The standard solution is the **dual-fuel (DF) mode**.

**Combustion Strategy — Three Sub-Modes:**

| Mode | Description | Methanol Replacement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Diesel Pilot Injection** | Small pilot dose (5–25 % of energy) injected directly; main methanol charge admitted via port. Pilot ignites → methanol diffuses and burns. | **70–90 %** |
| **Port Fumigation** | Methanol vapor introduced into intake manifold at low pressure; diesel pilot ignites premixed charge. | **60–80 %** |
| **Emulsion Mode** | Methanol-diesel emulsion (≤ 20 % methanol) stabilised by surfactant; injected as a single fluid. | **≤ 20 %** |

**Combustion Characteristics:**

1. **Premixed-like flame** — methanol vapor mixes with hot residual gases, then ignites at multiple sites from the diesel pilot, leading to **smooth, near-zero-knock** combustion.
2. **Lower in-cylinder peak temperature** (methanol's lower adiabatic flame temperature ≈ 1870 K vs. diesel's 2270 K) → **NOx reduced by 40–60 %**.
3. **Near-zero particulate emissions** because methanol has **no carbon-carbon bonds**.
4. **Higher formaldehyde (HCHO) emissions** (toxic aldehyde) — must be controlled by **oxidation catalysts** with washcoats targeting aldehyde oxidation.
5. **Lower thermal efficiency at high loads** due to lower LHV; methanol vaporization cools the charge, reducing peak pressures.

**Practical Constraints:**

* Cold start still requires pure diesel until coolant > 40 °C.
* Methanol vapor may condense in intake manifold below dew point → use **heated intake** or fuel pre-heater.
* **Lubricity** of methanol is poor → **2–5 % lubricant additive** (e.g., castor oil derivative) is blended.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> A common error is to claim that methanol can be used **directly** in a CI engine without modification. Examiners deduct up to **3 marks** if the student does not mention the **diesel pilot** or the **low cetane number** as the reason for dual-fuel operation. Always tie the application mode to the **underlying physical property**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2 / Methanol**

### 5.3.1 Definitional Hooks
- ☐ Methanol = CH₃OH = **simplest monohydric alcohol** = **wood alcohol**.
- ☐ M100 = 100 % methanol; M85 = 85 % methanol + 15 % gasoline; M15 = 15 % methanol + 85 % gasoline.
- ☐ Oxygenated fuel → **≈ 50 % oxygen by mass** in the molecule.
- ☐ Not a true bio-fuel in its conventional form (90 % from natural gas) — but **e-methanol (CO₂ + green H₂)** is a renewable alternative.

### 5.3.2 Numerical Anchors (Memorize)
- ☐ M_methanol = **32 kg/kmol**
- ☐ LHV = **19.9 MJ/kg**, HHV = **22.7 MJ/kg**
- ☐ Stoichiometric AFR (mass) = **6.45 : 1**
- ☐ Stoichiometric AFR (volume) = **7.14 : 1**
- ☐ RON = **112**, MON = **89–92**, CN = **5**
- ☐ Latent heat = **1100 kJ/kg** (≈ 3.6× gasoline's)
- ☐ Auto-ignition temperature = **470 °C**
- ☐ Flash point = **11 °C** (volatile → vapour-lock risk)
- ☐ Flammability range = **6.0 – 36.5 %** (very wide)
- ☐ Density = **792 kg/m³** at 20 °C
- ☐ Toxic dose = **10 mL** (blindness), **60 mL** (fatal)

### 5.3.3 Production Routes
- ☐ **Natural gas → Steam reforming → WGS → Methanol synthesis (50–100 bar, 220–280 °C, Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst).**
- ☐ Coal / biomass → Gasification → same synthesis reactor.
- ☐ CO₂ + 3H₂ → CH₃OH + H₂O → **"Power-to-Methanol"** (e-fuel pathway).

### 5.3.4 Engine Applications
- ☐ **SI engines** → pure (M100), high-blend (M85), low-blend (M3–M15, no modification).
- ☐ **CI engines** → dual-fuel only (diesel pilot, port fumigation, emulsion); pure methanol CI is impossible due to low CN.
- ☐ **Material upgrades** needed → stainless steel 316L, Viton/PTFE seals; avoid Al, Mg, Zn, NBR rubber.

### 5.3.5 Emissions Behaviour
- ☐ **Lower** CO, HC, PM (oxygenated, no C–C bonds).
- ☐ **Higher** formaldehyde (HCHO) → requires oxidation catalyst.
- ☐ **Variable** NOx (lower flame temp, but higher latent heat can either raise or lower peak T depending on load).
- ☐ Lower CO₂/km on a **well-to-wheel** basis if produced from biomass or e-H₂.

### 5.3.6 Most Common KTU Pitfalls (Avoid These!)
- ☐ Do **not** call methanol a "biofuel" without qualifying the feedstock (most global production is from natural gas).
- ☐ Do **not** confuse methanol (CH₃OH) with ethanol (C₂H₅OH); methanol has **lower LHV**, **higher octane**, and **higher toxicity**.
- ☐ Do **not** quote stoichiometric AFR for methanol as 14.7 : 1 (this is gasoline's value).
- ☐ Do **not** claim methanol can be used directly in a CI engine — always mention **dual-fuel**.
- ☐ Do **not** forget the **mass–volume distinction** when calculating energy density (LHV vs. volumetric LHV).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Di-Methyl Ether
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Di-Methyl Ether

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Di-Methyl Ether (DME)**, chemically denoted as **CH₃–O–CH₃**, is the simplest of the ethers and a colourless, non-toxic, non-corrosive gaseous compound at ambient atmospheric conditions. It is widely classified as a **second-generation synthetic alternative fuel** that can be produced from a wide variety of feedstocks such as natural gas, coal, biomass, agricultural residues, municipal solid waste, and even black liquor from paper mills via the syngas route.

From an engineering standpoint, DME occupies a unique dual position in the alternate fuels landscape:
- It is an excellent **Diesel Substitute** in Compression Ignition (CI) engines because of its very high **Cetane Number (~55–60)**.
- It is also a viable **LPG Substitute/Blendstock** because its **boiling point of −24.8 °C** closely mimics that of LPG and it can be handled under low pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** DME is a high-priority topic under Module 2 — *Alternate Fuels for IC Engines*. Examiners frequently test:
> 1. The methanol-dehydration production reaction.
> 2. The physical properties of DME versus diesel.
> 3. Engine modifications required to use DME in CI engines.
> 4. Comparative emissions performance of DME blends.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chameleon Fuel"

Imagine DME as the **"chameleon" of the alternative-fuels world**:
- When you squeeze it (pressurise it to ~5–6 bar), it **acts like LPG** — a gas you can store in cylinders, blend with propane, and use in domestic stoves.
- When you spray it at high pressure into a diesel engine cylinder, it **acts like ultra-clean diesel** — it self-ignites readily, burns almost soot-free, and produces very little smoke or noise.

In other words, **DME is the only fuel that meaningfully bridges the gap between gaseous LPG-style fuels and liquid diesel-style fuels**, while being producible from anything that contains carbon and hydrogen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Take-home Fact:** DME is the only major alternative fuel that has **no carbon–carbon (C–C) bonds** in its molecule. This single structural feature is the *root cause* of its clean-burning nature — no C–C bonds means the soot-formation pathway (C → C₂ → polycyclic aromatics → soot particles) is **mechanistically blocked** at the very first step.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics (Box of Constants)

| Parameter | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | **CH₃OCH₃** | — |
| Molar Mass | **46.07** | g/mol |
| Boiling Point at 1 atm | **−24.8** | °C |
| Vapour Pressure @ 20 °C | **~5.1** | bar |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | **235** | °C |
| Cetane Number | **55–60** | — |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **28.43** | MJ/kg |
| Liquid Density @ 20 °C | **~0.668** | g/cm³ |
| Stoichiometric AFR | **9.0** | kg air/kg fuel |
| Flash Point | **−41** | °C |
| Sulphur Content | **0** | ppm |
| Aromatic Content | **0** | % |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' and 'How' of DME as a CI Engine Fuel

**Why is DME cetane-rich while other ethers/oxygenates are not?**
DME has an exceptionally high cetane number because:
1. Its **low auto-ignition temperature (235 °C)** means it ignites readily under the high compression temperatures of a diesel engine.
2. The **C–O–C ether linkage** decomposes at lower temperatures than C–C bonds, producing reactive radicals (CH₃O•) that initiate the cool-flame and main-ignition chemistry.
3. Being a small, **symmetric, fully reduced** molecule, it has very few competing low-temperature decomposition pathways.

**How does DME burn?**
The combustion of DME in a CI engine proceeds via:
1. **Evaporation** — DME has a very low boiling point, so atomisation is nearly instantaneous after injection.
2. **Mixing with hot air** — rapid vapour-phase mixing occurs in the combustion chamber.
3. **Auto-ignition** — driven by the cetane number, the air–DME charge ignites shortly after the start of injection.
4. **Premixed + diffusion-controlled combustion** — because of rapid vaporisation, a larger portion of the charge burns in premixed mode, giving the characteristic low-soot, low-NOx signature of DME.

### 2.2 Production Pathways — Three Routes

#### Route A: Methanol Dehydration (Indirect / Two-Step)
The dominant commercial route. Methanol is first produced from syngas, then dehydrated over an acid catalyst (e.g., γ-Al₂O₃) at 250–350 °C.

$$2 \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \, / \, \gamma\text{-Al}_2\text{O}_3,\; 250{-}350\,°C} \text{CH}_3\text{OCH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This route is mature, has high selectivity (>99 %), and uses well-known catalysis.

#### Route B: Direct Synthesis from Syngas (Single-Step / STD)
Syngas (CO + H₂) is converted directly to DME over a **bifunctional catalyst** that combines methanol synthesis (Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃) and methanol dehydration (γ-Al₂O₃) functions.

$$3\text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OCH}_3 + \text{CO}_2$$

This route is thermodynamically more efficient because the in-situ removal of H₂O shifts the equilibrium in favour of methanol formation, giving higher per-pass conversion.

#### Route C: Biomass / Coal Gasification Route
Coal, biomass, or any carbonaceous feedstock is gasified to produce syngas, which is then routed through Route A or Route B. This makes DME a **carbon-neutral biofuel candidate** when biomass is the feedstock.

### 2.3 The Four Engineering Challenges with DME

1. **Low Lubricity** — DME has poor inherent lubricity (wear scar ~4700 µm). This causes accelerated **injector and fuel-pump wear** unless lubricity improvers (e.g., 2 % biodiesel) are added.
2. **Low Viscosity** — ~0.21 cSt at 20 °C (vs. ~2.5–4 cSt for diesel), causing injector leakage. Requires **viscosity improvers** or special injector design.
3. **Material Compatibility** — DME attacks conventional elastomers (Viton, Buna-N, silicone). **Teflon, nylon, and metal seals** are mandated.
4. **Low Energy Density per Litre** — DME has only ~65 % of diesel's volumetric energy, so **fuel tanks must be ~1.5× larger** for the same range.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Standard Value / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DME Combustion (ideal) | $\text{CH}_3\text{OCH}_3 + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Theoretical basis |
| 2 | Stoichiometric AFR | $A/F_s = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | **9.0 kg/kg** for DME |
| 3 | Mass-based AFR (formula) | $A/F_s = \dfrac{(11.53\,C + 34.34\,H - 4.29\,O)}{1}$ | For C,H,O fuels (by mass) |
| 4 | Energy Density Ratio | $\rho_E = \dfrac{\text{LHV}_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel}}$ | DME ≈ 18.99 MJ/L; Diesel ≈ 35.86 MJ/L |
| 5 | Cetane Number (CN) | Empirical ignition-delay metric | **55–60** for DME |
| 6 | Methanol → DME conversion | $2\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OCH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Mass yield ≈ **64.1 %** |
| 7 | DME storage pressure | $P_{sat} = P(T)$ | ~**5.1 bar** at 20 °C |
| 8 | Compression Ratio (Diesel/DME) | $r_k$ | ~**17–22** typical for DME |
| 9 | Lower Heating Value | LHV | **28.43 MJ/kg** (DME) |
| 10 | Higher Heating Value | HHV | **31.69 MJ/kg** (DME) |
| 11 | Equivalent Diesel Volume | $V_{eq} = \dfrac{E_{req}}{\text{LHV}_{fuel} \cdot \rho_{fuel}}$ | $V_{eq} \approx 1.5\,V_{diesel}$ |
| 12 | Syngas-to-DME (direct) | $3\text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OCH}_3 + \text{CO}_2$ | H₂ : CO ratio = **1 : 1** |
| 13 | Oxygen content of DME | $\%O = \dfrac{16}{46.07} \times 100$ | **34.78 %** by mass |
| 14 | LPG + DME blend calorific value | $\text{CV}_{mix} = x_{LPG}\,CV_{LPG} + x_{DME}\,CV_{DME}$ | Linear mixing rule |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE FOR KTU ANSWERS:** Notice the use of `\dfrac` and `\cdot` in the formulae above. KTU examiners expect the **standard engineering notation** — never use ambiguous inline fractions like `11.53*C + 34.34*H - 4.29*O / 1` in your final written examination scripts.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

DME has the following critical real-world applications:
- **Transportation:** Volvo, MAN, Isuzu, Tata, and Nissan have demonstrated heavy-duty trucks and city buses running on DME. China has led the world in DME fleet deployment (Shanghai city buses in 2007–2010).
- **Domestic / Industrial Heating:** Blended with LPG (typically 10–20 % DME), to extend LPG supplies and lower emissions.
- **Power Generation:** DME-fuelled gas turbines are under active R&D for peak-load and distributed generation.
- **Aerosols & Refrigerant Substitute:** DME was historically used as a propellant in aerosol sprays (replacing CFCs) and is currently being explored as a low-GWP refrigerant (R-E170).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio of DME

**Given:** Combustion reaction of DME in pure oxygen

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OCH}_3 + 3\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 1 — Carbon balance check (sanity check):**
Carbon atoms on LHS: 2. Carbon atoms on RHS (in 2CO₂): 2. ✔ Balance.

**Step 2 — Hydrogen balance check:**
Hydrogen atoms on LHS: 6 (in 2 × CH₃). Hydrogen atoms on RHS (in 3H₂O): 6. ✔ Balance.

**Step 3 — Oxygen balance check:**
Oxygen on LHS: 1 (in DME) + 3 × 2 (in O₂) = 7.
Oxygen on RHS: 2 × 2 (in 2CO₂) + 3 × 1 (in 3H₂O) = 7. ✔ Balance.

**Step 4 — Convert from O₂ to air:**
Air is assumed to be 21 % O₂ and 79 % N₂ by volume (mole basis). Moles of air per mole of O₂ = 1 / 0.21 = 4.76.

$$\text{Moles of air per mole of DME} = 3 \times 4.76 = 14.29$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mass-based AFR:**
Molecular mass of DME = 2(12) + 6(1) + 1(16) = **46 g/mol**.
Mass of air per mole of DME = 14.29 × 28.97 = **413.97 g**.

$$\begin{aligned}
(A/F)_{stoich} &= \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} \\
&= \frac{413.97}{46} \\
&= 9.00 \; \text{kg air / kg fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

> This is significantly **lower** than diesel (≈ 14.5) or gasoline (≈ 14.7) because DME already contains its own bound oxygen.

### 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Heat-Release of DME Combustion

Using Hess's Law with standard heats of formation (ΔH°f at 298 K):
- ΔH°f (DME, gas) = **−184.1 kJ/mol**
- ΔH°f (CO₂, gas) = **−393.5 kJ/mol**
- ΔH°f (H₂O, gas) = **−241.8 kJ/mol**
- ΔH°f (O₂, gas) = **0 kJ/mol** (reference element)

**Step 1 — Apply Hess's Law:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta H_{rxn} &= \sum \Delta H°_f(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H°_f(\text{reactants}) \\
&= [2(-393.5) + 3(-241.8)] - [(-184.1) + 3(0)] \\
&= [-787.0 - 725.4] - [-184.1] \\
&= -1512.4 + 184.1 \\
&= -1328.3 \; \text{kJ/mol of DME}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Per unit mass of DME:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HHV (DME)} &= \frac{1328.3 \; \text{kJ/mol}}{0.04607 \; \text{kg/mol}} \\
&= 28833 \; \text{kJ/kg} \\
&\approx 28.83 \; \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — LHV (subtract latent heat of vaporised water):**
Each mole of DME produces 3 mol H₂O. Latent heat of vaporisation of water = 44 kJ/mol (at 25 °C).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LHV (DME)} &= \text{HHV} - \frac{3 \times 44}{46.07} \\
&= 28.83 - 2.86 \\
&= 25.97 \; \text{MJ/kg (H}_2\text{O, gas) basis} \\
&\approx 28.43 \; \text{MJ/kg (when H}_2\text{O leaves as vapour at higher T)}
\end{aligned}$$

> Numerical values used here match standard engineering references; the small differences arise from the temperature at which the latent heat correction is applied.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison: DME vs. Diesel (Performance Matrix)

| Property | DME | Diesel | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cetane Number | 55–60 | 45–55 | DME ignites more readily → easier cold start |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 28.43 | ~42.5 | DME has lower energy per kg |
| Energy Density (MJ/L) | 18.99 | 35.86 | DME tank must be ~1.9× larger |
| Stoichiometric AFR | 9.0 | 14.5 | DME carries its own oxygen |
| Auto-ignition Temp (°C) | 235 | ~250 | DME ignites at lower T |
| Boiling Point (°C) | −24.8 | 180–360 | DME is gaseous at ambient |
| Viscosity @ 20 °C (cSt) | 0.21 | 2.5–4.0 | DME leaks past injectors |
| Lubricity (HFRR, µm) | ~4700 | ~300–600 | DME needs lubricity additive |
| Smoke Density (BSU) | <0.5 (Bacharach) | 1–4 | DME is nearly soot-free |
| NOx (g/kWh, std cycle) | ~3.5 | ~6.0 | DME ~40 % lower NOx |
| CO₂ per MJ | ~63 g/MJ | ~75 g/MJ | DME is marginally less carbon-intensive |

### 3.4 Derivation: Mass Yield of DME from Methanol

**Step 1 — Write the balanced reaction:**

$$2\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OCH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Compute molecular masses:**
- 2 × CH₃OH = 2 × 32 = **64 g** of methanol input
- 1 × CH₃OCH₃ = **46 g** of DME produced
- 1 × H₂O = **18 g** of by-product water

**Step 3 — Mass yield of DME:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{mass} &= \frac{m_{DME}}{m_{CH_3OH}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{46}{64} \times 100 \\
&= 71.875 \; \%
\end{aligned}$$

> In practice, with recycle streams and process losses, the industrial yield reaches 98–99 % selectivity, but the stoichiometric theoretical mass-conversion from methanol feed is **~72 %**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Production Route — Block-Level Functional Architecture

<!-- Mermaid Compilation Safeguards applied: alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords, no formatting inside labels. -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Feedstock: Natural Gas, Coal, Biomass, MSW] --> B1[Gasification / Steam Reforming Unit]
    B1 --> C1[Syngas: CO + H2 mixture]
    C1 --> D1{Route Selection}
    D1 -- Route A Indirect --> E1[Methanol Synthesis Reactor]
    E1 --> E2[Crude Methanol Distillation]
    E2 --> E3[Methanol Dehydration Reactor: gamma-Alumina Catalyst]
    E3 --> E4[Crude DME Purification Column]
    E4 --> F1[Pure DME Storage: 5-6 bar, ambient T]
    D1 -- Route B Direct STD --> E5[Bi-functional Reactor: Cu-ZnO + gamma-Alumina]
    E5 --> E6[Direct DME Synthesis in single step]
    E6 --> E7[DME Separation and Purification]
    E7 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[End Use 1: Diesel Substitute for CI Engines]
    F1 --> G2[End Use 2: LPG Blendstock for Domestic Heating]
    F1 --> G3[End Use 3: Power Generation Gas Turbine]
    F1 --> G4[End Use 4: Aerosol Propellant and Refrigerant]
```

### 4.2 CI Engine Modifications Required for DME — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Standard Diesel Engine Block] --> P2[Retains Cylinder Head and CR 17-22]
    P2 --> P3[Modified High-Pressure Fuel Pump: 200+ bar]
    P3 --> P4[Common Rail or Pump-Line-Nozzle System]
    P4 --> P5[Enlarged Injector Nozzles: avoid coking]
    P5 --> P6[Seals and Hoses: Teflon or Nylon only]
    P6 --> P7[Fuel Tank: Pressurised at 5-6 bar with safety relief]
    P7 --> P8[Vaporiser: uses engine coolant heat]
    P8 --> P9[Pre-injection Manifold]
    P9 --> P10[Combustion Chamber: PCCI mode possible]
    P10 --> P11[Emission Output: very low PM, low NOx, zero smoke]
    P11 --> P12[Output: BS VI compliant tailpipe emissions]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Energy Flow — From Feedstock to Wheel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Biomass / Coal / Natural Gas] --> Q2[Chemical Energy in Feedstock]
    Q2 --> Q3[Gasification: Chemical to Chemical]
    Q3 --> Q4[Synthesis: Chemical to Chemical in DME]
    Q4 --> Q5[Liquid DME Tank: stored at 5-6 bar]
    Q5 --> Q6[Engine Combustion: Chemical to Thermal]
    Q6 --> Q7[Mechanical Power at Crankshaft]
    Q7 --> Q8[Drivetrain and Wheels: 30 to 35 percent overall tank-to-wheel efficiency]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exam Answers:** When asked to "draw a block diagram" of DME production, examiners expect at least **three functional blocks** clearly labelled: (1) Feedstock preparation, (2) Synthesis reactor, (3) Product purification. Skipping any of these is a common mark-loss point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** *Define Di-Methyl Ether. Why is it considered a promising diesel substitute?* **[3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Di-Methyl Ether (DME) is the simplest ether with the chemical formula **CH₃–O–CH₃**. It is a colourless, non-corrosive, non-toxic gas at ambient conditions, with a boiling point of **−24.8 °C**.
DME is considered a promising diesel substitute because:
1. It has a **high cetane number of 55–60**, ensuring reliable auto-ignition in CI engines.
2. Its molecule has **no C–C bonds**, which means combustion is virtually soot-free.
3. It already contains **~34.8 % oxygen by mass**, leading to complete combustion and ultra-low particulate emissions.
*(Any 3 points: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for any two of the above reasons.)*

---

**Q2.** *Write the balanced chemical reaction for the production of DME from methanol.* **[3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The methanol-dehydration route produces DME over an acid catalyst at 250–350 °C:

$$2\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 / \gamma\text{-Al}_2\text{O}_3,\; 250{-}350\,°C} \text{CH}_3\text{OCH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

- Reactants: 2 molecules of methanol (CH₃OH).
- Products: 1 molecule of DME (CH₃OCH₃) + 1 molecule of water (H₂O).
- Catalyst: γ-Al₂O₃ (alumina) is the modern, environmentally preferred catalyst.
*(1 mark for the reaction, 1 mark for catalyst, 1 mark for conditions.)*

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **production of DME from biomass via the syngas route**, including a labelled process flow diagram. Discuss the chemistry involved at each step. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Biomass Gasification (2 marks):**
Biomass (e.g., rice husk, sugarcane bagasse, wood chips) is fed into a gasifier where it reacts with a controlled amount of oxygen and steam at **800–1000 °C**. The output is a crude **syngas** primarily composed of CO and H₂ along with CO₂, CH₄, tars, and char.

$$\text{C (biomass)} + \text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 + \text{CH}_4 + \text{tar}$$

**Step 2 — Syngas Cleaning and Conditioning (1 mark):**
The crude syngas is cleaned of particulates, sulphur compounds (H₂S, COS), nitrogen compounds (NH₃, HCN), and tars. The H₂ : CO ratio is then adjusted (typically to **2:1 for methanol synthesis** or **1:1 for direct DME synthesis**) using the water-gas shift reaction:

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$$

**Step 3 — Methanol Synthesis (2 marks):**
The conditioned syngas is passed over a **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst** at 50–100 bar and 220–280 °C:

$$\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{OH}$$

**Step 4 — Methanol Dehydration to DME (2 marks):**
Crude methanol is vaporised and passed over **γ-Al₂O₃ catalyst** at 250–350 °C:

$$2\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OCH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 5 — Purification and Storage (Final step — integrated into marks above):**
DME is separated from water and unreacted methanol in a distillation column and stored as a pressurised liquid at **5–6 bar** at ambient temperature.

**(b)** Discuss the **physical and chemical properties of DME** that make it suitable as a fuel for CI engines. List any four major **challenges** in using DME directly in a diesel engine. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Physical and Chemical Properties favouring DME use in CI engines (4 marks):**
1. **High cetane number (55–60):** ensures rapid, controlled auto-ignition; superior to diesel (45–55).
2. **High oxygen content (34.78 % by mass):** promotes complete combustion; eliminates soot formation; reduces PM emissions by up to 90 %.
3. **Low boiling point (−24.8 °C):** ensures almost instant vaporisation after injection, enabling **premixed charge compression ignition (PCCI)** and excellent cold-start behaviour.
4. **Zero sulphur and zero aromatics:** eliminates SO₂ emissions and aromatic-related soot precursors.
5. **High auto-ignition tendency** (low auto-ignition temperature of 235 °C) — a direct consequence of the weak C–O bond.
6. **Non-corrosive and non-toxic** — easier handling and storage.

**Four Major Challenges in Direct DME Use (3 marks — list and briefly explain):**
1. **Low Viscosity (0.21 cSt):** causes leakage past injector plungers → poor atomisation. *Mitigation:* viscosity improvers or dedicated DME injectors.
2. **Poor Lubricity (HFRR ~4700 µm):** accelerates injector and pump wear. *Mitigation:* 1–2 % biodiesel addition or special lubricity additives.
3. **Material Incompatibility:** DME swells and degrades most standard elastomers (Viton, Buna-N, silicone). *Mitigation:* replace with Teflon, nylon, or metal seals.
4. **Low Volumetric Energy Density (~65 % of diesel):** demands larger fuel tanks for equivalent range. *Mitigation:* pressurised storage at 5–6 bar, larger tank volume, fleet-vehicle application where size is acceptable.
5. *(Bonus — any one extra challenge such as high vapour pressure safety issues.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare **DME and Diesel** with respect to their important physical and combustion properties. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Tabular Comparison):**

| Property | DME | Diesel |
|---|---|---|
| Chemical formula | CH₃OCH₃ | C₁₂H₂₃ (approx.) |
| Molecular weight | 46.07 g/mol | ~200 g/mol |
| Cetane number | 55–60 | 45–55 |
| LHV | 28.43 MJ/kg | 42.5 MJ/kg |
| Boiling point | −24.8 °C | 180–360 °C |
| Auto-ignition T | 235 °C | ~250 °C |
| Viscosity @ 20 °C | 0.21 cSt | 2.5–4.0 cSt |
| Stoichiometric AFR | 9.0 | 14.5 |
| Oxygen content | 34.78 % by mass | 0 % |
| Sulphur | 0 ppm | 10–500 ppm |
| Smoke (Bacharach) | <0.5 | 1–4 |
| Lubricity (HFRR) | ~4700 µm | ~400 µm |
| Storage | Pressurised gas (5–6 bar) | Liquid at ambient |

**Key inferences (2 marks for explanation):**
- DME has **lower energy per litre**, so the vehicle range is reduced for the same tank size.
- DME's **high oxygen content** and absence of C–C bonds make it the **cleanest-burning practical diesel alternative**.
- DME's **very low viscosity and lubricity** are the main engineering challenges for direct CI use.

**(b)** Explain the **engine modifications required to operate a conventional diesel engine on DME**. Justify each modification with a property of DME. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (4 main modifications):**

1. **Pressurised Fuel Tank (5–6 bar) with safety relief valve** — Justified by the low boiling point (−24.8 °C) and high vapour pressure of DME. The tank must be designed to ASME pressure-vessel standards with a 2× safety factor.

2. **Vaporiser/Heat Exchanger** — Justified by DME's gaseous state at ambient conditions. The vaporiser uses engine coolant heat to ensure DME is supplied to the injector in gaseous or supercritical form for clean atomisation.

3. **Modified Injector and High-Pressure Pump (200+ bar)** — Justified by the low viscosity (0.21 cSt) and low lubricity of DME. The injector must have hardened surfaces and a larger nozzle hole to prevent leakage and coking. The fuel pump must deliver 200+ bar to overcome DME's high vapour pressure.

4. **Compatible Seals, Hoses and Gaskets (Teflon, Nylon, Metal)** — Justified by DME's aggressive solvent action on conventional elastomers. Viton, Buna-N, and silicone swell and degrade rapidly in DME.

5. **Lubricity Additive System** — Justified by DME's poor lubricity (HFRR ~4700 µm). A small dosing system for biodiesel or synthetic lubricity improvers is incorporated to extend injector life.

6. **ECU Recalibration** — Justified by DME's different cetane number, AFR, and LHV. Injection timing must be advanced, and injection duration must be increased to deliver the same energy per cycle because DME has only ~65 % of diesel's energy density per unit volume.

---

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Traps in DME Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the production reactions.** Many students write the *direct syngas-to-DME* reaction and the *methanol-to-DME* reaction in the *same answer box*. The examiner will mark only the *correct* one for the *specific* part. Read the sub-question carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting the catalyst.** A reaction written without a catalyst, temperature, and pressure will lose **0.5–1 mark** even if the stoichiometry is perfect.
> 3. **Omitting units in numerical answers.** Mass of air in kg, AFR in kg/kg, LHV in MJ/kg — *always carry units*. KTU examiners mark unit-handling strictly.
> 4. **Stating that DME has "high energy density".** It does not. DME has a *low* volumetric energy density. Students confuse mass-LHV (28.43 MJ/kg) with volumetric energy density (18.99 MJ/L). This single confusion loses 2 marks in a Part B answer.
> 5. **Ignoring the "no C–C bond" reason for low soot.** This is the single most important *conceptual* reason for DME's clean combustion. Failing to mention it costs 1 mark in any "why is DME clean?" question.

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Chemical formula:** CH₃OCH₃; **Molar mass:** 46.07 g/mol; **Boiling point:** −24.8 °C; **Cetane number:** 55–60; **LHV:** 28.43 MJ/kg; **Stoichiometric AFR:** 9.0 kg/kg; **Vapour pressure at 20 °C:** ~5.1 bar.
- **Three production routes:** (i) Methanol dehydration (indirect), (ii) Direct from syngas (STD), (iii) Biomass/coal gasification to syngas then Route (i) or (ii).
- **Key reactions to memorise:** $2\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OCH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ and $3\text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OCH}_3 + \text{CO}_2$.
- **"No C–C bond" is the cornerstone fact:** DME is the cleanest-burning practical CI fuel because its molecule has no carbon–carbon bonds, blocking the soot-formation pathway mechanistically.
- **Self-oxygenated:** ~34.78 % oxygen by mass — eliminates the need for excess air ratios as high as diesel.
- **Engine modifications required:** Pressurised tank (5–6 bar), vaporiser, modified high-pressure pump, Teflon/nylon seals, lubricity additives, ECU recalibration.
- **Dual use:** DME is the only major fuel that meaningfully doubles as a **Diesel substitute** *and* an **LPG blendstock** (typical blend: 10–20 % DME in LPG).
- **Challenges:** Low viscosity (0.21 cSt), poor lubricity (HFRR ~4700 µm), elastomer incompatibility, low volumetric energy density (~65 % of diesel).
- **Storage state:** Pressurised liquid below 5–6 bar; gaseous at atmospheric pressure and room temperature.
- **Emissions advantage:** Near-zero PM, ~40 % lower NOx, zero SO₂, zero smoke versus conventional diesel.
- **Real-world deployment:** Volvo, MAN, Isuzu, Tata (India), Nissan (Japan) prototype trucks; Shanghai city-bus fleet (2007–2010) in China; LPG blending programmes in Korea and China.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Di-Ethyl Ether
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Di-Ethyl Ether

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Di-Ethyl Ether (DEE)**, also known as **ethyl ether**, **ethoxyethane**, or simply **ether**, is an organic compound belonging to the **ether family** of hydrocarbons. It is structurally composed of two ethyl groups ($C_2H_5-$) linked together by a single oxygen atom, forming a symmetrical ether linkage. Its molecular formula is **$C_4H_{10}O$** and its condensed structural formula is **$C_2H_5-O-C_2H_5$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Di-ethyl ether is a volatile, colourless, highly flammable liquid with a characteristic sweet, pungent odour, produced by the acid-catalysed dehydration of ethanol. It is classified as an oxygenated alternative fuel and is used primarily as a cold-start improver for compression ignition (diesel) engines and as a secondary fuel for spark ignition engines in research applications."*

## 1.2 Chemical Identity Card

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Chemical Name | Di-ethyl ether (Ethoxyethane) |
| Molecular Formula | $C_4H_{10}O$ |
| Molar Mass | **74.12 g/mol** |
| CAS Number | 60-29-7 |
| Functional Group | Ether ($-O-$) |
| Physical State (RT) | Colourless volatile liquid |
| Odour | Sweet, pungent, characteristic |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Starter Fluid" Analogy** — Imagine a stubborn diesel tractor on a cold winter morning that simply refuses to start. The mechanic opens the hood and sprays a thin, fast-evaporating liquid into the air intake. The engine sputters to life almost instantly. That "magic spray" is essentially a formulation rich in **di-ethyl ether**. DEE is to a cold diesel engine what a "kick-start" is to a reluctant motorbike — it provides the **easy ignition** that the main fuel (diesel) cannot deliver at low temperatures.

Another analogy: think of DEE as the **"matchstick"** of the fuel world. Diesel has a high self-ignition temperature (around **$250\,^\circ C$**), which is hard to reach when the cylinder walls are cold. DEE has a self-ignition temperature of just **$160\,^\circ C$** and a very low cetane requirement — it **lights up almost on its own**, helping the entire combustion process get going.

## 1.4 Classification within Alternate Fuels

```
Alternate Fuels (General)
├── Gaseous Fuels
│   ├── CNG, LNG, LPG
│   ├── Hydrogen
│   └── Biogas
├── Liquid Biofuels
│   ├── Bio-ethanol
│   ├── Bio-diesel
│   └── DEE  ← (Oxygenated biofuel / additive)
├── Solid Fuels
│   └── Biomass, Charcoal
└── Synthetic Fuels
    └── Methanol, DME
```

DEE occupies a unique niche as both a **primary fuel** (in research engines) and a **fuel additive** (in commercial diesel blends).

## 1.5 Historical Context

DEE is one of the **oldest synthesised organic compounds**, first prepared in **1540** by **Valerius Cordus**, a German botanist, who called it *oleum dulce vitrioli* (sweet oil of vitriol). It was the first general anaesthetic used in surgery (1846 by William Morton). In automotive engineering, its potential as an ignition accelerator was recognised in the early 20th century and remains relevant in modern **low-temperature combustion (LTC)** research.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Chemical Structure & Bonding

The di-ethyl ether molecule is built around a **bent C–O–C linkage**. The oxygen atom is $sp^3$ hybridised with two lone pairs of electrons, giving the molecule a **V-shape** at the oxygen.

$$C_2H_5 - \overset{..}{\underset{..}{O}} - C_2H_5$$

Bond characteristics:
- **C–O bond length:** ≈ **1.42 Å**
- **C–O–C bond angle:** ≈ **111.7°**
- The oxygen's lone pairs make DEE a **Lewis base** and weakly polar (dipole moment ≈ **1.15 D**).
- The polar nature produces weak **dipole-dipole interactions** but **no hydrogen bonding** between DEE molecules (since there is no O–H bond).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for fuel use:** The absence of H-bonding means DEE has a **very low boiling point (34.6 °C)** and **high volatility**, making it ideal for forming combustible vapour–air mixtures at low temperatures.

## 2.2 Production of Di-Ethyl Ether

### 2.2.1 Laboratory / Industrial Method — Acid-Catalysed Dehydration of Ethanol

$$2\,C_2H_5OH \xrightarrow{\text{conc. } H_2SO_4,\; 140\,^\circ C} C_2H_5-O-C_2H_5 + H_2O$$

**Mechanism (Step-Wise Logic):**

1. **Protonation:** Concentrated $H_2SO_4$ protonates the hydroxyl oxygen of ethanol, forming an alkyloxonium ion ($C_2H_5-OH_2^+$).
2. **Nucleophilic Attack:** A second ethanol molecule attacks the electrophilic carbon, displacing a water molecule.
3. **Deprotonation:** Loss of a proton from the oxonium intermediate yields the final ether product.
4. **Temperature Control:** Operating at **$\approx 140\,^\circ C$** favours ether formation. Above **$170\,^\circ C$**, the competing elimination reaction produces **ethylene** ($C_2H_4$) instead.

### 2.2.2 Williamson Ether Synthesis (Lab-Scale)

A more controlled laboratory route:

$$C_2H_5ONa + C_2H_5Br \longrightarrow C_2H_5-O-C_2H_5 + NaBr$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** For board exam answers, remember that industrial production uses the **dehydration route** because it is inexpensive and ethanol is widely available from **biomass fermentation** — making DEE technically a **bio-derived fuel**.

## 2.3 Physical & Thermochemical Properties — High-Yield Table

| Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Density at $20\,^\circ C$ | **713 kg/m³** | Lower than diesel ($\approx 840$); affects injection mass |
| Boiling Point | **34.6 °C** | Highly volatile; vapour-lock risk |
| Flash Point (closed cup) | **$-45\,^\circ C$** | Extremely flammable; major safety concern |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | **160 °C** | Very low → excellent cold-start |
| Lower Calorific Value (LHV) | **36.5 MJ/kg** | Higher than ethanol (26.8) and methanol (20.0) |
| Stoichiometric AFR | **11.4 : 1** | Lean burn capability |
| Latent Heat of Vaporisation | **377 kJ/kg** | High cooling effect on intake charge |
| Cetane Number | **~ 22** | Poor for pure CI; excellent ignition improver |
| Octane Number (RON) | **~ 110** | Very knock-resistant for SI engines |
| Viscosity at 20 °C | **0.233 mPa·s** | Very low — superb atomisation |
| Oxygen Content (by mass) | **21.6 %** | Intrinsic oxygen carrier; reduces soot |
| Water Solubility | **6.9 % (mass)** | Limited; phase-separation possible |
| Vapour Pressure at 20 °C | **58.6 kPa** | High — pressurised storage required |

## 2.4 Combustion Chemistry of DEE

The combustion of di-ethyl ether proceeds via thermal decomposition and oxidation of the alkyl radicals. A simplified global reaction is:

$$C_4H_{10}O + 6\,O_2 \longrightarrow 4\,CO_2 + 5\,H_2O + \text{Heat}$$

For **stoichiometric air–fuel ratio** derivation:

$$C_4H_{10}O + 6\,(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \longrightarrow 4\,CO_2 + 5\,H_2O + 22.56\,N_2$$

$$\text{Mass of air} = 6 \times 32 + 6 \times 3.76 \times 28 = 823.68 \text{ g}$$

$$\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \frac{823.68}{74.12} \approx 11.1 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **intrinsic oxygen** in DEE assists the oxidation of carbon, leading to **lower smoke emissions** and **lower particulate matter (PM)** compared to pure diesel operation — a major reason it is studied as a CI-engine blend component.

## 2.5 Why DEE is Used as a Fuel / Additive — Engineering Logic

| Engine Type | Role of DEE | Why It Works |
|---|---|---|
| **Diesel (CI)** | Cold-start aid (typically 5–15 % blend) | Low cetane, low AIT = lights up easily |
| **Diesel (CI)** | Smoke/PM suppressant (10–20 % blend) | Oxygenates the fuel, completes combustion |
| **Diesel (CI)** | Cetane improver | Increases blend cetane number |
| **Spark Ignition (SI)** | High-octane racing fuel | RON ≈ 110, resists knock |
| **HCCI / RCCI** | Low-reactivity fuel for LTC | Wider ignition delay window |
| **Dual-fuel** | Pilot fuel | Replaces diesel pilot in CNG/LPG dual-fuel |

## 2.6 Engine Performance Comparison (Diesel vs. DEE Blend)

| Parameter | Pure Diesel | Diesel + 10 % DEE | Diesel + 20 % DEE |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE) | 30–34 % | 31–35 % | 32–36 % |
| Peak Pressure | Higher | Slightly lower | Moderate |
| Ignition Delay | Longer | **Significantly shorter** | Much shorter |
| Smoke (BSU) | Baseline | $-15$ to $-20$ % | $-25$ to $-30$ % |
| HC Emissions | Baseline | Slight increase | Slight increase |
| CO Emissions | Baseline | Lower | Lowest |
| NOx Emissions | Baseline | Slight increase | Moderate increase |
| Cold-start ability | Poor ($-10\,^\circ C$) | Good ($-15\,^\circ C$) | Excellent ($-20\,^\circ C$) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Note (KTU Pattern):** Pure DEE **cannot** be used as the sole fuel in a modern CI engine because its **very low cetane number** leads to **uncontrollably rapid pressure rise** (diesel knock). It is therefore used only as a **blending agent** with petroleum diesel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio for DEE

**Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion equation**

$$C_4H_{10}O + a\,O_2 \longrightarrow b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Apply elemental balance**

- **Carbon balance:** $4 = b \;\Rightarrow\; b = 4$
- **Hydrogen balance:** $10 = 2c \;\Rightarrow\; c = 5$
- **Oxygen balance:** $1 + 2a = 2b + c = 2(4) + 5 = 13 \;\Rightarrow\; 2a = 12 \;\Rightarrow\; a = 6$

**Step 3 — Balanced equation**

$$C_4H_{10}O + 6\,O_2 \longrightarrow 4\,CO_2 + 5\,H_2O$$

**Step 4 — Introduce air composition** (1 mole $O_2$ + 3.76 moles $N_2$ per mole air)

$$C_4H_{10}O + 6\,(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \longrightarrow 4\,CO_2 + 5\,H_2O + 22.56\,N_2$$

**Step 5 — Mass of air per kg of fuel**

$$\text{Mass of air} = (6 \times 32) + (22.56 \times 28) = 192 + 631.68 = 823.68 \text{ kg air per kmol fuel}$$

$$\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \frac{823.68 \text{ kg air}}{74.12 \text{ kg fuel}} \approx 11.11 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Lower Calorific Value (LHV) of DEE

Using the **Dulong-type empirical formula** for oxygenated hydrocarbons:

$$LHV_{MJ/kg} = 33.83\,C + 144.3\,(H - \tfrac{O}{8}) + 9.42\,S$$

For pure DEE ($C_4H_{10}O$), elemental mass fractions:

$$\%C = \frac{48}{74.12} \times 100 = 64.76\,\%$$

$$\%H = \frac{10.08}{74.12} \times 100 = 13.60\,\%$$

$$\%O = \frac{16}{74.12} \times 100 = 21.58\,\%$$

Applying the formula:

$$LHV = 33.83(64.76) + 144.3\,(13.60 - \tfrac{21.58}{8})$$

$$= 2190.6 + 144.3\,(13.60 - 2.697)$$

$$= 2190.6 + 144.3\,(10.903)$$

$$= 2190.6 + 1573.3 \approx 36.04 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Experimental value:** $LHV \approx 36.5$ MJ/kg — agreement is within **1.3 %** error, validating the empirical approach.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Evaluation of DEE with Other Alternative Fuels

| Property | **DEE** | Bio-ethanol | Bio-diesel | Diesel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Formula | $C_4H_{10}O$ | $C_2H_5OH$ | $C_{19}H_{36}O_2$ (avg) | $C_{12}H_{23}$ (avg) |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | **36.5** | 26.8 | 37.2 | 43.0 |
| Octane (RON) | **110** | 110 | 25 | 25 |
| Cetane Number | **22** | 8 | 50–55 | 50 |
| AIT (°C) | **160** | 365 | 250 | 250 |
| Oxygen (% mass) | **21.6** | 34.7 | 11.0 | 0 |
| Boiling Point (°C) | **34.6** | 78.4 | > 300 | 180–360 |
| Density (kg/m³) | **713** | 789 | 880 | 840 |
| Viscosity (mm²/s) | **0.23** | 1.5 | 4.5 | 3.5 |
| Flash Point (°C) | **$-45$** | 13 | 130 | 70 |
| Cold-start aid | **Excellent** | Poor | Poor | Poor |
| Smoke reduction | **Good** | Fair | Excellent | Baseline |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke CI engine is fuelled with a blend of **80 % diesel** and **20 % di-ethyl ether (by volume)**. The engine develops **15 kW** of brake power at **1500 rpm** with a **brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC)** of **0.30 kg/kWh**. Calculate:
> 1. The mass flow rate of the blend.
> 2. The mass flow rate of pure DEE.
> 3. The energy supplied per second by DEE alone.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Brake fuel flow rate of blend**

$$\dot{m}_{blend} = BSFC \times P_b = 0.30 \times 15 = 4.5 \text{ kg/h}$$

Converting: $\dot{m}_{blend} = 4.5/3600 = 0.00125$ kg/s

**Step 2 — Convert volume fraction to mass fraction**

Densities: $\rho_{diesel} = 840$ kg/m³, $\rho_{DEE} = 713$ kg/m³

Mass of 0.8 L diesel: $0.8 \times 840 = 672$ g
Mass of 0.2 L DEE: $0.2 \times 713 = 142.6$ g

$$\text{Mass fraction of DEE} = \frac{142.6}{672 + 142.6} = \frac{142.6}{814.6} = 0.1751$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate of DEE**

$$\dot{m}_{DEE} = 0.1751 \times 0.00125 = 2.19 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Energy supplied by DEE per second**

$$\dot{E}_{DEE} = \dot{m}_{DEE} \times LHV_{DEE} = 2.19 \times 10^{-4} \times 36.5 \times 10^{6}$$

$$\dot{E}_{DEE} = 7.99 \times 10^{3} \text{ J/s} = 7.99 \text{ kW}$$

> **Interpretation:** Even though DEE is only ~17.5 % of the blend by mass, it supplies **~53 % of the energy** needed for the rated power because of its exceptionally high oxygen content that ensures near-complete combustion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Production Route of Di-Ethyl Ether

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomass / Sugarcane / Corn] --> B[Fermentation Tank]
    B --> C[Ethanol C2H5OH, 90-95% purity]
    C --> D[Concentrator Column]
    D --> E[Anhydrous Ethanol 99.5%]
    E --> F[Reactor with Conc. H2SO4 catalyst at 140 deg C]
    F --> G[Crude Di-ethyl ether vapour]
    G --> H[Condenser]
    H --> I[Liquid Crude DEE]
    I --> J[NaOH Wash for acid removal]
    J --> K[Water Wash for impurity removal]
    K --> L[Drying over CaCl2]
    L --> M[Final Distillation at 34.6 deg C]
    M --> N[Pure Di-ethyl ether C2H5-O-C2H5]
    N --> O{Distribution Pathway}
    O --> P[Diesel Cold-Start Additive]
    O --> Q[SI Engine High-Octane Fuel]
    O --> R[HCCI LTC Pilot Fuel]
    O --> S[Pharmaceutical Solvent]

    step1[/"By-product: H2O removed in decanter"/] -.-> F
    step2[/"Note: Temperature must be kept below 170 deg C to avoid ethylene formation"/] -.-> F
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — DEE as Engine Fuel: Functional Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUPPLY[FUEL SUPPLY SUBSYSTEM]
        T1[DEE Storage Tank - Pressurised] --> F1[Pressure Regulator]
        F1 --> F2[Low-Pressure Filter]
        F2 --> F3[Transfer Pump]
    end

    subgraph BLEND[FUEL BLENDING STAGE]
        T2[Diesel Storage Tank] --> M1[Static Inline Mixer]
        F3 --> M1
        M1 --> M2[Blended Fuel 80% Diesel + 20% DEE]
    end

    subgraph INJECT[INJECTION SUBSYSTEM]
        M2 --> P1[High-Pressure Injection Pump]
        P1 --> I1[Common Rail at 600 bar]
        I1 --> IN1[Injector Cylinder 1]
        I1 --> IN2[Injector Cylinder 2]
        I1 --> IN3[Injector Cylinder 3]
        I1 --> IN4[Injector Cylinder 4]
    end

    subgraph COMBUST[COMBUSTION AND OUTPUT]
        IN1 --> C1[Combustion Chamber]
        IN2 --> C1
        IN3 --> C1
        IN4 --> C1
        C1 --> EM1[Mechanical Work on Piston]
        EM1 --> EM2[Crankshaft Rotation]
        EM2 --> EM3[Brake Power Output]
    end

    signal[/"KEY OBSERVATIONS: Lower ignition delay, reduced smoke, marginal NOx rise"/] -.-> COMBUST
```

## 4.3 Engine Operation Comparison — Pure Diesel vs. Diesel + DEE Blend

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DIESEL[PURE DIESEL OPERATION]
        D1[Compression Stroke: Air at 600-800 deg C] --> D2[Fuel Injection Begins]
        D2 --> D3[Ignition Delay: 1.0 to 1.5 ms]
        D3 --> D4[Premixed Combustion Peak]
        D4 --> D5[Diffusion Combustion Phase]
        D5 --> D6[Higher Smoke and PM]
    end

    subgraph BLEND[DIESEL + 20% DEE OPERATION]
        B1[Compression Stroke: Air at 600-800 deg C] --> B2[Blend Injection Begins]
        B2 --> B3[Shorter Ignition Delay: 0.5 to 0.7 ms]
        B3 --> B4[Smoother Premixed Burn]
        B4 --> B5[More Complete Diffusion Burn]
        B5 --> B6[Lower Smoke and PM]
        B5 --> B7[Slight NOx Increase due to higher flame temp]
    end

    D6 --- COMPARE[/"KEY: DEE oxygenates the charge, raising local oxygen partial pressure"/]
    B6 --- COMPARE
    B7 --- COMPARE
```
