# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]

> *List the salient physical and chemical properties of di-ethyl ether that make it suitable as a cold-start improver for compression ignition engines.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Low auto-ignition temperature (160 °C):** Ensures spontaneous ignition at cylinder conditions that would not light up pure diesel. *(1 mark)*
2. **Low cetane number (~22) and high volatility (BP 34.6 °C):** Forms a combustible air–fuel mixture very rapidly. *(1 mark)*
3. **Intrinsic oxygen content (21.6 % by mass):** Promotes complete combustion of the air–fuel charge, reducing ignition delay and smoke. *(1 mark)*

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2023, KTU Model Question Bank]

> *Explain the industrial production of di-ethyl ether from ethanol. State the role of the catalyst and the critical temperature range.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Di-ethyl ether is industrially produced by the **acid-catalysed dehydration of ethanol**:

$$2\,C_2H_5OH \xrightarrow{\text{conc. } H_2SO_4,\; 140\,^\circ C} C_2H_5OC_2H_5 + H_2O$$

1. **Role of $H_2SO_4$:** Acts as a dehydrating agent and protonates ethanol to facilitate nucleophilic attack by a second ethanol molecule. *(1 mark)*
2. **Temperature control:** Reaction must be maintained at **$140\pm5\,^\circ C$** to favour ether formation; above **$170\,^\circ C$**, **ethylene** ($C_2H_4$) is produced by elimination. *(1 mark)*
3. **Process flow:** Crude ether is condensed, washed with NaOH to remove acid traces, dried over $CaCl_2$, and finally distilled at $34.6\,^\circ C$. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Module 2 Instruction (KTU 2024):** Answer ANY ONE full question from Module 2. Each full question carries **14 marks** split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, KTU Model Paper 2024 Set B]

**(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the production of di-ethyl ether from bio-ethanol. Discuss the chemistry and operating conditions in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the properties of di-ethyl ether with ethanol and diesel. Based on this comparison, justify why DEE is preferred over ethanol as a CI-engine cold-start improver. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for **A(a):**

**Step 1 — Feedstock Preparation (1 mark)**
Bio-ethanol from fermentation of biomass is concentrated by distillation to **95 % azeotrope** and then dehydrated using molecular sieves or azeotropic distillation to obtain **anhydrous ethanol (99.5 %+)**.

**Step 2 — Catalytic Dehydration (2 marks)**
The anhydrous ethanol is vapourised and passed through a packed reactor containing **concentrated $H_2SO_4$ on a solid support** (or alternatively a fixed bed of **alumina at $\approx 240\,^\circ C$** for vapour-phase process).

The reaction:

$$2\,C_2H_5OH \;\rightleftharpoons\; C_2H_5OC_2H_5 + H_2O$$

is **exothermic** with $\Delta H \approx -23$ kJ/mol.

**Step 3 — Product Separation (2 marks)**
The reactor effluent (DEE + unreacted ethanol + water) is cooled in a condenser. The aqueous layer is decanted, and the organic layer is:
- Washed with **dilute NaOH** (neutralises residual acid),
- Washed with water,
- Dried over **anhydrous $CaCl_2$**,
- Finally **fractionally distilled** at **$34.6\,^\circ C$** to obtain pure DEE.

**Step 4 — Operating Conditions Summary (2 marks)**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Temperature | $140$–$150\,^\circ C$ (liquid-phase) / $240\,^\circ C$ (vapour-phase) |
| Catalyst | Conc. $H_2SO_4$ / $\gamma-Al_2O_3$ |
| Pressure | Atmospheric |
| Conversion per pass | 60–80 % |
| Selectivity to DEE | $\geq$ 85 % at $140\,^\circ C$ |

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.

#### Model Solution for **A(b):**

**Comparative Property Table (4 marks)**

| Property | DEE | Ethanol | Diesel |
|---|---|---|---|
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 36.5 | 26.8 | 43.0 |
| AIT (°C) | 160 | 365 | 250 |
| Cetane | 22 | 8 | 50 |
| Oxygen % | 21.6 | 34.7 | 0 |
| BP (°C) | 34.6 | 78.4 | 180–360 |
| Viscosity (mm²/s) | 0.23 | 1.5 | 3.5 |

**Justification (3 marks):**

1. **Lower AIT (160 vs 365 °C):** DEE auto-ignites at temperatures that ethanol cannot reach in a CI cylinder, providing **instantaneous cold-start**. *(1 mark)*
2. **Higher Cetane than ethanol (22 vs 8):** A fuel with too-low cetane misfires; DEE is just low enough to delay ignition for mixing, yet high enough to ignite reliably. *(1 mark)*
3. **Lower latent heat and higher volatility:** DEE vaporises faster, forming a combustible mixture in the cold cylinder, whereas ethanol's high latent heat cools the charge excessively. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, KTU Model Paper 2024 Set A]

**(a)** A diesel engine is to be fuelled with a blend of **85 % diesel** and **15 % DEE by volume**. Given $\rho_{diesel} = 840$ kg/m³ and $\rho_{DEE} = 713$ kg/m³, calculate the mass fraction of DEE in the blend and the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio of the blend. Take LHV of DEE as 36.5 MJ/kg and of diesel as 43 MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the engine performance, emission characteristics, and storage/handling challenges of using DEE as an automotive fuel. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for **B(a):**

**Step 1 — Mass of each component in 1 L of blend (1 mark)**

$$m_{diesel} = 0.85 \times 1\,\text{L} \times 840\,\text{kg/m}^3 = 0.714 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{DEE} = 0.15 \times 1\,\text{L} \times 713\,\text{kg/m}^3 = 0.10695 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Total mass and mass fraction of DEE (1 mark)**

$$m_{total} = 0.714 + 0.10695 = 0.82095 \text{ kg}$$

$$X_{DEE} = \frac{0.10695}{0.82095} = 0.1303 \text{ (or 13.03 %)}$$

$$X_{diesel} = 1 - 0.1303 = 0.8697 \text{ (or 86.97 %)}$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric AFR of pure components (2 marks)**

For diesel ($C_{12}H_{23}$, assumed), approximate AFR ≈ **14.5 : 1**
For DEE, AFR = **11.1 : 1** (derived in Section 3.1)

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric AFR of blend (mass-weighted) (2 marks)**

$$AFR_{blend} = \frac{1}{\frac{X_{DEE}}{AFR_{DEE}} + \frac{X_{diesel}}{AFR_{diesel}}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{\frac{0.1303}{11.1} + \frac{0.8697}{14.5}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.01174 + 0.05998} = \frac{1}{0.07172} = 13.94 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Energy content per kg of blend (1 mark)**

$$LHV_{blend} = (0.1303 \times 36.5) + (0.8697 \times 43) = 4.756 + 37.40 = 42.16 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Final Answer:** Mass fraction of DEE = **13.03 %**; Stoichiometric AFR = **13.94 : 1**; LHV = **42.16 MJ/kg**.

#### Model Solution for **B(b):**

**Engine Performance (2.5 marks):**

- **Ignition delay reduced by 30–50 %** compared to diesel, especially at low loads and low temperatures.
- **BTE improves by 2–5 %** in the mid-load range due to better combustion completeness.
- **Cold-start improved** significantly (engine starts at temperatures as low as $-15\,^\circ C$).
- At very high DEE percentages (>25 %), **premixed burn fraction is excessive** causing a sharp pressure rise (diesel knock) and slight **drop in BTE**.

**Emission Characteristics (2.5 marks):**

- **Smoke opacity reduced by 25–40 %** due to oxygenated charge.
- **CO reduced by 20–35 %** from more complete combustion.
- **HC slightly increased** (DEE's high volatility causes wall-quenching losses).
- **NO$_x$ slightly increased** (due to higher peak flame temperatures from oxygen enrichment).
- **Particulate matter (PM) reduced by 30–50 %** — major environmental benefit.

**Storage & Handling Challenges (2 marks):**

- **Flash point $-45\,^\circ C$:** Extreme fire hazard; requires **pressurised storage tanks** and **flame-proof fittings**.
- **High vapour pressure (58.6 kPa at 20 °C):** Tanks must be **sealed and pressure-rated**.
- **Peroxide formation:** On prolonged exposure to air/light, DEE forms **explosive peroxides**; storage requires **inert gas blanketing** and **UV-protected containers**.
- **Material compatibility:** DEE attacks certain plastics and rubbers — fuel lines and seals must be **Teflon or stainless steel**.
- **Hygroscopic:** Absorbs moisture → must be kept dry to avoid corrosion in fuel systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting temperature control** in the production of DEE (must mention the **140 °C vs 170 °C** boundary) — costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing cetane and octane ratings** of DEE (DEE has **low cetane ~22** but **very high RON ~110**) — major conceptual error.
> 3. **Not stating the stoichiometric equation** before calculating AFR — loses the **first step marks**.
> 4. **Ignoring density conversion** when asked for mass fraction from a volume-blend ratio — this is the **most common numerical error**.
> 5. **Calling DEE a "diesel substitute"** instead of a "diesel additive / cold-start improver" — examiners mark this wrong because pure DEE **cannot** run a CI engine on its own.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Chemical Identity:** Di-ethyl ether = ethoxyethane, formula **$C_2H_5-O-C_2H_5$**, molar mass **74.12 g/mol**, oxygen content **21.6 %**.
- **Production:** Acid-catalysed dehydration of ethanol at **$140\,^\circ C$**; **$H_2SO_4$** catalyst; must avoid temperatures above $170\,^\circ C$ (ethylene forms).
- **Physical Traits:** Colourless, very volatile (**BP $34.6\,^\circ C$**), extremely flammable (**Flash $-45\,^\circ C$**), low density (**$713$ kg/m³**).
- **Energy Content:** LHV $\approx$ **36.5 MJ/kg**; Stoichiometric AFR $\approx$ **11.1 : 1**.
- **Cetane Number:** Low (**~22**) → poor CI fuel alone, **excellent** as ignition improver.
- **Octane Number:** Very high (**RON ~110**) → resists knock in SI engines.
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature:** **$160\,^\circ C$** — the lowest among common liquid fuels; the core reason for its cold-start utility.
- **Engine Use:** Primarily a **diesel blend additive (5–20 %)** for cold-start, smoke reduction, and better combustion completeness.
- **Emission Impact:** **Reduces** smoke, PM, CO; **slightly increases** HC and NO$_x$.
- **Storage Hazards:** Peroxide formation, very low flash point, vapour-lock risk, material compatibility issues.
- **Engineering Insight:** DEE is **not a stand-alone fuel** in conventional engines — it is a **performance-enhancing oxygenate additive** that unlocks cleaner, more complete combustion when blended with diesel.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Availability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Availability ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Availability (Exergy) **Availability**, also known as **Exergy** or **Available Energy**, is formally defined as the **maximum useful work** that can be extracted from a system as it is brought reversibly from its initial state to the **dead state** (thermodynamic equilibrium with the environment), while interacting only with the surroundings at constant pressure $P_0$ and temperature $T_0$. Mathematically, for a closed system, the availability is expressed as: $$\phi = (U - U_0) + P_0(V - V_0) - T_0(S - S_0)$$ where the subscript '0' denotes properties at the **dead state**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Availability quantifies the *quality* of energy, not just its quantity. It is the only true measure of a fuel's or energy stream's capacity to do useful work, making it indispensable in evaluating alternate fuels and energy systems. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine two reservoirs of water, both containing **1000 liters** at the same temperature. - **Reservoir A** is held at the top of a hill, 100 m above sea level. - **Reservoir B** is sitting in a valley, at the same level as the sea (the "environment"). Both have the same *amount* of water, but only **Reservoir A** can spin a turbine to generate electricity. The water in **Reservoir B** is "dead" — it is in equilibrium with its surroundings and has no potential to do work. In this analogy: - **Energy (Quantity)** = the 1000 liters of water - **Availability (Quality)** = the height above the surroundings - **Dead State** = the valley (equilibrium with the environment) Similarly, the high-temperature exhaust gases from a biomass combustor may carry enormous **enthalpy** (quantity), but their **availability** (capacity to do useful work) is far less because they cannot be cooled below the ambient temperature $T_0$. ### 1.3 Available vs. Unavailable Energy Any energy $E$ possessed by a system can be partitioned into two parts: $$E = \text{Available Energy (Availability)} + \text{Unavailable Energy}$$ The **unavailable energy** is the portion that *must* be discarded to the surroundings (it is "wasted" by the Second Law of Thermodynamics): $$\text{Unavailable Energy} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants Used:** > - Standard ambient temperature: $T_0 = 298.15 \text{ K}$ (**25 °C**, **77 °F**) > - Standard ambient pressure: $P_0 = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ (**1 atm**) > - Universal Gas Constant: $R_u = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)** > - These are the reference "dead state" conditions used universally in KTU problems unless otherwise specified. ### 1.4 The Dead State The **Dead State** is the unique state of a system that is in complete thermodynamic equilibrium with its environment. At this state: - Mechanical equilibrium: $P = P_0$ - Thermal equilibrium: $T = T_0$ - Chemical equilibrium: $\mu_i = \mu_{i,0}$ for all species > [!IMPORTANT] > At the dead state, the system possesses **zero availability** — it has no potential to produce any useful work. This is the conceptual foundation of why waste heat at ambient temperature is "useless." ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework Availability analysis rests upon three foundational pillars: 1. **The First Law of Thermodynamics** — Energy is conserved (it cannot be created or destroyed). 2. **The Second Law of Thermodynamics** — Not all energy can be converted to work; entropy generation destroys *quality*. 3. **The concept of equilibrium with the environment** — Only differences from the surroundings can produce useful work. The "Why" and "How" behind Availability analysis: - **Why Availability?** Because the First Law only tells us *how much* energy is in a system, not *how useful* it is. A 1 kg block of hot copper at 1000 K and a 1 kg block at 300 K both contain the same internal energy (well, not exactly, but the point is they can do very different amounts of work), and the First Law cannot distinguish them. The Second Law, through Availability, captures this difference. - **How is it calculated?** By hypothetically bringing the system to its dead state via a **reversible process** (with surroundings at $P_0$, $T_0$) and computing the maximum work done in the process. ### 2.2 Types of Availability There are four principal forms of availability relevant to KTU problems on alternate fuels and energy systems: | # | Type | Physical Meaning | Mathematical Form | |---|------|------------------|-------------------| | 1 | **Non-Flow (Closed System) Availability, $\phi$** | Maximum work extractable from a closed system at rest, interacting with environment | $(U - U_0) + P_0(V - V_0) - T_0(S - S_0)$ | | 2 | **Flow Availability (Open System / Steady-Flow), $\psi$** | Maximum work from a flowing stream (turbine, nozzle, etc.) | $(H - H_0) - T_0(S - S_0) + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$ | | 3 | **Chemical Availability** | Work from chemical composition differences (fuel oxidation) | $\sum_i n_i \mu_{i,0}^{ch}$ | | 4 | **Physical Availability** | Work from $T, P$ differences only (no chemical reactions) | Combinations of the above without chemical terms | > [!NOTE] > For **alternate fuels and energy systems** (the focus of OEMUT615), **chemical availability** is the most relevant because fuels like hydrogen, ethanol, biodiesel, and biogas release energy *and* create availability through chemical reactions. ### 2.3 Irreversibility and Gouy-Stodola Theorem Every real process is **irreversible** — it generates entropy. The **Irreversibility (I)** or **Lost Work ($W_{lost}$)** is the difference between the ideal reversible work and the actual work. By the **Gouy-Stodola Theorem** (1889, French thermodynamicist): $$W_{lost} = I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$$ where $S_{gen}$ is the total entropy generation of the process. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Pearl:** Whenever a problem says "find the lost work" or "irreversibility," always compute entropy generation $S_{gen}$ first, then multiply by $T_0$. This is worth 2-3 marks every time it appears in KTU papers. ### 2.4 Second Law Efficiency (Exergy Efficiency) The **First Law efficiency** measures how much energy is conserved, but the **Second Law efficiency** measures how much *availability* is preserved: $$\eta_{II} = \frac{\text{Exergy Recovered (Output)}}{\text{Exergy Supplied (Input)}} = 1 - \frac{\text{Exergy Destroyed}}{\text{Exergy Supplied}}$$ Alternative forms (depending on the system): - **Work-producing device:** $\eta_{II} = \frac{W_{actual}}{W_{reversible}} = 1 - \frac{T_0 \cdot S_{gen}}{W_{reversible}}$ - **Combustion process:** $\eta_{II} = 1 - \frac{T_0 \cdot S_{gen}}{\text{Chemical Exergy of Fuel}}$ > [!NOTE] > For alternate fuels like hydrogen or biogas, the second law efficiency of the combustor is often quite low (50-70%) because of large entropy generation from high-temperature heat transfer and irreversible combustion. This is a frequent KTU Part B (14-mark) question. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table contains the complete formula set you must memorize for KTU Board Exams on Availability.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\phi = (U - U_0) + P_0(V - V_0) - T_0(S - S_0)$ | Closed-system availability | kJ | | 2 | $\psi = (H - H_0) - T_0(S - S_0)$ | Flow availability (neglecting KE, PE) | kJ/kg | | 3 | $\psi_{total} = (H - H_0) - T_0(S - S_0) + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$ | Flow availability (with KE & PE) | kJ/kg | | 4 | $W_{max,closed} = \phi_1 - \phi_2$ | Max. work from closed system | kJ | | 5 | $W_{max,flow} = \psi_1 - \psi_2$ | Max. work from open system | kJ/kg | | 6 | $I = W_{rev} - W_{actual}$ | Irreversibility (lost work) | kJ | | 7 | $I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ | Gouy-Stodola theorem | kJ | | 8 | $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surroundings}$ | Total entropy generation | kJ/K | | 9 | $\eta_{II} = 1 - \dfrac{I}{\text{Exergy input}}$ | Second law efficiency | — | | 10 | $\Delta \phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1$ | Availability change of closed system | kJ | | 11 | $\text{Unavailability} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ | Unavailable energy | kJ | | 12 | $\phi_{incomp} = c \left[ (T - T_0) - T_0 \ln\left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right) \right]$ | Availability of incompressible substance | kJ/kg | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility of Availability Analysis:** > - In **hydrogen fuel cells**, availability analysis reveals whether the electrochemical work or the wasted heat is the dominant output. > - In **biogas engines**, it quantifies whether 1 MJ of methane energy can really deliver 1 MJ of mechanical work (it cannot — only ~30-40% is "available"). > - In **solar PV and wind systems**, it correctly accounts for the low availability of diffuse, low-temperature energy, helping designers size storage and backup systems. > - In **CO₂ capture from flue gas**, it pinpoints exactly where the highest exergy destruction occurs (typically in the regenerator/reboiler). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Non-Flow Availability (Closed System) Consider a closed system of mass $m$ in a state $(T_1, P_1, V_1, U_1, S_1)$ that is allowed to interact with the environment at $(T_0, P_0)$ and reversibly reaches the dead state $(T_0, P_0, V_0, U_0, S_0)$. **Step 1:** Apply the First Law to the closed system during this hypothetical reversible process. The reversible work done by the system is $W_{rev}$. The only heat transfer is to the surroundings at $T_0$, which is the dead-state temperature. Therefore: $$W_{rev} = Q_{rev} - (U_1 - U_0)$$ **Step 2:** Write the heat transfer for a reversible process. For a reversible process, the change in entropy of the universe (system + surroundings) is zero: $$\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surroundings} = 0$$ **Step 3:** Express the change in entropy of the surroundings. The surroundings exchange heat $Q_{rev}$ at constant temperature $T_0$, so: $$\Delta S_{surroundings} = -\frac{Q_{rev}}{T_0}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute Step 3 into Step 2. $$\Delta S_{system} - \frac{Q_{rev}}{T_0} = 0$$ $$Q_{rev} = T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{system} = T_0 (S_0 - S_1)$$ **Step 5:** Substitute Step 4 into Step 1. $$W_{rev} = T_0 (S_0 - S_1) - (U_1 - U_0)$$ **Step 6:** Rearrange. $$W_{rev} = (U_1 - U_0) - T_0 (S_1 - S_0)$$ **Step 7:** Now account for the change in volume. When the system expands or contracts at the dead-state pressure $P_0$, it does boundary work on (or receives work from) the surroundings. The surroundings' volume change at constant $P_0$ contributes: $$W_{boundary} = P_0 (V_1 - V_0)$$ **Step 8:** Add this to the reversible work to get the total maximum useful work. The total maximum useful work includes the work the system does *plus* the work the surroundings recover from the volume change. The "useful work" excludes the work used to push back the atmosphere (since that work is not useful to us). So: $$W_{max,useful} = (U_1 - U_0) + P_0(V_1 - V_0) - T_0(S_1 - S_0)$$ **Step 9:** Define this as the closed-system availability. $$\boxed{\phi = (U - U_0) + P_0(V - V_0) - T_0(S - S_0)}$$ This is the **non-flow availability** of the system. ### 3.2 Derivation of Flow Availability (Open System / Steady-Flow Process) Consider a control volume with a single inlet and single outlet under steady-state conditions. The fluid enters at state 1 and exits at state 2. **Step 1:** Apply the Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE). For a control volume at steady state with one inlet and one outlet: $$q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)$$ where $w$ is the shaft work output (the "useful" work). **Step 2:** Solve for the useful work output. $$w = q - (h_2 - h_1) - \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} - g(z_2 - z_1)$$ **Step 3:** Apply the Second Law to the control volume. For a reversible process with the surroundings at $T_0$: $$q_{rev} = T_0 (s_2 - s_1)$$ **Step 4:** Substitute Step 3 into Step 2. $$w_{rev} = T_0 (s_2 - s_1) - (h_2 - h_1) - \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} - g(z_2 - z_1)$$ **Step 5:** Rearrange. $$w_{rev} = (h_1 - h_2) - T_0 (s_1 - s_2) - \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} - g(z_2 - z_1)$$ **Step 6:** Define the specific flow availability as the maximum work per unit mass. $$\psi = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0)$$ **Step 7:** Extend to include kinetic and potential energy. For processes where changes in kinetic energy $\frac{V^2}{2}$ and potential energy $gz$ are significant (e.g., wind turbines, hydroelectric systems): $$\boxed{\psi_{total} = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz}$$ **Step 8:** The maximum useful work for a flow process is then: $$W_{max,flow} = m \cdot (\psi_1 - \psi_2)$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Irreversibility from Gouy-Stodola Theorem **Step 1:** For an actual (irreversible) process, entropy is generated. $$S_{gen} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surroundings} \geq 0$$ **Step 2:** The work output of an actual process is less than the reversible (maximum) work. $$W_{actual} = W_{rev} - W_{lost}$$ **Step 3:** For a steady-flow device with heat input $q$ at $T_0$: $$W_{rev} = q - \Delta h$$ $$W_{actual} = q - \Delta h - T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$$ **Step 4:** Equating the two expressions for $W_{rev}$: $$W_{rev} - W_{actual} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$$ **Step 5:** Therefore, the lost work (irreversibility) is: $$\boxed{I = W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}}$$ This is the **Gouy-Stodola Theorem**, valid for any system in thermal contact with the environment at $T_0$. ### 3.4 Derivation of Availability for an Ideal Gas (Important for KTU Problems) For an ideal gas, the change in internal energy depends only on temperature, and the change in entropy is given by the well-known formula. Using these: **Step 1:** Write the closed-system availability equation. $$\phi = (U - U_0) + P_0(V - V_0) - T_0(S - S_0)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $U - U_0 = m c_v (T - T_0)$ for an ideal gas. **Step 3:** Substitute $V = \dfrac{mRT}{P}$ and $V_0 = \dfrac{mRT_0}{P_0}$. **Step 4:** Substitute the entropy change: $$S - S_0 = m \left[ c_p \ln\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right) - R \ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) \right]$$ **Step 5:** Combine all terms to get the final result. $$\phi = m \left[ c_v (T - T_0) + P_0 \left(\frac{RT}{P} - \frac{RT_0}{P_0}\right) - T_0 \left( c_p \ln\frac{T}{T_0} - R \ln\frac{P}{P_0} \right) \right]$$ **Step 6:** Simplify using $c_p - c_v = R$ and $P_0 V = m R T_0$ at the dead state: $$\boxed{\phi = m \left[ c_p (T - T_0) - T_0 \ln\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right) + R T_0 \ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) \right]}$$ This is the **standard form of availability for an ideal gas** and is a KTU exam favorite. > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical Note:** When $P = P_0$ (constant-pressure process), the last term vanishes, and we get a much simpler form. When $T = T_0$ (isothermal), the first two terms vanish. Both are special cases the examiner may test. ### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern) **Problem:** A piston-cylinder contains 2 kg of air at 500 K and 200 kPa. The surroundings are at $T_0 = 300$ K and $P_0 = 100$ kPa. Calculate the availability of the air. Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K). **Step 1:** Identify the given values. - $m = 2$ kg, $T = 500$ K, $P = 200$ kPa - $T_0 = 300$ K, $P_0 = 100$ kPa **Step 2:** Apply the ideal gas availability formula from §3.4, Step 6. $$\phi = m \left[ c_p (T - T_0) - T_0 \ln\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right) + R T_0 \ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) \right]$$ **Step 3:** Compute each term. Term 1: $c_p (T - T_0) = 1.005 \times (500 - 300) = 1.005 \times 200 = 201.0$ kJ/kg Term 2: $T_0 \ln\left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right) = 300 \times \ln\left(\dfrac{500}{300}\right) = 300 \times \ln(1.6667)$ $$\ln(1.6667) = 0.5108$$ $$T_0 \ln\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right) = 300 \times 0.5108 = 153.25 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Term 3: $R T_0 \ln\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right) = 0.287 \times 300 \times \ln\left(\dfrac{200}{100}\right)$ $$\ln(2) = 0.6931$$ $$R T_0 \ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = 0.287 \times 300 \times 0.6931 = 59.68 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4:** Combine. $$\phi/m = 201.0 - 153.25 + 59.68 = 107.43 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5:** Multiply by mass. $$\phi = 2 \times 107.43 = 214.86 \text{ kJ}$$ **Answer:** The availability of 2 kg of air at the specified state is **214.86 kJ**. > [!NOTE] > **Verification by physical reasoning:** The internal energy of 2 kg of air at 500 K relative to 300 K is $2 \times 0.718 \times 200 = 287.2$ kJ. Of this energy, only 214.86 kJ can be converted to useful work — the rest (72.34 kJ) must be discarded as waste heat to the surroundings, in accordance with the Second Law. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Availability Analysis Framework for Alternate Fuels ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Alternate Fuel Input

After conversion, the work output is always less than the chemical exergy input because of **irreversibility** (combustion entropy, heat transfer across finite temperature differences, friction, etc.). The Second Law efficiency $\eta_{II}$ captures this gap. ### 4.2 Schematic: Exergy Destruction in a Combustion Process ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input["AVAILABILITY INPUT"] InChem["Chemical Exergy of Fuel

I equals T0 times Sgen"] end InChem --> C1 C1 --> OutWork C1 --> OutHeat C1 --> OutStack C1 --> Des Des -.->|"Exergy Destroyed"| C1 ``` **Interpretation:** In any combustion process, the chemical exergy of the fuel is split into four destinations: useful work, useful heat (above ambient), stack/flue gas exergy, and **destroyed exergy** (irreversibility). The sum of outputs plus destroyed exergy equals the input — this is the **exergy balance**. ### 4.3 Decision Tree: When to Use Closed vs. Flow Availability ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"Is the system

psi total equals h minus h0 minus T0 s minus s0 plus V squared over 2 plus gz"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define the term "availability" of a system. What is the "dead state"?** **Model Answer:** Availability is defined as the maximum useful work that can be obtained from a system as it is brought reversibly from its initial state to the dead state, while interacting only with the environment at constant pressure $P_0$ and temperature $T_0$. For a closed system: $$\phi = (U - U_0) + P_0(V - V_0) - T_0(S - S_0)$$ The **dead state** is the unique thermodynamic state at which the system is in complete equilibrium (mechanical, thermal, and chemical) with the environment. At this state, the system has zero availability and zero capacity to produce useful work. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **State the Gouy-Stodola theorem. How is it used in second law analysis?** **Model Answer:** The **Gouy-Stodola theorem** states that the lost work (irreversibility) in any process is directly proportional to the total entropy generation of the universe, with the proportionality constant being the absolute temperature of the surroundings: $$W_{lost} = I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$$ It is used in second law analysis as follows: - For a given process, $S_{gen} \geq 0$ (Second Law). - This implies $I \geq 0$, confirming that the actual work is always less than the reversible work. - The lost work represents the **exergy destroyed** during the process, which is a direct measure of thermodynamic inefficiency. - It is the foundation for computing the **second law efficiency**: $$\eta_{II} = 1 - \frac{T_0 \cdot S_{gen}}{W_{rev}}$$ --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Starting from the First and Second Laws of thermodynamics, derive the expression for the **non-flow availability** of a closed system. **(b)** A rigid tank contains 5 kg of air at 600 kPa and 400 K. The surroundings are at 100 kPa and 300 K. Calculate the maximum useful work that can be obtained from this air as it comes to equilibrium with the surroundings. Assume air behaves as an ideal gas with $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K) and $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K). --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** The derivation is exactly as in **§3.1** of these notes. The student should write the following key steps (each worth ~1 mark): 1. State the First Law for a closed system in differential form: $\delta Q = dU + \delta W_{rev}$. 2. Apply the Second Law: $dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T_{boundary}}$. For a reversible process with the environment at $T_0$: $\delta Q_{rev} = T_0 \, dS$. 3. Substitute to eliminate $\delta Q_{rev}$: $$\delta W_{rev} = -dU + T_0 \, dS$$ 4. The total work includes boundary work: $\delta W_{boundary} = P_0 \, dV$. The **useful** work excludes this: $$\delta W_{useful} = -dU + T_0 \, dS - P_0 \, dV$$ 5. Integrate from the initial state to the dead state: $$W_{max,useful} = (U_1 - U_0) + P_0(V_1 - V_0) - T_0(S_1 - S_0)$$ 6. State the final boxed result: $\phi = (U - U_0) + P_0(V - V_0) - T_0(S - S_0)$. **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Given:** - $m = 5$ kg, $P_1 = 600$ kPa, $T_1 = 400$ K - $P_0 = 100$ kPa, $T_0 = 300$ K - $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K) - Rigid tank: $V_1 = V_0$ (constant volume) **Step 1:** Calculate $T_0$ and $V_1$. Find the volume using the ideal gas law: $$V_1 = \frac{m R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{5 \times 0.287 \times 400}{600} = \frac{574}{600} = 0.9567 \text{ m}^3$$ Since the tank is rigid, $V_0 = V_1 = 0.9567$ m³. **Step 2:** Find $T_0$ when the air reaches the dead state (constant volume, $P_0$): $$T_0 = \frac{P_0 V_0}{mR} = \frac{100 \times 0.9567}{5 \times 0.287} = \frac{95.67}{1.435} = 66.67 \text{ K}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interesting observation:** The dead-state temperature for this rigid-tank air is 66.67 K, not 300 K! This is because the air must expand to reach 100 kPa, but the tank is rigid. So the air cools down to a very low temperature to maintain pressure equilibrium. This is a classic KTU trap question. **Step 3:** Compute $(U_1 - U_0)$: $$U_1 - U_0 = m c_v (T_1 - T_0) = 5 \times 0.718 \times (400 - 66.67) = 5 \times 0.718 \times 333.33 = 1196.66 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 4:** Compute $P_0(V_1 - V_0)$: $$P_0(V_1 - V_0) = 100 \times (0.9567 - 0.9567) = 0 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 5:** Compute $T_0(S_1 - S_0)$: For an ideal gas at constant volume: $$S_1 - S_0 = m \left[ c_v \ln\left(\frac{T_1}{T_0}\right) + R \ln\left(\frac{V_1}{V_0}\right) \right]$$ Since $V_1 = V_0$, the second term is zero: $$S_1 - S_0 = 5 \times 0.718 \times \ln\left(\frac{400}{66.67}\right) = 5 \times 0.718 \times \ln(6.0)$$ $$\ln(6.0) = 1.7918$$ $$S_1 - S_0 = 5 \times 0.718 \times 1.7918 = 6.434 \text{ kJ/K}$$ $$T_0(S_1 - S_0) = 100 \times 6.434 = 643.4 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 6:** Substitute into the availability equation. $$\phi = (U_1 - U_0) + P_0(V_1 - V_0) - T_0(S_1 - S_0) = 1196.66 + 0 - 643.4 = 553.26 \text{ kJ}$$ **Answer:** The maximum useful work that can be extracted is **$\phi = 553.26$ kJ**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1 (2 marks lost):** Using $T_0 = 300$ K (the surroundings' temperature) instead of finding the actual final temperature $T_0^{final} = 66.67$ K in a rigid-tank problem. Always check whether the volume is constant or whether the system can expand. > - **Common Mistake 2 (1-2 marks lost):** Forgetting to compute $V_1$ from the ideal gas law. Many students assume $V_1 = 1$ m³, which is not given. > - **Common Mistake 3 (1 mark lost):** Writing $T_0 \ln(T/T_0)$ but using $T_0 = 300$ K when the system is rigid — this is a logical error. The dead state must be calculated from the constraints of the problem, not assumed. > - **Common Mistake 4 (1-2 marks lost):** Not specifying units in the final answer or not stating that the answer is in kJ. --- **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative — KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]** **(a)** Derive the expression for **flow availability (flow exergy)** for a steady-flow open system. State clearly the assumptions made. **(b)** A gas turbine receives air at 1000 K and 800 kPa and exhausts it at 700 K and 100 kPa. The mass flow rate is 5 kg/s. The surroundings are at 300 K and 100 kPa. Calculate: - (i) The specific flow availability at the inlet and outlet - (ii) The maximum power output of the turbine - (iii) The second law efficiency if the actual power output is 1500 kW Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) and $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K). --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** The derivation follows **§3.2** of these notes. Key points the student should write: 1. State the **Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** and identify the system as a control volume. 2. Apply the **Second Law** to the control volume in differential form. 3. State the assumptions: - Steady state, single inlet and single outlet. - Heat transfer occurs only at the surroundings' temperature $T_0$ (for the reversible case). - Kinetic and potential energy changes are initially neglected. 4. Combine the SFEE and the Second Law equation. 5. Derive the specific flow availability: $$\psi = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0)$$ 6. State the final answer and note the units (kJ/kg for specific; kJ for total when multiplied by mass). **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Given:** - $T_1 = 1000$ K, $P_1 = 800$ kPa - $T_2 = 700$ K, $P_2 = 100$ kPa - $T_0 = 300$ K, $P_0 = 100$ kPa - $\dot{m} = 5$ kg/s - $W_{actual} = 1500$ kW - $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K) **Step (i):** Compute $\psi_1$ and $\psi_2$ using the ideal-gas flow availability formula (derived by substituting $h - h_0 = c_p(T - T_0)$ and $s - s_0 = c_p \ln(T/T_0) - R \ln(P/P_0)$): $$\psi = c_p(T - T_0) - T_0 \left[ c_p \ln\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right) - R \ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) \right]$$ **At inlet (state 1):** $$\psi_1 = 1.005 \times (1000 - 300) - 300 \times \left[ 1.005 \ln\left(\frac{1000}{300}\right) - 0.287 \ln\left(\frac{800}{100}\right) \right]$$ $$\psi_1 = 1.005 \times 700 - 300 \times \left[ 1.005 \times 1.2040 - 0.287 \times 2.0794 \right]$$ $$\psi_1 = 703.5 - 300 \times [1.2100 - 0.5968]$$ $$\psi_1 = 703.5 - 300 \times 0.6132 = 703.5 - 183.96 = 519.54 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **At outlet (state 2):** $$\psi_2 = 1.005 \times (700 - 300) - 300 \times \left[ 1.005 \ln\left(\frac{700}{300}\right) - 0.287 \ln\left(\frac{100}{100}\right) \right]$$ Note: $P_2 = P_0 = 100$ kPa, so the last term is zero. $$\psi_2 = 1.005 \times 400 - 300 \times \left[ 1.005 \times \ln(2.333) - 0 \right]$$ $$\ln(2.333) = 0.8473$$ $$\psi_2 = 402.0 - 300 \times 1.005 \times 0.8473 = 402.0 - 255.46 = 146.54 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step (ii):** Maximum power output. $$\dot{W}_{max} = \dot{m} (\psi_1 - \psi_2) = 5 \times (519.54 - 146.54) = 5 \times 373.0 = 1865 \text{ kW}$$ **Step (iii):** Second law efficiency. $$\eta_{II} = \frac{\dot{W}_{actual}}{\dot{W}_{max}} = \frac{1500}{1865} = 0.8043 = 80.43\%$$ **Answers:** - (i) $\psi_1 = 519.54$ kJ/kg, $\psi_2 = 146.54$ kJ/kg - (ii) $\dot{W}_{max} = 1865$ kW - (iii) $\eta_{II} = 80.43\%$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1 (2 marks lost):** Using the *First Law efficiency* (which would be based on enthalpy difference) instead of the *Second Law efficiency* (which is based on availability difference). For the same turbine, the First Law efficiency is $1 - T_2/T_1 \approx 30\%$, while the Second Law efficiency is $80.43\%$. The exam specifically asks for the Second Law efficiency, so the answer must be based on $\psi$, not on $h$. > - **Common Mistake 2 (1-2 marks lost):** Forgetting to take the logarithm of $P_2/P_0$ correctly. Since $P_2 = P_0 = 100$ kPa, the term $R \ln(P_2/P_0) = 0$. Many students incorrectly compute $R \ln(100/300)$ for the outlet — this is wrong because $P_0 = 100$ kPa, not 300 kPa. > - **Common Mistake 3 (1 mark lost):** Reporting $\eta_{II}$ as a fraction (0.8043) instead of a percentage (80.43%). The KTU marking scheme explicitly awards 1 mark for the correct format. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam — Module 2, Availability:** - **Availability** is the *maximum useful work* extractable from a system brought reversibly to the **dead state** (equilibrium with the environment at $T_0, P_0$). - **Two key equations to memorize cold:** - Closed system: $\phi = (U - U_0) + P_0(V - V_0) - T_0(S - S_0)$ - Open system: $\psi = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0)$ - **Dead State Conditions** (always check before solving): - $T = T_0$ (thermal equilibrium) - $P = P_0$ (mechanical equilibrium) - Chemical equilibrium (for chemical availability problems) - For rigid tanks, $V$ is constant, so $T_0$ and $P_0$ are reached *at a different temperature* than 300 K — compute it! - **Gouy-Stodola Theorem:** $W_{lost} = I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ — the *most important equation* for second law analysis. - **Second Law Efficiency:** $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{\text{Exergy recovered}}{\text{Exergy supplied}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_0 S_{gen}}{W_{rev}}$ - **Irreversibility is everywhere** — friction, heat transfer across finite $\Delta T$, mixing, chemical reactions (especially combustion), throttling. Each is a source of exergy destruction. - **For ideal gases**, the working formulas are: - $\phi = m \left[ c_v(T - T_0) + P_0 \left( \dfrac{RT}{P} - \dfrac{RT_0}{P_0} \right) - T_0 \left( c_p \ln\dfrac{T}{T_0} - R \ln\dfrac{P}{P_0} \right) \right]$ - $\psi = c_p(T - T_0) - T_0 \left( c_p \ln\dfrac{T}{T_0} - R \ln\dfrac{P}{P_0} \right)$ - **Alternate Fuels Connection:** - Hydrogen has the **highest chemical availability per unit mass** of any fuel. - Biogas (mostly CH₄ + CO₂) has reduced chemical availability due to the CO₂ dilution. - Biodiesel has lower chemical availability than diesel due to oxygen content. - Combustion has the **lowest second law efficiency** of any energy conversion (often only 30-50%) because of the large entropy generation in the flame. - **Units to remember:** kJ (total), kJ/kg (specific), kJ/K (entropy), K (temperature), kPa (pressure). - **Exam Strategy:** - Always write the dead state explicitly before starting any calculation. - Always state assumptions (ideal gas? KE/PE neglected? Steady state?). - Always check whether the process is closed or open before choosing $\phi$ or $\psi$. - The "trap" of $P_0$ vs. $P_2$ is common — the surroundings' pressure is 100 kPa, not the inlet or outlet pressure. - **Key Distinction:** First Law efficiency measures *quantity* conservation; Second Law efficiency measures *quality* (availability) conservation. They are *not* the same number. > [!NOTE] > **Final Study Tip:** KTU 2024 Scheme questions on Availability are typically framed around a real alternate-fuel scenario (hydrogen fuel cell, biogas engine, biodiesel combustor, etc.) and ask you to compute availability, lost work, or second law efficiency. Practice at least **3 numerical problems** from each of the formula categories above before the exam. The 14-mark questions in Section 5.2 are directly modeled on KTU's actual board paper style.

topics.title: Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **OEMUT615 – Alternate Fuels and Energy Systems**, the term **"Performance"** refers to the systematic evaluation and quantification of how efficiently and effectively an **alternate fuel** (biodiesel, ethanol, methanol, hydrogen, CNG, LPG, biogas, etc.) delivers usable energy, produces useful work, and behaves in thermodynamic systems (primarily Internal Combustion engines and energy conversion devices) when compared against a baseline conventional fuel (petrol/diesel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *Performance of an alternate fuel is the measure of its combustion behavior, energy release characteristics, and resulting engine/system outputs—quantified through parameters such as Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE), Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC), volumetric efficiency, power output, energy efficiency, and emission profile—when the fuel is used either in pure form or as a blend in a standard prime mover.*

The three pillars of performance assessment under KTU Module-2 evaluation are:

1. **Energy Release Performance** – How completely and cleanly the fuel releases its chemical energy.
2. **Work Output Performance** – How much of that energy is converted to useful mechanical/electrical work.
3. **Operational Performance** – How the fuel behaves in terms of storage, handling, knock resistance, and emissions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English View)

Imagine you are running a **restaurant kitchen** powered by gas stoves. Petrol/diesel is your *standard LPG cylinder* — predictable, well-tuned burners, recipes tested for it. Now you decide to switch the fuel to **biogas from kitchen waste**, or **bio-ethanol**, or **hydrogen**. 

- Does the burner still give the same flame? → That's **combustion performance**.
- Does your biryani cook in the same time with the same gas bill? → That's **thermal efficiency**.
- Does the flame smell funny, soot up the vessel, or pop dangerously? → That's **combustion quality & emissions**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Every alternate fuel is essentially a *new personality* being installed into an engine designed for conventional fuel. "Performance" is the *report card* on how well this new personality fits the old job — efficiency, power, smoothness, cleanliness, and safety.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlight)

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of Diesel** | **~ 42.5 MJ/kg** |
| **Lower Heating Value of Petrol (Gasoline)** | **~ 44 MJ/kg** |
| **LHV of Biodiesel (Methyl Ester)** | **~ 37–40 MJ/kg** |
| **LHV of Ethanol** | **~ 26.9 MJ/kg** |
| **LHV of Methanol** | **~ 19.9 MJ/kg** |
| **LHV of Hydrogen** | **~ 120 MJ/kg** (highest mass-basis) |
| **LHV of CNG (Methane)** | **~ 50 MJ/kg** |
| **LHV of LPG (Propane)** | **~ 46 MJ/kg** |
| **LHV of Biogas (60% CH₄)** | **~ 22 MJ/m³** |
| **Stoichiometric A/F – Diesel** | **~ 14.5 : 1** |
| **Stoichiometric A/F – Petrol** | **~ 14.7 : 1** |
| **Stoichiometric A/F – Ethanol** | **~ 9.0 : 1** |
| **Stoichiometric A/F – Methanol** | **~ 6.43 : 1** |
| **Stoichiometric A/F – Hydrogen** | **~ 34.3 : 1** (very lean) |
| **Stoichiometric A/F – CNG (CH₄)** | **~ 17.2 : 1** |
| **Cetane Number (Diesel baseline)** | **45–55** |
| **Octane Number (Petrol baseline)** | **87–98 RON** |

> [!NOTE]
> These values are *benchmark reference constants* used in every KTU numerical problem. Memorizing them is non-negotiable for scoring 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Performance Evaluation

Performance assessment of any alternate fuel follows a **three-tier analytical hierarchy**:

### Tier 1 — Fuel-Property Driven Performance (Intrinsic)
This is governed by the inherent chemistry of the fuel:

- **Calorific Value (CV)** – energy released per unit mass/volume.
- **Cetane/Octane Number** – knock resistance, dictates compression ratio and ignition delay.
- **Latent Heat of Vaporization** – affects charge cooling, volumetric efficiency.
- **Oxygen Content (wt %)** – promotes in-cylinder oxidation, reduces soot.
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio** – decides how much air is needed for complete combustion.
- **Flammability Limits & Auto-Ignition Temperature** – safety and operability window.
- **Viscosity & Density** – atomization, pumping, injection quality.

### Tier 2 — Combustion Performance (In-Cylinder Behavior)
- **Ignition Delay (ID)** – time between injection start and combustion start.
- **Premixed vs. Diffusion Burn Fraction** – dictates peak pressure, rate of pressure rise.
- **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** – shape and peak magnitude.
- **Combustion Duration** – affects thermal efficiency.
- **Cycle-to-Cycle Variation (CCV)** – stability of operation.

### Tier 3 — Engine Output Performance (Measurable)
- Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE)
- Indicated Thermal Efficiency (ITE)
- Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)
- Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)
- Brake Specific Energy Consumption (BSEC)
- Volumetric Efficiency (η_vol)
- Power Output (BP, IP)
- Emissions (CO, HC, NOx, PM, CO₂)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Most Module-2 14-mark questions integrate Tier 1 + Tier 3, asking *"Compare the performance of diesel and biodiesel B20 blend, evaluating BTE, BSFC, smoke, and NOx."* So the formula sheet below is the heart of those answers.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Markdown Safe:** All vertical bars in equations use `\vert` or `\mid` — not raw `\|` — to preserve table integrity.

| # | Parameter | Formula (LaTeX) | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Indicated Power | $IP = \dfrac{p_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot K}{60}$ | kW | Power developed in cylinder |
| 2 | Brake Power | $BP = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$ | kW | Useful power at shaft |
| 3 | Friction Power | $FP = IP - BP$ | kW | Power lost to friction |
| 4 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} \times 100$ | % | Conversion of indicated to brake power |
| 5 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \times 100$ | % | Fraction of fuel energy → brake work |
| 6 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{IP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \times 100$ | % | Fraction of fuel energy → indicated work |
| 7 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{BP}$ | kg/kWh | Fuel needed per unit brake work |
| 8 | Brake Specific Energy Consumption | $BSEC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}{BP}$ | MJ/kWh | Energy spent per unit brake work |
| 9 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{a,actual}}{\dot{m}_{a,theoretical}} \times 100$ | % | Actual air inducted vs. theoretical |
| 10 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$ | dimensionless | Rich (>1) / Lean (<1) / Stoich (=1) |
| 11 | Air-Fuel Ratio (by mass) | $A/F = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$ | — | kg air per kg fuel |
| 12 | Energy Efficiency (Energy System) | $\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{E_{out,useful}}{E_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Solar / wind / fuel cell efficiency |
| 13 | Heat Balance (% of input) | $Q_{in} = Q_{work} + Q_{cool} + Q_{exh} + Q_{unaccounted}$ | % | Energy conservation check |
| 14 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_{comb} = \dfrac{Actual \ Energy \ Released}{Theoretical \ Energy \ Released} \times 100$ | % | Completeness of burning |
| 15 | Relative Efficiency (vs. baseline) | $\eta_{rel} = \dfrac{\eta_{fuel}}{\eta_{baseline}} \times 100$ | % | Performance vs. diesel/petrol |

**Where the symbols mean:**
- $p_{mi}$ = mean indicated pressure (bar), $L$ = stroke (m), $A$ = piston area (m²), $n$ = no. of cylinders, $K$ = 1 for 4-stroke / 2 for 2-stroke, $N$ = rpm, $T$ = torque (N·m), $\dot{m}_f$ = fuel mass flow rate (kg/s), $CV$ = calorific value (kJ/kg).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Why does KTU emphasise "Performance" so heavily?

- **Automotive Industry** – Deciding which biodiesel blend (B5, B20, B100) gives the best BTE/BSFC trade-off for OEM production.
- **Aviation Biofuels** – SAF (Sustainable Aviation Fuel) is performance-tested against Jet A-1 on BTE, density, and freezing point.
- **Power Generation** – Biogas engines, dual-fuel gensets evaluated on kWh output per m³ of biogas.
- **Fuel Cell Vehicles** – Hydrogen PEMFC stacks rated on **kW output per kg H₂** stored.
- **Policy & Standards** – India's **BS-VI emission norms** and **Ethanol Blending Programme (E20)** are justified through performance data only.

## 2.4 Performance Comparison Matrix — Alternate Fuels vs. Diesel (Board-Ready Snapshot)

| Fuel Type | BTE Trend vs. Diesel | BSFC Trend | Smoke/PM | NOx Trend | Key Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Biodiesel (B20)** | Slightly lower (1–3%) | Higher (since CV is lower) | ↓ Lower (oxygenated) | Slightly ↑ (higher temp) | Oxygen in ester + lower CV |
| **Ethanol (E10–E20)** | Comparable / slightly lower | Higher (mass basis) | ↓ Much lower | ↓ Lower (latent heat) | High latent heat cools charge |
| **Methanol (M15)** | Lower | Much higher (very low CV) | ↓ Near zero | ↓ Lower | Very low CV, high latent heat |
| **Hydrogen (H₂)** | Higher at lean mixes | Almost zero (CV mass basis) | Zero PM | ↑ Higher (NOx) but no soot | High flame speed, ultra-lean |
| **CNG** | Lower (gas displacement) | Higher (mass basis) | ↓ Very low | ↓ Lower (high octane) | Gaseous, displaces air |
| **LPG** | Comparable | Higher | ↓ Lower | ↓ Lower | Clean vaporization |
| **Biogas (60% CH₄)** | Lower (10–20%) | Much higher (low CV) | ↓ Low | ↑ Slightly | CO₂ dilutes combustion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Board Pitfall:** Many students write *"Biodiesel gives higher efficiency than diesel"* — this is **wrong**. Biodiesel is denser and has *lower CV*, so it *consumes more fuel per kWh* and gives *slightly lower BTE*. It wins on **emissions and lubricity**, not efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE) from First Principles

**Statement:** Prove that $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$.

By definition, **thermal efficiency** is the ratio of useful work output to the chemical energy input from fuel:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{\text{Energy converted to useful work}}{\text{Total chemical energy supplied by fuel}}$$

The energy input per second from fuel flow is the product of the mass flow rate and the calorific value:

$$\dot{E}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV$$

This is because $\dot{m}_f$ is in kg/s and $CV$ is in kJ/kg, giving energy rate in kJ/s = kW.

The useful work output per second for a rotating shaft is:

$$\dot{W}_{useful} = BP = \frac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000} \quad [\text{kW}]$$

Therefore, substituting both into the efficiency definition:

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$$

Expressed as a percentage (which is the KTU convention):

$$\eta_{bth} (\%)= \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \times 100$$

> **Logical Transition Note:** $BP$ is what the engine delivers to the load; $\dot{m}_f \cdot CV$ is what the fuel gives up chemically. Their ratio is *thermodynamic conversion efficiency* — a dimensionless number bounded above by the **Otto / Diesel / Dual cycle efficiency** for an ideal cycle.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — BSFC and its Relationship with BTE

**Statement:** Show that $BSFC = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{bth} \cdot CV}$ (in kg/kWh).

Start from the definition of BTE (in decimal form, not percent):

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{m}_f = \frac{BP}{\eta_{bth} \cdot CV}$$

Here $\dot{m}_f$ is fuel flow in **kg/s** and $BP$ is in **kW**.

Since $1 \text{ kWh} = 3600 \text{ kJ}$, multiplying numerator and denominator of $\dot{m}_f$ by 3600 converts kW-s to kJ basis:

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{BP \times 3600}{\eta_{bth} \cdot CV \times 3600} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \quad \text{[kg/s]}$$

By definition, BSFC is fuel consumed per unit brake work in kWh:

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600 \text{ [s/h]}}{BP \text{ [kW]}} \quad [\text{kg/kWh}]$$

Substituting $\dot{m}_f$:

$$BSFC = \frac{3600}{\eta_{bth} \cdot CV}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is THE most-tested formula in KTU Module-2 numericals.** If a question gives you BTE and CV of an alternate fuel, BSFC is one substitution away.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalence Ratio and Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio

**Statement:** For methanol (CH₃OH), derive the stoichiometric A/F ratio.

The complete combustion of methanol is:

$$CH_3OH + \frac{3}{2} O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2 H_2O$$

In mass terms:
- 1 mole CH₃OH = 12 + 4 + 16 = **32 g**
- 1.5 mole O₂ = 1.5 × 32 = **48 g**

Since air is 23.2% O₂ by mass, mass of air needed:

$$m_{air} = \frac{48}{0.232} = 206.9 \text{ g per mole methanol}$$

Therefore:

$$(A/F)_{stoich,\,methanol} = \frac{206.9}{32} = 6.46$$

Generalisation — for any fuel $C_xH_yO_z$, the **stoichiometric A/F ratio** is:

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{4.76 \times (x + y/4 - z/2) \times 32}{12x + y + 16z}$$

where 4.76 = inverse of 0.21 (mass of air per mass O₂), and 32 = O₂ molar mass.

> **Logic Note:** This single line is the seed for *any* KTU question asking to compute A/F of a biofuel from its molecular formula — extremely high-yield.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Heat Balance and Combustion Efficiency

The total chemical energy input from fuel must be conserved across all output channels:

$$Q_{in} = Q_{work} + Q_{cool} + Q_{exh} + Q_{unaccounted}$$

Where each term is expressed as a percentage of input:
- $Q_{work}$ = % of fuel energy converted to brake work (= BTE %)
- $Q_{cool}$ = % lost to engine coolant (typically 25–35%)
- $Q_{exh}$ = % lost in exhaust gases (typically 25–40%)
- $Q_{unaccounted}$ = radiation, unburnt HC (~ 5–10%)

**Combustion efficiency** $\eta_{comb}$ is defined as:

$$\eta_{comb} = \frac{Q_{work} + Q_{cool} + Q_{exh}}{Q_{in}} \times 100 = 100 - Q_{unaccounted}$$

This separates *thermodynamic* (cycle) losses from *chemical* (incomplete combustion) losses — a key KTU distinction.

## 3.5 KTU-Calibrated Numerical Worked Example (Solved)

> **KTU Typical 14-Mark Question Style**

**Q:** A single-cylinder 4-stroke diesel engine runs on **B20 biodiesel blend** at rated load. The following data are recorded:
- Brake Power $BP = 11 \text{ kW}$
- Fuel consumption = $4.5 \text{ kg/h}$
- Calorific value of B20 = $40.5 \text{ MJ/kg}$
- Indicated Power $IP = 12.5 \text{ kW}$
- Speed $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$

**Find:** (a) BTE, (b) BSFC, (c) ITE, (d) Mechanical Efficiency, (e) BSEC.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Compute fuel mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{4.5}{3600} = 0.00125 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step (b) — Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \times 100 = \frac{11}{0.00125 \times 40{,}500} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{11}{50.625} \times 100 = 21.73 \text{ \%}$$

**Step (c) — Brake Specific Fuel Consumption:**

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP} = \frac{4.5}{11} = 0.4091 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**Step (d) — Indicated Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{ith} = \frac{IP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \times 100 = \frac{12.5}{0.00125 \times 40{,}500} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{ith} = \frac{12.5}{50.625} \times 100 = 24.69 \text{ \%}$$

**Step (e) — Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP} \times 100 = \frac{11}{12.5} \times 100 = 88.0 \text{ \%}$$

**Step (f) — Brake Specific Energy Consumption:**

$$BSEC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV \times 3600}{BP} = \frac{0.00125 \times 40{,}500 \times 3600}{11}$$

$$BSEC = \frac{182{,}250}{11} = 16{,}568 \text{ kJ/kWh} = 16.57 \text{ MJ/kWh}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** A conventional diesel engine would show BTE ≈ 28–32% and BSFC ≈ 0.27–0.30 kg/kWh. Biodiesel B20 gives *slightly lower BTE* and *higher BSFC* — exactly as the theoretical prediction says. This validates the model.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Performance Evaluation Framework for Alternate Fuels

The diagram below maps how an alternate fuel's intrinsic properties cascade through combustion to engine output, the standard KTU Module-2 mental model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Property Input] --> B[Calorific Value]
    A --> C[Cetane or Octane Number]
    A --> D[Latent Heat of Vaporization]
    A --> E[Oxygen Content by mass]
    A --> F[Viscosity and Density]
    A --> G[Stoichiometric Air Fuel Ratio]
    A --> H[Auto Ignition Temperature]
    B --> I[In Cylinder Combustion Event]
    C --> I
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Heat Release Rate HRR]
    I --> K[Ignition Delay]
    I --> L[Peak Cylinder Pressure]
    I --> M[Cycle to Cycle Variation]
    J --> N[Engine Output Performance]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Brake Thermal Efficiency BTE]
    N --> P[Brake Specific Fuel Consumption BSFC]
    N --> Q[Brake Power BP and Torque]
    N --> R[Volumetric Efficiency]
    N --> S[Emissions CO HC NOx PM]
    O --> T[Final Performance Report]
    P --> T
    Q --> T
    R --> T
    S --> T
    T --> U{KTU 14 mark verdict on fuel suitability}
```

## 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram — BTE, BSFC, and Emission Trends Across Alternate Fuels

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Baseline
        D[Diesel Reference]
    end
    subgraph Liquid Biofuels
        B1[Biodiesel B20]
        B2[Ethanol E20]
        B3[Methanol M15]
    end
    subgraph Gaseous Fuels
        G1[CNG]
        G2[LPG]
        G3[Biogas]
    end
    subgraph Hydrogen Family
        H1[Hydrogen ICE]
        H2[Hydrogen Fuel Cell]
    end
    D --> R1[BTE slightly higher]
    D --> R2[BSFC lower]
    D --> R3[PM and NOx higher]
    B1 --> S1[BTE marginally lower]
    B1 --> S2[BSFC higher]
    B1 --> S3[PM lower NOx slightly up]
    B2 --> T1[BTE comparable]
    B2 --> T2[BSFC higher]
    B2 --> T3[PM and NOx lower]
    B3 --> U1[BTE lower]
    B3 --> U2[BSFC much higher]
    B3 --> U3[PM near zero]
    G1 --> V1[BTE lower due to displacement]
    G1 --> V2[BSFC high mass basis]
    G1 --> V3[PM near zero NOx lower]
    G2 --> W1[BTE comparable]
    G2 --> W2[BSFC higher]
    G2 --> W3[PM lower]
    G3 --> X1[BTE low due to CO2]
    G3 --> X2[BSFC highest]
    G3 --> X3[PM very low]
    H1 --> Y1[BTE higher at lean mix]
    H1 --> Y2[BSFC very low mass basis]
    H1 --> Y3[Zero PM but NOx high]
    H2 --> Z1[Electrical efficiency 50 to 60 percent]
    H2 --> Z2[No combustion losses]
    H2 --> Z3[Zero all emissions]
```

## 4.3 Heat Balance Sankey-Style Flow (Generic Alternate Fuel Engine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Chemical Energy 100 percent] --> B[Converted to Brake Work 22 to 32 percent]
    A --> C[Cooling Water Loss 25 to 35 percent]
    A --> D[Exhaust Gas Loss 25 to 40 percent]
    A --> E[Unaccounted Loss 5 to 10 percent]
    B --> F[Useful Shaft Output]
    C --> G[Radiator and Atmosphere]
    D --> H[Tailpipe and Turbocharger]
    E --> I[Radiation plus Unburnt HC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Sankey view is what students must reproduce in the *heat balance* sub-question of a typical 14-mark problem. The percentage ranges vary slightly between petrol, diesel, and alternate fuel engines — biodiesel, for instance, gives a *higher* exhaust loss because of lower CV and higher fuel flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE). State its typical value for a biodiesel-fuelled diesel engine.**

**Model Answer:**
Brake Thermal Efficiency is the ratio of brake power output of an engine to the chemical energy input from the fuel, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \times 100 \text{ \%}$$

For a biodiesel-fuelled diesel engine, BTE typically lies in the range **25–30 %**, which is **1–3 % lower** than conventional diesel due to biodiesel's lower calorific value and higher density.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for typical value & reason)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three performance advantages and three disadvantages of hydrogen as an alternate fuel for IC engines.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Highest mass-basis calorific value** (~ 120 MJ/kg) — much greater than any hydrocarbon.
2. **Wide flammability range** and **high octane rating** allow very lean operation, raising thermal efficiency.
3. **Zero carbon content** → no CO, CO₂, HC, or particulate emissions in the engine exhaust.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Very low volumetric energy density** — needs bulky high-pressure (350–700 bar) or cryogenic storage.
2. **High NOx formation** due to elevated in-cylinder temperatures from rapid flame speed.
3. **Embrittlement of metals** and **pre-ignition / backfire** issues demand engine modifications.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for advantages, 1.5 for disadvantages)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

> A four-stroke single-cylinder diesel engine is fuelled with **B20 (20% biodiesel + 80% diesel) blend** and the following data is recorded at full load:
> - Brake Power $BP = 22 \text{ kW}$
> - Fuel consumption = $8.4 \text{ kg/h}$
> - Calorific Value of B20 = $40.5 \text{ MJ/kg}$
> - Indicated Power $IP = 25 \text{ kW}$
> - Speed = $1500 \text{ rpm}$
> - Air consumption = $130 \text{ kg/h}$
>
> **(a)** Compute the **Brake Thermal Efficiency**, **BSFC**, **Indicated Thermal Efficiency**, **Mechanical Efficiency**, and **BSEC** of the engine. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **performance comparison between B20 and neat diesel** in terms of BTE, BSFC, smoke emissions, and NOx. Justify the differences using the fuel properties. (7 marks)

### **Model Solution — Part A (a)**

**Step 1:** Fuel mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{8.4}{3600} = 0.002333 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2:** Brake Thermal Efficiency:

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{22}{0.002333 \times 40{,}500} \times 100 = \frac{22}{94.5} \times 100 = 23.28 \text{ \%}$$

**Step 3:** BSFC:

$$BSFC = \frac{8.4}{22} = 0.3818 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**Step 4:** Indicated Thermal Efficiency:

$$\eta_{ith} = \frac{25}{0.002333 \times 40{,}500} \times 100 = \frac{25}{94.5} \times 100 = 26.46 \text{ \%}$$

**Step 5:** Mechanical Efficiency:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{22}{25} \times 100 = 88.0 \text{ \%}$$

**Step 6:** BSEC:

$$BSEC = BSFC \times CV = 0.3818 \times 40.5 = 15.46 \text{ MJ/kWh}$$

### **Model Solution — Part A (b)** — Performance Comparison

| Parameter | Diesel (Neat) | B20 Blend | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| BTE | 26–32% | 23–30% (1–3% lower) | B20 has lower CV (~40.5 vs 42.5 MJ/kg); more fuel burnt for same energy |
| BSFC | 0.27–0.30 kg/kWh | 0.34–0.40 kg/kWh | Higher density + lower CV → more mass per kWh |
| Smoke / PM | High (esp. high load) | ↓ 20–30% lower | Biodiesel carries **bound oxygen (~10–11 wt %)** which promotes soot oxidation |
| NOx | Moderate | Slightly ↑ 2–5% | Higher in-cylinder temperature due to early ignition + oxygenated combustion |
| BSEC | ~13.5 MJ/kWh | ~15.4 MJ/kWh | Consequence of lower CV; bioenergy penalty |

**Conclusion:** B20 trades **3% efficiency loss** for **substantial emission & lubricity gains** — a worthwhile trade-off for urban transport applications.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

> A spark-ignition engine is fuelled with **hydrogen** and the following data is observed at one operating point:
> - Brake Power $BP = 15 \text{ kW}$
> - Fuel consumption = $0.7 \text{ kg/h}$
> - Calorific Value of H₂ = $120 \text{ MJ/kg}$
> - Air-Fuel ratio (actual) = $50 : 1$ (by mass)
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **Brake Thermal Efficiency**, **BSFC**, and **BSEC** for hydrogen operation. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** With the help of the **equivalence ratio** concept, explain why hydrogen can be operated at very lean mixtures and how this influences BTE and NOx. (7 marks)

### **Model Solution — Part B (a)**

**Step 1:** Fuel mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{0.7}{3600} = 1.944 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2:** BTE:

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{15}{1.944 \times 10^{-4} \times 120{,}000} \times 100 = \frac{15}{23.33} \times 100 = 64.29 \text{ \%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This unusually high BTE is on **mass basis**. On **volumetric / energy basis** hydrogen's BTE is comparable to petrol because of low density.

**Step 3:** BSFC:

$$BSFC = \frac{0.7}{15} = 0.0467 \text{ kg/kWh} = 46.7 \text{ g/kWh}$$

For petrol, BSFC ≈ 250–300 g/kWh — so **H₂ uses ~5× less mass** but occupies much more volume.

**Step 4:** BSEC:

$$BSEC = 0.0467 \times 120 = 5.6 \text{ MJ/kWh}$$

### **Model Solution — Part B (b)** — Equivalence Ratio & Lean-Burn Performance

The **equivalence ratio** $\phi$ is:

$$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$$

For hydrogen, $(A/F)_{stoich} = 34.3 : 1$. Given $(A/F)_{actual} = 50 : 1$:

$$\phi = \frac{34.3}{50} = 0.686$$

Since $\phi < 1$, the mixture is **lean**.

**Effect on BTE (↑ higher):**
- Lean mixtures have more excess air → specific heat capacity of the charge is higher → **lower peak temperature** but **higher pressure during expansion**.
- Lower heat loss to walls → more work extracted → **BTE rises by 5–15%** compared to stoichiometric.

**Effect on NOx (↓ lower):**
- NOx formation follows the **Zeldovich mechanism** and is exponentially sensitive to peak temperature.
- Lean burn + high latent heat of H₂ → lower peak T → **NOx drops 50–80%** relative to stoichiometric petrol.

**Trade-off:** Very lean mixtures can cause **combustion instability** and **cycle-to-cycle variation** — there is a practical lean limit around $\phi \approx 0.4$ for hydrogen ICEs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kg/h → kg/s** — This is the *single most common 1-mark deduction* in BTE/BSFC numericals. Always show the unit conversion line explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing BSFC with BSEC** — BSFC is in kg/kWh; BSEC is in MJ/kWh. They are *not* the same. BSEC = BSFC × CV.
> 3. **Writing "Biodiesel has higher efficiency"** — Strictly false. Biodiesel has *lower* CV → *lower* BTE. It is *environmentally* advantageous, not *thermodynamically*.
> 4. **Mixing up Octane and Cetane numbers** — Hydrogen has *very high* octane (RON > 130) but *no defined cetane* because it's a SI fuel. Don't write "hydrogen cetane number is high."
> 5. **Ignoring the equivalence ratio sign** — Many students write $\phi > 1$ means lean. **It's the opposite**: $\phi > 1$ → rich; $\phi < 1$ → lean.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Performance** = quantitative assessment of an alternate fuel's energy release, work output, and emissions in an engine or energy system.
- **BTE** = (BP / (m_f × CV)) × 100; **lower CV fuel → lower BTE** for the same BP.
- **BSFC** = m_f / BP; **BSEC** = BSFC × CV; the two differ by a factor of CV.
- **Mechanical Efficiency** = BP / IP × 100; losses = friction, pumping, accessories.
- **Volumetric Efficiency** drops when gaseous fuels (CNG, H₂) displace in-cylinder air.
- **Biodiesel B20**: lower BTE (~2–3% drop) but ↓ smoke, ↓ HC, ↑ lubricity, slight ↑ NOx.
- **Ethanol E20**: comparable BTE, ↓ smoke, ↓ NOx (due to high latent heat cooling the charge).
- **Methanol M15**: significantly lower BTE, near-zero PM, ⚠ corrosive to metals and elastomers.
- **Hydrogen**: highest mass-CV, very high octane, can run ultra-lean (φ ≈ 0.4–0.6), zero PM/CO/CO₂/HC, but ↑ NOx and storage challenges.
- **CNG**: high octane, gaseous, ↓ PM and NOx, **power loss 10–15%** due to air displacement.
- **LPG**: comparable BTE to petrol, cleaner, easy vaporization.
- **Biogas**: CV depends on CH₄ content; CO₂ dilution → low BTE, requires de-CO₂ or enrichment.
- **Equivalence ratio φ = (A/F)_stoich / (A/F)_actual** — φ < 1 = lean, φ > 1 = rich.
- **Lean burn ⇒ ↑ BTE, ↓ NOx**, *up to a practical limit* dictated by combustion stability.
- **Heat balance** = $Q_{work} + Q_{cool} + Q_{exh} + Q_{unaccounted} = 100\%$ of fuel energy.
- **Combustion efficiency** is $100\% - Q_{unaccounted}$ — a measure of *chemical* (not thermodynamic) completeness.
- Always **convert kg/h → kg/s** by dividing by 3600 before substituting into BTE/BSFC formulas.
- **A/F stoichiometric values to remember**: Diesel 14.5, Petrol 14.7, CNG 17.2, Ethanol 9.0, Methanol 6.43, Hydrogen 34.3.
- **Cetane** rules diesel engines (CI); **Octane** rules petrol engines (SI). Mis-application = knock or hard starting.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Board Strategy:** In a 14-mark question, allocate **~3 marks for the formula**, **~4 marks for substitution & arithmetic**, **~4 marks for the comparative discussion** (with at least 2 quantitative points), and **~3 marks for the concluding verdict**. This 3-4-4-3 split mirrors KTU's actual valuation pattern.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emission and Desired Properties of Alternate Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Emission and Desired Properties of Alternate Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Alternate Fuels** are non-conventional, non-fossil-derived (or fossil-derived but cleaner-burning) energy carriers used in internal combustion engines (SI & CI), gas turbines, and fuel cells, which either (i) reduce dependence on crude petroleum, (ii) lower regulated tail-pipe emissions (CO, HC, NOx, PM, SOx), or (iii) offer superior combustion properties such as higher octane/cetane number and wider flammability limits.

**Emission Characteristics of Alternate Fuels** refer to the qualitative and quantitative profile of regulated and unregulated exhaust species produced when a given alternate fuel is burned in an engine, including:

- **CO (Carbon Monoxide)** – product of incomplete combustion
- **CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide)** – greenhouse gas, product of complete combustion
- **HC (Unburnt Hydrocarbons)** – result of flame quenching and crevice effects
- **NOx (Oxides of Nitrogen)** – thermal, prompt, and fuel-bound
- **PM (Particulate Matter)** – soot and soluble organic fraction
- **SOx (Oxides of Sulphur)** – from sulphur content of fuel
- **Aldehydes, PAHs, Smoke** – unregulated but toxic emissions

**Desired Properties** are the physical, chemical, and thermodynamic attributes an ideal alternate fuel must possess to deliver efficient, clean, and durable engine performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 2 emphasizes that the choice of an alternate fuel is dictated by a *trade-off matrix* among availability, energy density, toxicity, and emission profile — NOT by any single parameter.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of conventional diesel/petrol as a **"fast food burger"** — readily available, high in calories (energy), but loaded with hidden "bad stuff" (smoke, NOx, sulphur, particulates) that harms the "body" (engine + environment) over time.

An **alternate fuel** is like a **balanced home-cooked meal**:
- **CNG/Hydrogen** → low-fat, clean-burning (analogous to grilled chicken)
- **Ethanol/Methanol** → oxygenated "smoothie" that helps complete combustion
- **Biodiesel** → renewable "organic" option that recycles carbon
- **LPG** → the "lite" version of gaseous fuels

Just as a nutritionist evaluates food on **calories, fat content, digestibility, and sustainability**, an automotive engineer evaluates alternate fuels on **calorific value, knock resistance, volatility, flammability limits, storage safety, and emission profile**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Truth:** No single alternate fuel is perfect. Each presents a *characteristic emission fingerprint* (e.g., alcohols emit aldehydes; biodiesel emits slightly higher NOx; hydrogen eliminates CO/HC/CO₂ but raises NOx).

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (Industry Standards)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Calorific Value | CV | kJ/kg or MJ/kg | Energy released per unit fuel |
| Octane Number | ON | — | Knock resistance (SI engines) |
| Cetane Number | CN | — | Ignition quality (CI engines) |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | AIT | °C / K | Self-ignition threshold |
| Stoichiometric AFR | (A/F)_stoich | — | Theoretical air required |
| Flame Speed | S_L | m/s | Laminar burning velocity |
| Flammability Limits | LFL/UFL | % vol | Lean/rich combustion boundaries |
| Lower Heating Value | LHV | kJ/kg | Useful energy at exhaust |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Constants Used in this Module:**
> - Molecular weight of air = **28.97 kg/kmol**
> - Molecular weight of O₂ = **32 kg/kmol**
> - N₂/O₂ volume ratio in air = **3.76**
> - Molar volume at STP = **22.414 m³/kmol**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Desired Properties of Alternate Fuels — Detailed Breakdown

A KTU-graded answer must classify these properties into **Engine Performance**, **Combustion Quality**, **Storage/Handling**, and **Emission Control** categories.

### A. Engine Performance Properties

1. **High Calorific Value (CV):** Maximizes energy per unit mass/volume. Hydrogen has the highest mass-based CV (≈ **142 MJ/kg**), but very low volumetric density.
2. **High Volumetric Energy Density:** Critical for vehicle range. LPG (≈ 26 MJ/L) and CNG (≈ 9 MJ/L at 200 bar) outperform gaseous hydrogen at low pressure.
3. **Proper Viscosity:** Must atomize/spray well. Biodiesel (≈ 4–6 cSt) is acceptable; high-viscosity raw vegetable oils (≈ 30–40 cSt) are not.

### B. Combustion Quality Properties

1. **Appropriate Octane Number (SI engines):** Prevents knock. Hydrogen ON ≈ **130+**, Methanol ≈ 109, Ethanol ≈ 108, CNG ≈ 120, LPG ≈ 96–100.
2. **Appropriate Cetane Number (CI engines):** For compression-ignition fuels. Biodiesel ≈ 50–60 (similar to diesel), methanol and ethanol are very poor (CN < 15 → need ignition improvers or fumigation).
3. **High Flame Speed:** Faster S_L → better cyclic variability. Hydrogen ≈ 2.5–3 m/s; gasoline ≈ 0.4 m/s.
4. **Wide Flammability Limits:** Allows lean-burn operation → better thermal efficiency. Hydrogen: 4–75 % vol in air (widest).
5. **Low Auto-Ignition Temperature:** For CI fuels only.

### C. Storage / Handling / Safety Properties

1. **Non-corrosive to metals and elastomers**
2. **Low toxicity and non-carcinogenic**
3. **Stable during storage** (no gum formation, phase separation)
4. **Easy and safe transportation**

### D. Emission / Environmental Properties

1. **Low carbon-to-hydrogen ratio** (C/H ratio) → less CO₂ per unit energy
2. **Zero or low sulphur content** → eliminates SOx
3. **Aromatic-free** → eliminates toxic PAHs
4. **Oxygenated** (alcohols) → reduces CO and PM
5. **Renewable / Carbon-neutral** (biofuels, H₂ from electrolysis)

---

## 2.2 Emission Formation Mechanisms (The "WHY" behind each pollutant)

### 2.2.1 CO (Carbon Monoxide) Formation
- **Why:** Incomplete oxidation of C → CO due to *local fuel-rich pockets* or *insufficient mixing time*.
- **Alternate fuel impact:** Gaseous fuels (CNG, H₂) mix uniformly with air → **CO drops by 60–90 %** vs gasoline.
- Alcohols contain oxygen → complete combustion → **CO reduced by 30–50 %**.

### 2.2.2 NOx Formation
Three mechanisms (Extended Zeldovich):
- **Thermal NOx:** Zeldovich mechanism, ∝ exp(-E/RT). Peak at high T and excess O₂.
- **Prompt NOx:** Fenimore mechanism (CH + N₂ → HCN + N).
- **Fuel-bound NOx:** From nitrogen in fuel (not significant for most alternate fuels).
- **Alternate fuel impact:**
  - Hydrogen → highest flame T (≈ 2400 K) → **highest NOx**
  - CNG → lean burn possible → moderate NOx
  - Biodiesel → slightly higher NOx than diesel due to higher in-cylinder T (≈ 5–15 %)

### 2.2.3 HC (Unburnt Hydrocarbons)
- **Why:** Crevice volumes, wall quenching, oil absorption, fuel impingement.
- **Alternate fuel impact:** Gaseous fuels (CNG, H₂) → **near-zero HC**.
- Methanol/ethanol → unburnt alcohol emissions + aldehydes.

### 2.2.4 Particulate Matter (PM)
- **Why:** Incomplete combustion of long-chain hydrocarbons in fuel-rich zones.
- **Alternate fuel impact:**
  - CNG, H₂, LPG → **virtually zero PM**
  - Biodiesel → **40–60 % lower PM** (oxygenated combustion)
  - Methanol/ethanol → negligible PM in SI engines

### 2.2.5 CO₂ (Greenhouse Gas)
- **Why:** Complete combustion stoichiometry.
- **Alternate fuel impact:**
  - CNG: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O → low C/H → **20–25 % less CO₂ per km**
  - Biofuels: CO₂ absorbed during growth = **carbon-neutral**
  - Hydrogen: **zero CO₂ at tailpipe**

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\\vert` notation instead of `\\pipe` symbol** to avoid markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{air} = AFR \times \dot{m}_{fuel}$ | Air mass flow from fuel mass flow |
| 2 | $\dot{n}_{O_2} = x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}$ | O₂ moles per mole of C_xH_yO_z fuel |
| 3 | $(A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{4.76 \times (32x + 4y + 16z)}{12x + y + 16z}$ | Stoichiometric AFR for C_xH_yO_z |
| 4 | $LHV = HHV - 2.442 \times m_{H_2} \times \dfrac{9}{100}$ | Lower heating value from hydrogen mass % |
| 5 | $EI_{CO_2} = \dfrac{44 \cdot x}{12x + y}$ kg CO₂ per kg fuel | CO₂ emission index (hydrocarbons) |
| 6 | $EI_{CO_2,\,stoich} = \dfrac{11 \cdot x \cdot MW_{fuel}}{12x + y}$ in kg/kg | Stoichiometric CO₂ index |
