| 7 | $NO_x \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT_{flame}}\right)$ | Thermal NOx (Zeldovich) |
| 8 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Otto cycle efficiency |
| 9 | $\rho_{CNG} = \dfrac{P \cdot MW}{Z \cdot R_u \cdot T}$ | CNG density at high pressure (real gas, Z = compressibility) |
| 10 | $\dot{V}_{air} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\rho_{air}}$ | Volumetric air flow for gaseous fuel mixing |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These properties and emission metrics directly feed into:
1. **Engine calibration maps** (ECU injection timing, AFR targets)
2. **Exhaust after-treatment design** (3-way catalyst sizing for alcohol cars, SCR for biodiesel NOx)
3. **Emission certification** (BIS, Euro VI/BS-VI norms in India)
4. **Vehicle range estimation** (CV × tank capacity × η_th)
5. **Policy & taxation** (carbon tax, renewable fuel mandates)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)

### Step 1: Write balanced combustion equation

For ethanol C₂H₅OH (which can be written as C₂H₆O):

$$C_2H_6O + aO_2 + 3.76aN_2 \rightarrow bCO_2 + cH_2O + 3.76aN_2$$

### Step 2: Apply atom balance
- Carbon: $2 = b \Rightarrow b = 2$
- Hydrogen: $6 = 2c \Rightarrow c = 3$
- Oxygen: $1 + 2a = 2b + c \Rightarrow 1 + 2a = 2(2) + 3 = 7 \Rightarrow 2a = 6 \Rightarrow a = 3$

### Step 3: Write balanced equation

$$C_2H_6O + 3O_2 + 11.28\,N_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2 + 3H_2O + 11.28\,N_2$$

### Step 4: Compute molecular weights
- $MW_{C_2H_6O} = 2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = 46$ kg/kmol
- $MW_{3O_2 + 11.28N_2} = 3(32) + 11.28(28) = 96 + 315.84 = 411.84$ kg of air

### Step 5: Compute stoichiometric AFR

$$(A/F)_{stoich,\,EtOH} = \frac{411.84}{46} = 8.95 \approx 9.0$$

**Interpretation:** Ethanol needs ~9 kg air per kg fuel (much lower than gasoline's 14.7) because the oxygen atom in ethanol's molecular structure pre-supplies part of the oxidizer.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — CO₂ Emission Index for CNG (Methane, CH₄)

### Step 1: Stoichiometric reaction

$$CH_4 + 2O_2 + 7.52\,N_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O + 7.52\,N_2$$

### Step 2: CO₂ emitted per kg CH₄ burned

$$EI_{CO_2} = \frac{MW_{CO_2}}{MW_{CH_4}} = \frac{44}{16} = 2.75 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg CH}_4$$

### Step 3: Compare with gasoline (iso-octane, C₈H₁₈, MW = 114)
$$EI_{CO_2,\,C_8H_{18}} = \frac{8 \times 44}{114} = \frac{352}{114} = 3.09 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

### Step 4: Energy-normalized CO₂ (per MJ of LHV)
- $LHV_{CH_4} \approx 50$ MJ/kg → CO₂/MJ = $2.75/50 = 0.055$ kg/MJ
- $LHV_{C_8H_{18}} \approx 44$ MJ/kg → CO₂/MJ = $3.09/44 = 0.070$ kg/MJ

$$\Rightarrow \text{CNG emits } \frac{0.055}{0.070} = 78.6\% \text{ of gasoline's CO}_2 \text{ per MJ}$$

**≈ 21 % lower CO₂ per unit energy** → confirms CNG's environmental advantage.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Hydrogen's Zero-Carbon Emission Proof

$$2H_2 + O_2 + 3.76\,N_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O + 3.76\,N_2$$

- CO formed = 0 (no carbon in fuel)
- HC formed = 0 (no carbon in fuel)
- CO₂ formed = 0 (no carbon in fuel)
- NOx: only emitted species (from thermal mechanism due to high flame T)

**Hydrogen's only regulated emission** is NOx, controlled by lean burn and EGR.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Emission Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Fuel | CO (%) | HC (%) | NOx (%) | PM (%) | CO₂ (%) | Aldehydes | SOx |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gasoline (baseline)** | 100 | 100 | 100 | 100 | 100 | Low | Med |
| **CNG** | 10–30 | 10–30 | 50–80 | <5 | 75–80 | Low | Negligible |
| **LPG** | 20–50 | 30–50 | 80–110 | <5 | 85–90 | Low | Negligible |
| **Methanol** | 30–60 | 50–100 | 50–90 | <10 | 80 | **High (formaldehyde)** | Negligible |
| **Ethanol** | 30–60 | 50–100 | 50–90 | <10 | 80 | **High (acetaldehyde)** | Negligible |
| **Biodiesel** | 70–90 | 40–60 | 105–115 | 40–60 | 75–90 (closed-loop) | Low | Negligible |
| **Hydrogen (SI)** | 0 | 0 | 100–150 (T-dep.) | 0 | **0** | 0 | 0 |
| **Hydrogen (Fuel Cell)** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **0** | 0 | 0 |

*All values are **% of baseline gasoline emissions** under comparable engine operating conditions.*

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation 4 — Energy Density Trade-off for Hydrogen

Mass energy density:
$$E_{mass} = \frac{LHV}{\rho} = \frac{142 \text{ MJ/kg}}{71 \text{ kg/m}^3 \text{ (liquid H}_2\text{)}} = 10.1 \text{ MJ/L}$$

Compare to gasoline:
$$E_{mass,\,gasoline} = \frac{44 \text{ MJ/kg}}{750 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 33 \text{ MJ/L}$$

$$\Rightarrow \text{Hydrogen volumetric energy density} = \frac{10.1}{33} \approx 31\% \text{ of gasoline}$$

**Why this matters for KTU:** This is *the* fundamental challenge for H₂ vehicles — addressed by **700 bar compressed tanks** or **cryogenic liquid storage**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Emission Formation Pathways in an IC Engine with Alternate Fuels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alternate Fuel Injection] --> B[In-Cylinder Mixing]
    B --> C{Combustion Quality}
    C --> D[Flame Temperature Field]
    C --> E[Local Equivalence Ratio phi]
    C --> F[Residence Time]
    D --> G1[Thermal NOx]
    E --> G2[CO if phi greater than 1]
    E --> G3[PM and Soot if phi greater than 1]
    E --> G4[HC from Crevices]
    F --> G5[Aldehydes from Alcohols]
    G1 --> H[Tailpipe Exhaust]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    H --> I[After Treatment Catalyst]
    I --> J[Regulated Emissions to Atmosphere]
    H2[Fuel Sulphur] --> H3[SOx Emission]
    H2 --> H
    H4[Incomplete C Burn] --> G2
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Desired Properties Decision Matrix for Fuel Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Candidate Alternate Fuel] --> B1[Engine Performance Check]
    A --> B2[Combustion Quality Check]
    A --> B3[Storage and Safety Check]
    A --> B4[Emission Profile Check]
    B1 --> C1{High CV and Volumetric Density}
    B2 --> C2{Proper ON or CN and Wide Flammability Limits}
    B3 --> C3{Non corrosive and Safe Handling}
    B4 --> C4{Low CO, NOx, PM, Aldehydes}
    C1 --> D[Pass and Proceed]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Recommended for Engine Application]
    F1[Reject if CV is too Low e.g. Raw Methanol Fumigation] --> X[Not Suitable]
    F2[Reject if High Aldehyde Emission without Catalyst] --> X
    F3[Reject if High NOx cannot be Controlled] --> X
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Emission Profile Comparison Across Fuels

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Regulated_Pollutants
        P1[CO]
        P2[HC]
        P3[NOx]
        P4[PM]
        P5[CO2]
    end
    subgraph Conventional_Fuels
        G1[Gasoline]
        D1[Diesel]
    end
    subgraph Alternate_Fuels
        AL[Alcohols]
        BG[CNG and LPG]
        BD[Biodiesel]
        HY[Hydrogen]
    end
    G1 --> P1
    G1 --> P2
    G1 --> P3
    G1 --> P4
    G1 --> P5
    D1 --> P1
    D1 --> P2
    D1 --> P3
    D1 --> P4
    D1 --> P5
    AL --> P1
    AL --> P2
    AL --> P3
    AL --> P4
    AL --> P5
    BG --> P1
    BG --> P2
    BG --> P3
    BG --> P4
    BG --> P5
    BD --> P1
    BD --> P2
    BD --> P3
    BD --> P4
    BD --> P5
    HY --> P1
    HY --> P2
    HY --> P3
    HY --> P4
    HY --> P5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List and briefly explain any three desired properties of alternate fuels for SI engines.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **High Octane Number:** Alternate fuels for SI engines must resist knock. Hydrogen (ON > 130), methanol (ON ≈ 109), ethanol (ON ≈ 108), and CNG (ON ≈ 120) all have ON greater than gasoline (≈ 91–95), enabling higher compression ratios and better thermal efficiency.
2. **Wide Flammability Limits:** Hydrogen's range (4–75 % vol in air) permits ultra-lean burn operation, improving fuel economy and reducing NOx at moderate loads.
3. **High Flame Speed:** Hydrogen has laminar burning velocity 6–8× higher than gasoline, leading to faster, more complete combustion and reduced cyclic variability.

*(Any 3 properties with one-line justification = full 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Why are aldehyde emissions significant for alcohol-fuelled engines?**

**Model Answer:**

Aldehydes (formaldehyde from methanol, acetaldehyde from ethanol) are produced during the **partial oxidation** of alcohol molecules at lower in-cylinder temperatures, especially during cold-start and idle conditions. They are **photochemically reactive** (contribute to ground-level ozone/smog), **carcinogenic**, and have **pungent odor**. Unlike conventional fuels, alcohol combustion pathways pass through an aldehyde intermediate (R-CH₂OH → R-CHO → R-COOH), necessitating a properly designed oxidation catalyst in the exhaust system.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set A]`

#### (a) Discuss the emission characteristics of hydrogen as an alternate fuel. Compare with CNG and gasoline. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Pollutant | Hydrogen | CNG | Gasoline |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO | 0 | Very Low | Moderate |
| HC | 0 | Very Low | High |
| NOx | **High (lean burn can reduce)** | Low–Medium | Medium |
| CO₂ | 0 | Low | High |
| PM | 0 | 0 | Low–High |
| SOx | 0 | 0 | Trace |

**Key Points (each ≈ 1 mark):**

1. **Zero carbon-based emissions:** Since H₂ has no carbon, all C-containing species (CO, HC, CO₂, PM) = 0 at tailpipe.
2. **NOx is the only regulated concern:** Hydrogen's adiabatic flame temperature is highest (~2400 K in air), increasing thermal NOx via the Zeldovich mechanism.
3. **Mitigation strategies:** Lean burn operation, EGR, water injection, and reduced combustion duration lower NOx to acceptable limits.
4. **Unregulated emissions:** Minor H₂O and trace OH radicals — neither toxic nor regulated.
5. **NOx comparison with CNG:** CNG runs richer, lower flame T, less NOx; H₂ lean burn gives comparable NOx to gasoline.
6. **CO₂ comparison:** CNG emits ~75 % of gasoline CO₂ per MJ; H₂ emits 0.
7. **Conclusion:** H₂ is the cleanest fuel thermodynamically but requires sophisticated engine management to control NOx.

---

#### (b) Explain the desired properties of biodiesel as an alternate fuel for CI engines. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

1. **High Cetane Number (50–60):** Comparable to diesel, ensuring short ignition delay and smooth CI combustion.
2. **Oxygenated Structure (~11 wt% O):** Promotes complete combustion → lower CO, HC, PM.
3. **Renewable & Biodegradable:** Carbon-neutral life cycle; non-toxic; degrades rapidly.
4. **High Lubricity:** Better than low-sulphur ULSD, reducing fuel pump/injector wear.
5. **Near-zero Aromatics and Sulphur:** Eliminates SOx and PAH emissions.
6. **High Flash Point (>130 °C):** Safer storage and transport.
7. **Limitations:** Higher viscosity → requires heating or blending (B20); slightly higher NOx due to elevated in-cylinder temperature; hygroscopic → storage care needed. **(Drawback earns 1 mark deduction if missed.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Set B]`

#### (a) Explain the desired properties of alternate fuels for SI engines with suitable examples. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

1. **High Octane Number:** Essential to prevent knock. CNG (~120), methanol (109), ethanol (108), hydrogen (>130).
2. **High Latent Heat of Vaporization:** Alcohols (methanol ~1100 kJ/kg) reduce charge temperature → higher volumetric efficiency and knock resistance.
3. **Wide Flammability Limits:** Hydrogen (4–75 %), methane (5–15 %), ethanol (3.3–19 %).
4. **High Flame Speed:** Hydrogen ~3 m/s; methane ~0.4 m/s → faster combustion.
5. **Low Carbon Content / Carbon-Hydrogen Ratio:** Reduces CO₂ per unit energy.
6. **Non-corrosive / Compatible with Lubricants:** Methanol/ethanol can degrade certain elastomers → material upgrades needed.
7. **Clean Burning (Low Sulphur, Aromatics):** All listed gaseous and alcohol fuels satisfy this.

---

#### (b) Compare the NOx emission behaviour of various alternate fuels in IC engines. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Fuel | NOx Trend vs Gasoline | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Hydrogen | **↑ 10–50 %** | High flame T (~2400 K), Zeldovich |
| CNG | **↓ 20–50 %** | Lean burn, lower flame T |
| LPG | **Comparable / slight ↓** | Similar flame T to gasoline |
| Methanol/ethanol | **↓ 10–40 %** | High latent heat cools charge |
| Biodiesel | **↑ 5–15 %** | Higher in-cylinder T from O₂ |

**Key insights (each ≈ 1 mark):**
1. **Trade-off:** Oxygenated fuels (alcohols, biodiesel) cut PM/CO but raise or maintain NOx.
2. **Lean burn** reduces NOx for gaseous fuels by lowering peak T.
3. **EGR** is universally required to meet BS-VI NOx norms with all alternate fuels.
4. **Hydrogen** is uniquely challenging despite zero CO₂.
5. **After-treatment:** LNT (Lean NOx Trap) or SCR essential for biodiesel and H₂ engines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't write "alternate fuels are pollution-free"** — every alternate fuel has at least one regulated emission issue (e.g., H₂→NOx, biodiesel→NOx, alcohols→aldehydes).
> 2. **Always normalize emissions** to either per MJ of energy OR per km of driving — KTU examiners explicitly look for "energy-equivalent" comparison, not bare "kg/kg" comparison.
> 3. **Octane vs Cetane confusion:** Don't assign CN to gasoline or ON to diesel in long answers — guaranteed 1–2 mark penalty.
> 4. **Stoichiometric AFR must include N₂:** A balanced equation without the 3.76 × N₂ term loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Biodiesel emission trade-off (CO/PM ↓ but NOx ↑)** is a favorite KTU short-answer trap — students often write "biodiesel reduces all emissions" and lose 2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Alternate fuels reduce petroleum dependence and lower tailpipe emissions, but no fuel is universally emission-free.
- **Three pillars** of fuel selection: **Engine compatibility → Storage safety → Emission profile**.
- **Hydrogen:** Zero carbon emissions; NOx is the *only* regulated challenge.
- **CNG:** Best *overall* gaseous alternate fuel; very low CO, HC, PM, CO₂; moderate NOx.
- **LPG:** Intermediate between gasoline and CNG; cheap retrofit option.
- **Methanol/Ethanol:** Oxygenated; reduce CO and PM significantly but **emit aldehydes**.
- **Biodiesel:** Excellent for CI; reduces PM/CO/HC by 40–60 % but slightly **increases NOx** (~5–15 %).
- **Key Octane Numbers:** H₂ > 130; CNG ≈ 120; Methanol 109; Ethanol 108; LPG 96–100; Gasoline 91–98.
- **Key Cetane Numbers:** Biodiesel 50–60; Diesel 45–55; Methanol/Ethanol < 15 (poor).
- **Wide flammability limit:** Hydrogen (4–75 %) > Methanol (6–36 %) > Ethanol (3.3–19 %) > Methane (5–15 %) > Gasoline (1–6 %).
- **Stoichiometric AFR formula:** $(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{4.76(32x + 4y + 16z)}{12x + y + 16z}$.
- **CO₂ emission index:** $EI_{CO_2} = \frac{44x}{12x + y}$ kg CO₂/kg fuel for C_xH_y.
- **NOx ∝ exp(-E_a/RT_flame)** — drives all lean-burn and EGR strategies.
- **"Carbon neutrality"** applies only to **biofuels** — CNG, LPG, and grey H₂ are still fossil-derived.
- **After-treatment is mandatory** for BS-VI / Euro-VI emission compliance, regardless of fuel.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relative Merits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relative Merits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Relative Merits** of alternate fuels is a structured comparative study that evaluates various non-conventional automotive fuels (CNG, LNG, LPG, hydrogen, biodiesel, ethanol, methanol, biogas, producer gas, electric/hybrid, and fuel cells) against a common reference framework. The comparison is performed against conventional gasoline/diesel and against each other, using standardized engineering parameters such as calorific value, storage requirements, safety indices, emission profiles, engine compatibility, and economic viability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Relative Merits" refers to a quantitative and qualitative comparison of alternative fuels based on their advantages (merits) and disadvantages (demerits) over conventional petroleum fuels and over each other, considering performance, emissions, cost, safety, availability, and infrastructure readiness.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are choosing a vehicle for your family from a showroom that offers petrol, diesel, CNG, electric, and hybrid options. Each option is like a "fuel package deal" — it comes with its own **price tag (cost)**, **fuel tank (storage)**, **running expenses (mileage)**, **pollution certificate (emissions)**, **safety rating**, and **service network (availability)**. 

Just as you would create a **scorecard** to rank cars (Mileage: 5/5, Safety: 4/5, Comfort: 3/5), engineers use a similar **engineering scorecard** to rank alternate fuels. The "Relative Merits" topic is essentially that **scorecard** — a systematic comparison framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Relative"?** Because no single alternate fuel is universally the "best." Each fuel has scenarios where it dominates and other scenarios where it loses. Hence we always compare *relative* to something: relative to gasoline, relative to diesel, or relative to another alternate fuel.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (Used Universally)

The following physical constants and reference values are mandatory for solving KTU problems:

- **Density of air at NTP: 1.293 kg/m³**
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of gasoline: 44 MJ/kg**
- **LHV of diesel: 42.5 MJ/kg**
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) for gasoline: 14.7:1**
- **AFR for diesel: 14.5:1**
- **Universal Gas Constant: R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Standard Temperature and Pressure: 273.15 K, 101.325 kPa**

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Comparison

| Pillar | What It Measures |
|---|---|
| **Energy Pillar** | Calorific value, energy density, stoichiometric ratio |
| **Environmental Pillar** | CO₂, CO, HC, NOx, PM, SO₂ emissions |
| **Practical Pillar** | Storage, safety, cost, infrastructure, availability |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Parameters Used for Relative Merit Analysis

When comparing alternate fuels, KTU examiners expect students to analyze along the following dimensions:

**1. Energy Parameters**
- Calorific Value (CV) — Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV)
- Energy density (MJ/L) and energy density per unit mass (MJ/kg)
- Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)
- Equivalence Ratio (λ)
- Flammability limits (LFL & UFL)
- Octane Number / Cetane Number
- Auto-ignition temperature

**2. Storage & Handling Parameters**
- Storage state (gaseous/liquid)
- Storage pressure (for gases)
- Storage temperature (cryogenic for LNG, LH₂)
- Energy required to compress/liquefy

**3. Emissions Parameters**
- CO₂ per unit energy (g/MJ)
- CO per km (g/km)
- NOx per km (g/km)
- Unburnt hydrocarbons (HC)
- Particulate Matter (PM)
- Aldehydes (especially for methanol/ethanol)

**4. Engine Compatibility Parameters**
- Compression ratio tolerance
- Octane requirement / Cetane requirement
- Material compatibility (corrosion, lubrication)
- Cold-start behavior
- Power derating (%)

**5. Economic & Strategic Parameters**
- Production cost per MJ
- Well-to-wheel efficiency
- Distribution infrastructure
- Government subsidies

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Comparison (How Examiners Mark)

**Step 1:** Identify the reference fuel (typically gasoline or diesel).

**Step 2:** Tabulate all relevant properties of candidate fuels side-by-side.

**Step 3:** Normalize values (e.g., per MJ of energy delivered) so that fuels with different energy densities become comparable.

**Step 4:** Assign merit ratings:
- **Merit ↑** when fuel is *better* than reference
- **Merit ↓** when fuel is *worse* than reference
- **Merit =** when fuel is *comparable*

**Step 5:** Conclude with a ranking justified by the dominant parameter (urban use → emissions; heavy haulage → energy density; cold climate → cold-start).

### 2.3 Why Each Parameter Matters (The 'Why' Behind the Numbers)

- **Why Calorific Value?** A fuel with higher CV carries more energy per kilogram → longer range per tank.
- **Why Stoichiometric AFR?** Determines the air induction system sizing and the theoretical combustion temperature → controls NOx.
- **Why Octane Number?** Higher octane allows higher compression ratio → higher thermal efficiency → more km per litre.
- **Why Auto-ignition temperature?** Determines knock resistance for SI engines and starting ease for CI engines.
- **Why Flammability limits?** Wider limits = easier combustion across varying conditions; too wide = safety hazard.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **high-yield** formulas for any KTU numerical on relative merits or fuel comparison.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot CV$ | Heat released from fuel mass $m$ | J or kJ |
| 2 | $\rho_{energy} = \rho_{fuel} \cdot CV$ | Volumetric energy density | MJ/L |
| 3 | $AFR_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}\bigg\vert_{stoich}$ | Theoretical air requirement per kg fuel | kg/kg |
| 4 | $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio (φ = 1 at stoich) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\phi}$ | Excess air ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $V_{gas} = \dfrac{mRT}{P}$ | Gas volume from ideal gas law (for CNG, biogas storage) | m³ |
| 7 | $W_{comp} = \dfrac{nRT}{\gamma - 1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ | Work to compress gas for storage (CNG at 200 bar) | J |
| 8 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | Otto cycle efficiency (linked to octane) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $CO_2\text{ reduction} = \left(1 - \dfrac{(C/F)_{alt}}{(C/F)_{ref}}\right) \times 100\%$ | % reduction in CO₂ vs reference fuel | % |
| 10 | $Power_{derating} = \left(1 - \dfrac{CV_{alt}}{CV_{ref}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Power loss when swapping fuel without engine mods | % |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, this comparison is used for:
- **OEMs (Tata, Maruti, Hyundai, Toyota)** deciding which alternate fuel to factory-fit.
- **Fleet operators (Ola, Uber, DTC, KSRTC)** choosing between CNG buses and electric buses.
- **Refineries and gas companies** planning LPG/CNG/LNG infrastructure.
- **Government policy (Ministry of Petroleum, NITI Aayog)** drafting ethanol-blending and hydrogen-mission roadmaps.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Power Derating When Switching from Gasoline to CNG

**Problem Context:** A 4-cylinder, 2.0 L gasoline engine produces **110 kW** at wide-open throttle. If it is retrofitted to run on CNG, calculate the **expected power output**, given:
- LHV of gasoline = **44 MJ/kg**
- LHV of CNG = **50 MJ/kg**
- Volumetric efficiency assumed unchanged
- Air charge limited to stoichiometric

**Step 1:** Establish the governing principle.

For a given engine displacement and volumetric efficiency, the **mass of air inducted per cycle is constant**. Hence the **mass of fuel per cycle is determined by the stoichiometric AFR**, which differs for each fuel.

**Step 2:** Express power in terms of fuel energy rate.

$$P = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot CV \cdot \eta_{th}$$

Since $\eta_{th}$ is approximately constant for the same compression ratio, and $\dot{m}_{fuel}$ depends on the AFR:

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$P_{alt} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{(A/F)_{alt}} \cdot CV_{alt} \cdot \eta_{th}$$

$$P_{ref} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{(A/F)_{ref}} \cdot CV_{ref} \cdot \eta_{th}$$

**Step 4:** Take the ratio to eliminate $\dot{m}_{air}$ and $\eta_{th}$.

$$\dfrac{P_{alt}}{P_{ref}} = \dfrac{(A/F)_{ref}}{(A/F)_{alt}} \cdot \dfrac{CV_{alt}}{CV_{ref}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute stoichiometric AFR values.

For gasoline: $(A/F)_{ref} = 14.7$
For methane (CNG): $(A/F)_{alt} = 17.2$

$$\dfrac{P_{CNG}}{P_{gasoline}} = \dfrac{14.7}{17.2} \times \dfrac{50}{44}$$

**Step 6:** Compute numerically.

$$\dfrac{P_{CNG}}{P_{gasoline}} = 0.8547 \times 1.1364 = 0.9710$$

**Step 7:** Express as percentage.

$$P_{CNG} = 0.9710 \times 110 \text{ kW} \approx 106.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8:** State the conclusion.

The CNG-converted engine loses about **3% power** due to higher air-fuel ratio (more air per kg fuel), despite CNG having a slightly higher calorific value than gasoline. This is why CNG vehicles often feel sluggish on highways but perform acceptably in city driving.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Side-by-Side Comparison Table (The "Master Sheet")

This matrix is the **single most important high-yield content** for KTU Module 2 questions on Relative Merits.

| Property | Gasoline | Diesel | CNG (CH₄) | LPG (C₃H₈/C₄H₁₀) | Hydrogen (H₂) | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) | Biodiesel (FAME) | Biogas (60% CH₄) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Molecular formula** | C₈H₁₈ (avg) | C₁₂H₂₆ (avg) | CH₄ | C₃H₈ / C₄H₁₀ | H₂ | C₂H₅OH | C₁₉H₃₆O₂ (avg) | CH₄ + CO₂ |
| **State at STP** | Liquid | Liquid | Gas | Gas (liq. under press.) | Gas | Liquid | Liquid | Gas |
| **LHV (MJ/kg)** | 44.0 | 42.5 | 50.0 | 46.0 | 120.0 | 26.9 | 37.2 | 22.0 |
| **Density (kg/L)** | 0.74 | 0.85 | 0.00072 (gas) | 0.00050 (gas) | 0.00009 (gas) | 0.79 | 0.88 | 0.00115 (gas) |
| **Energy density (MJ/L)** | 32.6 | 36.1 | 0.036 (gas) | 0.025 (gas) | 0.011 (gas) | 21.3 | 32.7 | 0.025 (gas) |
| **Stoich. AFR (kg/kg)** | 14.7 | 14.5 | 17.2 | 15.7 | 34.3 | 9.0 | 12.5 | 11.5 |
| **Octane Number** | 88–98 | — | 130 | 100–110 | 130+ | 108 | — | 130 |
| **Cetane Number** | — | 45–55 | — | — | — | — | 50–65 | — |
| **Auto-ignition (°C)** | 280 | 250 | 540 | 450 | 585 | 365 | 150 | 540 |
| **LFL (% vol in air)** | 1.4 | 0.6 | 5.0 | 2.1 | 4.0 | 4.3 | — | 5.0 |
| **UFL (% vol in air)** | 7.6 | 5.5 | 15.0 | 9.5 | 75.0 | 19.0 | — | 15.0 |
| **CO₂ (g/MJ)** | 73 | 74 | 56 | 65 | 0 | 70 | 78 | 78 |
| **CO (relative)** | High | Med | Very Low | Low | 0 | Med | Low | Very Low |
| **NOx (relative)** | High | High | Med | Med | Low | Med | Higher | Med |
| **PM (relative)** | Low | High | Very Low | Very Low | 0 | Low | Low | Very Low |
| **Power derating (%)** | 0 (ref) | 0 (ref) | 3–5 | 5–8 | 15–25 | 10–15 | 5–10 | 5–10 |
| **Storage form** | Liquid tank | Liquid tank | 200–250 bar cyl. | 8–10 bar cyl. | 350–700 bar cyl. / LH₂ at −253°C | Liquid tank | Liquid tank | Gas holder / cyl. |
| **Cost (relative)** | 1.0 | 1.0 | 0.6–0.8 | 0.9 | 3–10 | 1.2–1.5 | 1.3–1.8 | 0.4–0.6 |
| **Renewability** | No | No | No (fossil) | No (fossil) | Yes (green H₂) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Engine type fit** | SI | CI | SI (CI rare) | SI / dual | SI / FCV | SI (FFV) | CI | SI / dual |

### 3.3 Derivation: CO₂ Reduction from Ethanol Blending (E20)

**Problem Context:** A vehicle consumes **500 kg of gasoline per year**. If it is switched to **E20** (20% ethanol, 80% gasoline by volume, density of blend ≈ 0.76 kg/L), calculate the annual CO₂ reduction.

Assume:
- Density of gasoline = 0.74 kg/L
- Density of ethanol = 0.79 kg/L
- LHV gasoline = 44 MJ/kg → CO₂ ≈ 3.16 kg CO₂ per kg gasoline
- LHV ethanol = 26.9 MJ/kg → CO₂ ≈ 1.91 kg CO₂ per kg ethanol

**Step 1:** Convert the gasoline volume to mass.

$$m_{gasoline} = 500 \text{ kg} \text{ (given)}$$

**Step 2:** Determine equivalent mass of E20 blend for the same energy output.

Energy from original gasoline:

$$E_{orig} = 500 \times 44 = 22000 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 3:** Mass of E20 blend needed to deliver the same energy.

Average LHV of E20 (vol-weighted, approx):

$$CV_{E20} \approx 0.80 \times 44 + 0.20 \times 26.9 = 35.2 + 5.38 = 40.58 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

$$m_{E20} = \dfrac{22000}{40.58} \approx 542 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4:** Compute CO₂ from E20.

CO₂ fraction in blend (mass-weighted):
- Mass fraction of gasoline in blend = $\dfrac{0.80 \times 0.74}{0.542_{total mass per unit vol}}$ — simpler approach: assume CO₂ per kg of E20 = weighted average of CO₂ per kg of components

$$CO_2^{E20} = 0.80 \times 3.16 + 0.20 \times 1.91 = 2.528 + 0.382 = 2.91 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 5:** CO₂ from blend for 542 kg:

$$m_{CO_2,E20} = 542 \times 2.91 \approx 1577 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year}$$

**Step 6:** CO₂ from original 500 kg gasoline:

$$m_{CO_2,orig} = 500 \times 3.16 = 1580 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year}$$

**Step 7:** Compute reduction.

$$\Delta CO_2 = 1580 - 1577 = 3 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year}$$

Reduction percentage:

$$\dfrac{\Delta CO_2}{m_{CO_2,orig}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{3}{1580} \times 100\% \approx 0.19\%$$

**Step 8:** Interpretation.

A modest **0.2% direct CO₂ reduction** per vehicle per year. The big advantage of ethanol is **displacing petroleum import** (energy security) and **biogenic carbon cycle** (CO₂ absorbed by sugarcane is re-released, net zero).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The CO₂ reduction from ethanol blending is often **much less than students assume** because the energy density penalty (~30%) means more fuel is burned per km. The true benefit lies in renewability and net carbon cycle, not tailpipe reduction alone.

### 3.4 Comparison Logic: When to Choose Which Fuel

| Use Case | Best Alternate Fuel | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Urban bus fleet | **CNG** | Low emissions, low fuel cost, lighter than air (safety) |
| Long-haul trucks | **LNG** or **Diesel** | High energy density needed for range |
| Three-wheelers (autorickshaw) | **CNG / LPG** | Low cost, simple retrofit |
| Premium passenger cars | **Hybrid / Electric** | Comfort, low running cost |
| Farm tractors | **Biodiesel** | Lubricity, agricultural waste feedstock |
| Two-wheelers | **CNG** (retrofit) or **Battery** | Affordability, low weight |
| Remote village electrification | **Biogas / Producer gas** | Local feedstock, off-grid |
| Heavy industry (steel, cement) | **Hydrogen** | Decarbonizing process heat |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Fuel Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Alternate Fuel] --> B{Primary Need?}
    B -- Low emissions for city --> C{Heavy vehicle?}
    B -- Long range / heavy haul --> D{Cold climate?}
    B -- Decentralized rural use --> E{Local feedstock?}

    C -- Yes --> F[CNG for buses]
    C -- No --> G[CNG or Hybrid for cars]

    D -- Yes --> H[Electric with battery heater]
    D -- No --> I[LNG for trucks]

    E -- Yes --> J[Biogas plant]
    E -- No --> K[Producer gas from wood/coal]

    F --> L[End]
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture — Fuel Property Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT [Reference Fuel]
        A1[Gasoline or Diesel Baseline]
    end

    subgraph PARAMS [Comparison Parameters]
        P1[Calorific Value MJ per kg]
        P2[Energy Density MJ per L]
        P3[Stoichiometric AFR]
        P4[Octane or Cetane Number]
        P5[Auto Ignition Temperature]
        P6[Emission Profile CO CO2 NOx PM]
    end

    subgraph CANDIDATES [Alternate Fuels]
        C1[CNG]
        C2[LPG]
        C3[Hydrogen]
        C4[Ethanol]
        C5[Biodiesel]
        C6[Biogas]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT [Relative Merit Scorecard]
        O1[Merit per parameter]
        O2[Merit per use case]
        O3[Final ranking]
    end

    A1 --> P1
    A1 --> P2
    A1 --> P3
    A1 --> P4
    A1 --> P5
    A1 --> P6

    C1 --> P1
    C2 --> P1
    C3 --> P1
    C4 --> P1
    C5 --> P1
    C6 --> P1

    P1 --> O1
    P2 --> O1
    P3 --> O1
    P4 --> O1
    P5 --> O1
    P6 --> O1

    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Merits vs Demerits Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F[Alternate Fuel Choice] --> M{Merits}
    F --> D{Demerits}

    M --> M1[Lower CO2 and PM]
    M --> M2[Renewable feedstock]
    M --> M3[High octane allows CR]
    M --> M4[Domestic availability]

    D --> D1[Lower energy density]
    D --> D2[Storage infrastructure cost]
    D --> D3[Power derating]
    D --> D4[Material compatibility issues]

    M1 --> BAL[Engineering Tradeoff]
    M2 --> BAL
    M3 --> BAL
    M4 --> BAL
    D1 --> BAL
    D2 --> BAL
    D3 --> BAL
    D4 --> BAL

    BAL --> OUT[Final Fuel Selection based on Application]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three relative merits of CNG over gasoline as an automotive fuel.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Lower emissions:** CNG produces significantly lower CO, HC, and PM emissions; no lead or benzene.
2. **Higher octane number (≈130):** Allows higher compression ratio, improving thermal efficiency.
3. **Cheaper fuel cost:** Roughly 40–60% lower per km than gasoline in India.
4. **Safer (lighter than air):** Disperses upward in case of leak; non-corrosive; no spill risk.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why does switching an engine from gasoline to LPG result in a power loss of about 5–8%?*

**Model Answer:**
LPG has a stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of **15.7** versus gasoline's **14.7**. For the same volume of air inducted, the engine burns slightly less mass of LPG per cycle (because more air is required per kg of fuel). Even though LPG has a slightly higher calorific value (46 MJ/kg vs 44 MJ/kg for gasoline), the higher AFR results in less fuel energy released per cycle. Mathematically: $P_{LPG}/P_{gasoline} = (AFR_{gas}/AFR_{LPG}) \times (CV_{LPG}/CV_{gas}) = (14.7/15.7) \times (46/44) \approx 0.979$, i.e., ~2–8% power loss depending on engine tuning.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** *Compare the relative merits and demerits of CNG, LPG, and hydrogen as automotive fuels based on (i) calorific value, (ii) storage method, (iii) safety, and (iv) emissions. [7 Marks]*

**(b)** *An engine rated at 90 kW on gasoline is to be retrofitted to CNG. If the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of gasoline is 14.7 and that of methane is 17.2, calculate the power output on CNG. Assume no change in volumetric efficiency and thermal efficiency. [7 Marks]*

---

**Model Solution — (a):**

| Parameter | CNG | LPG | Hydrogen |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Calorific value (LHV, MJ/kg) | 50 | 46 | 120 |
| (ii) Storage method | Compressed gas at 200–250 bar in cylinders | Liquefied under 8–10 bar pressure | Compressed gas at 350–700 bar or liquid at −253°C |
| (iii) Safety | Lighter than air → disperses upward; non-toxic | Heavier than air → pools near ground; fire hazard | Wide flammability range (4–75%); invisible flame |
| (iv) Emissions | Very low CO, HC, PM; ~25% less CO₂ than gasoline | Low CO, HC; some unburnt LPG leakage | Zero CO₂ at tailpipe; only H₂O vapour; upstream emissions depend on production method |

**Merits — CNG:** Cheap, abundant, cleanest of the three.
**Merits — LPG:** High energy density, easier distribution as liquid.
**Merits — Hydrogen:** Zero tailpipe CO₂; only water emission.

**Demerits — CNG:** Power derating, heavy cylinders.
**Demerits — LPG:** Safety (heavier than air), non-renewable.
**Demerits — Hydrogen:** Very high storage cost, low energy density by volume, embrittlement of metals.

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$\dfrac{P_{CNG}}{P_{gasoline}} = \dfrac{(A/F)_{gasoline}}{(A/F)_{CNG}} \times \dfrac{CV_{CNG}}{CV_{gasoline}}$$

$$\dfrac{P_{CNG}}{P_{gasoline}} = \dfrac{14.7}{17.2} \times \dfrac{50}{44}$$

$$\dfrac{P_{CNG}}{90} = 0.8547 \times 1.1364 = 0.9710$$

$$P_{CNG} = 0.9710 \times 90 \approx 87.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Answer:** Approximately **87.4 kW** — a derating of about **2.9%**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** *Explain the relative merits of biodiesel over conventional diesel with respect to (i) renewability, (ii) lubricity, (iii) emissions, and (iv) cold-flow properties. [7 Marks]*

**(b)** *A diesel engine consumes 6 L/hr of diesel (density 850 kg/m³, LHV 42.5 MJ/kg). Compute the equivalent mass flow rate of biodiesel (density 880 kg/m³, LHV 37.2 MJ/kg) required to deliver the same energy output. What is the percent increase in fuel mass? [7 Marks]*

---

**Model Solution — (a):**

**(i) Renewability:** Biodiesel is derived from vegetable oils (soybean, palm, jatropha, waste cooking oil) or animal fats — **renewable, locally available**; reduces import dependence. Conventional diesel is finite fossil resource.

**(ii) Lubricity:** Biodiesel has excellent inherent lubricity due to fatty acid methyl ester (FAME) structure; actually **improves engine wear** when blended at 5–20% with petro-diesel (B5–B20). Petro-diesel has lost lubricity due to severe hydrodesulfurization in modern refining.

**(iii) Emissions:** Biodiesel reduces:
- CO by ~50%, HC by ~30%, PM by ~40%
- BUT slightly increases NOx (~10%) due to higher combustion temperatures from bound oxygen.
- Net lifecycle CO₂ reduction: 50–80% (biogenic carbon cycle).

**(iv) Cold-flow properties:** Biodiesel has **poor cold-flow** — cloud point typically 0 to 10°C, pour point −5 to 5°C. Conventional diesel works at −20°C. This is biodiesel's biggest demerit for cold-climate use.

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute mass flow rate of diesel.

$$\dot{m}_{diesel} = 6 \text{ L/hr} \times 0.85 \text{ kg/L} = 5.1 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 2:** Energy output rate from diesel.

$$\dot{E} = 5.1 \times 42.5 = 216.75 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

**Step 3:** Required mass flow rate of biodiesel.

$$\dot{m}_{biodiesel} = \dfrac{\dot{E}}{CV_{biodiesel}} = \dfrac{216.75}{37.2} \approx 5.827 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 4:** Percent increase in fuel mass.

$$\%\text{ increase} = \dfrac{5.827 - 5.1}{5.1} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.727}{5.1} \times 100\% \approx 14.25\%$$

**Answer:** Biodiesel mass flow = **5.83 kg/hr**, which is **~14.3% higher** than diesel for the same energy output.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in "Relative Merits" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Comparing per kg instead of per MJ** — Always normalize to energy output. A fuel with high MJ/kg but low kg/L may still be worse per litre.
> 2. **Ignoring volumetric efficiency changes** — Gaseous fuels displace air in the cylinder; this is the **primary** reason for power derating, not just the stoichiometric ratio.
> 3. **Forgetting that higher octane ≠ higher CV** — Octane number measures **knock resistance**, not energy content. Hydrogen has high octane but low energy density per litre.
> 4. **Confusing LHV and HHV** — Use **LHV** for engine calculations because water leaves as vapour at exhaust.
> 5. **Writing "CNG is renewable"** — CNG is fossil-fuel-based methane. It is cleaner, not renewable.
> 6. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 7. **Claiming "hydrogen is the perfect fuel"** — Mention the storage cost and "well-to-wheel" losses. Hydrogen from electrolysis has 60–70% energy loss.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Rapid Revision Checklist — "Relative Merits"**

- **Definition:** Comparative study of alternate fuels against conventional fuels and against each other, based on energy, environment, and practicality.
- **Most critical parameters:** LHV (MJ/kg), energy density (MJ/L), stoichiometric AFR, octane/cetane number, auto-ignition temperature, CO₂ per MJ, storage pressure/temperature.
- **CNG is best for urban transport:** Low cost, low emissions, but power derates ~3–5% and cylinder weight is high.
- **LNG is best for long-haul heavy vehicles:** High energy density by volume due to cryogenic liquefaction at −162°C.
- **LPG is heavier than air** — safety hazard in enclosed spaces; CNG is lighter than air — safer in leaks.
- **Hydrogen has the highest LHV (120 MJ/kg)** but the **lowest volumetric energy density** — storage is its biggest challenge.
- **Biodiesel gives ~14% more fuel consumption** than diesel for the same energy; reduces PM and CO but slightly raises NOx.
- **Ethanol E20 gives ~0.2% direct tailpipe CO₂ reduction** per vehicle per year; the real benefit is renewable displacement of fossil imports.
- **Power derating formula (high-yield):** $P_{alt}/P_{ref} = (AFR_{ref}/AFR_{alt}) \times (CV_{alt}/CV_{ref})$
- **CO₂ reduction is best expressed per MJ of useful work**, not per km, because fuels with different LHV travel different distances per kg.
- **Renewable ≠ zero emissions:** Production, transport, and processing of all alternate fuels also create emissions (well-to-wheel analysis).
- **Octane number vs Cetane number:** Octane → SI engines; Cetane → CI engines.
- **Memorize the magic numbers:** CNG octane = 130, hydrogen octane = 130+, ethanol octane = 108, methane AFR = 17.2, gasoline AFR = 14.7.
- **Always mention the safety angle** in the answer — flammability limits and density relative to air.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For any "Compare X and Y" question, structure your answer in a **3-column table** (Property | X | Y) and conclude with a **3-line application-specific verdict**. Examiners reward clarity and structured presentation generously in the 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Merits and Demerits of Alternate Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Merits and Demerits of Alternate Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Alternate Fuels?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Alternate fuels* (also called *non-conventional* or *alternative fuels*) are any fuel sources or energy carriers that can substitute for conventional petroleum-based fuels (gasoline and diesel) in internal combustion engines and other energy-conversion systems. They include gaseous fuels (CNG, LPG, LNG, H₂), liquid biofuels (ethanol, methanol, biodiesel), and electric/hybrid propulsion systems.

### Intuitive Analogy

Imagine your kitchen uses LPG cylinders instead of firewood. Why? Because LPG is **cleaner**, **easier to transport**, and **more efficient** per kilogram. The same logic applies to vehicles and industries — **alternate fuels are the "LPG cylinder" replacing the "firewood" of conventional gasoline/diesel.** They are chosen because they offer advantages in emissions, renewability, or availability, but they also come with trade-offs (cost, storage, infrastructure) — just like LPG needs special cylinders and gas stoves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The 2024 Scheme requires students to *critically evaluate* the merits (advantages) and demerits (limitations) of every alternate fuel studied. This is a **high-weightage descriptive topic** frequently appearing as a 7 or 14-mark question in End Semester Examinations (ESE).

## 1.2 Scope of the Topic

Alternate fuels are broadly classified into four families, each with distinct merit/demerit profiles:

| S.No | Family | Examples |
|------|--------|----------|
| 1 | Gaseous Fuels | **CNG, LPG, LNG, Hydrogen** |
| 2 | Liquid Biofuels | **Ethanol, Methanol, Biodiesel** |
| 3 | Electric Propulsion | **BEV, HEV, PHEV, FCEV** |
| 4 | Renewable/Solar | **Solar PV, Solar Thermal, Bio-H₂** |

> [!TIP]
> A common examiner trick is asking *"Compare the merits of CNG over diesel."* — Students who rote-learn lose marks. Understand the *underlying physics* (e.g., high octane rating → better knock resistance).

## 1.3 Why Evaluate Merits and Demerits?

Any fuel substitution decision must balance:

- **Energy Density** (MJ/kg or MJ/m³)
- **Emission Profile** (CO₂, NOx, PM, HC, SOx)
- **Renewability / Carbon Cycle**
- **Infrastructure & Distribution**
- **Safety & Storage**
- **Engine Compatibility / Efficiency**

These six criteria form the **universal evaluation matrix** used in KTU board examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Evaluation Framework

## 2.1 Comparative Analysis: Merits and Demerits of Each Alternate Fuel

### 2.1.1 Compressed Natural Gas (CNG)

CNG is natural gas (mostly **CH₄**, ~85–95%) compressed to **200–250 bar**.

**Merits:**
- **High Octane Rating** (~120) → permits **high compression ratio** → higher thermal efficiency.
- **Lower CO₂ emissions** (~25% less than gasoline per km).
- **Virtually zero particulate matter (PM)** and very low NOx.
- Non-corrosive, non-diluting to lubricating oil.
- Abundant global reserves; well-developed pipeline distribution.
- **Lean burn capability** improves fuel economy further.

**Demerits:**
- **Low energy density per unit volume** (~9 MJ/m³ at 200 bar vs. gasoline ~32,000 MJ/m³).
- Requires **bulky, heavy storage cylinders** → reduced luggage space.
- **Reduced range** — frequent refueling needed.
- **High initial conversion cost** of vehicles.
- Refueling infrastructure is **geographically limited**.
- Slow refueling time compared to liquid fuels.
- Slight power loss (~10–15%) due to displacement of intake air by fuel.

### 2.1.2 Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)

LPG = mixture of **propane (C₃H₈)** and **butane (C₄H₁₀)**, liquefied at moderate pressure (~7–8 bar).

**Merits:**
- **Higher energy density** than CNG (liquid state).
- Clean burning, lower CO and HC emissions than gasoline.
- **Easier to store** than CNG; existing gasoline vehicles can be retrofitted.
- Good cold-start performance due to high vapor pressure.
- Established distribution network (cooking gas industry).

**Demerits:**
- **Higher cost** per km than CNG or diesel in many regions.
- **Safety hazard** — heavier than air, pools at low levels → explosion risk on leakage.
- Limited availability for automotive use in some countries.
- Slightly higher NOx than CNG.
- Non-renewable fossil fuel — still contributes to net CO₂.

### 2.1.3 Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG)

LNG is natural gas cooled to **–162 °C** to become liquid (1/600th the volume of gas).

**Merits:**
- **Highest energy density** among gaseous fuels (liquid form).
- Suitable for **heavy-duty trucks, ships, and long-haul transport**.
- Lower emissions than diesel.
- Useful for transporting stranded gas reserves where pipelines are uneconomical.

**Demerits:**
- **Cryogenic storage** required → expensive, vacuum-insulated tanks.
- **Boil-off gas (BOG)** losses during storage and transport.
- Very high capital cost for LNG infrastructure.
- Not practical for light-duty passenger vehicles.
- Safety risk from cryogenic burns and asphyxiation.

### 2.1.4 Hydrogen (H₂)

Hydrogen is the **ultimate clean fuel** — combustion product is essentially water.

**Merits:**
- **Zero carbon emissions** at point of use (only H₂O exhaust).
- Very high energy content per unit mass (**142 MJ/kg**, ~3× gasoline).
- Can be produced from **water electrolysis** using renewable electricity (green H₂).
- Used in **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEV)** with 50–60% efficiency (vs. ~25% for ICE).
- Rapid refueling (3–5 minutes).

**Demerits:**
- **Extremely low volumetric energy density** (~10 MJ/m³ at 700 bar).
- **Difficult to store** — needs cryogenic liquid or 350–700 bar compressed gas.
- **Highly flammable** with wide explosion limit (4–75% in air).
- **Embrittlement** of metal tanks.
- **Production is energy-intensive** — most H₂ today is "grey" (from fossil fuels).
- Expensive fuel cell catalysts (platinum).
- Lack of widespread refueling infrastructure.

### 2.1.5 Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)

Produced by **fermentation of sugars** (sugarcane, corn, molasses) or **cellulose** (second-generation).

**Merits:**
- **Renewable** — closed CO₂ cycle (CO₂ absorbed during crop growth).
- High octane number (~108) → anti-knock.
- Can be blended with gasoline (E10, E20, E85) without major engine modifications for low blends.
- Reduces dependence on crude oil imports.
- Burns cleaner, lower aromatic emissions.

**Demerits:**
- **Lower energy density** than gasoline (~30 MJ/kg vs. 44 MJ/kg).
- **Corrosive** to some engine materials (rubber, aluminum, magnesium).
- **Hygroscopic** — absorbs water → phase separation in fuel tanks.
- Cold-start issues in E85 at low ambient temperatures.
- **Food vs. fuel debate** — diverts agricultural land.
- Requires engine modifications for high blends (E85).

### 2.1.6 Methanol (CH₃OH)

Produced from **natural gas, coal, or biomass** (syngas → methanol synthesis).

**Merits:**
- Can be produced from a wide range of feedstocks.
- **High octane rating** (~109).
- Cleaner combustion, lower NOx and PM.
- Liquid at ambient conditions → easy storage/transport.
- Promising for **fuel cells** (direct methanol fuel cells).

**Demerits:**
- **Toxic** — ingestion or inhalation causes blindness/death.
- Corrosive to metals, plastics, elastomers.
- Lower energy density than gasoline (~20 MJ/kg).
- **Cold-start difficulty** (low vapor pressure at low T).
- Formaldehyde emissions on incomplete combustion.
- Currently not widely used as a transport fuel.

### 2.1.7 Biodiesel (FAME)

Biodiesel is **Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME)** produced by **transesterification** of vegetable oils or animal fats with methanol.

**Merits:**
- **Renewable and biodegradable**.
- **Cetane number ~55–60** (better than diesel ~45).
- **Lubricity additive** — reduces engine wear.
- Compatible with existing diesel engines (B20 = 20% biodiesel, 80% diesel).
- Lower CO, HC, and PM emissions.
- Net carbon-neutral over life cycle.

**Demerits:**
- **Higher viscosity** than diesel → cold-flow problems, injector deposits.
- **Oxidation instability** — shelf life limited to ~6 months.
- **Solvent effect** — can degrade rubber seals and gaskets in older engines.
- Slightly higher NOx emissions than diesel.
- Energy content ~9% lower than diesel.
- Cost is higher than fossil diesel without subsidies.
- Glycerol by-product needs disposal.

### 2.1.8 Electric Vehicles (EV / BEV)

**Merits:**
- **Zero tailpipe emissions** — best urban air quality.
- High efficiency (~80–90% for motor + battery).
- **Low operating cost** (~₹1/km vs. ₹6/km for petrol).
- Quiet operation, low vibration.
- Regenerative braking recovers kinetic energy.
- No oil change, fewer moving parts → low maintenance.

**Demerits:**
- **Limited range** (~200–500 km per charge).
- **Long charging time** (4–8 hours slow charge; 30 min fast charge).
- **Battery degradation** over time (8–10 year life).
- **High upfront cost** despite subsidies.
- **Electricity source matters** — if grid is coal-based, indirect emissions are high.
- **Range anxiety** and inadequate charging infrastructure.
- Lithium/cobalt mining has environmental and ethical concerns.

### 2.1.9 Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEV)

**Merits:**
- **Combines ICE + electric motor** — best of both worlds.
- **20–35% better fuel economy** than conventional vehicles.
- No external charging needed (self-charging via regen braking).
- Lower emissions without range limitations.

**Demerits:**
- Higher cost than pure ICE.
- Complex dual powertrain → expensive maintenance.
- Battery replacement cost is significant.
- Marginal environmental benefit if grid is fossil-fueled.

### 2.1.10 Solar Energy (PV / Thermal)

**Merits:**
- **Abundant, inexhaustible** — ~173,000 TW reaches Earth's surface.
- Zero operating emissions.
- Low operating and maintenance cost.
- Modular, scalable from mW to GW.
- **Silent operation** — no moving parts in PV.

**Demerits:**
- **Intermittency** — depends on sunlight (day/night, clouds, seasons).
- **Low power density** (~100–200 W/m² at noon) — large area required.
- **High initial capital cost** for panels and BOS (balance of system).
- Energy storage (batteries) is expensive and has its own environmental footprint.
- Manufacturing of solar panels involves toxic materials (Cd, Pb).
- Geographic dependency — better near equator.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Evaluation Matrix (Comparative Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important summary table** for KTU ESE. Memorize the relative trends (>, <, ≈), not exact numbers.

| Property | Gasoline | Diesel | CNG | LPG | H₂ | Ethanol | Biodiesel | Electricity |
|----------|----------|--------|-----|-----|-----|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Energy Density (MJ/kg) | **44** | **43** | **50** | **46** | **142** | **30** | **38** | — (MJ/kWh ≈ 3.6) |
| Octane Number | 90–95 | — | 120 | 100 | 130+ | 108 | — | — |
| Cetane Number | — | 45–55 | — | — | — | — | 55–60 | — |
| CO₂ (relative) | 100% | 110% | 75% | 80% | 0% (TTW) | 70% | 60% | 30% (grid avg.) |
| NOx | High | Very High | Low | Med | Low | Low | Slightly High | Zero (TTW) |
| Particulate Matter | Med | High | Negligible | Low | None | Low | Low | None |
| Renewability | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (if green) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (if renewable) |
| Storage Difficulty | Low | Low | High | Med | Very High | Low | Low | Med |

**Legend:** ✓ = Yes, ✗ = No, TTW = Tank-to-Wheel

## 2.3 Engineering Application & Decision Framework

In real-world engineering practice (e.g., public bus transport, fleet management), the choice of alternate fuel depends on:

1. **Mission Profile** — Urban (CNG/EV good) vs. Long-haul (LNG/Biodiesel good).
2. **Local Energy Mix** — Coal-rich grid → CNG/Hybrid may be cleaner than BEV.
3. **Cost of Infrastructure** — Per filling station: H₂ ≈ ₹5 Cr > CNG ≈ ₹1 Cr > EV DC fast charger ≈ ₹0.3 Cr.
4. **Climate** — Biodiesel cold-flow problem in winter; CNG ok.
5. **Policy and Subsidies** — FAME-II in India, NGV policy, etc.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Why is CNG preferred for city buses in India?"*
> **Model answer anchor:** High octane allows lean burn → low emissions in dense traffic; well-suited for stop-go urban duty cycle; indigenously available natural gas; established pipeline infrastructure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Analytical Derivations & Quantitative Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is fundamentally a **comparative/qualitative** topic, full mathematical derivations are limited. However, KTU frequently tests **energy density**, **volumetric analysis**, and **emission calculations**. The following quantitative sections are essential for scoring full marks.

## 3.1 Volumetric Energy Density Calculation — Worked Example

**Problem:** A CNG cylinder has volume **V = 50 L** and is filled to **P = 200 bar (20 MPa)** at temperature **T = 298 K**. Methane behaves approximately as an ideal gas. Compare the stored energy to a 50 L gasoline tank.

**Given:**
- Lower Heating Value of CH₄: **LHV_CH₄ = 50 MJ/kg**
- Molar mass of CH₄: **M = 16 g/mol**
- Universal gas constant: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- Density of gasoline: **ρ_gas = 750 kg/m³**
- LHV of gasoline: **LHV_gas = 44 MJ/kg**

**Step 1 — Mass of CNG stored using Ideal Gas Law:**

$$PV = nRT \implies n = \frac{PV}{RT}$$

$$n = \frac{(20 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa}) \times (50 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3)}{8.314 \times 298}$$

$$n = \frac{1.0 \times 10^6}{2477.6} \approx 403.6 \, \text{mol}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mass:**

$$m_{\text{CNG}} = n \times M = 403.6 \times 16 \, \text{g} = 6458 \, \text{g} \approx 6.46 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Energy stored in CNG cylinder:**

$$E_{\text{CNG}} = m_{\text{CNG}} \times LHV_{\text{CH}_4}$$

$$E_{\text{CNG}} = 6.46 \, \text{kg} \times 50 \, \text{MJ/kg} = 323 \, \text{MJ}$$

**Step 4 — Energy stored in 50 L gasoline tank:**

$$m_{\text{gas}} = \rho_{\text{gas}} \times V = 750 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \times 0.05 \, \text{m}^3 = 37.5 \, \text{kg}$$

$$E_{\text{gas}} = 37.5 \times 44 = 1650 \, \text{MJ}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison Ratio:**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{E_{\text{CNG}}}{E_{\text{gas}}} = \frac{323}{1650} \approx 0.196$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A 50 L CNG cylinder stores only about **19.6%** the energy of a 50 L gasoline tank. This is the *quantitative root* of the demerit "limited range" — and is a high-yield KTU derivation.

## 3.2 Emission Reduction Calculation — Biodiesel

**Problem:** A diesel bus consumes **1,000 L/month**. B20 blend (20% biodiesel) is used. Calculate the CO₂ reduction if biodiesel lifecycle CO₂ = 60% of diesel.

**Given:**
- Diesel density: **ρ = 0.85 kg/L**
- LHV of diesel: **43 MJ/kg**
- CO₂ emission factor (diesel): **3.2 kg CO₂ per liter**

**Step 1 — Monthly diesel volume in B20 blend:**

$$V_{\text{diesel, B20}} = 0.80 \times 1000 = 800 \, \text{L/month}$$

**Step 2 — CO₂ from diesel portion (well-to-wheel):**

$$CO_2^{\text{diesel}} = 800 \, \text{L} \times 3.2 \, \text{kg/L} = 2560 \, \text{kg/month}$$

**Step 3 — CO₂ from biodiesel portion (life-cycle):**

$$CO_2^{\text{bio}} = (0.20 \times 1000) \times 3.2 \times 0.60 = 384 \, \text{kg/month}$$

**Step 4 — Total CO₂ with B20:**

$$CO_2^{\text{total}} = 2560 + 384 = 2944 \, \text{kg/month}$$

**Step 5 — Compare to 100% diesel (3200 kg/month):**

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{3200 - 2944}{3200} \times 100\% = 8\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU answer bonus:** State that the *life-cycle* (Well-to-Wheel) CO₂ reduction is higher (~15–20%) because carbon absorbed during crop growth offsets tailpipe CO₂. Be careful to distinguish TTW vs. WTW emissions.

## 3.3 Efficiency Comparison: ICE vs. Fuel Cell

The **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)** is a high-value KTU topic. The efficiency chain is:

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{production}} \times \eta_{\text{storage}} \times \eta_{\text{fuel cell}} \times \eta_{\text{motor}}$$

For **Grey H₂** (from natural gas steam reforming):

$$\eta_{\text{prod}} \approx 0.70, \quad \eta_{\text{storage}} \approx 0.90, \quad \eta_{\text{FC}} \approx 0.55, \quad \eta_{\text{motor}} \approx 0.90$$

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.70 \times 0.90 \times 0.55 \times 0.90 \approx 0.31 \;(31\%)$$

For **Green H₂** (renewable electrolysis): Production efficiency drops to ~30%, making end-to-end efficiency depend heavily on the renewable electricity source.

Compare to a gasoline ICE: **η ≈ 20–25%** (tank-to-wheel).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even with current technology, a hydrogen fuel cell drivetrain is more efficient than a gasoline ICE. The merit of hydrogen is **fundamentally thermodynamic**, not just emission-based.

## 3.4 Hybrid Vehicle — Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery

**Problem:** A HEV of mass **m = 1500 kg** decelerates from **v = 60 km/h** to stop at a traffic signal. Calculate the kinetic energy recovered if regen efficiency is 60%.

**Step 1 — Kinetic Energy:**

$$KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times \left(\frac{60 \times 1000}{3600}\right)^2$$

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (16.67)^2 \approx 208,333 \, \text{J} \approx 208 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Recovered energy:**

$$E_{\text{regen}} = 0.60 \times 208 = 125 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent fuel saved:**

$$\text{Gasoline LHV} = 44 \, \text{MJ/kg} \implies m_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{0.125}{44} = 2.84 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg} \approx 3.7 \, \text{mL}$$

For a city bus making **50 such stops/day**:

$$E_{\text{daily}} = 50 \times 125 = 6250 \, \text{kJ} \approx 0.16 \, \text{L of gasoline equivalent saved/day}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite KTU numerical.** It demonstrates the *quantitative merit* of hybrid technology in urban driving cycles.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
