# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: List any three merits and three demerits of CNG as an automotive fuel.** [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Merits:**
1. High octane rating (~120) allows lean burn operation and higher compression ratio.
2. Significantly lower emissions of CO, HC, and particulates compared to gasoline/diesel.
3. Cheaper per running kilometer in India due to indigenous natural gas supply.

**Demerits:**
1. Very low volumetric energy density → bulky, heavy cylinders.
2. Reduced vehicle range; frequent refueling required.
3. Limited refueling infrastructure outside major cities.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "CNG is cheaper" without qualifying with "per km, in India." Examiners deduct marks for absolute, unqualified statements. Always add context.

### Question 2
**Q: Why is hydrogen considered the fuel of the future despite not being a primary energy source?** [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

Hydrogen is called the *fuel of the future* because:
1. **Zero carbon emissions at point of use** — only water is produced on combustion or in a fuel cell.
2. **Highest energy content per unit mass** (~142 MJ/kg, 3× gasoline).
3. It is a **versatile energy carrier** — can be produced from water using renewable electricity (green H₂), enabling energy storage for intermittent renewables.

It is *not a primary energy source* because it does not occur freely in usable quantities on Earth and must be **extracted/produced** from compounds (CH₄, H₂O, biomass) — hence, it is an **energy vector**, not an energy source.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**Q: Discuss in detail the merits and demerits of biodiesel as an alternative to conventional diesel fuel. Explain the transesterification process and its effect on the fuel properties.** **[14 Marks, Module 2]**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Production of Biodiesel — Transesterification Process [7 Marks]**

Biodiesel is produced by reacting vegetable oil or animal fat (triglyceride) with an alcohol (methanol) in the presence of a catalyst (NaOH or KOH) to yield **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME)** and glycerol.

The chemical reaction is:

$$TG + 3 ROH \xrightleftharpoons{\text{Catalyst}} 3 R^{\prime}COO\,R + \text{Glycerol}$$

Where:
- $TG$ = Triglyceride (vegetable oil)
- $ROH$ = Alcohol (typically methanol, $CH_3OH$)
- $R^{\prime}COO\,R$ = Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (biodiesel)
- $Glycerol$ = By-product (used in soap, pharma industries)

**Process Steps:**
1. **Pretreatment** of oil — remove water, free fatty acids (FFA) by acid esterification.
2. **Reaction** — Oil + Methanol + NaOH catalyst at 60–70 °C for 1–2 hours.
3. **Separation** — Biodiesel (top layer) separates from glycerol (bottom layer) in a settling tank.
4. **Purification** — Water washing to remove residual catalyst and soap.
5. **Drying** — Remove water by vacuum drying.
6. **Quality Testing** — Check viscosity, flash point, cetane number, acid value, water content as per **ASTM D6751** or **EN 14214** standards.

**Effect on Fuel Properties:** Transesterification **reduces viscosity** from ~40 cSt (raw oil) to ~4–5 cSt (biodiesel), comparable to diesel (~3 cSt). It also improves volatility and cetane number while retaining the oxygen content that aids clean combustion.

**(b) Merits and Demerits of Biodiesel [7 Marks]**

**Merits:**
1. **Renewable** — produced from vegetable oils; closed CO₂ cycle.
2. **Biodegradable** and non-toxic.
3. **High cetane number (~55–60)** → better combustion quality than diesel.
4. **Excellent lubricity** → reduces engine wear; extends engine life.
5. **Compatible** with existing diesel engines up to B20 blend with no major modification.
6. **Lower emissions** of CO, HC, and particulate matter.

**Demerits:**
1. **Higher viscosity** (even after esterification) → cold-flow problems, injector fouling in cold climates.
2. **Oxidation instability** — shelf life only ~6 months due to unsaturated fatty acid chains.
3. **Solvent effect** — degrades rubber hoses and seals in older engines; requires replacement with FKM/FKM-Viton seals.
4. **Higher NOx emissions** due to high oxygen content and adiabatic flame temperature.
5. **Lower energy density** (~9% less than diesel) → slightly higher fuel consumption.
6. **Higher cost** than fossil diesel (without subsidies).
7. **Food vs. fuel issue** — competes with edible oil supply.
8. **Glycerol disposal** — by-product needs further processing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Costly Mistake):** Many students state "biodiesel produces more NOx than diesel" without explaining the *reason* (oxygen content + higher adiabatic flame temperature + prompt-NOx mechanism). Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for incomplete technical reasoning.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**Q: Compare CNG, LPG, LNG, and Hydrogen as alternate gaseous fuels for automotive applications. Discuss their relative advantages in terms of energy density, storage, safety, and emissions.** **[14 Marks, Module 2]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Analysis [7 Marks]**

| Parameter | CNG | LPG | LNG | Hydrogen |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|----------|
| Composition | CH₄ (85–95%) | C₃H₈ + C₄H₁₀ | CH₄ (liquid) | H₂ |
| Storage State | Gas @ 200–250 bar | Liquid @ 7–8 bar | Cryogenic liquid @ –162 °C | Gas @ 350–700 bar or cryo liquid |
| Energy Density (MJ/kg) | 50 | 46 | 50 | 142 |
| Energy Density (MJ/m³) | ~9 (at 200 bar) | ~25,000 (liquid) | ~22,000 (liquid) | ~10 (at 700 bar) |
| Octane Number | ~120 | ~100 | ~120 | 130+ |
| CO₂ (vs gasoline) | –25% | –20% | –25% | 0% (TTW) |
| Refueling Time | 4–6 min | 3–5 min | 5–10 min | 3–5 min |

**(b) Detailed Discussion [7 Marks]**

**CNG** has the best safety profile among gaseous fuels (lighter than air, disperses on leakage) and is the most widely adopted for urban buses in India. Its high octane allows lean burn with high thermal efficiency. However, the very low volumetric energy density demands bulky high-pressure cylinders.

**LPG** offers the advantage of liquid-phase storage at low pressure, making it compatible with existing gasoline vehicle platforms via simple retrofit kits. It has the highest volumetric energy density among these four fuels, but its heavier-than-air property creates a **safety hazard in confined spaces** (tunnels, garages).

**LNG** is essentially a logistic solution for long-distance transport of natural gas where pipelines are uneconomical. Its cryogenic storage is impractical for light passenger vehicles, but it is the **emerging fuel for heavy-duty trucks and maritime shipping**.

**Hydrogen** is the **cleanest fuel** with the highest mass energy density and zero carbon emissions. It enables fuel cell powertrains with 50–60% efficiency. However, it is hampered by **storage difficulties, high cost of production, infrastructure scarcity, and safety concerns** (wide flammability limit 4–75%, embrittlement of tanks).

**Verdict:**
- **Urban Light Duty:** CNG > LPG > H₂
- **Heavy Duty / Long Haul:** LNG > CNG > H₂
- **Future Zero-Emission:** H₂ (FCEV) > BEV (electricity vs hydrogen)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not write "H₂ is the cleanest fuel" without adding "at point of use" or "tank-to-wheel." Most hydrogen is currently produced from natural gas (grey H₂), so its *well-to-wheel* carbon footprint is comparable to gasoline. Examiners want this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 2 – Merits & Demerits):**

- **CNG:** High octane (120) + clean burning, but **low volumetric density** → bulky cylinders.
- **LPG:** Liquid at low pressure → easy retrofit, but **heavier than air** → explosion risk.
- **LNG:** Best for **heavy trucks/ships**, needs **cryogenic tanks**, boil-off gas loss.
- **Hydrogen:** **142 MJ/kg** (highest), zero CO₂ at use, but **hydrogen embrittlement** and 700 bar storage.
- **Ethanol:** **E10/E20/E85** blends; **hygroscopic** and corrosive; **food vs. fuel** issue.
- **Methanol:** Toxic (causes blindness); good for **direct methanol fuel cells**.
- **Biodiesel:** **Transesterification** → FAME; **cetane 55–60**; **cold-flow problem**; **rubber seal attack**.
- **EV (BEV):** Zero tailpipe emission; **range anxiety**; **battery cost** main hurdle.
- **HEV:** **20–35% better fuel economy**; regenerative braking recovers **125 kJ per stop** (1500 kg @ 60 km/h).
- **Solar PV:** **Intermittent** and **low power density** (100–200 W/m²); needs storage.

**Universal Merit / Demerit Anchors for Any Fuel:**

- **Renewable** = merit
- **High energy density** = merit
- **Clean tailpipe emissions** = merit
- **Wide availability** = merit
- **High capital cost** = demerit
- **Storage/safety issue** = demerit
- **Infrastructure dependency** = demerit

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU ESE:** For 14-mark questions, always follow this 4-part structure: (1) Production/availability, (2) Energy properties with numbers, (3) Merits in bullets, (4) Demerits in bullets, (5) Application areas. This structure alone can add 2–3 marks even on unfamiliar fuels.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biofuels - Biodiesel and Biogas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Biofuels - Biodiesel and Biogas ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Biofuels — The Foundation **Biofuels** are liquid, gaseous, or solid fuels produced from **biomass** — recently living organic matter or organic waste. They are categorized by their generation technology and source: | Generation | Source | Examples | |------------|--------|----------| | 1st Gen | Food crops (sugar, starch, vegetable oil) | Ethanol from sugarcane, Biodiesel from soybean | | 2nd Gen | Non-food lignocellulosic biomass, waste | Cellulosic ethanol, Biogas from MSW | | 3rd Gen | Algae | Algal biodiesel, Algal biogas | | 4th Gen | Genetically engineered organisms | Engineered cyanobacteria fuels | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Focus (Module 2):** The two most commercially significant biofuels for **automotive and stationary power** are **Biodiesel** (liquid, diesel replacement) and **Biogas** (gaseous, CNG/PNG replacement). Both are derived from biomass but through fundamentally different chemical pathways. --- ### 1.2 Biodiesel — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *Biodiesel is a mixture of mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids derived from vegetable oils, animal fats, or recycled grease through a chemical process called **transesterification** with a short-chain alcohol (methanol/ethanol) in the presence of a catalyst (acid/base/enzyme).* In plain English: Biodiesel is **"de-greased" cooking oil**. Take any oil — sunflower, coconut, used fryer oil — react it with methanol, and out comes a fuel that looks and smells similar to mineral diesel but burns cleaner. > **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of biodiesel as the "lighter cousin" of vegetable oil. Pure vegetable oil is too thick (viscous) to flow easily in an engine — like cold honey. Transesterification is the chemical process that "thins" the oil by chopping the big fat molecules into smaller pieces, making the fuel behave just like diesel in a compression-ignition engine. **Key Reaction:** *Triglyceride + 3 Methanol → 3 Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME) + Glycerol* --- ### 1.3 Biogas — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *Biogas is a flammable gaseous mixture primarily composed of **methane (CH₄, 50–70 %)** and **carbon dioxide (CO₂, 30–40 %)**, with trace amounts of H₂S, N₂, water vapor, and siloxanes, produced by the **anaerobic digestion (biochemical decomposition in the absence of oxygen)** of organic biomass by a consortium of microorganisms.* In plain English: Biogas is **"swamp gas"** — the bubbles that emerge from the bottom of a stagnant pond when you stir it. Nature has been producing it for billions of years; we just capture it in tanks. > **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a cow's stomach. Cows cannot digest grass on their own — billions of microbes living inside their four stomach chambers do it for them, releasing methane as a byproduct. A biogas digester is essentially a **"mechanical cow's stomach"** — a sealed, oxygen-free tank where the same microbes digest our kitchen waste, sewage, or farm manure, and we collect the methane they burp out. **Key Reaction:** *Organic Waste + Microbes (no O₂) → CH₄ + CO₂ + H₂S + Digestate* --- ### 1.4 Why These Two Biofuels Matter for KTU > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Outcome Mapping (CO₂):** After studying this topic, you must be able to: > - Explain the **chemistry** of biodiesel (transesterification) and biogas (anaerobic digestion). > - Compare fuel **properties** with conventional petroleum fuels. > - Describe the **production process flow** and identify engineering bottlenecks. > - Justify the **suitability** of each fuel for IC engines and stationary applications. **Standard Metrics to Remember:** - **Calorific Value of Biodiesel:** ≈ **37–40 MJ/kg** - **Calorific Value of Biogas:** ≈ **18–26 MJ/m³** (depending on CH₄ %) - **Cetane Number of Biodiesel:** ≈ **48–60** (vs. diesel ≈ 45–55) - **Methane Density (STP):** ≈ **0.717 kg/m³** ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Biodiesel — Transesterification Chemistry The core reaction is **transesterification** (also called *alcoholysis*). It occurs in three reversible steps, each replacing one fatty acid chain with a methyl group: **Step 1:** Triglyceride + Methanol → Diglyceride + FAME **Step 2:** Diglyceride + Methanol → Monoglyceride + FAME **Step 3:** Monoglyceride + Methanol → Glycerol + FAME **Overall:** $$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{Catalyst}]{} 3\,\text{R-COOCH}_3 + \text{Glycerol}$$ **Process Variables (Why each matters):** - **Molar Ratio (Alcohol : Oil):** Stoichiometric ratio is **3:1**, but in practice **6:1** is used to drive equilibrium toward ester formation. Too much methanol → higher separation cost. - **Catalyst Type:** - *Alkali (NaOH, KOH, NaOCH₃):* Fast (≈ 1 h), cheap, but sensitive to **Free Fatty Acids (FFA > 2 %)** → saponification (soap formation, wasted oil). - *Acid (H₂SO₄):* Slower, but tolerates higher FFA; useful for low-grade waste oils. - *Enzyme (Lipase):* Eco-friendly, reusable, but expensive and slow. - *Heterogeneous (CaO, MgO):* Reusable, no soap, but slower kinetics. - **Reaction Temperature:** Typically **60–65 °C** (just below methanol boiling point of 64.7 °C). - **Reaction Time:** **1–4 hours** for alkali-catalyzed process. - **Mixing Intensity:** Mass transfer limited; vigorous stirring essential. **Why transesterification is needed:** Pure vegetable oil has viscosity **10–20× higher** than diesel. The esterification cuts molecular weight by **≈ 1/3**, drops viscosity to **≈ 4–5 mm²/s** (vs. diesel 2–3 mm²/s), and produces a fuel that atomizes properly in a diesel injector. --- ### 2.2 Biodiesel Feedstocks | Category | Examples | Oil Yield (L/ha) | Notes | |----------|----------|------------------|-------| | Edible oils | Soybean, Sunflower, Rapeseed, Palm | 450–6,000 | Food vs. fuel conflict | | Non-edible | Jatropha, Karanja, Mahua, Neem | 800–2,500 | India's priority feedstocks | | Waste oils | Used cooking oil (UCO), Animal tallow, Yellow grease | Variable | Low cost, ethical | > [!IMPORTANT] > **India-Specific Note:** KTU frequently tests **Jatropha curcas** as a biodiesel feedstock because it grows on marginal land (not competing with food), has ≈ **30–40 % oil content in seeds**, and is drought-resistant. The **National Biodiesel Mission (2003)** identified Jatropha as the primary feedstock. --- ### 2.3 Biogas — Anaerobic Digestion (AD) Pathway Anaerobic digestion is performed by **four sequential microbial communities** working in symbiosis: 1. **Hydrolysis:** Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) → Monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids). *Slowest step for particulate feedstocks.* 2. **Acidogenesis:** Monomers → Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs) + Alcohols + CO₂ + H₂. *Fastest step; risk of acidification if unbalanced.* 3. **Acetogenesis:** VFAs + Alcohols → Acetic acid + H₂ + CO₂. *Performed by acetogenic bacteria.* 4. **Methanogenesis:** Acetate + H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ + H₂O. *Most sensitive step; archaea are easily poisoned by O₂, ammonia, or H₂S.* **Overall Stoichiometry (Glucose Model):** $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 3\,\text{CH}_4 + 3\,\text{CO}_2$$ **Buswell Equation (General Formula for CₙHₐOᵦ Organic Matter):** $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2$$ This is the **single most tested formula** in KTU biogas questions. --- ### 2.4 Biogas Process Parameters | Parameter | Mesophilic Range | Thermophilic Range | Why It Matters | |-----------|------------------|--------------------|----------------| | Temperature | **30–40 °C** | **50–60 °C** | Affects microbe growth rate | | Retention Time | 20–40 days | 10–20 days | Longer = larger digester cost | | pH | **6.8–7.4** | **7.0–7.5** | Methanogens die below pH 6.5 | | C/N Ratio | **20:1 to 30:1** | Same | < 20 → ammonia inhibition; > 35 → N deficiency | | Organic Loading Rate (OLR) | 1–4 kg VS/m³/day | 2–6 kg VS/m³/day | Higher OLR → VFA accumulation | | Moisture (for solid AD) | 50–80 % TS | Same | Required for microbial mobility | --- ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application | |---|--------------------|-----------|-------|-------------| | 1 | Biodiesel yield | $Y = \frac{m_{\text{ester}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Process efficiency | | 2 | Theoretical methanol requirement | $m_{\text{MeOH}} = m_{\text{oil}} \times \frac{3 \times 32}{M_{\text{oil}}}$ | kg | Stoichiometric calc | | 3 | Methane fraction in biogas | $f_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{n/2 - a/8 + b/4}{n - a/4 - b/2}$ | — | Buswell analysis | | 4 | Calorific value of biogas | $CV_{\text{biogas}} = f_{\text{CH}_4} \times 35.8\,\text{MJ/m}^3$ | MJ/m³ | Energy content | | 5 | Energy content of CH₄ | 35.8 MJ/m³ @ NTP | — | Reference | | 6 | Saponification value | $SV = \frac{56.1 \times V_{\text{KOH}} \times N_{\text{KOH}}}{m_{\text{oil}}}$ | mg KOH/g | Avg. molecular weight | | 7 | Acid value | $AV = \frac{56.1 \times V_{\text{KOH}} \times N_{\text{KOH}}}{m_{\text{oil}}}$ | mg KOH/g | Free fatty acid check | | 8 | Iodine value | $IV = \frac{126.9 \times (V_b - V_s) \times N_{\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3}}{m_{\text{oil}}}$ | g I₂/100g | Unsaturation measure | | 9 | Kinetic growth (Monod) | $\mu = \mu_{\max}\,\frac{S}{K_s + S}$ | 1/h | Microbial kinetics | | 10 | Digester volume | $V_d = \frac{m_{\text{biomass}}}{\text{OLR}}$ | m³ | Sizing a biogas plant | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever a question asks "find methane composition," always reduce the molecular formula to its simplest **CₙHₐOᵦ form** (e.g., glucose C₆H₁₂O₆, ethanol C₂H₆O) and substitute directly into the Buswell equation. --- ### 2.6 Fuel Property Comparison — Diesel vs. Biodiesel vs. Biogas | Property | Petro-Diesel | Biodiesel (FAME) | Biogas | |----------|--------------|-----------------|--------| | Density (kg/m³) | 830–860 | 860–900 | 1.16 (at NTP) | | Viscosity (mm²/s @ 40 °C) | 2–4 | 3.5–5 | — (gas) | | Calorific value | 42–45 MJ/kg | 37–40 MJ/kg | 18–26 MJ/m³ | | Cetane number | 45–55 | 48–60 | N/A (spark ignition) | | Flash point (°C) | 60–80 | **120–170** | — | | Sulfur content | 50–500 ppm | **< 10 ppm** | H₂S removed | | Oxygen content | 0 % | **10–12 %** | — | | Stoichiometric A/F | 14.5 | **12.5** | Variable | | Octane number | 20–30 | 20–25 | **120–130** | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Methane Yield from Glucose via Buswell Equation **Problem:** A KTU question states: *"Calculate the volume of methane produced at STP from complete anaerobic digestion of 1 kg of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)."* **Given:** - Substrate: Glucose → C₆H₁₂O₆, so **n = 6, a = 12, b = 6** - Mass of glucose = 1 kg = 1000 g - Molar mass of glucose (M) = 180 g/mol - Molar volume of gas at STP = 22.4 L/mol = 0.0224 m³/mol **Step 1 — Apply Buswell Equation for CH₄ stoichiometric coefficient:** $$\text{Moles of CH}_4 \text{ per mole of glucose} = \frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute n = 6, a = 12, b = 6:** $$\frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4} = 3.0 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3.0$$ **Step 3 — Calculate moles of glucose:** $$n_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{1000\,\text{g}}{180\,\text{g/mol}} = 5.556\,\text{mol}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate moles of CH₄ produced:** $$n_{\text{CH}_4} = 5.556 \times 3.0 = 16.667\,\text{mol}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to volume at STP:** $$V_{\text{CH}_4} = 16.667 \times 22.4 = 373.33\,\text{L} = 0.3733\,\text{m}^3$$ **Step 6 — Calculate biogas total volume** (50 % CH₄ assumption): $$V_{\text{biogas}} = \frac{V_{\text{CH}_4}}{0.5} = 0.7467\,\text{m}^3$$ **Answer:** **0.3733 m³ CH₄** per kg glucose at STP, equivalent to ≈ **0.747 m³ biogas** (assuming 50 % CH₄ fraction). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Methane Yield from Acetic Acid **Problem:** *"Determine the CH₄ and CO₂ volumes produced when 1 kg of pure acetic acid (CH₃COOH) is anaerobically digested."* **Given:** - Acetic acid = C₂H₄O₂, so **n = 2, a = 4, b = 2** - Molar mass = 60 g/mol **Step 1 — Apply Buswell for CH₄:** $$\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4} = \frac{2}{2} - \frac{4}{8} + \frac{2}{4} = 1.0 - 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0$$ **Step 2 — Apply Buswell for CO₂:** $$\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4} = 1.0 + 0.5 - 0.5 = 1.0$$ **Step 3 — Moles of acetic acid:** $$n_{\text{acid}} = \frac{1000}{60} = 16.667\,\text{mol}$$ **Step 4 — Volumes:** $$V_{\text{CH}_4} = 16.667 \times 22.4 = 373.33\,\text{L}$$ $$V_{\text{CO}_2} = 16.667 \times 22.4 = 373.33\,\text{L}$$ **Step 5 — Ratio & Volume Composition:** $$\text{CH}_4 : \text{CO}_2 = 1 : 1 \rightarrow 50\,\% : 50\,\%$$ **Reaction:** $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2$ **Sanity Check:** This matches the empirical observation that pure acetate digestion gives 50/50 biogas — **a very common KTU viva question.** --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Biodiesel Yield Calculation **Problem:** *"500 kg of Jatropha oil (M = 870 g/mol average) is reacted with methanol at 6:1 molar ratio in the presence of NaOH. If conversion efficiency is 96 %, calculate: (a) theoretical yield of biodiesel, (b) methanol required, (c) glycerol byproduct."* **Given:** - Oil mass = 500 kg; M_oil = 870 g/mol - Molar ratio MeOH : Oil = 6 : 1 - Efficiency η = 0.96 - M_FAME (methyl oleate) ≈ 296 g/mol; M_glycerol = 92 g/mol **Step 1 — Moles of oil:** $$n_{\text{oil}} = \frac{500{,}000}{870} = 574.71\,\text{mol} = 0.5747\,\text{kmol}$$ **Step 2 — Theoretical moles of FAME (3 per oil molecule):** $$n_{\text{FAME,theor}} = 3 \times 574.71 = 1724.14\,\text{mol}$$ **Step 3 — Theoretical biodiesel mass:** $$m_{\text{FAME,theor}} = 1724.14 \times 296 = 510{,}345\,\text{g} \approx 510.3\,\text{kg}$$ **Step 4 — Actual biodiesel yield:** $$m_{\text{FAME,actual}} = 510.3 \times 0.96 = 489.9\,\text{kg}$$ **Step 5 — Methanol required (theoretical, 3 mol/mol oil):** $$n_{\text{MeOH,theor}} = 3 \times 574.71 = 1724.14\,\text{mol}$$ $$m_{\text{MeOH,theor}} = 1724.14 \times 32 = 55{,}172\,\text{g} = 55.17\,\text{kg}$$ **Step 6 — Methanol required (practical, 6:1 ratio):** $$n_{\text{MeOH,pract}} = 6 \times 574.71 = 3448.28\,\text{mol}$$ $$m_{\text{MeOH,pract}} = 3448.28 \times 32 = 110{,}345\,\text{g} \approx 110.3\,\text{kg}$$ **Step 7 — Glycerol byproduct (1 mol glycerol per mol oil):** $$m_{\text{glycerol}} = 574.71 \times 92 = 52{,}873\,\text{g} \approx 52.87\,\text{kg}$$ **Answer Summary:** - Biodiesel yield = **489.9 kg** - Methanol required (practical) = **110.3 kg** - Glycerol byproduct = **52.87 kg** --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Biofuel Application | Decision Criterion | Use Biodiesel When… | Use Biogas When… | |--------------------|---------------------|------------------| | Existing engine | Compression-ignition (diesel) | Spark-ignition (petrol/CNG retrofitted) | | Feedstock available | Vegetable oil, UCO, animal fat | Wet organic waste, sewage, manure | | Energy density need | High (transport) | Moderate (stationary power, cooking) | | Storage | Easy (liquid, ambient) | Difficult (pressurized cylinder or pipeline) | | Capital cost | Medium (reactors, settling) | Low-Medium (digester, gas holder) | | By-product value | Glycerol (pharma) | Digestate (fertilizer) | | Indian context | Rural transport, gensets | **Rural household cooking** (dominant use) | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Biodiesel Production Process Flow (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feedstock Oil
Vegetable or Waste Oil] --> B[Pre-treatment
Degumming and Dewaxing] B --> C[Acid Esterification
H2SO4 Catalyst
FFA Reduction] C --> D[Base Transesterification
NaOH or KOH
Methanol 6 to 1 Ratio] D --> E[Phase Separation
Gravity Settler] E --> F[Biodiesel Layer
FAME] E --> G[Glycerol Layer
Crude Glycerol] F --> H[Water Washing
Remove Soap and Catalyst] H --> I[Drying
Vacuum or Hot Air] I --> J[Final Biodiesel
ASTM D6751 Grade] G --> K[Glycerol Purification
Distillation Column] K --> L[Pharma Grade Glycerol] J --> M[Storage Tank] M --> N[Blending with Diesel
B20 or B100] ``` ### 4.2 Biogas Plant — Anaerobic Digester Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Biomass Feedstock
Manure or Waste] --> B[Substrate Preparation
Mixing and Size Reduction] B --> C[Digester Tank
Sealed Anaerobic Chamber] C --> D[Stage 1 Hydrolysis
Complex Polymers to Monomers] D --> E[Stage 2 Acidogenesis
Monomers to VFAs] E --> F[Stage 3 Acetogenesis
VFAs to Acetate and H2] F --> G[Stage 4 Methanogenesis
Acetate to CH4 and CO2] G --> H[Gas Collection Dome
Gas Holder] H --> I[Purification Unit
H2S Scrubber and CO2 Removal] I --> J[Compressed Biogas
CBG Storage] J --> K[End Use
Cooking or Engine Fuel] C --> L[Digestate Outlet
Liquid and Solid Fraction] L --> M[Composting Unit] M --> N[Organic Fertilizer
Field Application] ``` ### 4.3 Transesterification Reaction Mechanism (Three-Step Block) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Triglyceride] -->|Step 1
Methanol Attack| B[Diglyceride plus FAME 1] B -->|Step 2
Methanol Attack| C[Monoglyceride plus FAME 2] C -->|Step 3
Methanol Attack| D[Glycerol plus FAME 3] A -. Stoichiometry .-> A D --> E[Final Products
3 FAME Molecules plus 1 Glycerol] ``` ### 4.4 Comparative Application Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Biofuels] --> B[Biodiesel] A --> C[Biogas] B --> B1[Transport Sector
Diesel Engine Replacement] B --> B2[Power Generation
Diesel Gensets] B --> B3[Domestic Heating
Industrial Boilers] C --> C1[Domestic Cooking
Rural Households India] C --> C2[Electricity Generation
Gas Engines and Microturbines] C --> C3[Compressed Biogas CBG
Vehicle Fuel under SATAT] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Diagram Note:** Always label each block with **mass/energy flow direction** and mention **operating conditions** (T, P) at key units. In ESE answers, even a hand-drawn block diagram with proper labels scores 2–3 marks out of 7. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define biodiesel. List any four physical properties that differentiate it from petro-diesel.* **Model Answer:** Biodiesel is mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids produced by transesterification of vegetable oils or animal fats with methanol. Four differentiating properties: 1. **Higher viscosity (3.5–5 mm²/s)** compared to diesel (2–4 mm²/s). 2. **Higher flash point (120–170 °C)** — safer to handle and store. 3. **Lower calorific value (≈ 37–40 MJ/kg)** vs. diesel (42–45 MJ/kg) → slightly higher fuel consumption. 4. **Oxygen content (10–12 %)** ensures more complete combustion, lower CO and particulate emissions, but slightly higher NOx. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the Buswell equation. What is its significance in biogas engineering?* **Model Answer:** The Buswell equation gives the **stoichiometric yield of methane and CO₂** from any organic substrate of formula CₙHₐOᵦ: $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2$$ **Significance:** 1. Predicts **theoretical CH₄ yield** from any biomass without experimentation. 2. Helps **size digesters** based on feed composition. 3. Computes **CO₂ fraction**, which determines the energy density and upgrading requirements of the biogas. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe the **transesterification process** for biodiesel production with a neat labeled flow diagram. Explain the role of (i) methanol, (ii) NaOH catalyst, and (iii) the 6:1 molar ratio in achieving high ester yield. **(b)** *(7 marks)* For a Jatropha oil having **saponification value = 192 mg KOH/g** and **acid value = 4 mg KOH/g**, determine: (i) Average molecular weight of the oil. (ii) Theoretical mass of methanol required to convert **1000 kg** of this oil completely. (iii) Volume of glycerol produced (density = 1.26 g/cm³), assuming complete conversion. --- ### **Solution to Question A:** **(a) Transesterification Process Description (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Pre-treated oil (FFA < 2 %) is heated to **60 °C** in a stirred batch reactor. **Step 2 (1 mark):** Methanol + NaOH (≈ 1 % w/w oil) are mixed separately to form **sodium methoxide (CH₃ONa)**, the active catalyst. **Step 3 (2 marks):** Sodium methoxide solution is added to oil at **6:1 methanol:oil molar ratio** under vigorous stirring at **60–65 °C for 1–2 hours**. **Step 4 (1 mark):** Reaction mixture is transferred to a **settling tank**. By gravity, two layers separate — upper **FAME (biodiesel)** and lower **crude glycerol**. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Biodiesel is **water-washed** to remove soap/catalyst, dried, and tested against **ASTM D6751 / EN 14214** specifications. **Step 6 (1 mark):** Roles: - *Methanol:* Provides –OCH₃ group; 6:1 ratio shifts equilibrium toward ester side (Le Chatelier). - *NaOH:* Generates CH₃O⁻ nucleophile; attacks carbonyl carbon of triglyceride. - *6:1 ratio:* Excess compensates for methanol lost to glycerol layer and pushes equilibrium forward. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Molecular Weight from Saponification Value:** $$\text{SV} = \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{M_{\text{oil}}}$$ $$192 = \frac{168{,}300}{M_{\text{oil}}}$$ $$M_{\text{oil}} = \frac{168{,}300}{192} = 876.6\,\text{g/mol}$$ > **(1.5 marks)** **(ii) Theoretical Methanol Required:** $$n_{\text{oil}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{876.6} = 1140.7\,\text{mol}$$ Stoichiometric methanol = **3 mol per mol oil**: $$n_{\text{MeOH}} = 3 \times 1140.7 = 3422.0\,\text{mol}$$ $$m_{\text{MeOH}} = 3422.0 \times 32 = 109{,}504\,\text{g} \approx 109.5\,\text{kg}$$ > **(2.5 marks)** **(iii) Glycerol Volume:** $$n_{\text{glycerol}} = n_{\text{oil}} = 1140.7\,\text{mol}$$ $$m_{\text{glycerol}} = 1140.7 \times 92 = 104{,}944\,\text{g} = 104.94\,\text{kg}$$ $$V_{\text{glycerol}} = \frac{104{,}944}{1.26} = 83{,}289\,\text{cm}^3 = 83.29\,\text{L}$$ > **(3 marks)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** *(7 marks)* With a labeled block diagram, describe the **four stages of anaerobic digestion**. What are the operational conditions (pH, temperature, retention time) for mesophilic digesters? **(b)** *(7 marks)* A community biogas plant processes **200 kg/day of kitchen waste** with average composition **C₁₀H₁₈O₅** (moisture = 70 %). Calculate using the Buswell equation: (i) Daily CH₄ volume at STP. (ii) Daily CO₂ volume at STP. (iii) Calorific value of the biogas produced per day (assume CH₄ CV = 35.8 MJ/m³). --- ### **Solution to Question B:** **(a) Four Stages of Anaerobic Digestion (7 marks):** **Stage 1 — Hydrolysis (2 marks):** Polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) → Monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids). Carried out by **extracellular enzymes** (amylases, proteases, lipases). *Rate-limiting for particulate wastes like food scraps.* **Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (2 marks):** Monomers → Volatile fatty acids (acetic, propionic, butyric), alcohols, H₂, CO₂. Performed by **acidogenic bacteria**. *Fastest step; risk of pH drop if VFAs accumulate.* **Stage 3 — Acetogenesis (1 mark):** VFAs + alcohols → Acetate + H₂ + CO₂. Performed by **acetogenic bacteria**. Syntrophic with methanogens that consume H₂ (keeping partial pressure low). **Stage 4 — Methanogenesis (2 marks):** Acetate → CH₄ + CO₂ (70 % of CH₄) H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ + H₂O (30 % of CH₄) Performed by **methanogenic archaea**. *Most sensitive step; killed by O₂, ammonia, H₂S.* **Mesophilic Conditions (extra credit):** - Temperature: **30–40 °C** (optimum 35 °C) - pH: **6.8–7.4** - Retention Time: **20–40 days** - C/N ratio: **20:1 to 30:1** --- **(b) Buswell Equation Calculation (7 marks):** **Given:** C₁₀H₁₈O₅ → **n = 10, a = 18, b = 5** - Wet waste = 200 kg/day; Moisture = 70 % → **Dry solids = 60 kg/day** - Molar mass of substrate = (10×12) + (18×1) + (5×16) = **218 g/mol** **Step 1 — Moles of substrate per day:** $$n_{\text{substrate}} = \frac{60{,}000}{218} = 275.23\,\text{mol/day}$$ **Step 2 — CH₄ moles per mole substrate (Buswell):** $$n_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4} = \frac{10}{2} - \frac{18}{8} + \frac{5}{4} = 5 - 2.25 + 1.25 = 4.0$$ > **(1.5 marks)** **Step 3 — Daily CH₄ moles:** $$N_{\text{CH}_4} = 275.23 \times 4 = 1100.92\,\text{mol/day}$$ > **(1 mark)** **Step 4 — Daily CH₄ volume at STP:** $$V_{\text{CH}_4} = 1100.92 \times 22.4\,\text{L} = 24{,}660.6\,\text{L} = 24.66\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$$ > **(1.5 marks)** **Step 5 — CO₂ moles per mole substrate:** $$n_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4} = 5 + 2.25 - 1.25 = 6.0$$ > **(1 mark)** **Step 6 — Daily CO₂ volume:** $$V_{\text{CO}_2} = 275.23 \times 6 \times 22.4 = 36{,}990.9\,\text{L} = 36.99\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$$ > **(1 mark)** **Step 7 — Daily energy output:** $$E_{\text{biogas}} = 24.66 \times 35.8 = 882.8\,\text{MJ/day}$$ > **(1 mark)** **Sanity Check:** Total biogas = 24.66 + 36.99 = **61.65 m³/day** with CH₄ fraction = 24.66/61.65 = **40 %** — typical for food waste with high oxygen content. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Before Writing Exam** > 1. **Forgetting moisture correction:** Many students calculate Buswell on wet mass, leading to 30–50 % overestimation. Always extract **dry organic matter** first. > 2. **Confusing SV with AV:** Saponification value uses **3 × MW of KOH (56.1)** in the numerator; acid value uses **1 × 56.1**. Wrong factor = wrong MW = wrong methanol quantity. > 3. **Using STP vs. NTP interchangeably:** STP = 22.4 L/mol, NTP = 22.4 L/mol (both 0 °C, 1 atm). For 25 °C, 1 atm, use **24.5 L/mol**. KTU usually expects STP. > 4. **Methanol stoichiometry:** Stoichiometric is 3:1, but **practical is 6:1**. Examiners specifically test whether you know the difference. > 5. **Process diagram must have arrows AND labels** — not just boxes. Direction of flow, operating unit, and conditions are mandatory for full marks. > 6. **Free Fatty Acid (FFA) threshold:** If FFA > 2 %, alkali catalyst fails → soap forms. KTU loves this trap question. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember > **Biodiesel — Rapid Revision Checklist:** > - Definition: Mono-alkyl esters via **transesterification**. > - Reaction: Triglyceride + 3 MeOH → 3 FAME + Glycerol. > - Process: 6:1 methanol:oil ratio, 60 °C, NaOH/KOH catalyst, 1–2 h. > - Best Indian feedstock: **Jatropha** (non-edible, drought-resistant). > - Properties: CV ≈ 38 MJ/kg, CN ≈ 50, Flash > 120 °C. > - Oxygen (10–12 %) lowers CO/PM emissions but raises NOx. > - B20 = 20 % biodiesel + 80 % diesel (common blend). > - Saponification value: $M_{\text{oil}} = \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV}$. > - ASTM D6751 / EN 14214 — international standards. > **Biogas — Rapid Revision Checklist:** > - Definition: CH₄ (50–70 %) + CO₂ (30–40 %) from anaerobic digestion. > - Four stages: Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis. > - Methanogens are **archaea** — most sensitive step. > - Mesophilic: 30–40 °C, 20–40 day retention, pH 6.8–7.4. > - Buswell equation gives CH₄ and CO₂ moles from CₙHₐOᵦ substrate. > - CV of biogas: ≈ 18–26 MJ/m³ (CV of pure CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³). > - India's **SATAT scheme** targets CBG (compressed biogas) production. > - Byproduct digestate = **valuable organic fertilizer** (N, P, K). > - Per kg glucose → ≈ 0.373 m³ CH₄ at STP. > **Golden Formulas for Last-Minute Revision:** > > $$\text{Buswell CH}_4 = \frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}$$ > > $$\text{Buswell CO}_2 = \frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}$$ > > $$M_{\text{oil}} = \frac{3 \times 56{,}100}{SV}$$ > > $$CV_{\text{biogas}} = f_{\text{CH}_4} \times 35.8\,\text{MJ/m}^3$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Mantra:** Always identify the substrate formula, extract the dry mass, apply Buswell, then compute gas volume at STP. For biodiesel, identify feedstock → oil → transesterification → FAME. Mention **environmental benefits, FFA limits, and standards** for full marks.

topics.title: Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Production" in Alternate Fuels?

In the context of **OEMUT615 – Alternate Fuels and Energy Systems**, the term **"Production"** refers to the **industrial, biochemical, and thermochemical processes used to convert raw feedstocks (biomass, agricultural waste, organic matter, or inorganic compounds) into usable alternative fuels** such as **biodiesel, ethanol, biogas, methanol, and hydrogen**. It encompasses the upstream manufacturing, feedstock preparation, conversion chemistry, purification, and yield-optimization techniques that make non-conventional fuels commercially viable substitutes for petroleum-derived gasoline and diesel.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> "Production of alternate fuels encompasses the complete chain of chemical and biological conversion pathways — from feedstock selection and pre-treatment to transesterification, fermentation, anaerobic digestion, gasification, and reforming — required to synthesize fuels that can directly replace or blend with conventional fossil fuels."

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Kitchen to Fuel Pump" Concept

Think of alternate fuel production like a **kitchen recipe scaled to industrial size**:

- **Feedstock = Raw Ingredients** (vegetable oil, sugarcane, food waste)
- **Pre-treatment = Washing & Chopping** (removing impurities, crushing)
- **Conversion Process = Cooking Method** (frying, fermenting, baking — analogous to transesterification, fermentation, pyrolysis)
- **Purification = Straining & Filtering** (washing, drying, distillation)
- **Final Fuel = Ready-to-Serve Dish** (biodiesel, ethanol, biogas ready for engines)

Just as the same ingredient (potato) can be cooked differently to make french fries or mashed potatoes, the **same biomass feedstock can be converted by different processes** to produce different alternate fuels. The "recipe" (process conditions: temperature, pressure, catalyst, residence time) determines the final product and yield.

## 1.3 The Big Picture — Five Major Alternate Fuels & Their Production Routes

| Alternate Fuel | Primary Feedstock | Core Production Process | Key Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Biodiesel** | Vegetable oils, animal fats, used cooking oil | **Transesterification** | Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME) |
| **Ethanol** | Sugarcane, corn, molasses, cellulosic biomass | **Fermentation + Distillation** | Bio-ethanol (C₂H₅OH) |
| **Biogas** | Animal dung, sewage, food waste, landfill | **Anaerobic Digestion** | Methane (CH₄) + CO₂ |
| **Methanol** | Coal, natural gas, biomass, CO₂ | **Steam Reforming / Gasification** | CH₃OH |
| **Hydrogen** | Water, natural gas, biomass, organic waste | **Electrolysis / Reforming / Gasification** | H₂ gas |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):** The production of **biodiesel (transesterification), ethanol (fermentation), biogas (anaerobic digestion), and hydrogen (electrolysis & reforming)** are the **highest-weightage topics** and are asked regularly in both Continuous Assessment (CA) and End Semester Examinations (ESE).

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of biodiesel** ≈ **0.88 g/cm³** at 15 °C
- **Calorific value of biodiesel** ≈ **37.2 MJ/kg**
- **Stoichiometric molar ratio (oil : methanol)** for transesterification = **1 : 3**
- **Methane content in biogas** = **55–70 %** by volume
- **Energy content of pure methane** = **55.5 MJ/kg**
- **LHV of hydrogen** = **120 MJ/kg**
- **Electrolyzer theoretical minimum voltage** = **1.23 V**
- **Conversion efficiency (sugar → ethanol)** ≈ **51 %** (theoretical maximum)
- **Biogas yield from cattle dung** ≈ **0.04–0.06 m³/kg of dry matter per day**
- **Density of ethanol** = **0.789 g/cm³**

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Biodiesel Production — Transesterification Process

**Transesterification** (also called **alcoholysis**) is the **core chemical reaction** in which a triglyceride molecule (vegetable oil/fat) reacts with a short-chain alcohol (methanol or ethanol) in the presence of a catalyst to produce **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME)** — i.e., biodiesel — and **glycerol** as a by-product.

### 2.1.1 The Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Feedstock Selection:** Crude vegetable oil (mustard, soybean, palm, jatropha, Karanja) or animal tallow or used cooking oil. Free Fatty Acid (FFA) content must be checked — if FFA > 2 %, a two-step process (esterification + transesterification) is needed.
2. **Pre-treatment:** Degumming, dewaxing, drying to remove water and phospholipids. Water is the **enemy** of transesterification (causes soap formation via saponification).
3. **Alcohol Selection:** Methanol is preferred over ethanol because:
   - Lower boiling point (64.7 °C) → easier recovery
   - Cheaper
   - Faster reaction kinetics
   - Less prone to emulsion formation
4. **Catalyst Selection:**
   - **Homogeneous base catalysts:** NaOH, KOH (most common; cheap; fast)
   - **Homogeneous acid catalysts:** H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄ (used for high-FFA oils)
   - **Heterogeneous catalysts:** CaO, MgO, zeolites (reusable, greener)
   - **Enzymatic catalysts (lipases):** Highly selective, no soap formation
5. **Reaction Conditions:** Temperature = **60–65 °C** (just below methanol boiling point), pressure = atmospheric, reaction time = **1–4 hours**, stirring = vigorous.
6. **Phase Separation:** After reaction, the mixture settles into two layers — **upper biodiesel layer** and **lower glycerol layer** (separated by gravity in a settling tank).
7. **Purification:** Biodiesel is washed with warm water to remove residual catalyst and glycerol, then dried under vacuum.

### 2.1.2 The Governing Chemistry

The overall reversible reaction can be written as:

$$ \text{Triglyceride} + 3 \, \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{catalyst}]{\text{heat}} 3 \, \text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol} $$

In reality, the reaction proceeds in **three sequential reversible steps**, where the triglyceride is converted to diglyceride, then monoglyceride, and finally to glycerol, releasing one FAME molecule at each step.

### 2.1.3 Why and How Each Parameter Matters

- **Excess methanol (6:1 to 12:1 molar ratio instead of 3:1):** Drives the equilibrium forward (Le Chatelier's principle) → higher yield.
- **Anhydrous conditions:** Water hydrolyses the ester, producing FFA → FFA + NaOH → soap (saponification) → reduces yield and complicates separation.
- **Temperature control:** Below 60 °C → slow reaction; above 65 °C → methanol evaporates, lowering molar ratio.
- **Catalyst concentration:** Typically **1 % w/w** of oil. Too little → incomplete conversion; too much → soap formation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** The molar ratio of **3:1 (oil:methanol) is stoichiometric**, but industrial practice uses **6:1 to 12:1** to drive the reaction to near-completion (> 95 % yield).

### 2.1.4 Real-World Utility

Biodiesel production is the **backbone of India's National Mission on Biodiesel (2003)** and the **SATAT (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation) scheme**. India targets **5 % biodiesel blending by 2030**. Used cooking oil (UCO) is being commercially tapped by Indian Oil under the SATAT program.

---

## 2.2 Ethanol Production — Fermentation Process

Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) for fuel use (E10, E20, E85 blends) is produced primarily by **fermentation of sugars** followed by **distillation and dehydration**.

### 2.2.1 The Process Steps

1. **Feedstock Selection:**
   - **1st Generation:** Sugarcane juice, molasses, corn starch, sugar beet
   - **2nd Generation:** Cellulosic biomass (rice straw, bagasse, wood chips) — requires pre-treatment
   - **3rd Generation:** Algae-based sugars
2. **Pre-treatment (for 2nd Gen):** Physical (chopping, milling) + Chemical (dilute acid hydrolysis) + Enzymatic (cellulase enzymes) to break down cellulose into glucose.
3. **Fermentation:** Yeast *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* converts glucose to ethanol and CO₂ in anaerobic conditions.
4. **Distillation:** Brewed liquor (≈ 8–12 % ethanol) is heated; ethanol boils at 78.4 °C and is collected as vapor and condensed.
5. **Dehydration (Molecular Sieves):** Distillation cannot exceed **95.6 % ethanol** (azeotrope with water). Molecular sieves (3Å zeolite beads) adsorb water, producing **anhydrous (100 %) ethanol**.

### 2.2.2 Governing Reaction

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{yeast}} 2 \, \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2 \, \text{CO}_2 $$

### 2.2.3 Theoretical Yield Calculation (KTU High-Yield)

- Molecular weight of glucose = **180 g/mol**
- Molecular weight of ethanol = **46 g/mol**
- Theoretical yield from 1000 kg of glucose:

$$ m_{\text{ethanol, max}} = 1000 \times \frac{2 \times 46}{180} = 511.11 \, \text{kg} $$

This confirms the **51.1 % theoretical maximum conversion** by mass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical yield** is **90–95 % of theoretical**, giving **≈ 460–485 kg of ethanol per 1000 kg of glucose**.

---

## 2.3 Biogas Production — Anaerobic Digestion

**Biogas** is a mixture of **methane (CH₄, 55–70 %)** and **carbon dioxide (CO₂, 30–45 %)** with traces of H₂S, N₂, and water vapor. It is produced by **anaerobic digestion** — the microbial breakdown of organic matter in the absence of oxygen.

### 2.3.1 The Four Stages of Anaerobic Digestion

| Stage | Microorganism | Substrate → Product | Optimum pH |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Hydrolysis** | Acidogenic bacteria | Complex polymers (carbs, proteins, lipids) → Monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) | 5–6 |
| **2. Acidogenesis** | Acidogenic bacteria | Monomers → Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs), H₂, CO₂ | 5–6 |
| **3. Acetogenesis** | Acetogenic bacteria | VFAs → Acetate, H₂, CO₂ | 6–7 |
| **4. Methanogenesis** | Methanogenic archaea | Acetate / H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ | 7–8 |

### 2.3.2 Overall Reaction

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 3 \, \text{CH}_4 + 3 \, \text{CO}_2 $$

For a generic organic waste (CxHyOz):

$$ \text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + \left(x - \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}\right) \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \left(\frac{x}{2} - \frac{y}{8} + \frac{z}{4}\right) \text{CH}_4 + \left(\frac{x}{2} + \frac{y}{8} - \frac{z}{4}\right) \text{CO}_2 $$

### 2.3.3 Key Process Parameters

- **Temperature:** Mesophilic (30–40 °C) — most common; Thermophilic (50–55 °C) — faster but less stable
- **Retention time:** 20–40 days (mesophilic)
- **C/N ratio of feedstock:** 20:1 to 30:1 (optimal)
- **pH range overall:** 6.5–7.5

---

## 2.4 Hydrogen Production — Three Core Routes

### 2.4.1 Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) — 48 % of global H₂

**Step 1 — Reforming (endothermic, 700–1000 °C):**

$$ \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3 \, \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \, \text{kJ/mol} $$

**Step 2 — Water-Gas Shift (exothermic, 200–400 °C):**

$$ \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \, \text{kJ/mol} $$

### 2.4.2 Electrolysis of Water — Green Hydrogen

$$ 2 \, \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2 \, \text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \quad E^0 = 1.23 \, \text{V (theoretical)} $$

- Practical cell voltage = **1.8–2.4 V** (due to overpotentials)
- Efficiency = **60–80 %** (HHV basis)
- Types: **Alkaline (most mature), PEM (proton exchange membrane), Solid Oxide (SOEC)**

### 2.4.3 Biomass Gasification

$$ \text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 + \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{tar} + \text{char} $$

Producer gas is cleaned and water-gas shifted to enrich H₂.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Concepts

- **India's Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP):** E20 target (20 % ethanol blending) by 2025. The distillation, dehydration, and fermentation stoichiometry directly govern plant economics.
- **SATAT Scheme:** Biodiesel plants producing 5 TPD by IOCL — transesterification yield is the financial pivot.
- **Biogas National Programme (Ministry of New & Renewable Energy):** 5 million biogas plants in rural India — anaerobic digestion design drives rural energy security.
- **National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023):** ₹19,744 crore outlay — electrolyzer efficiency and renewable electricity cost are the key engineering levers.
- **Methanol Economy (NITI Aayog):** Coal-to-methanol, biomass-to-methanol plants — reforming + gasification chemistry underpin the entire roadmap.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Biodiesel Yield from 1000 kg of Soybean Oil

### Given:
- Mass of soybean oil (triglyceride of linoleic acid predominant) = **1000 kg**
- Molar mass of triglyceride (tripalmitin C₅₁H₉₈O₆ as model) = **807 g/mol**
- Molar mass of FAME (methyl oleate C₁₉H₃₆O₂) = **296.5 g/mol**
- Molar mass of methanol = **32 g/mol**
- Molar mass of glycerol = **92 g/mol**

### Step 1 — Write the balanced reaction

$$ \text{C}_{51}\text{H}_{98}\text{O}_6 + 3 \, \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow 3 \, \text{C}_{19}\text{H}_{36}\text{O}_2 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3 $$

### Step 2 — Calculate moles of triglyceride

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{oil}} &= \frac{m_{\text{oil}}}{M_{\text{oil}}} \\
&= \frac{1000 \times 1000 \, \text{g}}{807 \, \text{g/mol}} \\
&= 1239.16 \, \text{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Stoichiometric methanol requirement

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{MeOH, stoich}} &= 3 \times n_{\text{oil}} \\
&= 3 \times 1239.16 \\
&= 3717.49 \, \text{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to mass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{MeOH, stoich}} &= n_{\text{MeOH, stoich}} \times M_{\text{MeOH}} \\
&= 3717.49 \times 32 \\
&= 118959.6 \, \text{g} \\
&\approx 118.96 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Theoretical moles of FAME produced

By stoichiometry, 1 mole of triglyceride → 3 moles of FAME:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{FAME, theoretical}} &= 3 \times n_{\text{oil}} \\
&= 3 \times 1239.16 \\
&= 3717.49 \, \text{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Theoretical mass of FAME

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{FAME, theoretical}} &= n_{\text{FAME}} \times M_{\text{FAME}} \\
&= 3717.49 \times 296.5 \\
&= 1{,}102{,}235.9 \, \text{g} \\
&\approx 1102.24 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Mass yield

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{biodiesel}} &= \frac{m_{\text{FAME}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{1102.24}{1000} \times 100\% \\
&= 110.22\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biodiesel yield > 100 %** because the triglyceride (MW = 807) accepts 3 methanol methyl groups (MW = 32 × 3 = 96) and is split into 3 FAME molecules (MW = 296.5 × 3 = 889.5) + 1 glycerol (MW = 92). Mass balance: $1102.24 + 102.96 = 1205.2$ kg of products. The extra 205.2 kg comes from the 118.96 kg of methanol + ~86 kg of water formed in glycerol.

### Step 7 — Mass of glycerol by-product

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{glycerol}} &= n_{\text{oil}} \times M_{\text{glycerol}} \\
&= 1239.16 \times 92 \\
&= 114{,}002.7 \, \text{g} \\
&\approx 114.0 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Practical yield (with 95 % conversion efficiency)

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{FAME, practical}} &= 0.95 \times 1102.24 \\
&= 1047.13 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 9 — Industrial excess methanol (using 9:1 molar ratio)

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{MeOH, industrial}} &= 9 \times 1239.16 = 11{,}152.5 \, \text{mol} \\
m_{\text{MeOH, industrial}} &= 11{,}152.5 \times 32 = 356{,}880 \, \text{g} = 356.88 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Insight:** The 9:1 ratio means you use **356.88 kg of methanol** vs. the **118.96 kg stoichiometric**. The excess methanol is recovered (≈ 90–95 %) by distillation and recycled, which is why industrial plants are economically viable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ethanol Yield from 1 Tonne of Sugarcane Molasses

### Given:
- Mass of molasses = **1000 kg**
- Total fermentable sugar (sucrose) content = **45 %** (typical Indian cane molasses)
- Fermentation efficiency (sugar → ethanol) = **90 % of theoretical**

### Step 1 — Mass of fermentable sugar

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{sugar}} &= 1000 \times 0.45 = 450 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Theoretical ethanol yield (using 51.1 % factor)

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{ethanol, theoretical}} &= 450 \times \frac{2 \times 46}{180} \\
&= 450 \times 0.5111 \\
&= 230.0 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Practical yield at 90 % efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{ethanol, practical}} &= 230.0 \times 0.90 \\
&= 207.0 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to litres (volume of ethanol)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{ethanol}} &= \frac{m_{\text{ethanol}}}{\rho_{\text{ethanol}}} = \frac{207.0}{0.789} \\
&= 262.36 \, \text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Energy content produced

Using CV of ethanol = **26.9 MJ/kg**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{ethanol}} &= 207.0 \times 26.9 \\
&= 5568.3 \, \text{MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is equivalent to ≈ **149.4 litres of gasoline** in energy terms.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Biogas Yield & Energy from a 10 m³ Anaerobic Digester

### Given:
- Volume of digester = **10 m³**
- Feedstock = **Cattle dung (dry matter)**
- Loading rate = **4 kg DM/m³/day**
- Biogas yield = **0.05 m³/kg DM/day**
- Methane fraction = **60 %**

### Step 1 — Daily dry matter input

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{DM, daily}} &= 10 \, \text{m}^3 \times 4 \, \text{kg/m}^3/\text{day} \\
&= 40 \, \text{kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Daily biogas production

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{biogas}} &= 40 \times 0.05 \\
&= 2.0 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Daily methane production

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{CH}_4} &= 2.0 \times 0.60 \\
&= 1.2 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Mass of methane (at NTP, ρ = 0.716 kg/m³)

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{CH}_4} &= 1.2 \times 0.716 \\
&= 0.859 \, \text{kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Energy output (CV of methane = 55.5 MJ/kg)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{daily}} &= 0.859 \times 55.5 \\
&= 47.69 \, \text{MJ/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equivalent to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{daily}} &= \frac{47.69}{3.6} \\
&= 13.25 \, \text{kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is enough to cook **3 meals for a family of 4** or run a **1.5 kW genset for ~9 hours**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hydrogen Production by Electrolysis (KTU Standard Numerical)

### Given:
- Electrolyzer operates at **2.0 V** cell voltage (commercial alkaline)
- Current = **1000 A**
- Operation time = **8 hours**
- Faradaic efficiency = **95 %**
- Energy efficiency (HHV basis) = **65 %**

### Step 1 — Total charge passed

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= I \times t = 1000 \times 8 \times 3600 \\
&= 2.88 \times 10^7 \, \text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Theoretical H₂ mass (using Faraday's law)

Using the equation: $m = \dfrac{Q \times M}{n \times F}$, where M(H₂) = 2.016 g/mol, n = 2, F = 96,485 C/mol:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{H}_2, \text{theoretical}} &= \frac{2.88 \times 10^7 \times 2.016}{2 \times 96{,}485} \\
&= \frac{5.806 \times 10^7}{1.9297 \times 10^5} \\
&= 300.88 \, \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Actual H₂ produced (with 95 % Faradaic efficiency)

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{H}_2, \text{actual}} &= 300.88 \times 0.95 \\
&= 285.84 \, \text{g} \\
&= 0.2858 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Energy consumed

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{in}} &= V \times I \times t = 2.0 \times 1000 \times 8 \\
&= 16{,}000 \, \text{Wh} = 16 \, \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Energy content of H₂ produced (HHV = 142 MJ/kg)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{H}_2} &= 0.2858 \times 142 \\
&= 40.58 \, \text{MJ} = 11.27 \, \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Energy efficiency (check)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{energy}} &= \frac{11.27}{16} \times 100\% \\
&= 70.4\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The HHV-based energy efficiency is slightly higher than the rated 65 % because the actual H₂ mass includes Faradaic losses already accounted for. Students are expected to know that **commercial alkaline electrolyzers operate at 60–70 % LHV efficiency**.

### Step 7 — Volume of H₂ at NTP

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{H}_2} &= \frac{m}{M} \times 22.414 = \frac{0.2858 \times 1000}{2.016} \times 22.414 \\
&= 141.77 \times 22.414 \\
&= 3177.6 \, \text{L} = 3.178 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Biodiesel Production via Transesterification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feedstock: Vegetable Oil or UCO] --> B[Pre-treatment]
    B --> B1[Degumming]
    B --> B2[Neutralization]
    B --> B3[Drying to less than 0.1 percent moisture]
    B1 --> C[FFA Check]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D{FFA less than 2 percent?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Direct Transesterification]
    D -->|No| F[Acid Pre-esterification with H2SO4]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Add Methanol and NaOH or KOH Catalyst at 60 to 65 C]
    G --> H[Stir for 1 to 4 hours at 300 to 600 rpm]
    H --> I[Gravity Settling for 8 to 12 hours]
    I --> J[Upper Layer: Biodiesel FAME]
    I --> K[Lower Layer: Crude Glycerol]
    J --> L[Water Washing at 50 C]
    L --> M[Drying under vacuum at 100 C]
    M --> N[Quality Testing: Viscosity, Flash Point, FAME Content]
    N --> O[Final Biodiesel B100]
    K --> P[Glycerol Purification]
    P --> Q[Pharma or Cosmetic Grade Glycerol]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The "FFA check" is a critical **decision point (diamond shape)** — Indian UCO typically has FFA > 5 %, mandating the acid pre-treatment route. Ignoring this is a common production-level pitfall.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Ethanol Production Process (1st Generation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Sugarcane or Molasses] --> A2[Crushing and Juice Extraction]
    A2 --> A3[Clarification and Sterilization at 90 C]
    A3 --> A4[Yeast Inoculum: Saccharomyces cerevisiae]
    A4 --> A5[Fermentation Tank: 30 C, 48 to 72 hours, Anaerobic]
    A5 --> A6[Beer: 8 to 12 percent Ethanol]
    A6 --> A7[Distillation Column: 95.6 percent Ethanol Azeotrope]
    A7 --> A8[Molecular Sieve Dehydration: 3 Angstrom Zeolite]
    A8 --> A9[Anhydrous Ethanol: 99.5 to 99.9 percent]
    A9 --> A10[Denaturation with 2 to 5 percent Petrol]
    A10 --> A11[Final Fuel Ethanol E100]
    A6 --> CO2[CO2 Byproduct: Captured and Liquefied]
    A7 --> A12[Spent Wash: Distiller Effluent for Biogas Plant]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Anaerobic Digester Stages & Microbial Community

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Organic Waste Input] --> S1[Stage 1: Hydrolysis]
    S1 --> M1[Acidogenic Bacteria]
    M1 --> P2[Monomers Sugars Amino Acids Fatty Acids]
    P2 --> S2[Stage 2: Acidogenesis]
    S2 --> M2[Acidogenic Bacteria Continued]
    M2 --> P3[Volatile Fatty Acids VFAs H2 CO2]
    P3 --> S3[Stage 3: Acetogenesis]
    S3 --> M3[Acetogenic Bacteria]
    M3 --> P4[Acetate H2 CO2]
    P4 --> S4[Stage 4: Methanogenesis]
    S4 --> M4[Methanogenic Archaea]
    M4 --> P5[Biogas: 60 percent CH4 plus 40 percent CO2]
    P4 --> P5
    S1 --> R1[Digestate: Liquid and Solid]
    S2 --> R1
    S3 --> R1
    S4 --> R1
    R1 --> R2[Nutrient Rich Biofertilizer]
    P5 --> U1[Cooking Gas Engine Genset]
    P5 --> U2[CNG Vehicle via Upgrading]
    P5 --> U3[Boiler Fuel]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the recycling arrows:** In a well-designed digester, **heat from CHP can pre-heat the incoming feedstock** to maintain mesophilic temperature (35 °C), improving digester stability and net energy output.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define transesterification. Write the balanced chemical equation for the production of biodiesel from triglyceride.**

**Model Answer:**

Transesterification is the chemical reaction in which an ester (triglyceride) reacts with a short-chain alcohol (methanol or ethanol) in the presence of a catalyst (acid, base, or enzyme) to produce fatty acid alkyl esters (biodiesel) and glycerol as a by-product.

$$ \text{Triglyceride} + 3 \, \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH/KOH, 60–65 °C}} 3 \, \text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol} $$

This reaction proceeds in three sequential reversible steps (tri- → di- → monoglyceride → glycerol), each releasing one FAME molecule.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any four process parameters that affect biogas yield in an anaerobic digester.**

**Model Answer:**

The four major process parameters affecting biogas yield are:

1. **Temperature** — Mesophilic range (30–40 °C) is optimal; thermophilic (50–55 °C) gives faster but less stable digestion.
2. **pH** — Must be maintained between **6.5 and 7.5**; methanogens are highly pH-sensitive.
3. **C/N ratio of feedstock** — Optimum is **20:1 to 30:1**; lower C/N causes ammonia toxicity; higher C/N causes nitrogen deficiency for microbes.
4. **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** — Typically **20 to 40 days** in mesophilic digesters; insufficient HRT leads to incomplete digestion and washout of methanogens.

*(Other accepted parameters: feedstock composition, mixing rate, inhibitor concentration, organic loading rate.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Describe in detail the **transesterification process** for biodiesel production. Discuss the role of catalyst, the effect of molar ratio of oil to alcohol, and the effect of free fatty acids (FFA) on the process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a transesterification reaction, **1000 kg of jatropha oil (average molecular weight = 854 g/mol)** reacts with methanol (molecular weight = 32 g/mol) using a **9:1 molar ratio** and a conversion efficiency of **92 %**. Calculate: **(i)** the mass of methanol required, **(ii)** the theoretical mass of FAME produced, and **(iii)** the actual mass of FAME obtained. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

- **Definition & overall reaction** (1 mark)
- **Three sequential steps** (1 mark)
- **Catalyst role:** NaOH/KOH homogeneous base catalyst at 1 % w/w of oil; lowers activation energy; provides alkoxide ion (CH₃O⁻) as nucleophile. (1.5 marks)
- **Molar ratio effect:** Stoichiometric is 1:3; industrial 6:1 to 12:1 (typically 9:1) is used to drive Le Chatelier equilibrium forward. Excess methanol increases yield but raises recovery cost. (1.5 marks)
- **FFA effect:** If FFA > 2 %, NaOH reacts with FFA → soap (saponification), consumes catalyst, reduces yield, causes emulsification. Solution: two-step process (acid pre-esterification with H₂SO₄ to convert FFA to FAME, then base-catalyzed transesterification). (2 marks)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks (Numerical Solution)**

**Given:**
- $m_{\text{oil}} = 1000 \, \text{kg}$, $M_{\text{oil}} = 854 \, \text{g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{MeOH}} = 32 \, \text{g/mol}$, Molar ratio oil:MeOH = 1:9
- Conversion efficiency = 92 %; assuming FAME molecular weight = 296 g/mol (methyl oleate average)

**Step 1 — Moles of oil**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{oil}} &= \frac{1000 \times 1000}{854} = 1170.96 \, \text{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of methanol at 9:1 ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{MeOH}} &= 9 \times 1170.96 = 10{,}538.64 \, \text{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of methanol required (i)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{MeOH}} &= 10{,}538.64 \times 32 = 337{,}236.5 \, \text{g} \approx 337.24 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical moles of FAME**

By stoichiometry, 1 mole of triglyceride → 3 moles of FAME:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{FAME, theoretical}} &= 3 \times 1170.96 = 3512.88 \, \text{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical mass of FAME (ii)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{FAME, theoretical}} &= 3512.88 \times 296 = 1{,}039{,}812.5 \, \text{g} \approx 1039.81 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Actual mass at 92 % conversion (iii)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{FAME, actual}} &= 1039.81 \times 0.92 = 956.63 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Mass of methanol = **337.24 kg**
- (ii) Theoretical FAME = **1039.81 kg**
- (iii) Actual FAME = **956.63 kg**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain the **four stages of anaerobic digestion** for biogas production. Discuss the role of methanogenic bacteria and the operating conditions required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A continuous stirred-tank anaerobic digester of **20 m³ capacity** is fed with **80 kg/day of dry cattle dung** (biogas yield = **0.05 m³/kg DM/day**, methane content = **65 %**). Calculate: **(i)** daily biogas production, **(ii)** daily methane volume, **(iii)** daily energy output, and **(iv)** power output if a 30 % efficient CHP genset is used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

- **Stage 1: Hydrolysis** (1.5 marks) — Acidogenic bacteria secrete extracellular enzymes (amylases, proteases, lipases) that break down complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) into soluble monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids). pH = 5–6.
- **Stage 2: Acidogenesis** (1.5 marks) — Acidogenic bacteria convert monomers into Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs — acetate, propionate, butyrate), H₂, CO₂, and alcohols. pH = 5–6.
- **Stage 3: Acetogenesis** (1.5 marks) — Acetogenic bacteria (e.g., *Syntrophomonas*) convert higher VFAs into acetate, H₂, and CO₂ — substrates for methanogens. pH = 6–7. This is the rate-limiting step in many digesters.
- **Stage 4: Methanogenesis** (1.5 marks) — Methanogenic archaea (e.g., *Methanobacterium*, *Methanosarcina*) convert acetate and H₂/CO₂ into CH₄. Strict anaerobes; highly sensitive to pH, temperature, and toxic compounds. pH = 7–8. Two main pathways: acetoclastic (≈ 70 % of CH₄) and hydrogenotrophic (≈ 30 %).
- **Operating conditions** (1 mark) — Mesophilic (35 °C) or thermophilic (55 °C); HRT 20–40 days; C/N 20:1 to 30:1; continuous mixing; absence of O₂.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks (Numerical Solution)**

**Given:** V_digester = 20 m³; $m_{DM}$ = 80 kg/day; Biogas yield = 0.05 m³/kg DM/day; CH₄ fraction = 65 %; η_CHP = 30 %; CV of CH₄ = 55.5 MJ/kg; density of CH₄ at NTP = 0.716 kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Daily biogas production (i)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{biogas}} &= 80 \times 0.05 = 4.0 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily methane volume (ii)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{CH}_4} &= 4.0 \times 0.65 = 2.6 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of methane**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{CH}_4} &= 2.6 \times 0.716 = 1.862 \, \text{kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Daily energy output (iii)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{daily}} &= 1.862 \times 55.5 = 103.34 \, \text{MJ/day} \\
&= \frac{103.34}{3.6} = 28.71 \, \text{kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Electrical power output (iv)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{electrical}} &= 28.71 \times 0.30 = 8.61 \, \text{kWh/day} \\
\text{If operated 24 h:} \quad P &= \frac{8.61}{24} = 0.359 \, \text{kW} = 359 \, \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Biogas = **4.0 m³/day**
- (ii) Methane = **2.6 m³/day**
- (iii) Energy = **103.34 MJ/day ≈ 28.71 kWh/day**
- (iv) Electrical energy = **8.61 kWh/day** (≈ 359 W continuous)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read Before Writing Your Exam**
>
> 1. **Never confuse "molar ratio" with "mass ratio"** in transesterification. Molar ratio of 9:1 means moles, not kg. Many students lose 2–3 marks by writing mass-based ratios.
> 2. **Biodiesel yield > 100 %** by mass — this is correct, NOT a calculation error. Always verify mass balance: FAME + glycerol + water formed ≈ oil + methanol consumed.
> 3. **Do not write "biogas contains methane" alone** — specify **55–70 % methane by volume** and mention CO₂ as the second major component. Examiners deduct marks for vague answers.
> 4. **For electrolysis problems, ALWAYS state the Faradaic constant** F = 96,485 C/mol — and the **n-factor** (2 for H₂, 4 for O₂). Using wrong n-factor is a 2-mark killer.
> 5. **Distillation gives only 95.6 % ethanol** (azeotrope) — students who write "100 % ethanol by distillation" lose 1 mark. Dehydration requires **molecular sieves**.
> 6. **Methanol-to-oil ratio is MOLAR, not volume** — examiners will deduct marks if you say "use 200 mL methanol" without specifying the moles.
> 7. **Always mention catalyst concentration** (e.g., 1 % w/w NaOH) — vague answers like "use a catalyst" earn only partial credit.
> 8. **In anaerobic digestion, state the microorganism class** — methanogens are **archaea**, not bacteria. This is a common 1-mark deduction in viva-voce and written exams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This Page)**

- ⭐ **Transesterification:** Triglyceride + 3 CH₃OH → 3 FAME + Glycerol; catalyst = NaOH/KOH; 60–65 °C; stoichiometric 3:1, industrial 6:1–12:1.
- ⭐ **Two-step process** is mandatory when FFA > 2 % — acid esterification first, then base transesterification.
- ⭐ **Biodiesel yield > 100 %** by mass is mathematically correct due to methanol incorporation.
- ⭐ **Theoretical ethanol yield** from glucose = 51.1 % by mass (180 → 2 × 46 + 2 × 44 = 92 + 88 = 180 g verified).
- ⭐ **Distillation maxes out at 95.6 % ethanol** (azeotrope with water); molecular sieves required for anhydrous.
- ⭐ **Four stages of anaerobic digestion:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis. Methanogens are **archaea**, the rate-limiting and most pH-sensitive step.
- ⭐ **Biogas composition:** 55–70 % CH₄ + 30–45 % CO₂ + trace H₂S, N₂, water vapor.
- ⭐ **Optimum digester conditions:** 35 °C (mesophilic), pH 6.5–7.5, C/N 20:1–30:1, HRT 20–40 days.
- ⭐ **Biogas yield** from cattle dung ≈ 0.04–0.06 m³/kg DM/day.
- ⭐ **Steam Methane Reforming:** CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ (endothermic, 850 °C); followed by Water-Gas Shift: CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂ (exothermic).
- ⭐ **Electrolysis minimum voltage = 1.23 V** (thermodynamic); commercial cells operate at **1.8–2.4 V**; Faradaic constant F = 96,485 C/mol.
- ⭐ **Green Hydrogen = Renewable electricity + Electrolysis**; current efficiency 60–80 % (HHV basis).
- ⭐ **Energy content benchmarks:** Biodiesel ≈ 37.2 MJ/kg; Ethanol ≈ 26.9 MJ/kg; Methane ≈ 55.5 MJ/kg; Hydrogen LHV = 120 MJ/kg, HHV = 142 MJ/kg.
- ⭐ **Stoichiometric AFR of biodiesel is higher than diesel** (due to oxygen content) → minor power loss (~5–7 %) but better combustion.
- ⭐ **India's flagship programs:** SATAT (biodiesel), EBP-E20 (ethanol), Biogas National Programme, National Green Hydrogen Mission.
- ⭐ **Methanol production route:** Syngas (CO + 2H₂) → CH₃OH at 50–100 atm, 200–300 °C over Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst.
- ⭐ **Always include units in numerical answers** and quote final answers to **2 decimal places** for marks.
- ⭐ **For free marks**, mention government schemes, real-world yield data, and CO₂ savings per litre of fuel substituted.

---
