topics.title: Blending Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Blending Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Blending Criteria** refers to the comprehensive set of physicochemical, thermodynamic, and performance-based parameters that govern the formulation, stability, and operational suitability of a mixture between an **alternate (non-conventional) fuel** and a **conventional baseline fuel** (typically diesel or gasoline) for use in internal combustion engines and energy systems.

In the context of KTU Module 2 (Alternate Fuels), blending criteria establish the **technical envelope** within which a fuel mixture:
1. Remains physically and chemically stable (no phase separation)
2. Preserves acceptable engine performance (power output, efficiency)
3. Maintains emissions compliance with regulatory standards
4. Ensures material compatibility with engine components (seals, gaskets, fuel lines)
5. Remains economically viable and safe to handle, store, and transport

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** Blending is NOT a mere mixing operation — it is a *deliberate engineering design process* governed by standards such as **ASTM D975** (diesel), **ASTM D6751** (biodiesel), **EN 590** (European diesel), and **BIS IS 1460** (Indian standard for high-speed diesel).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of blending fuels exactly like **brewing a cup of tea with milk**:

| Tea Brewing Parameter | Fuel Blending Analogy |
|---|---|
| Tea : Milk ratio (e.g., 70:30) | Biodiesel : Diesel ratio (e.g., B20 = 20:80) |
| Both liquids must mix uniformly (no curdling) | Both fuels must be fully miscible (no phase separation) |
| Too much milk weakens flavor | Too much alternate fuel reduces energy density |
| Tea leaves must steep properly | Blend must atomize and combust properly |
| Hot tea scalds — temperature limit | Flash point — safety temperature limit |
| Pour point of honey | Cold-flow properties (cloud point, pour point) |

Just as you would NOT add cold milk to boiling tea (causes curdling / phase separation), you cannot arbitrarily mix two fuels — the **blend ratio is constrained** by a matrix of physical, chemical, and combustion properties.

## 1.3 Why Blending? The Engineering Motivation

Pure alternate fuels often have drawbacks:
- **Biodiesel (B100):** Higher viscosity (~4–6 mm²/s), poor cold-flow properties, slightly lower calorific value (~37 MJ/kg vs 43 MJ/kg for diesel)
- **Ethanol (E100):** Lower energy density (~26.8 MJ/kg), hygroscopic, attacks certain rubber seals
- **Methanol:** Very low cetane number (~3–5), high latent heat of vaporization
- **Hydrogen:** Extremely low density, high flame speed, pre-ignition risk

Blending with the base fuel **dilutes these limitations** while retaining a portion of the alternate fuel's benefits (renewability, lower emissions, indigenous availability).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept — Linear Blending Rule (Foundation Principle):** For most first-order fuel properties (density, calorific value, cetane number), the blend property is approximately a **weighted linear average** of the component properties weighted by their volume or mass fractions. This is called the **Linear Blending Rule (LBR)** and is the most heavily tested formula in KTU exams on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Blending Criteria

Every alternate fuel blend (be it B20 biodiesel, E10 ethanol-gasoline, M15 methanol-gasoline, or a hydrogen-CNG mixture) is judged against **seven fundamental parameter groups**:

### Pillar 1: Miscibility & Phase Stability
- Fuels must be **completely miscible** across the entire operating temperature range (typically **–5 °C to 60 °C** for tropical Indian conditions).
- Miscibility is governed by **polarity compatibility** — biodiesel (polar) blends easily with diesel (non-polar to mildly polar); ethanol (highly polar) requires **co-solvents** when blended with diesel.
- **Test method:** ASTM D1796 (centrifuge test for water and sediment), visual inspection at controlled temperatures.

### Pillar 2: Density (ρ)
- Density governs **energy content per unit volume** and **fuel metering**.
- Acceptable range for diesel blends: **860 – 900 kg/m³** at 15 °C.
- Linear blending: $\rho_{\text{blend}} = x_A \rho_A + x_B \rho_B$ (volume-weighted).

### Pillar 3: Viscosity (ν)
- Viscosity controls **atomization quality**, **injector spray pattern**, and **lubricity**.
- Biodiesel viscosity (~4.5 mm²/s at 40 °C) is much higher than diesel (~2.5 mm²/s at 40 °C).
- Blending **linearly reduces** viscosity toward the base fuel value.
- Acceptable range for diesel blends: **2.0 – 4.5 mm²/s** at 40 °C (per **ASTM D445**).

### Pillar 4: Calorific Value (CV / Heating Value)
- Determines **energy delivered per kg** of fuel.
- Alternate fuels typically have **lower CV** than petroleum fuels.
- Mass-weighted blending:
$$CV_{\text{blend}} = \frac{m_A \cdot CV_A + m_B \cdot CV_B}{m_A + m_B}$$
- Reduced CV translates directly to **specific fuel consumption increase** — a critical exam point.

### Pillar 5: Cetane Number (CN) / Octane Number (ON)
- **CN** for diesel engines (ignition quality); **ON** for spark-ignition engines.
- Biodiesel has higher CN (~50–60) than diesel (~45–55) — a *blending benefit*.
- Methanol has very low CN (~3–5) — a *blending drawback*.
- Cetane number blends **non-linearly** but is approximated linearly for exam purposes.

### Pillar 6: Flash Point & Fire Point (Safety)
- **Flash point** = lowest temperature at which vapor ignites momentarily.
- Biodiesel has high flash point (~150 °C) — **safer to handle** than diesel (~60–80 °C).
- Ethanol and methanol have **low flash points** — safety hazard.
- Must satisfy **PESO / OISD** norms for transport and storage.

### Pillar 7: Cold-Flow Properties (Cloud Point CP, Pour Point PP, Cold Filter Plugging Point CFPP)
- Biodiesel has **poor cold-flow** (CP ~ 13 °C for palm biodiesel).
- Diesel has better cold-flow (CP ~ 5 °C).
- Blending improves cold-flow only marginally; **winterization additives** often required.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Linear Blending Formula (Binary A + B) | Units | Standard Test |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density | $\rho_{\text{blend}} = x_A \rho_A + x_B \rho_B$ | kg/m³ | ASTM D1298 |
| 2 | Viscosity (approx.) | $\nu_{\text{blend}} = x_A \nu_A + x_B \nu_B$ | mm²/s (cSt) | ASTM D445 |
| 3 | Calorific Value (mass basis) | $CV_{\text{blend}} = (m_A \cdot CV_A + m_B \cdot CV_B) / (m_A + m_B)$ | MJ/kg | ASTM D240 |
| 4 | Cetane Number (approx.) | $CN_{\text{blend}} \approx x_A \cdot CN_A + x_B \cdot CN_B$ | dimensionless | ASTM D613 |
| 5 | Flash Point (approx.) | $FP_{\text{blend}} \approx$ log-mixing rule | °C | ASTM D93 |
| 6 | Cloud Point (approx.) | $CP_{\text{blend}} \approx x_A \cdot CP_A + x_B \cdot CP_B$ | °C | ASTM D2500 |
| 7 | Sulfur Content | $S_{\text{blend}} = x_A \cdot S_A + x_B \cdot S_B$ | % mass | ASTM D4294 |
| 8 | Water Content | $W_{\text{blend}} = x_A \cdot W_A + x_B \cdot W_B$ | ppm | ASTM D6304 |
| 9 | Ash Content | $Ash_{\text{blend}} = x_A \cdot Ash_A + x_B \cdot Ash_B$ | % mass | ASTM D482 |
| 10 | Volume Fraction Check | $x_A + x_B = 1$ | dimensionless | — |

**Legend:** $x_i$ = volume fraction of component *i*; $m_i$ = mass of component *i*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The Volume Fraction Identity $x_A + x_B = 1$ must be explicitly used in your KTU answers whenever you set up a blending equation. Examiners award **½ to 1 bonus mark** for clearly stating it.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Blending criteria are not academic — they drive global fuel policy:
- India's **B5 mandate** (5% biodiesel blending) under the **National Policy on Biofuels 2018**.
- Brazil's **E27 mandate** (27% ethanol in gasoline).
- The European Union's **B7 standard** (EN 14214 + EN 590).
- Standards dictate blend ratios for **import-export fuel contracts**, refinery blending operations, and **vehicle warranty compliance**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Linear Blending Rule for Binary Fuel Mixtures

Consider two fuels **A** (alternate fuel, e.g., biodiesel) and **B** (base fuel, e.g., diesel) to be blended in a volume ratio such that the volume fraction of A is $x_A$ and the volume fraction of B is $x_B$.

**Step 1: Define Volume Conservation**
$$V_{\text{blend}} = V_A + V_B$$

**Step 2: Define Volume Fractions**
$$x_A = \frac{V_A}{V_{\text{blend}}}, \quad x_B = \frac{V_B}{V_{\text{blend}}}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Volume Fraction Identity**
$$x_A + x_B = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_B = 1 - x_A$$

**Step 4: Express Any Intensive Property $P$ (e.g., density, cetane number)**
For an **intensive property** (does not depend on the amount of substance), the blend property equals the **weighted arithmetic mean**:
$$P_{\text{blend}} = x_A \cdot P_A + x_B \cdot P_B$$

**Step 5: Substitute the Identity into the Blend Property Equation**
$$P_{\text{blend}} = x_A \cdot P_A + (1 - x_A) \cdot P_B$$

**Step 6: Simplify**
$$P_{\text{blend}} = x_A \cdot P_A + P_B - x_A \cdot P_B$$

$$P_{\text{blend}} = P_B + x_A \cdot (P_A - P_B)$$

This is the **canonical exam form** of the linear blending rule — used when one component (B) is the base fuel and $x_A$ is the fraction of alternate fuel being added.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: Properties of a B20 Biodiesel-Diesel Blend

**Problem Statement:**
A B20 blend is prepared using **20% by volume of biodiesel (BD)** and **80% by volume of petro-diesel (PD)**. The following data are given:

| Property | Biodiesel (BD) | Petro-diesel (PD) |
|---|---|---|
| Density at 15 °C | 880 kg/m³ | 835 kg/m³ |
| Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C | 4.5 mm²/s | 2.8 mm²/s |
| Calorific value | 37.2 MJ/kg | 43.0 MJ/kg |
| Cetane number | 55 | 48 |
| Flash point | 150 °C | 65 °C |

**Compute** the density, viscosity, calorific value, and cetane number of the B20 blend.

### Solution — Part (a): Density of B20 Blend

$$x_{BD} = 0.20, \quad x_{PD} = 0.80$$

$$\rho_{\text{B20}} = x_{BD} \cdot \rho_{BD} + x_{PD} \cdot \rho_{PD}$$

$$\rho_{\text{B20}} = (0.20)(880) + (0.80)(835)$$

$$\rho_{\text{B20}} = 176 + 668 = 844 \; \text{kg/m}^3$$

### Solution — Part (b): Viscosity of B20 Blend

$$\nu_{\text{B20}} = x_{BD} \cdot \nu_{BD} + x_{PD} \cdot \nu_{PD}$$

$$\nu_{\text{B20}} = (0.20)(4.5) + (0.80)(2.8)$$

$$\nu_{\text{B20}} = 0.90 + 2.24 = 3.14 \; \text{mm}^2/\text{s}$$

### Solution — Part (c): Calorific Value of B20 Blend

First, calculate the **mass of each component** per unit volume of blend.
Mass of biodiesel in 1 m³ blend:
$$m_{BD} = x_{BD} \cdot \rho_{BD} = 0.20 \times 880 = 176 \; \text{kg}$$

Mass of petro-diesel in 1 m³ blend:
$$m_{PD} = x_{PD} \cdot \rho_{PD} = 0.80 \times 835 = 668 \; \text{kg}$$

Total mass per m³:
$$m_{\text{total}} = 176 + 668 = 844 \; \text{kg}$$

Apply the mass-weighted blending rule for calorific value:
$$CV_{\text{B20}} = \frac{m_{BD} \cdot CV_{BD} + m_{PD} \cdot CV_{PD}}{m_{\text{total}}}$$

$$CV_{\text{B20}} = \frac{(176)(37.2) + (668)(43.0)}{844}$$

$$CV_{\text{B20}} = \frac{6547.2 + 28724.0}{844} = \frac{35271.2}{844}$$

$$CV_{\text{B20}} \approx 41.79 \; \text{MJ/kg}$$

### Solution — Part (d): Cetane Number of B20 Blend

$$CN_{\text{B20}} = x_{BD} \cdot CN_{BD} + x_{PD} \cdot CN_{PD}$$

$$CN_{\text{B20}} = (0.20)(55) + (0.80)(48)$$

$$CN_{\text{B20}} = 11.0 + 38.4 = 49.4$$

### Summary Table for B20 Blend

| Property | Biodiesel (BD) | Petro-diesel (PD) | **B20 Blend (Computed)** | Acceptable Range? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | 880 | 835 | **844** | 860–900 (close, ✓) |
| Viscosity (mm²/s) | 4.5 | 2.8 | **3.14** | 2.0–4.5 (✓) |
| Calorific Value (MJ/kg) | 37.2 | 43.0 | **41.79** | > 38 (✓) |
| Cetane Number | 55 | 48 | **49.4** | ≥ 46 (✓) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the B20 cetane number (**49.4**) is *higher* than pure petro-diesel (48) — a **free performance benefit** of blending biodiesel. However, the calorific value drops by ~2.8%, which translates to ~2.8% higher brake-specific fuel consumption (BSFC) — the classic **emissions vs. efficiency trade-off** of alternate fuels.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Blending Criteria Decision Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alternate Fuel Selection] --> B{Is Fuel Miscible\nwith Base Fuel?}
    B -- No --> C[Use Co-solvent\nor Surfactant]
    B -- Yes --> D[Determine Volume Ratio\nx_A and x_B]
    D --> E[Apply Linear Blending Rule\nfor Each Property]
    E --> F1[Density Check]
    E --> F2[Viscosity Check]
    E --> F3[Cetane Number Check]
    E --> F4[Calorific Value Check]
    E --> F5[Flash Point Safety Check]
    E --> F6[Cold-Flow Check]
    E --> F7[Sulfur and Ash Check]
    F1 --> G{All Properties Within\nAcceptable Envelope?}
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    F7 --> G
    G -- No --> H[Adjust Ratio or\nAdd Fuel Additives]
    G -- Yes --> I[Validate via Engine\nTest Bench Trials]
    I --> J[Certify Blend as per\nASTM EN BIS Standards]
    H --> D
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Property Classification under Blending

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IntensiveProperties [Intensive Properties - Volume Weighted]
        propA[Density]
        propB[Cetane Number]
        propC[Viscosity Approx]
        propD[Sulfur Content]
    end
    subgraph ExtensiveProperties [Extensive-Derived Properties - Mass Weighted]
        propE[Calorific Value]
        propF[Energy per Unit Volume]
    end
    subgraph NonLinearProperties [Non Linear Properties - Special Rules]
        propG[Flash Point Log Mixing]
        propH[Vapor Pressure Raoult Law]
        propI[Cloud Point with Interactions]
    end
    propA --> BlendResult[Fuel Blend]
    propB --> BlendResult
    propC --> BlendResult
    propD --> BlendResult
    propE --> BlendResult
    propF --> BlendResult
    propG --> BlendResult
    propH --> BlendResult
    propI --> BlendResult
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Typical Blend Ratios used in Industry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Biodiesel Diesel Blends]

    R1 --> S1[B5 B10 B20 B100]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The block diagrams map the *decision logic*, *property classification*, and *industrial blend nomenclature* for blending criteria. They are designed as functional architectures because physical schematics of multi-property fuel blending cannot be rendered as simple flow diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define blending criteria for alternate fuels. List any four essential properties that must be satisfied by a fuel blend.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Blending criteria are the set of physicochemical and performance parameters that govern the formulation, stability, and operational suitability of a mixture between an alternate fuel and a conventional base fuel, ensuring engine compatibility, emissions compliance, and material safety.

**Four Essential Properties (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Miscibility** with the base fuel across operating temperature range.
2. **Kinematic viscosity** within 2.0 – 4.5 mm²/s at 40 °C.
3. **Cetane number** ≥ 46 for diesel blends (per ASTM D613).
4. **Flash point** above 55 °C for safe transport and storage (per PESO norms).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the Linear Blending Rule with its mathematical statement for a binary fuel blend.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Linear Blending Rule (LBR)** states that for any intensive property $P$ of a binary fuel blend composed of components A and B, the blend property equals the volume-weighted arithmetic mean of the component properties:

$$P_{\text{blend}} = x_A \cdot P_A + x_B \cdot P_B, \quad \text{where} \quad x_A + x_B = 1$$

This rule applies approximately to **density, cetane number, viscosity, sulfur content, and ash content**. It does **NOT** apply to flash point (uses log-mixing), vapor pressure (uses Raoult's Law), or calorific value (uses mass-weighted average instead of volume-weighted).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major parameters that govern the blending of biodiesel with petro-diesel. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A B30 biodiesel blend (30% biodiesel + 70% petro-diesel by volume) is to be characterized. The properties of neat biodiesel and petro-diesel are given below:

| Property | Biodiesel | Petro-diesel |
|---|---|---|
| Density at 15 °C (kg/m³) | 885 | 832 |
| Calorific value (MJ/kg) | 37.0 | 43.5 |
| Cetane number | 58 | 47 |

Calculate the density, calorific value, and cetane number of the B30 blend using the linear blending rule. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks

The major parameters governing biodiesel-diesel blending are grouped as follows:

**1. Physicochemical Parameters (3 Marks):**
- **Miscibility:** Biodiesel (methyl esters of fatty acids) is fully miscible with petro-diesel in all proportions due to similar polarity.
- **Density:** Pure biodiesel (~880 kg/m³) is denser than diesel (~835 kg/m³); blend density should lie within 860–900 kg/m³.
- **Viscosity:** Biodiesel viscosity (~4.5 cSt at 40 °C) is ~1.6× that of diesel; blending linearly reduces it.
- **Flash Point:** Biodiesel has high flash point (~150 °C) — a safety advantage.

**2. Combustion Parameters (2 Marks):**
- **Cetane Number:** Biodiesel (CN 50–60) > diesel (CN 45–55) → improved ignition quality.
- **Calorific Value:** Biodiesel has ~10–12% lower CV → increased BSFC.

**3. Cold-Flow and Stability (1 Mark):**
- **Cloud Point and Pour Point:** Biodiesel has poor cold-flow properties; blending provides limited improvement.
- **Oxidative Stability:** Biodiesel is more prone to oxidation; antioxidants are added.

**4. Emissions & Material Compatibility (1 Mark):**
- Lower CO, HC, and PM emissions; marginally higher NOx.
- Compatible with most engine elastomers except certain natural rubber.

---

### Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks

Given: $x_{BD} = 0.30$, $x_{PD} = 0.70$

**(i) Density of B30 blend:**
$$\rho_{\text{B30}} = (0.30)(885) + (0.70)(832)$$
$$\rho_{\text{B30}} = 265.5 + 582.4 = 847.9 \; \text{kg/m}^3 \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

**(ii) Calorific Value of B30 blend:**
First compute mass contributions per m³:
$$m_{BD} = 0.30 \times 885 = 265.5 \; \text{kg}$$
$$m_{PD} = 0.70 \times 832 = 582.4 \; \text{kg}$$
$$m_{\text{total}} = 265.5 + 582.4 = 847.9 \; \text{kg}$$

Mass-weighted blend CV:
$$CV_{\text{B30}} = \frac{(265.5)(37.0) + (582.4)(43.5)}{847.9}$$
$$CV_{\text{B30}} = \frac{9823.5 + 25334.4}{847.9} = \frac{35157.9}{847.9}$$
$$CV_{\text{B30}} \approx 41.46 \; \text{MJ/kg} \quad \textbf{(3 Marks)}$$

**(iii) Cetane Number of B30 blend:**
$$CN_{\text{B30}} = (0.30)(58) + (0.70)(47)$$
$$CN_{\text{B30}} = 17.4 + 32.9 = 50.3 \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

**Conclusion:** The B30 blend has density 847.9 kg/m³, calorific value 41.46 MJ/kg, and cetane number 50.3 — all within acceptable diesel engine operating ranges.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**(a)** Discuss the role of viscosity, density, flash point, and cold-flow properties as blending criteria for alternate fuels. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An E10 ethanol-gasoline blend (10% ethanol + 90% gasoline by volume) is prepared. Given ethanol density = 789 kg/m³, gasoline density = 740 kg/m³, ethanol calorific value = 26.8 MJ/kg, gasoline calorific value = 44.0 MJ/kg. Compute the blend density and blend calorific value. Comment on the energy density reduction compared to pure gasoline. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Viscosity (2 Marks):**
Viscosity governs **atomization quality** in the combustion chamber. Alternate fuels like biodiesel (ν ≈ 4.5 cSt) have high viscosity, leading to poor spray atomization, larger droplet size, and incomplete combustion. Blending with diesel (ν ≈ 2.8 cSt) linearly reduces viscosity, restoring acceptable spray characteristics per **ASTM D445** standard (2.0–4.5 cSt at 40 °C).

**2. Density (1.5 Marks):**
Density determines **mass of fuel injected per unit volume**. Higher-density fuels (e.g., biodiesel) deliver more mass per injection stroke. The blend density must remain within the **860–900 kg/m³** envelope for proper air-fuel ratio control and ECU calibration.

**3. Flash Point (1.5 Marks):**
Flash point determines **safety classification** for storage, transport, and handling. Biodiesel has high flash point (~150 °C) — much safer than diesel (~65 °C). Conversely, ethanol (FP ~13 °C) and methanol (FP ~11 °C) drastically **reduce blend flash point** when added to gasoline, requiring strict PESO / OISD safety compliance.

**4. Cold-Flow Properties (2 Marks):**
**Cloud Point (CP)** — temperature at which wax crystals first appear. **Pour Point (PP)** — lowest temperature at which fuel flows. Biodiesel has poor cold-flow (CP ~ 10–15 °C). Blending provides **limited improvement**; winterization additives (e.g., polymeric pour point depressants) are usually necessary for cold-climate operation.

---

### Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks

Given: $x_{\text{EtOH}} = 0.10$, $x_{\text{gas}} = 0.90$

**(i) Density of E10 Blend:**
$$\rho_{\text{E10}} = (0.10)(789) + (0.90)(740)$$
$$\rho_{\text{E10}} = 78.9 + 666.0 = 744.9 \; \text{kg/m}^3 \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

**(ii) Calorific Value of E10 Blend:**
Mass of ethanol per m³:
$$m_{\text{EtOH}} = 0.10 \times 789 = 78.9 \; \text{kg}$$
Mass of gasoline per m³:
$$m_{\text{gas}} = 0.90 \times 740 = 666.0 \; \text{kg}$$
Total mass:
$$m_{\text{total}} = 78.9 + 666.0 = 744.9 \; \text{kg}$$

Mass-weighted CV:
$$CV_{\text{E10}} = \frac{(78.9)(26.8) + (666.0)(44.0)}{744.9}$$
$$CV_{\text{E10}} = \frac{2114.52 + 29304.0}{744.9} = \frac{31418.52}{744.9}$$
$$CV_{\text{E10}} \approx 42.18 \; \text{MJ/kg} \quad \textbf{(3 Marks)}$$

**(iii) Energy Density Reduction Comment (2 Marks):**
The energy density reduction in **MJ/kg** is:
$$\Delta CV = 44.0 - 42.18 = 1.82 \; \text{MJ/kg}$$
$$\% \; \text{reduction} = \frac{1.82}{44.0} \times 100\% \approx 4.14\%$$

The E10 blend delivers **~4.14% less energy per kg** than pure gasoline. Additionally, because the **density also drops** (from 740 to 744.9 kg/m³ — actually slightly higher, indicating modest volumetric advantage), the volumetric energy content is:

$$EV_{\text{E10}} = 744.9 \times 42.18 \approx 31{,}427 \; \text{kJ/m}^3$$

This is still lower than pure gasoline energy density, leading to **higher volumetric fuel consumption** in flexible-fuel vehicles.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Using volume fraction for calorific value instead of mass fraction** — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks. Always convert to mass for CV calculations.
> 2. **Forgetting the identity $x_A + x_B = 1$** — Costs 0.5–1 mark; always state it explicitly.
> 3. **Units mismatch in density blending** — If density is in g/cc vs kg/m³, normalize first.
> 4. **Applying LBR to flash point** — Flash point follows a **log-mixing rule**, NOT a linear rule. Writing $FP_{blend} = x_A FP_A + x_B FP_B$ will be marked **wrong**.
> 5. **Not stating the standard** (ASTM / EN / BIS) — Examiners in KTU 2024 scheme appreciate standards cited. Mention ASTM D975, D6751, EN 590, EN 14214, or BIS IS 1460 wherever applicable.
> 6. **Skipping the engineering comment at the end** — Always end with a one-line interpretation of what the numerical result means for engine operation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Blending Criteria** = set of physicochemical + performance rules governing alternate fuel + base fuel mixtures.
- **Linear Blending Rule (LBR):** $P_{\text{blend}} = x_A P_A + x_B P_B$ for intensive properties (density, CN, viscosity, sulfur).
- **Mass-Weighted Rule:** Used for calorific value: $CV_{\text{blend}} = \frac{m_A CV_A + m_B CV_B}{m_A + m_B}$.
- **Volume Fraction Identity:** Always enforce $x_A + x_B = 1$.
- **Seven Key Parameters:** Miscibility, Density, Viscosity, Calorific Value, Cetane Number, Flash Point, Cold-Flow Properties.
- **Biodiesel Benefits:** High CN, high flash point, renewable, lower emissions.
- **Biodiesel Drawbacks:** High viscosity, poor cold flow, lower CV → higher BSFC.
- **Common Blend Ratios:** B5, B20 (biodiesel); E5, E10, E85 (ethanol); M5, M15 (methanol); H20 (hydrogen-CNG).
- **Standards to Remember:** ASTM D975 (diesel), ASTM D6751 (biodiesel), EN 590 (Euro diesel), EN 14214 (Euro biodiesel), BIS IS 1460 (Indian HSD).
- **Trade-off Triangle:** Emissions ↓ vs. Efficiency ↓ vs. Cost ↑ — every blend sits on this trade-off surface.
- **Engineering Comment:** Always close numerical answers with a one-sentence implication for engine performance, emissions, or economy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blending Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Blending Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Alternate Fuels and Energy Systems**, the **Blending Process** refers to the controlled physical mixing of two or more liquid fuels (typically a conventional petroleum-based fuel and an alternate biofuel such as ethanol, methanol, or biodiesel) to produce a homogeneous fuel mixture with tailored physico-chemical properties suitable for use in internal combustion (IC) engines, without requiring major engine modification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Blending is the most commercially adopted route for introducing biofuels into the transportation fuel pool. It is governed by **fuel standards (BIS, ASTM, EN, IS 1460)** and is governed by miscibility, oxidation stability, water tolerance, and energy density considerations.

The standard nomenclature for blended fuels as adopted by **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** and **ASTM D975/D6751** is:

| Blend Nomenclature | Composition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **E5, E10, E20, E85, E100** | Ethanol % in petrol | Gasoline blends |
| **B2, B5, B10, B20, B100** | Biodiesel % in diesel | Diesel blends |
| **M5, M15, M85, M100** | Methanol % in petrol | Methanol blends |
| **E-diesel** | Ethanol + diesel (with emulsifier) | Dual fuel |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Tea–Milk Mixture**
> Imagine you are preparing a cup of tea by mixing milk (conventional diesel) with strong tea decoction (alternate biofuel like biodiesel). The **ratio** of milk to decoction determines the strength, colour, taste, and aroma. If the decoction is too thick, it will not mix uniformly — the same way an immiscible biofuel (e.g., methanol in diesel) will separate into layers, leaving the engine starved of fuel. Therefore, we sometimes add a small amount of an emulsifier (think of it as a pinch of "sugar stabiliser" in the tea) to keep the mixture homogeneous.
>
> **The Blending Process is essentially that "kitchen science" scaled up to refinery level — controlling ratio, temperature, agitation, and additive use to deliver a uniform, stable, engine-friendly fuel.**

## 1.3 Why Blending is Necessary

1. **Modification Avoidance**: Pure alternate fuels (e.g., neat methanol, neat ethanol) require engine modifications due to their lower cetane number, lower lubricity, and higher latent heat of vaporisation. Blending allows partial substitution without hardware change.
2. **Renewable Energy Mandate**: India's **E20 target (20% ethanol blending) by 2025–2030** (as per the National Policy on Biofuels, 2018) and **B7 mandate** make blending a national energy-security priority.
3. **Emission Reduction**: Blended fuels reduce particulate matter (PM), unburnt hydrocarbons (HC), and carbon monoxide (CO) emissions.
4. **Octane/Cetane Boosting**: Ethanol has a high Research Octane Number (RON ≈ **108**), so it is blended to upgrade low-octane gasoline streams.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Key Metrics

- Density of standard petroleum diesel ≈ **840 kg/m³** at 15 °C.
- Density of biodiesel (FAME) ≈ **880 kg/m³** at 15 °C.
- Density of anhydrous ethanol ≈ **789 kg/m³** at 20 °C.
- Calorific value of diesel ≈ **42.5 MJ/kg**; biodiesel ≈ **37.2 MJ/kg**; ethanol ≈ **26.9 MJ/kg**.
- Cetane number of diesel ≈ **45–55**; biodiesel ≈ **50–65**; ethanol ≈ **5–8**.
- Octane number of petrol ≈ **84–98**; ethanol ≈ **108** (RON).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Blending Processes (Classification)

The blending of alternate fuels with conventional fuels is broadly classified into the following categories:

### A. **Splash Blending (In-Tank Blending)**
- Two fuels (e.g., biodiesel and diesel) are added sequentially into the vehicle fuel tank.
- Agitation during vehicle motion provides limited mixing.
- **Disadvantages**: Non-homogeneous mixture, water absorption risk, phase separation at low temperatures.
- **Best suited for**: B5–B20 biodiesel blends and low-ethanol blends (≤ E10).

### B. **In-Line Blending (On-line / Continuous Blending)**
- Both fuels are metered using calibrated flow meters and injected simultaneously into a static mixer or into the pipeline feeding the vehicle/dispenser.
- **Advantages**: Precise ratio control, homogeneous blend, suitable for high bio-content fuels.
- **Application**: Refineries, terminal depots, ethanol flex-fuel pumps.

### C. **Co-Solvent Blending**
- A third component (co-solvent) like **butanol, isopropanol, or aromatic compounds** is added to assist miscibility of ethanol/methanol in diesel.
- Co-solvents increase the polarity bridge between hydrocarbon and alcohol molecules.
- **Used for**: E-diesel, M-diesel blends where the two fuels are not directly miscible.

### D. **Emulsification**
- Biodiesel/ethanol is dispersed as micro-droplets in diesel using a surfactant (e.g., Span 80, Tween 80).
- Produces a thermodynamically stable microemulsion.
- **Used for**: High bio-content diesel substitutes that would otherwise phase-separate.

### E. **Reactive Blending / Transesterification Blending**
- Oils are pre-reacted (transesterified) to form fatty acid methyl esters (FAME), which are then blended.
- This is the standard industrial process for biodiesel blending.

## 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. **Pre-treatment**: Remove water and free fatty acids (FFAs) from the biofuel; the conventional fuel is filtered.
2. **Metering**: Flow meters (Coriolis, turbine) precisely measure volumetric or mass flow of each fuel stream.
3. **Mixing**: Static in-line mixer or stirred tank promotes homogeneous dispersion. Shear forces break agglomerates.
4. **Additive Injection**: Anti-oxidants (BHT, BHA), cold-flow improvers, cetane improvers, and corrosion inhibitors are dosed.
5. **Storage**: Blended fuel is stored in sealed, dry tanks to prevent moisture ingress and oxidation.
6. **Quality Control**: Sampling for density, viscosity, flash point, water content (Karl Fischer), and oxidation stability (Rancimat).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_{blend} = \sum_i x_i \rho_i$ | Linear volumetric blend density (ideal mixing) | kg/m³ |
| 2 | $\rho_{blend} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i \dfrac{w_i}{\rho_i}}$ | Mass-fraction based blend density (harmonic) | kg/m³ |
| 3 | $CV_{blend} = \sum_i w_i \, CV_i$ | Weighted calorific value of blend | MJ/kg |
| 4 | $\mu_{blend}^{1/3} = \sum_i x_i \, \mu_i^{1/3}$ | Kendall–Monroe equation (viscosity of blend) | mm²/s |
| 5 | $FP_{blend} = \sum_i x_i \, FP_i$ | Flash point (ideal mixing, only for miscible liquids) | °C |
| 6 | $CN_{blend} = \sum_i x_i \, CN_i$ | Cetane number (linear approx.) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $RON_{blend} = \sum_i x_i \, RON_i$ | Research Octane Number (linear approx.) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $BSFC_{blend} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{th} \cdot CV_{blend}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh |
| 9 | $\phi_{air\text{-}fuel} = \dfrac{(A/F)_{st}}{(A/F)_{act}}$ | Equivalence ratio (used to compute blend A/F) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $V_{b} = \dfrac{m_{bio}}{\rho_{bio}} + \dfrac{m_{pet}}{\rho_{pet}}$ | Volumetric requirement per blend batch | m³ |

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Refinery Process Engineers** use these formulae to size static mixers and predict blend-line pressure drops.
- **Automotive R&D** uses blend property tables to map BSFC vs. blend ratio curves for fleet testing.
- **Petroleum Marketing (IOCL, BPCL, HPCL)** deploys in-line blending at depots to deliver BIS-standard E10/E20 petrol.
- **Emission Regulators** rely on these equations to certify that blended fuels meet **BS-VI** and **Euro 6** norms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Density of an Ideal Binary Blend

We wish to derive the density of a blend composed of **fuel A (volume fraction $x_A$)** and **fuel B (volume fraction $x_B$)**.

**Step 1 — Mass balance:**
The total mass of the blend equals the sum of the masses of the constituents.

$$
m_{blend} = m_A + m_B
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting mass = density × volume:**

$$
\rho_{blend} \cdot V_{blend} = \rho_A \cdot V_A + \rho_B \cdot V_B
$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by $V_{blend}$:**

$$
\rho_{blend} = \rho_A \cdot \dfrac{V_A}{V_{blend}} + \rho_B \cdot \dfrac{V_B}{V_{blend}}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognising $x_A = V_A / V_{blend}$ and $x_B = V_B / V_{blend}$:**

$$
\boxed{\rho_{blend} = x_A \rho_A + x_B \rho_B}
$$

This is the **volume-weighted linear mixing rule** (Equation 1 of the cheat sheet). For multi-component blends, the equation generalises to:

$$
\rho_{blend} = \sum_i x_i \rho_i \quad ; \quad \sum_i x_i = 1
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass-Fraction Density (For Incompressible Liquids)

If the user knows the **mass fractions** $w_i$ instead of volume fractions, conversion is needed because density is an intensive volumetric property.

**Step 1 — Express $x_i$ in terms of $w_i$:**

$$
x_i = \dfrac{w_i / \rho_i}{\sum_j (w_j / \rho_j)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the volume-weighted density equation:**

$$
\rho_{blend} = \sum_i \left[ \dfrac{w_i / \rho_i}{\sum_j (w_j / \rho_j)} \right] \rho_i
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify numerator and denominator:**

$$
\rho_{blend} = \dfrac{\sum_i w_i}{\sum_j (w_j / \rho_j)} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_j (w_j / \rho_j)}
$$

Since $\sum_i w_i = 1$ for a normalised blend:

$$
\boxed{\rho_{blend} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i \dfrac{w_i}{\rho_i}}}
$$

This is the **harmonic mixing rule** for density, valid when the volume change of mixing is negligible — a valid assumption for most biodiesel–diesel blends.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Calorific Value of the Blend

**Step 1 — Energy content balance:**
Total energy released by the blend = sum of energies released by each component.

$$
E_{blend} = E_A + E_B
$$

**Step 2 — Express energy in terms of mass and calorific value:**

$$
m_{blend} \cdot CV_{blend} = m_A \cdot CV_A + m_B \cdot CV_B
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $m_{blend}$ and recognise mass fractions:**

$$
\boxed{CV_{blend} = w_A \cdot CV_A + w_B \cdot CV_B}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effect on BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption)

**Step 1 — Definition of BSFC:**

$$
BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}
$$

where $\dot{m}_f$ is the mass fuel flow rate (kg/s) and $P_b$ is the brake power (kW).

**Step 2 — Expressing in terms of calorific value and thermal efficiency:**

$$
\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{P_b}{\eta_{th} \cdot CV_{blend}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad BSFC = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{th} \cdot CV_{blend}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to g/kWh:**

$$
BSFC = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{th} \cdot CV_{blend}} \quad \text{[g/kWh]}
$$

**Step 4 — Implication for blending:**
As the bio-content of the blend increases, $CV_{blend}$ decreases (since biofuels have lower calorific values). Therefore, **BSFC increases**, meaning the engine must burn more mass of fuel per kWh of brake work — a critical design trade-off.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A **B20 blend** is prepared by mixing **20 % biodiesel (volume)** and **80 % petroleum diesel**. Given $\rho_{bio} = 880$ kg/m³, $\rho_{diesel} = 840$ kg/m³, $CV_{bio} = 37.2$ MJ/kg, $CV_{diesel} = 42.5$ MJ/kg, compute the density and calorific value of the blend.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Density (volume-fraction basis):**

$$
\rho_{blend} = (0.20 \times 880) + (0.80 \times 840)
$$

$$
\rho_{blend} = 176 + 672 = 848 \;\text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Convert volume fractions to mass fractions:**
Mass of biodiesel per unit blend volume = $0.20 \times 880 = 176$ kg.
Mass of diesel per unit blend volume = $0.80 \times 840 = 672$ kg.
Total mass = $176 + 672 = 848$ kg.

Mass fraction of biodiesel:

$$
w_{bio} = \dfrac{176}{848} = 0.2075
$$

Mass fraction of diesel:

$$
w_{diesel} = \dfrac{672}{848} = 0.7925
$$

**Step 3 — Calorific value (mass-fraction basis):**

$$
CV_{blend} = (0.2075 \times 37.2) + (0.7925 \times 42.5)
$$

$$
CV_{blend} = 7.719 + 33.681 = 41.40 \;\text{MJ/kg}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\rho_{B20} = 848 \;\text{kg/m}^3 \quad ; \quad CV_{B20} = 41.40 \;\text{MJ/kg}
$$

Note the slight drop in calorific value compared to neat diesel (**42.5 MJ/kg**) — explaining the ~2.6 % BSFC increase observed experimentally.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Define the term 'Blending Process' as applied to alternate fuels. List any two commonly used blend nomenclature with their composition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **blending process** in alternate fuels refers to the controlled physical mixing of a conventional petroleum fuel with an alternate biofuel (such as ethanol, methanol, or biodiesel) to obtain a homogeneous liquid fuel mixture with desired physico-chemical and combustion properties suitable for use in existing internal combustion engines without significant modification.

Two common blend nomenclatures:

- **E10** — A mixture of **10 % ethanol (by volume)** with **90 % petrol (gasoline)**. Widely adopted in India under the Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) programme.
- **B20** — A mixture of **20 % biodiesel (FAME, by volume)** with **80 % petroleum diesel**. Used as a fleet-fuel in commercial transport.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for nomenclatures = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Sample Question Bank]**
**State any three factors that affect the stability of a biodiesel–diesel blend.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Water Content:** Presence of moisture (even trace levels > **200 ppm**) promotes hydrolysis of FAME into free fatty acids, accelerating phase separation.
2. **Temperature Drop:** At low temperatures, biodiesel components (especially saturated FAME like methyl stearate) crystallise, leading to filter plugging.
3. **Oxidation Stability:** Unsaturated fatty acid chains (linoleic, linolenic) react with atmospheric O₂ to form peroxides, gums, and acids; this degrades blend stability over storage.

*(1 mark per correct factor with brief justification = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with **internal choice**. The typical sub-division is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Solutions below follow this pattern.

---

### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Module 2, OEMUT615]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **splash blending** and **in-line blending** methods for biodiesel. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuel depot prepares a **B5 blend** for municipal buses. Given:
- Volume of diesel = **9 500 L** with $\rho_{diesel} = 840$ kg/m³ and $CV_{diesel} = 42.5$ MJ/kg,
- Volume of biodiesel = **500 L** with $\rho_{biodiesel} = 880$ kg/m³ and $CV_{biodiesel} = 37.2$ MJ/kg.

Compute the **density**, **mass fractions**, and **calorific value** of the B5 blend. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Splash vs In-line Blending (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Splash Blending | In-line Blending |
|---|---|---|
| Mixing location | Inside vehicle fuel tank | Inside refinery pipeline / dispenser |
| Equipment | Two fuel tanks + pump | Two flow meters + static mixer |
| Mix quality | Non-uniform, dependent on motion | Homogeneous, precise |
| Ratio accuracy | Low (depends on driver refuelling) | Very high (±0.2 %) |
| Suitable blend range | B2 to B20, E5 to E10 | Up to E85, B100 |
| Capital cost | Low | Moderate to high |
| Risk of phase separation | High at low temperature | Low |

*(3 marks for splash explanation, 3 marks for in-line explanation, 1 mark for tabulated comparison = 7 marks)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total mass of each fuel:**

$$
m_{diesel} = 9500 \times 10^{-3} \times 840 = 7980 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
m_{biodiesel} = 500 \times 10^{-3} \times 880 = 440 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
m_{blend} = 7980 + 440 = 8420 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Density of blend (volume-weighted):**

$$
\rho_{blend} = (0.95 \times 840) + (0.05 \times 880) = 798 + 44 = 842 \;\text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Mass fractions:**

$$
w_{biodiesel} = \dfrac{440}{8420} = 0.05225
$$

$$
w_{diesel} = \dfrac{7980}{8420} = 0.94775
$$

**Step 4 — Calorific value of blend:**

$$
CV_{blend} = (0.05225 \times 37.2) + (0.94775 \times 42.5)
$$

$$
CV_{blend} = 1.9437 + 40.279 = 42.22 \;\text{MJ/kg}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\rho_{B5} = 842 \;\text{kg/m}^3 \quad ; \quad CV_{B5} = 42.22 \;\text{MJ/kg}}
$$

*(1 mark for density, 2 marks for mass fractions, 2 marks for CV, 2 marks for final values with units and presentation = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Module 2, OEMUT615, Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **co-solvents** in blending ethanol with diesel. Why is co-solvent blending necessary and what are the typical co-solvents used industrially? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **E10 blend** for two-wheeler scooters uses:
- Ethanol: 10 % by volume, $\rho_{ethanol} = 789$ kg/m³, $CV_{ethanol} = 26.9$ MJ/kg, $RON_{ethanol} = 108$,
- Petrol: 90 % by volume, $\rho_{petrol} = 740$ kg/m³, $CV_{petrol} = 44.0$ MJ/kg, $RON_{petrol} = 88$.

Calculate (i) the blend density, (ii) the blend calorific value, and (iii) the resulting RON. Comment on the **BSFC penalty** expected in a scooter engine. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of Co-solvents (7 marks):**

Ethanol is a polar molecule due to its –OH group, whereas diesel is non-polar hydrocarbon. Due to this polarity mismatch, ethanol and diesel are **not directly miscible** beyond ~5 % at room temperature. Above this concentration, the blend phase-separates into two layers.

A **co-solvent** is a third amphiphilic liquid that acts as a **polarity bridge**, increasing the mutual solubility of ethanol and diesel. Co-solvents typically have:
- One polar end (compatible with ethanol),
- One non-polar hydrocarbon chain (compatible with diesel).

Common industrial co-solvents:
- **n-Butanol** — most common, ~5–10 % by volume of ethanol needed.
- **Iso-propanol**
- **Aromatic compounds** (toluene, xylene)
- **Fatty acid methyl esters (FAME)** — act as both biofuel and co-solvent.

Co-solvent blending enables stable E-diesel blends up to **E15–E20** without surfactants.

*(2 marks for polarity-miscibility explanation, 3 marks for co-solvent mechanism, 2 marks for examples and limits = 7 marks)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Blend density (volume-fraction basis):**

$$
\rho_{E10} = (0.10 \times 789) + (0.90 \times 740) = 78.9 + 666 = 744.9 \;\text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Mass fractions:**
Mass of ethanol per 1 L blend ≈ $0.10 \times 0.789 = 0.0789$ kg.
Mass of petrol per 1 L blend ≈ $0.90 \times 0.740 = 0.6660$ kg.
Total mass ≈ $0.7449$ kg.

$$
w_{ethanol} = \dfrac{0.0789}{0.7449} = 0.1059
$$

$$
w_{petrol} = \dfrac{0.6660}{0.7449} = 0.8941
$$

**Step 3 — Calorific value:**

$$
CV_{E10} = (0.1059 \times 26.9) + (0.8941 \times 44.0)
$$

$$
CV_{E10} = 2.849 + 39.340 = 42.19 \;\text{MJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — RON of blend (linear approximation):**

$$
RON_{E10} = (0.10 \times 108) + (0.90 \times 88) = 10.8 + 79.2 = 90.0
$$

**Step 5 — BSFC penalty:**
Since $CV_{E10} = 42.19$ MJ/kg < $CV_{petrol} = 44.0$ MJ/kg, the BSFC increases by:

$$
\Delta BSFC\,\% = \dfrac{44.0 - 42.19}{44.0} \times 100 = 4.11\,\%
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\rho_{E10} = 744.9 \;\text{kg/m}^3, \quad CV_{E10} = 42.19 \;\text{MJ/kg}, \quad RON_{E10} = 90.0, \quad BSFC \uparrow 4.11\,\%}
$$

**Comment:** The scooter engine will experience a **~4 % rise in BSFC** and slightly improved knock resistance (RON increased from 88 to 90). In practice, ethanol's high latent heat of vaporisation (~**840 kJ/kg** vs. ~**350 kJ/kg** for petrol) also improves volumetric efficiency, partially offsetting the BSFC penalty.

*(2 marks for density, 2 marks for CV, 1 mark for RON, 2 marks for BSFC and comment = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Blending Process Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistake:** Many students forget to convert litres to m³ (or multiply by density) when computing mass. A drop of **2 marks** is typical.
> 2. **Volume vs Mass Fraction Confusion:** Density-based blending uses **volume fractions**, while calorific-value blending uses **mass fractions**. Mixing them is the single most common error — losing **3–4 marks**.
> 3. **Ignoring Phase Separation:** In ethanol–diesel numerical problems, students often forget to mention that direct blending above ~5 % ethanol is not possible without a co-solvent. Examiners award a dedicated mark for this insight.
> 4. **Skipping the Comment on BSFC:** Numerical answers without a concluding engineering comment are marked down **1–2 marks**. Always link your result to engine performance.
> 5. **Forgetting the Standard Reference:** Always cite the **BIS standard (IS 1460, IS 15607)** or **ASTM D975/D6751** where applicable — it shows syllabus depth and earns a 'depth' mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Blending** = controlled physical mixing of biofuel + fossil fuel; goal is homogeneous, stable, engine-compatible fuel.
- [x] **Nomenclature rule**: Letter = biofuel type (E = Ethanol, B = Biodiesel, M = Methanol); Number = volume % of biofuel.
- [x] **Methods**: Splash, In-line, Co-solvent, Emulsification, Reactive (transesterification).
- [x] **Splash blending** = in-tank, simple, low-cost, only for low bio-content blends (≤ B20).
- [x] **In-line blending** = continuous, accurate, suited for high bio-content blends at depots.
- [x] **Co-solvents** (n-butanol, FAME) enable ethanol–diesel miscibility above 5 %.
- [x] **Density blend** = $\rho_{blend} = \sum x_i \rho_i$ (volume-fraction weighted).
- [x] **CV blend** = $CV_{blend} = \sum w_i \, CV_i$ (mass-fraction weighted).
- [x] **Kendall–Monroe equation** governs blend viscosity.
- [x] **BSFC penalty** is inevitable as bio-content rises due to lower calorific value of biofuels.
- [x] **India's mandates**: E20 by 2025–2030 (Ethanol Blended Petrol Programme), B7 (diesel) — MoPNG notifications.
- [x] **Standards**: BIS IS 1460, IS 15607; ASTM D975, D6751, D4806.
- [x] **Additives used**: anti-oxidants (BHT), cold-flow improvers, cetane improcers (2-EHN), corrosion inhibitors.
- [x] **Stability threats**: water content > 200 ppm, oxidation, low-temperature crystallisation, microbial growth.
- [x] **Always state units** (kg/m³, MJ/kg) and **round to 2–3 decimals** in numerical answers — KTU examiners award presentation marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages of Blending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advantages of Blending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blending**, in the context of alternate fuels and energy systems, is defined as the **homogeneous physical mixing of two or more fuels** — typically a conventional petroleum-based fuel (gasoline, diesel) with an alternative/renewable fuel (ethanol, methanol, biodiesel, CNG, hydrogen, etc.) — in precisely controlled volume or mass ratios to produce a **composite fuel** that leverages the beneficial properties of each constituent while mitigating their individual limitations.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, fuel blending is the **primary pathway for introduction of alternate fuels** into the existing automotive, industrial, and power-generation infrastructure, because it enables utilization of alternate fuels **without requiring major engine hardware modifications**.

Common KTU-mandated blend nomenclature:

| Blend | Composition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **E10** | 10% Ethanol + 90% Gasoline | Commercial petrol vehicles |
| **E20** | 20% Ethanol + 80% Gasoline | India Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP) target |
| **E85** | 85% Ethanol + 15% Gasoline | Flex-fuel vehicles (FFVs) |
| **M15** | 15% Methanol + 85% Gasoline | Pilot markets (China) |
| **B5** | 5% Biodiesel + 95% Diesel | Commercial diesel vehicles |
| **B20** | 20% Biodiesel + 80% Diesel | Fleet vehicles, heavy transport |
| **B100** | 100% (Neat) Biodiesel | Modified diesel engines |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Cocktail" Model)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of fuel blending like mixing **fresh orange juice with water** to stretch its supply, or like a **doctor compounding a syrup** by mixing a strong active ingredient with a sweet base. The active ingredient (alternate fuel) is too strong/expensive/unstable to use alone, the base (conventional fuel) is widely available and well-understood, but their mixture (blend) gives you a balanced, usable, affordable product. You also get side-benefits (like vitamins from juice → cleaner emissions from blends) that the individual components did not offer.

This analogy captures the three pillars of blending:
- **Stretching** the conventional fuel supply with renewable content.
- **Diluting** the harsh/inconvenient properties of the pure alternate fuel.
- **Extracting** the synergy of benefits (e.g., ethanol's high octane + gasoline's high energy density).

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Blending

> [!TIP]
> **Key Reference Values (must memorize for KTU exams):**
> - Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Gasoline): **A/F = 14.7:1** (mass basis)
> - Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Ethanol): **A/F = 9.0:1** (oxygenated fuel)
> - Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Diesel): **A/F ≈ 14.5:1**
> - Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Biodiesel): **A/F ≈ 12.5:1** (oxygenated fuel)
> - Lower Heating Value (Gasoline): **~44 MJ/kg**
> - Lower Heating Value (Ethanol): **~26.8 MJ/kg**
> - Lower Heating Value (Diesel): **~42.5 MJ/kg**
> - Lower Heating Value (Biodiesel): **~37 MJ/kg**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Blending Works

The underlying scientific rationale for blending rests on **three pillars** of engineering logic:

**Step 1 — Property Modulation (The Arithmetic Mean Principle)**
Most thermophysical properties of a homogeneous blend can be approximated by the **mass-weighted** or **volume-weighted arithmetic mean** of the parent fuels. This is *not* exact for all properties (e.g., viscosity shows non-linear behavior), but serves as a robust first-order KTU-acceptable approximation.

**Step 2 — Additive and Non-Additive Synergy**
Certain properties (e.g., octane number) exhibit a **non-linear blending response**, often modeled by blending octane numbers (RON of mixtures does not follow simple linear averaging). The engine sees a "boosted" property beyond the arithmetic expectation.

**Step 3 — Phase Stability and Miscibility Window**
Blends must remain **single-phase** across the operating temperature range. For example, ethanol-gasoline blends above E10 can suffer **phase separation** when water ingress occurs (≈ 0.5% water tolerance). This is a critical *disadvantage* and a KTU favourite question.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the most exam-relevant equations for "Advantages of Blending".**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{blend} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot P_i$ | Mass-weighted property blending rule | $x_i$ = mass fraction, $P_i$ = property of component $i$ |
| 2 | $P_{blend} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \cdot P_i$ | Volume-weighted property blending rule | $v_i$ = volume fraction |
| 3 | $LHV_{blend} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot LHV_i$ | Lower Heating Value of blend on mass basis | kJ/kg or MJ/kg |
| 4 | $\rho_{blend} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \cdot \rho_i$ | Density of blend (volume-weighted) | kg/m³ |
| 5 | $\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{blend} = \dfrac{1}{\sum \dfrac{x_i}{(A/F)_i}}$ | Stoichiometric A/F of blend (mass basis) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $BSFC_{blend} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{th} \cdot LHV_{blend}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption of blend | kg/kWh |
| 7 | $E_{den,blend} = \sum_{i} v_i \cdot \rho_i \cdot LHV_i$ | Volumetric energy density of blend | MJ/L |
| 8 | $CO_2_{blend} = \sum_{i} m_i \cdot C_{frac,i} \cdot \dfrac{44}{12}$ | Specific CO₂ emission per kg of fuel blend | kg CO₂/kg fuel |
| 9 | $RON_{blend} = RON_{base} + \Delta RON_{additive}$ | Research Octane Number after blending (additive model) | Dimensionless |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility of the Blending Approach

- **Bridge technology:** Enables renewable fuel adoption *before* engine technology matures.
- **Logistics reuse:** Leverages the existing liquid-fuel pipeline, storage, and dispensing infrastructure (saves CAPEX).
- **Regulatory compliance:** Provides a low-cost path to meet emission norms (BS-VI, Euro-VI) and renewable fuel mandates (e.g., India's **E20 target by 2025-2030**).
- **Tunable property design:** Engineers can "dial in" desired cetane, octane, viscosity, or volatility by adjusting blend ratio.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** A 1% reduction in crude oil imports via ethanol blending saves India approximately **$1.5 billion annually** at current import parity prices — this is a high-value KTU viva answer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Blended Heating Value (Mass-Weighted Mean)

**Problem Setup:** A blend consists of **fraction $m_1$ (mass)** of fuel 1 (LHV₁) and **fraction $m_2$ (mass)** of fuel 2 (LHV₂). Derive the blended LHV.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Let $m$ be the total mass of the blend. Then $m_1 = m \cdot x_1$ and $m_2 = m \cdot x_2$, with the constraint $x_1 + x_2 = 1$.

The total energy released upon combustion of the blend is the sum of energies from each component:

$$
E_{total} = m_1 \cdot LHV_1 + m_2 \cdot LHV_2
$$

The specific (per unit mass) heating value of the blend is then:

$$
LHV_{blend} = \frac{E_{total}}{m} = \frac{m_1 \cdot LHV_1 + m_2 \cdot LHV_2}{m}
$$

Substituting $m_1 = m \cdot x_1$ and $m_2 = m \cdot x_2$:

$$
LHV_{blend} = \frac{(m \cdot x_1) LHV_1 + (m \cdot x_2) LHV_2}{m}
$$

Canceling $m$ from numerator and denominator:

$$
LHV_{blend} = x_1 \cdot LHV_1 + x_2 \cdot LHV_2
$$

**Result:** The blended LHV is a **mass-weighted arithmetic mean** of the parent LHV values.

> This confirms Formula #3 of the cheat sheet and extends naturally to n-component systems.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoichiometric A/F Ratio of a Two-Fuel Blend

**Problem Setup:** Derive the stoichiometric Air-Fuel ratio of a binary blend of fuel A and fuel B.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

For each pure fuel, the stoichiometric oxygen requirement (kg O₂ per kg fuel) is denoted $(A/F)_A$ and $(A/F)_B$ in mass terms (which represents air mass per fuel mass). The reciprocal, $1/(A/F)_i$, is the specific oxygen (or air) demand per unit mass of fuel.

For 1 kg of blend, fuel A contributes $x_A$ kg and fuel B contributes $x_B = (1-x_A)$ kg. The total stoichiometric air required is the **sum of the individual air requirements** (since the constituents burn independently in the cylinder charge):

$$
A_{blend} = x_A \cdot (A/F)_A \cdot 1 + x_B \cdot (A/F)_B \cdot 1
$$

Wait — careful: $(A/F)_i$ *is* the air mass per kg of fuel $i$. So the air needed for $x_A$ kg of fuel A is $x_A \cdot (A/F)_A$, and similarly for B. Thus:

$$
A_{blend, \text{ per kg blend}} = x_A \cdot (A/F)_A + x_B \cdot (A/F)_B
$$

Since 1 kg of blend requires exactly $A_{blend}$ kg of air, the blend's stoichiometric A/F is **this air mass per 1 kg of fuel blend**:

$$
\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{blend} = x_A \cdot \left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_A + x_B \cdot \left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_B
$$

However, for the *more accurate* mixing rule used in combustion engineering (mass of air per mass of blend in a *common charge* assumption):

$$
\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{blend}^{-1} = x_A \cdot \left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_A^{-1} + x_B \cdot \left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_B^{-1}
$$

This second form is the **harmonic mean** and is generally preferred because each component oxidizes independently in a common air charge.

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{blend} = \left[ x_A \cdot \left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_A^{-1} + x_B \cdot \left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_B^{-1} \right]^{-1}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Numerical Example: E20 Blend Properties

**Problem:** Compute (a) the blended LHV, (b) the blended density, and (c) the stoichiometric A/F of an E20 blend (20% ethanol by volume, 80% gasoline by volume). Use the following data:

- Ethanol: LHV = 26.8 MJ/kg, ρ = 789 kg/m³, (A/F) = 9.0
- Gasoline: LHV = 44.0 MJ/kg, ρ = 750 kg/m³, (A/F) = 14.7

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert volume fractions to mass fractions.**

Consider 1 L of blend: 0.2 L ethanol + 0.8 L gasoline.

Mass of ethanol: $m_e = 0.2 \times 789 = 157.8$ g
Mass of gasoline: $m_g = 0.8 \times 750 = 600.0$ g
Total mass: $m = 757.8$ g

Mass fractions:

$$
x_e = \frac{157.8}{757.8} = 0.2083, \quad x_g = \frac{600.0}{757.8} = 0.7917
$$

**Step 2 — Blended LHV (Formula 3, mass-weighted).**

$$
LHV_{blend} = (0.2083)(26.8) + (0.7917)(44.0)
$$

$$
LHV_{blend} = 5.5824 + 34.8348 = 40.42 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Blended density (Formula 4, volume-weighted).**

$$
\rho_{blend} = (0.2)(789) + (0.8)(750) = 157.8 + 600.0 = 757.8 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric A/F (Formula 5, harmonic form).**

$$
\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{blend} = \left[ 0.2083 \cdot \frac{1}{9.0} + 0.7917 \cdot \frac{1}{14.7} \right]^{-1}
$$

Compute inside the brackets:

$$
0.2083 \cdot 0.1111 = 0.02315
$$
$$
0.7917 \cdot 0.06803 = 0.05386
$$
$$
\text{Sum} = 0.07701
$$

$$
\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{blend} = \frac{1}{0.07701} = 12.99 \approx 13.0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final answers for E20 blend:**
> - **LHV ≈ 40.42 MJ/kg** (lower than pure gasoline's 44 MJ/kg → higher fuel consumption expected)
> - **ρ ≈ 757.8 kg/m³** (slightly higher than pure gasoline)
> - **(A/F) ≈ 13.0** (lower than gasoline's 14.7 → engine ECU must be recalibrated for proper fueling)

This numerical example is **highly exam-likely** at KTU — memorize the procedure, not just the answers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
