## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*List any **six** advantages of blending alternate fuels with conventional fuels in internal combustion engines.*

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 mark each):**

1. **Reduction in tailpipe emissions** — Particularly CO, unburnt HC, and particulate matter (PM) due to the oxygenated nature of alcohol/biodiesel fuels.
2. **Improved octane rating** — Ethanol has RON = 108, raising the blend's resistance to knock in SI engines.
3. **Renewable energy utilization** — The bio-component is derived from agricultural feedstocks (sugarcane, soybean, rapeseed), making the fuel partially carbon-neutral.
4. **Lower life-cycle CO₂ emissions** — The bio-component's CO₂ is reabsorbed by next-season crops, closing the carbon loop.
5. **Reduced dependence on crude oil imports** — Improves national energy security and foreign exchange reserves.
6. **No major engine modification required** — Existing engines can run on E10/B5 with only minor fuel-system material upgrades (e.g., stainless steel lines to resist ethanol's corrosivity).
7. *(Bonus point — pick any 6)*: Better lubricity, improved cold-start with additives, regional rural employment through feedstock cultivation, compliance with biofuel mandates.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Explain how ethanol blending improves the octane number of gasoline. State the stoichiometric A/F ratio of pure ethanol.*

**Model Answer:**

Ethanol has a **Research Octane Number (RON) of ~108** and a **Motor Octane Number (MON) of ~92**, both significantly higher than regular gasoline (RON 88–92, MON 82–85). When blended into gasoline, ethanol acts as a **high-octane oxygenate** that resists auto-ignition (knocking) in SI engines. The blend's effective RON is computed using a **blending octane number** model, which gives:

$$
RON_{blend} = RON_{base} + \Delta RON \cdot x_{ethanol}
$$

with $\Delta RON$ typically +5 to +10 per 10% ethanol addition. The result is an **anti-knock margin gain** that allows higher compression ratios, more advanced ignition timing, and therefore **better thermal efficiency**.

**Stoichiometric A/F ratio of pure ethanol: 9.0:1** (mass basis).
Reason: Ethanol's molecular formula $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$ requires only 3 moles of O₂ per mole of ethanol (vs. 11 for octane), and its molecular weight is 46 g/mol, leading to a low A/F ratio that must be accommodated in ECU fueling maps.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

**(a)** *With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **fuel-blending process architecture** for an E20 ethanol-gasoline blending plant. List the **key quality-control parameters** monitored at each stage.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A blend contains 30% by mass ethanol and 70% by mass gasoline. Given that ethanol has LHV = 26.8 MJ/kg and (A/F) = 9.0, while gasoline has LHV = 44 MJ/kg and (A/F) = 14.7, compute the **(i)** LHV of the blend, **(ii)** the stoichiometric A/F ratio of the blend, and **(iii)** the brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC) if the brake thermal efficiency is 30%.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Schematic and Process Description (7 marks):**

*Schematic:* (Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1)

*Process description (6 key points, ~1 mark each):*

1. **Base fuel storage** — Gasoline and ethanol are stored in **separate atmospheric tanks** with floating-roof or nitrogen blanketing to prevent moisture ingress and vapor losses.
2. **Metered pumping** — Each fuel is drawn through **positive-displacement or mass flow meters** that regulate the volume/mass ratio to within ±0.1% accuracy.
3. **In-line static mixing** — The two streams converge at an **in-line static mixer** (e.g., Kenics-type helical elements) that produces a homogeneous single-phase blend.
4. **Additive injection** — Corrosion inhibitors, anti-oxidants, and **detergent additives** are dosed at ppm levels.
5. **Quality control** — A **lab-grade analysis** is performed every batch: density (ASTM D1298), water content (Karl Fischer, ASTM D6304), acidity (ASTM D974), and RON (ASTM D2699).
6. **Storage and dispatch** — Blended fuel is stored in **dedicated tanks** (to prevent cross-contamination) and dispatched via the existing fuel-station network.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

*Given:* $x_e = 0.30$ (mass), $x_g = 0.70$ (mass), $\eta_{th} = 0.30$.

**(i) Blended LHV (2 marks):**

$$
LHV_{blend} = (0.30)(26.8) + (0.70)(44.0)
$$

$$
LHV_{blend} = 8.04 + 30.80 = 38.84 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$

**(ii) Blended (A/F) (2 marks):**

$$
\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{blend} = \left[ 0.30 \cdot \frac{1}{9.0} + 0.70 \cdot \frac{1}{14.7} \right]^{-1}
$$

$$
= \left[ 0.03333 + 0.04762 \right]^{-1} = \left[ 0.08095 \right]^{-1}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{blend} = 12.35
$$

**(iii) BSFC (3 marks):**

Using Formula 6 from the cheat sheet:

$$
BSFC = \frac{3600}{\eta_{th} \cdot LHV_{blend}}
$$

Convert $LHV_{blend} = 38.84$ MJ/kg = $38840$ kJ/kg:

$$
BSFC = \frac{3600}{0.30 \times 38840} = \frac{3600}{11652} = 0.309 \text{ kg/kWh}
$$

> *Note: This is higher than gasoline's typical BSFC (~0.25 kg/kWh) due to ethanol's lower calorific value.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Internal Choice

**(a)** *Compare the **advantages and disadvantages** of E20 (ethanol-gasoline) and B20 (biodiesel-diesel) blends in terms of (i) engine modifications required, (ii) emissions impact, (iii) cold-start behavior, and (iv) material compatibility.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A 1000 kg batch of **B20 blend** is to be prepared. Biodiesel has density 880 kg/m³ and LHV 37 MJ/kg. Diesel has density 840 kg/m³ and LHV 42.5 MJ/kg. Calculate:*
   - *(i) The volume of each component required.* **(2 marks)**
   - *(ii) The volumetric energy density of the blend in MJ/L.* **(2 marks)**
   - *(iii) The mass of CO₂ produced per kg of blend, assuming the carbon content is 75% for diesel and 77% for biodiesel. Use $\text{CO}_2 = C_{frac} \times \frac{44}{12}$.* **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | E20 (Ethanol-Gasoline) | B20 (Biodiesel-Diesel) |
|---|---|---|
| **Engine modifications** | Minor — may need stainless fuel lines, upgraded fuel pump, recalibrated ECU | Minor — may need heated fuel lines in cold climates, rubber hose replacement |
| **Emissions impact** | ↓ CO (~30%), ↓ HC (~10%), ↑ NOx slightly due to lean burn | ↓ PM (~20%), ↓ CO (~15%), ↓ HC (~10%), slight ↑ NOx |
| **Cold-start behavior** | **Poor** — ethanol's low vapor pressure (17 kPa at 38 °C) causes cold-start issues; requires cold-start enrichment | **Moderate** — biodiesel's higher cloud point (~5–15 °C) causes waxing in cold weather; needs winter-grade B20 or heated lines |
| **Material compatibility** | **Aggressive** — ethanol attacks aluminum, natural rubber, and certain plastics; needs fluorocarbon seals | **Mildly aggressive** — biodiesel softens nitrile rubber; B20 is generally safe for modern fuel systems |

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** B20 = 20% biodiesel + 80% diesel (by **volume**, standard convention).

**(i) Volume of each component (2 marks):**

Take a basis of 1 m³ of B20 blend. Then:
- Volume of biodiesel: $V_b = 0.20 \times 1.0 = 0.20$ m³ = 200 L
- Volume of diesel: $V_d = 0.80 \times 1.0 = 0.80$ m³ = 800 L

Masses:
- Mass of biodiesel: $m_b = 0.20 \times 880 = 176$ kg
- Mass of diesel: $m_d = 0.80 \times 840 = 672$ kg
- Total mass: $848$ kg

**(ii) Volumetric energy density (2 marks):**

Using Formula 7:

$$
E_{den} = v_b \cdot \rho_b \cdot LHV_b + v_d \cdot \rho_d \cdot LHV_d
$$

$$
= (0.20)(880)(37) + (0.80)(840)(42.5)
$$

$$
= 6512 + 28560 = 35072 \text{ MJ/m}^3 = 35.07 \text{ MJ/L}
$$

**(iii) CO₂ per kg of blend (3 marks):**

Mass fractions:
- $x_b = 176/848 = 0.2075$
- $x_d = 672/848 = 0.7925$

Using Formula 8 (mass-weighted):

$$
CO_2 = x_b \cdot C_{frac,b} \cdot \frac{44}{12} + x_d \cdot C_{frac,d} \cdot \frac{44}{12}
$$

$$
= 0.2075 \times 0.77 \times 3.667 + 0.7925 \times 0.75 \times 3.667
$$

$$
= 0.5860 + 2.1798 = 2.766 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ per kg blend}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and where marks are silently deducted:**
>
> 1. **Mixing volume and mass fractions** — The E20 is 20% by **volume**, but the LHV must be blended on a **mass** basis. Many students blindly apply volume fractions to all properties → full 1–2 mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the harmonic-mean form for A/F** — Some texts use a simple arithmetic mean for A/F, but the **correct physical model** is the harmonic mean (reciprocal of the sum of mass-fraction-weighted reciprocal A/Fs). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 3. **Unit conversion slips** — $BSFC$ in KTU papers is expected in **kg/kWh**. Forget to convert seconds to hours (×3600) and MJ to kJ (×1000) → wrong answer by a factor of 1000.
>
> 4. **Ignoring material compatibility in advantages** — E20 and B20 are NOT drop-in fuels at high concentrations. Examiners award 1–2 marks for students who mention **"with minor engine recalibration/material upgrades"** when listing advantages.
>
> 5. **Not citing a numerical example** — In "explain the advantages" questions, an unattached generic answer scores 4–5 marks. Adding a *specific numerical example* (e.g., "BSFC of E20 is 0.31 kg/kWh vs. 0.25 for gasoline") pushes the answer to 7+ marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision bullets — read 5 times before exam:**
>
> - **Definition:** Blending = homogeneous physical mixing of conventional + alternate fuel in controlled ratios.
> - **E10 / E20 / E85** are ethanol-gasoline blends; **B5 / B20 / B100** are biodiesel-diesel blends.
> - **Key advantages:** Emission reduction, octane/cetane improvement, renewability, no major engine mods, energy security, policy compliance.
> - **LHV blending rule (mass basis):** $LHV_{blend} = \sum x_i \cdot LHV_i$ (Formula 3).
> - **Density blending rule (volume basis):** $\rho_{blend} = \sum v_i \cdot \rho_i$ (Formula 4).
> - **Stoichiometric A/F rule (harmonic form):** $(A/F)_{blend}^{-1} = \sum x_i \cdot (A/F)_i^{-1}$ (Formula 5).
> - **BSFC formula:** $BSFC = 3600 / (\eta_{th} \cdot LHV_{blend})$ — answer in **kg/kWh**.
> - **Standard A/F values to memorize:** Gasoline 14.7, Ethanol 9.0, Diesel 14.5, Biodiesel 12.5.
> - **Standard LHV values to memorize:** Gasoline 44, Ethanol 26.8, Diesel 42.5, Biodiesel 37 (all in MJ/kg).
> - **Phase separation is a critical DISadvantage of E20+ blends with water ingress.**
> - **Cold-start is a known DISadvantage of ethanol blends** — requires cold-start enrichment.
> - **Material compatibility:** Ethanol attacks aluminum, rubber; Biodiesel softens nitrile rubber.
> - **India's E20 mandate:** Target year ~2025-2030; expected forex savings ≈ $1.5 B per 1% blending.
> - **Numerical format expected:** Always show given data → write formula → substitute → compute → state result with units.
> - **Examiner's pet topic:** Derive blended A/F using harmonic mean — this distinguishes toppers from average students.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance and Emission of Biodiesel Fuelled Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Performance and Emission of Biodiesel Fuelled Vehicles ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Biodiesel** is a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids derived from renewable biological sources such as vegetable oils (soybean, sunflower, palm, jatropha, pongamia/karanja), animal fats, or used cooking oil through a chemical process called **transesterification**. When used to fuel Compression Ignition (CI) engines—either in its pure form (B100) or blended with conventional petroleum diesel (e.g., B20 = 20% biodiesel + 80% diesel)—it forms a class of vehicles known as **Biodiesel Fuelled Vehicles (BFVs)**. **Performance** refers to the engine's ability to convert the chemical energy of the fuel into useful mechanical work, evaluated through parameters like **Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE)**, **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)**, **brake power**, **brake mean effective pressure (BMEP)**, and **torque output**. **Emission** refers to the unwanted byproducts expelled from the engine exhaust, including **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**, **Unburned Hydrocarbons (UHC/HC)**, **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)**, **Particulate Matter (PM)**, **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)**, and **smoke opacity**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The study of biodiesel vehicles is critical because India has mandated **B5 blending (5% biodiesel)** under the **Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP)** and **Biodiesel Blending Programme** by the Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas, making this a high-weightage topic for both theory and viva-voce. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a campfire. If you pour water on it, it smokes heavily and produces lots of unburnt residue. If you add dry, oxygen-rich material, it burns cleaner and hotter. **Biodiesel is like that oxygen-rich "dry wood"** for a diesel engine. Conventional diesel fuel is made of long hydrocarbon chains (purely carbon and hydrogen). When burnt, some carbon doesn't get enough oxygen to become CO₂, leading to soot (PM) and CO. Biodiesel, on the other hand, contains **bound oxygen (≈10–11% by mass)** within its molecular structure—think of it as fuel that "carries its own oxygen supply." This intrinsic oxygen promotes **more complete combustion**, reducing smoke and CO. However, this richer oxidation environment also creates more **NOx** (formed when high temperatures meet abundant oxygen and nitrogen). > [!NOTE] > **Simple Analogy:** Think of biodiesel as a "pre-spiced dish" — the spices (oxygen) are already mixed in, so the cooking (combustion) is faster and more uniform, producing a "cleaner taste" (fewer emissions) but sometimes a slightly "hotter flame" (higher NOx). ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Performance & Emission Analysis | Metric | Standard Unit | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Cetane Number (CN)** | Dimensionless | Ignition quality | | **Calorific Value (CV)** | **kJ/kg** or **MJ/kg** | Energy content | | **Density (ρ)** | **kg/m³** | Mass per unit volume | | **Kinematic Viscosity (ν)** | **mm²/s (cSt)** | Flow characteristics | | **Flash Point** | **°C** | Safety parameter | | **BSFC** | **kg/kWh** | Fuel economy | | **BTE** | **%** | Thermal efficiency | | **Smoke Opacity** | **% (BSU)** | Visible smoke measurement | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Value:** Pure biodiesel (B100) has a calorific value of approximately **37–40 MJ/kg**, compared to **42–45 MJ/kg** for petro-diesel. This 8–10% lower energy density is the root cause of most performance deviations. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Performance Characteristics — The "Big Four" Parameters #### 2.1.1 Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE) BTE measures how effectively the engine converts the fuel's chemical energy into brake power (useful work at the crankshaft). - **Why BTE is slightly LOWER for biodiesel (typically 1–4% drop):** - Biodiesel has **lower calorific value** (≈37–40 MJ/kg) than diesel (≈42–45 MJ/kg). - Higher **density and viscosity** cause poor atomization, leading to incomplete combustion in some operating zones. - However, the **bound oxygen** partially compensates, often keeping BTE nearly comparable—especially at higher loads. #### 2.1.2 Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) BSFC is the mass of fuel consumed per unit of brake power output per hour. - **Why BSFC is HIGHER for biodiesel (typically 10–15% increase):** - Lower calorific value → engine demands MORE fuel to produce the same power. - The relation: $\text{BSFC} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{Calorific Value}}$ for a given power output. #### 2.1.3 Engine Torque and Brake Power - Brake power output is **slightly lower** (2–5%) for biodiesel due to lower energy density. - Torque curves follow a similar trend, often showing a marginal drop, especially at low speeds where poor atomization dominates. #### 2.1.4 Cold Start Performance - Biodiesel has a **higher cloud point and pour point** than diesel. - In cold climates, it tends to **gel or crystallize**, clogging fuel lines and filters. - Solution: **WINTERIZATION** process and using **anti-gel additives**. ### 2.2 Emission Characteristics — The "Pollutant Pentagon" Biodiesel alters the engine emission profile in a unique way: | Pollutant | Trend with Biodiesel | Reason | |---|---|---| | **CO (Carbon Monoxide)** | ↓ 30–50% reduction | Bound oxygen promotes complete combustion | | **UHC (Unburned HC)** | ↓ 20–40% reduction | Better combustion; oxygenated fuel | | **PM (Particulate Matter)** | ↓ 40–60% reduction | Lower aromatic content; oxygen in fuel | | **Smoke (BSU %)** | ↓ 50–70% reduction | Near-complete combustion | | **NOx (Oxides of Nitrogen)** | ↑ 5–15% increase | Higher combustion temperature + bound oxygen | | **CO₂ (well-to-wheel)** | ≈ Net 50–80% lower | Closed CO₂ cycle from biomass | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept — The NOx Trade-off:** This is the **single most examined trade-off** in biodiesel engine studies. Biodiesel reduces almost every emission EXCEPT NOx, which increases. Examiners frequently ask: "Why does NOx increase despite cleaner combustion?" The answer: **Extended ignition delay** leads to a rapid, near-simultaneous energy release (higher peak cylinder temperature), and the **bound oxygen** accelerates the Zeldovich mechanism for NOx formation. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{BTE} = \dfrac{\text{Brake Power} \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times \text{CV}} \times 100\%$ | Brake Thermal Efficiency | **%** | | 2 | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{\text{Brake Power}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | **kg/kWh** | | 3 | $\text{BSEC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times \text{CV}}{\text{Brake Power}}$ | Brake Specific Energy Consumption | **kJ/kWh** | | 4 | $\text{BMEP} = \dfrac{W_b}{V_d \times \dfrac{N}{2} \times n_r}$ | Brake Mean Effective Pressure | **bar** | | 5 | $\eta_{\text{vol}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a \times R \times T_{a1}}{P_{a1} \times V_d \times \dfrac{N}{2} \times n_r}$ | Volumetric Efficiency | **%** | | 6 | $\text{SFC}_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{\text{SFC}_{\text{bio}}}{\text{SFC}_{\text{diesel}}}$ | Relative SFC for comparison | Dimensionless | | 7 | $\text{CV}_{\text{blend}} = x \cdot \text{CV}_{\text{bio}} + (1-x) \cdot \text{CV}_{\text{diesel}}$ | Calorific value of blend (mass basis) | **kJ/kg** | | 8 | $\rho_{\text{blend}} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{x}{\rho_{\text{bio}}} + \dfrac{1-x}{\rho_{\text{diesel}}}}$ | Density of blend (volume basis) | **kg/m³** | | 9 | $\nu_{\text{blend}} \approx \nu_{\text{d}}^{1-x} \cdot \nu_{\text{b}}^{x}$ | Viscosity of blend (Arrhenius approx.) | **cSt** | | 10 | $\text{NO}_{x} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{\text{peak}}}\right)$ | Zeldovich mechanism (extended form) | **ppm** | > [!NOTE] > **Critical:** In the table above, the symbol $\vert$ has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to maintain markdown table integrity. For BMEP formula, $n_r$ = number of revolutions per power stroke (2 for 4-stroke, 1 for 2-stroke). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Automotive Industry:** Manufacturers like Tata Motors, Mahindra, and Ashok Leyland have launched vehicles compatible with B5–B20 blends under the **SATAT (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation)** scheme. 2. **Railways:** Indian Railways successfully tested a **5% biodiesel blend (B5)** in a diesel locomotive (WDG4 class) on the Rewari–Rohtak route in 2018 — a landmark KTU viva question. 3. **Aviation:** Biodiesel is being tested as a **Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF)** for jet engines. 4. **Agricultural Pumpsets:** B100 is extensively used in Indian rural agricultural diesel engines due to locally sourced feedstock (pongamia, jatropha). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Relating BSFC Increase to Calorific Value Reduction **Problem Statement:** A diesel engine delivers the same brake power on both petro-diesel and B20 biodiesel blend. Prove analytically that BSFC of B20 is higher than diesel in proportion to the inverse ratio of their calorific values, assuming other losses are negligible. **Step 1 — Start with the fundamental energy balance equation:** The brake power output is the rate at which useful mechanical energy is produced by the engine. This brake power comes from the chemical energy released by the fuel consumed per unit time. $$ P_b = \dot{m}_f \times \text{CV} \times \eta_{\text{th}} $$ where: - $P_b$ = Brake power (kW) - $\dot{m}_f$ = Mass flow rate of fuel (kg/s) - $\text{CV}$ = Calorific value of fuel (kJ/kg) - $\eta_{\text{th}}$ = Thermal efficiency **Step 2 — Define BSFC in terms of fuel flow rate and brake power:** Brake specific fuel consumption is the fuel mass flow rate normalized by the brake power output. $$ \text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute $\dot{m}_f$ from Step 1 into Step 2:** From Step 1, we know $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{P_b}{\text{CV} \times \eta_{\text{th}}}$. Substituting this into the BSFC formula: $$ \text{BSFC} = \dfrac{P_b / (\text{CV} \times \eta_{\text{th}})}{P_b} $$ **Step 4 — Cancel out $P_b$ from numerator and denominator:** $$ \text{BSFC} = \dfrac{1}{\text{CV} \times \eta_{\text{th}}} $$ **Step 5 — State the condition for comparison (constant $P_b$):** If the engine produces the same brake power on diesel and B20, and we assume the thermal efficiency is approximately the same ($\eta_{\text{th,d}} \approx \eta_{\text{th,B20}}$ — a valid approximation for small blend ratios), then: $$ \text{BSFC}_d = \dfrac{1}{\text{CV}_d \times \eta_{\text{th,d}}} $$ $$ \text{BSFC}_{\text{B20}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{CV}_{\text{B20}} \times \eta_{\text{th,B20}}} $$ **Step 6 — Form the ratio:** $$ \dfrac{\text{BSFC}_{\text{B20}}}{\text{BSFC}_d} = \dfrac{\text{CV}_d}{\text{CV}_{\text{B20}}} $$ **Step 7 — Express the calorific value of the blend:** The mass-weighted calorific value of the blend (x = mass fraction of biodiesel): $$ \text{CV}_{\text{B20}} = 0.20 \cdot \text{CV}_{\text{bio}} + 0.80 \cdot \text{CV}_d $$ **Step 8 — Substitute numerical values (Standard Reference):** Take $\text{CV}_{\text{bio}} = 38{,}000$ kJ/kg and $\text{CV}_d = 43{,}000$ kJ/kg: $$ \text{CV}_{\text{B20}} = (0.20 \times 38{,}000) + (0.80 \times 43{,}000) $$ $$ \text{CV}_{\text{B20}} = 7{,}600 + 34{,}400 = 42{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 9 — Compute the BSFC ratio:** $$ \dfrac{\text{BSFC}_{\text{B20}}}{\text{BSFC}_d} = \dfrac{43{,}000}{42{,}000} = 1.0238 $$ **Step 10 — Conclude:** This means B20 consumes **2.38% more fuel by mass** to deliver the same brake power. **Q.E.D.** > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** For B100, this ratio becomes $\frac{43{,}000}{38{,}000} = 1.1316$, implying a **~13.2% increase in BSFC**—matching the empirical "10–15% increase" observed in experimental KTU lab studies. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: NOx Formation — The Zeldovich Mechanism (Simplified) **Step 1:** The thermal (Zeldovich) NOx formation rate is governed by the extended Arrhenius-type expression: $$ \dfrac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{\text{peak}}}\right) \cdot [\text{O}_2]^{0.5} \cdot [\text{N}_2] $$ **Step 2:** Take the natural logarithm to linearize the temperature dependence: $$ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt}\right) = \ln(A) - \dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{\text{peak}}} + 0.5 \ln[\text{O}_2] + \ln[\text{N}_2] $$ **Step 3:** The activation energy for the Zeldovich reaction is $E_a \approx 319$ kJ/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). The temperature-dependent term is: $$ \dfrac{E_a}{R} = \dfrac{319{,}000}{8.314} \approx 38{,}370 \text{ K} $$ **Step 4:** For diesel, typical $T_{\text{peak}} \approx 2200$ K. For B20, the bound oxygen raises $T_{\text{peak}}$ to $\approx 2270$ K (a modest +70 K). **Step 5:** Compute NOx rate ratio (assuming $[\text{O}_2]_{\text{B20}} > [\text{O}_2]_d$ by a factor of 1.05): $$ \dfrac{(d[\text{NO}]/dt)_{\text{B20}}}{(d[\text{NO}]/dt)_d} = (1.05)^{0.5} \times \exp\!\left[\dfrac{38{,}370}{2200} - \dfrac{38{,}370}{2270}\right] $$ **Step 6:** Calculate the exponential term: $$ \dfrac{38{,}370}{2200} = 17.44, \quad \dfrac{38{,}370}{2270} = 16.90 $$ $$ \exp(17.44 - 16.90) = \exp(0.54) \approx 1.716 $$ **Step 7:** Combine: $$ \text{Ratio} = 1.025 \times 1.716 \approx 1.76 $$ **Step 8 — Reconciling with observed data:** This 1.76× theoretical ratio seems too high compared to the observed 5–15% increase. This discrepancy is because in **real engines**, the peak temperature rise is mitigated by: - Heat losses to walls - Lower flame temperature due to biodiesel's higher water content - Lower combustion efficiency in some regimes - Modified ignition delay This explains why practical NOx increase is **modest (~10%)** despite the exponential sensitivity of the Zeldovich mechanism. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Performance vs. Emission Trade-off | Property Change | BTE | BSFC | NOx | PM | CO | HC | Smoke | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Lower CV** | ↓ | ↑ | — | — | — | — | — | | **Higher Density** | ↓ | — | — | — | — | — | — | | **Higher Viscosity** | ↓ | ↑ | — | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | | **Bound Oxygen** | ↑ | — | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | | **Higher CN** | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | — | ↓ | | **Lower Aromatics** | — | — | ↓ | ↓↓ | — | — | ↓↓ | | **Higher Flash Pt** | — | — | (Safety+) | — | — | — | — | Legend: ↓ = decrease, ↑ = increase, — = negligible effect ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Biodiesel Performance–Emission System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feedstock Selection
Vegetable Oil / Animal Fat] --> B[Transesterification
Methanol + NaOH Catalyst] B --> C[Purified Biodiesel
B100 FAMEs] C --> D[Blending Stage
B5 / B20 / B100] D --> E[CI Engine Fuel System
Injection Pump + Injector] E --> F[Combustion Chamber
High T and P] F --> G1[Performance Output
BTE / BSFC / Power] F --> G2[Emission Formation
CO / NOx / PM / HC] G1 --> H1[Brake Power Calculation
Dynamometer Reading] H1 --> I1[Efficiency Plot
vs Load Percentage] G2 --> H2[Exhaust Gas Analyzer
NDIR / Chemiluminescence] H2 --> I2[Emission Compliance Check
BS IV / BS VI Norms] I1 --> J[KTU Performance Report] I2 --> J F --> K[Energy Losses
Heat / Friction / Exhaust] K --> I1 ``` ### 4.2 Process Flow: Biodiesel Combustion and Emission Formation Pathways ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph COMBUSTION[Combustion Chamber Environment] direction TB P1[High Cylinder Pressure
60 to 80 bar] --> P2[Peak Temperature
2200 to 2400 K] P2 --> P3[Ignition Delay Period
2 to 5 ms] end subgraph OXY[Oxygen Mediated Reactions] direction TB Q1[Bound O2 in Biodiesel
10 to 11 percent by mass] --> Q2[Complete Oxidation of C to CO2] Q2 --> Q3[Reduced CO and HC Emissions] end subgraph NOXPATH[NOx Formation Zone] direction TB R1[High T Peak Region] --> R2[Zeldovich Reactions
N2 plus O gives NO] R2 --> R3[Thermal NOx Increase] end subgraph PMPATH[PM Formation Zone] direction TB S1[Fuel Spray Atomization] --> S2[Local Rich Zones] S2 --> S3[Soot Precursors] S3 --> S4[Particulate Matter PM] end COMBUSTION --> OXY COMBUSTION --> NOXPATH COMBUSTION --> PMPATH OXY --> RESULT[Net Engine Output
Performance plus Emissions] NOXPATH --> RESULT PMPATH --> RESULT ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Biodiesel Vehicle Powertrain ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Biodiesel Storage Tank
With Heating Coil] --> B[Fuel Filter Unit
Heated Type] B --> C[Transfer Pump] C --> D[Inline Fuel Heater
Reduces Viscosity] D --> E[Injection Pump
Rotary or Inline] E --> F[Common Rail or Conventional Injector] F --> G[Combustion Chamber
CI Engine Cylinder] G --> H[Turbocharger Optional] G --> I[Piston and Crank Assembly] I --> J[Clutch and Transmission] J --> K[Final Drive and Wheels] G --> L[Exhaust Manifold] L --> M[DOC Diesel Oxidation Catalyst] M --> N[DPF Diesel Particulate Filter] N --> O[NOx After Treatment
SCR or EGR] O --> P[Tailpipe Emission Compliance] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Safety Notes:** All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., node1, stepA). Reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph` have been avoided as standalone node names. All node labels use clean alphanumeric uppercase text only—no markdown bold/italics or HTML inside the quoted labels—to ensure Mermaid compiles cleanly. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List any three performance differences between biodiesel (B20) and conventional diesel in a CI engine.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE):** B20 shows a marginal decrease (1–4%) compared to diesel due to its lower calorific value and slightly higher viscosity, which causes poorer atomization. 2. **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC):** B20 exhibits approximately 2–4% higher BSFC because the lower heating value demands more fuel mass to produce equivalent brake power. 3. **Brake Power Output:** A slight reduction (2–5%) is observed in B20 operation due to the combined effect of lower energy density and incomplete atomization in certain load zones. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark per valid point with brief justification. Total: 3 marks. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Explain why NOx emissions tend to increase when biodiesel is used as fuel in a CI engine.** **Model Answer:** NOx formation in CI engines is primarily governed by the **Zeldovich (thermal) mechanism**, which is exponentially sensitive to peak combustion temperature. Biodiesel leads to higher NOx because: - **Bound oxygen content (≈10–11% by mass):** The intramolecular oxygen accelerates the oxidation of N₂ to NO, supplying O atoms at the high-temperature reaction sites. - **Higher cetane number and shorter ignition delay:** This causes a more rapid, near-instantaneous energy release, raising peak cylinder temperature. - **Advanced combustion phasing:** The earlier start of combustion often pushes the heat release closer to TDC, increasing peak local temperatures. This results in a **5–15% increase in NOx** emissions, which is the well-known **"biodiesel NOx trade-off."** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for stating the Zeldovich mechanism, 1 mark for bound oxygen effect, 1 mark for peak temperature argument. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) **ESE Module 2 – Module Internal Choice** --- **Q.A [14 Marks]:** **(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss in detail the **performance characteristics** of a CI engine fuelled with biodiesel blends (B20 and B100) compared to conventional diesel. Cover BTE, BSFC, brake power, and torque variations with load. **(b)** [7 Marks] With the help of a labeled graph and a **comparative table**, explain the emission characteristics of biodiesel (CO, HC, PM, NOx, and smoke). State the reason for each emission trend. #### Model Solution for Q.A (a): **Performance Characteristics of Biodiesel in CI Engine:** 1. **Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE):** - For B20: Marginal reduction of 1–4% compared to diesel. The bound oxygen partially compensates for the lower calorific value. - For B100: Reduction of 3–7%, more pronounced at part-load due to poor atomization. - At full load, BTE values often converge because of better combustion conditions. 2. **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC):** - B20: Increases by 2–4% (mass basis). - B100: Increases by 10–15% (mass basis). - Cause: Lower calorific value (~37–40 MJ/kg vs 42–45 MJ/kg for diesel). - On **volumetric basis**, BSFC may be similar or even slightly lower due to higher density of biodiesel. 3. **Brake Power and Torque:** - Slight drop of 2–5% in brake power for B20. - Torque curve shows minor depression, especially at low rpm where atomization is poor. - At high loads, the difference narrows considerably. 4. **Cold Start and Idling:** - Higher viscosity and pour point cause cold-start difficulty. - White smoke may appear at startup until the fuel warms up. **Graphical Representation (text description):** A typical **BTE vs Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP)** plot shows diesel slightly above B20 and B100, with curves converging at higher loads. The **BSFC vs BMEP** plot shows the inverse trend—BSFC values for biodiesel blends lying above the diesel curve. #### Model Solution for Q.A (b): **Emission Characteristics of Biodiesel — Comparative Table:** | Pollutant | Diesel Baseline | B20 | B100 | Reason for Change | |---|---|---|---|---| | **CO** | 100% | ↓ 30–40% | ↓ 40–50% | Bound oxygen promotes complete CO → CO₂ oxidation | | **UHC** | 100% | ↓ 20–30% | ↓ 30–40% | Higher cetane number, better combustion | | **PM** | 100% | ↓ 20–30% | ↓ 40–60% | Lower aromatic content + bound oxygen reduces soot | | **NOx** | 100% | ↑ 5–10% | ↑ 10–15% | Higher peak temperature + bound oxygen | | **Smoke (BSU)** | 100% | ↓ 40–50% | ↓ 50–70% | Near-elimination of rich combustion zones | | **CO₂ (WTW)** | 100% | ↓ 15–20% | ↓ 50–80% | Biogenic carbon cycle | **Graph Sketch (text description):** A **bar chart** with pollutant type on x-axis and percentage change on y-axis. Diesel bar at 100% baseline. B20 and B100 bars below baseline for CO, UHC, PM, smoke, and above baseline for NOx. **Conclusion:** Biodiesel is an **environmentally superior fuel** with one notable drawback—NOx increase, which can be mitigated through **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)**, **selective catalytic reduction (SCR)**, and **retarded injection timing**. --- **Q.B [14 Marks] — Alternative Choice:** **(a)** [7 Marks] What is **transesterification**? Explain the process with a balanced chemical equation. Discuss how the properties of biodiesel (viscosity, density, cetane number, calorific value) influence engine performance and emissions. **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of **"biodiesel NOx trade-off"** in detail. Describe **at least three strategies** to control NOx emissions when using biodiesel. #### Model Solution for Q.B (a): **Transesterification:** Transesterification is the chemical reaction in which triglyceride molecules (the main constituents of vegetable oils and animal fats) react with a short-chain alcohol (typically methanol or ethanol) in the presence of a catalyst (NaOH or KOH) to yield **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAMEs)**—which is biodiesel—and glycerol as a byproduct. **Balanced Chemical Equation:** $$ \text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH (cat.)}} 3\,\text{RCOOCH}_3 + \text{Glycerol} $$ **Property–Performance/Emission Influence:** | Biodiesel Property | Typical Value | Effect on Engine | |---|---|---| | **Kinematic Viscosity** | 3.5–5.0 cSt (B100) | Higher than diesel (2–3 cSt). Poor atomization → ↑ BSFC, ↑ PM at low loads | | **Density** | 860–900 kg/m³ | Higher → more mass per unit volume injected → ↓ BSFC (volumetric basis) | | **Cetane Number** | 50–65 | Higher than diesel (45–55) → shorter ignition delay, smoother combustion → ↓ HC, ↓ CO | | **Calorific Value** | 37–40 MJ/kg | Lower than diesel (42–45 MJ/kg) → ↑ BSFC, slight ↓ BTE | | **Flash Point** | 130–170°C | Much higher than diesel (60–80°C) → safer handling, storage | | **Oxygen Content** | 10–11% by mass | Intramolecular O₂ → ↓ CO, ↓ PM, ↑ NOx | #### Model Solution for Q.B (b): **The Biodiesel NOx Trade-off:** Biodiesel reduces nearly every regulated emission **except NOx**, which increases by 5–15%. This paradoxical behavior is called the **"biodiesel NOx trade-off"** and is rooted in: - **Higher in-cylinder peak temperature** due to faster energy release. - **Intramolecular oxygen** accelerating Zeldovich NO formation. - **Advanced combustion phasing** from higher cetane number. **Three Strategies to Control NOx:** 1. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR):** - Recirculates 5–15% of exhaust gas back into the intake. - Dilutes the intake charge, lowering peak flame temperature. - Can reduce NOx by 30–60%. 2. **Retarded Injection Timing:** - Shifting injection 2–4° BTDC delays the combustion peak, reducing peak temperature. - Trade-off: slight decrease in BTE (1–2%) and slight increase in PM. 3. **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) After-Treatment:** - Injects urea (AdBlue) into the exhaust stream. - Reduces NOx to N₂ and H₂O via the reaction: $4\text{NO} + 4\text{NH}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{N}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$ - Achieves >90% NOx conversion efficiency. **Other strategies:** Water injection, low-temperature combustion (LTC), and use of cetane number improvers to lengthen ignition delay and reduce temperature spikes. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting to mention bound oxygen content** (10–11% by mass) when explaining emission reductions — this is the SINGLE most missed keyword in answer sheets. Examiners explicitly look for it. > 2. **Writing "Biodiesel has higher calorific value"** — this is FALSE. Always state biodiesel has ~8–10% LOWER calorific value. > 3. **Confusing Cetane Number with Octane Number.** Biodiesel has HIGH cetane (50–65) and should NEVER be discussed in terms of octane. > 4. **Failing to state the NOx trade-off explicitly.** When asked "advantages of biodiesel," students list CO/PM/HC reductions but forget to acknowledge NOx increase — losing 1–2 marks. > 5. **Omitting units.** Every numerical answer must include proper units (e.g., kJ/kg, kg/kWh, %, cSt). Unanswered units lead to 0.5 mark deductions. > 6. **Forgetting the chemical equation of transesterification.** Examiners expect the balanced equation; partial credit is given for the un-balanced form. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Points:** - [ ] Biodiesel is a **mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids** produced via **transesterification** of vegetable oils/animal fats with methanol. - [ ] Biodiesel's calorific value is **37–40 MJ/kg** — about **8–10% lower** than petro-diesel. - [ ] Biodiesel contains **10–11% bound oxygen** by mass — the key to its emission reduction profile. - [ ] **BTE** decreases marginally (1–4% for B20); **BSFC** increases (2–4% for B20, 10–15% for B100). - [ ] **Cetane number** of biodiesel is higher (50–65) than diesel (45–55), giving smoother combustion. - [ ] **Kinematic viscosity** of biodiesel is higher (3.5–5.0 cSt) than diesel (2–3 cSt), causing atomization issues. - [ ] **Flash point** of biodiesel is much higher (130–170°C) — making it safer to store and transport. - [ ] Emissions trend: **CO ↓, HC ↓, PM ↓, smoke ↓, NOx ↑, CO₂ (WTW) ↓↓**. - [ ] The **NOx increase** is called the **"biodiesel NOx trade-off"** — caused by bound oxygen + higher peak temperature. - [ ] Strategies to control NOx: **EGR, retarded injection timing, SCR, water injection, LTC**. - [ ] **Transesterification** equation: Triglyceride + 3 Methanol → 3 FAMEs + Glycerol (NaOH catalyst). - [ ] **B5** is the current Indian blending mandate (5% biodiesel in diesel). - [ ] **Indian Railways** has successfully tested B5 in diesel locomotives (WDG4 class). - [ ] **SATAT scheme** by GoI promotes biodiesel production from used cooking oil and non-edible oils. - [ ] **Cold-flow properties** of biodiesel are poor — pour point and cloud point are higher; requires winterization for cold climates. - [ ] The **Zeldovich mechanism** governs NOx formation — exponentially sensitive to peak temperature. - [ ] **BSFC (volumetric basis)** may be similar to diesel due to higher density, even though mass BSFC is higher. - [ ] Biodiesel is **biodegradable, non-toxic, and sulphur-free** — a major environmental advantage. - [ ] The **blend ratio** dramatically affects performance: B5 ≈ negligible change; B20 = minor change; B100 = significant change.

topics.title: Hydrogen Fuels - Sources of Hydrogen, Production Methods, Storage and Handling, Hydrogen Induction Techniques in IC Engines, Modifications Required in the Engine for Hydrogen Fuel, Performance and Emission Characteristics, Safety Aspects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Fuels - Sources of Hydrogen, Production Methods, Storage and Handling, Hydrogen Induction Techniques in IC Engines, Modifications Required in the Engine for Hydrogen Fuel, Performance and Emission Characteristics, Safety Aspects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Hydrogen Fuels

## 1.1 Defining Hydrogen as an Alternate IC Engine Fuel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim-aligned):**
> Hydrogen is a colorless, odorless, tasteless, non-metallic, monatomic gas (H₂) under standard conditions, possessing the highest energy-to-mass ratio (≈ **142 MJ/kg**) of any known fuel. As a zero-carbon energy carrier, it is positioned as the most promising long-term substitute for petroleum-based fuels in Internal Combustion (IC) engines under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Alternate Fuels curriculum.

### Physical & Chemical Identity of Molecular Hydrogen

| Parameter | Value | Significance for IC Engines |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | H₂ | Simplest, lightest molecule |
| Molecular Weight | 2.016 g/mol | 14.4× lighter than air → escapes rapidly |
| Density (STP) | **0.0899 kg/m³** | 1/10th of gasoline density; major storage challenge |
| Boiling Point | **−252.87 °C** | Requires cryogenic storage |
| Flame Temperature | ≈ 2045 °C (in air) | Higher than gasoline → causes NOₓ |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | **571 °C** | High — can be stored safely |
| Minimum Ignition Energy | **0.02 mJ** | 1/10th of gasoline → backfire risk |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Combustion Reaction of Hydrogen in Oxygen:**
> 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O + Energy (ΔH = −285.8 kJ/mol at 25 °C, 1 atm).
> The product is *only* water. Hence, hydrogen is a true **zero-carbon fuel**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Energy Balloon" Concept

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Imagine hydrogen as a *"balloon filled with invisible energy"*. If you open the balloon, the energy escapes almost instantly because the molecule is **14× lighter than air** (it literally floats up and away). Unlike coal or petrol, which you can "hold" in a container as a liquid, hydrogen refuses to sit still. To use it as a fuel, we have three strategies:
> 1. **Squeeze the balloon harder** (Compressed gaseous H₂ at 350–700 bar)
> 2. **Cool the balloon until it liquefies** (Cryogenic liquid H₂ at −253 °C)
> 3. **Absorb the gas into a sponge (metal)** (Metal hydrides like LaNi₅ or MgH₂)
> 
> This "squeezing, cooling, or absorbing" is the entire essence of **Hydrogen Storage Engineering**.

---

## 1.3 Why Hydrogen is Considered a Future Fuel for IC Engines

1. **Renewable-Ready Energy Vector** — Can be produced from water + renewable electricity, completing a true zero-carbon cycle.
2. **Wide Flammability Limits** — Burns efficiently in mixtures from **4 % to 75 % by volume** in air (vs. 1–7.6 % for gasoline).
3. **High Octane Rating (RON ≈ 130+)** — Permits lean-burn operation, increasing thermal efficiency.
4. **High Flame Speed (≈ 2.5–3 m/s)** — Almost flat flame propagation → more uniform combustion.
5. **Zero Tailpipe CO₂, CO, and Unburned Hydrocarbons** — Only NOₓ and trace H₂O emissions remain.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiners' Note:** Hydrogen is *not* a primary energy source — it is an *energy carrier*. Producing it requires more energy than releasing it. This is a frequent 3-mark conceptual question.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Hydrogen

Hydrogen is the **most abundant element in the universe** (≈ 75 % of all baryonic mass), but on Earth, it occurs almost exclusively in *combined* form. The viable engineering sources are tabulated below.

| Source | Chemical Form | % of Earth's Mass | Practical Viability for H₂ Production |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water (oceans, rivers) | H₂O | ~71 % of Earth surface | **Primary commercial route** (electrolysis) |
| Hydrocarbons (Natural Gas CH₄) | CₙHₘ | — | **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** — 95 % of global H₂ today |
| Biomass | C₆H₁₀O₅ + others | — | Gasification / bio-photolysis |
| Fossil fuels (coal, oil) | C, CₙHₘ | — | Coal gasification |
| Mineral Hydrides | FeTiH₂, MgH₂, NaBH₄ | Trace | Chemical storage route |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Key-Point:** Free molecular hydrogen (H₂) does *not* exist naturally in usable quantities on Earth. Every kg of H₂ used must be **manufactured** from a hydrogen-bearing compound.

---

## 2.2 Production Methods — Engineering Taxonomy

### (A) Electrolytic Route — "Splitting Water with Electricity"

Three industrial electrolyzer architectures dominate the KTU syllabus:

| Electrolyzer Type | Electrolyte | Operating Temp | Cell Voltage | H₂ Purity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Alkaline (AEL)** | KOH (25–30 %) | 60–80 °C | 1.8–2.4 V | 99.5 % |
| **PEM (Proton Exchange Membrane)** | Polymer membrane | 50–80 °C | 1.8–2.2 V | 99.99 % |
| **Solid Oxide (SOEL)** | YSZ ceramic | 700–850 °C | 1.0–1.5 V | 99.9 % |

**Half-reactions (Alkaline Electrolysis):**
- Cathode: 2H₂O + 2e⁻ → H₂ + 2OH⁻
- Anode: 2OH⁻ → ½O₂ + H₂O + 2e⁻
- **Overall:** 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂ (ΔG° = +237.2 kJ/mol)

### (B) Thermochemical Routes

1. **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** — produces ≈ 95 % of global H₂.
   CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂  (endothermic, 700–1000 °C)
   CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂  (Water-Gas Shift, exothermic)

2. **Partial Oxidation of Methane (POX):**
   CH₄ + ½O₂ → CO + 2H₂

3. **Coal Gasification:**
   C + H₂O → CO + H₂

4. **Biomass Gasification:**
   C₆H₁₀O₅ → 6CO + 5H₂

### (C) Emerging / Solar Routes

- **Photoelectrochemical Water Splitting** (sunlight + semiconductor catalyst).
- **Bio-photolysis** (algae + sunlight).
- **Thermochemical Sulfur–Iodine Cycle** (high-temp nuclear + water).
- **Dark Fermentation** (bacteria + organic waste).

---

## 2.3 Storage and Handling — The "Hydrogen Storage Trilemma"

The volumetric energy density of H₂ at ambient conditions is only **8.5 MJ/m³** versus gasoline's **31,000 MJ/m³**. Hence three storage families dominate:

| Storage Mode | Conditions | Volumetric Density | Engineering Maturity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compressed Gas (CGH₂)** | 350 bar (Type III) / 700 bar (Type IV) | 4.7 MJ/L @ 700 bar | Commercial (Toyota Mirai) |
| **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)** | −253 °C, 1 atm | 8.5 MJ/L | Aerospace; boil-off losses |
| **Metal Hydrides (MH)** | LaNi₅H₆, MgH₂ | 2–3 MJ/L | R&D / niche |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Parameter — Compressed H₂ Tank Pressure Scaling:**
> Real-gas compressibility factor Z must be used because H₂ deviates from ideal behavior above 200 bar.

---

## 2.4 Hydrogen Induction Techniques in IC Engines

Three industrial induction strategies are catalogued:

1. **Carburetion / Fumigation** — H₂ metered into the intake manifold upstream of the throttle body. Simplest, lowest cost, prone to backfire.
2. **Port Fuel Injection (PFI)** — Timed pulse injection in the intake port. Used in modern hydrogen cars (BMW Hydrogen 7).
3. **Direct Injection (DI)** — High-pressure injection directly into the cylinder. Best cycle-to-cycle control, eliminates backfire, used in Mazda RX-8 Hydrogen RE.

---

## 2.5 Engine Modifications Required

Because of hydrogen's unique properties (high flame speed, wide flammability limits, no lubricating oil film), the following changes are mandatory:

| Component | Modification | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Compression Ratio | Reduce from ~10:1 to **7–9:1** | Prevent abnormal combustion (knock) |
| Valves & Seats | Hard-faced (Stellite, Inconel) | No lubrication from fuel → wear |
| Spark Plug | Higher-energy iridium/platinum | Defeat pre-ignition |
| Intake Manifold | Single or dual-fuel compatible | Fumigation/PFI mount |
| Oil Sump | Crankcase ventilation redesigned | Prevent H₂ accumulation |
| Cooling System | Enhanced (EGR-cooled) | Manage higher NOₓ |
| Lubricant | Low-ash synthetic | Prevent pre-ignition |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict KTU Convention:** All symbols below are reproduced in the standard form expected in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). Memorize these — they appear in 8 out of 10 numerical questions on hydrogen.

| # | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{HHV} = 141.9 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | Higher Heating Value of H₂ | Energy release with water condensed |
| 2 | $Q_{LHV} = 119.9 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | Lower Heating Value of H₂ | Real engine use (water as vapor) |
| 3 | $AFR_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{34.48}{1}$ | Air-Fuel Ratio by mass | Stoichiometric combustion |
| 4 | $\Phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence Ratio | Lean/rich classification |
| 5 | $\rho_{H_2}^{STP} = 0.0899 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Standard Density | Tank sizing |
| 6 | $V_{cylinder,H_2} = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{\rho_{H_2}} \times Z(P,T)$ | Compressed volume | CGH₂ tank volume |
| 7 | $\eta_{thermal} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Otto efficiency | Theoretical max efficiency |
| 8 | $W_{compressor} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | Compression work | H₂ compressor sizing |
| 9 | $\dot{V}_{H_2,loss} = K \cdot \dfrac{A}{x} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{\sqrt{MT}}$ | Leakage rate | Safety analysis |
| 10 | $E_{ignition,min} = 0.02 \text{ mJ}$ | Min ignition energy | Safety threshold |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Contrast Value for Numericals:** H₂ has an AFR stoichiometric of **34.48:1** (mass basis), whereas gasoline is **14.7:1**. This 2.35× higher air demand directly impacts induction-system sizing and is the root cause of hydrogen *power derating*.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation:** Lockheed Martin and Airbus ZEROe use LH₂ for zero-CO₂ flight.
- **Automotive:** Toyota Mirai (2nd gen) uses Type IV 700 bar CGH₂ tanks; range ≈ 650 km.
- **Stationary Power:** Hydrogen fuel cells (PEMFC) for grid backup, used by Siemens Silyzer.
- **Heavy-Duty IC Engines:** MAN Hydrogen Combustion Engine (H95) for trucks; Wärtsilä W31H for marine.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of Hydrogen

**Starting Combustion Equation (mass basis, complete combustion in air):**

$$2H_2 + O_2 + 3.76 N_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O + 3.76 N_2$$

**Step 1 — Convert moles to mass:**
- Molar mass of H₂ = 2.016 g/mol → 2 moles of H₂ = 2 × 2.016 = 4.032 g
- Molar mass of O₂ = 32 g/mol → 1 mole of O₂ = 32 g
- Molar mass of N₂ = 28 g/mol → 3.76 moles of N₂ = 3.76 × 28 = 105.28 g

**Step 2 — Total Air Mass:**
The "air" component is O₂ + 3.76 N₂ = 32 + 105.28 = 137.28 g

**Step 3 — Mass Ratio:**
$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = \frac{137.28}{4.032}$$

$$\boxed{AFR_{stoich} = 34.04 \text{ (by mass)}}$$

**Step 4 — KTU Accepted Refined Value:**
Using standard atomic masses (H = 1.008, N = 14.0067, O = 15.999):

$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{1 \times 32 + 3.76 \times 28.0134}{2 \times 2.016} = \frac{137.330}{4.032}$$

$$\boxed{AFR_{stoich} \approx 34.48 \text{ kg air/kg H}_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value (34.48) is the canonical figure cited across all KTU Board solutions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Volume of Compressed Hydrogen Gas Required

**Problem Statement:** An IC engine consumes 1.0 kg of H₂ per hour. Calculate the volume of compressed hydrogen needed at **350 bar** and ambient temperature **25 °C (298.15 K)** using the real-gas compressibility factor Z ≈ 1.13.

**Step 1 — Convert mass to moles:**
$$n = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{1.0 \text{ kg}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}}$$

$$n = \frac{1000}{2.016} = 496.03 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Real-Gas Equation of State:**
$$PV = ZnRT$$

$$V = \frac{ZnRT}{P}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values:**
- Z = 1.13
- n = 496.03 mol
- R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- T = 298.15 K
- P = 350 bar = 350 × 10⁵ Pa

$$V = \frac{1.13 \times 496.03 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{350 \times 10^{5}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator:**
$$1.13 \times 496.03 = 560.514$$
$$560.514 \times 8.314 = 4660.51$$
$$4660.51 \times 298.15 = 1,389,447.0 \text{ J}$$

**Step 5 — Final Volume:**
$$V = \frac{1,389,447.0}{35,000,000}$$

$$\boxed{V = 0.0397 \text{ m}^3 \approx 39.7 \text{ liters}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** At 1 atm, the same 1 kg would occupy ≈ **11,131 L**. Compression to 350 bar reduces this by a factor of ≈ 281×, illustrating why CGH₂ is the standard automotive solution.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Energy Density Comparison — Hydrogen vs Gasoline (by Volume)

**Step 1 — H₂ LHV by Volume at 700 bar (Compressed):**
- At 700 bar, density of H₂ ≈ 40 kg/m³
- Energy density = 40 × 119.9 = 4,796 MJ/m³

**Step 2 — Gasoline LHV by Volume:**
- Density of gasoline ≈ 745 kg/m³
- LHV of gasoline ≈ 44 MJ/kg
- Energy density = 745 × 44 = 32,780 MJ/m³

**Step 3 — Ratio:**
$$\frac{E_{gasoline}}{E_{H_2,700bar}} = \frac{32,780}{4,796} \approx 6.83$$

$$\boxed{\text{H}_2 \text{ at 700 bar carries only } 14.6\% \text{ of gasoline's volumetric energy.}}$$

**Step 4 — By Mass Basis:**
$$\frac{E_{H_2,HHV}}{E_{gasoline,LHV}} = \frac{141.9}{44} \approx 3.225$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conclusion:** Hydrogen is *superior by mass* (3.2× gasoline) but *inferior by volume* (≈ 7× worse). This dichotomy is the central engineering challenge for hydrogen IC engines.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Power Derating of a Hydrogen-Fueled SI Engine

**Problem Statement:** A 4-cylinder gasoline SI engine delivers 75 kW at stoich A/F = 14.7. If converted to pure hydrogen (A/F = 34.48), with volumetric efficiency and heating-value losses neglected for simplicity, estimate the % power derating due solely to the energy intake change.

**Step 1 — Energy Per Unit Mass of Intake Charge:**
Energy density of fuel per kg of air (for stoich mixture):
$$e_{gasoline} = \frac{LHV_{gas}}{AFR_{gas}} = \frac{44,000 \text{ kJ/kg}}{14.7} = 2,993 \text{ kJ/kg air}$$

$$e_{H_2} = \frac{LHV_{H_2}}{AFR_{H_2}} = \frac{119,900 \text{ kJ/kg}}{34.48} = 3,477 \text{ kJ/kg air}$$

**Step 2 — Ratio of Energy Delivery:**
$$\frac{e_{H_2}}{e_{gasoline}} = \frac{3,477}{2,993} = 1.161$$

**Step 3 — Apparent Power Increase:**
This calculation suggests a **16 % gain**, but the practical reality is opposite due to:

**Step 4 — Actual Power Loss Mechanisms (Cumulative):**

| Loss Mechanism | Typical % Reduction |
|---|---|
| Reduced volumetric efficiency (H₂ displaces air) | −15 to −25 % |
| Lower adiabatic flame temperature at lean limit | −5 to −10 % |
| Reduced displacement of intake air due to early combustion | −5 % |
| **Net Power Derating** | **≈ 15 to 30 %** |

$$\boxed{P_{H_2,actual} \approx 0.75 \times P_{gasoline} \text{ for the same displacement}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Compressor Work for H₂ from 1 atm to 350 bar (Multi-Stage with Intercooling)

**Step 1 — Single-Stage Work (Isentropic):**
$$W_1 = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$$

For H₂, γ = 1.405, R = 4.124 kJ/(kg·K), T₁ = 298 K, P₂/P₁ = 350.

$$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.405}{1.405} = 0.2883$$

$$\left(350\right)^{0.2883} = e^{0.2883 \times \ln 350} = e^{0.2883 \times 5.858} = e^{1.689} = 5.414$$

$$W_1 = \frac{1.405}{0.405} \times 4.124 \times 298 \times (5.414 - 1)$$

**Step 2 — Computation:**
$$\frac{1.405}{0.405} = 3.469$$
$$3.469 \times 4.124 = 14.31 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$
$$14.31 \times 298 = 4,264.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$4,264.4 \times 4.414 = 18,823 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{W_{single} = 18.82 \text{ MJ/kg of H}_2}$$

**Step 3 — Multi-Stage Optimal Pressure Ratio (N stages):**
For equal work distribution across N stages:
$$r_{stage} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/N}$$

For N = 3 stages:
$$r_{stage} = (350)^{1/3} = 7.046$$

$$\left(7.046\right)^{0.2883} = e^{0.2883 \times \ln 7.046} = e^{0.2883 \times 1.953} = e^{0.563} = 1.756$$

$$W_{3-stage} = 3 \times 3.469 \times 4.124 \times 298 \times (1.756 - 1)$$
$$W_{3-stage} = 3 \times 4,264.4 \times 0.756 = 9,672 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{W_{3-stage} \approx 9.67 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **3-Stage Compression Reduces Work by ≈ 48.6 %.** This is the engineering rationale behind multi-stage H₂ compressors used in refueling stations.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**"Why is hydrogen considered an energy carrier and not a primary energy source? Mention its calorific value."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Hydrogen is an **energy carrier**, not a primary energy source, because free molecular hydrogen (H₂) does **not exist in usable quantities on Earth** in its elemental form. It must always be *manufactured* from hydrogen-rich compounds such as water (H₂O), natural gas (CH₄), or biomass, **using an external input of energy** (electricity, heat, or light). The energy required to *produce* hydrogen (e.g., 237.2 kJ/mol to electrolyze water) is always greater than the energy released upon its combustion — hence it merely *carries* energy from one form to another.
>
> **Calorific Values:**
> - Higher Heating Value (HHV) = **141.9 MJ/kg**
> - Lower Heating Value (LHV) = **119.9 MJ/kg**

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"List any three advantages and three challenges of using hydrogen as a fuel in IC engines."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Advantages | Challenges |
|---|---|
| 1. Zero CO₂ / CO / HC tailpipe emissions | 1. Very low volumetric energy density |
| 2. Wide flammability limits (4–75 %) enable lean burn | 2. On-board storage is bulky and heavy |
| 3. High octane rating permits high efficiency | 3. Abnormal combustion (backfire / pre-ignition) |
| **Bonus:** High flame speed (≈ 3 m/s) | **Bonus:** Hydrogen embrittlement of metals |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module 3 — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module 3 carries 14 marks, and students answer **ONE out of TWO** options. Each sub-question is **7 marks**. Below are two full alternative sets, each with complete model solutions.

---

### 🔹 Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q.A (a)** *With neat sketches, explain the Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) process for hydrogen production. Derive the overall stoichiometry and discuss its energy efficiency. (7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Process Description:**
Steam Methane Reforming is the **dominant commercial method**, accounting for ≈ **95 % of global hydrogen production**. It involves two coupled catalytic reactions:

- **Primary Reforming (Endothermic, 700–1000 °C, Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst):**

$$CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- **Water-Gas Shift Reaction (Exothermic, 200–400 °C, Fe/Cr catalyst):**

$$CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**(ii) Overall Stoichiometry:**
$$CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2$$

**Theoretical H₂ yield per kg of CH₄:**

$$n_{CH_4} = \frac{1000}{16.04} = 62.34 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_{H_2,ideal} = 4 \times 62.34 = 249.36 \text{ mol}$$

$$m_{H_2} = 249.36 \times 2.016 = 502.7 \text{ g} \approx 0.50 \text{ kg H}_2 \text{ per kg CH}_4$$

**(iii) Energy Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{SMR} = \frac{LHV_{H_2,produced}}{LHV_{CH_4,input} + E_{heat,input}}$$

Typical overall efficiency ≈ **65–75 %** (LHV basis).

**(iv) Schematic:** (Refer to Section 4.1 — Production Methods diagram.)

**Conclusion:** SMR is mature, cost-effective (USD 1–2/kg H₂), but produces CO₂ — thus the hydrogen is *grey* unless CO₂ is captured (*blue* hydrogen).

---

**Q.A (b)** *Compare compressed gaseous, liquid, and metal hydride storage of hydrogen in terms of volumetric density, energy required, and safety. (7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | CGH₂ (700 bar) | LH₂ (−253 °C) | Metal Hydride (LaNi₅) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volumetric Energy Density** | 4.7 MJ/L | 8.5 MJ/L | 2–3 MJ/L |
| **Storage Mass Penalty** | Tank ≈ 15–20 kg | Dewar ≈ 30 kg | Hydride bed ≈ 50 kg |
| **Energy to Store** | 10–15 % of H₂ LHV | 30–40 % of H₂ LHV | 5–10 % of H₂ LHV |
| **Boil-off / Loss** | Negligible | 0.1–1 % per day | None |
| **Safety** | High-pressure rupture risk | Cryogenic burn + asphyxiation | Low pressure, but dust hazard |
| **Refueling Time** | 3–5 min | 5–10 min | 30–60 min (heat-up cycle) |
| **Maturity** | Commercial | Aerospace only | R&D / niche |

**Engineering Choice:**
- **Automotive:** CGH₂ at 700 bar (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo).
- **Aerospace:** LH₂ (NASA, Airbus ZEROe).
- **Stationary:** Underground salt caverns + metal hydrides for buffering.

---

### 🔹 Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q.B (a)** *Explain with diagrams three induction techniques used for supplying hydrogen to an SI engine. Discuss their relative merits. (7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Carburetion / Fumigation Technique**
Hydrogen is metered through a pressure regulator into the intake manifold *upstream* of the throttle body, mixing with incoming air as it flows to the cylinders.

- **Advantages:** Simple, low cost, retrofit-friendly.
- **Disadvantages:** Prone to backfire into the intake manifold due to wide flammability limits and presence of hot residual gases; poor transient response.

**2. Port Fuel Injection (PFI)**
Hydrogen is injected via solenoid injectors into the intake port, *downstream* of the throttle, near the intake valve. Timing and duration are ECU-controlled.

- **Advantages:** Accurate metering, eliminates manifold backfire, faster transient response.
- **Disadvantages:** Still subject to pre-ignition from hot residual gases; requires fuel-line pressurization.

**3. Direct Injection (DI)**
Hydrogen is injected at high pressure (100–300 bar) directly into the combustion chamber, late in the compression stroke.

- **Advantages:** **No backfire possible** (no H₂ in intake); high volumetric efficiency; excellent cold-start.
- **Disadvantages:** Most expensive; injector durability concerns; requires major engine redesign.

**Comparative Verdict:**

| Technique | Backfire Risk | Cost | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carburetion | High | Low | Research / stationary |
| PFI | Moderate | Medium | Most automotive conversions |
| DI | **Negligible** | High | Modern OEM hydrogen vehicles |

---

**Q.B (b)** *Discuss the performance and emission characteristics of a hydrogen-fueled SI engine compared to a gasoline SI engine. (7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Performance Characteristics:**

| Parameter | Gasoline SI | Hydrogen SI | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal Efficiency | 28–35 % | 35–42 % | Lean burn advantage |
| Power Output | Baseline | **15–30 % lower** | Volumetric derating |
| Torque | Baseline | Slightly lower | Reduced air displacement |
| Cold Start | Easy | Difficult | High ignition energy needed |
| Range | 600 km | 400–500 km | Storage penalty |

**(ii) Emission Characteristics:**

| Pollutant | Gasoline SI | Hydrogen SI |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | 180–250 g/km | **0 g/km** |
| CO | 1–5 g/km | **0 g/km** |
| Unburned HC | 0.1–0.5 g/km | **0 g/km** |
| NOₓ | 0.05–0.2 g/km | **0.02–0.5 g/km** (stoich = higher, lean = lower) |
| Particulates | 0.005 g/km | **0 g/km** |
| H₂O | Trace | Significant |

**(iii) Discussion:**
Hydrogen engines achieve **higher thermal efficiency** because lean operation reduces pumping losses and heat-transfer losses. However, at the *stoichiometric* mixture, NOₓ emissions can spike due to the high adiabatic flame temperature (≈ 2045 °C). With lean-burn calibration (λ > 1.5) and EGR, NOₓ drops below gasoline levels while efficiency rises.

**(iv) Abnormal Combustion Issues:**
- **Pre-ignition:** Triggered by hot residual gases.
- **Backfire:** Flame propagation back into intake manifold during fumigation.
- **Knock:** Hydrogen has a high octane rating, so knock is rare — a major advantage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **wrongly claim** hydrogen produces "zero NOₓ" — it does NOT. At stoichiometric air-fuel ratio, NOₓ is *higher* than gasoline due to elevated flame temperature. Lean burn is the mitigation.
> - Confusing **HHV vs LHV** in numerical problems costs marks. The KTU convention: use **LHV (119.9 MJ/kg)** for engine cycle analysis.
> - Students often miss stating the **stoichiometric AFR = 34.48** in derivations. Always state it before any energy-density calculation.
> - In SMR derivations, **forgetting the Water-Gas Shift step** results in an incorrect H₂ yield.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Print and Carry to Exam Hall.**

### ✅ Definitional Anchors
- **Hydrogen is an energy carrier, NOT a primary source.**
- **HHV = 141.9 MJ/kg ; LHV = 119.9 MJ/kg.**
- **Stoichiometric AFR (mass) = 34.48 : 1.**
- **Auto-ignition temp = 571 °C ; Min ignition energy = 0.02 mJ.**
- **Flammability limits = 4 to 75 % by volume in air.**

### ✅ Production Methods — Rank by Global Share
1. **SMR (95 %)** → CH₄ + 2H₂O → CO₂ + 4H₂
2. Electrolysis (4 %) → 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂
3. Coal/Oil gasification (<1 %)
4. Solar / Biological (R&D)

### ✅ Storage Hierarchy (by Volumetric Density)
- **LH₂ (8.5 MJ/L) > CGH₂ 700 bar (4.7 MJ/L) > Metal Hydrides (2–3 MJ/L)**

### ✅ Induction Techniques (by Backfire Resistance)
- **Direct Injection (DI) > PFI > Fumigation/Carburetion**

### ✅ Engine Modifications — Mandatory
1. Reduce compression ratio (10 → 7)
2. Hardened valve seats (Stellite/Inconel)
3. Higher-energy ignition system
4. Low-ash synthetic lubricant
5. Enhanced cooling / EGR system
6. Hydrogen leak detectors + ECU H₂ map

### ✅ Emission Profile of H₂ Engine
- **Zero CO₂, CO, HC, Particulates.**
- **NOₓ: HIGH at stoich / LOW at lean-burn.**
- **Primary product: H₂O.**

### ✅ Safety Hierarchy
- **Risk:** Wide flammability + low ignition energy.
- **Mitigation:** Sealed joints, ventilation, H₂ sensors, flame arrestors, anti-static materials.

### ✅ Quick Numerical Anchors
- Compression Work (3-stage, 350 bar) ≈ **9.67 MJ/kg H₂**
- 1 kg H₂ occupies **39.7 L @ 350 bar / 11,131 L @ 1 atm**
- Power derating on conversion ≈ **15–30 %**
- Energy density by volume ≈ **14.6 %** of gasoline

### ✅ Examiner's "Favourite" 14-Mark Question Pattern
> *"Compare three production methods + explain storage strategy + modifications needed + performance/emission characteristics."*

> **Final Mantra for KTU 2024:** *Hydrogen is clean at the tailpipe but expensive at the well-to-tank. Memorize the three numbers — **142, 119.9, 34.48** — and the rest of the derivations will follow naturally.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Cell Vehicles - Layout of a Fuel Cell Vehicle, Fuel Cell Types - Alkaline Fuel Cell, Proton Exchange Membrane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fuel Cell Vehicles - Layout of a Fuel Cell Vehicle, Fuel Cell Types - Alkaline Fuel Cell, Proton Exchange Membrane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fuel Cell Vehicles (FCV) — Formal Definition

A **Fuel Cell Vehicle (FCV)** is an electric vehicle that employs a **fuel cell powertrain** to generate electricity onboard through an electrochemical reaction between a fuel (commonly **hydrogen**) and an oxidant (commonly **oxygen from ambient air**). The generated electricity drives an electric motor, which propels the vehicle. The only by-products are water and heat, making FCVs classified under **Zero Emission Vehicles (ZEVs)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A fuel cell is *not* a heat engine. It converts chemical energy directly into electrical energy via electrochemical means, bypassing the **Carnot efficiency** limitation entirely. This is why FCVs are a cornerstone of KTU Module 3 on Alternate Fuels & Energy Systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Refillable Battery" Mental Model

Imagine a regular AA battery that produces electricity through a chemical reaction. Once the chemicals are used up, you throw it away. A **fuel cell** works on the *same principle* but with two crucial differences:

1. **The reactants are supplied continuously** from external tanks (hydrogen tank + air intake), just like a car burning petrol from a fuel tank.
2. **The by-products (water + heat) are removed continuously**, so the cell never "dies" as long as fuel is supplied.

Think of a fuel cell as a **"rechargeable battery that you don't charge — you refuel"**. You simply refill hydrogen in **3–5 minutes**, just like petrol, but get the silent, vibration-free ride of an electric vehicle.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Metric | Typical FCV Value |
|---|---|
| **Energy Efficiency** | **50–60%** (vs. 20–30% for ICE) |
| **Refuelling Time** | **3–5 minutes** |
| **Driving Range** | **500–650 km** per tank |
| **Operating Temperature (PEMFC)** | **60–100 °C** |
| **Operating Temperature (AFC)** | **60–90 °C** |
| **Cold Start Time** | Approx. **30 seconds** (warm-up) |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard Faradays constant **F = 96,485 C/mol** and the universal gas constant **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** are foundational constants used throughout every fuel cell equation. Memorize these values.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of a Generic Hydrogen Fuel Cell

A single fuel cell consists of three primary components:

1. **Anode (negative electrode):** Where hydrogen gas is oxidized, releasing electrons and protons.
2. **Cathode (positive electrode):** Where oxygen is reduced by combining with protons and electrons.
3. **Electrolyte:** The medium that selectively conducts ions (H⁺ or OH⁻) while blocking electrons, forcing them to flow through an external circuit.

### The Universal Half-Cell Reactions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Anode:} \quad & \mathrm{H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-} \\[4pt]
\text{Cathode:} \quad & \mathrm{\tfrac{1}{2} O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O} \\[4pt]
\text{Net Cell Reaction:} \quad & \mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The electrolyte type determines which ion is transported. This is the fundamental distinction between **AFC (OH⁻ ion transport)** and **PEMFC (H⁺ ion transport)** — both covered below in detail.

## 2.2 Electrochemical Thermodynamics — "Why" a Fuel Cell Produces Voltage

The reversible cell voltage arises from the **Gibbs Free Energy** change of the reaction. At standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm):

$$
\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ
$$

Where:
- $\Delta G^\circ$ = Standard Gibbs free energy change (**−237.2 kJ/mol** for H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O liquid)
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred (**2** for hydrogen oxidation)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant (**96,485 C/mol**)
- $E^\circ$ = Standard reversible cell potential (**1.229 V** at 25 °C)

Solving for $E^\circ$:
$$
E^\circ = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} = -\frac{-237{,}200}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.229 \text{ V}
$$

## 2.3 The Nernst Equation — Real-World Voltage Under Non-Standard Conditions

In practice, temperature and pressure deviate from standard conditions. The **Nernst equation** gives the actual cell potential:

$$
E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left( \frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)
$$

Where:
- $R$ = Universal gas constant
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)
- $P_{H_2}$, $P_{O_2}$, $P_{H_2O}$ = Partial pressures of reactants/products

## 2.4 Fuel Cell Efficiency — Comparison With Heat Engines

The **thermodynamic (ideal) efficiency** of a fuel cell is given by:

$$
\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}
$$

Two values of $\Delta H$ exist, giving two efficiencies:

| Quantity | ΔH Value (H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O) | Efficiency η |
|---|---|---|
| **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** — water produced as liquid | **−285.8 kJ/mol** | $\eta_{HHV} = \frac{237.2}{285.8} = 83\%$ |
| **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** — water produced as vapor | **−241.8 kJ/mol** | $\eta_{LHV} = \frac{237.2}{241.8} = 98\%$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Real fuel cell efficiencies are **40–60%** due to:
> - **Activation losses** (slow electrode kinetics)
> - **Ohmic losses** (resistance of electrolyte and electrodes)
> - **Concentration losses** (mass transport limitations)
> This is still **~2× better than internal combustion engines** (~20–30%).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Application / Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$ | Standard cell voltage from free energy |
| 2 | $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ / nF$ | Rearranged form for $E^\circ$ calculation |
| 3 | $E = E^\circ + (RT/nF) \ln Q$ | Nernst equation (full form) |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \Delta G / \Delta H$ | Ideal thermodynamic efficiency |
| 5 | $\eta_{HHV} = \Delta G / \Delta H_{HHV}$ | Efficiency using HHV |
| 6 | $\eta_{LHV} = \Delta G / \Delta H_{LHV}$ | Efficiency using LHV |
| 7 | $V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{thermo}} - V_{\text{act}} - V_{\text{ohmic}} - V_{\text{conc}}$ | Actual cell voltage (polarization curve) |
| 8 | $W_{\text{elec}} = n \cdot F \cdot E_{\text{cell}}$ | Electrical work per mole of fuel |
| 9 | $q = n \cdot F$ | Charge transferred per mole of electrons |

> Real-World Engineering Utility: These equations are used by automotive OEMs (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo, Honda Clarity) to size fuel cell stacks. A typical FCV stack operates at **0.6–0.7 V per cell**, so ~300 cells are stacked in series to produce ~250–350 V traction voltage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Gibbs Free Energy to Reversible Cell Voltage

We begin with the fundamental thermodynamic identity at constant temperature and pressure:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S
$$

The **maximum non-PV (non-expansion) electrical work** extractable from a chemical reaction equals the negative of Gibbs free energy change:

$$
W_{\text{elec, max}} = -\Delta G
$$

For the hydrogen oxidation reaction, **n = 2** electrons are transferred per mole of H₂. The total charge transferred is:

$$
q = nF
$$

The electrical work performed when charge $q$ is moved across a potential difference $E$ is:

$$
W_{\text{elec}} = qE = nFE
$$

Equating the two expressions for electrical work:

$$
nFE = -\Delta G
$$

Solving explicitly for the reversible cell potential $E$:

$$
\boxed{E = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}}
$$

### Numerical Verification — Standard Conditions

Using values for the hydrogen–oxygen reaction at 25 °C:
- $\Delta G^\circ = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol} = -237{,}200 \text{ J/mol}$
- $n = 2$
- $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$

Substituting:
$$
E^\circ = -\frac{-237{,}200}{2 \times 96{,}485}
$$

Step-by-step arithmetic:
- Numerator: $-237{,}200$ J/mol (note the negative sign)
- Denominator: $2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970$ C/mol
- Division: $237{,}200 \div 192{,}970 = 1.2291$ V

$$
\boxed{E^\circ = 1.229 \text{ V}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Nernst Equation from First Principles

Starting from the general Gibbs free energy expression at non-standard state:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q
$$

Where $Q$ is the reaction quotient. Dividing both sides by $-nF$:

$$
-\frac{\Delta G}{nF} = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

Since $E = -\Delta G/nF$ and $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ/nF$:

$$
\boxed{E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q}
$$

For the hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell reaction $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O}$:

$$
Q = \frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}
$$

Substituting into the Nernst equation:

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left( \frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}} \right)
$$

By the property $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$, we can equivalently write:

$$
E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left( \frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)
$$

### Numerical Example — Effect of Pressure on Cell Voltage

A PEMFC operates at **80 °C (T = 353 K)** with $P_{H_2} = 3$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 3$ atm, and $P_{H_2O}$ = saturation pressure at 80 °C ≈ **0.467 atm**.

Plugging values (using $E^\circ_{80°C} \approx 1.18$ V):

$$
E = 1.18 + \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485} \ln\left( \frac{3 \times 3^{0.5}}{0.467} \right)
$$

Step 1 — Calculate the temperature factor:
$$
\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{2{,}934.8}{192{,}970} = 0.01521 \text{ V}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the argument of the logarithm:
$$
\frac{3 \times \sqrt{3}}{0.467} = \frac{3 \times 1.732}{0.467} = \frac{5.196}{0.467} = 11.126
$$

Step 3 — Compute the natural logarithm:
$$
\ln(11.126) = 2.409
$$

Step 4 — Multiply and add:
$$
E = 1.18 + (0.01521 \times 2.409) = 1.18 + 0.0367 = 1.2167 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 1.217 \text{ V at 80 °C, 3 atm operation}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Thermal Efficiency of Fuel Cell

The thermal efficiency of any energy conversion device is defined as:

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Useful Energy Output}}{\text{Total Energy Input}}
$$

For a fuel cell, the useful output is the electrical work ($-\Delta G$), and the total energy input from the fuel is the higher or lower heating value ($-\Delta H$):

$$
\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{-\Delta G}{-\Delta H} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}
$$

### Numerical Example — LHV vs HHV Efficiency

Using $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$:
- $\Delta G^\circ = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H^\circ_{HHV} = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (liquid water product)
- $\Delta H^\circ_{LHV} = -241.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (vapor water product)

HHV-based efficiency:
$$
\eta_{HHV} = \frac{237.2}{285.8} = 0.830 = 83.0\%
$$

LHV-based efficiency:
$$
\eta_{LHV} = \frac{237.2}{241.8} = 0.981 = 98.1\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The LHV efficiency exceeds 100%? **No** — it's valid because part of the reaction enthalpy is released as heat ($T\Delta S$), and if this heat is also converted to work (e.g., in a hybrid system with cogeneration), the total can approach 100%. For pure electrical output only, the HHV-based 83% is the practical upper limit.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Fuel Cell Types (AFC vs PEMFC)

| Parameter | **Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC)** | **Proton Exchange Membrane FC (PEMFC)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrolyte** | Liquid KOH (typically 30–40 wt%) | Polymer membrane (Nafion™) |
| **Mobile Ion** | OH⁻ (hydroxide) | H⁺ (proton) |
| **Operating Temperature** | 60–90 °C (low) / up to 200 °C | 60–100 °C (low) |
| **Charge Carrier Direction** | Cathode → Anode | Anode → Cathode |
| **Water Management** | Produced at anode | Produced at cathode |
| **CO₂ Sensitivity** | **Highly sensitive** (forms K₂CO₃) | Moderately tolerant (Pt catalyst poisoning at high CO) |
| **Catalyst** | Non-precious (Ni, Ag, MnO₂) | Platinum (Pt) — expensive |
| **Power Density** | Lower (~0.1–0.3 W/cm²) | **Higher (~0.5–1.0 W/cm²)** |
| **Start-up Time** | Fast | Fast |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Primary Use Case** | Space programs (Apollo, Space Shuttle) | **Automotive FCVs** (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo) |
| **Anode Reaction** | $\mathrm{H_2 + 2OH^- \rightarrow 2H_2O + 2e^-}$ | $\mathrm{H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-}$ |
