| Effective BSFC sweet spot | $bsfc_{opt}$ | $\approx 220 \text{ g/kWh}$ for modern petrol | g/kWh |
| System Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{sys}$ | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{trans}$ | Dimensionless |
| Energy Equivalence | $E_{bat}$ | $E_{bat} = V_{bat} \cdot C_{bat} \cdot 3600$ | Joules (J) |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Critical Rule:** In the formula sheet, observe that **every conversion step has an efficiency loss**. The cumulative loss is why the engine is held at its BSFC sweet spot — to maximize $\eta_{ICE}$ and thereby $\eta_{sys}$.

### 2.4 Why the Engine is "Decoupled" — The Core Engineering Rationale

The central insight of a Series hybrid is **operational freedom of the ICE**. In a conventional car, the engine's RPM is forced to follow the wheel speed (via a transmission). This means the engine constantly operates across a wide range of inefficient points.

In a Series hybrid:  
- The ICE can be held at **a single rotational speed** corresponding to minimum BSFC.  
- The traction motor independently varies its torque and speed to meet wheel demand.  
- The **battery hides the transient mismatch** between steady genset output and variable traction demand.

> **Real-world utility:** This is why the **Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1)**, **BMW i3 with Range Extender (REx)**, and **Nissan e-POWER** vehicles all use a Series hybrid architecture — they are EV-first, range-extender designs optimized for urban + inter-city duty cycles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Balance Equation for a Series Hybrid

We start from **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to the electrical series loop.

**Step 1:** Mechanical power output of ICE  

$$
P_{ICE} = T_{ICE} \cdot \omega_{ICE}
$$

where $T_{ICE}$ is engine torque and $\omega_{ICE}$ is engine angular speed.

**Step 2:** Electrical power generated (after generator losses)  

$$
P_{gen,elec} = \eta_{gen} \cdot P_{ICE}
$$

where $\eta_{gen}$ is the generator efficiency (typically **0.90 – 0.95** for a PMSG).

**Step 3:** This electrical power is added to the battery (or subtracted) to feed the DC bus.  

$$
P_{DCbus} = P_{gen,elec} + P_{bat}
$$

**Step 4:** DC bus feeds the traction inverter, which drives the motor.  

$$
P_{motor,elec} = \eta_{inv} \cdot P_{DCbus}
$$

**Step 5:** Mechanical power at the motor shaft:  

$$
P_{motor,mech} = \eta_{motor} \cdot P_{motor,elec} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot (P_{gen,elec} + P_{bat})
$$

**Step 6:** Mechanical power at the wheels:  

$$
P_{wheel} = \eta_{trans} \cdot P_{motor,mech} = \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot (\eta_{gen} \cdot P_{ICE} + P_{bat})
$$

**Step 7:** Substituting the full chain and equating to the tractive demand $P_{tr} = F_{tr} \cdot v$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{tr} &= \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot P_{ICE} + \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot P_{bat}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8:** Final **Master Power Balance Equation** for a Series Hybrid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,P_{tr}(t) = \underbrace{\eta_{sys,\,ICE}\, P_{ICE}(t)}_{\text{Genset contribution}} + \underbrace{\eta_{sys,\,bat}\, P_{bat}(t)}_{\text{Battery contribution}}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\eta_{sys,ICE} = \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{trans}$ and $\eta_{sys,bat} = \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{trans}$.

> **Conversion Logic Note:**  
> The equation shows the **fundamental "decoupling" property** of the Series topology — $P_{ICE}(t)$ and $P_{tr}(t)$ are completely independent variables. The battery $P_{bat}(t)$ is the *bridge* that reconciles them at every instant.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — State of Charge (SOC) Dynamic Equation

The battery's SOC evolves in time according to the net power flow.

**Step 1:** Define SOC as the remaining charge fraction:  

$$
SOC(t) = \frac{Q_{remaining}(t)}{Q_{rated}}
$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to time:  

$$
\frac{dSOC}{dt} = \frac{1}{Q_{rated}} \cdot \frac{dQ}{dt}
$$

**Step 3:** Current is $I = dQ/dt$, and electrical power is $P = V \cdot I$ (assuming a simple battery model with constant $V$):  

$$
\frac{dSOC}{dt} = \frac{1}{Q_{rated}} \cdot \frac{P_{bat}(t)}{V_{bat}} = \frac{P_{bat}(t)}{V_{bat} \cdot Q_{rated}}
$$

**Step 4:** Recognizing that energy capacity $E_{bat} = V_{bat} \cdot Q_{rated}$:  

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,\frac{dSOC}{dt} = \frac{P_{bat}(t)}{E_{bat}}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Integrating over a driving cycle of duration $T$:  

$$
\Delta SOC = \frac{1}{E_{bat}} \int_{0}^{T} P_{bat}(t) \, dt
$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:**  
> This differential equation is the **heart of every Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** for a Series hybrid. The controller's job is to choose $P_{ICE}(t)$ at every instant such that $SOC$ remains within the sustainable window $[0.3, 0.8]$ over the entire trip.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operating Mode vs. Power Flow

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Examiner's Analytical Matrix — Power-Flow Sign Convention:**  
> **+ve** = Power flowing *into* the DC bus; **-ve** = Power flowing *out of* the DC bus.

| **Driving Condition** | **ICE** | **Generator** | **Battery** | **Traction Motor** | **Net $P_{bat}$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Idle / Stopped | OFF | OFF | Idle (no flow) | OFF | 0 |
| Low-speed city | OFF | OFF | Discharging | Motoring | $-P_{motor}/\eta_{inv}$ |
| Cruising (steady) | ON (optimum) | Generating | May charge/discharge slightly | Motoring | $\approx 0$ |
| Hard acceleration | ON (full) | Generating | **Discharging (peak)** | Motoring (max) | $-(\vert P_{motor}/\eta_{inv}\vert - P_{gen})$ |
| Sustained highway climb | ON (full) | Generating | **Discharging** (SOC drops) | Motoring (high) | Negative |
| Deceleration / Braking | OFF | OFF | **Charging** (regen) | Generating | $+P_{regen} \cdot \eta_{inv}$ |
| Descending hill | OFF | OFF | **Charging** (regen) | Generating | Positive |

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Series vs. Other Topologies (KTU Favourite)

| **Parameter** | **Series** | **Parallel** | **Series-Parallel (PS)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical link to wheels | None (purely electrical) | Yes (direct via clutch) | Both modes available |
| ICE operating flexibility | **Highest** — single point | Lowest — must follow wheels | Intermediate |
| Transmission complexity | **Lowest** (single-speed) | Highest (multi-gear) | Highest (planetary gear) |
| Number of motors | 1 traction + 1 generator | 1 traction (or both) | 2 traction + 1 generator |
| Fuel Economy (urban) | **Best** | Moderate | Best overall |
| Fuel Economy (highway) | Moderate | Good | **Best** |
| Cost | Higher (more electrical hardware) | Lower | **Highest** |
| Example vehicles | BMW i3 REx, Chevy Volt Gen1, Nissan e-POWER | Honda Civic Hybrid (IMA) | Toyota Prius, Ford Escape Hybrid |

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When asked to compare topologies, **always quote at least one real-world example vehicle** for each. Examiners award 1–2 marks just for citing industry examples.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Series Hybrid Drive-Train Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[ICE INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINE]
    B --> C[GENERATOR PMSG]
    C --> D[RECTIFIER / GENERATOR INVERTER]
    D --> E[DC BUS HIGH VOLTAGE]
    F[HIGH VOLTAGE BATTERY PACK] <--> E
    E --> G[TRACTION INVERTER]
    G --> H[TRACTION MOTOR PMSM or IM]
    H --> I[REDUCTION GEARBOX]
    I --> J[DRIVE SHAFT]
    J --> K[DIFFERENTIAL]
    K --> L[WHEELS - ROAD]
    M[VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT VCU] -.controls.-> B
    M -.controls.-> C
    M -.controls.-> G
    M -.monitors.-> F
    N[BATTERY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM BMS] -.monitors.-> F
    O[12V DC-DC CONVERTER] -.steps down.-> E
```

### 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Series Hybrid Energy Management

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> EV_Mode
    EV_Mode --> Series_Mode : SOC below threshold and high load demand
    Series_Mode --> EV_Mode : SOC above threshold and low load demand
    Series_Mode --> Peak_Assist : Sudden acceleration and SOC available
    Peak_Assist --> Series_Mode : Acceleration demand ends
    Series_Mode --> Regen_Brake : Brake pedal pressed
    Peak_Assist --> Regen_Brake : Brake pedal pressed
    EV_Mode --> Regen_Brake : Brake pedal pressed
    Regen_Brake --> EV_Mode : Vehicle stopped
    Regen_Brake --> Series_Mode : SOC still low and load resumes
    EV_Mode --> [*] : Ignition off
    Series_Mode --> [*] : Ignition off
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Power Flow Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PRIME_MOVER_SUBSYSTEM
        PM1[Fuel Tank] --> PM2[ICE Engine]
    end

        PM3 --> PM4[Generator Inverter]
    end

        PM4 --> BUS1[DC Bus]
        BAT1[Battery Pack] <--> BUS1
    end
    subgraph TRACTION_SUBSYSTEM
        BUS1 --> TR1[Traction Inverter]
        TR1 --> TR2[3 Phase AC Motor]
        TR2 --> TR3[Reduction Gearbox]
        TR3 --> TR4[Drive Wheels]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL_SUBSYSTEM
        CTRL[VCU] -.-> PM2
        CTRL -.-> PM3
        CTRL -.-> BAT1
        CTRL -.-> TR1
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**  
*Define a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle. Why is the Internal Combustion Engine mechanically decoupled from the wheels in this configuration?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
A **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)** is a hybrid drive-train in which the ICE is **not mechanically connected** to the driving wheels. The ICE drives a generator to produce electrical energy, which is used to charge a battery and/or power a traction motor that propels the vehicle.  
The ICE is decoupled because this arrangement allows the engine to operate at its **single most fuel-efficient point on the BSFC map**, independent of vehicle speed or driver demand. The battery acts as a buffer to absorb or supply transient power mismatches. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
*List any **three** advantages and **two** disadvantages of the Series Hybrid drive-train topology.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
**Advantages:**  
1. ICE can always operate at optimum BSFC point → high urban fuel economy.  
2. Simple mechanical transmission (often single-speed) → lower manufacturing cost and weight.  
3. Pure EV mode possible → zero local emissions in city driving.  

**Disadvantages:**  
1. **Three energy conversions** (mech → elec → mech) result in cumulative efficiency loss.  
2. Requires a large traction motor + generator → higher cost and mass. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Series Hybrid Architecture and Power Flow Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**  
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and working principle of a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. List its major components and trace the power flow under three different operating conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **master power balance equation** for a Series Hybrid and explain how the battery State of Charge (SOC) equation is used in the Energy Management Strategy. Show the analytical form of $dSOC/dt$. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution (a):

The Series Hybrid architecture consists of the following components arranged in an **electrical series loop**:  

1. **Fuel tank**  
2. **ICE** (petrol/diesel engine)  
3. **Generator** (typically PMSG)  
4. **Generator inverter / rectifier**  
5. **DC bus** (high-voltage, ~300–400 V)  
6. **High-voltage battery pack** (Li-ion or NiMH)  
7. **Traction inverter**  
8. **Traction motor** (PMSM or IM)  
9. **Single-speed reduction gearbox**  
10. **Differential and drive wheels**  
11. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** for supervisory control.

**Power flow under three operating conditions:**

- **City cruising (low speed):** ICE OFF, Battery discharges → Traction motor drives wheels. (Pure EV mode)  
- **Highway cruising (steady speed):** ICE ON at optimum BSFC, Generator feeds DC bus directly → Traction motor. Battery idle or trickle charging. (Sustaining mode)  
- **Hard acceleration:** ICE ON at full power + Battery discharges (peak assist) → Traction motor at maximum torque. (Peaking mode)  

(Diagrammatic representation: 3 marks, components: 2 marks, operating modes: 2 marks)

##### Model Solution (b):

**Master Power Balance Equation Derivation:**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{From ICE shaft:} \quad P_{ICE} &= T_{ICE} \cdot \omega_{ICE} \\
\text{After generator:} \quad P_{gen} &= \eta_{gen} \cdot P_{ICE} \\
\text{DC bus power:} \quad P_{bus} &= P_{gen} + P_{bat} \\
\text{After traction inverter:} \quad P_{mot,elec} &= \eta_{inv} \cdot P_{bus} \\
\text{After motor mechanical:} \quad P_{mot,mech} &= \eta_{mot} \cdot P_{mot,elec} \\
\text{At the wheels:} \quad P_{wheel} &= \eta_{tx} \cdot P_{mot,mech}
\end{aligned}
$$

Combining all stages and equating to tractive power demand $P_{tr} = F_{tr} \cdot v$:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{tr} = \eta_{tx}\eta_{mot}\eta_{inv}\eta_{gen}\,P_{ICE} + \eta_{tx}\eta_{mot}\eta_{inv}\,P_{bat}\,}
$$

**SOC Dynamic Equation:**  

The battery SOC rate of change is given by:

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{dSOC}{dt} = \frac{P_{bat}(t)}{E_{bat}} = \frac{P_{bat}(t)}{V_{bat} \cdot C_{bat}}\,}
$$

where $E_{bat}$ is the battery energy capacity in Joules.  
**Role in EMS:** The Energy Management Strategy (EMS) controller solves this ODE in real time to decide $P_{ICE}(t)$ such that $SOC(t)$ remains within the sustainable window $[0.3, 0.8]$ over the entire trip. Common strategies include: rule-based (thermostat control), fuzzy logic, and Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS).

(Equation derivation: 4 marks, EMS discussion: 3 marks)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**  
**(a)** Compare the **Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel hybrid drive-trains** in terms of mechanical complexity, ICE operating flexibility, fuel economy, and typical applications. Cite **one real-world example** for each topology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Series Hybrid car has the following specifications:  
- Vehicle mass $M = 1500 \text{ kg}$  
- Rolling resistance coefficient $f_r = 0.015$  
- Drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$, Frontal area $A = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$  
- Air density $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$  
- Final drive ratio + gearbox efficiency $\eta_{tx} = 0.92$  
- Combined genset and traction efficiency $\eta_{gen}\eta_{inv}\eta_{mot} = 0.80$  
- The car climbs a $5\%$ gradient at constant $v = 60 \text{ km/h}$.  

**Calculate:**  
(i) The total tractive force required. **(3 marks)**  
(ii) The mechanical power required at the wheels. **(2 marks)**  
(iii) The fuel power required from the ICE (assume $P_{bat} = 0$ for steady cruising). **(2 marks)**

##### Model Solution (a):

| **Parameter** | **Series** | **Parallel** | **Series-Parallel (PS)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical complexity | Lowest (1 motor + 1 generator) | Moderate (clutch + gearbox) | Highest (planetary gearset) |
| ICE flexibility | Highest (single BSFC point) | Lowest (follows wheels) | Moderate to high |
| Urban fuel economy | **Best** | Moderate | Best overall |
| Highway fuel economy | Moderate | Good | **Best** |
| Example | BMW i3 REx / Nissan e-POWER | Honda Civic IMA | Toyota Prius / Ford Escape |

(7 marks: 1 mark per row + 1 mark for citing examples)

##### Model Solution (b):

**Given data:**  
$M = 1500$ kg, $f_r = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$, $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $v = 60 \text{ km/h} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}$, gradient $i = 5\% = 0.05$.

**(i) Tractive Force Calculation:**  

The total tractive force equation is:  

$$
F_{tr} = M g f_r \cos\theta + 0.5 \rho C_d A v^2 + M g \sin\theta
$$

For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = 0.05$ and $\cos\theta \approx 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{rolling} &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times 1 = 220.7 \text{ N} \\
F_{aero} &= 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (16.67)^2 = 110.2 \text{ N} \\
F_{grade} &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 = 735.8 \text{ N} \\
F_{tr} &= 220.7 + 110.2 + 735.8 = 1066.7 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{tr} \approx 1067 \text{ N}}
$$

**(ii) Wheel Power:**  

$$
P_{wheel} = F_{tr} \cdot v = 1066.7 \times 16.67 = 17{,}782 \text{ W} \approx 17.78 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{wheel} \approx 17.78 \text{ kW}}
$$

**(iii) ICE Fuel Power (with $P_{bat} = 0$):**  

For steady cruising, the entire wheel power comes from the genset. Accounting for the transmission and electrical chain efficiencies:

$$
P_{ICE} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{tx} \cdot \eta_{gen}\eta_{inv}\eta_{mot}} = \frac{17{,}782}{0.92 \times 0.80} = \frac{17{,}782}{0.736}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{ICE} \approx 24{,}160 \text{ W} \approx 24.16 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**  
> 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** (loses 1–2 marks).  
> 2. **Ignoring aerodynamic drag** — many students compute only rolling + grade resistance.  
> 3. **Using $\sin\theta$ instead of $\tan\theta = $ grade \% / 100** for gradient angle.  
> 4. **Confusing $P_{ICE}$ with $P_{wheel}$** — the ICE must supply MORE power than the wheel demand due to conversion losses.  
> 5. **In comparison questions, missing real-world examples** costs 1 mark minimum.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Series Hybrid Drive-Train**

- ✅ The ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from the wheels — *this is the defining feature*.
- ✅ Power flow: **Fuel → ICE → Generator → DC Bus → Inverter → Traction Motor → Wheels**.
- ✅ The ICE is sized for **average**, not peak, demand (typically $0.6$ to $0.8 \times$ traction motor rating).
- ✅ The battery **buffers** transient power mismatches and hides the engine's slow dynamics.
- ✅ Master power balance: $P_{tr} = \eta_{sys,ICE}\,P_{ICE} + \eta_{sys,bat}\,P_{bat}$.
- ✅ SOC dynamics: $dSOC/dt = P_{bat}(t) / E_{bat}$ — this ODE governs the entire Energy Management Strategy.
- ✅ Three operating modes: **EV mode, Series mode (sustain + peak assist), Regenerative braking**.
- ✅ **Best fuel economy in urban duty cycles** due to ICE held at optimum BSFC.
- ✅ Major drawback: **three energy conversions** → cumulative efficiency loss.
- ✅ Real-world examples to memorize: **BMW i3 REx, Chevrolet Volt Gen 1, Nissan e-POWER, Mazda MX-30 e-Skyactiv R-EV**.
- ✅ The traction motor and transmission are the **only** mechanical path to the road.
- ✅ VCU (Vehicle Control Unit) supervises all power flow decisions in real time.
- ✅ Battery SOC operating window: typically maintained between **30% and 80%**.
- ✅ Key efficiency chain: $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{trans}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parallel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Parallel Hybrid Configuration**: A parallel hybrid energy system is a hybrid powertrain architecture in which **two or more energy conversion devices** (typically an Internal Combustion Engine and an Electric Motor) are **mechanically coupled in parallel** to a common driveline so that each can independently, or simultaneously, deliver tractive power to the wheels.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Module 4 – Hybrid Energy Systems), the *parallel* arrangement is one of the three classical architectures of a **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** — the other two being **series** and **series–parallel (power-split)**. The defining feature is the **mechanical summation of torque** delivered by two distinct prime movers onto a **single transmission shaft**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Tug-of-War Team:**
> Imagine a heavy cart that needs to be pulled up a hill. You have **two helpers**: Helper A (the ICE — strong, but needs fuel and gets tired on slow speeds) and Helper B (the Electric Motor — quiet, instant torque, but limited by battery). In a **parallel** configuration, *both helpers pull the SAME rope at the SAME time*, combining their effort. Helper B can also charge a backup battery by acting as a generator when the cart is coasting downhill. This is exactly how a parallel hybrid works — **two power sources, one driveline, working together or one-at-a-time as the road demands**.

## 1.3 System-Level Boundary Conditions

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| **ICE Power Share** | 60 % – 80 % of total tractive demand |
| **EM Power Share** | 20 % – 40 % of total tractive demand |
| **Battery Nominal Voltage** | **144 V – 300 V** (Ni-MH / Li-ion) |
| **EM Peak Efficiency** | **90 % – 95 %** |
| **Regenerative Braking Recovery** | up to **30 %** of kinetic energy |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Logic — The Five Modes of a Parallel Hybrid

A parallel HEV can operate in **five distinct modes**, selected by the Hybrid Control Unit (HCU) based on driver demand, battery **State of Charge (SOC)**, and vehicle speed.

1. **ICE-Only Mode** — At steady highway cruising (typically > 50 km/h), the ICE alone drives the wheels. The electric motor is decoupled via a clutch.
2. **EM-Only Mode (EV / Zero-Emission Mode)** — At low speeds or light loads (urban driving), the ICE is shut off and the EM alone propels the vehicle. This is the **highest-efficiency mode**.
3. **Parallel Traction Mode** — Both ICE and EM deliver torque *simultaneously* to the driveline. Used during hard acceleration or hill climbing.
4. **ICE Drive + EM Generator Mode** — The ICE drives the wheels *and* spins the EM (now acting as a generator) to recharge the battery. Excess ICE power is captured.
5. **Regenerative Braking Mode** — The EM acts as a generator during deceleration, converting kinetic energy into electrical energy stored in the battery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The defining mathematical feature of a parallel hybrid is the **additive torque equation** at the coupling node — unlike a series hybrid where powers add.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Description & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tractive Power Demand** | $P_{demand} = \frac{m \cdot v \cdot a}{\eta_{driveline}} + P_{aux}$ | $m$ = mass (kg), $v$ = velocity (m/s), $a$ = acceleration (m/s²), $\eta_{driveline}$ = drivetrain efficiency |
| 2 | **Torque Summation (Parallel Identity)** | $T_{wheels} = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}$ | The signature equation of parallel topology (N·m) |
| 3 | **Power Partition** | $P_{total} = P_{ICE} + P_{EM}$ | Valid *only when* both operate at same shaft speed $\omega$ |
| 4 | **Shaft-Speed Coupling Constraint** | $\omega_{wheels} = \omega_{ICE} = \omega_{EM}$ | Parallel = same speed, additive torque |
| 5 | **Fuel Consumption Rate** | $\dot{m}_f = \frac{P_{ICE}}{\eta_{ICE} \cdot LHV_{fuel}}$ | kg/s; $LHV$ = Lower Heating Value (J/kg) |
| 6 | **Battery SOC Dynamics** | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \frac{1}{Q_{bat}} \int I_{bat}(t) \, dt$ | $Q_{bat}$ = battery capacity (A·s), $I_{bat}$ = current |
| 7 | **Regenerative Energy Recovered** | $E_{regen} = \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{regen}$ | Joules; $\eta_{regen}$ ≈ 0.6 – 0.8 |
| 8 | **Overall Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency** | $\eta_{TTW} = \frac{\int P_{wheels} \, dt}{\int \dot{m}_f \cdot LHV \, dt}$ | Dimensionless, 0 < $\eta_{TTW}$ < 1 |
| 9 | **Equivalent Fuel Economy** | $MPG_{eq} = \frac{D_{distance}}{V_{fuel} + \frac{E_{elec}}{LHV_{gasoline}}}$ | Distance per energy equivalent |
| 10 | **Torque Coupling Ratio** | $k = \frac{T_{EM}}{T_{ICE}}$ | Dimensionless; $k > 1$ → EM-dominant |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Parallel hybrid systems are widely used in mass-market sedans (Toyota Prius uses series–parallel, but Honda Civic Hybrid and Hyundai Sonata Hybrid use **true parallel**) because they:
- Retain the conventional **multi-speed transmission**, reducing development cost.
- Allow downsizing of the ICE (engine operates near its **BSFC island** of best efficiency).
- Enable **pure EV mode** for short urban commutes, satisfying ZEV mandates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Balance Across the Coupling Node

We begin with the fundamental premise of a parallel hybrid: both the ICE and the EM share a common mechanical node before the transmission.

$$
P_{coupling} = P_{ICE} + P_{EM}
$$

Since mechanical power is the product of torque and angular velocity ($P = T\omega$), and in the parallel arrangement the shaft speeds are identical:

$$
\omega_{ICE} = \omega_{EM} = \omega_{shaft}
$$

we can divide the entire power equation by the common shaft speed:

$$
\frac{P_{coupling}}{\omega_{shaft}} = \frac{P_{ICE}}{\omega_{shaft}} + \frac{P_{EM}}{\omega_{shaft}}
$$

$$
T_{coupling} = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}
$$

This additive torque identity is the **mathematical signature of any parallel hybrid** and is a guaranteed KTU question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Flow During a Single Drive Cycle

Consider a vehicle of mass $m = 1500$ kg, cruising at $v_1 = 20$ m/s, then braking to $v_2 = 10$ m/s over 5 seconds.

**Step 1 — Initial Kinetic Energy**

$$
E_{k1} = \frac{1}{2} m v_1^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (20)^2
$$

$$
E_{k1} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times 400 = 300{,}000 \; \text{J} = 300 \; \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Final Kinetic Energy**

$$
E_{k2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (10)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times 100 = 75{,}000 \; \text{J} = 75 \; \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Kinetic Energy Released (Available for Regeneration)**

$$
\Delta E_k = E_{k1} - E_{k2} = 300 - 75 = 225 \; \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Regenerative Efficiency** $\eta_{regen} = 0.7$:

$$
E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \Delta E_k = 0.7 \times 225 = 157.5 \; \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Equivalent Fuel Mass Saved** (gasoline $LHV = 44$ MJ/kg):

$$
m_{fuel\_saved} = \frac{E_{regen}}{LHV} = \frac{157{,}500}{44 \times 10^6} = 3.58 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg}
$$

$$
m_{fuel\_saved} \approx 3.58 \; \text{grams}
$$

This is the **fuel saved per braking event** thanks to the regenerative capability enabled by the parallel topology.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — State of Charge (SOC) Conservation

The battery SOC evolves according to the current drawn or supplied. For a discharge current $I > 0$:

$$
\frac{d(SOC)}{dt} = -\frac{I(t)}{Q_{bat}}
$$

Integrating over a charging interval $\Delta t$ where the EM acts as a generator with current $I_g$:

$$
\Delta SOC = \frac{1}{Q_{bat}} \int_{0}^{\Delta t} I_g(t) \, dt = \frac{Q_{charged}}{Q_{bat}}
$$

For a typical parallel hybrid battery pack: $Q_{bat} = 6.5 \; \text{Ah} = 6.5 \times 3600 = 23{,}400 \; \text{C}$.

If regenerative charging delivers $Q_{charged} = 1.5 \; \text{Ah} = 5400 \; \text{C}$ during one downhill event:

$$
\Delta SOC = \frac{5400}{23{,}400} = 0.2308 = 23.08 \%
$$

This is the **per-event SOC recovery** — a frequently tested KTU calculation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Parallel Mode Fuel Economy Improvement

Baseline ICE-only fuel rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{f,ICE} = \frac{P_{demand}}{\eta_{ICE} \cdot LHV}
$$

With parallel hybrid, ICE operates at its BSFC sweet spot with effective efficiency $\eta_{ICE,opt} = 0.38$ instead of average $\eta_{ICE,avg} = 0.28$. The fractional fuel saving is:

$$
F_{save} = 1 - \frac{\eta_{ICE,avg}}{\eta_{ICE,opt}} = 1 - \frac{0.28}{0.38} = 0.263 = 26.3\%
$$

This validates the typical **25 %–35 % urban fuel-economy gain** quoted for parallel HEVs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Parallel HEV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[Internal Combustion Engine]
    B --> C{Mechanical Coupling Node}
    D[BATTERY PACK] --> E[Power Electronic Converter]
    E --> F[Electric Motor Generator]
    F --> C
    C --> G[Multi-Speed Transmission]
    G --> H[Final Drive and Differential]
    H --> I[DRIVE WHEELS]
    J[Brake Pedal Signal] --> K[Hybrid Control Unit HCU]
    L[Accelerator Pedal] --> K
    M[SOC Sensor] --> K
    N[Wheel Speed Sensor] --> K
    K --> B
    K --> F
```

## 4.2 Energy Flow State Diagram (Five Modes)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    s1: ICE-Only Mode
    s2: EM-Only Mode (Zero Emission)
    s3: Parallel Traction Mode
    s4: ICE Drive plus EM Generator
    s5: Regenerative Braking
    s1 --> s2 : low speed, SOC high
    s2 --> s3 : high acceleration demand
    s3 --> s4 : SOC below threshold
    s4 --> s1 : cruise speed reached
    s1 --> s5 : brake pedal pressed
    s5 --> s2 : SOC topped up, low demand
```

## 4.3 Torque-Coupling Schematic (Mechanical View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ParallelShaft [Common Driveline Shaft]
        T1[ICE Torque Source] -- same omega --> N1{Clutch 1}
        T2[EM Torque Source] -- same omega --> N2{Clutch 2}
        N1 --> SUM((Torque Summation Node))
        N2 --> SUM
    end
    SUM --> TRANS[Multi-Speed Gearbox]
    TRANS --> DIFF[Differential]
    DIFF --> W1[Wheel Left]
    DIFF --> W2[Wheel Right]
```

## 4.4 Power-Flow Sankey (Textual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FUEL[Fuel Chemical Energy] -->|100 percent| ICE[Internal Combustion Engine]
    BAT[Battery Electrical Energy] --> INV[Inverter]
    INV --> EM[Electric Motor]
    ICE --> COUP{Parallel Coupling}
    EM --> COUP
    COUP -->|minus losses| TRANS[Transmission]
    TRANS --> WHEEL[Wheels]
    COUP -.->|excess| GEN[EM as Generator]
    GEN -.-> BAT
    WHEEL -.->|decel| REG[Regenerative Braking]
    REG -.-> BAT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between series and parallel hybrid vehicle configurations with a suitable sketch.**

**Model Answer:**
In a **series hybrid**, the ICE drives a generator that produces electricity; only the electric motor propels the wheels. The ICE is *mechanically decoupled* from the wheels. Power flows: ICE → Generator → Battery/Motor → Wheels.

In a **parallel hybrid**, the ICE and the EM are *mechanically coupled* to the same driveline via a clutch-coupling node. Both can drive the wheels independently or simultaneously. Power flows: ICE **+** EM → Coupling → Transmission → Wheels.

| Feature | Series | Parallel |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical link ICE–Wheels | None | Direct |
| Torque summation | No (power sum only) | Yes ($T_w = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}$) |
| Transmission | Simple single-speed | Conventional multi-speed |
| City efficiency | Higher | Moderate |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any three operating modes of a parallel hybrid electric vehicle.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **ICE-only mode** — Highway cruising.
2. **EM-only mode (pure EV)** — Low-speed urban driving.
3. **Parallel traction mode** — High acceleration; both ICE and EM deliver torque.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and power flow** in a parallel hybrid electric vehicle. Discuss any **four operating modes**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The parallel hybrid architecture consists of two prime movers — the **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and the **Electric Motor/Generator (EMG)** — mechanically coupled to a common driveline through clutches C1 and C2 respectively.

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.1 above).**

**Power Flow Description:**
- Fuel energy enters the ICE; electrical energy from the battery enters the EM via an inverter.
- Both outputs merge at the **torque coupling node**, where $T_w = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}$ and $\omega$ is common.
- The summed torque passes through a **multi-speed transmission**, the **final drive**, and reaches the **drive wheels**.

**Four Operating Modes:**

1. **ICE-Only Mode:** At cruising speeds above ~50 km/h, the ICE alone drives the vehicle. The EM is decoupled via C2. Highest efficiency on highways.

2. **EM-Only Mode:** At low speeds (urban traffic, < 40 km/h) when SOC is adequate, the ICE is shut off and C1 disengages. The EM alone propels the vehicle — zero tailpipe emissions.

3. **Parallel Traction Mode:** During hard acceleration, both clutches engage. Both ICE and EM deliver torque simultaneously. This is the mode where the **additive torque equation** $T_w = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}$ is operational. Provides maximum tractive effort.

4. **Regenerative Braking Mode:** During deceleration, the ICE fuel supply is cut. The EM operates as a generator, converting kinetic energy to electrical energy stored in the battery. Energy recovered: $E_{regen} = \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{regen}$.

**(b)** A parallel hybrid vehicle of mass **1500 kg** decelerates from **60 km/h to 20 km/h**. The regenerative braking system recovers **65 %** of the available kinetic energy. Calculate the **electrical energy stored** in the battery and the **equivalent mass of gasoline saved** ($LHV = 44$ MJ/kg). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert velocities to SI units:**
$v_1 = 60 \text{ km/h} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}$
$v_2 = 20 \text{ km/h} = 5.56 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 2 — Available kinetic energy change:**
$\Delta E_k = \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$
$\Delta E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (16.67^2 - 5.56^2)$
$\Delta E_k = 750 \times (277.89 - 30.91) = 750 \times 246.98$
$\Delta E_k = 185{,}235 \; \text{J} \approx 185.24 \; \text{kJ}$

**Step 3 — Energy stored in battery:**
$E_{bat} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \Delta E_k = 0.65 \times 185.24 = 120.40 \; \text{kJ}$

**Step 4 — Equivalent gasoline mass saved:**
$m_{fuel} = \frac{E_{bat}}{LHV} = \frac{120{,}400}{44 \times 10^6} = 2.736 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg}$
$m_{fuel} \approx 2.74 \; \text{grams}$

**Final Answer:** $E_{bat} = 120.4 \; \text{kJ}$ and $m_{fuel} \approx 2.74 \; \text{g of gasoline equivalent}$.

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **torque-coupling equation** for a parallel hybrid configuration and explain why it differs from the power-coupling equation of a series hybrid. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In a parallel hybrid, the ICE and EM share a common shaft. Mechanical power at any node is:

$$
P = T \cdot \omega
$$

At the coupling node, total power is the sum of individual powers (assuming losses are neglected at the coupling):

$$
P_{total} = P_{ICE} + P_{EM}
$$

Since both machines rotate at the **same shaft speed** $\omega$ (parallel coupling constraint):

$$
T_{total} \cdot \omega = T_{ICE} \cdot \omega + T_{EM} \cdot \omega
$$

Dividing throughout by $\omega$:

$$
T_{total} = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}
$$

**Why this differs from Series Hybrid:**
In a **series** hybrid, the ICE does not mechanically touch the wheels. Power flows through a chain: ICE → Generator → Battery → Motor → Wheels. Therefore the **power** at each stage must be summed (with efficiency losses), not the torque. There is no common shaft. In a **parallel** hybrid, because of the shared mechanical node, **torques add directly** while speed is common — a much more efficient mechanical summation.

**(b)** A parallel hybrid vehicle has an ICE rated at **75 kW** at 4500 rpm and an EM rated at **30 kW** at 3000 rpm. The transmission couples both to the wheels at a fixed ratio such that the wheel speed is **$\omega_w = \omega_{ICE} = \omega_{EM}$** during parallel mode. Compute **(i)** the peak combined torque at the coupling node, and **(ii)** the combined shaft speed in rpm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Peak ICE Torque:**
$T_{ICE} = \frac{P_{ICE}}{\omega_{ICE}} = \frac{75{,}000}{4500 \times \frac{2\pi}{60}} = \frac{75{,}000}{471.24}$
$T_{ICE} \approx 159.15 \; \text{N·m}$

**Step 2 — Peak EM Torque:**
$T_{EM} = \frac{P_{EM}}{\omega_{EM}} = \frac{30{,}000}{3000 \times \frac{2\pi}{60}} = \frac{30{,}000}{314.16}$
$T_{EM} \approx 95.49 \; \text{N·m}$

**Step 3 — Combined Torque (Parallel Mode):**
$T_{coupling} = T_{ICE} + T_{EM} = 159.15 + 95.49 = 254.64 \; \text{N·m}$

**Step 4 — Common Shaft Speed:**
Since both prime movers are rigidly coupled in parallel mode:
$\omega_{shaft} = \omega_{ICE} = \omega_{EM} = 4500 \; \text{rpm}$ (limited by ICE red-line)

*Note:* In practice, an epicyclic gear-set or torque-coupler manages the speed mismatch; in this problem we apply the parallel-mode constraint directly.

**Final Answer:** $T_{coupling} \approx 254.6 \; \text{N·m}$, $\omega_{shaft} = 4500 \; \text{rpm}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write $P_{total} = P_{ICE} \cdot P_{EM}$ or any multiplicative form. *Power sums, never multiplies, in a parallel hybrid.*
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse torque-coupling (parallel) with power-coupling (series). Examiners specifically award **2 marks** for correctly stating $T_w = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}$ with justification.
> 3. **Always convert km/h to m/s** before kinetic-energy calculations. A unit error here costs **1 full mark**.
> 4. When asked about *operating modes*, name **all five** if possible; partial credit is heavily weighted toward completeness.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Parallel hybrid = ICE + EM mechanically coupled to a **single driveline shaft**.
- **Signature equation:** $T_w = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}$ at common shaft speed $\omega$.
- **Five modes:** ICE-only, EM-only, Parallel traction, ICE + Generator, Regenerative braking.
- **Key advantage:** Retains conventional transmission → lower cost, easier packaging.
- **Key disadvantage:** Complex HCU control strategy required; ICE must be frequently started/stopped.
- **Regenerative energy** per braking event: $E_{regen} = \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{regen}$.
- **SOC balance** must be maintained in a band (typically **30 % – 80 %**) to prevent battery degradation.
- **Typical fuel economy gain** over ICE-only: **25 % – 35 %** in urban driving cycles.
- **Compared with series:** Parallel is mechanically simpler but control-wise more complex.
- **Compared with series-parallel:** Parallel uses one planetary gear-set less, hence lower cost but lower efficiency at part-load.
- **Real-world examples:** Honda Civic Hybrid (1st gen), Honda Insight, Hyundai Sonata Hybrid.
- **KTU-favourite formula trio:** (1) Torque summation, (2) KE → regen energy, (3) SOC integral.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series-Parallel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Series-Parallel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Series-Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SP-HEV)**, also known as a **Power-Split Hybrid** or **Combined Hybrid**, is an advanced hybrid powertrain architecture that mechanically couples an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) with two or more electric motor-generators (typically designated **MGA** and **MGB**) through a **planetary gear set (PGS)** functioning as an electromechanical **Power Split Device (PSD)**. This configuration simultaneously enables both **series hybrid** power flow (where the ICE drives a generator to produce electricity that subsequently powers a traction motor) and **parallel hybrid** power flow (where the ICE and an electric motor deliver mechanical torque directly to the wheels), with the instantaneous ratio between the two modes being continuously and variably controlled by an electronic **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Series-Parallel configuration is universally recognized as the most commercially dominant HEV architecture. The Toyota Hybrid System (THS), introduced in the 1997 Toyota Prius, is the canonical textbook example, and the THS-II/THS-III versions of the same architecture continue to dominate global hybrid vehicle sales exceeding **20 million units** cumulatively.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are riding a bicycle up a hill. You have **two ways** to move forward:
- **Series mode (helper pushing you from behind):** Your friend stands on the back and pushes you. They convert their energy through a chain (electricity → motor) to drive you. You are decoupled from their effort.
- **Parallel mode (friend pedaling alongside you):** Your friend rides their own bicycle right beside you, both pedaling together to move a common load.

A **Series-Parallel hybrid** is like a **clever, continuously variable transmission** that automatically decides — thousands of times per second — how much of your friend's effort should push you from behind (series) and how much should pedal alongside you (parallel). The **planetary gear set** acts as the "smart mechanical brain" that splits and recombines the two power flows seamlessly, ensuring the engine always runs at its most efficient operating point regardless of vehicle speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Philosophy:** The ICE in a Series-Parallel HEV is **decoupled from the wheels** by the planetary gear. This means the engine speed is *independent* of vehicle speed, allowing it to operate on its optimal Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) curve — something impossible in a pure parallel hybrid.

### 1.3 System Components (Bolded Core Metrics)

The standard Series-Parallel architecture consists of:

| Component | Standard Symbol/Notation | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|
| Internal Combustion Engine | **ICE** | Primary thermal energy source |
| Motor-Generator 1 | **MGA** | Primarily acts as a **starter-generator**; controls ICE speed |
| Motor-Generator 2 | **MGB** | Primary **traction motor**; delivers torque to wheels |
| Planetary Gear Set | **PGS / PSD** | Mechanical power-splitting device |
| Ring Gear | **R** | Output to final drive / wheels |
| Sun Gear | **S** | Coupled to MGA |
| Carrier | **C** | Coupled to ICE |
| High-Voltage Battery | **HVB** (typically **201.6 V** Ni-MH or Li-ion) | Energy buffer for MGA & MGB |
| Inverter | **INV** | DC–AC conversion at switching frequencies up to **~20 kHz** |

### 1.4 The Two Distinct Power Paths

1. **Mechanical Path (Parallel Component):** ICE → Planetary Carrier → Ring Gear → Wheels. Torque is transmitted mechanically with high efficiency (typically **> 96 %** through the gear set).

2. Electrical Path (Series Component): ICE → MGA (acting as generator) → Inverter → HVB/MGB (acting as motor) → Wheels. Electrical losses are higher (**~ 85–90 %** round-trip efficiency), but provide flexibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Planetary Gear Set — Heart of the Series-Parallel System

A **simple planetary gear set** (also called an **epicyclic gear train**) consists of three rotating members:

- **Sun Gear (S):** The central gear, with teeth $N_s$ and angular velocity $\omega_s$.
- **Ring Gear (R):** The outer annular gear, with internal teeth $N_r$ and angular velocity $\omega_r$.
- **Planet Carrier (C):** Holds the planet pinions in mesh, rotates at $\omega_c$. The planet pinions themselves do not constitute a power node — they are merely idler gears that constrain the relative motion of S and R.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Note:** In the canonical Toyota THS arrangement, the **Sun gear is connected to MGA**, the **Carrier is connected to the ICE**, and the **Ring gear is connected to MGB and the final drive (wheels)**. Memorizing this mapping is essential for board exam derivations.

### 2.2 Operating Modes of a Series-Parallel HEV (Detailed Logic)

**Mode 1 — Pure Electric Launch (EV Mode):**
- ICE is OFF (decoupled, idling or clutch disengaged).
- MGB alone drives the ring gear and thus the wheels.
- MGA is stationary (locked, no generator action).
- Triggered at low speeds (typically **< 40 km/h**) and low loads.
- Battery State of Charge (SOC) must be above a threshold (typically **> 40 %**).

**Mode 2 — Engine-Only Propulsion (Direct Mechanical Drive):**
- ICE drives the carrier.
- MGB provides supplementary torque to the ring gear if needed.
- MGA acts as a generator to control engine speed (transmitting or receiving small power to maintain optimal $\omega_{ICE}$).
- This is the **most efficient steady-state cruising mode** at highway speeds (typically **> 60 km/h**).

**Mode 3 — Series Mode (ICE → MGA → MGB):**
- ICE drives the carrier.
- MGA (acting as generator) is held stationary, forcing the entire engine torque to be converted to electricity.
- MGB consumes this electricity to drive the ring gear (wheels).
- The ring gear rotates while both ICE and MGA provide input — the system behaves like a series hybrid with mechanical torque summation.
- Used during **moderate-to-high load transients** when the engine alone is not sufficient.

**Mode 4 — Parallel Mode (ICE + MGB direct to wheels):**
- ICE and MGB both add mechanical torque to the driveline through the planetary gear.
- MGA is used only as a generator/control element.
- Used during **hard acceleration** (full-throttle overtaking).

**Mode 5 — Regenerative Braking:**
- ICE is OFF.
- MGB acts as a generator, converting wheel kinetic energy into electrical energy stored in HVB.
- MGA may also be used to control ring gear speed.
- Recovery efficiency can reach **30–50 %** of braking energy.

**Mode 6 — Battery Charging at Standstill:**
- ICE drives the carrier; with the ring gear locked (vehicle stopped), MGA acts as a generator to charge the HVB.
- MGB is inactive.

### 2.3 Speed and Torque Relationships — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Mathematical Relationships for the Board Exam.** All speeds are in **rad/s** and torques in **N·m** unless specified.

#### A. Kinematic (Speed) Relationships

The Willis Equation for a simple planetary gear set:

$$\omega_c = \frac{N_s \cdot \omega_s + N_r \cdot \omega_r}{N_s + N_r}$$

In the THS arrangement ($\omega_c = \omega_{ICE}$, $\omega_s = \omega_{MGA}$, $\omega_r = \omega_{MGB} = \omega_{wheels} \cdot G_{fd}$):

$$\boxed{\omega_{ICE} = \frac{N_s \cdot \omega_{MGA} + N_r \cdot \omega_{MGB}}{N_s + N_r}}$$

**Special case — MGA held stationary** ($\omega_{MGA} = 0$, used in pure series mode):

$$\omega_{ICE} = \frac{N_r}{N_s + N_r} \cdot \omega_{MGB}$$

**Special case — MGB held stationary** ($\omega_{MGB} = 0$, used during standstill charging):

$$\omega_{ICE} = \frac{N_s}{N_s + N_r} \cdot \omega_{MGA}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** If the ratio $N_r / (N_s + N_r) = 0.7$ (a typical THS value), and MGA is locked, then the ICE rotates at 70 % of the wheel speed. This decoupling is the **fundamental advantage** of the architecture.

#### B. Static Force/Torque Relationships

Sum of torques on the planetary gear (neglecting inertia and friction):

$$T_{ICE} + T_{MGA} + T_{MGB} = 0$$

Wait — this is the **dynamic equilibrium** of torques ON the gear, which is zero. The **power balance** gives us:

$$T_{ICE} \cdot \omega_{ICE} + T_{MGA} \cdot \omega_{MGA} + T_{MGB} \cdot \omega_{MGB} = 0$$

The **torque ratio** on the gear members is:

$$T_{MGA} \cdot N_r = T_{MGB} \cdot N_s$$

And the relationship between engine torque and wheel-side torques:

$$T_{ring} = T_{ICE} \cdot \frac{N_s + N_r}{N_r}$$

$$T_{sun} = T_{ICE} \cdot \frac{N_s + N_r}{N_s}$$

#### C. KTU Cheat-Sheet Table (All Critical Formulas)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega_{ICE} = \dfrac{N_s \omega_{MGA} + N_r \omega_{MGB}}{N_s + N_r}$ | Willis speed equation for THS | rad/s |
| 2 | $T_{MGA} \cdot N_r = T_{MGB} \cdot N_s$ | Torque distribution on gear members | N·m |
| 3 | $P_{ICE} = P_{MGA,elec} + P_{MGB,mech}$ | Power split conservation | W or kW |
| 4 | $\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{P_{fuel}} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{elec}$ | Overall drivetrain efficiency | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | $SOC_{min} < SOC(t) < SOC_{max}$ (typically 40 % – 80 %) | Battery management window | % |
| 6 | $E_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 \cdot \eta_{regen}$ | Regenerative braking energy | J |
| 7 | $v_{vehicle} = \omega_{ring} \cdot r_{wheel} / G_{fd}$ | Vehicle speed from ring gear speed | m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention for Board Exams:** When the ICE delivers power, take $T_{ICE} > 0$ and $\omega_{ICE} > 0$. When MGA is generating, $T_{MGA}$ and $\omega_{MGA}$ are of the **same sign** in our convention; the electrical power is $P_{elec} = T_{MGA} \cdot \omega_{MGA} \cdot \eta_{gen}$. Always state the convention explicitly to avoid losing marks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Fuel Economy:** Series-Parallel HEVs typically deliver **40–55 % improvement** in combined-cycle fuel economy over equivalent conventional ICE vehicles.
2. **Emissions Reduction:** Tailpipe CO₂ reduced by **30–45 %**; NOx and PM reduced substantially since the ICE runs in a narrow optimal window.
3. **No Plug Required:** All energy comes from liquid fuel — a key advantage over Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) in regions with limited charging infrastructure.
4. **Scalability:** The same architecture is used from compact cars (Prius) to luxury SUVs (Lexus RX 500h) and even heavy-duty applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Willis Equation for a Simple Planetary Gear

**Statement to Prove:** 
$$\omega_c = \frac{N_s \omega_s + N_r \omega_r}{N_s + N_r}$$

**Method 1 — The Classical Kinematic Derivation (Willis, 1852):**

Consider the planet pinion P with $N_p$ teeth. It meshes externally with the sun gear and internally with the ring gear.

**Step 1 — Velocity of the planet center.**

The center of the planet pinion is rigidly attached to the carrier, so it moves with velocity:

$$v_{center} = \omega_c \cdot R_c$$

where $R_c$ is the distance from the system axis to the planet center.

**Step 2 — Pitch radii relationships.**

For a meshing pair, pitch radii are proportional to tooth counts. Let $r_s$, $r_r$, $r_p$ be the pitch radii of the sun, ring, and planet, respectively:

$$r_s = k \cdot N_s, \quad r_r = k \cdot N_r, \quad r_p = k \cdot N_p$$

**Step 3 — Velocity of the sun gear at its pitch point.**

The velocity of the sun at the meshing point with the planet equals the planet's tangential velocity at the same point:

$$\omega_s \cdot r_s = v_{center} - \omega_{p/spin} \cdot r_p$$

where $\omega_{p/spin}$ is the spin angular velocity of the planet about its own center (relative to the carrier).

**Step 4 — Velocity of the ring gear at its pitch point.**

The ring gear's internal mesh velocity:

$$\omega_r \cdot r_r = v_{center} + \omega_{p/spin} \cdot r_p$$

**Step 5 — Adding the two equations.**

Adding Step 3 and Step 4:

$$\omega_s \cdot r_s + \omega_r \cdot r_r = 2 \cdot v_{center} = 2 \cdot \omega_c \cdot R_c$$

**Step 6 — Geometry of the carrier radius.**

The planet center is at radius equal to $R_c = r_s + r_p$. Also, the ring center to ring meshing point is $R_c = r_r - r_p$. Adding: $2 R_c = r_s + r_r$. Thus:

$$R_c = \frac{r_s + r_r}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Substituting the geometry relation.**

\begin{aligned}
\omega_s \cdot r_s + \omega_r \cdot r_r &= 2 \cdot \omega_c \cdot \frac{r_s + r_r}{2} \\
\omega_s \cdot r_s + \omega_r \cdot r_r &= \omega_c \cdot (r_s + r_r) \\
\omega_c &= \frac{r_s \omega_s + r_r \omega_r}{r_s + r_r}
\end{aligned}

**Step 8 — Replacing radii with tooth numbers (since $r \propto N$):**

$$\boxed{\omega_c = \frac{N_s \omega_s + N_r \omega_r}{N_s + N_r}} \quad \text{Q.E.D.}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Ratio $T_{MGA} \cdot N_r = T_{MGB} \cdot N_s$

**Method — Power Conservation with the Willis Speed Relation:**

**Step 1 — Power balance on the planetary gear set.**

In a stationary analysis (neglecting rotational kinetic energy changes), the total power entering the gear set must equal zero:

$$P_{ICE} + P_{MGA} + P_{MGB} = 0$$

$$T_{ICE} \cdot \omega_{ICE} + T_{MGA} \cdot \omega_{MGA} + T_{MGB} \cdot \omega_{MGB} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Express all speeds relative to the carrier.**

In the carrier's reference frame, the planet carrier is stationary. Using the relative-speed form of the Willis equation:

$$0 = \frac{N_s (\omega_{MGA} - \omega_{ICE}) + N_r (\omega_{MGB} - \omega_{ICE})}{N_s + N_r}$$

This simplifies to:

$$N_s \omega_{MGA} + N_r \omega_{MGB} = (N_s + N_r) \omega_{ICE}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the relation into the power equation.**

The power equation becomes, after substituting $\omega_{ICE}$:

$$T_{ICE} \cdot \frac{N_s \omega_{MGA} + N_r \omega_{MGB}}{N_s + N_r} + T_{MGA} \cdot \omega_{MGA} + T_{MGB} \cdot \omega_{MGB} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Collect terms in $\omega_{MGA}$ and $\omega_{MGB}$.**

\begin{aligned}
\omega_{MGA} \left[ \frac{T_{ICE} N_s}{N_s + N_r} + T_{MGA} \right] + \omega_{MGB} \left[ \frac{T_{ICE} N_r}{N_s + N_r} + T_{MGB} \right] = 0
\end{aligned}

**Step 5 — Since $\omega_{MGA}$ and $\omega_{MGB}$ are kinematically independent (two degrees of freedom), each bracketed term must independently equal zero.**

\begin{aligned}
T_{MGA} &= -T_{ICE} \cdot \frac{N_s}{N_s + N_r} \\
T_{MGB} &= -T_{ICE} \cdot \frac{N_r}{N_s + N_r}
\end{aligned}

**Step 6 — Form the ratio.**

$$\frac{T_{MGA}}{T_{MGB}} = \frac{N_s}{N_r}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$\boxed{T_{MGA} \cdot N_r = T_{MGB} \cdot N_s} \quad \text{Q.E.D.}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Board-Style Numerical

> **Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]:** A Toyota-Prius-style THS uses a planetary gear set with $N_s = 30$ teeth (sun) and $N_r = 78$ teeth (ring). The vehicle cruises at **60 km/h** with the ring gear rotating at $\omega_{MGB} = 200$ rad/s. The ICE delivers **T_{ICE} = 80 N·m** at **3000 rpm** (i.e., $\omega_{ICE} \approx 314.16$ rad/s). Determine:
> (a) The required MGA speed $\omega_{MGA}$.
> (b) The torque on MGB and the net torque delivered to the wheels.
> (c) The electrical power generated by MGA (acting as a generator with efficiency $\eta_{gen} = 0.92$).

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — MGA Speed:**

Using the Willis equation:

\begin{aligned}
\omega_{ICE} &= \frac{N_s \omega_{MGA} + N_r \omega_{MGB}}{N_s + N_r} \\
314.16 &= \frac{30 \cdot \omega_{MGA} + 78 \cdot 200}{30 + 78} \\
314.16 &= \frac{30 \cdot \omega_{MGA} + 15{,}600}{108} \\
314.16 \times 108 &= 30 \cdot \omega_{MGA} + 15{,}600 \\
33{,}929.28 &= 30 \cdot \omega_{MGA} + 15{,}600 \\
30 \cdot \omega_{MGA} &= 33{,}929.28 - 15{,}600 = 18{,}329.28 \\
\omega_{MGA} &= \frac{18{,}329.28}{30} = 610.98 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}

Converting to rpm:

$$\omega_{MGA} = 610.98 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} \approx 5834 \text{ rpm}$$

**Part (b) — Torques:**

Using the derived torque formulas:

\begin{aligned}
T_{MGB} &= -T_{ICE} \cdot \frac{N_r}{N_s + N_r} = -80 \cdot \frac{78}{108} = -57.78 \text{ N·m} \\
T_{MGA} &= -T_{ICE} \cdot \frac{N_s}{N_s + N_r} = -80 \cdot \frac{30}{108} = -22.22 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}

The negative sign indicates these torques oppose the engine torque (i.e., they are reaction torques on the ring and sun). The net torque on the carrier is balanced. The wheel-side torque is the absolute value:

$$T_{wheels} = \vert T_{MGB} \vert = 57.78 \text{ N·m}$$

**Part (c) — Electrical Power Generated by MGA:**

\begin{aligned}
P_{MGA,mech} &= T_{MGA} \cdot \omega_{MGA} = 22.22 \times 610.98 = 13{,}575.6 \text{ W} \\
P_{MGA,elec} &= P_{MGA,mech} \cdot \eta_{gen} = 13{,}575.6 \times 0.92 = 12{,}489.5 \text{ W} \approx 12.49 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marker Note:** In board answers, always clearly state the **sign convention**, show all intermediate numerical substitutions, and convert final answers to standard SI units. Marks are deducted for skipping unit conversions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Series-Parallel HEV Powertrain Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ICE["ICE Engine"]
    CARRIER["Planetary Carrier C"]
    SUN["Sun Gear S"]
    MGA["Motor Generator A MGA"]
    RING["Ring Gear R"]
    MGB["Motor Generator B MGB"]
    INV["Inverter DC AC"]
    HVB["High Voltage Battery HVB"]
    FD["Final Drive and Wheels"]

    ICE --> CARRIER
    CARRIER --> SUN
    SUN --> MGA
    CARRIER --> RING
    RING --> MGB
    RING --> FD
    MGA --> INV
    MGB --> INV
    INV --> HVB
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Power Flow Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Operating Condition"] --> Q1{"Vehicle Speed Below 40 km/h"}
    Q1 -- YES --> M1["Mode 1 Pure EV MGB drives wheels"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"Highway Cruise Moderate Load"}
    Q2 -- YES --> M2["Mode 2 Engine Only ICE drives wheels via carrier and ring"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{"Hard Acceleration Full Throttle"}
    Q3 -- YES --> M4["Mode 4 Parallel ICE and MGB both drive wheels"]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{"Brake Applied"}
    Q4 -- YES --> M5["Mode 5 Regenerative Braking MGB generates"]
    Q4 -- NO --> M6["Mode 6 Standstill Charging ICE drives MGA generator only"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Energy Flow Summary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F["Fuel Tank"] --> ICE2["ICE"]
    ICE2 --> MECH["Mechanical Power"]
    ICE2 --> MGAGEN["MGA Generator"]
    MGAGEN --> ELECPWR["Electrical Power"]
    ELECPWR --> INV2["Inverter"]
    INV2 --> HVB2["HVB Battery"]
    INV2 --> MGBMOT["MGB Motor"]
    MGBMOT --> RING2["Ring Gear to Wheels"]
    MECH --> RING2
```

### 4.4 Component Coupling Matrix (Logic Map)

| Component | Connected To | Function | Energy Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ICE** | Planetary Carrier | Prime Mover | Chemical → Mechanical |
| **MGA** | Sun Gear | Generator / Starter | Mechanical ↔ Electrical |
| **MGB** | Ring Gear | Traction Motor / Generator | Electrical ↔ Mechanical |
| **Ring Gear** | Final Drive | Output to Wheels | Mechanical |
| **HVB** | Inverter (DC link) | Energy Buffer | Electrical / Chemical |
| **Inverter** | MGA, MGB, HVB | Power Conversion | DC ↔ AC |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question A1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
*What is a Series-Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle? How does it differ from a pure series hybrid?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Series-Parallel HEV is a hybrid powertrain that combines the characteristics of both series and parallel hybrids using a **planetary gear set** as a power-split device. It has two electric machines (MGA and MGB) coupled to a single prime mover (ICE).
- In a **pure series hybrid**, the ICE is *mechanically decoupled* from the wheels and drives only a generator; the traction motor alone propels the vehicle.
- In a **Series-Parallel hybrid**, the ICE *can mechanically drive the wheels* (parallel path) through the planetary gear ring/coupler, while also being able to drive the generator (series path) — the HCU continuously modulates the split ratio. This decoupling allows the ICE to operate at its most efficient point independent of vehicle speed — a feature absent in pure parallel and pure series configurations. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for differentiation, 1 for stating the advantage.)*

**Question A2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
*State the Willis equation for a simple planetary gear set and identify each term.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Willis equation is:
$$\omega_c = \frac{N_s \omega_s + N_r \omega_r}{N_s + N_r}$$
where:
- $\omega_c$ = angular velocity of the planet carrier (rad/s)
- $\omega_s$ = angular velocity of the sun gear (rad/s)
- $\omega_r$ = angular velocity of the ring gear (rad/s)
- $N_s$ = number of teeth on the sun gear
- $N_r$ = number of teeth on the ring gear *(3 marks: 1 for the equation, 2 for correct term identification with units/notation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the architecture of a Series-Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle. Identify and label the components: ICE, MGA, MGB, planetary gear set (sun, ring, carrier), inverter, and high-voltage battery. Explain the role of the planetary gear set as a continuously variable power split device.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Toyota THS-type Series-Parallel HEV has a planetary gear set with $N_s = 28$ and $N_r = 72$. At an instant, the ICE rotates at **2500 rpm** with torque **90 N·m**, MGB rotates at **180 rad/s**, and MGB efficiency is **0.90**. Calculate:
(i) The MGA speed in rad/s and rpm.
(ii) The mechanical power generated/consumed at MGA.
(iii) Whether MGA is acting as a motor or generator (justify with sign).

**Model Solution to (a):**

The architecture is described in **Section 1.4** and **Section 2.1** above. The student should draw a block diagram showing:
- ICE → Carrier (C)
- Sun (S) → MGA
- Ring (R) → MGB → Final drive → Wheels
- MGA & MGB ↔ Inverter ↔ HVB

The planetary gear set functions as a **Power Split Device (PSD)**. By varying $\omega_{MGA}$, the controller can adjust the ratio of mechanical-to-electrical power flow continuously, without needing a stepped gearbox. The relationship is governed by the Willis equation. *(Full marks: 2 for sketch, 2 for component identification, 3 for functional explanation.)*

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $N_s = 28$, $N_r = 72$, $\omega_{ICE} = 2500$ rpm, $T_{ICE} = 90$ N·m, $\omega_{MGB} = 180$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Convert $\omega_{ICE}$ to rad/s:**
$$\omega_{ICE} = 2500 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 261.80 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Willis equation to find $\omega_{MGA}$:**

\begin{aligned}
\omega_{ICE} &= \frac{N_s \omega_{MGA} + N_r \omega_{MGB}}{N_s + N_r} \\
261.80 &= \frac{28 \cdot \omega_{MGA} + 72 \cdot 180}{28 + 72} \\
261.80 &= \frac{28 \cdot \omega_{MGA} + 12{,}960}{100} \\
26{,}180 &= 28 \cdot \omega_{MGA} + 12{,}960 \\
28 \cdot \omega_{MGA} &= 13{,}220 \\
\omega_{MGA} &= 472.14 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}

Converting to rpm:
$$\omega_{MGA} = 472.14 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} \approx 4508 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute torque on MGA:**
$$T_{MGA} = -T_{ICE} \cdot \frac{N_s}{N_s + N_r} = -90 \cdot \frac{28}{100} = -25.2 \text{ N·m}$$

The negative sign (reaction torque) combined with **positive** $\omega_{MGA}$ means MGA absorbs mechanical power (acts as a generator).

**Step 4 — Mechanical power at MGA:**
$$P_{MGA,mech} = T_{MGA} \cdot \omega_{MGA} = 25.2 \times 472.14 = 11{,}897.9 \text{ W} \approx 11.90 \text{ kW}$$

The mechanical power is delivered *out* of the MGA (to the inverter), so MGA is **acting as a generator**, charging the battery or feeding MGB. *(Full marks: 2 for formula setup, 3 for numerical computation, 2 for physical interpretation/sign analysis.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel** hybrid powertrain architectures in terms of: (i) mechanical coupling of ICE to wheels, (ii) necessity of a multi-speed transmission, (iii) typical fuel economy improvement, (iv) component count/complexity, and (v) representative production vehicles.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the torque relationship $T_{MGA} \cdot N_r = T_{MGB} \cdot N_s$ for the planetary gear set in a Series-Parallel HEV using the principle of power conservation. State clearly the assumptions made.

**Model Solution to (a):**

| Parameter | Series HEV | Parallel HEV | Series-Parallel HEV |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Mechanical coupling of ICE to wheels | **None** (fully decoupled) | **Direct** mechanical link | **Indirect** via planetary gear |
| (ii) Multi-speed transmission | Not required | **Required** (to match ICE to wheel speeds) | **Not required** (PSD replaces it) |
| (iii) Fuel economy improvement | 25–35 % | 25–40 % | **40–55 %** |
| (iv) Component count / complexity | High (large generator + traction motor) | Low–Moderate | **High** (two MGs + PSD) |
| (v) Representative production vehicles | Chevrolet Volt (gen 1), BMW i3 REx | Honda Insight (gen 1), Honda Civic Hybrid | **Toyota Prius, Ford Escape Hybrid, Lexus RX** |

*(Marks: 1 mark per row × 5 rows = 5; 2 marks for any relevant additional insight.)*

**Model Solution to (b):**

The full derivation is provided in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The student must reproduce it step-by-step with the following key elements:

1. **Assumption statement:** Steady-state, frictionless gear, rigid members.
2. **Power balance:** $P_{ICE} + P_{MGA} + P_{MGB} = 0$.
3. **Substitute:** $T \cdot \omega$ form for each term.
4. **Use Willis equation** to eliminate one of the three speeds (typically $\omega_{ICE}$).
5. **Group terms** by independent variables $\omega_{MGA}$ and $\omega_{MGB}$.
6. **Argue independence** of the two motor speeds (two-DOF system) — therefore each bracket equals zero.
7. **Take the ratio** to obtain $T_{MGA}/T_{MGB} = N_s/N_r$.
8. **Cross-multiply** for the boxed final result.

*(Full marks: 1 for assumptions, 3 for substitution & grouping, 2 for the ratio step, 1 for final boxed equation.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Series-Parallel HEV Problems:**
> 1. **Wrong member-to-component mapping** (e.g., confusing which gear is connected to the engine). KTU expects the **Toyota THS convention**: Carrier = ICE, Sun = MGA, Ring = MGB/Wheels. Drawing a wrong diagram loses **2–3 marks** outright.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s** (or vice versa) in numerical problems. Always show the conversion: $\omega \text{ (rad/s)} = N \text{ (rpm)} \times 2\pi/60$.
> 3. **Sign convention errors** in torque analysis. If a student computes positive $P_{MGA,mech}$ without assigning a sign, the examiner cannot tell whether MGA is motoring or generating. Always annotate "MGA generating → $P_{MGA,mech}$ is mechanical power extracted *from* the sun gear."
> 4. **Skipping the derivation of Willis equation** when asked. Even if the equation is well-known, KTU 2024 scheme values the **derivation** as a critical-thinking indicator. A bare statement of the formula gets only **1 of 4 marks**; the derivation earns full credit.
> 5. **Confusing the planetary gear ratio** with a regular gear ratio. The "ratio" in a planetary gear is not constant; it varies with the operating mode. Students who write "gear ratio = 2.5" without qualifying the conditions lose marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Series-Parallel**

- **Architecture:** ICE + MGA + MGB + Planetary Gear Set (PGS) + HVB + Inverter.
- **Standard Coupling (Toyota THS):** Carrier ↔ ICE; Sun ↔ MGA; Ring ↔ MGB ↔ Wheels.
- **Key Equations:**
  - *Willis (speed):* $\omega_c = \dfrac{N_s \omega_s + N_r \omega_r}{N_s + N_r}$
  - *Torque ratio:* $T_{MGA} \cdot N_r = T_{MGB} \cdot N_s$
  - *Power balance:* $P_{ICE} + P_{MGA} + P_{MGB} = 0$
- **Six Operating Modes:** Pure EV, Engine-Only, Series-Assist, Parallel-Boost, Regenerative Braking, Standstill Charging.
- **Core Advantage:** ICE speed is **decoupled** from wheel speed → engine always operates near its BSFC sweet spot.
- **Representative System:** Toyota Hybrid System (THS) — Prius is the canonical example.
- **Efficiency Benchmark:** 40–55 % fuel economy improvement; 30–45 % CO₂ reduction.
- **Failure Mode to Remember:** If MGA fails, the system can still operate in limited series mode (MGB alone propels, engine charges via MGA acting as a passive brake — not recommended for prolonged use).
- **Unit Conversions (must memorize):** $1 \text{ rpm} = \pi/30 \text{ rad/s}$; $1 \text{ kW} = 1000 \text{ W}$; $1 \text{ N·m} \times 1 \text{ rad/s} = 1 \text{ W}$.
- **Examiner Expectations (KTU 2024):** Always include a labeled diagram; always state assumptions; always show unit conversions; always present the final answer in a **box**.
- **Comparison Triangle:** Series (decoupled, simple control, large motors) | Parallel (coupled, complex transmission, small motors) | **Series-Parallel (best of both, complex PSD, planetary gear as CVT replacement).**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Flow Control in Hybrid Drive-Train Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT615 - ALTERNATE FUELS AND ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Flow Control in Hybrid Drive-Train Topologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework for *Alternate Fuels and Energy Systems*, **Power Flow Control in Hybrid Drive-Train Topologies** refers to the coordinated management of mechanical and electrical energy transfer among multiple energy sources (Internal Combustion Engine, Fuel Cell, Battery, Ultra-Capacitor) and the propulsion path (electric machines, transmission, wheels) within a hybrid electric vehicle (HEV).

A **Hybrid Drive-Train Topology** is the architectural arrangement of prime movers and transmission elements that defines how power is split, combined, or routed between the engine and one or more electric machines. The principal topologies classified by the KTU syllabus are:

| Topology | Energy Path Identifier |
|----------|------------------------|
| **Series Hybrid** | ICE → Generator → Battery → Traction Motor → Wheels |
| **Parallel Hybrid** | ICE ‖ Traction Motor → Mechanical Coupler → Wheels |
| **Series-Parallel (Power-Split)** | ICE → Planetary Gearset + Two Motor-Generators → Wheels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Energy Management Strategy (EMS) is the supervisory controller that decides, at every instant, *how much* power each source must deliver. Its objective is to minimize equivalent fuel consumption while satisfying drive-ability, battery State-of-Charge (SOC) sustainment, and emission constraints.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a restaurant kitchen where two chefs (the ICE and the electric motor) must jointly prepare meals (deliver power to the wheels). The head chef (the *Energy Management Controller / EMS*) decides:
> - **Who cooks what** (torque split),
> - **Who rests** (engine on/off),
> - **Who stores leftovers for later** (battery charging/discharging).
>
> The **kitchen layout** is the *topology*. A **Series layout** is like a single delivery boy — one chef cooks, hands food to a server, who walks it to the table. A **Parallel layout** is like two chefs working side-by-side at the same table — both serve directly. A **Series-Parallel layout** is a hybrid kitchen with both direct and relayed delivery paths.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Gravitational acceleration:** **$g = 9.81\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$**
- **Wheel rolling resistance coefficient:** $\mu_{rr} \approx 0.008$ to $0.015$ for passenger cars
- **Aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d$:** typically **0.25 – 0.35**
- **Standard battery cell voltage:** $\sim \mathbf{3.6\text{–}3.7\ \mathrm{V}}$ (Li-ion nominal)
- **SOC operating window:** typically **$\mathbf{0.3} \leq \mathrm{SOC} \leq \mathbf{0.8}$**
- **Brake-specific fuel consumption (BSFC) minimum:** $\sim \mathbf{240\ \mathrm{g/kWh}}$ for modern gasoline engines

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Decomposition of Power Flow Control

A complete hybrid power-flow control system is decomposed into four functional layers:

1. **Driver Demand Layer** — Converts accelerator pedal position $\alpha \in [0,1]$ and brake pedal $\beta \in [0,1]$ into a *required wheel power* $P_{wheel}^*$.
2. **Energy Management Layer (EMS)** — Decides the power split ratios $u_1, u_2$ subject to SOC and emission constraints.
3. **Component Control Layer** — Operates the engine throttle, MG1 torque, MG2 torque, clutch engagement, and transmission gear.
4. **Supervisory / Fault Layer** — Detects faults, enforces limp-home modes, and protects battery limits.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Topology Choice

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why Series?** The ICE is mechanically decoupled from the wheels, so it can operate **permanently at its BSFC sweet-spot** (e.g., 250 g/kWh). However, **two energy conversions** (mechanical → electrical → mechanical) introduce $\sim \mathbf{15\%}$ extra losses. Series is best for **city buses** with frequent stop-go cycles.
> - **Why Parallel?** A **single energy conversion** path with both sources driving wheels directly gives highest efficiency on highways. However, mechanical coupling demands a **complex clutch/gearbox logic**. Best for **compact passenger cars**.
> - **Why Series-Parallel (e.g., Toyota THS)?** Combines the *engine sweet-spot operation* of series and the *direct mechanical drive efficiency* of parallel through a **planetary gearset (Power-Split Device, PSD)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Law | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|----------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Vehicle load demand (longitudinal dynamics) | $P_{req} = \frac{v}{\eta_t}\left(mgf_r\cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A v^2 + mg\sin\theta + \delta m \frac{dv}{dt}\right)$ | W; $\eta_t$ = drivetrain efficiency |
| 2 | Series hybrid power balance | $P_{eng} = P_{bat}/\eta_{gen} + P_{aux}$ | $P_{bat}>0 \Rightarrow$ discharge |
| 3 | Parallel hybrid torque balance | $T_{wheel} = (T_{eng} + T_{mot})\cdot i_g\cdot i_f\cdot \eta_t$ | Nm; $i_g$ = gear ratio |
| 4 | Parallel hybrid speed relation (single shaft) | $\omega_{wheel} = \omega_{eng} = \omega_{mot}$ | rad/s when coupled |
| 5 | Planetary gearset (Willis equation) | $(\omega_s - \omega_c)/(\omega_r - \omega_c) = -N_r/N_s$ | $N$ = tooth count; subscript $s$=sun, $r$=ring, $c$=carrier |
| 6 | Battery power model | $P_{bat} = V_{oc}\cdot I_{bat} - I_{bat}^2 R_{int}$ | W; $V_{oc}$ = open-circuit voltage |
| 7 | SOC dynamics | $\dot{\mathrm{SOC}} = -\dfrac{V_{oc} - \sqrt{V_{oc}^2 - 4R_{int}P_{bat}}}{2R_{int}Q_{bat}}$ | $\mathrm{S^{-1}}$ |
| 8 | Equivalent fuel-consumption factor (ECMS) | $\dot{m}_f^{eq} = \dot{m}_{f,eng} + s\cdot P_{bat}$ | kg/s; $s$ = equivalence factor (kg/W·s) |
| 9 | Engine BSFC | $\mathrm{BSFC} = \dot{m}_f / P_{eng}$ | g/kWh |
| 10 | Efficiency of electrical path | $\eta_e = \eta_{mot/inv}\cdot\eta_{gen/inv}$ | dimensionless $\approx 0.85$–$0.92$ each |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** When a question mentions *wheel speed $\omega_w$* and *wheel torque $T_w$*, you may write $P_w = T_w\omega_w$ **without** the vertical pipe symbol — use it only when expressing *magnitude*. Use $\lvert P \rvert$ typeset via `\vert`.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Power-flow control governs how a Toyota Prius delivers **4.7 L/100 km**, how a Volvo hybrid bus recaptures **30 %** of braking energy, and how a Formula-1 MGU-K (Motor Generator Unit – Kinetic) deploys **120 kW** during a qualifying lap. Every modern OEM employs *some* form of the formulations listed in §2.3.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Balance in a Series Hybrid Topology

**Given:** ICE produces mechanical power $P_{eng}$, generator converts to electrical with efficiency $\eta_g$, battery acts as a buffer with charging power $P_{ch}$ or discharging $P_{dis}$, traction motor drives wheels with efficiency $\eta_m$, auxiliaries draw $P_{aux}$.

**Step 1 — At the generator output:**
Electrical power leaving the generator equals mechanical power input multiplied by generator efficiency.

$$
P_{gen,elec} = \eta_g \cdot P_{eng}
$$

**Step 2 — At the DC bus, battery branches off:**
By Kirchhoff's Current Law (power analogue), the remainder goes to the traction inverter.

$$
P_{bus} = P_{gen,elec} - P_{ch}\quad \text{(charging mode)}
$$

**Step 3 — At the traction motor shaft:**
Mechanical power at wheels after subtracting auxiliary loads.

$$
P_{wheel} = \eta_m \cdot P_{bus} - P_{aux}
$$

**Step 4 — Combined power-balance equation for series hybrid:**

$$
\boxed{P_{wheel} = \eta_m\bigl(\eta_g P_{eng} - P_{ch}\bigr) - P_{aux}}
$$

**Step 5 — In discharging mode** ($P_{dis}>0$ supplied by battery to bus):

$$
P_{wheel} = \eta_m\bigl(\eta_g P_{eng} + P_{dis}\bigr) - P_{aux}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** The examiner often asks to "derive the power-flow equation for a series HEV." A common student error is forgetting the **two cascaded efficiencies** $\eta_g$ and $\eta_m$, leading to a 2-mark deduction.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque and Speed Coupling in a Parallel Hybrid

**Given:** ICE and traction motor coupled to a single shaft via a clutch $C_1$ (or torque-coupler). Transmission ratio $i_g$, final-drive ratio $i_f$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t$.

**Step 1 — Speed equality (when clutch engaged):**
Both sources rotate at the same shaft speed.

$$
\omega_{eng} = \omega_{mot} = \omega_{shaft}
$$

**Step 2 — Torque addition:**
The mechanical coupler sums the individual torques.

$$
T_{shaft} = T_{eng} + T_{mot}
$$

**Step 3 — Torque at the wheel (after gear reduction):**

$$
T_{wheel} = (T_{eng} + T_{mot})\cdot i_g \cdot i_f \cdot \eta_t
$$

**Step 4 — Power identity check:**
Verify with $P = T\omega$.

$$
P_{wheel} = T_{wheel}\cdot \omega_{wheel} = (T_{eng}\omega_{eng} + T_{mot}\omega_{mot})\,i_g i_f \eta_t
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalence Statement:** $P_{wheel} = (P_{eng} + P_{mot})\eta_t$ only when speeds are equal. This is the **defining energy equation of a parallel hybrid**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Planetary Gearset Kinematics for Power-Split Hybrid

**Given:** Sun gear (S), ring gear (R), carrier (C). Engine on carrier, MG1 on sun, MG2 on ring (Toyota THS convention).

**Step 1 — Willis / Fundamental Relation of planetary gearing:**

$$
\frac{\omega_s - \omega_c}{\omega_r - \omega_c} = -\frac{N_r}{N_s} = -k
$$

where $k = N_r/N_s > 1$ (ring has more teeth).

**Step 2 — Solving for MG1 speed in terms of engine and wheel speed:**

$$
\omega_{MG1} = \omega_s = (1+k)\omega_c - k\omega_r
$$

$$
\omega_s = (1+k)\,\omega_{eng} - k\,\omega_{wheel}
$$

**Step 3 — Power balance at the gearset (kinetic, ignoring losses):**

$$
P_{eng} + P_{MG1} + P_{MG2} = 0 \quad \text{(sum of input powers at PSD)}
$$

**Step 4 — Sign convention — Take into gear as positive:**

$$
P_{MG1} = -k\,P_{eng}
$$

**Step 5 — Power at the ring (=wheel shaft):**

$$
P_{wheel} = (1+k)\,P_{eng}
$$

**Step 6 — MG2 handles residual demand:**

$$
P_{MG2} = P_{wheel,demand} - P_{wheel}
$$

This is the **fundamental reason** the power-split hybrid can continuously vary its effective gear ratio.
